topics.title: Timber, Steel, Aluminum, and Plastic Formwork
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timber, Steel, Aluminum, and Plastic Formwork

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Formwork

**Formwork** (also called **shuttering** or **molds**) is a temporary or permanent mold into which fresh concrete or similar materials are poured. The formwork holds the concrete in shape until it gains sufficient strength to become self-supporting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Formwork is the total system of support for freshly placed concrete, including the mold or sheathing that contacts the concrete as well as all supporting members, hardware, and necessary bracing.

### 1.2 Classification of Formwork Based on Material

| Material Type | Common Name | Reusability |
|---|---|---|
| **Timber** | Traditional formwork | 4–6 reuses |
| **Steel** | Heavy-duty formwork | 100+ reuses |
| **Aluminum** | Lightweight formwork | 200+ reuses |
| **Plastic** | Modular formwork | 100+ reuses |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Cake Pan" Concept:**
> Think of formwork exactly like a **cake pan (mold)** in a bakery. The batter (fresh concrete) is poured into the pan, and until it sets in the oven (cures), the pan holds its shape. Once the cake hardens, the pan is removed. Similarly, formwork holds the wet concrete in the desired geometric shape until the concrete achieves its design strength. The pan (formwork) is then stripped away.
>
> - **Wooden pan** = Timber formwork (cheap, used 4–6 times like a paper cake cup)
> - **Stainless-steel pan** = Steel formwork (expensive, used 100+ times like a commercial bakery pan)
> - **Aluminum pan** = Lightweight formwork (light, used 200+ times)
> - **Silicone/plastic mold** = Plastic formwork (modular, flexible, easy release)

---

## 1.4 Formal Definitions for Each Type

### 1.4.1 Timber Formwork
Timber formwork is the most traditional and widely used formwork system, constructed from **timber planks, joists, struts, and plywood sheathing**. It is built on-site by carpenters and is suitable for small to medium-scale projects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Reference:** The Indian Standard **IS 14687:1999** governs guidelines for timber formwork, and **IS 4990:2011** specifies plywood formwork.

### 1.4.2 Steel Formwork
Steel formwork consists of **steel panels fabricated from rolled steel sheets, angle sections, and stiffening ribs**, welded together to form a robust, modular, and reusable panel system.

### 1.4.3 Aluminum Formwork
Aluminum formwork is a **lightweight, factory-fabricated, modular panel system** made from aluminum alloy extrusions. It is widely used in **mass housing and high-rise construction** due to its low weight and high reusability.

### 1.4.4 Plastic Formwork
Plastic formwork is manufactured from **high-density polyethylene (HDPE), polypropylene, or fiberglass-reinforced plastic (FRP)**. It is primarily used for **modular repetitive construction** and **decorative concrete**.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Formwork Performance Metrics (BOLDED):**
> - **Density of fresh concrete** = **24 kN/m³** (≈ **2400 kg/m³**)
> - **Hydrostatic pressure** on vertical formwork = **γ_c × h** (where γ_c = unit weight of concrete, h = height)
> - **Standard plywood sheet size** = **1.22 m × 2.44 m (4 ft × 8 ft)**
> - **Maximum reuses:** Timber = **4–6**, Steel = **100–200**, Aluminum = **200–500**, Plastic = **100+**
> - **Lateral pressure (IS 14687):** **P_max = γ × h** for full hydrostatic (rate of placement < 2 m/hr)
> - **Design minimum strength of formwork concrete** at stripping = **2/3 of design strength** or as per IS code.

---

## 1.6 Why Study Different Formwork Materials?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Perspective:** The choice of formwork material directly impacts:
> 1. **Construction speed** (cycle time)
> 2. **Cost per use** (capital cost ÷ number of reuses)
> 3. **Quality of concrete finish** (fair-faced, architectural)
> 4. **Safety of workers** (collapse prevention)
> 5. **Environmental sustainability** (carbon footprint)
>
> The formwork cost can be **35–60% of the total RCC cost** — making material selection a critical engineering decision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Timber Formwork — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Components of Timber Formwork

A complete timber formwork system consists of:

1. **Sheathing (Sheeting)** — the surface in direct contact with concrete
   - Material: Timber planks or plywood
   - Thickness: 12 mm, 18 mm, 25 mm
2. **Studs (Joists)** — vertical members supporting the sheathing
3. **Wales (Walings)** — horizontal members supporting studs
4. **Bracing** — diagonal members for lateral stability
5. **Struts / Props** — vertical load-bearing members (typically adjustable steel props)
6. **Tie rods** — hold opposite formwork faces together to resist bursting pressure
7. **Spreader blocks** — maintain the wall thickness between opposite form faces

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic of Timber Formwork

1. **Fresh concrete** is poured into the cavity formed by two parallel timber formwork panels.
2. The **hydrostatic pressure** of the wet concrete pushes outward on the panels.
3. The **sheathing** transfers this load to the **studs** (vertical).
4. The studs transfer the load to the **wales** (horizontal beams).
5. The wales transfer the load to **tie rods** that connect the two opposite panels.
6. **Vertical props/struts** carry the entire assembly to the ground, supporting dead weight of wet concrete + construction live load.
7. After concrete cures to **2/3 of design strength**, the formwork is stripped.

### 2.1.3 Properties of Timber for Formwork

| Property | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Moisture content (acceptable)** | < 15% |
| **Minimum density** | 500 kg/m³ |
| **Common species** | Teak, Sal, Pine, Deodar |
| **Plywood grade (for formwork)** | Boiling Water Proof (BWP) / Exterior grade |
| **Plywood thickness** | 6, 9, 12, 18, 25 mm |
| **Permissible stress (typical)** | 10–15 N/mm² in bending |
| **Swelling/shrinkage (with moisture)** | Up to 2% (problem for re-use) |

### 2.1.4 Advantages of Timber Formwork

- **Low initial cost** (locally available)
- **Easy to cut, shape, and assemble** on site
- **Suitable for irregular shapes and complex geometries**
- **No heavy equipment required** for erection
- **Replaceable parts** can be purchased locally

### 2.1.5 Disadvantages of Timber Formwork

- **Limited reuses (4–6 times)** → high cost per use
- **Susceptible to moisture absorption** → warping, swelling
- **Lower quality finish** compared to steel/aluminum
- **Labor-intensive** → high cost in skilled-labor shortage areas
- **Fire hazard** — timber is combustible
- **Less dimensional accuracy** under load

---

## 2.2 Steel Formwork — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Components of Steel Formwork

1. **Steel Sheet (skin)** — 2 mm to 3 mm thick MS sheet
2. **Stiffening angle sections** — L 50×50×6 or L 75×75×8 angles welded to the back of the skin
3. **Edge channels** — for panel-to-panel connection
4. **Clamps, wedges, and bolts** — for fast assembly
5. **Tie rods & wing nuts** — hold opposite panels
6. **Props (adjustable steel props)** — carry vertical load

### 2.2.2 Working Logic of Steel Formwork

1. Pre-engineered **steel panels of standard size** (e.g., 600 mm × 900 mm, 600 mm × 1200 mm) are assembled by clamps.
2. The **steel skin** resists lateral hydrostatic pressure.
3. The **stiffening angles** transfer the pressure to the **panel edge channels**.
4. The **tie rods** balance horizontal pressure between two opposite walls.
5. The **steel props** carry the vertical load.
6. After concrete hardening, the formwork is unbolted and lifted out (often by crane).

### 2.2.3 Properties of Steel Formwork

| Property | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Steel grade** | Mild Steel IS 2062 / ASTM A36 |
| **Density** | **7850 kg/m³** |
| **Yield strength** | 250 N/mm² (MS) |
| **Young's modulus** | 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² |
| **Skin thickness** | 2–3 mm |
| **Panel weight (typical)** | 35–50 kg/m² |
| **Reuses** | 100–200+ |
| **Striking time** | Earlier than timber (better heat dissipation) |

### 2.2.4 Advantages of Steel Formwork

- **High reusability** (100–200+ times) → low long-term cost
- **Dimensional accuracy** → excellent quality of finish
- **High strength** → suitable for heavy loads and tall structures
- **Speed of construction** → modular assembly
- **Waterproof and fire-resistant**
- **Smooth concrete surface** → minimal plastering required

### 2.2.5 Disadvantages of Steel Formwork

- **High initial capital cost**
- **Heavy panels** → requires crane for handling
- **Susceptible to corrosion** → needs proper maintenance
- **Limited to modular/rectilinear shapes**
- **Difficult to make curved or irregular shapes**

---

## 2.3 Aluminum Formwork — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Concept
Aluminum formwork is a **factory-engineered, modular, lightweight panel system** primarily used in **mass housing projects** (e.g., Mivan formwork system). It is especially popular in India for high-rise apartments.

### 2.3.2 Working Logic
1. Aluminum panels are made from **high-strength aluminum alloy extrusions** (typically **6061-T6** or **6082-T6**).
2. Panels are **bolted together** on-site to form walls, slabs, beams, and columns simultaneously — often called a **monolithic pour**.
3. The system's **light weight (≈ 25–30 kg/m²)** allows **manual handling** without cranes.
4. The formwork supports the **entire floor cycle** (slab + walls + columns poured together) and is stripped in **24–48 hours**.
5. The achieved concrete finish is so smooth that it is often called a **"fair-faced" finish**, requiring minimal or no plastering.

### 2.3.3 Properties of Aluminum Formwork

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Alloy** | 6061-T6 / 6082-T6 |
| **Density** | **2700 kg/m³** (≈ 1/3 of steel) |
| **Yield strength** | 240–270 N/mm² |
| **Tensile strength** | 290 N/mm² |
| **Panel weight** | 18–25 kg/m² (very light) |
| **Reuses** | 200–500 times |
| **Construction cycle** | 4–6 days per floor |

### 2.3.4 Advantages of Aluminum Formwork

- **Very light** → manual handling, no crane required
- **High reusability** (200+ times) → economical for mass housing
- **Very fast construction** → 4-day floor cycle possible
- **Excellent finish** → often eliminates plastering
- **Corrosion-resistant** (aluminum oxide layer is protective)
- **Monolithic construction** → better structural integrity

### 2.3.5 Disadvantages of Aluminum Formwork

- **Very high initial cost** (3–5× steel)
- **Less versatile** for non-repetitive designs
- **Repair is difficult** (welding aluminum requires special techniques)
- **Higher thermal conductivity** → may affect concrete curing in hot climates
- **Not suitable for small / irregular projects**

---

## 2.4 Plastic Formwork — Deep Analysis

### 2.4.1 Concept
Plastic formwork is made from **engineered plastics** such as:
- **High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)**
- **Polypropylene (PP)**
- **Glass Fiber Reinforced Plastic (GFRP)**
- **ABS plastic**

It is used for **modular, repetitive, and small concrete elements** (e.g., columns, plinths, manholes, decorative panels).

### 2.4.2 Properties

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Density (HDPE)** | 950 kg/m³ |
| **Tensile strength** | 20–30 N/mm² |
| **Service temperature** | -20°C to +80°C |
| **Reuses** | 100+ times |
| **Panel weight** | Very light (5–10 kg/m²) |
| **Surface finish** | Smooth, glossy |

### 2.4.3 Advantages of Plastic Formwork

- **Lightweight** → easy to transport and handle
- **No release agent required** → concrete does not stick
- **Waterproof, corrosion-proof, chemical-resistant**
- **Easy to clean** (just water wash)
- **Modular** → fast assembly
- **Eco-friendly** options available (recycled plastic)

### 2.4.4 Disadvantages of Plastic Formwork

- **Lower strength** than steel → not suitable for high pressure
- **Limited to small/medium-height pours**
- **Sensitive to high temperatures** (warping at >80°C)
- **Limited sizes** available in market
- **UV degradation** if stored in sunlight

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Unit** | **Use in KTU Problems** |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure on vertical form | $P = \gamma_c \cdot h$ | kN/m² | Lateral pressure calculation |
| 2 | Density of fresh concrete | $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³ | kN/m³ | Numerical problems |
| 3 | Rate of placement formula (ACI) | $P = \gamma_c \left(0.6 + \frac{80}{T + 17.8}\right)$ | kN/m² | Limited to 150 kN/m² |
| 4 | Effective hydrostatic head | $h_{eff} = \frac{P}{\gamma_c}$ | m | Equivalent liquid head |
| 5 | Maximum placement rate for full hydrostatic | $R \le 2$ m/hr | m/hr | Condition check |
| 6 | Stripping strength requirement | $f_{strip} \ge \frac{2}{3} f_{ck}$ | N/mm² | Time of striking |
| 7 | Cost per use | $C_{use} = \frac{C_{capital} + C_{maintenance}}{N_{reuses}}$ | ₹/m² | Economic comparison |
| 8 | Bending stress in sheathing | $\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | N/mm² | Timber plywood design |
| 9 | Deflection of sheathing | $\delta = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI}$ | mm | Serviceability check |
| 10 | Tie rod tensile capacity | $T = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot f_y$ | N | Holding opposite formwork |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Legend:**
> - $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of fresh concrete = **24 kN/m³**
> - $h$ = height of fresh concrete (m)
> - $T$ = concrete temperature (°C)
> - $R$ = rate of concrete placement (m/hr)
> - $f_{ck}$ = characteristic compressive strength of concrete
> - $d$ = tie rod diameter; $f_y$ = yield strength of steel
> - $L$ = span; $E$ = Young's modulus; $I$ = moment of inertia
> - $w$ = uniformly distributed load; $M$ = bending moment
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Lateral Pressure on Vertical Formwork (IS 14687 / ACI Method)

### 3.1.1 Statement
Derive the expression for the **lateral pressure exerted by fresh concrete on vertical formwork walls**, considering the rate of placement and concrete temperature.

### 3.1.2 Given Data / Assumptions

- Unit weight of fresh concrete: $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³
- Rate of placement: $R$ m/hr
- Concrete temperature: $T$ in °C
- Type of cement: Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC)

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Hydrostatic Pressure (No Setting)**

If concrete behaves like a dense liquid (no setting), the pressure at depth $h$ is given by:

$$
P_{hydro} = \gamma_c \cdot h
$$

For $h = 1$ m: $P_{hydro} = 24 \times 1 = 24$ kN/m²

**Step 2: Modified Pressure Considering Setting Time (ACI Formula)**

Concrete begins to set and stiffen after some time, so the **effective pressure** is less than full hydrostatic. The ACI Committee 622 (now 347) gives:

$$
P = \gamma_c \cdot C_w \cdot \left(0.6 + \frac{80}{T + 17.8}\right)
$$

where $C_w$ is a **coefficient accounting for unit weight of concrete** (typically $C_w = 1$ for normal-weight concrete = 24 kN/m³).

**Step 3: Bound the Pressure**

The pressure is bounded as:

$$
P_{min} = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{and} \quad P_{max} = 150 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Equivalent Hydrostatic Head**

The depth corresponding to the design pressure:

$$
h_{eff} = \frac{P}{\gamma_c}
$$

**Step 5: Example Numerical Evaluation**

Suppose $T = 30$°C and rate of placement $R = 2$ m/hr. Compute $P$:

$$
P = 24 \times \left(0.6 + \frac{80}{30 + 17.8}\right)
$$

$$
P = 24 \times \left(0.6 + \frac{80}{47.8}\right)
$$

$$
P = 24 \times \left(0.6 + 1.6736\right)
$$

$$
P = 24 \times 2.2736 = 54.566 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Equivalent head:

$$
h_{eff} = \frac{54.566}{24} = 2.2736 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

For a 3 m tall wall poured in 1.5 hours, the formwork is **NOT** designed for full hydrostatic of 72 kN/m² but only for 54.57 kN/m² because concrete below the top 0.73 m has begun to stiffen.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost-Per-Use Analysis (Comparative Formwork Economics)

### 3.2.1 Objective
Compare the **cost per use** of timber, steel, aluminum, and plastic formwork.

### 3.2.2 Formula

$$
C_{use} = \frac{C_{cap} + C_{maint} \cdot N}{N}
$$

where:
- $C_{cap}$ = capital cost per m²
- $C_{maint}$ = maintenance cost per m² per use
- $N$ = total number of reuses

### 3.2.3 Sample Numerical Data

| Type | $C_{cap}$ (₹/m²) | $C_{maint}$ (₹/m²/use) | $N$ (uses) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Timber | 800 | 100 | 5 |
| Steel | 3,500 | 30 | 100 |
| Aluminum | 7,500 | 25 | 200 |
| Plastic | 4,000 | 20 | 80 |

**Step 1: Timber Formwork Cost/Use**

$$
C_{timber} = \frac{800 + 100 \times 5}{5} = \frac{800 + 500}{5} = \frac{1300}{5} = 260 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Steel Formwork Cost/Use**

$$
C_{steel} = \frac{3500 + 30 \times 100}{100} = \frac{3500 + 3000}{100} = \frac{6500}{100} = 65 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Aluminum Formwork Cost/Use**

$$
C_{aluminum} = \frac{7500 + 25 \times 200}{200} = \frac{7500 + 5000}{200} = \frac{12500}{200} = 62.5 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Plastic Formwork Cost/Use**

$$
C_{plastic} = \frac{4000 + 20 \times 80}{80} = \frac{4000 + 1600}{80} = \frac{5600}{80} = 70 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Ranking of Economy (Lowest = Best)**

| Rank | Type | Cost/Use (₹/m²) |
|:---:|---|:---:|
| 1 | Aluminum | 62.5 |
| 2 | Steel | 65.0 |
| 3 | Plastic | 70.0 |
| 4 | Timber | 260.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Despite high capital cost, **aluminum and steel formwork are far more economical than timber** for repetitive projects. This is a **high-yield KTU question** — it is often asked as a 7-mark or 14-mark problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Design Bending Moment for Sheathing

### 3.3.1 Problem
A 18 mm thick plywood sheathing is supported on studs at 400 mm spacing. The lateral pressure is 40 kN/m². Determine the maximum bending moment per meter width.

**Step 1: Convert Pressure to Line Load**

$$
w = P \times 1 \text{ m} = 40 \times 1 = 40 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the Support Condition**

Sheathing acts as a **continuous beam** on multiple supports. For a continuous beam with multiple equal spans, the maximum positive bending moment is approximately:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{10}
$$

where $L$ = center-to-center spacing between studs = 0.4 m.

**Step 3: Calculate M_max**

$$
M_{max} = \frac{40 \times (0.4)^2}{10} = \frac{40 \times 0.16}{10} = \frac{6.4}{10} = 0.64 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 4: Check Section Modulus**

For 18 mm plywood, $Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \times 18^2}{6} = 54000 \text{ mm}^3 = 5.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$

**Step 5: Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{0.64 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{5.4 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3} = 11.85 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 6: Check Against Allowable Stress**

For plywood, $\sigma_{perm} = 12$ N/mm². Since $11.85 \le 12$ → **SAFE**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most important table for KTU exams — memorize it!**

| **Parameter** | **Timber** | **Steel** | **Aluminum** | **Plastic** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 500–800 | 7850 | 2700 | 950 |
| **Initial cost** | Low | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Reuses** | 4–6 | 100–200 | 200–500 | 100+ |
| **Weight/m²** | Light | Heavy (35–50 kg) | Very light (18–25 kg) | Very light (5–10 kg) |
| **Quality of finish** | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Construction speed** | Slow | Fast | Very fast | Fast |
| **Skilled labor needed** | Yes (carpenter) | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Crane needed?** | No | Yes | No | No |
| **Irregular shapes** | Excellent | Poor | Poor | Limited |
| **Fire resistance** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Good (if dry) | Poor (needs paint) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Lifespan** | 4–6 pours | 100+ pours | 200+ pours | 100+ pours |
| **Eco-friendliness** | Medium (deforestation) | Medium | Good (recyclable) | Variable |
| **Best suited for** | Small / irregular | Heavy, modular | Mass housing | Modular, decorative |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Formwork System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FRESH CONCRETE LOAD]:::load
    B[Lateral Hydrostatic Pressure]:::load
    C[Vertical Dead Load]:::load
    D[Lateral Load]:::load

    E[FORMWORK SYSTEM]:::core
    F[SHEATHING]:::skin
    G[STUDS / JOISTS]:::support
    H[WALES / WALINGS]:::support
    I[TIE RODS]:::tie
    J[BRACING]:::support
    K[PROPS / STRUTS]:::support
    L[FOUNDATION / FLOOR]:::ground

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D

    B --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I -.Balances.-> H
    H --> J
    C --> K
    K --> L
    D --> J
    J --> L

    classDef load fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef skin fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tie fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ground fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart shows how the three load types (lateral hydrostatic, vertical dead, and lateral wind/bracing) are transferred step-by-step through the formwork components — from **sheathing → studs → wales → tie rods/bracing → props → foundation**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cross-Section of a Typical Timber Formwork Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[FRESH CONCRETE\nWall Thickness 200 mm]:::concrete
    T1[PLYWOOD SHEATHING\n18 mm]:::skin
    T2[STUD\nVertical 100x50 mm]:::wood
    T3[WALE\nHorizontal 150x100 mm]:::wood
    T4[TIE ROD\nHigh Tensile Steel]:::tie
    T5[BRACING\nDiagonal Strut]:::wood
    T6[STEEL PROP\nAdjustable]:::steel
    T7[FOUNDATION\nGround Level]:::ground

    C --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T3 --> T5
    T3 --> T6
    T6 --> T7

    classDef concrete fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef skin fill:#DEB887,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wood fill:#F4A460,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tie fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef steel fill:#708090,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef ground fill:#696969,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Comparative Material Property Radar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROPS[Material Property Comparison]
        direction TB
        P1[Cost-Effectiveness]
        P2[Reusability]
        P3[Lightweight]
        P4[Finish Quality]
        P5[Construction Speed]
        P6[Shape Versatility]
    end

    subgraph TIM[Timber Ratings]
        direction TB
        T1A[★★★★★ Shape Versatility]
        T1B[★★ Cost]
        T1C[★ Reusability]
    end

    subgraph STL[Steel Ratings]
        direction TB
        S1A[★★★★★ Finish]
        S1B[★★★★ Reusability]
        S1C[★ Lightweight]
    end

    subgraph ALU[Aluminum Ratings]
        direction TB
        AL1A[★★★★★ Lightweight]
        AL1B[★★★★★ Reusability]
        AL1C[★★★ Cost]
    end

    subgraph PLS[Plastic Ratings]
        direction TB
        PL1A[★★★★★ Lightweight]
        PL1B[★★★ Finish]
        PL1C[★★★ Reusability]
    end

    PROPS --> TIM
    PROPS --> STL
    PROPS --> ALU
    PROPS --> PLS
```

> **Reading the Block Architecture:** Each material has signature strengths. **Timber = versatility, Steel = strength & finish, Aluminum = lightweight & reusability, Plastic = modularity**. The choice is **engineering judgment** based on project constraints.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define formwork. List any four requirements of an ideal formwork.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Formwork is the temporary structure used as a mold to hold fresh concrete in the desired shape until it gains sufficient strength to be self-supporting.
>
> **Four Requirements of Ideal Formwork:**
> 1. **Strength** — must resist dead load of wet concrete + construction live loads
> 2. **Stiffness** — deflection should be within limits (L/270 for slabs)
> 3. **Economy** — low cost per use through reusability
> 4. **Stripping ease** — should be removable without damaging concrete
> 5. **Watertightness** — no leakage of cement slurry
> 6. **Accuracy** — should produce correct shape and dimensions

*(Any 4 of the above = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between timber formwork and steel formwork in terms of reusability and finish quality.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Timber Formwork | Steel Formwork |
|---|---|---|
| **Reusability** | 4–6 times only | 100–200 times |
| **Finish quality** | Moderate; needs plastering | Excellent; often needs no plastering |
| **Reason** | Timber absorbs moisture, warps | Steel is dimensionally stable, smooth surface |
| **Cost/use** | High (due to low reuse) | Low (due to high reuse) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain the **components and working of a timber formwork system** with a neat sketch. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wall 3 m high and 0.2 m thick is to be cast with concrete having a unit weight of 24 kN/m³. The rate of placement is 1.5 m/hr and the concrete temperature is 28°C. Calculate the **lateral pressure on the formwork** using the ACI formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Components of Timber Formwork:**

1. **Sheathing (Plywood)** — 12 to 18 mm thick, in direct contact with concrete. Transmits concrete pressure to the studs.
2. **Studs (Vertical Joists)** — 50×100 mm or 100×100 mm timber sections, spaced at 400–600 mm.
3. **Wales (Horizontal Walings)** — 75×150 mm or 100×150 mm horizontal members that hold studs together.
4. **Bracing** — diagonal members for stability against lateral forces.
5. **Tie Rods** — high-tensile steel rods that hold two opposite formwork faces together.
6. **Props / Struts** — adjustable steel or timber posts carrying vertical load to the ground.
7. **Spreaders** — small blocks to maintain wall thickness.

**Working:**
- Fresh concrete → hydrostatic pressure on sheathing → transferred to studs → to wales → to tie rods / props → to ground.
- After concrete attains **2/3 of characteristic strength** (e.g., for M25 concrete ≈ 16.67 N/mm²), formwork is stripped.

**Advantages (any 4):**
- Low initial cost; locally available material
- Easy to fabricate, cut, and shape
- Suitable for irregular / curved structures
- Replaceable parts available easily
- No heavy equipment needed for small works

**Disadvantages (any 4):**
- Limited reuses (4–6 times)
- High cost per use in long run
- Susceptible to moisture, warping, shrinkage
- Labor-intensive; needs skilled carpenters
- Fire hazard
- Inferior finish compared to steel/aluminum

*(Neat sketch mandatory — refer to the diagram in Section 4.3 for guidance.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Height of wall, $h = 3$ m
- Thickness = 0.2 m (irrelevant for pressure)
- Unit weight, $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³
- Rate of placement, $R = 1.5$ m/hr
- Concrete temperature, $T = 28$°C

**Step 1: Apply ACI Formula**

$$
P = \gamma_c \cdot C_w \cdot \left(0.6 + \frac{80}{T + 17.8}\right)
$$

Assuming normal weight concrete, $C_w = 1$.

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$
P = 24 \times 1 \times \left(0.6 + \frac{80}{28 + 17.8}\right)
$$

$$
P = 24 \times \left(0.6 + \frac{80}{45.8}\right)
$$

$$
P = 24 \times \left(0.6 + 1.747\right)
$$

$$
P = 24 \times 2.347 = 56.32 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Check Full Hydrostatic Pressure**

$$
P_{hydro} = \gamma_c \times h = 24 \times 3 = 72 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Take the Smaller Value**

Since $P_{ACI} = 56.32 \text{ kN/m}^2 < P_{hydro} = 72 \text{ kN/m}^2$, design pressure = **56.32 kN/m²**.

**Step 5: Equivalent Hydrostatic Head**

$$
h_{eff} = \frac{P}{\gamma_c} = \frac{56.32}{24} = 2.347 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**
- Design the formwork for a pressure of **56.32 kN/m²**.
- This is equivalent to the hydrostatic pressure of concrete of height **2.347 m** instead of full 3 m.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**(a)** Compare **timber, steel, aluminum, and plastic formwork** in terms of (i) reusability, (ii) cost per use, (iii) surface finish, (iv) suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A high-rise building project requires formwork for **200 floor cycles** of typical floor area 400 m². Compare the **total cost** of using (i) timber formwork (reusability = 5 cycles) and (ii) steel formwork (reusability = 100 cycles), assuming capital cost of timber = ₹1,000/m², steel = ₹4,000/m², and maintenance cost of ₹100/m²/use and ₹30/m²/use respectively. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

| Parameter | Timber | Steel | Aluminum | Plastic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Reusability** | 4–6 times | 100–200 times | 200–500 times | 100+ times |
| **(ii) Cost per use** | High (₹200–300/m²) | Low (₹60–70/m²) | Lowest (₹50–70/m²) | Low (₹70–80/m²) |
| **(iii) Surface finish** | Moderate; needs plaster | Excellent; fair-faced possible | Excellent; often no plaster | Good; glossy |
| **(iv) Suitability** | Small, irregular | Heavy, modular, repetitive | Mass housing, high-rise | Modular, decorative, prefab |

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Total area to be cast = 200 floors × 400 m² = 80,000 m²
- Timber: $C_{cap} = 1000$ ₹/m²; $C_{maint} = 100$ ₹/m²/use; $N = 5$ uses
- Steel: $C_{cap} = 4000$ ₹/m²; $C_{maint} = 30$ ₹/m²/use; $N = 100$ uses

**Step 1: Number of Formwork Sets Required**

For Timber:
$$
N_{sets, timber} = \frac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ sets}
$$

For Steel:
$$
N_{sets, steel} = \frac{200}{100} = 2 \text{ sets}
$$

**Step 2: Cost Per Use**

For Timber:
$$
C_{use, timber} = \frac{1000 + 100 \times 5}{5} = \frac{1500}{5} = 300 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

For Steel:
$$
C_{use, steel} = \frac{4000 + 30 \times 100}{100} = \frac{7000}{100} = 70 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Total Cost**

For Timber:
$$
C_{total, timber} = 80,000 \times 300 = ₹ 2,40,00,000 \quad (= ₹ 2.4 \text{ Crore})
$$

For Steel:
$$
C_{total, steel} = 80,000 \times 70 = ₹ 56,00,000 \quad (= ₹ 0.56 \text{ Crore})
$$

**Step 4: Savings Using Steel**

$$
\text{Savings} = 2.4 - 0.56 = ₹ 1.84 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**
Steel formwork saves **₹ 1.84 Crore** compared to timber for this high-rise project, despite its higher initial investment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $C_w$ coefficient** in the ACI formula — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Not bounding the pressure** between 30 kN/m² and 150 kN/m² as per ACI — lose 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Confusing reusability and lifespan** — reusability = number of pours, not years.
> 4. **Writing "timber is cheaper" without computing cost-per-use** — examiners want numerical comparison.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the sketch** in 7-mark descriptive answers — leads to 1–2 mark loss.
> 6. **For IS 14687 references** — use Indian Standards (IS 14687, IS 4990) where applicable.
> 7. **Unit errors in pressure calculation** — use kN/m² consistently, NOT kg/m².

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MUST MEMORIZE**
>
> ✅ **Formwork** is a temporary mold for fresh concrete; it is stripped once concrete attains **2/3 of $f_{ck}$**.
>
> ✅ **Timber formwork** — reuses 4–6 times, density 500–800 kg/m³, requires BWP plywood.
>
> ✅ **Steel formwork** — reuses 100–200 times, density 7850 kg/m³, skin 2–3 mm thick, panels weigh 35–50 kg/m².
>
> ✅ **Aluminum formwork** — reuses 200–500 times, density 2700 kg/m³, used in mass housing (Mivan system), 4-day floor cycle.
>
> ✅ **Plastic formwork** — made of HDPE / PP / GFRP, reuses 100+ times, very lightweight, decorative use.
>
> ✅ **Density of fresh concrete** = **24 kN/m³** (universal value in KTU problems).
>
> ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = \gamma_c \cdot h$ — used when rate of placement < 2 m/hr.
>
> ✅ **ACI formula** $P = 24 \times (0.6 + 80/(T+17.8))$ — bounded between 30 and 150 kN/m².
>
> ✅ **Cost-per-use formula** = $(C_{cap} + C_{maint} \times N) / N$ — use this for any economic comparison.
>
> ✅ **Components of any formwork**: Sheathing → Studs → Wales → Tie Rods → Bracing → Props → Foundation.
>
> ✅ **IS Standards to remember**: IS 14687 (formwork), IS 4990 (plywood formwork), IS 2062 (steel).
>
> ✅ **High-yield KTU facts**: Steel and aluminum formwork are 3–4× more economical than timber for repetitive works.
>
> ✅ **Exam tip**: Always draw a labeled sketch in 7-mark formwork questions — 1–2 marks reserved for diagram.
>
> ✅ **Most-asked question pattern**: "Compare X, Y, Z formworks" or "Calculate lateral pressure using ACI formula with given temperature."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties and Selection Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Defining Formwork: The Engineering Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Formwork (also called shuttering) is a temporary mould or structure used in construction to support and shape freshly placed concrete until it gains sufficient strength to be self-supporting. It determines the geometry, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy of the finished concrete element.*

In the vocabulary of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, formwork is treated as a **temporary structure** designed to carry the **vertical (gravity) and horizontal (lateral) loads** imposed by fresh concrete, construction workers, equipment, and environmental forces during the casting and curing phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECET742 – Module 1):**
> The properties and selection criteria of formwork govern the **economy**, **safety**, and **quality** of any reinforced cement concrete (RCC) construction project. A poorly selected formwork system accounts for nearly **35% to 60% of the total cost** of the concrete frame in a typical building project.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Cake Tin" Principle

Imagine baking a cake. The cake batter is wet, heavy, soft, and shapeless — much like freshly mixed concrete. Without a rigid container (the cake tin), the batter would simply collapse into a puddle. The cake tin must:
- Hold the heavy batter without breaking (**Strength**).
- Keep its shape so the cake has straight sides (**Rigidity**).
- Be easy to remove once the cake is set (**Strippability / Reusability**).
- Not leak batter through the joints (**Water-tightness**).
- Be lightweight enough to lift and clean (**Handling**).

**Formwork is the "cake tin" of civil engineering.** The wet concrete is the "batter," and the formwork holds it in the exact desired geometric shape until the concrete hardens and becomes self-supporting.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Insight:** When the formwork is stripped (removed), the concrete should be strong enough to carry its own weight plus any construction loads. This typically requires a minimum compressive strength of **2 N/mm² to 5 N/mm²** depending on structural element type.

## 1.3 Key Properties of Formwork — An Overview

The fundamental engineering properties demanded of a formwork system are listed below. Each is a **mandatory design parameter** under KTU Board Examinations.

### (a) Strength
The formwork must safely resist all imposed **vertical loads (dead + live)** and **lateral pressures** from fresh concrete without failure. Strength is governed by the **allowable stress** in the formwork material and the **bearing capacity** of its supports.

### (b) Rigidity (Stiffness)
Stiffness is the resistance of the formwork to **deflection** under load. Excessive deflection causes the finished concrete to sag, bulge, or wave — leading to aesthetic and structural issues. Formwork deflection is generally limited to **L/360 of the span** (where L is the unsupported length) for exposed surfaces.

### (c) Stability
The formwork must remain **plumb, level, and laterally braced** during concrete placement. Stability prevents overturning, sliding, and buckling, especially under wind loads or vibrator action.

### (d) Durability
Formwork must withstand **multiple repetitions (reuses)** without significant loss of strength or surface quality. Steel formwork can typically achieve **100+ repetitions**, while timber may only manage **5 to 20 repetitions**.

### (e) Economy
The total formwork cost includes **material, fabrication, labour, transport, stripping, cleaning, and storage**. An economical formwork system balances initial cost with the number of reuses — a metric known as the **cost-per-use**.

### (f) Water-tightness
Joints between panels must be tight enough to prevent the **escape of cement slurry** and water, which would otherwise cause honeycombing, sand streaking, and weakened surface concrete.

### (g) Smoothness & Surface Finish
The formwork contact surface determines the **texture and finish** of the hardened concrete. Steel and coated plywood produce smoother finishes; rough-sawn timber leaves a board-marked texture.

### (h) Reusability
The number of times a formwork unit can be reused without major repair — a direct economic and sustainability parameter.

### (i) Workability / Ease of Handling
Formwork should be **light enough for manual or crane handling**, with simple assembly and stripping procedures that don't require specialized skills.

### (j) Dimensional Accuracy
Formwork must conform precisely to the **drawings and tolerances** (typically ± 3 mm to ± 6 mm for plan dimensions) to ensure the structural member is built to the correct size.

### (k) Chemical Resistance
Concrete is alkaline (pH 12–13). Formwork materials must resist this alkaline environment to prevent corrosion (steel) or chemical degradation (certain plastics and timber treatments).

### (l) Safety
Formwork failures are one of the leading causes of construction accidents. The system must provide **safe access platforms, guard rails, and stable bracing** to protect workers.

## 1.4 Overview of Selection Criteria

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Criterion Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *A selection criterion is any engineering, economic, or site-specific factor that determines the most suitable type, material, and configuration of formwork for a given structural element.*

The principal selection criteria for formwork include:

- **Type of structural element** (slab, beam, column, wall, foundation)
- **Concrete type and required surface finish**
- **Magnitude of loads** (concrete pressure, live load, wind)
- **Number of repetitions** required
- **Site constraints** (access, crane availability, labour skill)
- **Cost and availability of materials**
- **Construction schedule** (speed of erection and stripping)
- **Architectural requirements** (curved surfaces, textures)
- **Safety and statutory regulations**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Discussion of Each Property

### 2.1.1 Strength of Formwork

The strength property is governed by the **maximum stress** that the formwork material can safely withstand. For a typical KTU problem:

$$
\sigma_{\text{actual}} = \frac{M}{Z} + \frac{P}{A} \leq \sigma_{\text{allowable}}
$$

where $M$ is bending moment, $Z$ is section modulus, $P$ is axial load, and $A$ is cross-sectional area.

**Key considerations:**
- The **allowable bending stress** in timber formwork is typically limited to **10 N/mm² to 15 N/mm²**.
- Steel formwork allows stresses up to **165 N/mm²** to **230 N/mm²** depending on grade.
- The **shear strength** of the formwork facing is critical near the supports where shear is maximum.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Strength Matters:** If the formwork fails under the wet concrete load, the consequences are catastrophic — fresh concrete collapses, formwork materials are destroyed, and workers below may be injured. Strength is therefore the **first** property to verify in any design.

### 2.1.2 Rigidity — The Deflection Criterion

Stiffness is measured by the **modulus of elasticity (E)** of the formwork material and the **moment of inertia (I)** of the cross-section. The deflection of a formwork member under load is:

$$
\delta = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot L^3}{E \cdot I}
$$

where $k$ is a coefficient depending on support and loading type, $W$ is the load, $L$ is the span, $E$ is Young's modulus, and $I$ is the moment of inertia.

**Permissible deflection limits (IS 14687 / ACI 347):**

| Element Type | Permissible Deflection |
|---|---|
| Exposed concrete surfaces | $\leq L/360$ but not more than **6 mm** |
| Concealed concrete surfaces | $\leq L/270$ but not more than **12 mm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Board Exam:** Always state the limiting deflection value and the corresponding span in any deflection-check problem. Marks are awarded for quoting the code reference.

### 2.1.3 Stability and Bracing

Stability is ensured by providing:
- **Lateral bracing** (diagonal struts) to prevent racking
- **Vertical shores / props** to carry vertical load
- **Ties and anchors** to resist uplift and lateral concrete pressure
- **Sole plates** at the base of props to distribute load to the ground

The Euler buckling load for a prop is:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_e^2}
$$

where $L_e$ is the effective length of the prop.

### 2.1.4 Durability and Reusability

| Material | Typical Number of Reuses |
|---|---|
| Untreated Timber | 5 – 10 |
| Treated Timber / Plywood | 20 – 30 |
| Steel Formwork | 100 – 200+ |
| Aluminium Formwork | 200 – 500+ |
| Plastic Formwork | 50 – 100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost per Use Formula:**
> $$ \text{Cost per Use} = \frac{\text{Total Cost (Material + Labour + Maintenance)}}{\text{Number of Reuses}} $$

### 2.1.5 Water-tightness

Achieved by:
- Tongue-and-groove joints
- Rubber gaskets / foam strips
- Tight clamping with bolts and wedges
- Use of **formwork liners** (e.g., ABS plastic sheets) for architectural concrete

### 2.1.6 Surface Finish

Concrete surface finish is classified per **IS 14687** as:
- **Class F1** – Rough (hidden surfaces)
- **Class F2** – Ordinary (普通 surfaces)
- **Class F3** – Smooth (architectural concrete)
- **Class F4** – Very smooth (fair-faced concrete)

The choice of formwork facing (steel, plywood, plastic, rough timber) directly determines which class can be achieved.

### 2.1.7 Workability

Workability considers:
- **Weight per panel** (manual handling limit ≈ **25 kg per worker**)
- **Crane handling** for heavier panels
- **Modular system** for quick assembly
- **Simple connection details** (clamps, pins, wedges)

## 2.2 Detailed Selection Criteria — Decision Framework

The selection of formwork follows a structured **decision tree** based on the following engineering parameters:

### Step 1: Identify the Structural Element
- **Foundations:** Simple timber or plywood — low finish quality required
- **Columns:** Modular panel system (steel or aluminium) — high repetition
- **Beams:** Timber-ply or proprietary beam formwork
- **Slabs:** Table form / flying form / conventional prop-and-beam
- **Walls:** Gang form / jump form / climb form
- **Shear walls & cores:** Self-climbing formwork
- **Curved structures:** Flexible formwork / fabric formwork

### Step 2: Determine the Concrete Pressure
The lateral pressure of fresh concrete on vertical formwork is a **critical design input**. For concrete placed at a normal rate, ACI 347 (also adopted by KTU reference material) gives:

$$
P_{\max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(hydrostatic limit)}
$$

$$
P_{\max} = 7.19 + \frac{785 \cdot R}{17.8 + T} \quad \text{(ACI 347, SI units, in kPa)}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of concrete (≈ **2400 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = height of fresh concrete
- $R$ = rate of placement (m/hr)
- $T$ = temperature of concrete (°C)

The **lesser** of the hydrostatic pressure and the ACI formula value is used for design.

### Step 3: Evaluate the Finish Quality Required
- **F1 / F2 finish:** Timber or rough plywood acceptable
- **F3 / F4 finish:** Phenol-coated plywood, steel, or fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP) facing required

### Step 4: Assess Site Constraints
- **Crane availability:** Determines panel weight limit
- **Skilled labour:** Determines system complexity
- **Site access:** Determines panel size
- **Storage space:** Determines modular system vs. custom

### Step 5: Economic Analysis
- Compare **first cost** vs. **cost per use**
- Include stripping, cleaning, transport, and storage costs

### Step 6: Safety and Code Compliance
- Confirm compliance with **IS 14687, IS 875, ACI 347, and OSHA** standards
- Check for proper bracing, ties, and access platforms

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic concrete pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | kN/m² or kPa | Maximum lateral pressure on vertical formwork |
| 2 | ACI 347 lateral pressure (SI) | $P = 7.19 + \dfrac{785 R}{17.8 + T}$ | kPa | When $H \geq R \cdot T$ limit exceeded |
| 3 | Effective length of shore | $L_e = K \cdot L$ | m | Buckling check of vertical prop |
| 4 | Euler buckling load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | kN | Critical load for slender prop |
| 5 | Bending stress check | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | N/mm² | Strength check of formwork sheathing |
| 6 | Deflection of formwork | $\delta = \dfrac{k W L^3}{E I}$ | mm | Stiffness / serviceability check |
| 7 | Permissible deflection | $\delta \leq L/360$ (max 6 mm exposed) | mm | Deflection limit for F3/F4 finish |
| 8 | Cost per use | $C_u = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{No. of Reuses}}$ | ₹/use | Economic selection of formwork |
| 9 | Vertical load on slab form | $W = (D_c + D_f) \cdot t + L_l$ | kN/m² | Dead + live load on horizontal formwork |
| 10 | Density of fresh concrete | $\rho_c \approx 24$ to 25 | kN/m³ | Used in hydrostatic and dead-load calculations |
| 11 | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r}$ | dimensionless | Buckling classification of shores |
| 12 | Section modulus (rectangle) | $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$ | mm³ | Bending stress calculation |
| 13 | Section modulus (solid log/timber) | $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$ | mm³ | Strength of circular timber shore |
| 14 | Maximum rate of placement | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{S}{C \cdot (T + 7.8)}$ | m/hr | When cement content or type changes |
| 15 | Pressure for retarded concrete | $P = 7.19 + \dfrac{785 R}{17.8 + T} \times C_f$ | kPa | With retarder coefficient $C_f$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conversion Reminders for KTU Board Exam:**
> - 1 m of water column = **9.81 kN/m²** = **9.81 kPa**
> - 1 m of fresh concrete column ≈ **24 kN/m²** = **24 kPa**
> - 1 kgf/cm² ≈ **98.1 kPa**
> - Density of fresh concrete (normal weight) = **24 to 25 kN/m³**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are applied in:
- **High-rise building construction** (self-climbing formwork for shear walls)
- **Bridge construction** (cantilever form travellers, segmental formwork)
- **Tunnels and metro projects** (custom steel tunnel formwork)
- **Mass concrete dams** (temperature-controlled, pressure-resistant formwork)
- **Pre-cast yards** (battery moulds, tilting tables, long-line beds)
- **Nuclear containment structures** (high-tolerance steel formwork)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 questions, the most frequently tested topics are (1) lateral pressure calculation, (2) deflection check, (3) cost-per-use comparison, and (4) selection of formwork material for a given structural element. Master these four areas for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Lateral Pressure of Fresh Concrete on Vertical Formwork

### 3.1.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Model

Consider a column of fresh concrete of height $h$ placed against a vertical formwork. The horizontal pressure at depth $h$ below the top surface is derived from the basic fluid pressure equation.

**Starting Principle — Pascal's Law for Fluids:**

Fresh concrete behaves as a **dense fluid** with density $\rho_c$ (in kg/m³) immediately after placement, before initial set. The pressure at the base of a fluid column of height $h$ is given by:

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Derivation Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Consider a small horizontal element of concrete at depth $h$, with cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $dh$.

**Step 2:** The weight of the column of concrete above this element is:

$$
W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h
$$

**Step 3:** This weight is balanced by the pressure $P$ acting on the area $A$ at the bottom:

$$
P \cdot A = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h
$$

**Step 4:** Cancelling $A$ from both sides:

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting numerical values for fresh concrete ($\rho_c = 2400$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$
P = 2400 \times 9.81 \times h = 23544 \cdot h \;\; \text{Pa} = 23.544 \cdot h \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 24 \cdot h \;\; \text{kPa} \quad (\text{for } h \text{ in metres})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Concrete Does Not Behave as a True Fluid:**
> Fresh concrete begins to stiffen once placed. The effective pressure on the formwork is **less than the full hydrostatic value** because the concrete at the top sets and stops transmitting its full weight downward. This is why ACI 347 introduced the **rate-of-placement formula**.

### 3.1.2 ACI 347 Lateral Pressure Formula (Derivation Context)

The ACI committee developed an empirical formula based on extensive field measurements. The general form is:

$$
P_{\max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.19 + \frac{785 \cdot R}{17.8 + T}\right) \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

where:
- $C_w$ = unit weight coefficient
- $C_c$ = chemistry coefficient (cement type, retarders, etc.)
- $R$ = rate of placement (m/hr)
- $T$ = concrete temperature (°C)

**The constants 7.19, 785, and 17.8 are empirical** — they are derived by curve-fitting experimental pressure data from numerous field tests. Students are NOT expected to re-derive these constants, but should **understand the form** and apply the formula correctly.

**Boundary Condition for the ACI Formula:**

The formula is valid only when:

$$
H \geq \frac{P_{\max}}{\rho_c \cdot g}
$$

If the actual placement height $H$ is less than $H_{\text{crit}}$, then the hydrostatic pressure controls:

$$
P = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot H
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Maximum Lateral Pressure on Column Formwork

> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):**
> A column formwork of height 4.5 m is to be filled with concrete at a rate of placement $R = 1.5$ m/hr. The concrete temperature is $T = 27$ °C and the density is **2400 kg/m³**. The formwork uses standard Type I cement (no retarders). Determine:
> 1. The maximum lateral pressure using ACI 347.
> 2. The hydrostatic pressure for comparison.
> 3. The design pressure to be used.

### Step 1: List Given Data
- $H = 4.5$ m
- $R = 1.5$ m/hr
- $T = 27$ °C
- $\rho_c = 2400$ kg/m³ = **24 kN/m³**
- $C_w = 1.0$ (normal weight concrete)
- $C_c = 1.0$ (Type I cement, no retarders)

### Step 2: Apply ACI 347 Formula

$$
P_{ACI} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.19 + \frac{785 \cdot R}{17.8 + T}\right)
$$

Substitute $R = 1.5$ and $T = 27$:

$$
P_{ACI} = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left(7.19 + \frac{785 \times 1.5}{17.8 + 27}\right)
$$

$$
= 7.19 + \frac{1177.5}{44.8}
$$

$$
= 7.19 + 26.28
$$

$$
= 33.47 \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Hydrostatic Pressure Formula

$$
P_{hydro} = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot H
$$

$$
P_{hydro} = 24 \times 4.5 = 108 \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

### Step 4: Compare and Select Design Pressure

$$
P_{ACI} = 33.47 \;\; \text{kPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_{hydro} = 108 \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

The **lesser** of the two governs the design (as per ACI 347):

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{design}} = 33.47 \;\; \text{kPa} \approx 33.5 \;\; \text{kPa}}
$$

### Step 5: Verification — Does Hydrostatic Apply?
Check if $H \geq P_{max} / (\rho_c \cdot g)$:

$$
H_{crit} = \frac{33.47}{24} = 1.395 \;\; \text{m}
$$

Since actual $H = 4.5$ m $> H_{crit} = 1.395$ m, the ACI formula controls. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The ACI formula gives the realistic pressure because it accounts for the **stiffening of concrete** with time. The hydrostatic model is too conservative in most practical cases where placement is gradual.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Deflection Check of a Plywood Sheathing

> **Problem:**
> A 12 mm thick plywood sheathing of width 300 mm spans between supports 250 mm apart. The load on the sheathing is 5 kN/m². Take $E = 9000$ N/mm² for plywood. Check if the deflection is within the permissible limit.

### Step 1: Convert Load to Line Load
Load per metre width: $w = 5$ kN/m² × 1 m = **5 kN/m** = 5 N/mm

### Step 2: Convert to Load per 300 mm Strip
For a 300 mm wide strip:

$$
w_{strip} = 5 \times 0.300 = 1.5 \;\; \text{N/mm}
$$

### Step 3: Maximum Deflection for Simply Supported Beam with UDL
For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with UDL $w$:

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Moment of Inertia of 12 mm Plywood
For a rectangular section of width $b = 300$ mm and thickness $t = 12$ mm:

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} = \frac{300 \times 12^3}{12} = 300 \times 144 = 43200 \;\; \text{mm}^4
$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Calculate

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 1.5 \times 250^4}{384 \times 9000 \times 43200}
$$

Numerator:
$$
5 \times 1.5 = 7.5
$$
$$
250^4 = 3.90625 \times 10^9
$$
$$
\text{Numerator} = 7.5 \times 3.90625 \times 10^9 = 2.9297 \times 10^{10}
$$

Denominator:
$$
384 \times 9000 = 3.456 \times 10^6
$$
$$
3.456 \times 10^6 \times 43200 = 1.493 \times 10^{11}
$$

Final Result:
$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{2.9297 \times 10^{10}}{1.493 \times 10^{11}} = 0.196 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

### Step 6: Check Permissible Deflection
Permissible deflection = $L/360 = 250/360 = 0.694$ mm (and ≤ 6 mm)

$$
\boxed{\delta_{max} = 0.196 \;\; \text{mm} < 0.694 \;\; \text{mm} \;\; \text{✓ SAFE}}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Cost-Per-Use Comparison

> **Problem:**
> A contractor is choosing between timber formwork (cost ₹50,000, expected 10 reuses) and steel formwork (cost ₹2,50,000, expected 100 reuses). Both have identical labour and accessory costs of ₹30,000 for the project. Compare on a cost-per-use basis.

### Timber:
$$
C_{timber} = \frac{50000 + 30000}{10} = \frac{80000}{10} = ₹8000/\text{use}
$$

### Steel:
$$
C_{steel} = \frac{250000 + 30000}{100} = \frac{280000}{100} = ₹2800/\text{use}
$$

### Decision:
$$
\boxed{\text{Steel is more economical at } ₹2800/\text{use vs. } ₹8000/\text{use for timber.}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sustainability Note:** The choice also has environmental implications — steel has high embodied energy but is recycled; timber is renewable but may need treatment with chemicals. Modern formwork selection balances cost, sustainability, and performance.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Material

| Property | Timber | Plywood | Steel | Aluminium | Plastic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strength | Moderate | Moderate | High | High | Moderate |
| Rigidity | Low | Moderate | Very High | High | Low |
| Durability (Reuses) | 5–10 | 20–30 | 100–200 | 200–500 | 50–100 |
| Weight | Heavy | Moderate | Heavy | Light | Very Light |
| Water-tightness | Moderate | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| Surface Finish | Coarse | Smooth | Very Smooth | Very Smooth | Smooth |
| Initial Cost | Low | Moderate | High | High | Moderate |
| Chemical Resistance | Low | Moderate | Moderate (needs coating) | Good | Excellent |
| Workability | Easy | Easy | Difficult (needs crane) | Moderate | Easy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Formwork Property Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six essential properties of a good formwork system and briefly explain why each is important.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Strength** — To safely resist the vertical and lateral loads from wet concrete, workers, and equipment without failure. *Importance:* Prevents catastrophic collapse during concrete placement.

2. **Rigidity (Stiffness)** — To keep the formwork from deflecting excessively under load. *Importance:* Ensures the finished concrete has the correct geometry and finish (deflection ≤ L/360 for exposed surfaces).

3. **Stability** — The formwork must remain plumb, level, and braced laterally. *Importance:* Prevents tipping, sliding, and buckling during vibration and concrete pour.

4. **Durability** — Should withstand multiple reuses without major repair. *Importance:* Reduces cost per use; steel achieves 100+ reuses vs. timber's 5–10.

5. **Water-tightness** — Joints must be tight to prevent leakage of cement slurry. *Importance:* Avoids honeycombing, sand streaking, and surface weakness.

6. **Surface Smoothness** — The facing determines the concrete finish quality. *Importance:* Architectural or fair-faced concrete (F3/F4 finish) demands smooth steel or coated plywood.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always support each property with a numerical value or code reference where possible (e.g., "L/360 limit per ACI 347" earns extra marks).

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Discuss the criteria for selecting formwork material for a multi-storey RCC building construction.**

**Model Answer:**

The selection of formwork material for a multi-storey RCC building depends on the following criteria:

1. **Type of Structural Element:** Columns and walls (high repetition) prefer steel or aluminium; slabs and beams may use timber-ply for economy.

2. **Number of Repetitions:** High-rise buildings use steel/aluminium due to ≥ 100 reuses, which offsets the high initial cost.

3. **Surface Finish Required:** Architectural exposed concrete (lift lobbies, columns) needs smooth steel or coated plywood; hidden slabs can use rough timber.

4. **Speed of Construction:** Fast-track projects use modular systems (table forms, flying forms) for rapid cycling.

5. **Crane Availability:** Steel panels are heavy; aluminium or plastic panels are preferred if crane capacity is limited.

6. **Cost Analysis:** Cost-per-use comparison is essential — although steel has high initial cost, its low cost-per-use makes it economical for large projects.

7. **Site Labour Skills:** Complex systems require trained labour; simple timber-ply is suitable for less-skilled sites.

8. **Safety:** Multi-storey work demands stable, well-braced formwork with proper access platforms.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**A high-rise building project uses a column formwork of height 5.0 m. Concrete is placed at a rate of 2.0 m/hr at a temperature of 25 °C. The concrete density is 24 kN/m³. Determine:**
**(a)** The maximum lateral pressure using the ACI 347 formula.
**(b)** The hydrostatic pressure for comparison and identify which controls the design.
**(c)** Briefly discuss the properties and selection criteria of the formwork material to be used.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — ACI 347 Lateral Pressure** (7 marks)

Given: $R = 2.0$ m/hr, $T = 25$ °C, $C_w = 1.0$, $C_c = 1.0$.

$$
P_{ACI} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.19 + \frac{785 \cdot R}{17.8 + T}\right)
$$

$$
= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left(7.19 + \frac{785 \times 2.0}{17.8 + 25}\right)
$$

$$
= 7.19 + \frac{1570}{42.8}
$$

$$
= 7.19 + 36.68
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{ACI} = 43.87 \;\; \text{kPa} \approx 43.9 \;\; \text{kPa}}
$$

**Part (b) — Hydrostatic Pressure and Design Pressure** (4 marks)

$$
P_{hydro} = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot H = 24 \times 5.0 = 120 \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

Check the critical height:

$$
H_{crit} = \frac{P_{ACI}}{\rho_c \cdot g} = \frac{43.87}{24} = 1.828 \;\; \text{m}
$$

Since actual $H = 5.0$ m $> H_{crit} = 1.828$ m, the **ACI formula controls**.

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{design}} = 43.87 \;\; \text{kPa}}
$$

**Part (c) — Properties and Selection Criteria** (3 marks)

For this multi-storey project, the following properties are critical:

- **Strength** to resist 43.87 kPa lateral pressure
- **Rigidity** to limit deflection to L/360 (max 6 mm for exposed columns)
- **Stability** with proper lateral bracing
- **Durability** for ≥ 100 reuses

**Selected material: Steel formwork** — recommended for high-rise due to high repetitions, dimensional accuracy, and excellent surface finish.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in This Type of Question:**
> 1. Forgetting to **multiply by $C_w$ and $C_c$** in the ACI formula (loses 2 marks).
> 2. Using **$P_{hydro}$** as the design pressure when ACI governs (loses 2 marks).
> 3. **Not converting units** correctly (e.g., kg/m³ to kN/m³) — loses 1 mark.
> 4. Failing to state the **design pressure** explicitly at the end (loses 1 mark).

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**A contractor must choose between:**
- **Option A:** Timber formwork costing ₹80,000 with 12 expected reuses
- **Option B:** Plywood formwork costing ₹1,40,000 with 40 expected reuses
- **Option C:** Steel formwork costing ₹4,00,000 with 150 expected reuses

**Each option has identical labour and accessory cost of ₹50,000 for the entire project.**
**(a)** Compute the cost per use for each option and recommend the most economical choice.
**(b)** Discuss the relevant properties of formwork and justify your selection with reference to these properties.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cost per Use Calculation** (7 marks)

**Option A (Timber):**

$$
C_A = \frac{80000 + 50000}{12} = \frac{130000}{12} = ₹10833.33/\text{use}
$$

**Option B (Plywood):**

$$
C_B = \frac{140000 + 50000}{40} = \frac{190000}{40} = ₹4750/\text{use}
$$

**Option C (Steel):**

$$
C_C = \frac{400000 + 50000}{150} = \frac{450000}{150} = ₹3000/\text{use}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Option | Total Cost (₹) | Reuses | Cost per Use (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A — Timber | 1,30,000 | 12 | 10,833 |
| B — Plywood | 1,90,000 | 40 | 4,750 |
| **C — Steel** | **4,50,000** | **150** | **3,000** ✓ |

$$
\boxed{\text{Steel formwork (Option C) is the most economical at } ₹3000/\text{use}}
$$

**Part (b) — Property-Based Justification** (7 marks)

Steel formwork excels in the following properties:

1. **Strength:** Steel can withstand high concrete pressures (up to 80–100 kPa) without failure. *(+2 marks)*

2. **Rigidity:** Very high modulus of elasticity (E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²) keeps deflection well within L/360. *(+1 mark)*

3. **Durability:** 150 reuses possible with proper maintenance, vastly superior to timber. *(+2 marks)*

4. **Surface Finish:** Steel produces very smooth concrete (F3/F4 finish) suitable for architectural concrete. *(+1 mark)*

5. **Dimensional Accuracy:** Factory-fabricated panels provide precise tolerances. *(+0.5 mark)*

6. **Cost Efficiency:** Lowest cost-per-use despite high initial cost. *(+0.5 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Cost-Per-Use Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the labour/accessory cost** to the material cost before dividing (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Comparing initial cost instead of cost-per-use** (loses 3 marks).
> 3. Not providing a **justification** based on properties (loses 3 marks).
> 4. Not presenting the answer in a **tabular form** for clarity (loses 0.5 mark for presentation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam (Module 1: Properties and Selection Criteria):**

- ☐ Formwork is a **temporary structure** supporting fresh concrete until it hardens.

- ☐ The **twelve essential properties** are: Strength, Rigidity, Stability, Durability, Economy, Water-tightness, Surface Smoothness, Reusability, Workability, Dimensional Accuracy, Chemical Resistance, and Safety.

- ☐ **Deflection limit** = L/360 (max 6 mm) for exposed concrete; L/270 (max 12 mm) for concealed concrete.

- ☐ **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ — upper limit of concrete pressure.

- ☐ **ACI 347 pressure** $P = 7.19 + \frac{785 R}{17.8 + T}$ kPa — accounts for concrete stiffening.

- ☐ Always take the **lesser** of hydrostatic and ACI pressures for design.

- ☐ **Density of fresh concrete** = 24–25 kN/m³ (use 24 unless stated otherwise).

- ☐ **Selection criteria** depend on element type, finish quality, repetitions, cost, site constraints, and safety.

- ☐ **Cost per use** = (Material + Labour + Maintenance) / Number of Reuses.

- ☐ **Steel formwork** is most economical for high-repetition projects despite high initial cost.

- ☐ **Timber** is suitable for low-repetition, small-scale, or complex-shape work.

- ☐ **Plywood** is the middle ground — good finish, moderate reuses, moderate cost.

- ☐ **Aluminium** is light and ideal when crane capacity is limited.

- ☐ Always check **both strength AND deflection** — deflection often controls the design.

- ☐ The **Bending stress** in a formwork member: $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}}$.

- ☐ The **Deflection** of a simply supported beam with UDL: $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$.

- ☐ **Section modulus** for a rectangular section: $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$.

- ☐ **Section modulus** for a circular section: $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$.

- ☐ **Surface finish classes** per IS 14687: F1 (rough) → F2 (ordinary) → F3 (smooth) → F4 (fair-faced).

- ☐ Always quote **code references** (IS 14687, ACI 347, IS 875) in your answers for full marks.

- ☐ For KTU exam: state **units clearly**, draw **neat free-body diagrams**, and present **tabular comparisons** wherever applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Formwork Material

**Formwork** is a temporary or permanent mould into which fresh concrete is poured and compacted. The choice of **formwork material** dictates the surface finish, dimensional accuracy, reusability, structural performance, and overall economy of a reinforced concrete structure. The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the most common materials as **Timber, Plywood, Steel, Aluminium, Plastic, Fabric, and Concrete** itself (for permanent formwork).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to *classify formwork based on the material used* and *critically compare their advantages and disadvantages* with respect to **cost, durability, finish quality, reuse cycles, labour requirement, and structural capacity**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Moulding Tray" Approach

Imagine you are a baker making jelly in different shapes. The jelly sets the shape of whatever container you pour it into.

- A **disposable plastic cup** = *Timber formwork* → cheap, single-use, gives rough finish.
- A **non-stick baking tray** = *Plywood with coating* → smoother finish, limited reuse.
- A **steel baking mould** = *Steel formwork* → expensive, but reusable thousands of times, perfect finish.
- A **flexible silicone mould** = *Fabric/Plastic formwork* → for curved and architecturally complex shapes.
- A **cardboard box used as a planter** = *Permanent concrete formwork* → stays in place forever, becomes part of the structure.

This analogy immediately clarifies **why different materials exist for different applications** in formwork engineering.

## 1.3 Classification of Formwork Materials

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Primary Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conventional / Timber-based** | Timber planks, joists, boards | Small projects, traditional construction |
| **Engineered Wood** | Plywood, Marine plywood, Film-faced plywood | Walls, slabs, columns, beams |
| **Metal-based** | Steel, Aluminium | Repetitive use, large infra projects |
| **Non-metallic Modern** | Plastic, FRP (Fibre-Reinforced Polymer), Rubber | Complex shapes, architectural concrete |
| **Permanent Formwork** | Precast concrete, Stay-in-place (SIP) systems | Composite construction, marine structures |
| **Flexible / Textile** | Fabric (PVC-coated polyester), Inflatable | Curved surfaces, shells, domes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Valuation:** Reusability of formwork is measured in **number of repetitions (cycles)**. Timber ≈ 5–20 cycles, Plywood ≈ 20–50 cycles, Steel ≈ 100–500+ cycles, Aluminium ≈ 200–1000+ cycles.

---
