# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 Material-Wise Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 TIMBER (Wood)

**How it works:** Natural sawn timber (softwood like pine, fir; hardwood like teak, sal) is cut into planks, joists, studs, and boards, then assembled manually at site using nails, bolts, and wire ties.

**Why it is used:** Timber is the **most traditional and universally available** formwork material. It is easy to cut, shape, and assemble on-site without sophisticated tools.

#### Advantages
- **Easily workable** — can be cut, nailed, drilled, and shaped using simple carpentry tools.
- **Lightweight** compared to steel, hence easy to handle without heavy lifting equipment.
- **Locally available**, especially in Kerala where timber resources are abundant.
- **Economical for small-scale projects** with low repetition requirements.
- Can be made to **any shape or size** with skilled carpentry.
- Provides reasonable **thermal insulation**, reducing heat of hydration effects on surface.

#### Disadvantages
- **Low reuse cycles** (typically **5–20 repetitions**), making it uneconomical for large projects.
- **Affected by moisture** — swells, warps, and shrinks with humidity changes.
- **Vulnerable to fungal decay, termite attack, and rotting**, especially in tropical Kerala climate.
- **Labour intensive** — requires skilled carpenters; the speed of construction is slow.
- **Quality of finish** is poor (rough) unless overlaid with plywood or liner sheets.
- **Fire hazard** — timber is combustible; storage and handling require fire safety measures.
- Cannot withstand high pressure of fresh concrete in deep members without additional bracing.

---

### 2.1.2 PLYWOOD

**How it works:** Plywood is an **engineered wood panel** made by gluing together thin layers (plies) of wood veneer with the grain of adjacent layers rotated by **90°**. The face is often coated with a phenolic resin film (film-faced plywood) for water resistance and smooth finish.

**Why it is used:** Plywood overcomes the dimensional instability of solid timber while retaining the workability of wood. It is the **most widely used formwork material in India today**, especially in residential and commercial RCC construction.

#### Advantages
- **Higher reuse cycles** (typically **20–50 repetitions**) compared to plain timber.
- **Lighter than steel** yet stronger due to cross-laminated structure.
- **Large panel sizes** available (commonly **1.2 m × 2.4 m**), reducing joint-mark lines on concrete.
- **Smooth surface finish** achievable with film-faced grade → minimizes plastering cost.
- Easy to cut and fix on site with simple tools.
- **Better dimensional stability** than solid timber — less warping and shrinkage.
- Standard sizes allow modular planning and design.

#### Disadvantages
- **Edges are vulnerable** to moisture ingress and damage — requires sealing with edge paint.
- **Lower reuse cycles** than steel or aluminium.
- **Lower load-bearing capacity** than steel — needs closer prop spacing.
- **Quality depends on grade** — MR (Moisture Resistant) vs BWR (Boiling Water Resistant) vs BWP (Boiling Water Proof) vs Marine.
- Phenolic coating can wear off after repeated uses, exposing the wood beneath.
- **Nail/screw holes** weaken the panel over repetitions.
- Susceptible to fungal and termite attack if not properly stored.

---

### 2.1.3 STEEL

**How it works:** Steel formwork is manufactured from **mild steel plates (typically 2–3 mm thick)** stiffened with rolled steel sections like angles, channels, and tees. Panels are bolted together to form walls, columns, beams, or slab soffits.

**Why it is used:** Steel provides the **highest reusability, dimensional accuracy, and surface finish**. It is the material of choice for **mass housing, infrastructure projects, high-rise buildings, and prefabricated systems** like Mivan formwork.

#### Advantages
- **Very high reuse cycles** — typically **100 to 500+ repetitions** with proper care.
- **Excellent surface finish** — produces concrete with near-mirror finish, sometimes eliminating plaster.
- **High strength and rigidity** — can withstand high concrete pressure in deep columns and walls.
- **Dimensional accuracy** — modular panels ensure consistent member sizes.
- **Fire resistant** and unaffected by humidity.
- **Impervious to water** — no swelling, warping, or shrinking.
- **Pest-proof** — no termite or fungal attack.
- **Speedy construction** — Mivan (aluminium-steel) systems can achieve **4-day floor cycle**.

#### Disadvantages
- **High initial capital cost** — requires substantial upfront investment.
- **Heavy weight** — needs **cranes and lifting equipment** for handling and assembly.
- **Corrosion-prone** — must be cleaned and oiled after every use; rust stains can spoil concrete finish.
- **Limited flexibility** — designed for specific shapes; not economical for non-repetitive or complex geometries.
- **Conducts heat** — temperature differentials can affect curing at surface.
- **Repair is difficult and costly** — damaged panels are usually replaced.
- Skilled labour and strict quality control are mandatory for proper assembly.

---

### 2.1.4 ALUMINIUM

**How it works:** Aluminium formwork is a **lightweight modular system** made of extruded aluminium alloy sections. The system is designed and fabricated in factory for a specific building layout, then assembled on site.

**Why it is used:** Aluminium combines the **reusability of steel with reduced weight**, making it ideal for **fast-track high-rise and mass housing projects**. The **Mivan system** is the most popular aluminium formwork brand in India.

#### Advantages
- **Lightweight** — roughly **1/3 the weight of steel**, easier manual handling and faster assembly.
- **Very high reuse cycles** — typically **200 to 1000+ repetitions**.
- **Excellent surface finish** — produces fair-faced concrete, reducing plastering.
- **Corrosion-resistant** — aluminium forms a passive oxide layer naturally.
- **Fast construction** — entire floor slabs can be cast in 4–7 days per cycle.
- **High dimensional accuracy** due to factory-fabricated modular panels.
- Reduced site labour requirement; assembly can be done by semi-skilled workers.

#### Disadvantages
- **Very high initial cost** — design and fabrication are project-specific.
- **Lower strength than steel** — requires **closer prop spacing and careful design** for high concrete pressures.
- **Cannot be modified on site** — cutting or welding is restricted; damage often requires replacement.
- **Limited flexibility** — uneconomical for projects with **varied floor plans** (e.g., villa-type buildings).
- **Conducts heat rapidly** — may cause surface cracking in hot climates if curing is improper.
- **Less robust** against rough handling compared to steel.
- Repairs and modifications require specialist knowledge.

---

### 2.1.5 PLASTIC (Plain & Reinforced)

**How it works:** Plastic formwork is made of **engineering plastics (ABS, polypropylene, PVC) or fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP)** using moulding processes. Panels are modular, lightweight, and often used in **tilting/turnover slab systems**.

#### Advantages
- **Lightweight** — can be manually handled.
- **Corrosion-proof** and **waterproof**.
- **Smooth finish** achievable.
- **Reusable** (typically **50–100 cycles** depending on grade).
- **Easier to clean** than steel; release agents work effectively.
- Suitable for **complex shapes** (curved, architectural).

#### Disadvantages
- **Lower strength and stiffness** compared to metals.
- **UV-degradation** when stored in sunlight.
- **Brittle at low temperatures**; can crack on impact.
- **Lower fire resistance** than metal formwork.
- **Higher unit cost** for small projects.
- Limited to low-pressure applications.

---

### 2.1.6 FABRIC (Textile Formwork)

**How it works:** High-strength **PVC-coated polyester fabric** is tensioned over a frame to form a flexible mould. Concrete is sprayed or poured on the fabric, which acts as both formwork and finish.

#### Advantages
- **Architectural flexibility** — can produce **double-curved, organic shapes** (shells, domes, columns with sculptural finish).
- **Lightweight and easy to transport**.
- **Reduces or eliminates plastering** — fabric texture imparts patterned finish.
- Reusable in many applications.

#### Disadvantages
- **Low structural capacity** — not suitable for thick, heavily loaded members.
- **Requires skilled design** for tensioning and pattern control.
- **Pumping of concrete must be carefully controlled** to avoid bursting the fabric.
- Limited to architectural and shell structures — not for general RCC.
- **High learning curve** for engineers and workers.

---

### 2.1.7 CONCRETE (Permanent / Stay-in-Place Formwork)

**How it works:** Precast concrete units (holcore slabs, lintels, column shells) or fibre-cement sheets are placed as permanent moulds, then filled with in-situ concrete. The formwork **becomes part of the structure** and is never removed.

#### Advantages
- **Eliminates stripping cost and time** — labour saving.
- **Provides additional structural capacity** — acts as composite section.
- **Dimensional accuracy** at joints is excellent.
- **Useful in marine environments**, bridge decks, and pile caps where formwork removal is impractical.
- **Reduces site congestion** — less formwork stored on site.

#### Disadvantages
- **No reuse** — initial cost is non-recoverable for the formwork element.
- **Weight of precast units** requires cranes.
- **Quality of joint between precast and in-situ concrete** must be ensured for monolithicity.
- **Limited architectural flexibility** — mostly used in repetitive or standard shapes.
- Higher material cost per project compared to reusable systems in repetitive work.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important table for KTU examinations** on this topic. Memorise it thoroughly.

| **Property** | **Timber** | **Plywood** | **Steel** | **Aluminium** | **Plastic/FRP** | **Fabric** | **Concrete (Permanent)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Unit Weight (kg/m²)** | 8–12 | 6–10 | 35–50 | 12–18 | 4–8 | 0.5–2 | 80–120 |
| **Reuse Cycles** | 5–20 | 20–50 | 100–500+ | 200–1000+ | 50–100 | 10–30 | **0 (Single use)** |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | Low–Medium | High | Very High | Medium | Medium–High | High |
| **Economy (per use)** | High cost/use | Medium | Lowest | Lowest | Medium | Medium | N/A |
| **Surface Finish** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Very Good | Textured | Good |
| **Moisture Resistance** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Strength / Load Capacity** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | Low–Medium | Low | Very High |
| **Workability on Site** | Excellent | Excellent | Difficult | Moderate | Easy | Moderate | Difficult |
| **Labour Intensity** | High | Medium | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Fire Resistance** | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Good | Poor | Poor | Excellent |
| **Corrosion Risk** | Yes (decay/termite) | Yes | **Yes (rust)** | Low | No | No | No |
| **Architectural Flexibility** | High | High | Low | Low | Medium | **Very High** | Low |
| **Typical Applications** | Small villas, rural | Walls, slabs, beams | High-rise, infra | Mass housing, Mivan | Modular slabs | Shells, domes | Bridge decks, pile caps |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Reasoning & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 The Economics-of-Reuse Derivation (Why Reusability Matters)

This is a critical KTU numerical/analytical concept. The **cost per use** of any formwork material is governed by the formula:

$$
C_{use} \;=\; \frac{C_{initial} \;+\; C_{maintenance} \;+\; C_{labour}}{N_{cycles}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{use}$ = Cost per use (₹ per m² per cycle)
- $C_{initial}$ = Initial procurement cost (₹)
- $C_{maintenance}$ = Cleaning, oiling, repair cost over life (₹)
- $C_{labour}$ = Cost of assembly + stripping labour over life (₹)
- $N_{cycles}$ = Total number of repetitions achieved

### Step-by-Step Comparison: Timber vs Plywood vs Steel

**Step 1: Define common assumptions**

Assume we are using **100 m² of formwork** for a housing project requiring **50 repetitions** of use.

**Step 2: Timber Calculation**

$$
C_{timber, initial} \;=\; ₹ \, 200 \,/\,\text{m}^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; ₹\,20{,}000
$$

$$
C_{timber, maintenance} \;=\; ₹\,5{,}000 \text{ (over 50 cycles, including frequent repairs)}
$$

$$
C_{timber, labour} \;=\; ₹\,50{,}000 \text{ (carpenters + helpers over 50 cycles)}
$$

Since timber typically gives only **8–10 useful cycles**, it must be **purchased 5–6 times** to complete 50 cycles:

$$
N_{actual, timber} \;=\; 10 \text{ cycles per purchase} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{5 purchases required}
$$

$$
C_{use, timber} \;=\; \frac{₹(20{,}000 \times 5) \;+\; ₹25{,}000 \;+\; ₹50{,}000}{50}
$$

$$
C_{use, timber} \;=\; \frac{₹100{,}000 \;+\; ₹25{,}000 \;+\; ₹50{,}000}{50}
$$

$$
C_{use, timber} \;=\; \frac{₹175{,}000}{50} \;=\; ₹\,3{,}500 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Plywood Calculation**

$$
C_{plywood, initial} \;=\; ₹\,350 \,/\,\text{m}^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; ₹\,35{,}000
$$

$$
C_{plywood, maintenance} \;=\; ₹\,10{,}000
$$

$$
C_{plywood, labour} \;=\; ₹\,40{,}000
$$

$$
N_{actual, plywood} \;=\; 30 \text{ cycles per purchase} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{2 purchases required}
$$

$$
C_{use, plywood} \;=\; \frac{₹(35{,}000 \times 2) \;+\; ₹10{,}000 \;+\; ₹40{,}000}{50}
$$

$$
C_{use, plywood} \;=\; \frac{₹70{,}000 \;+\; ₹10{,}000 \;+\; ₹40{,}000}{50}
$$

$$
C_{use, plywood} \;=\; \frac{₹120{,}000}{50} \;=\; ₹\,2{,}400 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4: Steel Calculation**

$$
C_{steel, initial} \;=\; ₹\,2{,}500 \,/\,\text{m}^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; ₹\,250{,}000
$$

$$
C_{steel, maintenance} \;=\; ₹\,30{,}000 \text{ (cleaning, oiling, minor repairs)}
$$

$$
C_{steel, labour} \;=\; ₹\,60{,}000
$$

Steel can comfortably achieve **200+ cycles** in a single procurement:

$$
C_{use, steel} \;=\; \frac{₹250{,}000 \;+\; ₹30{,}000 \;+\; ₹60{,}000}{200}
$$

$$
C_{use, steel} \;=\; \frac{₹340{,}000}{200} \;=\; ₹\,1{,}700 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion from Derivation**

$$
C_{use, steel} \;<\; C_{use, plywood} \;<\; C_{use, timber}
$$

$$₹\,1{,}700 \;\;\;<\;\;\; ₹\,2{,}400 \;\;\;<\;\;\; ₹\,3{,}500$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although **steel has the highest initial cost**, its **cost-per-use is the lowest** when repetition exceeds **50–100 cycles**. This is why steel and aluminium formwork dominate in **mass housing and infrastructure projects**, but remain uneconomical for small, non-repetitive works where **timber still wins**.

---

## 3.2 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix (Selection Flowchart Logic)

The choice of formwork material depends on four key parameters evaluated in the following order:

**Step 1: Assess Project Repetition Factor (R)**

- If $R < 10$ → **Use Timber** (cheapest initial cost).
- If $10 \leq R \leq 50$ → **Use Plywood** (best balance).
- If $R > 100$ → **Use Steel or Aluminium** (lowest cost-per-use).

**Step 2: Assess Geometric Complexity (G)**

- If $G$ is **standard rectangular** → Steel / Aluminium modular systems.
- If $G$ is **moderately complex** → Plywood (cuttable).
- If $G$ is **highly curved / sculptural** → **Fabric or Plastic** formwork.

**Step 3: Assess Finish Requirement (F)**

- If $F$ requires **mirror-smooth fair-faced concrete** → Steel or Aluminium.
- If $F$ is **standard with plaster allowed** → Plywood or Timber.
- If $F$ is **textured or patterned** → Fabric or rubber liners.

**Step 4: Assess Site Constraints (S)**

- If $S$ has **no crane access** → Lightweight materials (Plywood, Aluminium, Plastic).
- If $S$ has **abundant skilled carpenters** → Timber/Plywood.
- If $S$ requires **fast cycle time** → Aluminium (Mivan) or Steel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In exam answers, always conclude with a **short 2-line statement** that ties the four parameters (R, G, F, S) to your final material recommendation. Examiners reward structured reasoning.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four advantages of steel formwork over timber formwork.*

**Model Answer (4 points, 3 marks):**

1. **Higher reusability** — Steel formwork can be reused **100–500+ times** compared to **5–20 times** for timber.
2. **Superior surface finish** — Steel produces fair-faced concrete, often eliminating the need for plastering.
3. **Higher strength and rigidity** — Steel can withstand higher fresh-concrete pressure in deep columns and walls.
4. **Fire and pest resistant** — Steel is non-combustible and not affected by termites or fungal decay, unlike timber.

*(Note: 4 points written, marks awarded for any 3 best-answered points.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State two disadvantages of aluminium formwork.*

**Model Answer (2 points, 3 marks):**

1. **Very high initial cost** — Aluminium formwork systems are custom-designed and factory-fabricated, requiring substantial upfront capital investment.
2. **Limited flexibility on site** — Aluminium panels cannot be easily cut, welded, or modified on site; any damage usually requires replacement of the panel, making it uneconomical for projects with varied floor plans.

*(Third supporting point may be added: lower strength than steel requiring closer prop spacing.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose marks** by writing generic statements like *"steel is better than timber"* without **numbers** (reuse cycles, cost-per-use, finish grade). Always **quantify** your answer.
> - Do **not list advantages and disadvantages as a single combined paragraph** — examiners look for **clear categorised headings**.
> - In selection questions, **state the criteria first, then the conclusion**. A bare "use plywood" answer without justification gets only 50% credit.
> - Avoid mixing units (e.g., writing "kg/m³" for formwork when it should be **kg/m²** of panel area).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the advantages and disadvantages of **timber and plywood formwork** for a medium-scale residential construction project. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain with justification why **steel or aluminium formwork** is preferred for high-rise building projects with repetitive floor plans. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

| **Aspect** | **Timber Formwork** | **Plywood Formwork** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Material Nature** | Natural sawn wood; anisotropic and hygroscopic | Engineered cross-laminated wood veneer; more dimensionally stable |
| **Reuse Cycles** | Only 5–20 repetitions | 20–50 repetitions |
| **Workability** | Excellent — easily cut, nailed, drilled on site | Excellent — cuts easily; nails hold well |
| **Surface Finish** | Rough; often requires plastering | Smooth (especially film-faced); may avoid plaster |
| **Moisture Behaviour** | Swells, warps, shrinks with humidity | Resistant; film-faced variants are waterproof |
| **Durability** | Vulnerable to termite and fungal attack | Termite-resistant grades available (marine ply) |
| **Load Capacity** | Lower; needs close bracing | Moderate; cross-lamination improves stiffness |
| **Initial Cost** | Lowest | Slightly higher than timber |
| **Cost-per-Use** | High (due to low reuse) | Medium (better economy than timber) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For a medium-scale residential project (typically 5–15 repetitions), **plywood formwork offers better economy, finish, and durability** than plain timber while retaining ease of on-site handling.

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Project Characteristics (1 mark)**
- Repetition: Often > 100 cycles (similar floor plans repeated 20–30+ times).
- Vertical construction: High-rise demands high concrete pressure in columns and walls.
- Cycle time: Target of 4–7 days per floor in fast-track projects.

**Step 2: Why Steel (3 marks)**

- **Reusability 100–500+ cycles** — amortises high initial cost.
- **High strength and rigidity** — withstands lateral pressure in deep columns/walls.
- **Excellent dimensional accuracy** — typical tolerance of ±2 mm.
- **Mirror-finish concrete** — eliminates plastering cost.
- **Fire and pest resistant**.

**Step 3: Why Aluminium (2 marks)**

- **Lightweight (1/3 of steel)** — easier manual handling; faster assembly.
- **Reusability 200–1000+ cycles** — best for very high repetition.
- **Mivan system** can achieve **4-day floor cycle**, ideal for mass housing.

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**
For **high-rise repetitive construction**, both **steel and aluminium** outperform timber-based systems. **Steel** is chosen when **strength is critical** (deep members, high pressure); **aluminium** is chosen when **speed and reduced weight** are the priority (mass housing, mid-rise).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of **plastic and fabric (textile) formwork**. In which type of project are they most suitable? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is **permanent (stay-in-place) formwork**? State its advantages, limitations, and two typical applications in civil engineering. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Plastic Formwork (3 marks):**
- *Advantages:* Lightweight, waterproof, corrosion-proof, smooth finish, reusable (50–100 cycles), easy to clean.
- *Limitations:* Lower strength and stiffness than metals, UV-degradation on storage, brittle at low temperatures, not suitable for heavily loaded members.

**Fabric Formwork (3 marks):**
- *Advantages:* Provides **architectural flexibility** for double-curved and organic shapes (domes, shells, sculptural columns); lightweight; reusable; imparts **textured architectural finish** reducing plastering.
- *Limitations:* Low structural capacity, requires skilled tensioning design, careful concrete pumping, limited to architectural / non-structural applications.

**Most Suitable Projects (1 mark):**
- Plastic → modular slab systems, low-cost housing, columns and walls with simple geometry.
- Fabric → **architectural concrete projects, shell roofs, museum structures, sculptural facades**.

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Permanent formwork, also called **Stay-in-Place (SIP) formwork**, is a formwork system that remains in position after concrete is cast and becomes **an integral part of the finished structure**. Common examples include precast concrete shells, hollow core slabs acting as permanent soffit, fibre-cement boards, and metal deck slabs.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Eliminates stripping cost and time** — major labour saving.
2. **Acts compositely with in-situ concrete**, adding structural capacity.
3. **Reduces site congestion** — no formwork storage or handling.
4. **Useful where formwork removal is impossible** (e.g., underwater, deep foundations, marine piles).
5. **Dimensional accuracy and joint quality** are pre-controlled in factory.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- No reuse; hence **not economical for repetitive above-grade work** where conventional formwork can be reused.
- Higher initial material cost; requires crane handling of precast units.
- Bond between precast and in-situ concrete must be carefully designed for monolithic behaviour.

**Two Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Bridge deck slabs** — metal deck or precast panels act as permanent formwork.
2. **Pile caps and marine pile shells** — precast concrete shells remain in place below water/ground.
3. (Additional) Hollow core slabs in building floors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Seven material categories** for KTU Module 1: Timber, Plywood, Steel, Aluminium, Plastic, Fabric, Permanent (Concrete).
- ☐ **Reuse cycle numbers** are the most-favoured KTU exam value: Timber 5–20, Plywood 20–50, Steel 100–500+, Aluminium 200–1000+, Plastic 50–100, Fabric 10–30, Concrete = 0.
- ☐ **Cost-per-use formula:** $C_{use} = (C_{initial} + C_{maintenance} + C_{labour}) / N_{cycles}$.
- ☐ **Mivan = Aluminium formwork system**; used in **fast-track mass housing**.
- ☐ **Steel and Aluminium** dominate **repetitive, high-rise, and infrastructure** projects.
- ☐ **Plywood** is the **most widely used** formwork material in Indian residential/commercial RCC.
- ☐ **Timber** is preferred for **non-repetitive, small, or rural** projects.
- ☐ **Fabric** formwork = **architectural, double-curved, shell structures**.
- ☐ **Permanent (SIP) formwork** = **stays in place, composite action, no reuse**.
- ☐ **Always state the selection criteria** (Repetition, Geometry, Finish, Site constraints) before naming a material in your answer.
- ☐ **Avoid the trap of "more is better"** — high reuse only helps when $N_{cycles}$ is large; for small projects, timber's low initial cost wins.
- ☐ **Finish quality ranking (best → worst):** Steel ≈ Aluminium > Film-faced Plywood > Plastic > Plain Plywood > Timber.
- ☐ **Weight ranking (lightest → heaviest):** Fabric < Plastic < Aluminium < Plywood < Timber < Steel < Concrete.
- ☐ Always write answers in **tabular form for comparison** — KTU examiners reward structured presentation.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Materials (PECET742)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Formwork Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modern Formwork Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modern Formwork Systems** refer to engineered, modular, and highly mechanized temporary or permanent molds and their supporting structures used to shape and support freshly placed concrete until it gains sufficient strength to be self-supporting. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, these systems are classified as *engineered falsework* that integrates standardized components, systematic assembly logic, and high-cycle reuse capability to deliver structural quality, dimensional accuracy, and rapid construction cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET742 – Module 1):**
> Modern formwork systems encompass the shift from *conventional timber-based formwork* to *industrialized systems* including Mivan (aluminum), Doka/Peri (steel-framed), tunnel forms, table/flying forms, slip forms, jump forms, climbing forms, and modular plastic forms.

The primary engineering parameters that govern the selection of a modern formwork system are:

- **Vertical Load Capacity:** typically **20 kN/m² to 60 kN/m²** for slab systems and **50 kN/m² to 100 kN/m²** for wall systems
- **Reusability Cycle:** **100 to 1000+ repetitions** depending on material (steel > aluminum > plastic > timber)
- **Tolerance Limits:** ±**3 mm** in verticality and ±**5 mm** in plan for high-quality architectural concrete
- **Construction Cycle Time:** reduced to **3 to 5 days per floor** in high-rise applications

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine baking a layered cake. A *traditional timber formwork* is like shaping each layer with hand-cut cardboard — it works, but it is slow, inconsistent, and wears out quickly. A *modern formwork system* is like using a **precision-engineered stainless-steel cake mold** with snap-on sides and a release lever. The mold snaps together quickly, holds its shape for hundreds of cakes, and produces identical, smooth results every single time.

**In engineering terms:**
- The *mold* = **Formwork (sheathing)**
- The *snap-on sides* = **Walers / Soldiers**
- The *reinforcing ribs* = **Props / Shoring / Ties**
- The *release lever* = **Stripping Mechanism (form oil, taper pins, quick-release clamps)**
- The *reusability* = **Repetition Factor (number of reuse cycles)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Formwork is a temporary structure whose purpose is to provide support and containment for fresh concrete until it becomes self-supporting."* — Adapted from ACI SP-4 (Formwork for Concrete).

## 1.3 Why Modern Systems Replaced Conventional Timber

The transition from conventional to modern formwork was driven by four critical drivers in the Indian construction industry:

1. **Speed of Construction** — High-rise residential projects (typical **30+ floors**) demand **4-day floor cycles**, achievable only with Mivan or table-form systems.
2. **Quality & Finish** — Architectural concrete (fair-faced) requires **Class A finish** with no blowholes, bulges, or panel joints.
3. **Labour Reduction** — Modern systems use **0.05 to 0.1 man-hours/m²** vs. **0.6 to 1.0 man-hours/m²** for conventional timber.
4. **Safety & Repetition** — Engineered systems offer predictable behavior, codified load capacity, and **>200 reuse cycles** with proper maintenance.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Insight:** Examiners frequently award full marks when a student explicitly links the *formwork system choice* to the *construction method statement* (e.g., Mivan for monolithic shear-wall buildings, tunnel form for cellular repetitive units, jump form for core walls).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Modern Formwork Systems

Modern formwork systems can be classified on the basis of **material**, **structural action**, and **movement mechanism**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following seven primary families.

### 2.1.1 Mivan Formwork System (Aluminum)

- **Material:** High-strength aluminum alloy (typically **6061-T6**, yield strength **240 MPa**, density **2700 kg/m³**).
- **Components:** Wall panels, slab panels, beam panels, prop heads, soffit beams, deck panels, pin & wedge connectors.
- **Construction Logic:** *Monolithic pour* — walls, columns, beams, and slabs are cast in a single continuous operation (typically **80 to 120 m³ per pour**).
- **Repetition:** **200 to 250 cycles** with proper cleaning and re-pinning.
- **Tolerance:** ±**3 mm**.
- **Typical Project:** Residential towers (e.g., typical 1BHK/2BHK repetition layouts).

> [!NOTE]
> The Mivan system was originally developed by *Mivan Company (UK)* in the 1980s and is now licensed globally. In India, it is widely used in mass-housing projects under the PMAY (Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana) scheme.

### 2.1.2 Table Form / Flying Formwork

- **Concept:** A large pre-assembled table (typically **3 m × 6 m to 5 m × 10 m**) supporting the entire slab panel of one bay.
- **Movement:** "Flies" out of the building through openings and is craned up to the next floor.
- **Productivity:** Reduces slab cycle to **3 to 4 days**.
- **Ideal For:** Repetitive flat-slab or beam-slab parking decks, office buildings.

### 2.1.3 Tunnel Formwork

- **Concept:** Two L-shaped half-tunnels that are joined to form a complete room/shelter unit in a single pour.
- **Production Rate:** One complete dwelling unit per **24 hours**.
- **Best Application:** Mass housing, hostels, prison cells, hotels with repetitive cellular geometry.

### 2.1.4 Slip Formwork

- **Concept:** Continuously moving formwork that rises at a controlled rate (**100 mm/hr to 300 mm/hr**) as concrete is placed.
- **Application:** Silos, chimneys, high-rise core walls, cooling towers, bridge piers.
- **Continuous Operation:** 24×7 placement with **3-hourly concrete lifts**.

### 2.1.5 Jump Formwork (Crane-Lifted)

- **Concept:** Large wall-panel forms anchored to previously poured concrete; the form "jumps" upward using a crane after each pour.
- **Cycle Time:** **3 to 4 days per jump**.
- **Difference from Slip Form:** Discontinuous — each pour is allowed to harden before the next jump.

### 2.1.6 Climbing Formwork (Self-Climbing / Automatic)

- **Concept:** Hydraulic or screw-jack mechanism climbs the form without a crane, using anchors embedded in previously placed concrete.
- **Application:** Very tall core walls (60+ stories), bridge pylons, dam walls.

### 2.1.7 Modular Plastic Formwork

- **Concept:** Injection-molded polypropylene or ABS panels with click-lock joints.
- **Repetition:** **80 to 100 cycles**.
- **Best For:** Low-cost housing, plinth beams, footings, small columns.

## 2.2 Engineering Load Path in Modern Formwork

The structural load path of any modern formwork system follows a **four-tier transfer mechanism**:

$$
P_{\text{concrete}} \;\rightarrow\; P_{\text{form sheathing}} \;\rightarrow\; P_{\text{joists / walers}} \;\rightarrow\; P_{\text{props / shores}} \;\rightarrow\; P_{\text{ground / slab below}}
$$

Each tier must be checked for:
- **Bending stress** in sheathing/joists
- **Shear stress** in walers/soldiers
- **Axial buckling** in props
- **Lateral stability** of the entire assembly

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize This Table — Appears in 80% of KTU Calculations:**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Concrete Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | kN/m² | Full hydrostatic when $h \le H_c$ (critical head) |
| 2 | ACI 347 Maximum Lateral Pressure (limited by rate) | $P_{\max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \dfrac{800 R}{T + 17.8}\right)$ | kN/m² | Wall forms with $R \le 2\text{ m/hr}$ and $T \le 38\,°C$ |
| 3 | ACI 347 Pressure (columns) | $P_{\max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \dfrac{785 R}{T + 17.8}\right)$ | kN/m² | Column forms (higher coefficient due to friction) |
| 4 | CIRIA Report 108 (Pumping Concrete) | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{\rho g D}{2} \left(1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{v}{D \cdot g}}\right)$ | kN/m² | Pumped concrete with diameter $D$ and velocity $v$ |
| 5 | Density of Fresh Concrete (typical) | $\rho_c = 2400$ | kg/m³ | Used to convert pressure to equivalent fluid head |
| 6 | Minimum Density Factor $C_w$ | $C_w = 1.0$ for $D_c \ge 2240$; $C_w = 0.5(1 + D_c/2240)$ for $D_c < 2240$ | – | Density correction in ACI formula |
| 7 | Chemistry Factor $C_c$ | 1.0 for Type I cement; 1.2 for Type II/IV; 0.85 for Type III | – | Type III accelerates set, reduces pressure |
| 8 | Maximum Pressure Cap (ACI) | $P_{\max} \le \rho_c g h$ and $P_{\max} \le 95$ kN/m² | kN/m² | Upper bound on lateral pressure |
| 9 | Rate of Placement $R$ | $R = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Plan Area} \times \text{Time}}$ | m/hr | Drives lateral pressure build-up |
| 10 | Equivalent Fluid Head (concrete weight) | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{\rho_c g}$ | m | Used to design sheathing thickness |
| 11 | Bending Stress in Sheathing | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8 Z}$ | N/mm² | Check against allowable bending stress |
| 12 | Allowable Bending Stress (aluminum) | $\sigma_{allow} = 0.7 f_y$ ≈ **165 MPa** | N/mm² | Aluminum Mivan components |
| 13 | Allowable Bending Stress (mild steel) | $\sigma_{allow} = 0.66 f_y$ ≈ **165 MPa** (for 250 grade) | N/mm² | Doka/Peri steel components |
| 14 | Maximum Spacing of Walers | $L_{waler} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 Z \sigma_{allow}}{w}}$ | m | Optimize waler spacing |
| 15 | Axial Load on Prop | $P_{prop} = P_{slab} \times A_{trib}$ | kN | Check prop buckling capacity |
| 16 | Effective Length Factor (Prop) | $KL/r \le 200$ (steel) | – | Slenderness check |
| 17 | Stripping Time (KTU/IS 456) | **3 days** for props to beams; **7 days** for slabs; **14 days** for beams | days | Removal of formwork |
| 18 | Lateral Pressure Multiplier for Superplasticizer | $P_{mod} = 1.5 \times P_{max}$ | – | When HRWR admixture is used (delays set) |

> **Notation used:** $P$ = pressure; $\rho$ = density; $g$ = 9.81 m/s²; $h$ = head; $R$ = rate of placement (m/hr); $T$ = concrete temperature (°C); $D$ = pipe internal diameter; $v$ = flow velocity; $w$ = uniform load; $L$ = span; $Z$ = section modulus; $M$ = bending moment.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Modern formwork systems are not just "molds" — they are **load-bearing temporary structures** that govern the *quality, speed, and economy* of the entire construction project. In Indian context:

- **Cost Impact:** Formwork constitutes **35% to 60%** of the total concrete frame cost.
- **Time Impact:** Formwork cycle determines the *floor cycle time* of high-rises.
- **Quality Impact:** A 1 mm deviation in formwork accuracy causes **2 to 3 mm deviation** in finished concrete.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Viva:** If asked *"Why is Mivan preferred over Doka for residential high-rises in India?"* — answer: *Mivan offers a monolithic pour (eliminates beam-column joint vulnerability in seismic zones), has lower weight (aluminum density = 2700 vs. steel 7850 kg/m³), and the lighter panels reduce crane load during monsoon work.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Hydrostatic Head for Fresh Concrete

Fresh concrete behaves as a *thixotropic fluid* during placement. The lateral pressure on formwork walls is therefore derived from fluid statics.

**Step 1 — Define the hydrostatic pressure at any depth $h$ from the top of fresh concrete:**

$$
P(h) = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard density of fresh concrete $\rho_c = 2400$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:**

$$
P(h) = 2400 \times 9.81 \times h = 23{,}544 \, h \;\;\text{Pa} = 23.544 \, h \;\;\text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Round to a practical equivalent fluid density (KTU convention):**

$$
\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P(h) = 24 \cdot h \;\;\text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum pressure occurs at the base of the form (full hydrostatic head):**

$$
P_{max,hydro} = 24 \cdot H \;\;\text{kN/m}^2
$$

where $H$ = total height of fresh concrete pour (in meters).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **THEORETICAL MAXIMUM** — actual pressure is *less* because concrete sets and gains stiffness, transferring load internally. This is why empirical codes (ACI 347, CIRIA) cap the pressure well below full hydrostatic for tall pours.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: ACI 347 Lateral Pressure Equation (Wall Forms)

The ACI Committee 347 (Formwork for Concrete) provides a semi-empirical equation for lateral pressure on wall forms based on extensive field measurements.

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical relationship:**

$$
P = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{800 \cdot R}{T + 17.8}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify each term's physical meaning:**

- $7.2$ = base pressure in kN/m² (≈ 300 mm of fluid head — captures initial stiffening)
- $800 R / (T + 17.8)$ = rate-dependent pressure (greater rate → higher pressure)
- $C_w$ = density correction (heavier concrete exerts more pressure)
- $C_c$ = cement-type correction (Type III cement sets faster → less pressure)

**Step 3 — Apply the upper-bound constraints:**

$$
P_{max} \le \rho_c g H \quad \text{and} \quad P_{max} \le 95 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve a numerical example (KTU board-style):**

> *Given:* Wall height $H = 4$ m, Rate of placement $R = 1.5$ m/hr, Concrete temperature $T = 27$ °C, Type I cement, Normal density concrete.
>
> *Step 4a — Compute pressure term:*
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> P_{calc} &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left(7.2 + \frac{800 \times 1.5}{27 + 17.8}\right) \\
> &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left(7.2 + \frac{1200}{44.8}\right) \\
> &= 7.2 + 26.786 \\
> &= 33.986 \;\;\text{kN/m}^2
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> *Step 4b — Check hydrostatic cap:*
>
> $$
> P_{hydro} = 24 \times 4 = 96 \;\;\text{kN/m}^2
> $$
>
> *Step 4c — Check ACI 95 kN/m² cap:*
>
> $$
> 33.986 < 95 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{OK, no cap applies}
> $$
>
> *Step 4d — Design pressure:* $\boxed{P_{design} = 33.99 \text{ kN/m}^2}$
>
> *Step 4e — Equivalent head:*
>
> $$
> h_{eq} = \frac{33.99}{24} = 1.416 \text{ m}
> $$

This means the wall formwork is to be designed as if it were holding **1.416 m** of fluid concrete — a dramatic reduction from the full 4 m hydrostatic head, because the lower 2.6 m of concrete has *already set* by the time the upper concrete is placed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CIRIA Pumping Pressure Equation

When concrete is *pumped* into the form (common in modern Mivan pours), additional lateral pressure arises from the impact of pumped concrete against the formwork face.

**Step 1 — Start with the CIRIA pumping equation:**

$$
P_{pump} = \frac{\rho_c g D}{2} \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{v}{D \cdot g}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify parameters:**

- $D$ = internal diameter of delivery pipe (m)
- $v$ = concrete flow velocity in pipe (m/s)
- $\rho_c g$ = unit weight of concrete = 24 kN/m³

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**

> *Given:* $D = 0.125$ m (5-inch pipe), $v = 1.5$ m/s, $\rho_c g = 24$ kN/m³.
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> P_{pump} &= \frac{24 \times 0.125}{2} \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{1.5}{0.125 \times 9.81}}\right) \\
> &= 1.5 \times \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{1.5}{1.226}}\right) \\
> &= 1.5 \times \left(1 + \sqrt{1.223}\right) \\
> &= 1.5 \times (1 + 1.106) \\
> &= 1.5 \times 2.106 \\
> &= 3.159 \text{ kN/m}^2
> \end{aligned}
> $$

**Step 4 — Decision Logic:**

If $P_{pump} < P_{ACI}$ → use $P_{ACI}$ (gravity-dominated).
If $P_{pump} > P_{ACI}$ → use $P_{pump}$ (impact-dominated, design for higher).

In the above example, $P_{pump} = 3.16 \ll P_{ACI} = 33.99$, so **gravity pressure governs**. This is typical in most Mivan pours with moderate pump rates.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Modern Formwork System

| Selection Criterion | Mivan | Tunnel | Table Form | Jump Form | Slip Form | Modular Plastic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Building Height** | Mid to high (≤40 floors) | Low to mid (≤15 floors) | Mid (≤25 floors) | Very high (60+) | All heights | Low (≤3 floors) |
| **Pour Type** | Monolithic | Monolithic | Slab only | Wall only | Continuous | Element-by-element |
| **Repetition Cycles** | 200–250 | 500+ | 300+ | 150 | Infinite (consumable) | 80–100 |
| **Cycle Time** | 4 days | 24 hrs | 3–4 days | 3–4 days | Continuous | 7+ days |
| **Crane Dependency** | Medium | High | High (for flying) | High | None | Low |
| **Initial Cost (₹/m²)** | **1800–2500** | **2200–3000** | **2000–2800** | **2500–3500** | **3000–4500** | **900–1500** |
| **Best Use** | Residential towers | Mass housing | Parking/office | Core walls | Silos/chimneys | Individual homes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Key Takeaway:** Modern formwork systems are *engineered products* with codified geometry, codified load capacity, and codified procedures. Selection must balance **repetition × initial cost × cycle time** for economic viability.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Mivan Wall Panel Spacing Design

> *Given:* A 250 mm thick Mivan wall form, design lateral pressure $P = 34$ kN/m², aluminum sheathing thickness $t = 4$ mm, allowable bending stress $\sigma_{allow} = 165$ MPa, simply supported condition.
>
> *Step 1 — Compute uniform load per meter width:*
>
> $$
> w = 34 \times 1 = 34 \text{ kN/m}
> $$
>
> *Step 2 — Section modulus per meter width:*
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> Z &= \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \times 4^2}{6} = \frac{16000}{6} = 2666.67 \text{ mm}^3/\text{m}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> *Step 3 — Compute allowable moment:*
>
> $$
> M_{allow} = \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z = 165 \times 2666.67 = 440{,}000 \text{ N·mm/m} = 0.44 \text{ kN·m/m}
> $$
>
> *Step 4 — Compute maximum span:*
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> M &= \frac{w L^2}{8} \le M_{allow} \\
> L^2 &\le \frac{8 \times 0.44}{34} = 0.1035 \\
> L &\le \sqrt{0.1035} = 0.322 \text{ m}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> *Step 5 — Adopt practical spacing:*
>
> Take $L = 300$ mm center-to-center of walers (i.e., walers are placed every 300 mm vertically to support the sheathing).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** In Mivan walls, the sheathing is *integral* to the panel — the walers are factory-welded at fixed pitches (typically 250–300 mm). Students should *cross-check* their calculated spacing against the manufacturer's catalog pitch.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Formwork System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fresh Concrete Placement] --> B[Form Sheathing]
    B --> C[Joists / Horizontal Walers]
    C --> D[Vertical Soldiers / Strongbacks]
    D --> E[Tie Rods and Wing Nuts]
    E --> F[Opposite Sheathing]
    D --> G[Props / Shores]
    G --> H[Base Plates]
    H --> I[Floor Below / Foundation]
    D --> J[Lateral Bracing]
    J --> K[Verticality Control]
    A --> L[Lateral Pressure Load]
    L --> B
    L --> C
    L --> D

    styleA fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```


## 4.3 Mermaid: Selection Logic for Modern Formwork Systems

```mermaid

    Q1{Project Repetition?}
    Q1 -->|Low less than 20| R1[Modular Plastic Formwork]

    Q2 -->|Cellular Repetitive| R2[Tunnel Formwork]

    Q3 -->|Monolithic Pour| R4[Mivan Aluminum System]
    Q3 -->|Slab Only| R5[Table or Flying Form]
    Q3 -->|Vertical Element| Q4{Height?}

    Q4 -->|Moderate less than 30 floors| R6[Jump Formwork]
    Q4 -->|Tall 60 plus floors| R7[Climbing / Self-Climbing Form]
    Q4 -->|Silos / Towers| R8[Slip Formwork]
```
