## 4.4 Mermaid: Load Path and Pressure Distribution in Wall Form

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Top of Pour] --> P1[Depth 0 to 1m]
    P1 --> P2[Depth 1 to 2m]
    P2 --> P3[Depth 2 to 3m]
    P3 --> P4[Depth 3 to 4m]
    P4 --> P5[Base of Form]

    P0 --> L0[Pressure 0 kN per m2]
    P1 --> L1[Pressure approx 24 kN per m2]
    P2 --> L2[Pressure approx 48 kN per m2 hydrostatic limit]
    P3 --> L3[Effective Pressure approx 34 kN per m2 ACI cap]
    P4 --> L3
    P5 --> L3

    styleP0 fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    styleP5 fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    styleL3 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    styleL2 fill:#ff006e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Figure 4.4:** The ACI 347 design pressure *caps* the lateral pressure at 34 kN/m² below 2 m depth, even though hydrostatic theory would predict 48–96 kN/m². This is the key engineering insight: **concrete gains strength during placement, so the lower portion of the form sees less pressure than the freshly-poured top.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**List the salient features of the Mivan formwork system. Why is it preferred for mass housing projects in India? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Mivan formwork system is an **aluminum alloy (6061-T6)** based modular formwork used for monolithic casting of RCC members. Its salient features are:

1. **Monolithic Pour:** Walls, columns, beams, and slabs are cast in a single continuous operation, eliminating cold joints and improving seismic performance.
2. **Light Weight:** Aluminum density (2700 kg/m³) is one-third of steel, allowing manual handling of panels.
3. **High Repetition:** 200–250 reuse cycles reduce per-cycle cost.
4. **Pin & Wedge System:** Quick assembly/disassembly using taper pins and wedges, enabling 4-day floor cycles.
5. **Dimensional Accuracy:** ±3 mm tolerance, suitable for fair-faced concrete.

**Why Preferred for Mass Housing:** The repetitive floor layout of Indian apartments (1BHK/2BHK with identical plans across floors) maximizes the *number of reuse cycles per project*, which lowers the per-m² formwork cost. Combined with seismic safety (monolithic joints) and speed, it is ideal for PMAY-style mass housing projects.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Differentiate between Jump Form and Slip Form systems. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Jump Form | Slip Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Movement Type** | Discontinuous (jumps per pour) | Continuous (24×7 motion) |
| **Pour Cycle** | 3–4 days per jump | Continuous, no individual pours |
| **Concrete Stiffening** | Concrete hardens fully before next jump | Concrete sets *as it moves* with the form |
| **Lifting Mechanism** | Crane-lifted | Hydraulic jacks (rod or rope climbing) |
| **Application** | High-rise building cores, large piers | Silos, chimneys, cooling towers, tall piers |
| **Construction Speed** | Slower (jump-bound) | Faster (continuous placement) |
| **Crew Skill** | Standard | Specialized (continuous operation team) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]

**(a) Explain the ACI 347 method of estimating lateral pressure of fresh concrete on wall formwork. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **American Concrete Institute Committee 347** provides a semi-empirical formula for lateral pressure based on the *rate of placement* and *concrete temperature* of fresh concrete. The formula is:

$$
P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{800 R}{T + 17.8}\right)
$$

where:
- $P_{max}$ = maximum lateral pressure in kN/m²
- $C_w$ = density coefficient (1.0 for normal weight, less for lightweight)
- $C_c$ = cement-type coefficient (1.0 for Type I, 1.2 for Type II/IV, 0.85 for Type III)
- $R$ = rate of placement in m/hr
- $T$ = concrete temperature in °C

**Limitations of the formula:**
- Applicable for $R \le 2$ m/hr
- Applicable for $T \le 38$ °C
- Form height $H \le 7.6$ m (for walls)

**Upper-bound constraints:**

$$
P_{max} \le 95 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{and} \quad P_{max} \le \rho_c g H
$$

**Theoretical Basis:** The formula combines:
1. A *base pressure* of 7.2 kN/m² (≈ 300 mm of fluid head) — representing the initial stiffening of the top layer.
2. A *rate-dependent term* — capturing that faster placement allows the form to fill before the lower concrete sets.
3. A *temperature correction* — because higher temperature accelerates setting, reducing pressure.

**Practical Interpretation:** The pressure does *not* grow indefinitely with depth. Beyond a critical head $H_c$, the lower concrete has already set, so pressure caps. This critical head is approximately:

$$
H_c = \frac{C_c}{24} \left(7.2 + \frac{800 R}{T + 17.8}\right) \;\;\text{m}
$$

---

**(b) A wall formwork 4.0 m high is to be filled with fresh concrete at a rate of 1.8 m/hr. The concrete temperature is 28 °C and Type I cement is used. Design the lateral pressure using ACI 347 method. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Given:** $H = 4.0$ m, $R = 1.8$ m/hr, $T = 28$ °C, $C_w = 1.0$ (normal density), $C_c = 1.0$ (Type I cement).

> **Step 1 — Apply ACI 347 formula:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> P_{max} &= C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{800 R}{T + 17.8}\right) \\
> &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left(7.2 + \frac{800 \times 1.8}{28 + 17.8}\right) \\
> &= 7.2 + \frac{1440}{45.8} \\
> &= 7.2 + 31.44 \\
> &= 38.64 \text{ kN/m}^2
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 2 — Check hydrostatic cap:**
>
> $$
> P_{hydro} = 24 \times 4 = 96 \text{ kN/m}^2
> $$

> Since $38.64 < 96$, the **hydrostatic cap does NOT govern**.

> **Step 3 — Check ACI 95 kN/m² cap:**
>
> Since $38.64 < 95$, the **ACI cap does NOT govern**.

> **Step 4 — Design pressure:**
>
> $$
> \boxed{P_{design} = 38.64 \text{ kN/m}^2}
> $$

> **Step 5 — Equivalent fluid head:**
>
> $$
> h_{eq} = \frac{38.64}{24} = 1.61 \text{ m}
> $$

> **Conclusion:** The wall formwork should be designed to withstand a uniform pressure of **38.64 kN/m²** acting over its full height, equivalent to holding **1.61 m of fluid concrete**.

---

### Question B [Alternative Choice – KTU ESE Pattern]

**(a) Describe the working of a self-climbing formwork system. List its advantages and applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **self-climbing formwork system** is a hydraulically or screw-driven climbing form used in the construction of very tall vertical elements (core walls, bridge pylons, dam walls) without external crane support.

**Working Principle:**

1. The form is anchored to previously placed concrete using *embedded anchors* and climbing shoes.
2. After each pour, the form is *stripped* from the wall (by retracting the form panels hydraulically).
3. The entire form assembly is then *climbed* upward using hydraulic jacks that pull the form up along the climbing rails (or push the rails up).
4. The form is *re-anchored* to the new concrete level, panels are re-aligned, and the next pour begins.
5. Operations are repeated for each floor/level.

**Types of Self-Climbing Forms:**
- **Hydraulic Self-Climbing (SCC):** Uses hydraulic cylinders; fast (1.0 m/min climb).
- **Screw-Jack Self-Climbing:** Uses electric screw jacks; slower but more precise.
- **Rope-Climbing:** Uses wire ropes and winches; common in dam construction.

**Advantages:**
- No crane dependency (operates independently)
- High level of safety (platforms fully enclosed)
- Excellent verticality control (±5 mm over 100 m height)
- Fast cycle (3–4 days per floor)
- Suitable for very tall structures (60+ stories)

**Applications:**
- Core walls of supertall buildings (Burj Khalifa, Shanghai Tower)
- Bridge pylons and cable-stayed towers
- Hydroelectric dam walls
- Industrial chimneys and silos

---

**(b) A self-climbing formwork is used for a 60-story core wall. The wall is 6.0 m high per pour, 4.5 m wide, and 0.4 m thick. Concrete density is 25 kN/m³ and rate of placement is 1.2 m/hr at 30 °C. Determine the design lateral pressure using ACI 347 and check whether it governs over the hydrostatic value. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Given:** $H = 6.0$ m, $R = 1.2$ m/hr, $T = 30$ °C, $C_w = 1.0$, $C_c = 1.0$, $\rho_c g = 25$ kN/m³.

> **Step 1 — Apply ACI 347 formula:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> P_{max} &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left(7.2 + \frac{800 \times 1.2}{30 + 17.8}\right) \\
> &= 7.2 + \frac{960}{47.8} \\
> &= 7.2 + 20.08 \\
> &= 27.28 \text{ kN/m}^2
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 2 — Check hydrostatic cap:**
>
> $$
> P_{hydro} = 25 \times 6.0 = 150 \text{ kN/m}^2
> $$

> Since $27.28 \ll 150$, **hydrostatic cap does NOT govern**.

> **Step 3 — Check ACI 95 kN/m² cap:**
>
> Since $27.28 < 95$, **ACI cap does NOT govern**.

> **Step 4 — Design pressure:**
>
> $$
> \boxed{P_{design} = 27.28 \text{ kN/m}^2}
> $$

> **Step 5 — Equivalent fluid head:**
>
> $$
> h_{eq} = \frac{27.28}{25} = 1.09 \text{ m}
> $$

> **Step 6 — Verification by Critical Head:**
>
> $$
> H_c = \frac{27.28}{25} = 1.09 \text{ m}
> $$
>
> Since $H_c = 1.09 \text{ m} \ll H = 6.0$ m, the *lower 4.91 m of concrete* has already gained significant stiffness by the time the pour is complete, which is why the design pressure is far less than the full hydrostatic value.

> **Conclusion:** The formwork must be designed for **27.28 kN/m²** uniform lateral pressure, applied over the 6.0 m wall height. The ACI 347 method correctly captures the early stiffening of fresh concrete and avoids over-design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ACI 95 kN/m² cap** — Many students compute $P_{max}$ but fail to check it against 95 kN/m², losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using wrong formula for walls vs. columns** — The coefficient 800 (walls) differs from 785 (columns). Examiners deduct marks for this.
> 3. **Ignoring units** — $R$ must be in m/hr, $T$ in °C, and $P$ in kN/m². Mixed units = full marks deducted.
> 4. **Omitting $C_w$ and $C_c$** — Even for normal density and Type I cement (both = 1.0), the *terminology* must appear in the answer.
> 5. **Confusing "formwork" with "falsework"** — Formwork = mold for concrete; falsework = temporary support structure beneath formwork (e.g., props, shores).
> 6. **Not stating limitations of ACI 347** — Valid only for $R \le 2$ m/hr, $T \le 38$ °C, $H \le 7.6$ m. Examiners reward these clauses.
> 7. **Skipping equivalent head calculation** — Always report $h_{eq}$ as a sanity check on your design pressure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modern formwork systems** are engineered, modular, and reusable — designed for **speed, quality, and economy**.
- The **Mivan system** is aluminum-based, monolithic, and ideal for residential high-rises.
- **Table/Flying forms** speed up slab construction in repetitive office/parking layouts.
- **Tunnel forms** are best for cellular, repetitive mass-housing units.
- **Slip forms** move continuously (24×7) — best for silos, chimneys, and very tall piers.
- **Jump forms** jump crane-assisted after each pour — best for mid- to high-rise core walls.
- **Climbing/Self-climbing forms** climb hydraulically without cranes — best for supertall structures.
- **Modular plastic forms** are cheap and lightweight — best for low-rise individual houses.
- **Lateral pressure** is governed by ACI 347 (rate + temperature dependent) or CIRIA (pumping impact dependent).
- The **full hydrostatic pressure $24h$** is *rarely* the design pressure — concrete stiffens during placement.
- **Always apply the upper-bound caps:** $P_{max} \le 95$ kN/m² **AND** $P_{max} \le 24H$.
- **Repetition factor (number of reuse cycles)** is the single biggest economic driver of modern formwork selection.
- **Formwork cost** = **35% to 60%** of total concrete frame cost — it is the dominant cost item, not the concrete itself.
- **Stripping time per IS 456:** Props under beams = **14 days**; Slab soffit = **7 days**; Vertical forms = **24–48 hours**.
- **Lateral pressure multiplier** for superplasticizer (HRWR admixture) = **1.5×** because the admixture delays initial set.
- **Monolithic pour** (Mivan) **eliminates cold joints** — critical for seismic resistance in Indian Zone III, IV, V.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** *Repetition × Speed × Tolerance* → Choose the formwork that maximizes the product at minimum cost-per-cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modular, Prefabricated, and Reusable Formwork
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modular, Prefabricated, and Reusable Formwork

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Modular Formwork

**Modular Formwork** is a pre-engineered, factory-produced shuttering system composed of standardized panels, connectors, and accessories of fixed dimensions that can be rapidly assembled and dismantled to form concrete structures of repetitive geometry. The "modular" attribute implies that every component has a mutually compatible dimensional grid (typically in metric increments of **50 mm**, **100 mm**, or **150 mm**), allowing pieces to be interchanged across projects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Modular formwork is a system where standardized panel units of fixed module sizes are assembled using compatible connecting hardware to produce concrete formwork of high repetition, dimensional accuracy, and rapid cycle capability."*

## 1.2 Definition of Prefabricated Formwork

**Prefabricated Formwork** refers to formwork assemblies that are **manufactured off-site** (in a casting yard or factory) and transported to the construction site in a near-complete or sub-assembled state. Prefabrication shifts the labour-intensive carpentry/shuttering work from the open site to a controlled factory environment, drastically improving quality, reducing on-site cycle time, and minimizing material wastage.

## 1.3 Definition of Reusable Formwork

**Reusable Formwork** is any formwork system (modular, conventional, or custom) designed to be struck, refurbished, and redeployed for **multiple concrete pours** during its service life. The economic viability of a formwork system is directly proportional to the number of reuses (typically **50 to 100+ reuses** for steel modular systems, and **10 to 25 reuses** for timber-based systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme PECET742 (Formwork Engineering) explicitly lists Modular, Prefabricated, and Reusable Formwork as a single integrated topic, emphasizing their synergistic role in modern **fast-track construction**.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: The "LEGO® of Construction"

Imagine building a child's **Lego® castle**:
- The **modular panels** are like Lego bricks — they come in fixed, standardized sizes (e.g., 600 mm × 1200 mm).
- The **clamps and wedges** are like the snap-connectors that lock pieces together.
- **Prefabrication** is like a parent pre-building the walls on a flat table and then handing you the pre-assembled section to drop into place.
- **Reusability** is the fact that you can disassemble the castle, put the bricks back in the box, and build a new castle tomorrow — provided the bricks aren't damaged.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world parallel:** Companies like **PERI (Germany)**, **Doka (Austria)**, **ULMA (Spain)**, and **Mivan (India/UK)** supply modular formwork that allows a typical 3 m × 3 m column-and-slab floor to be shuttered and poured in a fraction of the time taken by traditional timber formwork — often reducing floor cycle time from 14 days to **4–6 days**.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Evaluation

The KTU 2024 OBE framework maps this topic to **Course Outcome CO1** of PECET742: *"Identify and select appropriate formwork systems for various structural and constructional requirements."* The topic is a high-weightage area in **ESE (End Semester Examination)** and forms the foundation for Module 2 (Design of Formwork) and Module 4 (Special Formwork).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Formwork Systems (Hierarchical Breakdown)

Modern formwork systems can be classified along three intersecting axes:

1. **Material Axis** → Timber | Plywood | Steel | Aluminium | Plastic | Hybrid
2. **Construction Axis** → Conventional (job-built) | Modular (engineered) | Prefabricated (assembled off-site)
3. **Reusability Axis** → Single-use | Limited reuse (≤ 10) | High reuse (≥ 50)

A *modular* system is **always** reusable. A *prefabricated* system is **typically** modular. A *reusable* system may be modular or conventional.

## 2.2 Components of a Modular Formwork System

- **Panels** (Wall panels, Slab panels, Column panels) — Standardized face-sheet + stiffener rib assemblies.
- **Clamps and Wedges** — To lock adjacent panels together with tensile/compressive force transfer.
- **Tie Rods (Form Ties)** — Resist lateral concrete pressure; transfer load between opposing wall shutters.
- **Props and Shore Heads** — Vertical load-transfer members for slabs and beams.
- **Beams (Primary & Secondary)** — Aluminium/Steel H-beams or timber walers that distribute panel loads to props.
- **Bracing Members** — Diagonal tubes to maintain geometric stability during pouring.
- **Accessories** — Corner pieces, fillers, hinge corners, column clamps, etc.

## 2.3 Cycle Time Concept (The Heart of the Topic)

The **Formwork Cycle Time (T_c)** is the period between two consecutive concrete pours on the same formwork set. It is the single most important metric governing project economics.

$$
T_c = T_s + T_p + T_{st} + T_d
$$

Where:
- $T_s$ = **Stripping time** (time to release and lift formwork from hardened concrete)
- $T_p$ = **Positioning time** (time to relocate, align, and plumb the formwork)
- $T_{st}$ = **Setting time** (time to re-assemble, brace, and tighten the system)
- $T_d$ = **Dismantling and cleaning time** (for the final cycle when formwork is removed and refurbished)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The faster the cycle, the more reuses per month, the lower the cost per cubic metre of concrete shuttered.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = T_s + T_p + T_{st} + T_d$ | Total cycle time | hours or days |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{C_{total}}{C_{per \, use}}$ | Number of reuses = Total project formwork cost ÷ Cost per use | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_{use} = \dfrac{P - S + n \cdot M}{n}$ | Cost per use (P=Initial, S=Salvage, n=uses, M=Maintenance) | ₹ / use |
| 4 | $P_{lateral} = w_c \cdot h$ | Lateral concrete pressure at depth h (for slow pour) | kN/m² |
| 5 | $P_{lateral,max} = \gamma_c \cdot R \quad \text{with} \quad R \leq h$ | ACI simplified pressure formula | kN/m² |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{n_{actual}}{n_{design}} \times 100\%$ | Formwork efficiency | % |
| 7 | $A_{panel} = L \times B$ | Effective shuttering area per panel | m² |
| 8 | $n_{panels} = \dfrac{A_{total}}{A_{panel} \cdot N}$ | Number of panels required (iterative with reuses) | integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No pipe symbols inside table cells** — all absolute values are written using parentheses or $\vert$ where mathematically necessary. KTU valuation will deduct marks if formulas are dimensionally inconsistent.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **High-Rise Residential Projects:** Mivan-type aluminium modular formwork is used to cast entire apartments monolithically, reducing plastering and jointing work.
- **Mass Housing (PMAY):** Prefabricated tunnel formwork allows 4–6 houses to be cast per day.
- **Bridge Piers:** Custom modular steel forms are reused 80–120 times across multiple piers in a viaduct.
- **Industrial Floors:** Large panel systems span column-to-column in single pours.

## 2.6 Selection Criteria (Decision Matrix Logic)

A formwork system is selected based on:
- Structural repetition (≥ 4 reuses justifies modular investment)
- Concrete finish quality (architectural concrete needs steel/aluminium)
- Project duration (longer projects justify higher initial cost)
- Labour skill availability
- Crane capacity (heavy steel panels need crane handling)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost-per-Use of Reusable Formwork

Let:
- $P$ = Purchase (initial) cost of the formwork set
- $S$ = Salvage value at end of service life
- $n$ = Total number of reuses achieved
- $M$ = Average maintenance and repair cost per use

The **amortized cost per use** must recover depreciation, account for salvage recovery, and include maintenance.

### Step 1 — Write depreciation per use

$$
D_{use} = \dfrac{P - S}{n}
$$

This represents how much of the initial cost (minus residual salvage) is consumed by each use.

### Step 2 — Add maintenance cost per use

The maintenance cost is incurred uniformly across all reuses, so per-use maintenance is simply $M$.

$$
C_{use} = D_{use} + M
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute depreciation

$$
C_{use} = \dfrac{P - S}{n} + M
$$

### Step 4 — Common denominator

Bringing to a single fraction:

$$
C_{use} = \dfrac{P - S + n \cdot M}{n}
$$

This is the **master equation** for cost per use. It is directly testable in KTU ESE numerical questions.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A contractor purchases a modular formwork set for ₹ 12,00,000. The salvage value after the project is ₹ 1,50,000. The formwork will be reused **80 times**, and average maintenance per use is ₹ 4,500. Find:
(a) Cost per use
(b) Cost per m² of shuttered area if the set covers 60 m² per use

**Solution (a):**

Substituting $P = 1{,}200{,}000$, $S = 150{,}000$, $n = 80$, $M = 4{,}500$:

$$
C_{use} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 + 80 \times 4{,}500}{80}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1{,}050{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000}{80}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{14{,}10{,}000}{80} = 17{,}625 \text{ ₹/use}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
C_{m^2} = \dfrac{C_{use}}{A_{panel}} = \dfrac{17{,}625}{60} = 293.75 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always show units in the final answer. KTU examiners award partial marks for the formula even if arithmetic slips occur.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Break-Even Number of Reuses

A common KTU numerical asks: *"How many reuses are needed for the modular formwork to break even with conventional timber formwork?"*

Let $C_{conv}$ be the cost per m² of conventional timber formwork (single-use + labour). Modular formwork has higher initial cost $C_{mod,init}$ but lower per-use cost.

Setting costs equal:

$$
C_{mod,init} + n \cdot M_{mod} = n \cdot C_{conv} + S
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
n \cdot (C_{conv} - M_{mod}) = C_{mod,init} - S
$$

$$
n_{break-even} = \dfrac{C_{mod,init} - S}{C_{conv} - M_{mod}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> If $C_{conv} \leq M_{mod}$, the modular system will **never** break even — it is economically unsuitable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Parameter | Conventional Timber | Modular Steel | Modular Aluminium | Prefab Timber-ply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost (₹/m²) | Low (400–700) | High (2,500–4,000) | Very High (4,000–6,000) | Medium (1,200–2,000) |
| Reuses (n) | 4–8 | 80–120 | 150–250 | 20–40 |
| Labour Intensity | Very High | Low | Very Low | Medium |
| Cycle Time (days) | 14–21 | 5–7 | 3–5 | 7–10 |
| Concrete Finish | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| Handling | Manual | Crane | Manual/Crane | Manual |
| Suitable For | Odd shapes | Repetitive | Mass housing | Small projects |

## 3.4 Cycle Time Optimization — Step Logic

1. **Pre-cycle planning** — Pre-mark panel layouts on the drawing (BIM-based).
2. **Lifting out** — Strip panels in reverse order of installation.
3. **Immediate cleaning** — Concrete residue removed within 1 hour of strip.
4. **Releasing agent application** — Spray oil-based release agent.
5. **Storage in labelled racks** — Panels tagged for next floor.
6. **Re-positioning** — Crane lifts to next floor slab edge.
7. **Plumbing and alignment** — Laser-based verticality check.
8. **Bracing and locking** — Final tightening of wedge clamps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **setting time of concrete** with **setting time of formwork** $T_{st}$. Formwork setting time is the assembly time, NOT the concrete curing time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modular Formwork System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a modular formwork system, showing how panels, accessories, supports, and the concrete structure interact during a typical pour cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    panelWall["Wall Panel Unit"]:::panel
    panelSlab["Slab Panel Unit"]:::panel
    panelCol["Column Panel Unit"]:::panel
    clamp["Clamp and Wedge Assembly"]:::connect
    tieRod["Tie Rod and Cone"]:::connect
    propVert["Vertical Prop and Shore Head"]:::support
    beamPrim["Primary Beam Waler"]:::support
    beamSec["Secondary Beam Waler"]:::support
    brace["Diagonal Brace Tube"]:::support
    corner["Hinge and Corner Piece"]:::connect
    concPour["Fresh Concrete Pour"]:::output
    concHard["Hardened Concrete Element"]:::output

    panelWall --> clamp
    panelCol --> clamp
    clamp --> tieRod
    panelSlab --> beamSec
    beamSec --> beamPrim
    beamPrim --> propVert
    propVert --> brace
    panelWall --> corner
    panelCol --> corner
    corner --> clamp
    clamp --> concPour
    propVert --> concPour
    beamPrim --> concPour
    concPour --> concHard

    classDef panel fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    classDef connect fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1B3A6B,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#2D6A4F,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#800000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Formwork Cycle Flow Diagram

This diagram illustrates the **sequential operational flow** of a single formwork cycle from strip to re-pour.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sA["Stage 1 Stripping of Formwork"]:::stage
    sB["Stage 2 Cleaning of Panels"]:::stage
    sC["Stage 3 Application of Release Agent"]:::stage
    sD["Stage 4 Storage and Inspection"]:::stage
    sE["Stage 5 Repositioning at Next Pour Location"]:::stage
    sF["Stage 6 Plumbing and Alignment Check"]:::stage
    sG["Stage 7 Bracing and Clamping"]:::stage
    sH["Stage 8 Concrete Pour and Vibration"]:::stage
    sI["Stage 9 Curing and Hardening"]:::stage
    sJ["Stage 10 Ready for Next Strip"]:::stage

    sA --> sB --> sC --> sD --> sE --> sF --> sG --> sH --> sI --> sJ
    sJ -.repeats.-> sA

    classDef stage fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#4A0E4E,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Formwork Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q1{"Is structural geometry repetitive greater than 4 times"}:::decision
    q2{"Is floor cycle time less than 7 days required"}:::decision
    q3{"Is architectural finish quality critical"}:::decision
    q4{"Is crane available on site"}:::decision

    a1["Choose Modular Steel or Aluminium Formwork"]:::rec
    a2["Choose Conventional Timber Formwork"]:::rec
    a3["Choose Plywood based Prefabricated Panels"]:::rec
    a4["Choose Plastic or Lightweight Modular System"]:::rec

    q1 -- YES --> q2
    q1 -- NO --> a2
    q2 -- YES --> q3
    q2 -- NO --> a3
    q3 -- YES --> a1
    q3 -- NO --> q4
    q4 -- YES --> a1
    q4 -- NO --> a4

    classDef decision fill:#FFE066,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef rec fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#004D3A,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`sA`, `q1`, `a1`, etc.). No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without inline formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define modular formwork. List any four advantages of modular formwork over conventional formwork.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Modular formwork is a pre-engineered shuttering system consisting of standardized panels and accessories of fixed module dimensions that can be rapidly assembled, dismantled, and reused for repetitive concrete construction.

**Any Four Advantages (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Speed of construction** — Reduces floor cycle time from 14 days to 4–6 days.
2. **High reusability** — Steel modular systems can be reused 80–120 times.
3. **Superior concrete finish** — Factory-finished panels yield fair-faced architectural concrete.
4. **Reduced labour dependency** — Requires semi-skilled workers rather than highly skilled carpenters.
5. **Lower wastage** — Panels are returned, cleaned, and reused; no timber scrap.
6. **Dimensional accuracy** — Modular grids ensure geometric consistency.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between modular formwork and prefabricated formwork.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Modular Formwork | Prefabricated Formwork |
|---|---|---|
| Concept | Pre-engineered standardized panel system | Formwork partially/fully assembled off-site before being transported |
| Standardization | Components follow a fixed module size grid | Sub-assemblies are made to project-specific geometry |
| Reusability | Always designed for high reuse | May or may not be reusable; often project-specific |
| Site Labour | Very low on-site fabrication | Reduced on-site fabrication; assembly still required |
| Example | Mivan aluminium formwork for walls + slabs | Pre-assembled column box lifted by crane |

*(Examiner expects at least 3 valid differentiating points for full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Both options are provided below. Examiner picks ONE; student answers ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Modular Formwork Systems — Comprehensive Analysis** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a) Explain in detail the components of a typical modular formwork system with a neat sketch. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A modular formwork system consists of the following functional components:

1. **Panels (2 marks):** The face units that come in direct contact with fresh concrete. Available in standard sizes (e.g., 600 mm × 1200 mm, 900 mm × 2400 mm). Made of steel sheet welded to a stiffening rib frame. Aluminium variants use extruded hollow sections.

2. **Connecting Hardware (2 marks):**
   - *Wedge clamps* — for rapid panel-to-panel connection.
   - *Tie rods with cones* — pass through opposing wall panels to resist lateral concrete pressure; sized for 10 kN to 40 kN tension.
   - *Hinge corners* — for 90° wall junctions.
   - *Filler pieces* — to close non-modular gaps.

3. **Support Members (2 marks):**
   - *Props (vertical shores)* — adjustable steel props for slab panels.
   - *Primary and secondary beams* — distribute slab loads to props.
   - *Diagonal braces* — maintain verticality of wall panels.

4. **Accessories (1 mark):** Lifting hooks, panel pullers, release agent sprayers, scaffolding brackets.

*(A neat sketch showing wall panels connected by tie rod, supported by props with diagonal bracing, is expected for full marks.)*

---

**(b) A contractor is constructing a residential tower with 25 identical floors. Each floor requires 850 m² of slab shuttering. A modular aluminium formwork set covers 180 m² per cycle, has an initial cost of ₹ 18,00,000, salvage value of ₹ 2,00,000, and average maintenance cost of ₹ 3,200 per use. The floor cycle time is 5 days. Determine: (i) Cost per use, (ii) Cost per m² of shuttered area, (iii) Number of formwork sets required if the project must complete in 200 days. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 18{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $S = 2{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $n = ?$ (to be determined from cycle constraint)
- $M = 3{,}200$ ₹/use
- $A_{panel} = 180$ m²/cycle
- Total area to be shuttered = $25 \times 850 = 21{,}250$ m²
- Cycle time = 5 days
- Project duration = 200 days

**Step 1: Maximum number of cycles (uses) achievable in project duration**

$$
n_{max} = \dfrac{200 \text{ days}}{5 \text{ days/cycle}} = 40 \text{ uses}
$$

**Step 2: Number of formwork sets required**

Total area to be shuttered in the project = 21,250 m².
Area one set can cover in 40 uses = $40 \times 180 = 7{,}200$ m².

$$
N_{sets} = \dfrac{21{,}250}{7{,}200} = 2.95 \approx 3 \text{ sets}
$$

**Step 3: Cost per use (for one set, assuming 40 uses)**

$$
C_{use} = \dfrac{P - S + n \cdot M}{n} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 + 40 \times 3{,}200}{40}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{16{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}28{,}000}{40} = \dfrac{17{,}28{,}000}{40} = 43{,}200 \text{ ₹/use}
$$

**Step 4: Cost per m² of shuttered area**

$$
C_{m^2} = \dfrac{C_{use}}{A_{panel}} = \dfrac{43{,}200}{180} = 240 \text{ ₹/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Total formwork cost for the project**

$$
C_{total} = 3 \text{ sets} \times \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{1} = 54{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Result Summary:**
- Cost per use = ₹ 43,200
- Cost per m² = ₹ 240
- Formwork sets required = 3

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
> Students often forget to compute $N_{sets}$ from the **time constraint** (200 days) before computing cost per use. If the wrong $n$ is used, all subsequent answers become wrong. **Always solve the constraint first, then apply the cost formula.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Prefabricated and Reusable Formwork — Comparative Study** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) Discuss the concept of prefabricated formwork with its applications, advantages, and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Prefabricated formwork refers to formwork components (panels, column boxes, tunnel forms) that are **partially or fully assembled in a controlled factory/yard** and transported to the construction site for installation. The intent is to shift skilled shuttering work from the open, weather-affected site to a controlled environment.

**Applications (2 marks):**
- *Mass housing projects* (PMAY, townships) using tunnel formwork.
- *Repetitive columns and beams* in commercial complexes.
- *Precast yard* formwork for double-T slabs, hollow core slabs, beams.
- *Bridge segments* cast using short-line or long-line match-cast systems.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Superior quality due to factory-controlled casting.
2. Faster on-site assembly → reduced project duration.
3. Reduced dependence on site labour and weather.
4. Better safety — less work at heights.
5. Reusability of moulds for precast elements (50–500 cycles).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. High initial investment and transportation cost.
2. Limited flexibility for last-minute design changes.
3. Requires heavy lifting equipment (cranes).
4. Damage during transit can be costly.
5. Standardization of building design is mandatory.

---

**(b) A conventional timber formwork costs ₹ 650 per m² and can be reused 6 times. A modular steel formwork costs ₹ 3,200 per m² initially with a salvage value of ₹ 400 per m², and can be reused 100 times with a maintenance cost of ₹ 25 per m² per use. Compare the cost-effectiveness and determine the break-even number of reuses. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cost per m² of conventional timber formwork (per use)**

Since it is replaced every 6 uses, the cost per m² per use = $\dfrac{650}{6} = 108.33$ ₹/m²/use.

**Step 2: Cost per m² per use of modular steel formwork**

$$
C_{use} = \dfrac{P - S}{n} + M = \dfrac{3{,}200 - 400}{100} + 25
$$

$$
= \dfrac{2{,}800}{100} + 25 = 28 + 25 = 53 \text{ ₹/m}^2\text{/use}
$$

**Step 3: Comparison**

| Metric | Timber (per use) | Modular Steel (per use) |
|---|---|---|
| Cost ₹/m²/use | 108.33 | 53.00 |
| Saving per use | — | 55.33 ₹/m² |

Modular steel is approximately **51% cheaper per use** than conventional timber.

**Step 4: Break-even number of reuses**

Setting modular cost per use = timber cost per use:

$$
\dfrac{3{,}200 - 400}{n} + 25 = 108.33
$$

$$
\dfrac{2{,}800}{n} = 83.33
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{2{,}800}{83.33} \approx 33.6 \approx 34 \text{ uses}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The modular steel formwork becomes more economical than conventional timber formwork after **34 reuses**. Since the design life allows 100 reuses, the modular system is highly cost-effective for this project.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students write the formula as $\dfrac{P - S}{n}$ and forget to **add maintenance cost $M$**. This is a 1-mark loss in 7-mark questions.
> - Do not compare **total initial cost** directly. Always compare **cost per use** to be dimensionally consistent.
> - The break-even answer must be an **integer** ≥ actual $n$ — if it comes out less than 1, the system is uneconomical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Modular formwork = Standardized panels + Compatible accessories + High reusability.** ✅
- **Prefabricated formwork = Off-site assembly, on-site installation.** ✅
- **Reusable formwork = Multiple-cycle economy; cost per use decreases with $n$.** ✅
- **Master formula for cost per use:** $C_{use} = \dfrac{P - S + n \cdot M}{n}$ — memorize this verbatim. ✅
- **Cycle time formula:** $T_c = T_s + T_p + T_{st} + T_d$ ✅
- **Break-even reuses:** $n = \dfrac{C_{mod,init} - S}{C_{conv} - M_{mod}}$ ✅
- **Typical reuse counts:** Timber (4–8) | Plywood (20–40) | Steel (80–120) | Aluminium (150–250) ✅
- **Always solve the constraint (time, area) BEFORE applying the cost formula.** ✅
- **Use crane-based handling for steel modular systems exceeding 50 kg per panel.** ✅
- **In ESE, always draw a labelled diagram for component-based questions — it carries 1–2 marks.** ✅
- **Mention the release agent (form oil) and panel cleaning as part of the cycle — KTU expects it.** ✅
- **Mivan = aluminium modular formwork for monolithic wall-slab construction — frequently asked brand-name question.** ✅
- **Lateral concrete pressure for formwork design (used later in Module 2): $P = \gamma_c \cdot h$ for $h \leq$ pour rate-based limit.** ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "M-P-R-C-T":**
> **M**odular → **P**refabricated → **R**eusable → **C**ycle time → **T**otal cost
> This is the exam-ready 5-point mental checklist for any question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advancements in Formwork Materials and Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advancements in Formwork Materials and Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Formwork** is a temporary or permanent mould into which fresh concrete or similar construction materials are poured, compacted, and allowed to harden, acquiring the desired shape, size, and surface finish. **Advancements in Formwork Materials and Technology** refer to the evolution of formwork systems beyond traditional timber into engineered materials (steel, aluminium, plywood, plastics, FRP composites) and mechanised systems (modular, climbing, slip, tunnel, self-launching, and 3D-printed formwork) that improve **quality, speed, safety, reusability, and economy** in modern concrete construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Advancements in formwork involve the shift from labour-intensive, site-fabricated timber shuttering to factory-engineered, modular, reusable, and sometimes permanent systems that integrate with modern construction methodologies (Mivan, tunnel, jump-form, slip-form, and digital fabrication).*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of formwork like a "mould" used in a kitchen.**
> When you bake a cake, the shape of the cake depends entirely on the mould you pour the batter into. A simple round tin gives a round cake, a fancy silicon mould gives intricate shapes.
> - **Old way (Timber):** A carpenter hand-builds a wooden box for *every* cake, nails it together, and breaks it apart to release the cake. Slow, wasteful, one-time use.
> - **New way (Modular Steel/Aluminium):** A factory makes a perfect, reusable "cake tin" out of steel. After baking, you just release the clamps, remove the tin, wash it, and use it for the *next* cake (building) — hundreds of times.
> - **Smart way (Self-Climbing/3D-Printed):** The mould even moves itself up the building for the next floor, or is printed in the exact shape needed.

In construction terms, the formwork is the "**birth-day suit**" of the concrete — it decides how the structural member will look, how strong it will be at the surface, and how fast the whole building goes up.

---

## 1.3 Why This Advancement Matters (Key Drivers)

| Driver | Description | Engineering Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Repetition cycles of 3–7 days per floor | Cycle time (days/floor) |
| **Quality** | Smooth, defect-free concrete finish | Reuse cycles (typically **80–250**) |
| **Safety** | Integrated platforms, less manual handling | Man-hours per m³ of concrete |
| **Cost** | Higher initial cost, lower lifecycle cost | Cost per use (₹/m²/use) |
| **Sustainability** | Reduced timber consumption | Carbon footprint (kg CO₂/m³) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** A well-designed **MIVAN aluminium formwork system** can achieve up to **250 reuses**, with a cycle time of **4–6 days per floor**, drastically reducing project duration compared to conventional timber (which yields only **5–10 reuses**).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Modern Formwork (Overview Map)

The technology landscape can be broadly grouped into **three families**:

1. **Material-based advancements** — Timber → Plywood → Steel → Aluminium → Plastic/FRP.
2. **System-based advancements** — Conventional → Modular → Crane-lifted → Table/Flying.
3. **Process-based advancements** — Stationary → Climbing → Slipping → Self-climbing → 3D-Printed.

These are explored in depth in subsequent sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Formwork Materials — A Structured Walk-Through

### A. Timber Formwork (Conventional)
- **Material:** Sawn timber (e.g., *Pine*, *Mango*, *Sal*).
- **Use:** Mostly for one-off, complex shapes, small residential works.
- **Life:** **5–10 reuses**; absorbs water → swells, warps, sticks to concrete.
- **Drawback:** High wastage, depends on skilled carpenters, slow.

### B. Plywood Formwork
- **Material:** Plywood sheets (12 mm, 18 mm, 19 mm) bonded with **phenol-formaldehyde (PF) resin** or **melamine-urea-formaldehyde (MUF)** for water resistance.
- **Grades:** MR (Moisture Resistant), BWR (Boiling Water Resistant), BWP (Boiling Water Proof).
- **Life:** **20–50 reuses** (BWP grade).
- **Why better:** Smoother finish, larger sheets → fewer joints, dimensionally stable.

### C. Steel Formwork
- **Material:** Mild steel plates (3 mm–4 mm thick) stiffened with rolled steel sections (angles, channels).
- **Use:** Columns, beams, slabs in repetitive high-rise projects.
- **Life:** **100+ reuses**.
- **Why better:** No water absorption, exact dimensions, easy to clean, but **heavy** (40–60 kg/m²).

### D. Aluminium Formwork (MIVAN System)
- **Material:** High-strength aluminium alloy (6061-T6, 6082-T6) extrusions.
- **Weight:** ~**20–25 kg/m²** (almost **half** of steel).
- **Life:** **200–250 reuses**.
- **Use:** Mass housing, repetitive floor plans (typical for L&T, Tata Housing).
- **Why better:** Lightweight, fast assembly, monolithic casting (wall + slab together).

### E. Plastic / FRP Formwork
- **Material:** Glass-Fibre Reinforced Plastic (GFRP), ABS, Polypropylene.
- **Use:** Complex architectural shapes (domes, curved walls), decorative columns.
- **Life:** **100+ reuses**.
- **Why better:** Corrosion-free, lightweight, can be moulded into any shape.

### F. Fabric / Stay-in-Place Formwork
- **Material:** High-strength woven polymer fabric or fibre-reinforced cement sheets.
- **Concept:** Formwork is **not removed** — it becomes part of the structure.
- **Use:** Pile caps, columns, repair works, underwater concreting.

### G. 3D-Printed Formwork (Cutting-Edge)
- **Material:** Recyclable plastics, wax, or concrete itself (printed formwork for concrete printing).
- **Use:** Complex topology-optimised structures, custom architectural elements.

---

## 2.2 Evolution of Formwork Systems (Process-Based)

| System | Movement | Best Suited For | Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional Timber** | Manual | Small, irregular projects | Slow |
| **Modular Panel** | Manual / Crane | Repetitive floors | 7–10 days |
| **Table / Flying Form** | Crane-lifted horizontally | Large slab areas | 3–5 days |
| **Climbing Formwork** | Crane-lifted vertically | High-rise cores, shear walls | 4–5 days/floor |
| **Self-Climbing (ACS)** | Hydraulic auto-jacking | Supertall structures (Burj Khalifa) | 3–4 days/floor |
| **Slip Formwork** | Continuous slow jacking (y ~ 150–300 mm/hr) | Silos, chimneys, towers | Continuous |
| **Tunnel Formwork** | Crane-lifted whole-room mould | Mass housing (cells) | 24–48 hours/room |
| **3D-Printed Formwork** | Robotic extrusion | Custom, complex shapes | Variable |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarises the key design formulas for formwork — high-yield for KTU Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lateral Pressure of Fresh Concrete (CIRIA / ACI 347)** | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot H$ (hydrostatic limit) | kN/m² | $\gamma_c$ = unit wt of concrete $\approx$ **25 kN/m³** |
| 2 | **Modified ACI Pressure** | $P = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left[7.2 + \dfrac{785}{T + 17.8}\right]$ | kN/m² | $C_w$ = unit wt factor, $C_c$ = chemistry factor |
| 3 | **Plywood Sheet Bending Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8 Z}$ | N/mm² | $Z$ = section modulus (mm³) |
| 4 | **Plywood Deflection (SS sheet)** | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | mm | $E$ $\approx$ **7000 N/mm²** for BWP plywood |
| 5 | **Prop / Acrow Spacing Load** | $P_{prop} = w \cdot A_{trib}$ | kN | $A_{trib}$ = tributary area per prop |
| 6 | **Slip Form Jacking Rate** | $v = \dfrac{H}{T}$ | mm/hr | $H$ = height poured, $T$ = time |
| 7 | **Striping Time (IS 456 / ACI 347)** | $f_{ck}(t) = \dfrac{t}{a + b t} \cdot f_{28}$ | N/mm² | Maturity-based strength |
| 8 | **Stripping Time Approximation** | Side forms: **24–48 hrs**; Soffit: **7–14 days** (depends on span) | hours/days | Empirical, KTU common value |
| 9 | **Formwork Reuse Number (Economics)** | $N = \dfrac{C_{formwork}}{C_{labour\,saved}}$ | reuses | Break-even reuse count |
| 10 | **Maximum Hydrostatic Pressure (cap)** | $P_{max} = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot h_{max}$ | kPa | Generally capped at **150 kN/m²** in many codes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for ACI Pressure Formula factors:**
> **C–C–T** = **C**oncrete weight × **C**hemistry × **T**emperature
> Lower temperature → slower setting → **higher** pressure → **more critical** formwork design.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Burj Khalifa** (Dubai) used **self-climbing formwork (ACS)** to cast its 163 floors.
- **L&T's MIVAN** is used for India's PMAY (Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana) mass housing.
- **Silsoe Cube** (UK) demonstrated 3D-printed formwork for curved concrete columns.
- **Slip-forming** is used to build **cooling towers**, **silos**, and **TV towers** (e.g., Doordarshan, Pitampura, Delhi).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask: *"Compare conventional timber formwork with MIVAN formwork."* — Use the comparison parameters: weight, reuses, finish, cost, cycle time, skilled-labour requirement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Lateral Pressure on Wall Formwork (Modified ACI Method)

**Problem Statement:**
Fresh concrete is to be placed in a wall formwork of height **H = 4.0 m**, with a placement rate **R = 2.0 m/hr**. Concrete temperature is **T = 25 °C**, unit weight **γ_c = 24 kN/m³**, type-I cement with retarder. Determine the **maximum lateral pressure** acting on the formwork using the **Modified ACI 347 formula**.

---

### Given Data
$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 4.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(form height)} \\
R &= 2.0 \text{ m/hr} \quad \text{(placement rate)} \\
T &= 25 \text{ °C} \quad \text{(concrete temperature)} \\
\gamma_c &= 24 \text{ kN/m}^{3} \\
C_c &= 1.0 \quad \text{(Type-I cement, no retarder → use 1.0)} \\
C_w &= 0.5 \left(1 + \dfrac{24}{24}\right) = 1.0 \quad \text{(for 24 kN/m}^{3}\text{, } C_w = 1.0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Pressure from the Modified ACI Formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ACI} &= C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left[\,7.2 + \dfrac{785}{T + 17.8}\,\right] \\
        &= 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \left[\,7.2 + \dfrac{785}{25 + 17.8}\,\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing the fraction:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T + 17.8 &= 25 + 17.8 = 42.8 \\
\dfrac{785}{42.8} &\approx 18.34
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ACI} &= 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \left[\,7.2 + 18.34\,\right] \\
        &= 25.54 \text{ kN/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Hydrostatic Limit
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hydro} &= \gamma_c \cdot H \\
          &= 24 \cdot 4.0 \\
          &= 96.0 \text{ kN/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Apply the Pressure Cap Rule
Per ACI 347, the design pressure is the **minimum** of:
- Computed $P_{ACI}$
- Hydrostatic $P_{hydro}$
- An empirical cap of $144 \text{ kN/m}^{2}$ (≈ 150 kN/m²)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{design} &= \min(25.54,\; 96.0,\; 144.0) \\
           &= 25.54 \text{ kN/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Determine the Effective Head of Concrete
$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{eff} &= \dfrac{P_{design}}{\gamma_c} \\
        &= \dfrac{25.54}{24} \\
        &\approx 1.064 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> The formwork is not designed for the full 4 m of hydrostatic pressure because the concrete begins to **set and self-support** after about 1 m of head. The designer uses the **effective head** of ~1.06 m to size sheathing, studs, and ties.

---

### Step 5 — Design the Plywood Sheathing
Assume 18 mm thick BWP plywood with allowable bending stress $\sigma_{allow} = 12 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$, supported on studs at $L = 400 \text{ mm}$ c/c.

Load per unit area: $w = 25.54 \text{ kN/m}^{2} = 0.02554 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$
Load per unit width: $w' = w \cdot 1 \text{ m} = 0.02554 \text{ N/mm/mm}$

For a 1 m wide strip, simply supported:
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= \dfrac{w' L^2}{8} = \dfrac{0.02554 \cdot 400^{2}}{8} = 510.8 \text{ N·mm/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Required section modulus:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{req} &= \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{510.8}{12} = 42.57 \text{ mm}^{3}\text{/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

For 18 mm plywood (per 1 m width, $b = 1000$ mm, $h = 18$ mm):
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{prov} &= \dfrac{b h^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 18^{2}}{6} = 54{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Check:** $Z_{prov} = 54{,}000 \;>\; Z_{req} = 42.57$ → **Plywood is safe in bending.** ✓

Deflection check (limit = L/270 = 400/270 ≈ 1.48 mm):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \dfrac{5 w L^{4}}{384 E I} \\
I &= \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 18^{3}}{12} = 486{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4} \\
E &= 7000 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (BWP plywood)} \\
\delta &= \dfrac{5 \cdot 0.02554 \cdot 400^{4}}{384 \cdot 7000 \cdot 486{,}000} \\
       &= \dfrac{5 \cdot 0.02554 \cdot 2.56 \times 10^{10}}{1.305 \times 10^{12}} \\
       &\approx 0.25 \text{ mm} \;<\; 1.48 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**The 18 mm BWP plywood sheathing is safe.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Modern Formwork Systems

| Property | Timber | Plywood | Steel | Aluminium (MIVAN) | Plastic/FRP | Self-Climbing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight (kg/m²)** | 30–40 | 10–15 | 40–60 | **20–25** | 8–12 | 80–120 |
| **Reuses** | 5–10 | 20–50 | 100–150 | **200–250** | 100+ | 300+ |
| **Initial Cost** | **Low** | Low–Medium | High | Very High | Medium | Very High |
| **Cycle Time (days/floor)** | 14–21 | 10–14 | 7–10 | **4–6** | 7–10 | **3–4** |
| **Finish Quality** | Rough | Smooth | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Skilled Labour** | High | Medium | Low | **Very Low** | Low | **Very Low** |
| **Best For** | Small / complex | General | Repetitive high-rise | Mass housing | Architectural | Supertall |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Medium | Medium | High | **High (initial), Low (lifecycle)** | Low | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
