\dfrac{C_i - S}{C_{single} - C_m}$ | $C_{single}$ = cost of single-use formwork per pour (₹) | Minimum repetitions to justify reusable system | | 10 | **Landfill Diversion** | $LD\% \;=\; \dfrac{M_{recycled} + M_{reused}}{M_{total\,waste}} \times 100$ | All masses in kg | Sustainability reporting | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cost Engineering:** The CPU formula is used by quantity surveyors to evaluate bids for *system formwork vs. conventional timber* in tenders. - **Green Building Certification:** GRIHA and LEED-India award **2–4 points** for C&D waste management plans; the LD% and RC% formulas feed directly into the calculator. - **Carbon Accounting:** The ECS formula enables compliance with the **Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023** notified by the Ministry of Power, Government of India. - **Project Scheduling:** A high RF reduces material *lead time* in the Critical Path Method (CPM) schedule, indirectly improving project cash flow. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Number of Repetitions ($n^{*}$) The **break-even** point of a reusable formwork system is the number of uses beyond which the per-use cost becomes lower than the single-use alternative. **Step 1:** Total cost of reusable formwork amortized over $n$ uses: $$C_{reusable}(n) \;=\; C_i + n \cdot C_m - S(n)$$ **Step 2:** Total cost of single-use formwork over $n$ pours: $$C_{single}(n) \;=\; n \cdot C_{single}$$ **Step 3:** Setting $C_{reusable}(n) = C_{single}(n)$ for break-even, with $S(n) \approx S$ (constant salvage): $$C_i + n \cdot C_m - S \;=\; n \cdot C_{single}$$ **Step 4:** Solving for $n$: $$C_i - S \;=\; n \cdot (C_{single} - C_m)$$ $$\boxed{\,n^{*} \;=\; \dfrac{C_i - S}{C_{single} - C_m}\,}$$ **Interpretation:** If the actual project requires $\geq n^{*}$ pours, the reusable system is economically justified. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Cost per Use (CPU$_{min}$) We want to find the value of $n$ that **minimizes** CPU. **Step 1:** Recall CPU: $$CPU(n) \;=\; \dfrac{C_i - S}{n} \;+\; C_m$$ **Step 2:** Take the derivative with respect to $n$ and set to zero: $$\dfrac{d(CPU)}{dn} \;=\; -\dfrac{C_i - S}{n^{2}} \;=\; 0$$ **Step 3:** Mathematical insight — as $n \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{C_i - S}{n} \to 0$. Thus: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} CPU(n) \;=\; C_m$$ **Step 4:** Hence, the *theoretical minimum* cost per use equals the **maintenance cost per use**, and is approached asymptotically. There is **no finite $n$** at which a strict minimum exists; the function is monotonically decreasing. However, in practice, $n$ is limited by the **physical fatigue life** of the formwork panel. $$\boxed{\,CPU_{\min} \;\approx\; C_m \quad \text{(as } n \to n_{fatigue}\text{)}\,}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Waste Reduction Percentage with Recycling Credit **Step 1:** Original waste stream from formwork activity: $$W_{before} \;=\; M_t - M_{retained}$$ where $M_{retained}$ is the mass that stayed in service (e.g., as part of the structure or as still-usable formwork). **Step 2:** After implementing the 5-R strategy, the effective waste sent to landfill is: $$W_{after} \;=\; M_t - M_{retained} - M_{recycled} - M_{reused}$$ **Step 3:** Reduction in waste: $$\Delta W \;=\; W_{before} - W_{after} \;=\; M_{recycled} + M_{reused}$$ **Step 4:** Reduction percentage: $$WR\% \;=\; \dfrac{M_{recycled} + M_{reused}}{W_{before}} \times 100$$ This is the precise metric used in GRIHA Criterion **12 (Construction Waste Management)**. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) ### Problem Statement A contractor is evaluating two formwork alternatives for a project requiring **30 pours** of a standard column section: | Parameter | Steel System (Reusable) | Timber System (Single-Use + Partial Reuse) | |-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------| | Initial cost ($C_i$) | ₹ 1,80,000 | ₹ 25,000 (per set, replaced every 5 pours) | | Salvage value ($S$) | ₹ 18,000 | ₹ 2,000 | | Maintenance per use ($C_m$) | ₹ 800 | ₹ 300 | | Reuse factor | 50 pours possible | 5 pours per timber set | **Required:** Compute the total project cost for each system and identify the most economical option. ### Solution **For the Steel System (single set used for 30 pours):** $$CPU_{steel} \;=\; \dfrac{180000 - 18000}{30} \;+\; 800$$ $$CPU_{steel} \;=\; \dfrac{162000}{30} \;+\; 800 \;=\; 5400 \;+\; 800 \;=\; \text{₹ } 6{,}200 \text{ per pour}$$ $$Total_{steel} \;=\; 6200 \times 30 \;=\; \text{₹ } 1{,}86{,}000$$ (plus salvage recovered at end: ₹ 18,000) **Net Steel Cost** = ₹ 1,86,000 − ₹ 18,000 = **₹ 1,68,000** --- **For the Timber System (sets replaced every 5 pours → 30/5 = 6 sets):** $$CPU_{timber} \;=\; \dfrac{25000 - 2000}{5} \;+\; 300 \;=\; 4600 \;+\; 300 \;=\; \text{₹ } 4{,}900 \text{ per pour}$$ $$Total_{timber} \;=\; 4900 \times 30 \;=\; \text{₹ } 1{,}47{,}000$$ **Net Timber Cost** = ₹ 1,47,000 − (₹ 2,000 × 6) = ₹ 1,47,000 − ₹ 12,000 = **₹ 1,35,000** --- ### Comparative Matrix (Analytical Output) | Criterion | Steel System | Timber System | Winner | |-----------|--------------|---------------|--------| | **Net Total Cost** | ₹ 1,68,000 | ₹ 1,35,000 | Timber ✅ | | **Waste Generated (kg)** | ~ 50 (minor wear) | ~ 1,200 (timber offcuts) | Steel ✅ | | **Embodied CO₂ (t)** | ~ 0.25 | ~ 1.20 | Steel ✅ | | **Reusability beyond project** | Yes (20 pours left) | No (discarded) | Steel ✅ | | **Site labour intensity** | Low | High | Steel ✅ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Verdict:** For this *single 30-pour project*, timber appears cheaper. **However**, if the same steel set is reused on the **next 3 projects** (cumulative 120 pours), the amortized steel cost drops to ₹ 1,350/pour, making it **3.6× cheaper** in the long run — the **circular economy argument**. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix for Formwork Selection | Decision Parameter | Weightage (%) | Timber Score (1–5) | Steel Score (1–5) | Aluminum Score (1–5) | Plastic Score (1–5) | |--------------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------------|---------------------| | Initial cost (lower = better) | 20 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 4 | | Reuse cycles (higher = better) | 25 | 1 | 4 | 5 | 3 | | Recyclability (higher = better) | 20 | 2 | 5 | 5 | 3 | | Site labour | 15 | 2 | 4 | 5 | 5 | | Concrete finish quality | 10 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 | | Carbon footprint (lower = better) | 10 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 4 | | **Weighted Total (out of 5)** | **100** | **2.65** | **3.90** | **4.30** | **3.65** | > [!TIP] > This matrix is a **favourite KTU Part-B question pattern** (analytical/comparative). Practice writing such matrices for any formwork selection problem. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The 5-R Waste Hierarchy Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Rethink: Adopt Circular Design Philosophy"] --> B["Reduce: Optimize Material Input"] B --> C["Reuse: Multiple Use Cycles with Maintenance"] C --> D["Recycle: Reprocess End of Life Materials"] D --> E["Recover: Energy Recovery Last Resort"] E --> F["Disposed to Landfill Absolute Last Option"] A1["Modular Formwork Selection"] -.-> A B1["Standard Panel Sizes"] -.-> B C1["Repair and Refurbishment Program"] -.-> C D1["Shredding and Melting Reprocessing"] -.-> D E1["Incineration with Heat Capture"] -.-> E style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff style D fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000 style E fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000 style F fill:#a9a9a9,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > The arrows flow from **highest priority (Rethink)** at the top to **lowest priority (Landfill)** at the bottom. Every downward step represents an *inferior* environmental choice. The dashed annotation arrows (A1, B1, C1, D1, E1) indicate *typical implementation actions* for each tier. ## 4.2 Formwork Life-Cycle Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Stage 1: Raw Material Extraction
Iron Ore, Bauxite, Timber, Crude Oil"] P2["Stage 2: Manufacturing
Steel Mills, Plywood Press, Plastic Moulding"] P3["Stage 3: Transportation to Site"] P4["Stage 4: Formwork Assembly and Use
Multiple Cycles n"] P5["Stage 5: Dismantling and Inspection"] P6["Stage 6A: Reuse Loop
Return to Stage 4"] P7["Stage 6B: Refurbish Loop
Repair and Return"] P8["Stage 6C: Recycle
Shredding and Reprocessing"] P9["Stage 6D: Landfill
Final Disposal"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 P5 --> Q{"Condition of
Formwork?"} Q -- Good --> P6 Q -- Repairable --> P7 Q -- Damaged --> P8 Q -- Beyond Repair --> P9 P6 --> P4 P7 --> P4 P8 --> P1 style P1 fill:#264478,color:#ffffff style P2 fill:#264478,color:#ffffff style P3 fill:#264478,color:#ffffff style P4 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style P5 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style P6 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style P7 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style P8 fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff style P9 fill:#7f7f7f,color:#ffffff style Q fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** The **red blocks** (Stages 4–5) are the *active use phase* where value is created. The **green blocks** (6A, 6B) are the *circular economy loops* — the most desirable outcomes. The **gold block** (6C) is the *recycling* fallback. The **grey block** (6D) is the *landfill endpoint* to be avoided. Notice that recycling feeds back into **Stage 1**, completing the *closed material loop*. ## 4.3 Material Recycling Decision Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Discarded Formwork Component"] --> T{"Material Type?"} T -- Timber or Plywood --> T1{"Chemically Treated?"} T1 -- Yes --> T2["Special Hazardous Waste Channel
IS 14687 Guidelines"] T1 -- No --> T3["Chip into Mulch or Biomass Fuel"] T -- Steel --> ST1["Shred and Melt in EAF"] ST1 --> ST2["Cast into New Sections"] T -- Aluminum --> AL1["Shred and Remelt at 660 deg C"] AL1 --> AL2["95 percent Energy Saving Achieved"] T -- Plastic --> PL1{"UV Degraded?"} PL1 -- Yes --> PL2["Downcycle to Non Structural Use"] PL1 -- No --> PL3["Reprocess into New Panels"] style S fill:#1f3864,color:#ffffff style T fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000 style T2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style T3 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style ST2 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style AL2 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style PL2 fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff style PL3 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > This decision tree helps a KTU student quickly identify the correct end-of-life channel for each formwork material during an exam. **Memorize the decision points**: (1) Is the timber treated? (2) Is the plastic UV-degraded? — these are the two most common exam traps. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define the term "Reuse Factor" of formwork and state its typical value for (i) timber, (ii) steel, and (iii) aluminum formwork systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Reuse Factor (RF)** of formwork is the ratio of the *actual number of repetitions* achieved on site to the *design number of repetitions* specified by the manufacturer: $$RF \;=\; \dfrac{n_{actual}}{n_{design}}$$ Typical values: - (i) **Timber formwork:** RF = **5–20** repetitions - (ii) **Steel formwork:** RF = **80–120** repetitions - (iii) **Aluminum formwork:** RF = **200–500** repetitions *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three typical values.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **List the "5-R" hierarchy of formwork waste management in the correct order of priority, with a one-line meaning of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 5-R hierarchy, in descending order of priority, is: 1. **Rethink** – Adopt circular design philosophy and select modular systems. 2. **Reduce** – Optimize design to minimize raw material input. 3. **Reuse** – Use formwork multiple times with proper maintenance. 4. **Recycle** – Reprocess end-of-life material into new usable products. 5. **Recover** – Extract energy from non-recyclable waste (last resort). *(0.5 marks for each correct R with a brief meaning. 0.5 marks extra for correct sequence.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout ⚠️** > Common mistakes students make in this topic (and where marks are lost): > 1. **Confusing "Reuse" with "Recycle"** — Reuse means *same purpose without processing*; Recycle means *processing required*. > 2. **Forgetting units** in cost-per-use problems — *always* specify ₹/pour or ₹/use. > 3. **Wrongly assuming 100% recovery** for plastic formwork — UV-degraded plastic has reduced mechanical properties. > 4. **Ignoring salvage value** in CPU calculations — this is a 1–2 mark deduction. > 5. **Mixing up embodied carbon values** — virgin steel = ~1.99 tCO₂/t, recycled = ~0.86 tCO₂/t (do *not* interchange). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** #### **(a)** *Discuss in detail the various sources of formwork waste during the construction life cycle. Propose a minimum of **six** waste-reduction strategies with quantitative targets.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** Formwork waste originates at four major stages: 1. **Design Stage** — over-specification, non-modular layouts, and poor detailing lead to excessive off-cuts (3–8% of material). 2. **Procurement & Storage Stage** — improper stacking, transit damage, duplicate procurement (2–5% loss). 3. **Site Operational Stage** — cutting waste (5–15%), damage during striking, and concrete spillage. 4. **End-of-Life Stage** — single-use timber discarded, mixed C&D waste streams. **Six Waste-Reduction Strategies with Quantitative Targets:** | # | Strategy | Quantitative Target | |---|----------|---------------------| | 1 | Adopt **modular system formwork** (steel/aluminum) | Achieve RF ≥ 80 | | 2 | **Standardize panel sizes** (300 mm grid) | Reduce off-cuts to < 3% | | 3 | Implement **just-in-time (JIT) procurement** | Cut storage losses to < 1% | | 4 | **Worker training** in proper striking techniques | Reduce damage loss to < 2% | | 5 | Set up **on-site refurbishment yard** | Salvage ≥ 90% of damaged panels | | 6 | **Digital tracking** (QR-coded panels) | Achieve ≥ 95% recovery at EOL | *(1 mark for identifying each stage, 1 mark for each pair of well-explained strategies = 7 marks.)* --- #### **(b)** *Explain the recycling process for **steel, aluminum, and plastic** formwork materials. For each, give the **embodied energy** and **CO₂ savings** compared to virgin material.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Steel Formwork Recycling (3 marks):** - Process: Collection → Shredding → Magnetic separation → Melting in **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) at 1600 °C** → Continuous casting → Rolling into new sections. - Recovery rate: **95–98%**. - Energy: Virgin steel ≈ **20–25 GJ/tonne**; Recycled ≈ **8–10 GJ/tonne** (≈ 60–70% saving). - CO₂: Virgin = **1.99 tCO₂/t**; Recycled = **0.86 tCO₂/t** (saves ≈ 1.13 tCO₂ per tonne recycled). **(ii) Aluminum Formwork Recycling (2 marks):** - Process: Collection → Shredding → De-coating (if painted) → Remelting at **660 °C** → Casting into ingots. - Energy: Virgin aluminum ≈ **170–200 GJ/tonne**; Recycled ≈ **8–10 GJ/tonne** (≈ 95% saving). - CO₂: Virgin = **≈ 11 tCO₂/t**; Recycled = **≈ 0.5 tCO₂/t** (saves ≈ 10.5 tCO₂ per tonne). **(iii) Plastic Formwork Recycling (2 marks):** - Process: Collection → Sorting (UV-degraded segregated) → Washing → Shredding → Extrusion/granulation. - Energy: Virgin PP ≈ **70–80 GJ/tonne**; Recycled PP ≈ **15–25 GJ/tonne** (≈ 70% saving). - **Constraint:** UV-degraded plastic loses 20–30% tensile strength → *downcycled* to non-structural products (kerbs, drainage pipes). - CO₂ saving: Virgin = **≈ 3.5 tCO₂/t**; Recycled = **≈ 1.0 tCO₂/t** (saves ≈ 2.5 tCO₂ per tonne). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) #### **(a)** *A contractor purchases a steel formwork set for ₹ 3,00,000 with an expected life of **100 uses**. The salvage value at the end of life is ₹ 30,000. Annual maintenance cost is ₹ 8,000. Calculate:* **(7 marks)** 1. *The cost per use (CPU).* 2. *The break-even number of repetitions ($n^{*}$) if the equivalent single-use formwork cost is ₹ 6,000 per pour.* 3. *The number of repetitions at which CPU equals ₹ 4,500 per pour.* **Model Answer:** **Given:** $C_i = 3{,}00{,}000$, $S = 30{,}000$, $n_{design} = 100$, $C_m = 8{,}000/100 = 80$ per use, $C_{single} = 6{,}000$ per pour. **1. Cost per Use:** $$CPU \;=\; \dfrac{C_i - S}{n} \;+\; C_m \;=\; \dfrac{300000 - 30000}{100} \;+\; 80$$ $$CPU \;=\; \dfrac{270000}{100} \;+\; 80 \;=\; 2700 \;+\; 80 \;=\; \text{₹ } 2{,}780 \text{ per use}$$ *(2 marks)* **2. Break-Even Number of Repetitions:** $$n^{*} \;=\; \dfrac{C_i - S}{C_{single} - C_m} \;=\; \dfrac{300000 - 30000}{6000 - 80} \;=\; \dfrac{270000}{5920}$$ $$\boxed{\,n^{*} \;\approx\; 45.6 \;\text{uses}\,}$$ Since the design life (100) > break-even (45.6), **the reusable system is justified** if the project has at least 46 pours. *(3 marks)* **3. Number of Repetitions for CPU = ₹ 4,500:** $$4500 \;=\; \dfrac{270000}{n} \;+\; 80 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dfrac{270000}{n} \;=\; 4420$$ $$n \;=\; \dfrac{270000}{4420} \;\approx\; 61.1 \;\text{uses}$$ $$\boxed{\,n \;\approx\; 62 \;\text{uses}\,}$$ *(2 marks)* --- #### **(b)** *Compare **traditional timber formwork** with **modular plastic formwork** on the following parameters: (i) reuse cycles, (ii) waste generation per m² of concrete, (iii) recyclability, (iv) initial cost, (v) site labour, (vi) embodied carbon, (vii) concrete finish quality. Provide a justified recommendation for a mass-housing project of 1,000 repetitive units.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Comparative Table (5 marks for the table + 2 marks for justified recommendation):** | Parameter | Traditional Timber | Modular Plastic | Winner | |-----------|--------------------|------------------|--------| | (i) Reuse cycles | 5–10 | 50–100 | Plastic ✅ | | (ii) Waste (kg/m² concrete) | 2.5–4.0 | 0.3–0.6 | Plastic ✅ | | (iii) Recyclability | Low (treated timber is hazardous) | High (PP/HDPE can be re-extruded) | Plastic ✅ | | (iv) Initial cost (₹/m²) | 250–400 | 800–1,200 | Timber ✅ | | (v) Site labour (man-hr/m²) | 1.2–1.8 | 0.4–0.7 | Plastic ✅ | | (vi) Embodied carbon (kg CO₂/m²) | 18–25 | 8–12 | Plastic ✅ | | (vii) Concrete finish | Fair (grain transfer) | Excellent (smooth, fair-faced) | Plastic ✅ | **Justified Recommendation (2 marks):** For a **mass-housing project of 1,000 repetitive units**, **modular plastic formwork is strongly recommended** despite the higher initial cost. Justification: - **Amortization across 1,000 units** drastically reduces the per-unit cost (high RF of plastic). - **Reduced waste disposal** saves ₹ 500–₹ 2,000/tonne in landfill fees. - **Faster cycle time** (lower labour) accelerates project delivery. - **Fair-faced finish** may eliminate plastering cost (₹ 80–₹ 120/m² saved). - **GRIHA/LEED points** (4–6 points) translate into tangible certification value. **Net Life-Cycle Cost** of plastic over 1,000 units is typically **30–45% lower** than timber, even after accounting for the 3–4× higher initial investment. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Pin These to Your Wall 📌** - 🔹 **5-R Hierarchy (in order):** Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover. Always prefer the topmost option. - 🔹 **Reuse Factors:** Timber = 5–20 | Steel = 80–120 | Aluminum = 200–500 | Plastic = 50–100. - 🔹 **Cost per Use formula:** $CPU = (C_i - S)/n + C_m$ — include **salvage value** to avoid losing marks. - 🔹 **Break-even repetitions:** $n^{*} = (C_i - S) / (C_{single} - C_m)$ — use it to decide reusability. - 🔹 **Steel recycling:** Recovery = 95–98%, Energy saved = 60–70%, CO₂ saved = 1.13 tCO₂/t. - 🔹 **Aluminum recycling:** Energy saved = ~95% (largest among all formwork materials), CO₂ saved = ~10.5 tCO₂/t. - 🔹 **Plastic recycling caveat:** UV-degraded plastic *loses 20–30% strength* → downcycled, not upcycled. - 🔹 **Treated timber** (CCA/creosote) is a **hazardous waste** under IS 14687 — cannot be simply chipped. - 🔹 **C&D waste share from formwork (India):** 8–12% by mass; targeting a 50% reduction is realistic. - 🔹 **Landfill Diversion %** is the master KPI for GRIHA Criterion 12 and LEED MR Credit. - 🔹 **Embodied carbon reference values** (memorize these): - Virgin steel: **1.99 tCO₂/t** | Recycled steel: **0.86 tCO₂/t** - Virgin aluminum: **11 tCO₂/t** | Recycled aluminum: **0.5 tCO₂/t** - 🔹 **Modular formwork (steel/aluminum/plastic)** outperforms timber on **all 7 evaluation parameters** except initial cost — but wins on life-cycle cost for projects with ≥ 30–50 repetitive pours. - 🔹 **Decisions to memorize for the exam:** Treated vs. untreated timber → different disposal routes. UV-degraded vs. fresh plastic → different recycling channels.

topics.title: Environmental Impact Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environmental Impact Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** in the context of Formwork Engineering is a systematic, anticipatory, and participatory process used to evaluate the probable environmental consequences (both beneficial and adverse) of selecting, designing, operating, and disposing of a formwork system for a specific construction project, prior to execution. It is mandated under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** of India, with the **EIA Notification, 2006** (and its 2020 amendments) providing the operational framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For formwork engineering, EIA extends beyond statutory compliance. It encompasses the **embodied energy**, **carbon footprint**, **resource depletion**, **waste generation**, and **reusability potential** of formwork materials (timber, plywood, steel, aluminium, plastic, bamboo) over the entire project lifecycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of formwork as a **"reusable temporary costume"** worn by concrete while it cures and hardens. Now imagine a theatrical production that runs for years. The producer (project manager) must decide:
- Will the costume be **hand-stitched silk** (traditional timber) — beautiful, but discarded after a few shows?
- A **sturdy polyester uniform** (steel panels) — durable, used 100+ times?
- A **lightweight synthetic fabric** (aluminium or plastic) — cheap to transport but harder to recycle?

**EIA is the "sustainability audit" of this costume decision.** It quantifies how much forest is cut, how much CO₂ is released in steel production, how many trees die, and how much landfill waste is generated — *before* the construction begins.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Formwork EIA

The following physical and standard metrics are universally used:

- **Embodied Energy (EE):** Energy consumed in material extraction, manufacturing, and transport, measured in **MJ/kg** or **GJ/m³**.
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP):** CO₂-equivalent emissions, measured in **kg CO₂-eq/m²** of formwork area.
- **Reuse Cycles (N):** Number of times a formwork unit can be used before failure.
- **Waste Factor (WF):** Percentage of material discarded as waste, typically **5–15%** for timber, **<2%** for steel.
- **Recycled Content (RC):** Percentage of recycled material in the formwork system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — Life Cycle Assessment (LCA):**
> LCA is the analytical tool underpinning EIA. It follows **ISO 14040 / ISO 14044** standards and divides a product's life into **5 stages**: Raw Material Extraction → Manufacturing → Transportation → Use (Construction Phase) → End-of-Life (Disposal/Recycling). This is commonly remembered as **"Cradle-to-Grave"** analysis.

## 1.4 Statutory Framework in India (KTU-Relevant)

| Instrument | Year | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|
| Water Act | 1974 | Prevention of water pollution from construction wash-out |
| Air Act | 1981 | Control of dust from timber cutting, formwork cleaning |
| EPA (Umbrella Act) | 1986 | Overarching power to regulate environmental damage |
| EIA Notification | 2006 | Mandatory EIA for projects ≥ 20,000 m² built-up area |
| EIA Notification (Amended) | 2020 | Streamlined clearance, post facto compliance barred |
| Construction & Demolition Waste Rules | 2016 | Mandates 50–75% reuse/recycling of C&D waste |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of EIA for Formwork Systems

The EIA process for a formwork decision follows a structured 6-step logic:

- **Step 1 — Screening:** Determine whether the project crosses the threshold requiring EIA (e.g., built-up area ≥ 20,000 m² for buildings as per 2020 notification).
- **Step 2 — Scoping:** Identify which formwork-related environmental aspects are relevant (timber sourcing, fuel for heaters, waste oil from release agents).
- **Step 3 — Baseline Study:** Document existing environmental conditions (local air quality, forest cover near site, water table).
- **Step 4 — Impact Prediction:** Quantify impacts using the **Leopold Matrix** or **Checklist Method**.
- **Step 5 — Mitigation Measures:** Propose alternatives (e.g., substituting timber with steel, using water-based release agents, recycling timber offcuts).
- **Step 6 — Monitoring & Auditing:** Track compliance during construction and at demolition.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Screening?** To avoid unnecessary paperwork for small projects (e.g., a 1500 m² residential villa).
- **Why Scoping?** Because not all environmental issues are material to formwork (e.g., biodiversity impact is low, but carbon footprint is high).
- **Why Baseline?** Without knowing the "before," the "after" cannot be measured.
- **Why Prediction?** To allow design changes *before* costly procurement.

## 2.3 Comparative Environmental Profile of Formwork Materials

| Material | Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) | GWP (kg CO₂-eq/kg) | Reuse Cycles | Waste Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Timber (softwood) | 7.4 | 0.45 | 3–5 | 10–15% |
| Plywood (Phenolic) | 15.0 | 1.10 | 8–12 | 5–8% |
| Steel (Modular Panels) | 25.0 | 1.99 | 100–200 | <2% |
| Aluminium | 155.0 | 8.24 | 200–500 | <1% |
| Plastic (PP/HDPE) | 70.0 | 2.50 | 50–100 | 3–5% |
| Bamboo (Engineered) | 5.0 | 0.30 | 5–8 | 5–10% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Despite aluminium's very high initial embodied energy, its **200+ reuse cycles** reduce the *amortized* environmental burden to nearly **0.04 kg CO₂-eq/use**, often lower than timber used only 3 times.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formatting Note:** All absolute values and divisions below use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` macros to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amortized Embodied Energy | $AEE = \dfrac{EE_{total}}{N_{reuses}}$ | MJ per use | Compares true lifecycle burden |
| 2 | Amortized GWP | $AGWP = \dfrac{GWP_{total}}{N_{reuses}}$ | kg CO₂-eq/use | Carbon accounting per cycle |
| 3 | Waste Volume | $V_{waste} = V_{formwork} \times WF$ | m³ | Landfill volume estimation |
| 4 | Timber Volume Required | $V_{timber} = \dfrac{A_{form} \times t}{\eta_{cut}}$ | m³ | Forest resource impact |
| 5 | Carbon Sequestered (Timber) | $C_{seq} = V_{timber} \times \rho \times 0.5$ | kg C | Net carbon credit calculation |
| 6 | Net Environmental Index | $NEI = AGWP_{chosen} - AGWP_{baseline}$ | kg CO₂-eq | Measures improvement vs. conventional |
| 7 | Net GWP (Timber) | $GWP_{net} = GWP_{emit} - C_{seq}$ | kg CO₂-eq | True timber carbon balance |
| 8 | EIA Significance Rating | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \times W_i$ | dimensionless | Leopold matrix aggregation |

Where:
- $EE_{total}$ = Total embodied energy of the formwork system (MJ)
- $N_{reuses}$ = Number of reuse cycles
- $A_{form}$ = Total formwork contact area (m²)
- $t$ = Average formwork thickness (m)
- $\eta_{cut}$ = Cutting efficiency (0.85–0.95)
- $\rho$ = Timber density (≈ 500 kg/m³ for softwood)
- $M_i$ = Magnitude of impact *i*
- $W_i$ = Weight/importance of impact *i*

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

This framework is used by:
- **Green Building Certifiers** (IGBC, GRIHA, LEED) to award credits for sustainable formwork.
- **Project Management Consultants (PMCs)** for tender evaluation.
- **Carbon Auditors** preparing mandatory **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)** disclosures for listed real-estate companies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Amortized Global Warming Potential (AGWP)

This derivation shows how the high initial carbon of aluminium formwork is justified by reuse.

**Given:**
- Mass of one aluminium formwork panel: $m = 25$ kg
- GWP of virgin aluminium: $g = 8.24$ kg CO₂-eq/kg
- Reuse cycles: $N = 250$

**Find:** $AGWP$ per use cycle.

**Step 1 — Total GWP of the panel**

$$
GWP_{total} = m \times g
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting the numerical values**

$$
GWP_{total} = 25 \text{ kg} \times 8.24 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplying out**

$$
GWP_{total} = 206.0 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 4 — Amortize over the 250 reuse cycles**

$$
AGWP = \frac{GWP_{total}}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
AGWP = \frac{206.0 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}}{250}
$$

**Step 6 — Final evaluation**

$$
AGWP = 0.824 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq per use}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with timber (1 m³, 600 kg, 4 uses)**

For timber with $g = 0.45$ kg CO₂-eq/kg:

$$
AGWP_{timber} = \frac{600 \times 0.45}{4} = \frac{270}{4} = 67.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq per use}
$$

**Conclusion:** Aluminium's amortized GWP is **~82× lower** than single-use timber, despite a 18× higher initial footprint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Carbon Balance of Timber Formwork (Carbon Sink Logic)

Timber is unique: it sequesters carbon while growing. The "true" impact is the net of sequestered vs. emitted carbon.

**Given:**
- Volume of timber used: $V = 50$ m³
- Softwood density: $\rho = 500$ kg/m³
- Carbon fraction in dry wood: $f_C = 0.50$
- GWP of timber (processing): $g_t = 0.45$ kg CO₂-eq/kg

**Step 1 — Mass of timber**

$$
m = V \times \rho
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
m = 50 \text{ m}^3 \times 500 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 25{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Carbon sequestered**

Carbon in timber (kg) is converted to CO₂-equivalent by multiplying by 44/12 (molar ratio of CO₂ to C):

$$
C_{seq} = m \times f_C \times \frac{44}{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$
C_{seq} = 25{,}000 \times 0.50 \times 3.6667
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate**

$$
C_{seq} = 45{,}833 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 6 — Total emissions from processing**

$$
E_{proc} = m \times g_t
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute**

$$
E_{proc} = 25{,}000 \times 0.45 = 11{,}250 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 8 — Net GWP**

$$
GWP_{net} = E_{proc} - C_{seq}
$$

**Step 9 — Substitute**

$$
GWP_{net} = 11{,}250 - 45{,}833 = -34{,}583 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 50 m³ of timber formwork represents a **net carbon sink of ~34.6 tonnes CO₂-eq** — a negative impact, i.e., a climate benefit. This is why sustainable forestry-certified timber (FSC/PEFC) is favoured in GRIHA-rated projects.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Leopold Matrix Applied to Formwork

| Activity | Magnitude (1–10) | Importance (1–10) | $M_i \times W_i$ | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Timber felling for formwork | 7 | 8 | 56 | Use FSC-certified timber; substitute with steel |
| Diesel for formwork transport | 4 | 6 | 24 | Source locally; use modular systems |
| Release agent VOC emissions | 6 | 7 | 42 | Switch to water-based agents |
| Formwork cleaning water runoff | 5 | 8 | 40 | Settling tanks; oil skimmers |
| Plywood waste to landfill | 8 | 6 | 48 | Offcut reuse for secondary elements |
| Noise from vibrators/hammering | 3 | 4 | 12 | Acoustic barriers; time-windowing |
| Steel panel refurbishment dust | 5 | 5 | 25 | Wet grinding; PPE; bag filters |
| **Total Significance Score** | — | — | **$\sum = 247$** | Threshold for EIA: typically $S > 200$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A project exceeding the **Significance Threshold (S = 200)** triggers a full EIA report. In this example, the project crosses the threshold and **mandates detailed mitigation monitoring**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EIA in Formwork Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Project Inception] --> nodeB[Screening Stage]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Built-up Area >= 20000 m2?}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Scoping Stage]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Exempted Category]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Baseline Data Collection]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Formwork Material Selection]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Impact Prediction Leopold Matrix]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Mitigation Strategy Formulation]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[EMMP Environmental Management and Monitoring Plan]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Construction Phase Execution]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Periodic Monitoring Audits]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Post Construction EIA Report]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Demolition and End of Life]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Recycling or Landfill Disposal]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Lessons Learned Database]
    nodeE --> nodeG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** The oval/rounded blocks (`nodeA`, `nodeB`...) are the EIA process steps. The diamond-shaped decision block (`nodeC`) is the **Screening Threshold Check**. Notice that even exempted projects (small scale) still loop through material selection to maintain best practice.

## 4.2 Formwork Material Selection Decision Tree (EIA-Informed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Start Material Choice] --> nodeQ1{Project Duration?}
    nodeQ1 -- Short Term less than 6 months --> nodeR1[Consider Timber or Plywood]
    nodeQ1 -- Long Term greater than 6 months --> nodeQ2{Reuse Demand?}
    nodeQ2 -- less than 10 cycles --> nodeR2[Plywood or Bamboo]
    nodeQ2 -- greater than 10 cycles --> nodeQ3{Budget Constraint?}
    nodeQ3 -- Tight --> nodeR3[Steel Modular Panels]
    nodeQ3 -- Flexible --> nodeR4[Aluminium Panels]
    nodeR1 --> nodeEnd[Evaluate via LCA]
    nodeR2 --> nodeEnd
    nodeR3 --> nodeEnd
    nodeR4 --> nodeEnd
    nodeEnd --> nodeFinal[Document EIA Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of Node Roles:**
> - `nodeQ1`, `nodeQ2`, `nodeQ3` — Decision diamonds (questions).
> - `nodeR1` through `nodeR4` — Recommended material outcomes.
> - `nodeEnd` and `nodeFinal` — Termination and reporting blocks.
> - The flow ensures that the final material choice is always validated through an LCA before documentation.

## 4.3 Lifecycle Stage Mapping (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL1[Stage 1 Raw Material Extraction] --> nodeL2[Stage 2 Manufacturing]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3[Stage 3 Transportation to Site]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4[Stage 4 Use on Site]
    nodeL4 --> nodeL5[Stage 5 End of Life]
    nodeL5 -- Reuse Path --> nodeL1
    nodeL5 -- Recycle Path --> nodeL6[Stage 5a Material Recovery]
    nodeL5 -- Dispose Path --> nodeL7[Stage 5b Landfill]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Environmental Impact Assessment. List any four environmental impacts specifically associated with formwork operations.**

**Model Answer:**
EIA is a systematic process to identify, predict, evaluate, and mitigate the biophysical, social, and other relevant effects of proposed construction projects prior to the commencement of construction.

Four environmental impacts of formwork operations:
1. **Deforestation** due to timber and plywood consumption.
2. **Air pollution** from VOC release agents and diesel-powered vibrators.
3. **Water pollution** from formwork cleaning runoff containing oil and cement slurry.
4. **Solid waste generation** from plywood offcuts, broken timber, and damaged panels.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is meant by Embodied Energy of a formwork material? Why is it important in EIA?**

**Model Answer:**
Embodied Energy is the total energy consumed in the extraction of raw materials, manufacturing, transportation, and fabrication of a formwork material, expressed in **MJ/kg** or **GJ/m³**.

**Importance in EIA:**
- It quantifies the **upstream environmental burden** before the formwork reaches the site.
- It enables **comparison between alternatives** (e.g., timber vs. steel).
- It is the **primary input** for carbon footprint calculation in green building certification (GRIHA/LEED).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **methodology of Environmental Impact Assessment** in detail as applicable to a formwork-intensive high-rise construction project. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the environmental performance of **timber, steel, and aluminium formwork** using the parameters: embodied energy, GWP, reuse cycles, and waste factor. Recommend the most sustainable option for a 50-storey repetitive-floor project. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

The EIA methodology for a high-rise formwork project follows an **8-step sequential framework**:

1. **Project Description & Site Analysis:** Identify built-up area, number of repetitions, and formwork type. For a 50-storey building with repetitive floor plates, **system formwork** is preferred.

2. **Screening:** A 50-storey project vastly exceeds the 20,000 m² threshold, so a **Category A (full EIA)** is mandatory under the 2020 notification.

3. **Scoping:** Focus areas include timber procurement, transport emissions, release agent chemistry, and end-of-life disposal.

4. **Baseline Studies:** Document regional AQI, ambient noise level (typically 60–70 dB), groundwater depth, and forest cover.

5. **Impact Identification (Leopold Matrix):** Score each activity for magnitude × importance. Formwork-related activities contribute significantly to **waste generation and air pollution**.

6. **Prediction & Evaluation:** Use software tools like **SimaPro** or **GaBi** for LCA modelling. Compute $GWP_{net}$ for proposed material mix.

7. **Mitigation Measures:** Specify:
   - Use of **aluminium flying formwork** (200+ reuses)
   - **Water-based release agents** instead of oil-based
   - **Recycling of plywood offcuts** into packaging material
   - **Rainwater harvesting** to offset cleaning water

8. **Environmental Management & Monitoring Plan (EMMP):** Define monitoring frequency (weekly air quality, monthly waste audit), responsibilities, and budget.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparative Environmental Performance Table:**

| Parameter | Timber | Steel | Aluminium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) | 7.4 | 25.0 | 155.0 |
| GWP (kg CO₂-eq/kg) | 0.45 | 1.99 | 8.24 |
| Reuse Cycles | 3–5 | 100–200 | 200–500 |
| Waste Factor (%) | 10–15 | <2 | <1 |
| AGWP (kg CO₂-eq/use)* | 67.5 | 0.50 | 0.82 |
| Recyclability | Low | High | High |

*For 1 m³ equivalent volume (timber: 600 kg, steel: 7850 kg, aluminium: 2700 kg)*

**Calculation for AGWP (per use, per m³ contact area):**
- Timber: $\dfrac{600 \times 0.45}{4} = 67.5$
- Steel: $\dfrac{7850 \times 1.99}{150} = 0.50 \times \text{scale factor}$
- Aluminium: $\dfrac{2700 \times 8.24}{250} = 0.89$

**Recommendation:** For a 50-storey repetitive-floor project, **Aluminium flying formwork** is the most sustainable option because:
- The 200+ reuse cycle amortizes its high initial embodied energy.
- It enables **4-day floor cycle**, reducing overall project duration and indirect emissions.
- Its low weight reduces crane diesel consumption.

Steel is the second-best alternative if budget constraints rule out aluminium.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology** in the context of formwork materials. List the **ISO standards** governing LCA. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A construction project uses **75 m³ of timber formwork** with the following data:
- Density: 500 kg/m³
- Carbon content in dry wood: 50%
- Processing GWP: 0.45 kg CO₂-eq/kg
- Reuse cycles: 4
Calculate the **net GWP** and the **amortized GWP per use cycle**. Conclude whether the formwork is a net carbon sink or source. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

**LCA Methodology (Cradle-to-Grave):**

LCA follows **ISO 14040** (Principles & Framework) and **ISO 14044** (Requirements & Guidelines). It consists of **4 phases**:

1. **Goal & Scope Definition:** For formwork, the functional unit is typically **"1 m² of formwork contact area used for 1 cycle"** or **"1 m³ of concrete supported."**

2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI):** Quantifies all inputs and outputs:
   - Inputs: timber mass, diesel for transport, water for cleaning
   - Outputs: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, VOCs, solid waste, wastewater

3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA):** Translates inventory into impact categories:
   - Global Warming Potential (GWP)
   - Acidification Potential
   - Eutrophication Potential
   - Ozone Depletion Potential
   - Human Toxicity

4. **Interpretation:** Identifies hotspots and recommends improvements.

**ISO Standards Governing LCA:**
- **ISO 14040:2006** – Principles and Framework
- **ISO 14044:2006** – Requirements and Guidelines
- **ISO 14025:2006** – Environmental Product Declarations (Type III)
- **ISO 14067:2018** – Carbon footprint of products

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Mass of timber**

$$
m = V \times \rho = 75 \text{ m}^3 \times 500 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
m = 37{,}500 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Carbon sequestered**

$$
C_{seq} = m \times f_C \times \frac{44}{12}
$$

$$
C_{seq} = 37{,}500 \times 0.50 \times 3.6667
$$

$$
C_{seq} = 68{,}750 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 3 — Total emissions from processing**

$$
E_{proc} = m \times g_t = 37{,}500 \times 0.45
$$

$$
E_{proc} = 16{,}875 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 4 — Net GWP**

$$
GWP_{net} = E_{proc} - C_{seq} = 16{,}875 - 68{,}750
$$

$$
GWP_{net} = -51{,}875 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 5 — Amortized GWP per use cycle**

$$
AGWP = \frac{GWP_{net}}{N} = \frac{-51{,}875}{4}
$$

$$
AGWP = -12{,}968.75 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq per use}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 75 m³ timber formwork is a **net carbon sink** of **51.875 tonnes CO₂-eq**, with each use cycle providing a negative emission (carbon credit) of approximately **12.97 tonnes CO₂-eq**. The formwork is environmentally beneficial from a carbon perspective, provided the timber is sourced from **sustainably managed forests** (FSC/PEFC certified).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often use the **mass ratio (44/12)** incorrectly. Remember: it is the **molar mass ratio of CO₂ (44) to elemental Carbon (12)**, not a 1:1 conversion. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **amortize** GWP over reuse cycles. A single 8.24 kg CO₂-eq/kg figure for aluminium is misleading without considering 200+ reuses. Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing timber GWP as *only* emissions and ignoring **carbon sequestration** in the embodied energy phase. Marks deducted: 2.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **EIA (statutory process)** with **LCA (analytical tool)**. EIA is a regulatory requirement; LCA is the ISO 14040 method that feeds quantitative data into EIA. Marks deducted: 1.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** In numerical problems, not specifying the **functional unit**. Always state: *"per m² contact area"* or *"per m³ concrete supported."* Marks deducted: 0.5–1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- **EIA** is a **statutory process** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** and **EIA Notification 2006/2020**.
- **LCA** is the **analytical tool** following **ISO 14040/14044**, with stages: Goal → Inventory → Impact Assessment → Interpretation.
- The **4 key environmental metrics** for formwork are: **EE, GWP, Reuse Cycles, Waste Factor**.
- **Amortized metrics** ($AEE$, $AGWP$) are more meaningful than initial values for reusable systems.
- **Carbon sequestration** in timber must be subtracted from processing emissions to get $GWP_{net}$.
- **Conversion factor:** 1 kg of Carbon = $\dfrac{44}{12}$ = 3.6667 kg of CO₂.
- **Aluminium formwork** has high initial EE/GWP but lowest amortized impact due to 200+ reuses.
- **Plywood/timber** formwork is a **net carbon sink** if sustainably sourced.
- **Steel** is the **best compromise** between cost, durability, and environmental performance.
- The **Leopold Matrix** is the standard qualitative-quantitative tool for impact prediction in EIA.
- For projects ≥ 20,000 m² built-up area, **Category A full EIA** is mandatory.
- **Mitigation hierarchy** in formwork: **Avoid → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Dispose**.
- Green building ratings (**GRIHA, IGBC, LEED**) award credits for sustainable formwork choice.
- Always state the **functional unit** in numerical answers to avoid valuation deductions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Construction of Formwork Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Construction of Formwork Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FORMWORK (Definition as per KTU 2024 PECET742 Syllabus):**
> *Formwork* is a **temporary structure or mould** used to support freshly placed concrete until it attains sufficient strength to be **self-supporting**. It is a precisely engineered system comprising sheathing, joists, stringers, shores, bracing, and accessories whose design governs the geometry, surface quality, dimensional accuracy, and safety of the finished concrete element.

In KTU's Outcome-Based framework, the design of formwork is treated as a **load-resisting structural system** in its own right — not merely as a "shutter" for concrete. The system must resist vertical loads (self-weight of wet concrete, reinforcement, construction live loads, equipment loads) and lateral loads (concrete pressure, wind, impact, seismic), while remaining within strict deflection limits so that the hardened concrete retains its intended shape and finish.

## 1.2 Constituents of a Formwork System

A complete formwork assembly (specifically for a beam-and-slab floor) consists of the following hierarchical components:

| S.No | Component | Function | Typical Material |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Sheathing / Skin** | Direct contact with concrete; gives surface finish | Plywood, steel plate, plastic |
| 2 | **Joists (Bearers)** | Support the sheathing between stringers | Timber, aluminium, steel |
| 3 | **Stringers (Ledgers)** | Support the joists and transfer load to shores | Timber, steel sections |
| 4 | **Shores (Props / Posts)** | Vertical members carrying load to the ground/slab below | Timber, adjustable steel props, tubular props |
| 5 | **Bracing (Diagonal)** | Resist lateral loads, prevent racking | Timber, tubular steel with couplers |
| 6 | **Ties & Anchors** | Resist concrete lateral pressure on opposite faces | Tie rods, coil ties, she-bolts |
| 7 | **Walers** | Distribute tie-rod reaction along the form face | Timber, double channels |
| 8 | **Sole Plates / Base Plates** | Distribute prop load over a larger ground area | Timber, steel plate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook (Examiner Tip):** The vertical **load path** in formwork is *Sheathing → Joists → Stringers → Shores → Sole Plate → Ground*. If you trace the path from wet concrete to the earth, you can never miss a component in your answer.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete Aquarium" Intuition

Imagine you are filling a **hollow aquarium** with a dense, heavy liquid (wet concrete, density ≈ **24 kN/m³**) that hardens after a few hours. The "glass walls" of this aquarium are the formwork. The walls must:
- Hold the heavy liquid without **bulging outward** (lateral pressure),
- Carry the **weight of the liquid** sitting on the floor of the mould (vertical bending),
- Stay **plumb and dimensionally stable** while the liquid is wet,
- Be **removable cleanly** once the liquid solidifies (striking).

The *sheathing* is the glass, the *joists and stringers* are the **frame holding the glass**, the *shores* are the **pillars supporting the floor mould**, and the *ties* are the **stainless-steel rods connecting opposite glass walls** so they don't push each other apart. The "aquarium" must be designed to resist all hydraulic and gravity loads *as a structure*, exactly as the formwork must be designed as a structural system.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Design Philosophy — The Three Pillars

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome CO2** (Module 2), formwork design must satisfy three concurrent criteria:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Formwork Design (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Strength** — Component stresses must be ≤ allowable stress (with appropriate Factor of Safety, typically **2.0 to 2.5** for timber, **1.67 to 2.0** for steel).
> 2. **Stiffness** — Deflection must be ≤ **L/360** for sheathing and **L/270** for joists/stringers (so visible concrete line and surface texture are not impaired).
> 3. **Stability** — The whole assembly must not buckle, overturn, or slide under combined vertical + lateral + wind loads.

A design that satisfies only *strength* but violates *stiffness* will produce a structurally safe but visually unacceptable (sagging, "bellying") concrete member — a common KTU exam trap.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Loads Acting on Formwork (The 5 Canonical Load Types)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **five** distinct load categories that must be combined in design. Each has a specific code-of-practice basis (IS 14687, ACI 347, CIRIA Report 108).

### Load Type 1 — Vertical Dead Load of Fresh Concrete
Density of reinforced concrete (wet) is universally taken as:
$$ \gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3 = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$

For a slab of thickness $t$ (in metres), the uniform dead load is:
$$ w_{DL} = \gamma_c \cdot t \quad \text{[kN/m}^2\text{]} $$

### Load Type 2 — Self-Weight of Formwork Itself
Timber formwork: **0.30 to 0.50 kN/m²**
Plywood + timber: **0.40 to 0.60 kN/m²**
Heavy steel formwork: **0.80 to 1.20 kN/m²**

### Load Type 3 — Construction Live Load
IS 14687 / ACI 347 specifies a **minimum live load of 2.5 kN/m²** for workmen, equipment, and material堆积. KTU exam problems often take this as **2.5 to 3.0 kN/m²**.

### Load Type 4 — Lateral Pressure of Fresh Concrete on Vertical Forms
This is the **most examination-critical** load — covered in detail in §2.3 below.

### Load Type 5 — Environmental & Special Loads
- **Wind load** (IS 875 Part 3) on exposed formwork.
- **Vibration/impact** from concrete pouring (≈ 25 % surcharge may be applied).
- **Seismic** — rarely critical for short-life temporary works, but a check for shores in seismically active zones of Kerala (IS 1893).

## 2.2 Design Load Combinations (IS 14687 / ACI 347R)

| Combination | Vertical | Lateral | When Used |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | DL + LL | 100 % Concrete Pressure | During concrete pour |
| 2 | DL only | Wind (or minimum 1.5 kN/m) | Before/after pour |
| 3 | DL + LL + Impact (25 %) | 100 % Concrete Pressure | Tremie/boom-placer pours |

## 2.3 Lateral Pressure of Fresh Concrete — The KTU Hot Topic

The lateral pressure $p$ exerted by fluid concrete on vertical formwork is **not** the full hydrostatic pressure $\gamma_c H$ because:
- Concrete begins to set soon after placement (initial set ≈ 30 to 90 min),
- Internal friction (the **"thixotropy"** and **"internal arching"** effect) mobilises and reduces wall pressure.

### ACI 347R (US) Formula — Most Commonly Tested
For concrete with slump ≤ 175 mm, placed at rate $R$ (m/hr), and temperature $T$ (°C):

$$ p_{\max} = \gamma_c \cdot C_c \quad \text{[kN/m}^2\text{]} $$

where $C_c$ is the **effective concrete head** calculated as the **lesser** of:

$$ C_c = 0.30 + \frac{9000 \cdot R}{T} \quad \text{[metres]} $$

$$ C_c = H \quad \text{(form height, if smaller)} $$

with **upper limits**:
- For **columns**: $C_c \le 2.4$ m
- For **walls**: $C_c \le 1.8$ m (with R ≤ 2.5 m/hr) or $1.2$ m (with R > 2.5 m/hr)

### CIRIA UK / Indian Simplified Form
For normal-weight concrete placed at rate $R$ (m/hr), temperature $T$ (°C), and formwork height $H$ (m):

$$ p = \gamma_c \cdot H \quad \text{(if } H \le 0.30C \text{ or } H \le \gamma_c H / 150\text{)} $$

or for taller forms:

$$ p = \gamma_c \cdot \left(0.30 + \frac{9000R}{T}\right) \quad \text{[kN/m}^2\text{]} $$

whichever gives the **lower** value, but **not less** than $30\gamma_c$ kN/m² or greater than $150\gamma_c$ kN/m².

## 2.4 Timber Plywood Sheathing — Design Check (Bending + Shear + Deflection)

For a sheathing panel of **thickness $t_s$ (mm), width $b$ (mm, usually 1 m = 1000 mm strip), span $L$ (mm)**, supporting uniform load $w$ (kN/m²):

### Bending Stress
$$ \sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{w L^2}{10} \cdot \frac{6}{1000 \, t_s^2} \cdot 10^3 \le F_b \quad \text{[N/mm}^2\text{]} $$

(For a 1-m strip: $M = wL^2/10$ assuming continuous, or $wL^2/8$ for simply supported.)

### Shear Stress
$$ \tau = \frac{1.5 V}{A} = \frac{1.5 w L/2}{1000 \, t_s} \le F_v \quad \text{[N/mm}^2\text{]} $$

### Deflection
$$ \Delta = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I} \le \frac{L}{360} \quad \text{[mm]} $$

where $I = \dfrac{1000 \, t_s^3}{12}$ mm⁴/m and $E$ is the modulus of elasticity (≈ 7000 N/mm² for MR-grade plywood, 10000 N/mm² for structural plywood).

## 2.5 Timber Joist Design

A joist of section $b \times d$ (mm) spanning $L$ (mm) between stringers:

$$ M = \frac{w_{j} L^2}{10}, \quad V = \frac{w_{j} L}{2}, \quad \Delta = \frac{5 w_{j} L^4}{384 EI} $$

with $w_j = w_{udl} \cdot s_j$ (where $s_j$ is joist spacing in metres) and allowable values:
- $F_b = 10$ to 12 N/mm² (seasoned timber)
- $F_v = 1.0$ to 1.2 N/mm²
- $E = 10000$ to 12000 N/mm²

## 2.6 Shore (Prop) Design — Euler's Buckling

The **most critical check** for shores is **axial buckling**, not crushing.

### Euler's Critical Load
$$ P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L_{eff})^2} \quad \text{[N]} $$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of shore material (N/mm²)
- $I$ = minimum second moment of area (mm⁴)
- $K$ = effective length factor (typically **1.0** for pinned-pinned, **0.8** for fixed-pinned, **0.65** for fixed-fixed)
- $L_{eff}$ = unbraced length of the shore (mm)

### Allowable Load
$$ P_{allow} = \frac{P_{cr}}{FS} \quad \text{(with } FS = 2.5 \text{ to 3.0 \textbf{ for timber shores, } 2.0 \text{ for steel props)}} $$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Concrete density | $\gamma_c = 24$ | kN/m³ | Standard for RCC (wet) |
| 2 | Concrete dead load (slab) | $w_{DL} = \gamma_c \cdot t$ | kN/m² | $t$ = slab thickness (m) |
| 3 | Construction LL | $LL = 2.5$ to $3.0$ | kN/m² | IS 14687 / ACI 347 |
| 4 | ACI effective head | $C_c = 0.30 + 9000R/T$ | m | $R$ in m/hr, $T$ in °C |
| 5 | Max concrete pressure | $p_{\max} = 24 \cdot C_c$ | kN/m² | $C_c$ capped (1.8 or 2.4 m) |
| 6 | Bending moment (SS beam) | $M = wL^2/8$ | kN·m | Sheathing, joist (simply supported) |
| 7 | Bending moment (continuous) | $M = wL^2/10$ | kN·m | Sheathing over $\geq 3$ supports |
| 8 | Bending stress | $\sigma_b = M/Z$ | N/mm² | $Z = bd^2/6$ |
| 9 | Deflection limit | $\Delta_{allow} = L/360$ | mm | Sheathing (most stringent) |
| 10 | Deflection limit (joist) | $\Delta_{allow} = L/270$ | mm | Joist/stringer (ACI 347) |
| 11 | Euler buckling | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI/(KL_{eff})^2$ | N | For long slender shores |
| 12 | Timber allow. bending | $F_b \approx 10$ to $12$ | N/mm² | Seasoned structural timber |
| 13 | Plywood $E$ | $E \approx 7000$ to $10000$ | N/mm² | MR / BWR / Structural |

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution Reminder:** When substituting $w$ in kN/m and $L$ in m, the resulting $M$ is in kN·m. Convert to N·mm by multiplying by $10^6$ before computing $\sigma_b$ in N/mm².

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design methodology is the cornerstone of:
- **High-rise construction** in Kerala (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut) where multi-storey table-form and MIVAN aluminium formwork systems are used with shore loads up to 40 kN per prop.
- **Bridge piers and girders** requiring special high-pressure formwork (lateral pressure > 100 kN/m² for deep pours).
- **Industrial raft foundations** up to 1.5 to 3.0 m thick (where CIRIA full hydrostatic pressure $24H$ may apply for the first few hours of placement).
- **Safety auditing** by KTU-affiliated structural consultants for "Formwork Safety" as mandated by the **Kerala Building Rules** and the **Factories Act 1948 (Formwork Section)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Head of Concrete ($C_c$)

The lateral pressure of fresh concrete is bounded above by **full hydrostatic pressure** $\gamma_c H$ and below by a **"set-pressure ceiling"** governed by the time it takes for the concrete to develop sufficient internal shear strength to arch over the formwork.

### Step 1 — Hydrostatic Limit
If concrete behaves as a true fluid (which it does for the first few minutes):
$$ p = \gamma_c H $$

### Step 2 — Initial Set Time Limit
Concrete begins to behave as a solid after the **initial setting time** $t_s$ (≈ 30 min for OPC at 30 °C, longer at lower $T$). If the rate of rise is $R$ (m/hr), the height of fluid concrete at time $t_s$ is:
$$ H_{fluid} = R \cdot t_s $$

### Step 3 — Effective Head
The hydrostatic head corresponding to this fluid height:
$$ C_c = H_{fluid} = R \cdot t_s $$

### Step 4 — Empirical ACI Form
From extensive tests, ACI fitted $t_s = 1/30$ hr (= 2 min) per °C (for OPC, normal weight). Substituting:
$$ C_c = R \cdot \frac{1}{30 T} \cdot 9000 \quad \text{(with a constant)} $$
giving the calibrated formula:
$$ \boxed{C_c = 0.30 + \frac{9000 \, R}{T} \quad \text{metres}} $$

### Step 5 — Verification of Units
$$ \frac{9000 \, R}{T}: \quad \frac{(\text{constant} \cdot \text{m/hr})}{°\text{C}} = \frac{\text{m}}{°\text{C}} \cdot \frac{°\text{C}}{1} \cdot \text{unit-fudge} \Rightarrow \text{metres} $$

The constant **9000** carries a hidden unit conversion: 9000/3600 ≈ 2.5 (sec-to-hr adjustment) with a temperature-dependent rate constant.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bending Stress in Plywood Sheathing

Consider a **1 m wide strip** of sheathing supported on joists at spacing $L$ (m). It is treated as a beam of length $L$ carrying a uniformly distributed load $w$ (kN/m²).

### Step 1 — Distributed Load on Strip
$$ w_{strip} = w \cdot 1 = w \text{ kN/m (on 1 m wide strip)} $$

### Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment
For a beam continuous over 3 or more supports (a typical 3-span continuous sheathing), the moment coefficient is taken as **+1/10** for the maximum positive moment at mid-span:
$$ M = \frac{w_{strip} \, L^2}{10} \quad \text{[kN·m]} $$

### Step 3 — Convert to N·mm
$$ M = \frac{w \, L^2}{10} \times 10^6 \quad \text{[N·mm]} $$

### Step 4 — Section Modulus (per 1 m wide strip)
$$ Z = \frac{b \, t_s^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \cdot t_s^2}{6} \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{]} $$

### Step 5 — Bending Stress Equation
$$ \sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{w L^2 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 6}{10 \cdot 1000 \, t_s^2} = \frac{600 \, w \, L^2}{t_s^2} \quad \text{[N/mm}^2\text{]} $$

> **Final Compact Form (per 1 m strip):**
> $$ \boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{600 \, w \, L^2}{t_s^2} \le F_b \text{ N/mm}^2} $$

where $w$ is in kN/m², $L$ in m, $t_s$ in mm.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deflection of Sheathing

For a continuous beam under UDL, the maximum deflection in the end span is:
$$ \Delta = \frac{w_{strip} \, L^4}{184 \, E I} \approx \frac{5 w_{strip} L^4}{384 EI} $$

### Step 1 — Section Inertia (per 1 m strip)
$$ I = \frac{1000 \, t_s^3}{12} \quad \text{[mm}^4\text{]} $$

### Step 2 — Deflection Equation
$$ \Delta = \frac{5 \, w \cdot L^4 \cdot 10^{12}}{384 \cdot E \cdot (1000 t_s^3 / 12)} \quad \text{[mm]} $$

### Step 3 — Simplification
$$ \Delta = \frac{5 \cdot 12 \cdot w \cdot L^4 \cdot 10^9}{384 \, E \, t_s^3} = \frac{w L^4 \cdot 10^9}{6.4 \, E \, t_s^3} \quad \text{[mm]} $$

> **Final Compact Form:**
> $$ \boxed{\Delta = \frac{w \, L^4 \times 10^9}{6.4 \, E \, t_s^3} \le \frac{L \times 10^3}{360} \text{ mm}} $$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Euler's Buckling Load for a Timber Shore

Consider a shore of length $L$, cross-section $b \times d$ (with $d < b$, so buckling is about the weaker axis), pinned at both ends ($K = 1.0$).

### Step 1 — Minimum Second Moment of Area
$$ I_{min} = \frac{d \, b^3}{12} \quad \text{[\textbf{minor axis}]} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{b \, d^3}{12} \text{ depending on orientation} $$

### Step 2 — Slenderness Check
Compute slenderness ratio:
$$ \lambda = \frac{K L}{r}, \quad r = \sqrt{I/A} $$

If $\lambda \le \lambda_0$ (transition slenderness ≈ 60 to 80 for timber), **crushing governs**; otherwise, **Euler governs**.

### Step 3 — Euler Load
$$ P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2} \quad \text{[N]} $$

### Step 4 — Allowable Load
$$ P_{allow} = \frac{P_{cr}}{FS} $$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
For a 75 × 75 mm timber shore, $L = 3$ m, $E = 10000$ N/mm², $FS = 2.5$, pinned-pinned ($K=1$):

$$ I = \frac{75^4}{12} = 2.637 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 $$

$$ P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 2.637 \times 10^6}{(1 \cdot 3000)^2} = \frac{2.602 \times 10^{11}}{9 \times 10^6} = 28.91 \text{ kN} $$

$$ P_{allow} = \frac{28.91}{2.5} = 11.56 \text{ kN per shore} $$

## 3.5 Complete Worked Numerical Example (Slab Formwork Design)

**Problem (KTU 2024 Style):**
Design the formwork for a reinforced concrete slab of thickness **150 mm** to be cast at a rate of **1.5 m/hr** at a temperature of **30 °C**. Use **MR-grade plywood sheathing** ($F_b = 8$ N/mm², $E = 7000$ N/mm²), **timber joists** (75 × 100 mm, $F_b = 11$ N/mm², $E = 10000$ N/mm²), and **timber stringers** (75 × 150 mm). Assume construction LL = 2.5 kN/m².

### Step 1 — Vertical Loads
$$ w_{DL} = 24 \times 0.150 = 3.60 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$
$$ w_{FW} = 0.50 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (assumed self-weight of formwork)} $$
$$ w_{LL} = 2.50 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$

$$ w_{design} = 3.60 + 0.50 + 2.50 = 6.60 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$

### Step 2 — Lateral Pressure (for side forms, e.g., beam sides)
$$ C_c = 0.30 + \frac{9000 \times 1.5}{30} = 0.30 + 450 = 450.3 \text{ m} $$

But the formwork height is small (say beam depth = 600 mm = 0.6 m), so $C_c$ is governed by full hydrostatic:
$$ p = 24 \times 0.6 = 14.4 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$

### Step 3 — Design Plywood Sheathing
Try 12 mm MR-grade plywood. Check bending:
$$ \sigma_b = \frac{600 \, w \, L^2}{t_s^2} $$

If joist spacing $L = 0.50$ m:
$$ \sigma_b = \frac{600 \times 6.60 \times 0.50^2}{12^2} = \frac{990}{144} = 6.88 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$

Since $6.88 < 8$ N/mm² ✓ (bending OK).

Check deflection:
$$ \Delta = \frac{w L^4 \times 10^9}{6.4 \, E \, t_s^3} = \frac{6.60 \times 0.50^4 \times 10^9}{6.4 \times 7000 \times 12^3} $$

$$ \Delta = \frac{6.60 \times 0.0625 \times 10^9}{6.4 \times 7000 \times 1728} = \frac{0.4125 \times 10^9}{77.41 \times 10^6} = 5.33 \text{ mm} $$

Allowable: $L/360 = 500/360 = 1.39$ mm. **Deflection EXCEEDS** — increase thickness to 18 mm.

Re-check with $t_s = 18$ mm:
$$ \Delta = \frac{6.60 \times 0.0625 \times 10^9}{6.4 \times 7000 \times 18^3} = \frac{0.4125 \times 10^9}{6.4 \times 7000 \times 5832} = 1.58 \text{ mm} $$

Still slightly over. Try 20 mm:
$$ \Delta = \frac{0.4125 \times 10^9}{6.4 \times 7000 \times 8000} = 1.15 \text{ mm < } 1.39 \text{ mm} \checkmark $$

> **Adopt 20 mm plywood sheathing, joist spacing 0.50 m.**

### Step 4 — Design Timber Joist (75 × 100 mm, span 1.0 m between stringers)
Load per joist = $w \cdot s_j = 6.60 \times 0.50 = 3.30$ kN/m.

$$ M = \frac{w L^2}{10} = \frac{3.30 \times 1.0^2}{10} = 0.33 \text{ kN·m} = 0.33 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} $$

$$ Z = \frac{75 \times 100^2}{6} = 1.25 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3 $$

$$ \sigma_b = \frac{0.33 \times 10^6}{1.25 \times 10^5} = 2.64 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 11 \text{ N/mm}^2 \checkmark $$

$$ \Delta = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI} = \frac{5 \times 3.30 \times 1^4 \times 10^{12}}{384 \times 10000 \times (75 \times 100^3/12)} $$

$$ \Delta = \frac{5 \times 3.30 \times 10^{12}}{384 \times 10000 \times 6.25 \times 10^6} = \frac{16.5 \times 10^{12}}{2.4 \times 10^{13}} = 0.69 \text{ mm} $$

Allowable: $L/270 = 1000/270 = 3.70$ mm ✓ (joist OK).

### Step 5 — Stringer and Shore Design
Load on stringer = $3.30 \times 1.0 = 3.30$ kN/m (for 1 m joist spacing).
Adopt a 75 × 150 mm stringer, span 2.5 m, with shores every 1.25 m. Check shore load:

$$ P_{shore} = w \cdot s_j \cdot s_{sh} = 6.60 \times 0.50 \times 1.25 = 4.125 \text{ kN per shore} $$

This is well within the 11.56 kN capacity of a 75 × 75 mm timber shore calculated in §3.4.

### Step 6 — Final Formwork Layout

> **Adopt:**
> - **Sheathing:** 20 mm MR plywood
> - **Joists:** 75 × 100 mm @ 0.50 m c/c
> - **Stringers:** 75 × 150 mm
> - **Shores:** 75 × 75 mm timber, 3.0 m long, @ 0.50 × 1.25 m c/c
> - **Cross-bracing** at every third shore row.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Vertical Load Path in a Slab Formwork System

The diagram below traces the hierarchical transfer of vertical loads from the wet concrete surface all the way to the supporting ground, identifying the role of each component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WET CONCRETE SLAB\nDensity 24 kN/m³\n+ Construction LL 2.5 kN/m²"] --> B["PLYWOOD SHEATHING\n20 mm MR Grade\nSpans between joists"]
    B --> C["TIMBER JOISTS\n75 x 100 mm\nSpacing 0.50 m c/c"]
    C --> D["TIMBER STRINGERS\n75 x 150 mm\nSpans between shores"]
    D --> E["TIMBER SHORES PROPS\n75 x 75 mm\n3.0 m long pinned"]
    E --> F["SOLE PLATE\nTimber 50 mm thick\nDistributes load"]
    F --> G["GROUND OR FLOOR BELOW\nBearing capacity check required"]
    H["DIAGONAL CROSS BRACING\nPrevents racking\nAt every 3rd shore row"] -.-> E
    I["LATERAL TIES and WALERS\nFor vertical form faces\nResist concrete pressure"] -.-> B
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Formwork Design Process Flow (KTU 2024 Algorithm)

This block diagram shows the **logical sequence** a designer follows from initial data to a final, safe design, in line with IS 14687 and ACI 347R.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START: Project Inputs\nSlab thickness, pour rate R, temperature T, concrete grade, height of form H"] --> stepB["STEP 1: Compute Loads\nVertical: DL + FW + LL\nLateral: ACI p = 24 x Cc"]
    stepB --> stepC["STEP 2: Choose Sheathing Material\nPlywood thickness initial guess 12 mm"]
    stepC --> stepD["STEP 3: Check Bending Stress\nsigma_b = 600 w L² / t_s² <= F_b"]
    stepD --> stepE{"STRESS OK?"}
    stepE -- No --> stepC
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF["STEP 4: Check Deflection\nDelta <= L/360 for sheathing"]
    stepF --> stepG{"DEFLECTION OK?"}
    stepG -- No --> stepC
    stepG -- Yes --> stepH["STEP 5: Design Joists\nM, V, Delta checks\nAdopt b x d section"]
    stepH --> stepI["STEP 6: Design Stringers\nM, V, Delta checks\nAdopt b x d section"]
    stepI --> stepJ["STEP 7: Compute Shore Load\nP_shore = w x s_j x s_st"]
    stepJ --> stepK["STEP 8: Check Shore Buckling\nP_cr = pi² E I / KL_eff²\nP_allow = P_cr / FS"]
    stepK --> stepL{"BUCKLING OK?"}
    stepL -- No --> stepM["Reduce shore length or increase section"] --> stepK
    stepL -- Yes --> stepN["STEP 9: Check Bracing and Lateral Stability\nWind, sway, overturning"]
    stepN --> stepO["STEP 10: Final Formwork Drawing and Safety Note\nIssue to site"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Lateral Pressure Distribution on a Wall Form

This schematic shows how the **ACI modified hydrostatic** pressure distribution looks in practice — a **rectangular block** of uniform pressure $p$ over the lower portion of the form, dropping to **zero** above the effective head $C_c$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    formFace["WALL FORM FACE\n(Lateral View)"]:::form
    p1["Region 1: FULL HYDROSTATIC\np = gamma_c x z\n(z = depth from top)"]:::zone1
    p2["Region 2: CONSTANT PRESSURE\np = p_max = gamma_c x Cc\n(for z from Cc to H)"]:::zone2
    p3["Region 3: ABOVE FORM TOP\np = 0\n(Concrete not yet placed)"]:::zone3
    ties["TIE RODS at z = H/2, H/4, 3H/4\nWith WALERS"]:::ties
    wale["WALERS distribute\ntie reaction"]:::wale

    formFace --- p1
    formFace --- p2
    formFace --- p3
    ties --- wale

    classDef form fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zone1 fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef zone2 fill:#D6E4FF,stroke:#06C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef zone3 fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ties fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef wale fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Shore Buckling Modes

Different end conditions produce different effective lengths and hence different capacities. This block diagram contrasts the four standard cases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mode1["CASE A: PINNED-PINNED\nK = 1.0\nP_cr = pi² E I / L²"]:::m1
    mode2["CASE B: FIXED-PINNED\nK = 0.8\nP_cr = pi² E I / 0.64 L²"]:::m2
    mode3["CASE C: FIXED-FIXED\nK = 0.65\nP_cr = pi² E I / 0.4225 L²"]:::m3
    mode4["CASE D: FIXED-FREE (Cantilever)\nK = 2.0\nP_cr = pi² E I / 4 L²\nDANGEROUS - avoid in formwork"]:::m4

    mode1 --> r1["BASELINE Capacity\nAdopt in most designs"]
    mode2 --> r2["HIGHER Capacity\n1.56 x Pinned-Pinned"]
    mode3 --> r3["HIGHEST Capacity\n2.37 x Pinned-Pinned"]
    mode4 --> r4["LOWEST Capacity\n0.25 x Pinned-Pinned\nNEVER use"]:::danger

    classDef m1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef m2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef m3 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef m4 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef danger fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px
    classDef r1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef r2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef r3 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef r4 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** In KTU site practice, shores are usually treated as **pinned-pinned** (K = 1.0) because actual end conditions (loose timber, uneven sole plate) are rarely truly fixed. A conservative designer may even use **K = 1.2** to account for this.
