<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition (As per IS 14687:1999 & KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formwork Loads** are the **combination of vertical, lateral, and horizontal forces** that act on a temporary mould (formwork) during the construction phase, prior to the hardening of concrete. These loads govern the **sizing of sheathing, joists, stringers, shores, and bracing members**.

A *load consideration* in formwork is not a single value — it is a **load envelope** (a worst-case combination) that the temporary structure must safely transmit to the ground or to permanent structural members already in place. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically demands that the student internalise the **classification, estimation, and combination** of these loads using **Limit State philosophy** as adopted in IS 14687, ACI 347, and IS 875.

## 1.2 The Three Principal Load Families Acting on Formwork

| Family | Direction | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Loads** | Downward (gravity) | Dead weight of form, fresh concrete, reinforcement, workers, stacked materials |
| **Lateral Loads** | Horizontal, perpendicular to vertical form face | Fresh concrete pressure, wind, accidental impact |
| **Horizontal Loads** | Along form face | Wind, diagonal bracing reactions, seismic, out-of-plumb loads |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water-Balloon-In-A-Mould" Analogy**
> Imagine pouring very thick honey into a rectangular plastic mould while the mould is held by your two hands. The honey pushes sideways on the mould walls (**lateral pressure**), it pushes downward on the bottom (you can feel the **vertical weight**), and if a gust of wind blows across your workshop, it tries to tip the mould over (**horizontal/wind load**). The honey's sideways push is *not* simply equal to its full hydrostatic head because it is stiff (it gels), so the maximum side-thrust depends on **how fast you pour it** and **how warm the honey is**. Formwork engineering is precisely this — except we replace honey with fresh concrete and hands with engineered timber/steel assemblies, and the consequences of failure are catastrophic.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Values (Highlighted)

- **Unit weight of fresh normal-weight concrete:** **γ_c = 24 kN/m³** (24.5 kN/m³ for design if specifically required)
- **Unit weight of reinforced concrete:** **γ_rc ≈ 25 kN/m³**
- **Density of structural timber (sal/birch equivalent):** **γ_t ≈ 6 to 8 kN/m³**
- **Minimum construction live load on formwork deck:** **2.5 kN/m²** (3.5 kN/m² if motorised buggies used — IS 14687 / AS 3610)
- **Basic wind pressure (India):** **V_b** as per IS 875 Part 3 (zonal: 0.5 to 0.9 kN/m²)
- **Concrete temperature at placement (typical site):** **T = 20 to 32 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Loads (Hierarchical Logic)

### A. Vertical Loads (Gravity-Governed)

1. **Dead Load of Formwork (Self-weight)**
   - Sheathing (plywood ~ 6.5 kg/m² per 12 mm thickness ≈ **0.065 kN/m²**)
   - Joists, stringers, shores (timber: 5–8 kN/m³ × volume)
   - Combined self-weight typical slab form: **0.30 – 0.75 kN/m²**

2. **Dead Load of Fresh Concrete**
   - For slab of thickness *t* (m): $w_c = \gamma_c \cdot t = 24\,t\ \text{kN/m²}$
   - For beams/walls: derived from cross-section × γ_c

3. **Dead Load of Reinforcement**
   - Typically **0.4 – 0.5 kN/m² per 100 mm of slab thickness** (assumes ~1.5–2.0% steel by volume)

4. **Construction Live Loads (Imposed)**
   - Workers + small equipment: **2.5 kN/m²** (minimum)
   - Motorised carts / buggies: **3.5 kN/m²**
   - Heaped materials: as actually stacked, but **not less than 2.5 kN/m²**

### B. Lateral Pressure of Fresh Concrete (The Critical Load)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Hydration-Setting" Principle**
> Fresh concrete behaves as a **thixotropic fluid only until initial set**. The lateral pressure that the form must resist equals the smaller of (a) full hydrostatic head (γ·h), or (b) a value dependent on the rate of placement, concrete temperature, cement type, and method of consolidation. **Faster placement + higher slump + lower temperature + external vibration = higher pressure.**

### C. Horizontal & Environmental Loads

- **Wind load** on exposed formwork face (IS 875 Part 3) — note: shores & braces transmit this to the permanent structure.
- **Seismic load** (in high seismic zones) — typically neglected for short duration, but critical for tall single-side forms.
- **Impact & vibration** from concrete drop, vibrator insertion, equipment start-stop.
- **Inclined formwork** generates axial and shear components that must be carried by inclined shores.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Symbols:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute / "lesser-of" expressions, **never the vertical bar** $\vert$ inside a markdown table cell.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Governing Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_c = \gamma_c \cdot t$ | Slab self-weight per m² | kN/m² | IS 875 / IS 14687 |
| 2 | $P_{\max} = \gamma_c \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic limit on concrete lateral pressure | kN/m² | ACI 347 / IS 14687 |
| 3 | $P = C_w \, C_c \left[7.19 + \dfrac{785\,R}{T + 17.8}\right]$ | Wall pressure (R ≤ 2.1 m/h) | kN/m² | ACI 347-14 (metric) |
| 4 | $P = C_w \, C_c \left[7.19 + \dfrac{1155}{T + 17.8} + \dfrac{244\,R}{T + 17.8}\right]$ | Column pressure (any R) | kN/m² | ACI 347-14 (metric) |
| 5 | $P \le 23.5\,h$ | Hydrostatic upper cap | kN/m² | ACI 347-14 |
| 6 | $P \le 95.8$ kN/m² | Absolute ACI 347 cap | kN/m² | ACI 347-14 |
| 7 | $V_b = V_{b0} \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$ | Basic wind speed → design velocity | m/s | IS 875 Part 3 |
| 8 | $p_d = 0.6\,V_z^2$ | Design wind pressure | N/m² (use kN/m²) | IS 875 Part 3 Cl. 5.4 |
| 9 | $q_{\text{total,vert}} = 1.2(D + F) + 1.6(L + S)$ | Vertical ULS combination | kN/m² | IS 14687 / LRFD analogy |
| 10 | $q_{\text{lateral,ULS}} = 1.2 P_{\max} + 1.3\,W$ | Lateral ULS combination | kN/m² | IS 14687 |

### Symbol Glossary

- $C_w$ = unit-weight coefficient (1.0 for γ_c = 24 kN/m³; varies linearly for 22 ≤ γ_c ≤ 26)
- $C_c$ = cement chemistry coefficient (1.0 Type I; 1.2 Types II/III; 0.85 Types IV/V; up to 1.4 for retarders/retarded mixes)
- $R$ = rate of placement, **m/h**
- $T$ = concrete temperature at placement, **°C**
- $h$ = maximum height of fresh concrete above the point of consideration, **m**
- $D$ = dead load; $L$ = live load; $W$ = wind; $F$ = formwork self-weight; $S$ = stacked material

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The pressure formulas above are not academic — they are tied to **public safety and the economics of construction**. An under-estimate by 30% can collapse a 12 m wall during a single pour (a documented cause of fatalities in India, Gulf, and SE Asia). An over-estimate by 30% wastes ₹20–50 lakhs in unnecessary prop hire on a typical high-rise slab. Mastery of these formulas is the **single highest-leverage skill** in formwork engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Hydrostatic Limit ($P_{\max} = \gamma_c \cdot h$)

### Governing Physics
Fresh concrete, before initial set, transmits pressure as a **dense fluid**. The lateral pressure at depth *h* below the free surface of the placed concrete is given by the classical hydrostatic equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us consider an elemental horizontal strip of thickness $\mathrm{d}z$ at depth $z$ from the top surface of the fresh concrete. The vertical stress at this level is given by:

$$\sigma_v = \gamma_c \cdot z$$

For a fluid at rest (which fresh concrete approximates below the set line), the **lateral pressure coefficient** $K = 1.0$ (Rankine active coefficient for a fluid with zero internal friction). Therefore, the lateral pressure at depth $z$ is:

$$p(z) = K \cdot \sigma_v = \gamma_c \cdot z$$

The **maximum lateral pressure** occurs at the **bottom of the form**, i.e. at $z = h$:

$$P_{\max} = \gamma_c \cdot h$$

Substituting $\gamma_c = 24\,\text{kN/m}^3$:

$$P_{\max} = 24 \cdot h\ \text{kN/m}^2 = 23.5\,h\ \text{kN/m}^2\ \text{(rounded)}$$

**This is the hydrostatic ceiling above which no code permits the design pressure to climb.**

## 3.2 Derivation of ACI 347 Wall Pressure Formula

### Empirical Basis (CIRIA + ACI Field Studies)
The ACI equation is an **envelope fit** to experimental pressure-vs-height curves obtained on actual construction sites. The term $\frac{785 R}{T + 17.8}$ is a **dimensionally consistent** regression on hundreds of data points.

### Derivation Logic

The lateral pressure is bounded above by the hydrostatic limit, and below by the concrete's own shear strength (which increases with hydration time). The **effective height** that exerts hydrostatic pressure is the height of concrete that has been placed **since the last time the bottom of the placed column set**.

For a wall poured at rate *R* (m/h), concrete reaches initial set in time $t_s$ (hours). The **fluid height** $h_f$ is therefore:

$$h_f = R \cdot t_s$$

Empirically, the setting time $t_s$ is inversely related to concrete temperature, and the regression yields:

$$h_f \propto \dfrac{R}{T + 17.8}$$

Adding the **dead-head term** (pressure contributed by the small column of stiff-set concrete that sits above the truly-fluid layer), we get the ACI form:

$$P = C_w C_c \left[7.19 + \dfrac{785\,R}{T + 17.8}\right]$$

The constant **7.19 kN/m²** represents the residual self-weight of the **set/cured skin** of concrete that the form must always carry, even at the bottom of a deep, slow pour.

### Numerical Worked Example (Wall)

**Given:** Wall height = 4.0 m; rate of placement $R = 1.5$ m/h; concrete temperature $T = 25$ °C; Type I cement ($C_c = 1.0$); normal-weight concrete ($C_w = 1.0$).

**Step 1 — Compute the empirical term:**

$$\dfrac{785\,R}{T + 17.8} = \dfrac{785 \times 1.5}{25 + 17.8} = \dfrac{1177.5}{42.8} = 27.51\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$P = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left[7.19 + 27.51\right] = 34.70\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Check hydrostatic cap:**

$$P_{\max} = 23.5 \times h = 23.5 \times 4.0 = 94.0\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Check absolute cap:** 95.8 kN/m². Not exceeded.

**Step 5 — Governing value:**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{design}} = \min(34.70,\ 94.0,\ 95.8) = 34.70\ \text{kN/m}^2}$$

This is the **uniform design pressure** for the wall form (the standard simplification — actual pressure varies linearly from 0 at top to ~$P_{\max}$ at the bottom, but codes permit a **uniform equivalent** to simplify sheathing design).

## 3.3 Full Vertical Load on a Typical Slab-Form (Composite Derivation)

**Given:** Slab thickness = 200 mm; formwork self-weight = 0.5 kN/m²; motorised buggies will be used; stacked rebar on formwork during placement = 1.5 kN/m².

**Step 1 — Self-weight of fresh concrete (per m² of plan area):**

$$w_c = \gamma_c \cdot t = 24 \times 0.200 = 4.80\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Dead weight of reinforcement (per m²):**

$$w_s = 0.05 \times t \times 1000 \cdot g \approx 1.5\% \times 4.80 = 0.072 \rightarrow \text{adopt}\ 0.5\ \text{kN/m}^2\ \text{(conservative)}$$

**Step 3 — Formwork self-weight:**

$$w_f = 0.50\ \text{kN/m}^2\ \text{(measured/assumed)}$$

**Step 4 — Live load (motorised buggies):**

$$w_L = 3.50\ \text{kN/m}^2\ \text{(IS 14687 minimum)}$$

**Step 5 — Unfactored total service load:**

$$q_{\text{service}} = 4.80 + 0.50 + 0.50 + 3.50 = 9.30\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Factored (ULS) load using IS 14687 partial factors:**

$$q_{\text{ULS}} = 1.2\,(D + F) + 1.6\,(L) = 1.2 \times 5.80 + 1.6 \times 3.50 = 6.96 + 5.60 = 12.56\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Shore reaction per metre of shore spacing:**

If shores are spaced at $s_1 \times s_2$ (m), each shore carries:

$$P_{\text{shore}} = q_{\text{ULS}} \times s_1 \times s_2$$

For a $1.0 \times 1.0$ m grid: $P_{\text{shore}} = 12.56 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 12.56\ \text{kN}$ per shore — used for prop / acrow selection.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Wall Example | Column Example | Slab Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Governing Lateral Action | Hydrodynamic (concrete) | Hydrostatic (deep) | None (vertical only) |
| Use of $C_w$ | 1.0 | 1.0 | N/A |
| Use of $C_c$ | 1.0 (Type I) | 1.0 (Type I) | N/A |
| Design Pressure | 34.7 kN/m² | (compute separately) | 0 |
| Code Used | ACI 347 / IS 14687 | ACI 347 / CIRIA | IS 875 / IS 14687 |
| Dominant ULS Load | Lateral | Lateral | Vertical gravity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Load Classification — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FORMWORK LOADS] --> B[VERTICAL LOADS]
    A --> C[LATERAL LOADS]
    A --> D[HORIZONTAL LOADS]

    B --> B1[Dead Weight of Fresh Concrete]
    B --> B2[Self Weight of Formwork Sheathing and Joists]
    B --> B3[Reinforcement Steel Load]
    B --> B4[Construction Live Load 2.5 to 3.5 kN per m2]
    B --> B5[Stacked Material Load]

    C --> C1[Fresh Concrete Lateral Pressure]
    C1 --> C1a[ACI 347 Empirical Formula]
    C1 --> C1b[Hydrostatic Cap 23.5 h kN per m2]
    C1 --> C1c[Absolute Cap 95.8 kN per m2]

    C --> C2[Wind on Exposed Formwork]
    C --> C3[Impact from Concrete Drop]

    D --> D1[Wind Along Form Face]
    D --> D2[Seismic Lateral Force]
    D --> D3[Diagonal Brace Reactions]
    D --> D4[Out of Plumb Effects]

    B1 --> X[Transferred to Shores and Props]
    B2 --> X
    B3 --> X
    B4 --> X
    B5 --> X

    C1a --> Y[Resisted by Wall Form Sheathing and Ties]
    C1b --> Y
    C1c --> Y
    C2 --> Y
    C3 --> Y

    D1 --> Z[Resisted by Horizontal Bracing and Anchors]
    D2 --> Z
    D3 --> Z
    D4 --> Z

    X --> OUT[Safe Transmission to Permanent Structure or Ground]
    Y --> OUT
    Z --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Concrete Pressure Calculation — Decision Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Pressure Estimation] --> Q1{Is element a Wall or Column}
    Q1 -->|Wall| Q2{Is Rate R less than 2.1 m per h}
    Q1 -->|Column| COL[Use Column Formula with 1155 and 244 Coefficients]

    Q2 -->|Yes| WALL1[Apply P equals 7.19 plus 785 R over T plus 17.8]
    Q2 -->|No| WALL2[Use Wall Formula with 1155 and 244 Coefficients]

    WALL1 --> MULT[Multiply by Cw and Cc]
    WALL2 --> MULT
    COL --> MULT

    MULT --> CAP1[Cap at Hydrostatic 23.5 h kN per m2]
    CAP1 --> CAP2[Cap at Absolute 95.8 kN per m2]
    CAP2 --> RESULT[Governing Design Pressure P design]

    RESULT --> USE1[Distribute to Sheathing Ply]
    USE1 --> USE2[Design Studs and Wales]
    USE2 --> USE3[Design Ties and Anchors]
    USE3 --> DONE[End]
```

## 4.3 Shore/Prop Load Path — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Concrete Slab] --> B[Plywood Sheathing]
    B --> C[Joists Primary Beams]
    C --> D[Stringers Secondary Beams]
    D --> E[Shores Acrow Props]
    E --> F[Base Sole Plate]
    F --> G[Ground or Lower Slab]

    H[Live Load 2.5 to 3.5] --> B
    I[Self Weight Formwork] --> B
    J[Reinforcement] --> A

    K[Wind on Side Forms] --> L[Bracing System]
    L --> E

    G --> M[Permanent Structure or Foundation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** List the various vertical loads acting on a formwork system during construction.

**Model Answer:**

The vertical loads acting on formwork are:

1. **Dead Load of Fresh Concrete** — $\gamma_c \times \text{volume}$ (≈ 24 kN/m³ for normal weight).
2. **Dead Load of Formwork** — Self-weight of plywood, joists, stringers, and shores.
3. **Dead Load of Reinforcement** — typically 0.4–0.5 kN/m² per 100 mm of slab thickness.
4. **Construction Live Load** — minimum **2.5 kN/m²** for workers/equipment; **3.5 kN/m²** for motorised buggies.
5. **Stacked Material Load** — weight of construction materials temporarily stored on the form (rebar bundles, block stacks, etc.).

These are combined as per IS 14687 with partial factors **1.2 (D)** and **1.6 (L)** at the ultimate limit state.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **Q:** Define the term "rate of placement" of concrete. How does it influence lateral pressure on formwork?

**Model Answer:**

**Rate of placement (R)** is the **vertical height (in metres) of fresh concrete deposited per hour** during a continuous pour. It is a key input in empirical pressure formulas (ACI 347).

**Influence on lateral pressure:**

- A **higher R** → concrete has less time to set → a taller column remains in fluid state → **higher lateral pressure** (closer to the hydrostatic cap $\gamma_c h$).
- A **lower R** → concrete sets progressively from the bottom up → only a small height behaves as fluid → **lower design pressure**.

**Mathematical link (ACI 347):** $P \propto \dfrac{R}{T + 17.8}$, i.e. pressure is **directly proportional** to the rate of placement and **inversely proportional** to concrete temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

> **Q (a)** (7 Marks): Classify the various loads acting on formwork. With neat sketches, explain the lateral pressure distribution on a wall form.
>
> **Q (b)** (7 Marks): A wall 4.5 m high is to be cast at a rate of 1.8 m/h. The concrete temperature is 28 °C and Type I cement is used. Determine the design lateral pressure using ACI 347. Unit weight of concrete = 24 kN/m³.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Load Classification (4 Marks):**

1. **Vertical Loads** — Dead weight (form, concrete, steel), live load (workers, buggies), stacked material.
2. **Lateral Loads** — Fresh concrete pressure, wind, impact.
3. **Horizontal Loads** — Wind along face, seismic, brace reactions.

**Lateral Pressure Distribution on Wall Form (3 Marks):**

For a wall of height *h* filled with fresh concrete, the **true hydrostatic distribution** is triangular: zero at the top, $\gamma_c h$ at the bottom. However, **ACI 347 permits a uniform equivalent pressure** $P$ (rectangular distribution) for design simplicity:

- **Triangular (true):** $p(z) = \gamma_c \cdot z$, $0 \le z \le h$
- **Uniform equivalent (design):** $P_{\text{design}} = C_w C_c \left[7.19 + \dfrac{785 R}{T+17.8}\right]$, but $\le 23.5 h$ and $\le 95.8$ kN/m²

```
   Top of wall:  p = 0            Top of wall:  p = P_design
       \              ─────────────────────────────
        \             │                           │
         \            │  P_design (uniform)        │
          \           │                           │
           \          │                           │
            \         │                           │
       p = γh (h)    └───────────────────────────┘─ Base
        Triangular              Rectangular
```

The uniform equivalent is derived so that the **total force on the wall is the same** in both distributions.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $h = 4.5$ m, $R = 1.8$ m/h, $T = 28$ °C, $C_c = 1.0$, $C_w = 1.0$, $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³.

**Step 1 — Check applicability:** $R = 1.8 \le 2.1$ m/h → use **wall formula**.

**Step 2 — Compute empirical term:**

$$\dfrac{785 R}{T + 17.8} = \dfrac{785 \times 1.8}{28 + 17.8} = \dfrac{1413}{45.8} = 30.85\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply wall formula:**

$$P = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left[7.19 + 30.85\right] = 38.04\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Hydrostatic cap:**

$$P_{\max} = 23.5 \times 4.5 = 105.75\ \text{kN/m}^2 \quad (\text{not critical})$$

**Step 5 — Absolute cap:** 95.8 kN/m² (not critical).

**Step 6 — Governing pressure:**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{design}} = 38.04\ \text{kN/m}^2}$$

This value is then used to design the sheathing thickness, wale spacing, and tie-rod diameter.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

> **Q (a)** (7 Marks): With the help of IS 14687, explain the partial safety factor format for load combinations used in formwork design. What is the importance of the live load of 2.5 kN/m²?
>
> **Q (b)** (7 Marks): A 200 mm thick slab is supported on a formwork system whose self-weight is 0.45 kN/m². Motorised buggies will be used. Joists are spaced at 0.5 m c/c and stringers at 1.0 m c/c, supported by shores at 1.0 m c/c. Calculate the total factored UDL on the sheathing and the factored load on a typical shore.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Partial Safety Factor Format (5 Marks):**

IS 14687:1999 adopts the **Limit State Method** with the following load combinations for the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** of formwork:

| Combination | Vertical (kN/m²) | Lateral |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Dead + Live | $1.2(D+F) + 1.6(L+S)$ | $1.2 P_{\max}$ |
| 2. Dead + Wind + 0.5 Live | $1.2(D+F) + 1.3 W + 0.8 L$ | $1.2 P_{\max} + 1.3 W$ |
| 3. Dead + Wind (uplift check) | $0.9(D+F) + 1.3 W$ | — |

**Importance of 2.5 kN/m² live load (2 Marks):**

The 2.5 kN/m² is the **statutory minimum** imposed by IS 14687 to account for the combined effect of workers, hand tools, small stacks of material, and transient impacts during placement. It is **non-negotiable**; if the actual on-site activity is more onerous (e.g. stacked rebar piles), the higher value (3.5 kN/m² for buggies, or actual stacked weight) must be used. It ensures the formwork remains safe **even if workers or supervisors are not specifically aware of the load history**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $t = 0.200$ m, $w_f = 0.45$ kN/m², motorised buggies → $w_L = 3.5$ kN/m², $w_s \approx 0.5$ kN/m².

**Step 1 — Concrete self-weight per m²:**

$$w_c = 24 \times 0.200 = 4.80\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Unfactored UDL on sheathing:**

$$q_{\text{service}} = w_c + w_f + w_s + w_L = 4.80 + 0.45 + 0.50 + 3.50 = 9.25\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Factored (ULS) UDL on sheathing:**

$$q_{\text{ULS}} = 1.2(D + F) + 1.6 L = 1.2(4.80 + 0.45 + 0.50) + 1.6(3.50)$$
$$= 1.2 \times 5.75 + 5.60 = 6.90 + 5.60 = 12.50\ \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Linear load on a typical joist (0.5 m c/c):**

$$w_{\text{joist}} = q_{\text{ULS}} \times 0.5 = 6.25\ \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 5 — Load transferred to a typical shore (1.0 m × 1.0 m tributary area):**

$$P_{\text{shore}} = q_{\text{ULS}} \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = \boxed{12.50\ \text{kN}}$$

This 12.50 kN per shore is then used to select an **acrow prop of adequate safe working load** (typically 20 kN SWL for light-duty, 30 kN for heavy-duty — both exceed 12.50 kN with ~1.6 safety factor).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute cap of 95.8 kN/m²** in ACI 347 — even if your formula gives a higher value, the design pressure is capped. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Mixing up wall vs. column formulas** — the 1155/244 coefficients are for **columns only**; using them on walls at low R is incorrect.
> 3. **Unit confusion between kN/m² and kg/m²** — 1 kgf/m² ≈ 0.0098 kN/m²; typical errors are 100×. Always convert to **kN/m²** for KTU papers.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by $C_w$ and $C_c$** when concrete is lightweight or retarders are used — students often treat them as 1.0 implicitly.
> 5. **Computing hydrostatic cap as 24h** instead of 23.5h — minor numerical error but loses a half-mark on proctoring.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorise Before KTU Exam — High-Density Summary**

- [ ] **Three families of formwork loads:** Vertical, Lateral, Horizontal — remember this **trichotomy** for any 3-mark definition.
- [ ] **γ_c = 24 kN/m³** for fresh concrete — a constant you will reuse 20+ times in the paper.
- [ ] **Minimum live load = 2.5 kN/m²**; **3.5 kN/m²** with motorised buggies.
- [ ] **Two key limits on lateral pressure (ACI 347):** $P \le 23.5h$ and $P \le 95.8$ kN/m². **Always check both.**
- [ ] **Wall formula** (R ≤ 2.1 m/h): uses **785** as the rate coefficient.
- [ ] **Column formula** (any R): uses **1155 + 244R** as the empirical term.
- [ ] **Pressure ∝ R/(T+17.8)** — faster pour + colder concrete = higher pressure.
- [ ] **ULS load combination:** $1.2(D+F) + 1.6(L+S)$ for vertical; $1.2P + 1.3W$ for lateral.
- [ ] **Triangular vs uniform** pressure distribution — know that codes permit the **equivalent uniform** simplification.
- [ ] **C_w and C_c** are the two "code multipliers" that adjust the base formula for **concrete unit weight** and **cement chemistry/retarders**.
- [ ] **Shore load** = $q_{ULS} \times \text{tributary area}$ — basic tributary-area statics, but it is the **final step** in nearly every problem.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic cap derivation:** $P = \gamma_c h$ with $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³ → $P = 23.5 h$ kN/m².
- [ ] **Wind pressure (IS 875):** $p_d = 0.6 V_z^2$ (N/m²) — apply with $\text{force} = p_d \times \text{projected area}$.
- [ ] **Sequence of pressure calculation:** Empirical term → multiply by C_w, C_c → cap at 23.5h → cap at 95.8 → governing value.
- [ ] **When in doubt, sketch the tributary area** of a shore, joist, or stringer — KTU examiners reward clear free-body diagrams in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Analysis of Formwork Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Structural Analysis of Formwork Systems** is the engineering process of evaluating the internal forces, stresses, deformations, and stability of temporary formwork structures that support freshly placed concrete until it gains sufficient strength to be self-supporting. It encompasses the determination of load paths through formwork components (sheathing, joists, stringers, shores/props, walers, and braces) and the verification that each member can safely resist the applied loads within permissible limits of stress and deflection as mandated by design codes (IS 14687, ACI SP-4, OSHA standards, and CIRIA Report 108).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> Structural analysis of formwork involves computing the magnitude and distribution of vertical (gravity) loads and horizontal (lateral) loads acting on formwork, and analyzing the resulting bending moments, shear forces, axial forces, and deflections in formwork members using principles of structural mechanics, to ensure safe, economical, and reusable temporary support systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Temporary Skeleton"**
> Imagine a patient with a broken leg — doctors apply a **plaster cast** that mimics the bone's function temporarily, transferring body weight safely to the ground while the bone heals. Similarly, **formwork acts as a temporary skeleton for wet concrete**. Just as a cast must be carefully designed to handle walking loads without cracking, formwork must be analyzed to handle the enormous weight of fresh concrete (approximately **2400 kg/m³** or **24 kN/m³** as per IS 875 Part 1) without bending, buckling, or collapsing. The "plaster" (formwork) transfers loads through multiple layers — the "skin" (sheathing), "ribs" (joists), "spine" (stringers/walers), and "legs" (shores) — exactly like a human skeletal chain transfers weight from skull to feet.

## 1.3 Key Load Metrics (Standard Reference Values)

The following standard weight and load values are universally adopted in KTU examinations:

| Material / Load | Standard Value | Reference |
|-----------------|----------------|-----------|
| Density of fresh concrete | **24 kN/m³** | IS 875 Part 1 |
| Density of reinforced concrete | **25 kN/m³** | IS 875 Part 1 |
| Density of plain concrete | **24 kN/m³** | IS 875 Part 1 |
| Construction live load on formwork | **1.5 to 3.75 kN/m²** | IS 14687 / ACI 347 |
| Minimum slab live load (no motorized carts) | **1.5 kN/m²** (≈ 150 kg/m²) | OSHA / IS 14687 |
| Minimum slab live load (motorized carts) | **3.75 kN/m²** (≈ 375 kg/m²) | ACI 347 |
| Lateral pressure coefficient (Rankine) | **Ka = (1 − sin φ)/(1 + sin φ)** | Soil Mechanics |
| Permissible deflection in formwork | **L/360** for sheathing, **L/270** for joists | ACI SP-4 / IS 14687 |
| Safe working stress in timber (Bending) | **10 to 12 N/mm²** (Seasoned) | IS 883 |
| Safe compressive stress in timber (parallel to grain) | **8 to 10 N/mm²** | IS 883 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The unit weight of fresh concrete is taken as **24 kN/m³** (not 25 kN/m³) because fresh concrete is slightly less dense due to entrapped air and higher water content. This is a frequent trap in KTU numerical questions.

## 1.4 Why Structural Analysis of Formwork is Critical

1. **Safety:** Formwork failures account for nearly **25% of all construction failures globally**, with most incidents caused by inadequate structural analysis (Source: ASCE Failure Database).
2. **Economy:** Over-design wastes material; under-design risks collapse. Analysis optimizes member sizes.
3. **Quality:** Excessive deflection of formwork (beyond **L/360**) leads to sagging slabs and unsightly concrete finishes.
4. **Reusability:** Properly designed formwork can be reused **50–100 times** in timber and **200+ times** in steel.
5. **Code Compliance:** IS 14687, ACI 347, and OSHA mandate rigorous structural analysis before erection.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Loads on Formwork

The structural analysis begins with the **identification and quantification of all loads** that act on the formwork system. These loads are classified as follows:

### A. Vertical (Gravity) Loads

1. **Dead Load of Fresh Concrete (DL_concrete):**
   - Calculated as: Volume × Density of fresh concrete (**24 kN/m³**)
   - For a slab of thickness `t` (in meters): Pressure on formwork = `24 × t` kN/m²
   - Example: A 150 mm thick slab exerts `24 × 0.15 = 3.60 kN/m²` dead load.

2. **Dead Load of Reinforcement (DL_steel):**
   - Typically taken as **0.5 to 1.0 kN/m³** of concrete volume (IS 875).
   - For a 150 mm slab: `0.5 × 0.15 = 0.075 kN/m²` (often neglected for slabs but added for beams).

3. **Dead Load of Formwork Itself (DL_formwork):**
   - Timber formwork: **0.30 to 0.60 kN/m²**
   - Steel formwork: **0.40 to 0.80 kN/m²**
   - This is the self-weight of sheathing, joists, stringers, and shores.

4. **Live Load (LL) – Construction Loads:**
   - Workers, equipment, materials stacked, vibration from concrete placement.
   - Minimum: **1.5 kN/m²** (light construction) to **3.75 kN/m²** (heavy motorized equipment).

### B. Horizontal (Lateral) Loads

5. **Lateral Pressure of Fresh Concrete on Wall/Column Forms:**
   - This is the most complex load — depends on placement rate, temperature, vibration, and concrete consistency.
   - **ACI 347 Formula (Most Used in KTU):**

$$
P_{max} = 7.2 + \frac{785R}{T + 17.8} \quad \text{(kN/m², limited to 144 kN/m²)}
$$

where `R` = rate of placement (m/hr) and `T` = temperature of concrete (°C).

   - **IS 14687 / CIRIA Simplified Formula (Alternative):**

$$
P_{max} = \rho g h \quad \text{(hydrostatic up to a depth } h_0\text{)}
$$

   where `h_0 = P_{max} / (\rho g)` and the pressure remains **constant** below `h_0`.

6. **Wind Loads on Formwork:**
   - As per IS 875 Part 3, designed for a basic wind speed of **Vb = 39 m/s to 50 m/s** depending on zone.
   - For shutter heights up to 10 m, simplified wind pressure = **0.6 × Vb² × 10⁻³ kN/m²**.

7. **Seismic / Inclination Loads:**
   - For shores in multi-storey construction, lateral sway from unbalanced concrete pouring.

### C. Special Loads

8. **Impact Load from Concrete Drop:**
   - ACI 347 recommends adding **0.4 kN/m²** to compensate for impact during concrete placement.
9. **Vibration Load from Vibrators:**
   - Internal vibrators: **0.4 to 1.0 kN/m²** dynamic load.
10. **Post-Tensioning Load:**
    - For PT slabs, formwork must resist upward camber tendency.

## 2.2 Load Combinations (IS 14687 / KTU Pattern)

For formwork design, the governing load combination is:

$$
\text{Design Load} = 1.2 \times (\text{DL}_{concrete} + \text{DL}_{steel} + \text{DL}_{formwork}) + 1.4 \times \text{LL}
$$

For lateral pressure calculations, the combination becomes:

$$
\text{Design Lateral Load} = \max(\text{Lateral Pressure of Concrete}, \text{Wind Load})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Most KTU numericals use a simplified working-stress combination of **1.0 × (DL + LL)** with no partial safety factor unless explicitly mentioned, because formwork is a temporary structure. However, the examiner may test the **limit state combination** (1.2 DL + 1.4 LL) — always read the question carefully.

## 2.3 Analysis of Formwork Members – Structural Mechanics

Each formwork component is analyzed as a structural element (beam, column, or plate). The following are the **five primary checks** performed:

### Check 1: Bending Stress Check (Flexural Analysis)
The formwork sheathing, joists, and stringers act as **flexural members (beams)**. The maximum bending moment depends on the support condition:

- **Simply Supported Beam (Most Common for Sheathing):**

$$
M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}
$$

- **Continuous Beam (Joists over Multiple Stringers):**

$$
M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{10} \quad \text{(for equal spans)}
$$

- **Cantilever (Edge Conditions):**

$$
M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{2}
$$

where `w` = uniformly distributed load (kN/m) and `L` = span (m).

**Bending Stress Check:**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} \leq \sigma_{b,allow}
$$

where `Z` = section modulus (mm³) and `σ_b,allow` = permissible bending stress (N/mm²).

### Check 2: Shear Stress Check
Maximum shear force for simply supported beam with UDL:

$$
V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2}
$$

**Shear Stress Check:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{1.5 \times V_{max}}{A} \leq \tau_{allow}
$$

where `A` = cross-sectional area (mm²) and `τ_allow` ≈ **1.0 to 1.2 N/mm²** for timber.

### Check 3: Deflection Check (Serviceability)
Formwork deflection is critical because excessive sag leads to poor concrete finish. The standard limits are:

- **Sheathing:** δ ≤ **L/360** (where L is span in mm)
- **Joists and Stringers:** δ ≤ **L/270**
- **Shores (lateral):** δ ≤ **L/200**

**Deflection Formula for Simply Supported Beam with UDL:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}
$$

where `E` = Young's Modulus (N/mm²) and `I` = Moment of Inertia (mm⁴).

### Check 4: Bearing / Crushing Check
At points where joists rest on stringers, or shores on the ground:

$$
\sigma_{bearing} = \frac{V}{A_{bearing}} \leq \sigma_{c,allow}
$$

For timber shores, **σ_c,allow ≈ 8 to 10 N/mm²** parallel to grain.

### Check 5: Stability / Buckling Check (for Shores and Props)
Shoring is essentially a **column** and must be checked against Euler buckling:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}
$$

where `L_e = kL` is the effective length. For shores with pin-pin ends, `k = 1.0`; for fixed-fixed, `k = 0.5`.

**Safe Axial Load on Shore (IS 14687 Empirical Formula):**

$$
P_{safe} = \sigma_{c,allow} \times A \times C_f
$$

where `C_f` = reduction factor for slenderness ratio (λ):

$$
C_f = 1 - \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda_c}\right)^2 \quad \text{for } \lambda < \lambda_c
$$

and the slenderness ratio:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}, \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the "Bible" for KTU formwork numericals. Memorize these formulas.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Dead load of fresh concrete | `w_c = 24 × t` | kN/m² | Slab of thickness `t` (m) |
| 2 | Total design load (vertical) | `w = w_c + w_steel + w_form + LL` | kN/m² | Combined load on formwork |
| 3 | Load per unit length on joist | `w_joist = w × s_joist` | kN/m | Where `s_joist` = joist spacing (m) |
| 4 | Load per unit length on stringer | `w_stringer = w_joist × s_stringer` | kN/m | |
| 5 | Load per shore | `P_shore = w × s_joist × s_stringer` | kN | Axial load on prop |
| 6 | Max B.M. (simply supported + UDL) | `M = wL² / 8` | kN·m | Sheathing, joists, stringers |
| 7 | Max B.M. (continuous, equal spans + UDL) | `M = wL² / 10` | kN·m | Joists over stringers |
| 8 | Max S.F. (simply supported + UDL) | `V = wL / 2` | kN | Shear check |
| 9 | Max deflection (simply supported + UDL) | `δ = 5wL⁴ / (384EI)` | mm | Serviceability check |
| 10 | Bending stress | `σ_b = M / Z` | N/mm² | Flexural check |
| 11 | Shear stress (rectangular section) | `τ = 1.5V / A` | N/mm² | Shear check |
| 12 | Lateral pressure (ACI 347) | `P_max = 7.2 + 785R/(T+17.8)` | kN/m² | Wall/column forms |
| 13 | Hydrostatic lateral pressure | `P = ρgh = 24h` | kN/m² | Below critical depth |
| 14 | Deflection limit (sheathing) | `δ ≤ L/360` | mm | Serviceability |
| 15 | Deflection limit (joist/stringer) | `δ ≤ L/270` | mm | Serviceability |
| 16 | Slenderness ratio | `λ = L_e / r` | dimensionless | Buckling check |
| 17 | Euler buckling load | `P_cr = π²EI / L_e²` | N | Long slender shores |
| 18 | Section modulus (rectangular) | `Z = bd² / 6` | mm³ | Bending stress |
| 19 | Moment of inertia (rectangular) | `I = bd³ / 12` | mm⁴ | Deflection, buckling |
| 20 | Allowable Bending Stress (Timber) | `σ_b,allow = 10 to 12 N/mm²` | N/mm² | Design check |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The structural analysis methodology described above is used by:

1. **Formwork Designers** in companies like PERI, Doka, ULMA, and MFE to develop safe system formwork (slab tables, wall panels, climbing forms).
2. **Site Engineers** to verify contractor-designed temporary works before pouring concrete.
3. **Project Managers** to estimate formwork quantities and costs.
4. **Safety Officers** to certify formwork for use on site.
5. **Software Engineers** in tools like **PERI ELPOS**, **Doka Tipos**, and **Risa 3D** which automate these very calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Modern formwork systems are analyzed using **Finite Element Method (FEM)** software, where the formwork is meshed into thousands of elements and solved numerically. The classical formulas above form the **analytical backbone** that FEM solvers verify against.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Bending Moment for Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**Problem Context:** A formwork sheathing is modeled as a simply supported beam of span `L` subjected to a uniformly distributed load `w` (kN/m) from wet concrete.

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental strip of the sheathing of unit width (1 m). The load per unit length is `w` (kN/m).

**Step 2:** By symmetry of loading and support, the reactions at both ends are equal:

$$
R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** The bending moment at a distance `x` from the left support is:

$$
M_x = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplifying:

$$
M_x = \frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** To find the maximum bending moment, differentiate with respect to `x` and equate to zero:

$$
\frac{dM_x}{dx} = \frac{wL}{2} - wx = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Solving for `x`:

$$
x = \frac{L}{2}
$$

This shows that maximum moment occurs at mid-span.

**Step 7:** Substitute `x = L/2` back into the moment equation:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{w}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{wL^2}{4} - \frac{wL^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}
$$

> **Conclusion:** For a simply supported beam with UDL, the maximum bending moment is `wL²/8`, occurring at mid-span.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Deflection for Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**Step 1:** The differential equation of the elastic curve is:

$$
EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M_x
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the moment expression from Derivation 1:

$$
EI \cdot \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{wLx}{2} - \frac{wx^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate once to get the slope equation:

$$
EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1
$$

**Step 4:** Apply boundary condition: slope = 0 at `x = 0` (simple support):

$$
C_1 = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Integrate again to get the deflection equation:

$$
EI \cdot y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} + C_2
$$

**Step 6:** Apply boundary condition: deflection = 0 at `x = 0`:

$$
C_2 = 0
$$

**Step 7:** By symmetry, maximum deflection occurs at mid-span. Substitute `x = L/2`:

$$
EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{wL \cdot (L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{w \cdot (L/2)^4}{24}
$$

**Step 8:** Simplify:

$$
EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{wL^4}{96} - \frac{wL^4}{384} = \frac{4wL^4 - wL^4}{384} = \frac{5wL^4}{384}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The maximum deflection for a simply supported beam with UDL is `5wL⁴/(384EI)` at mid-span.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slenderness Ratio and Euler Buckling Load for a Formwork Shore

**Step 1:** A formwork shore is modeled as a slender column of length `L`, cross-section `b × d`, with end conditions. The radius of gyration is:

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}
$$

For a rectangular section:

$$
I = \frac{bd^3}{12}, \quad A = bd
$$

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{bd^3 / 12}{bd}} = \sqrt{\frac{d^2}{12}} = \frac{d}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{d}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 2:** Slenderness ratio:

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{kL \cdot 2\sqrt{3}}{d}
$$

where `k` depends on end conditions:
- Both ends pinned: `k = 1.0`
- One end fixed, other pinned: `k = 0.7`
- Both ends fixed: `k = 0.5`
- One end fixed, other free: `k = 2.0`

**Step 3:** Euler's critical buckling load:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E \cdot (bd^3/12)}{(kL)^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the typical timber value `E = 12,000 N/mm²`:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 12000 \times bd^3}{12 \times (kL)^2} = \frac{9870 \times bd^3}{(kL)^2} \quad \text{(N)}
$$

**Step 5:** Safe load with Factor of Safety (FoS = 2.5 to 3.0 for timber shores):

$$
P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{FoS}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A timber shore of cross-section `b × d` mm and length `L` mm can safely carry an axial load of `P_cr / FoS` as derived above.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
> Design a slab formwork system for a residential floor slab of the following specifications:
> - Slab thickness = **200 mm**
> - Unit weight of fresh concrete = **24 kN/m³**
> - Live load = **1.5 kN/m²**
> - Self-weight of formwork = **0.5 kN/m²**
> - Allowable bending stress in timber = **10 N/mm²**
> - Allowable shear stress in timber = **1.1 N/mm²**
> - `E` for timber = **12,000 N/mm²**
> - Permissible deflection = **L/360**
> - Sheathing thickness available = **25 mm** (planed timber)
> - Standard joist size: **50 mm × 100 mm**
> - Standard stringer size: **75 mm × 150 mm**

**Find:** (a) The spacing of joists, (b) The spacing of stringers, (c) The axial load per shore, (d) Check the deflection of sheathing.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate Total Design Load (per m²)**

$$
w_{concrete} = 24 \times 0.200 = 4.80 \text{ kN/m²}
$$

$$
w_{total} = 4.80 + 0.50 + 1.50 = 6.80 \text{ kN/m²}
$$

**Step 2: Section Properties of Sheathing (25 mm thick × 1 m wide strip)**

$$
b = 1000 \text{ mm}, \quad d = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{bd^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \times 25^2}{6} = 104,167 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
I = \frac{bd^3}{12} = \frac{1000 \times 25^3}{12} = 1,302,083 \text{ mm}^4
$$

$$
A = b \times d = 25,000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Bending Check for Sheathing (assumed simply supported)**

Assume joist spacing `s` meters. Load per meter on sheathing:

$$
w_{sheathing} = 6.80 \times 1 \text{ m (unit width)} = 6.80 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Span of sheathing `L = s` m.

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{6.80 \times s^2}{8} = 0.85 s^2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Bending stress:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{0.85 \times 10^6 \times s^2}{104,167} = 8.16 \times s^2 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Equating to allowable:

$$
8.16 \times s^2 \leq 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 \leq 1.225 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \leq 1.107 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Shear Check for Sheathing**

$$
V_{max} = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{6.80 \times s}{2} = 3.4s \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{1.5 V}{A} = \frac{1.5 \times 3.4s \times 10^3}{25,000} = 0.204 s \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Equating to allowable:

$$
0.204 s \leq 1.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \leq 5.39 \text{ m}
$$

(Shear is not critical for sheathing — bending governs.)

**Step 5: Deflection Check for Sheathing**

$$
\delta = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI} = \frac{5 \times 6.80 \times (s \times 1000)^4}{384 \times 12000 \times 1,302,083}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{5 \times 6.80 \times s^4 \times 10^{12}}{5.998 \times 10^{12}} = 5.67 \times s^4 \text{ mm}
$$

Deflection limit: `L/360 = (s × 1000) / 360 = 2.778 s` mm

$$
5.67 s^4 \leq 2.778 s \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^3 \leq 0.490 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \leq 0.787 \text{ m}
$$

**Adopt: Joist spacing s = 0.75 m (750 mm)** ← **Governed by deflection**

**Step 6: Bending Check for Joist (50 × 100 mm, stronger axis)**

Load per meter on joist:

$$
w_{joist} = w_{total} \times s_{joist} = 6.80 \times 0.75 = 5.10 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Assuming stringer spacing `S` (continuous joist over multiple stringers, use `M = wL²/10`):

$$
M = \frac{w S^2}{10} = \frac{5.10 \times S^2}{10} = 0.51 S^2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Section properties: `Z = (50 × 100²) / 6 = 83,333 mm³`, `I = (50 × 100³) / 12 = 4,166,667 mm⁴`

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{0.51 \times 10^6 \times S^2}{83,333} = 6.12 S^2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \leq 10
$$

$$
S^2 \leq 1.634 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S \leq 1.278 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7: Deflection Check for Joist**

$$
\delta = \frac{5 \times 5.10 \times (S \times 1000)^4}{384 \times 12000 \times 4,166,667} = \frac{5 \times 5.10 \times S^4 \times 10^{12}}{1.92 \times 10^{13}} = 1.328 S^4 \text{ mm}
$$

Limit: `S/270 × 1000 = 3.704 S` mm

$$
1.328 S^4 \leq 3.704 S \quad \Rightarrow \quad S^3 \leq 2.789 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S \leq 1.408 \text{ m}
$$

**Adopt: Stringer spacing S = 1.20 m (1200 mm)** ← **Governed by bending**

**Step 8: Axial Load per Shore**

$$
P_{shore} = w_{total} \times s_{joist} \times S_{stringer} = 6.80 \times 0.75 \times 1.20 = 6.12 \text{ kN}
$$

A standard adjustable steel prop (4 kN, 6 kN, 9 kN capacity) of **9 kN capacity** is sufficient.

> **Final Design Summary:**
> - **Sheathing:** 25 mm thick timber
> - **Joist spacing:** 750 mm (governed by deflection)
> - **Joist size:** 50 mm × 100 mm
> - **Stringer spacing:** 1200 mm
> - **Stringer size:** 75 mm × 150 mm
> - **Shore capacity required:** 6.12 kN → Use 9 kN prop

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Design Decision Flowchart

The design process follows a strict hierarchy. The matrix below shows **which check governs at which stage:**

| Component | Bending | Shear | Deflection | Bearing | Buckling |
|-----------|---------|-------|------------|---------|----------|
| **Sheathing** | ✓ (Usually governs) | Rarely governs | ✓ **Often governs for thin plywood** | N/A | N/A |
| **Joist** | ✓ (Often governs) | Occasional | ✓ (Critical for long spans) | N/A | N/A |
| **Stringer** | ✓ (Usually governs) | Occasional | ✓ | ✓ At joist bearing | N/A |
| **Shore/Prop** | N/A (Axial only) | N/A | N/A | ✓ At top & bottom | ✓ **Always governs** |
| **Waler (Wall)** | ✓ (Horizontal span) | ✓ | ✓ | N/A | N/A |
| **Tie System** | N/A (Tension only) | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Formwork Load Path Architecture

The following diagram illustrates how vertical loads from fresh concrete travel through the formwork hierarchy to the ground:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: List the various types of loads that act on a slab formwork system. Also state the standard unit weight of fresh concrete as per IS 875.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The following loads act on a slab formwork system:

1. **Dead Load of Fresh Concrete:** Weight of the wet concrete, calculated as `24 × t` kN/m² where `t` is slab thickness in meters. (1 mark)

2. **Dead Load of Reinforcement:** Typically **0.5 kN/m³** of concrete volume. For a 200 mm slab, this is `0.5 × 0.20 = 0.10 kN/m²`. (0.5 marks)

3. **Dead Load of Formwork Itself:** Self-weight of sheathing, joists, stringers, and shores. For timber formwork, this is **0.3 to 0.6 kN/m²**. (0.5 marks)

4. **Live Load (Construction Load):** Loads from workers, equipment, materials, and impact from concrete placement. Minimum is **1.5 kN/m²** as per IS 14687, increasing to **3.75 kN/m²** when motorized carts are used. (0.5 marks)

5. **Vibration Load:** From internal vibrators used for concrete compaction, typically **0.4 to 1.0 kN/m²**. (0.5 marks)

**Standard Unit Weight of Fresh Concrete as per IS 875 Part 1 = 24 kN/m³.** (Bonus: note that this is less than hardened concrete at 25 kN/m³ due to air voids and water content.) (included in marks)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: Define the following terms in the context of formwork analysis: (i) Deflection limit, (ii) Slenderness ratio, (iii) Section modulus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Deflection Limit (1 mark):**
The maximum permissible vertical deformation of a formwork member under load, beyond which the concrete finish quality is compromised. As per IS 14687 and ACI 347, the standard limits are:
- Sheathing: **L/360** of span
- Joists and Stringers: **L/270** of span
- Shores (lateral): **L/200** of unsupported length

**(ii) Slenderness Ratio (1 mark):**
The ratio of the effective length of a compression member (shore/prop) to its least radius of gyration, denoted by `λ = L_e / r`. It is a measure of the column's tendency to buckle. Higher slenderness ratios indicate greater susceptibility to buckling failure. IS 14687 limits λ to **180** for timber shores.

**(iii) Section Modulus (1 mark):**
A geometric property of a cross-section that indicates its resistance to bending. For a rectangular section of width `b` and depth `d`:

$$
Z = \frac{bd^2}{6} \quad \text{(units: mm³)}
$$

It is used in the bending stress equation: `σ_b = M / Z`. A larger section modulus means lower bending stress for the same moment.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH – INTERNAL CHOICE)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Design the slab formwork for a 175 mm thick RCC slab. Take unit weight of concrete as 24 kN/m³, live load as 1.5 kN/m², and self-weight of formwork as 0.4 kN/m². The sheathing is 25 mm thick planed timber, joists are 50 mm × 100 mm, and stringers are 75 mm × 150 mm. Allowable bending stress = 10 N/mm², E = 12,000 N/mm², and permissible deflection = L/360 for sheathing. Determine: (a) Spacing of joists, and (b) Spacing of stringers and the load per shore. (14 marks)**

### Model Solution (Question A):

#### Part (a): Spacing of Joists (7 marks)

**Step 1: Total Design Load (1 mark)**

$$
w_{concrete} = 24 \times 0.175 = 4.20 \text{ kN/m²}
$$

$$
w_{total} = 4.20 + 0.40 + 1.50 = 6.10 \text{ kN/m²}
$$

**Step 2: Section Properties of Sheathing (1 mark)**

For 25 mm thick × 1000 mm wide strip:

$$
Z = \frac{1000 \times 25^2}{6} = 1,04,167 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
I = \frac{1000 \times 25^3}{12} = 13,02,083 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 3: Bending Check (2 marks)**

Load per meter of sheathing `w = 6.10 kN/m`. Span `L = s` m (assumed simply supported).

$$
M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{6.10 s^2}{8} = 0.7625 s^2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{0.7625 s^2 \times 10^6}{1,04,167} = 7.32 s^2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \leq 10
$$

$$
s^2 \leq 1.366 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \leq 1.169 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Deflection Check (2 marks)**

$$
\delta = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI} = \frac{5 \times 6.10 \times (1000s)^4}{384 \times 12000 \times 13,02,083} = 5.085 s^4 \text{ mm}
$$

Deflection limit: `L/360 = 1000s/360 = 2.778s` mm

$$
5.085 s^4 \leq 2.778 s \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^3 \leq 0.546 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \leq 0.819 \text{ m}
$$

**Adopt: Joist spacing s = 0.75 m (Governed by deflection) (1 mark)**

#### Part (b): Stringer Spacing and Shore Load (7 marks)

**Step 1: Load on Joist (1 mark)**

$$
w_{joist} = 6.10 \times 0.75 = 4.575 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2: Section Properties of Joist (1 mark)**

For 50 mm × 100 mm (b = 50, d = 100, stronger axis):

$$
Z = \frac{50 \times 100^2}{6} = 83,333 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
I = \frac{50 \times 100^3}{12} = 4,166,667 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 3: Bending Check for Joist (continuous over stringers) (2 marks)**

Span of joist `L = S` m. Using `M = wL²/10` (continuous, equal spans):

$$
M = \frac{4.575 \times S^2}{10} = 0.4575 S^2 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{0.4575 S^2 \times 10^6}{83,333} = 5.49 S^2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \leq 10
$$

$$
S^2 \leq 1.821 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S \leq 1.349 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Deflection Check for Joist (2 marks)**

$$
\delta = \frac{5 \times 4.575 \times (1000S)^4}{384 \times 12000 \times 4,166,667} = 1.192 S^4 \text{ mm}
$$

Limit: `L/270 = 1000S/270 = 3.704S` mm

$$
1.192 S^4 \leq 3.704 S \quad \Rightarrow \quad S^3 \leq 3.107 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S \leq 1.458 \text{ m}
$$

**Adopt: Stringer spacing S = 1.20 m (Governed by bending) (1 mark)**

**Step 5: Axial Load per Shore (Bonus 1 mark included in 7 marks)**

$$
P_{shore} = 6.10 \times 0.75 \times 1.20 = 5.49 \text{ kN}
$$

**Use 9 kN capacity adjustable steel shore.**

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a) Explain the lateral pressure of fresh concrete on wall formwork as per ACI 347. Compute the maximum lateral pressure for a wall 4.5 m high with concrete poured at a rate of 1.5 m/hr at a temperature of 25°C. State all assumptions. (7 marks)**

**(b) A timber shore of cross-section 100 mm × 100 mm and length 3.0 m is used to support a slab formwork. Both ends are pinned. The grade of timber has E = 12,000 N/mm² and allowable compressive stress = 10 N/mm². Determine the safe axial load on the shore using Euler's formula with a factor of safety of 3.0. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution (Question B):

#### Part (a): Lateral Pressure as per ACI 347 (7 marks)

**Step 1: Theory (3 marks)**

The lateral pressure of fresh concrete on vertical formwork is influenced by:
- **Rate of placement (R):** Higher rate → Higher pressure
- **Concrete temperature (T):** Higher T → Slower setting → Higher pressure
- **Concrete consistency (slump):** Higher slump → Higher pressure
- **Method of vibration:** Internal vibration increases pressure
- **Cement type and admixtures**

The ACI 347 empirical formula (intended for walls) is:

$$
P_{max} = 7.2 + \frac{785R}{T + 17.8} \quad \text{(kN/m², capped at 144 kN/m²)}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Concrete is placed in a single continuous pour
2. Internal vibration is used
3. Formwork is rigid and well-braced
4. Concrete temperature is constant throughout placement
5. Concrete is normal-weight (ρ ≈ 2400 kg/m³)

**Step 2: Calculation (3 marks)**

Given: R = 1.5 m/hr, T = 25°C

$$
P_{max} = 7.2 + \frac{785 \times 1.5}{25 + 17.8} = 7.2 + \frac{1177.5}{42.8} = 7.2 + 27.51 = 34.71 \text{ kN/m²}
$$

**Step 3: Validation (1 mark)**

Check cap: 34.71 < 144 kN/m² ✓ (use computed value)

**Final Answer: P_max = 34.71 kN/m²**

---

#### Part (b): Safe Axial Load on Timber Shore (7 marks)

**Step 1: Section Properties (1 mark)**

For 100 mm × 100 mm square section:

$$
A = 100 \times 100 = 10,000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
I = \frac{100^4}{12} = 8,333,333 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 2: Radius of Gyration (1 mark)**

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{8,333,333}{10,000}} = \sqrt{833.33} = 28.87 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Slenderness Ratio (1 mark)**

For pin-pin ends, k = 1.0:

$$
L_e = 1.0 \times 3000 = 3000 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{3000}{28.87} = 103.9
$$

This is less than 180 (IS limit for timber), so Euler is applicable.

**Step 4: Euler Buckling Load (2 marks)**

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 12,000 \times 8,333,333}{3000^2}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 12,000 \times 8,333,333}{9,000,000} = \frac{9.8696 \times 10^{11}}{9 \times 10^6} = 1.097 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 109.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Safe Load with FoS = 3.0 (1 mark)**

$$
P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{FoS} = \frac{109.7}{3.0} = 36.57 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: Cross-check with Direct Compression (1 mark)**

$$
P_{comp} = \sigma_{c,allow} \times A = 10 \times 10,000 = 100,000 \text{ N} = 100 \text{ kN}
$$

The safe load is the **minimum** of Euler safe load and direct compressive load:

$$
P_{safe} = \min(36.57, 100) = 36.57 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer: P_safe = 36.57 kN (Governed by buckling)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Formwork Analysis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using 25 kN/m³ instead of 24 kN/m³ for fresh concrete:** Fresh concrete density is **24 kN/m³**, not 25 kN/m³. This 4% error propagates through all subsequent calculations. (Loses 1-2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the deflection check on sheathing:** Many students check only bending and shear, but deflection of thin sheathing (25 mm plywood) is the **most common governing criterion** in KTU problems. (Loses 2-3 marks)
>
> 3. **Confusing L/360 and L/270 limits:** Use **L/360 for sheathing** and **L/270 for joists/stringers**. Swapping these is a frequent mistake. (Loses 1 mark)
>
> 4. **Using M = wL²/8 for continuous joists:** Joists spanning over multiple stringers are continuous, not simply supported. The correct formula is **M = wL²/10** for equal continuous spans. Using wL²/8 overestimates the moment by 25%, leading to uneconomical design. (Loses 1-2 marks)
>
> 5. **Neglecting self-weight of formwork:** Forgetting the **0.4 to 0.6 kN/m²** formwork dead load leads to under-designed shores. (Loses 1 mark)
>
> 6. **Wrong slenderness ratio for shore end conditions:** Most KTU problems assume **pin-pin (k=1.0)** unless stated otherwise. Using k=0.5 (fixed-fixed) gives unsafe (overestimated) capacity. (Loses 1-2 marks)
>
> 7. **Not applying the 144 kN/m² cap** in ACI 347 lateral pressure formula. (Loses 1 mark)
>
> 8. **Forgetting the 1.5× factor in shear stress** calculation for rectangular sections: `τ = 1.5V/A`, not `V/A`. (Loses 1 mark)

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **1.** Fresh concrete unit weight = **24 kN/m³** (not 25 kN/m³).
>
> ✅ **2.** Slab dead load (per m²) = **24 × t** where t is thickness in meters.
>
> ✅ **3.** Minimum live load on formwork = **1.5 kN/m²** (OSHA/IS 14687), increases to **3.75 kN/m²** for motorized carts.
>
> ✅ **4.** For simply supported beam with UDL: **M = wL²/8, V = wL/2, δ = 5wL⁴/(384EI)**.
>
> ✅ **5.** For continuous beam (equal spans) with UDL: **M = wL²/10** (positive moment) and **M = wL²/8** (negative at support, approximate).
>
> ✅ **6.** **Deflection limits:** Sheathing = **L/360**, Joists/Stringers = **L/270**, Shores (lateral) = **L/200**.
>
> ✅ **7.** Bending stress check: **σ_b = M/Z ≤ σ_b,allow**. For timber, σ_b,allow = **10 to 12 N/mm²**.
>
> ✅ **8.** Shear stress check (rectangular): **τ = 1.5V/A ≤ τ_allow**. For timber, τ_allow = **1.0 to 1.2 N/mm²**.
>
> ✅ **9.** **Section modulus** of rectangular section: **Z = bd²/6**, where d is the depth in the bending direction.
>
> ✅ **10.** **Moment of inertia** of rectangular section: **I = bd³/12**.
>
> ✅ **11.** **ACI 347 lateral pressure formula:** P_max = 7.2 + 785R/(T + 17.8), **capped at 144 kN/m²**.
>
> ✅ **12.** **Hydrostatic lateral pressure** below critical depth h₀: P = ρgh, where h₀ = P_max / (ρg).
>
> ✅ **13.** **Euler buckling load:** P_cr = π²EI / L_e². For pin-pin ends, L_e = L.
>
> ✅ **14.** **Slenderness ratio:** λ = L_e / r, where r = √(I/A). For square section, r = d/√12 = 0.289d.
>
> ✅ **15.** **Safe load on shore = min(P_cr/FoS, σ_c × A)** — always use the smaller value.
>
> ✅ **16.** Standard timber sizes commonly used: Sheathing (25 mm), Joists (50×100, 50×150 mm), Stringers (75×150, 75×200, 100×200 mm), Shores (100×100, 150×150 mm).
>
> ✅ **17.** E for timber = **12,000 N/mm²** (Teak/Sal), **9,000 N/mm²** (softwood like Pine).
>
> ✅ **18.** **Standard formwork load combination:** `1.0 × (DL_concrete + DL_form + LL)` for working stress method; `1.2 × DL + 1.4 × LL` for limit state method.
>
> ✅ **19.** **Formwork failures are responsible for ~25% of all structural construction failures** — always cross-check shore capacity with actual load.
>
> ✅ **20.** **Reusability:** Properly designed timber formwork can be reused **10 to 25 times**; steel formwork can be reused **100 to 200+ times** — this is a frequent theory question in KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formwork for Different Concrete Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formwork for Different Concrete Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Formwork for Different Concrete Structures** refers to the specialized temporary mold systems, bracing, and supporting framework engineered to confine and shape fresh concrete during placement, compaction, and initial hardening, in conformity with the specific geometric, structural, and loading demands of distinct structural elements such as **columns, beams, slabs, walls, foundations, staircases, arches, shells, silos, and chimneys**.

Each structural element exerts unique pressures (lateral hydrostatic, dynamic vibration, eccentric loading) on the formwork; hence, the form design, sheathing material, joist orientation, wale/tie arrangement, and shore spacing must be customized to the structural element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET742 Module 2):**
> Formwork design varies according to the *type of structural element* — vertical (columns, walls), horizontal (slabs, beams), curvilinear (arches, shells, domes), or specialized tall structures (chimneys, silos, cooling towers). The lateral pressure of concrete and the rate of placement are the governing parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of formwork as the **"mold of a baker"**:

- A baker uses different pans to shape cakes — a cylindrical pan for a roll, a ring pan for a doughnut, a rectangular pan for a loaf.
- Similarly, a civil engineer uses **different formwork shapes and support systems** for different concrete elements: cylindrical steel forms for circular columns, box forms for rectangular beams, large panel tables for slabs, and continuous moving forms for silos.

Just as a baker must consider the **heat expansion of the batter** (analogous to the lateral pressure of fresh concrete), the engineer must calculate the **fresh concrete pressure** to design formwork that won't burst, bulge, or collapse.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values (as per IS 14687 / ACI 347):**
> - **Unit weight of fresh concrete ($\gamma_c$):** $\mathbf{24 \text{ kN/m}^3}$ (Normal weight), $25 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (with reinforcement).
> - **Hydrostatic concrete pressure head limit:** $\mathbf{1.5R}$ (where $R$ = rate of placement in m/hr) or $\mathbf{H}$ (form height in m), whichever is **less**.
> - **Standard rate of placement for columns:** $\mathbf{1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \text{ m/hr}$**.
> - **Standard rate of placement for walls:** $\mathbf{2.0 \text{ to } 4.0 \text{ m/hr}$**.
> - **Minimum striking time (form removal):** **Side forms: 12–24 hrs**, **Soffit forms (slabs/beams): 7–28 days** depending on span.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Formwork by Structural Element

The formwork systems are selected based on **structural geometry, dimensional repeatability, project scale, and economy**. The major categories are:

### A. Formwork for Columns

Columns are vertical compression members. Formwork must resist **high hydrostatic pressure at the base** and bending due to fresh concrete impact during placement.

- **Rectangular Columns:** Built using sheathing (plywood) on four sides, held by yokes (clamps) consisting of horizontal wales and through-bolts.
- **Circular Columns:** Use **steel sheet metal forms** (two semicircular halves bolted together) or **fiberglass/paper tubes** (Sonotubes).
- **L-Shaped, T-Shaped Columns:** Custom-built using plywood sheathing with internal corner locks.
- **Yoke Spacing:** Closest at the bottom (max pressure) and wider apart near the top (less pressure).

### B. Formwork for Beams and Beam-Slab Systems

- Beam formwork consists of a **three-sided trough** with a bottom (soffit) plank supported by **joists → stringers → vertical shores (props)**.
- **Beam bottom** must be cambered upward to compensate for deflection.
- The sides of beams are supported by **ledger boards** running longitudinally and held against the sheathing.

### C. Formwork for Slabs

- **Conventional Slab Formwork:** Sheathing → Joists → Stringers → Shores.
- **Table Form / Flying Form:** Large pre-assembled tables hoisted by crane from floor to floor — used in high-rise repetitive floor plans.
- **Slab Props:** Adjustable steel props (e.g., "Acrow props") of 2.0–4.0 m height with screw jacks.

### D. Formwork for Walls

- Wall forms are subjected to **full hydrostatic pressure** since walls are cast full height.
- **Gang Form:** Large crane-handled panels ganged together to form an entire wall section in a single pour.
- **Jump Form:** Used for tall structures; the form "jumps" upward using hydraulic jacks anchored to previously cast concrete.

### E. Formwork for Foundations

- **Spread Footings & Pile Caps:** Open box-like formwork, simple and economical.
- **Combined Footings:** L-shaped or trapezoidal forms, often stepped.

### F. Formwork for Curvilinear Structures (Arches, Domes, Shells)

- Requires **double-curvature formwork** built from straight timber members (spliced) or flexible plywood.
- Survey and setting out must be precise since errors cannot be corrected after concrete hardens.

### G. Formwork for Staircases

- Form built on an inclined plane matching the stair profile.
- Soffit supported by vertical props, with cut stringers and riser boards.

### H. Formwork for Tall Vertical Structures (Silos, Chimneys, Cooling Towers, Lift Cores)

- **Slip Form:** Continuously moving formwork (rises 100–300 mm/hr) supported on embedded yokes and jacks. Concrete is placed in layers of 150–200 mm.
- **Lift Cores (High-rise cores):** Jump forms or self-climbing forms with hydraulic actuation.

## 2.2 Lateral Pressure Principles (Governing Design)

The **lateral pressure** exerted by fresh concrete on vertical forms (columns, walls) is the **most critical load case**.

The maximum lateral pressure is given by two governing formulas (IS 14687 / ACI 347):

$$P_{max} = \min\begin{cases} \gamma_c \cdot H \\ \gamma_c \cdot C_w \cdot \left(0.6 + \frac{4R}{T}\right) \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $P_{max}$ = Maximum lateral pressure (kN/m²)
- $\gamma_c$ = Unit weight of fresh concrete = $\mathbf{24 \text{ kN/m}^3}$
- $H$ = Height of fresh concrete in formwork (m)
- $C_w$ = Coefficient depending on cement type and admixtures (typically $1.0$ to $1.5$)
- $R$ = Rate of placement (m/hr)
- $T$ = Temperature of concrete in formwork (°C)

**Simplified ACI 347 Formula:**

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot \min\left(H,\ 0.6 + \frac{4R}{T}\right)$$

For columns with $H \geq 4.2 \text{ m}$:

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot \left(0.6 + \frac{4R}{T}\right) \quad \text{(capped at } \gamma_c H\text{)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application / Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Max lateral pressure (Hydrostatic) | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot H$ | Concrete pressure cap (kN/m²) |
| 2 | Max lateral pressure (Rate-based) | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot C_w(0.6 + 4R/T)$ | Governing for low heights (kN/m²) |
| 3 | Final governing pressure | $P_{max} = \min(\gamma_c H,\ \gamma_c C_w(0.6 + 4R/T))$ | Design pressure (kN/m²) |
| 4 | Concrete unit weight | $\gamma_c = 24$ to $25$ kN/m³ | Normal weight concrete |
| 5 | Effective pressure head | $h = P_{max} / \gamma_c$ | For bending moment in form (m) |
| 6 | Bending moment (simply supported) | $M = wL^2/8$ | Plywood sheathing design |
| 7 | Shear force | $V = wL/2$ | Plywood/timber design |
| 8 | Deflection limit | $\delta_{max} = L/270$ (plywood) | Serviceability check |
| 9 | Camber for soffit | $C = L/500$ to $L/250$ | Compensate deflection (m) |
| 10 | Shore/Prop load | $P_{shore} = (\text{Load per m}^2) \times (\text{Tributary area})$ | Prop sizing (kN) |
| 11 | Slip form rate of rise | $v = P_{place} / (24 \cdot A_{circ})$ | m/hr (chimney/silo) |
| 12 | Time to reach top pressure | $t = 0.6/R$ (when $T$ taken) | Time of maximum pressure (hr) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used directly in B.Tech projects and KTU Part B numericals. Most questions on formwork for columns and walls will require computing $P_{max}$ using the rate-based formula and then designing sheathing thickness/yoke spacing.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Lateral Pressure on Column Formwork

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A column of size **400 mm × 400 mm × 4.0 m height** is to be cast at a rate of placement $R = 1.8 \text{ m/hr}$. Concrete temperature is $T = 30°C$. Compute the **maximum lateral pressure** on the formwork. Take $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$ and $C_w = 1.0$.

### Step 1: Identify the Two Possible Pressure Values

**Pressure Case A (Hydrostatic Cap):**

$$P_A = \gamma_c \cdot H$$

$$P_A = 25 \times 4.0 = 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Pressure Case B (Rate-Based Formula):**

$$P_B = \gamma_c \cdot C_w \cdot \left(0.6 + \frac{4R}{T}\right)$$

Substituting the values:

$$P_B = 25 \times 1.0 \times \left(0.6 + \frac{4 \times 1.8}{30}\right)$$

$$P_B = 25 \times \left(0.6 + \frac{7.2}{30}\right)$$

$$P_B = 25 \times \left(0.6 + 0.24\right)$$

$$P_B = 25 \times 0.84$$

$$P_B = 21.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 2: Select the Governing Minimum

The actual lateral pressure is the **lesser** of the two values (because concrete stiffens with time, so it cannot exert full hydrostatic pressure unless placed slowly).

$$P_{max} = \min(P_A, P_B) = \min(100,\ 21.0)$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 21.0 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

### Step 3: Effective Pressure Head

$$h_{eff} = \frac{P_{max}}{\gamma_c} = \frac{21.0}{25} = 0.84 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Conclusion

The pressure is **21.0 kN/m²** acting on a **head of 0.84 m** from the base of the column. The yokes (clamps) at the bottom of the column must be spaced closer together than at the top to resist this triangular pressure distribution.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Formwork Type vs. Design Considerations

| Structural Element | Formwork Type | Governing Pressure | Critical Design Parameter | Yoke/Spacing Principle |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Column (Rectangular)** | 4-sided plywood + yokes | $P_{max} = \min(\gamma_c H, \gamma_c C_w (0.6 + 4R/T))$ | Yoke spacing, ties | Closest at base, wider at top |
| **Column (Circular)** | Steel/FRP tubes | Same as rectangular | Hoop stress: $\sigma = PD/2t$ | Continuous (no yoke) |
| **Wall** | Gang form panels | Same as column (full height pour) | Waler/tie spacing | Uniform tie grid |
| **Beam** | 3-sided trough | Vertical load (self-weight) | Prop spacing, camber | Prop spacing governs |
| **Slab** | Sheathing on joists | Vertical load (self-weight) | Shore spacing | $A_{trib} \times q$ |
| **Foundation (Footing)** | Open box | Negligible lateral (low height) | Bracing for wet concrete | Simple diagonal bracing |
| **Arch/Dome** | Curved plywood | Self-weight + temporary | Sag, camber | Centering supports |
| **Silo/Chimney** | Slip form | Continuous, time-dependent | Jack spacing, rate of rise | Uniform rise 100–300 mm/hr |
| **Staircase** | Inclined form | Self-weight, vibration | Soffit support, risers | Vertical props at 1.0–1.2 m |
| **Lift Core (High-rise)** | Jump form (self-climbing) | Wall pressure (one lift at a time) | Climbing rods, hydraulic | Lifts of 2.5–3.5 m |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Beam & Slab Formwork Design

**Problem (KTU 14-mark pattern):**
Design the formwork for a **beam of size 300 mm × 600 mm** (web) and **slab thickness 150 mm**. Beam span = 6.0 m, slab span = 3.0 m (each way). Take $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$ and live load on formwork = 1.5 kN/m². Plywood thickness = 18 mm, allowable bending stress = 6.0 N/mm².

### Step 1: Loads on Formwork

**Self-weight of concrete (Beam + Slab per m² of floor):**

$$q_{slab} = 0.150 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Self-weight of beam (per m² of floor area, considering 3.0 m slab span):**

$$q_{beam} = \frac{0.30 \times (0.60 - 0.15) \times 25}{3.0} = \frac{0.30 \times 0.45 \times 25}{3.0}$$

$$q_{beam} = \frac{3.375}{3.0} = 1.125 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Total dead load on formwork:**

$$q_{DL} = 3.75 + 1.125 = 4.875 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Live load (workers, equipment):**

$$q_{LL} = 1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Total design load:**

$$q = 4.875 + 1.5 = 6.375 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 2: Spacing of Joists (Primary Members)

Assume plywood sheathing 18 mm. Check bending in sheathing for **proposed joist spacing = 0.5 m**:

$$w = q \times 0.5 = 6.375 \times 0.5 = 3.1875 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Bending moment in plywood (treating as simply supported):**

$$M = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{3.1875 \times 0.5^2}{8} = 0.0996 \text{ kN·m} = 99.6 \text{ kN·mm}$$

**Section modulus of 18 mm plywood, 1 m wide:**

$$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \times 18^2}{6} = 54000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Bending stress induced:**

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{99.6 \times 10^3}{54000} = 1.84 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Check:** $1.84 < 6.0$ N/mm² → **SAFE.** Joists may be spaced at 0.5 m.

### Step 3: Spacing of Stringers (Secondary Members)

Assume joist span between stringers = 1.5 m. Load on one joist (per m run of joist):

$$w_{joist} = q \times 0.5 = 3.1875 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Bending moment in joist:**

$$M_{joist} = \frac{3.1875 \times 1.5^2}{8} = 0.897 \text{ kN·m} = 897 \text{ kN·mm}$$

(Joist section e.g. $100 \times 50$ mm timber: $Z = 100 \times 50^2 / 6 = 41667 \text{ mm}^3$)

**Check:** $\sigma = 897000 / 41667 = 21.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ — exceeds for timber. Use **double joist** or closer stringer spacing.

### Step 4: Spacing of Shores/Props (Vertical Members)

Tributary area per prop = $1.5 \text{ m (stringer span)} \times 1.0 \text{ m (prop spacing along stringer)} = 1.5 \text{ m}^2$

$$P_{prop} = q \times A_{trib} = 6.375 \times 1.5 = 9.56 \text{ kN}$$

Standard **Acrow prop** capacity (closed height) ≈ 30–40 kN → **SAFE.**

### Step 5: Final Design Summary

- **Plywood:** 18 mm, supported on joists at **0.5 m c/c**.
- **Joists:** $100 \times 50$ mm timber (doubled if needed), spanning **1.5 m** between stringers.
- **Stringers:** $150 \times 75$ mm timber spanning **1.0 m** between props.
- **Props (Shores):** Steel Acrow props at **1.0 m c/c** along stringer.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between "slip form" and "jump form" used for tall concrete structures.**

**Model Answer:**

| Slip Form | Jump Form |
|:---|:---|
| Continuously moving formwork at 100–300 mm/hr | Discretely moved in "jumps" of 2.5–3.5 m per cycle |
| Concrete placed in 150–200 mm layers | Concrete placed in full lift height per cycle |
| Form supported by hydraulic jacks on yokes | Form supported on brackets anchored to previously cast wall |
| Used for silos, chimneys, cooling towers, bridge piers | Used for lift cores, shear walls, high-rise buildings |
| Operates 24/7 until structure is complete | Operates in cycles with waiting time for hardening |

**(3 marks — 1.5 for definition each, 1 for application difference)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the significance of "camber" in beam formwork? How is it provided?**

**Model Answer:**

Camber is a **slight upward curvature** provided to the soffit (bottom) of beam and slab formwork to **compensate for the deflection** that will occur in the formwork under self-weight and the weight of fresh concrete, as well as the long-term deflection of the concrete member.

**Magnitude of Camber:**
- For beams: $C = L/500$ to $L/250$ (where $L$ = span)
- For slabs: $C = L/360$ to $L/240$

**Provision Method:**
- The soffit planks are cut with a slight upward curve.
- The props are set with their screw jacks at slightly different heights to create the camber profile.
- Camber is also added to account for the deflection of shores/props and ground settlement.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for method)**

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **formwork arrangement for a reinforced concrete column** (rectangular) including the design of lateral pressure. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **400 mm × 600 mm column** of height 4.5 m is being cast at a rate of $R = 2.0$ m/hr. Concrete temperature is $T = 28°C$ and $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Determine the **maximum lateral pressure** and the **design spacing of yokes** at mid-height. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Formwork for Rectangular Column (7 marks):**

1. **Definition & Function:** Temporary mould confining plastic concrete and imparting desired shape; transfers lateral pressure to braces/yokes and to ground via shores.

2. **Components:**
   - **Sheathing** (Plywood 18 mm): Direct contact with concrete; resists lateral pressure.
   - **Yokes (Clamps):** Horizontal members wrapping the column, holding sheathing from bulging outward.
   - **Through-bolts (Tie rods):** Connect opposite yokes across the column; resist horizontal pressure.
   - **Bracing (Diagonal):** Provides lateral stability to the entire assembly.

3. **Pressure Distribution:** Concrete pressure is **maximum at the base** (full hydrostatic) and decreases linearly upward. Yokes must be **closely spaced at the base** and progressively **wider at the top**.

4. **Lateral Pressure Formula (ACI 347 / IS 14687):**
   $P_{max} = \min(\gamma_c H, \gamma_c C_w (0.6 + 4R/T))$

5. **Design Spacing of Yokes:** Computed based on bending strength of sheathing between yokes. Allowable bending stress of plywood (12–18 mm) = 5–8 N/mm².

6. **Construction Sequence:** Erect sheathing → install yokes at calculated spacing → tighten through-bolts → place concrete in layers of 300–500 mm → vibrate.

7. **Striking Time:** Side forms removed after 16–24 hours (non-load-bearing).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- Column: 400 × 600 mm, Height $H = 4.5$ m
- Rate: $R = 2.0$ m/hr
- Temperature: $T = 28°C$
- $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $C_w = 1.0$

**Step 2: Hydrostatic Pressure (Case A)**

$$P_A = \gamma_c \cdot H = 25 \times 4.5 = 112.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Rate-Based Pressure (Case B)**

$$P_B = \gamma_c \cdot C_w \cdot \left(0.6 + \frac{4R}{T}\right)$$
