$$P_B = 25 \times 1.0 \times \left(0.6 + \frac{4 \times 2.0}{28}\right)$$

$$P_B = 25 \times \left(0.6 + 0.2857\right) = 25 \times 0.8857 = 22.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Governing Pressure**

$$P_{max} = \min(112.5, 22.14) = 22.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Effective Pressure Head at Mid-Height**

At mid-height of column, the concrete depth above = $2.25$ m. Rate-based pressure governs because $2.25 \text{ m} \times 25 = 56.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$ (hydrostatic) > 22.14 kN/m² (rate-based).

$$P_{mid} = 22.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6: Load on Sheathing at Mid-Height**

Width of column face = 0.6 m (longest side). For a strip of 1 m height:

$$w = P_{mid} \times 1.0 = 22.14 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 7: Maximum Allowable Spacing of Yokes**

For 18 mm plywood (allowable bending stress $f_b = 6.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$), section modulus per 1 m width:

$$Z = \frac{1000 \times 18^2}{6} = 54000 \text{ mm}^3 = 54 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3$$

Maximum bending moment allowed:

$$M_{allow} = f_b \times Z = 6.0 \times 54 \times 10^3 = 324000 \text{ N·mm} = 0.324 \text{ kN·m}$$

Assuming sheathing acts as simply supported between yokes, for $M = wL^2/8$:

$$L = \sqrt{\frac{8 M_{allow}}{w}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 0.324}{22.14}} = \sqrt{0.1171} = 0.342 \text{ m}$$

**Provide yoke spacing at mid-height = 0.30 m (30 cm c/c).**

**Final Answer:** $P_{max} = 22.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$, Yoke spacing at mid-height = 0.30 m c/c.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **slip form technique** of construction with a neat sketch. List its **advantages and disadvantages**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **formwork for circular water tanks** and mention the special precautions required. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Slip Form Technique (7 marks):**

**Working Principle:**
Slip form is a **continuously moving formwork** used to cast tall, uniform cross-section structures (silos, chimneys, cooling towers, lift cores, bridge piers). The form rises at a controlled rate of **100 to 300 mm per hour** while concrete is placed in layers of **150 to 200 mm**.

**Components:**
1. **Form Panels:** Steel-sheathed plywood, typically 1.0–1.2 m high, conforming to wall thickness.
2. **Yokes (H-frames):** Steel frames carrying the form; consist of two vertical legs and a horizontal top member.
3. **Hydraulic Jacks:** Mounted on yokes, lift the form by gripping smooth or deformed jack rods (25–32 mm dia MS rods).
4. **Working Platform:** A suspended platform at top for placing concrete and operating jacks.
5. **Hanging Platform (Suspended Scaffold):** Below the form for finishing and inspection.

**Construction Process:**
- Jack rods are embedded in the foundation.
- Yokes, jacks, and forms are erected initially for a 1.0–1.2 m height lift.
- Concrete is placed in layers; once the bottom layer hardens (0.2–0.3 MPa, ~6–10 hours), the jacks lift the form by the same height of a layer.
- This continues 24/7 until the structure is complete.

**Advantages:**
- Continuous, jointless concrete → no construction joints → superior waterproofing and durability.
- Speed: 3–4 m/day rise possible.
- Economical for very tall structures.
- Reduced labor at height.

**Disadvantages:**
- Capital cost of jacks and yokes is high.
- Skilled supervision essential — any stoppage causes cold joint.
- Limited to uniform cross-section structures.
- Sensitive to weather (rain, extreme heat/cold).
- Difficult to place heavy reinforcement.

**(b) Formwork for Circular Water Tanks (7 marks):**

**Special Features:**
Circular tanks are subjected to **hydrostatic water pressure from inside** (after construction) but during construction, the formwork must resist:
- Lateral pressure of fresh concrete.
- Tension in circumferential (hoop) direction.

**Formwork Types:**
1. **Steel Forms:** Preferred for economy in repetitive tank construction. Made of curved steel panels bolted together.
2. **Plywood Forms:** Curved plywood supported on **horizontal curved ribs (whales)** and **vertical stiffeners**. Curves generated by kerfing or laminating.
3. **Brick/Block Mould:** For small tanks, internal brick lining acts as permanent form.

**Design Considerations:**
- **Hoop Stress in Forms:** $\sigma_h = PD/2t$ (where $D$ = diameter, $P$ = lateral pressure, $t$ = form thickness).
- **Lateral Pressure:** Treated as **wall form** (full hydrostatic, since pour height = wall height).
- **Tie Rods:** Used to resist bursting pressure; arranged circumferentially.
- **Bracing:** External ring beams at intervals to maintain circular shape.

**Special Precautions:**
1. **Staging & Centering:** Top of wall must be perfectly level; use a center pole with rotating arm for accurate radius setting.
2. **Waterproofing Joints:** Minimize construction joints by continuous pour.
3. **Concrete Placement:** Avoid segregation; use tremie pipe for deeper sections.
4. **Curing:** Minimum 14 days wet curing for water tightness.
5. **Dome Roof Formwork:** Requires **centering with radial timber members** and circumferential lagging.
6. **Removal:** Tank formwork removed only after **7–14 days** to allow concrete to gain strength.
7. **Leak Testing:** Hydrostatic test of tank *before* backfilling.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams (PECET742 Module 2):**
> 
> **Pitfall 1 (Lateral Pressure Numerical):** Students often use **only the hydrostatic formula** $\gamma_c H$ and skip the rate-based formula. **Examiner will deduct 3–4 marks** for not computing both cases and selecting the **minimum**. Always show both.
>
> **Pitfall 2 (Yoke Spacing):** Students forget that the **pressure at the base** is highest. They specify **uniform spacing** throughout the column height. Examiner expects a **graduated spacing** (e.g., 0.30 m at base, 0.50 m at top).
>
> **Pitfall 3 (Slip Form vs. Jump Form):** Many students confuse the two. Remember: **Slip form = continuous, no joints, hydraulic jacks; Jump form = discrete lifts, 24-hr cycle, for high-rise walls/cores.**
>
> **Pitfall 4 (Camber):** Students write "camber = L/360" for *all* spans. KTU expects **L/500 to L/250** for beams and **L/360 to L/240** for slabs — show the range.
>
> **Pitfall 5 (No Diagram):** In slip form and beam-slab questions, **sketch carries 2–3 marks**. A neat labeled diagram (even simple) is mandatory.
>
> **Pitfall 6 (Unit Errors):** $P_{max}$ in **kN/m²**, NOT N/mm². 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ N/m² = 1000 kN/m². **Convert carefully.**
>
> **Pitfall 7 (Striking Time):** Side forms ≠ Soffit forms. Side forms: 12–24 hrs. Soffit props for slabs: **7 days** (span < 4.5 m), **14 days** (4.5–6 m), **21 days** (> 6 m).

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Formwork for Different Concrete Structures:**
>
> ☐ **1. Columns:** Plywood sheathing + yokes (horizontal clamps) + through-bolts. Pressure max at base, so yokes closely spaced at base. L/270 deflection check for plywood.
>
> ☐ **2. Walls:** Gang form (crane-handled panels) for one-pour walls. Jump form (cyclic 2.5–3.5 m lifts) for high-rise walls. Uniform tie grid. Full hydrostatic pressure if poured in one lift.
>
> ☐ **3. Beams:** 3-sided trough. Soffit (bottom) carries vertical load; sides carry lateral pressure. Camber of **L/500 to L/250** in soffit. Props at **1.0 m c/c**.
>
> ☐ **4. Slabs:** Sheathing → Joists (0.5 m c/c) → Stringers (1.0 m c/c) → Props (1.0 m c/c). Table/Flying form for repetitive floors. Plywood 18 mm.
>
> ☐ **5. Foundations:** Open box formwork. Low lateral pressure (low pour height). Bracing critical because formwork is open and concrete flows easily.
>
> ☐ **6. Arches & Domes:** Double-curvature formwork, requires precise surveying. Centering from below using timber ribs and lagging.
>
> ☐ **7. Staircases:** Inclined soffit form, riser boards, side forms. Props at 1.0–1.2 m c/c.
>
> ☐ **8. Silos & Chimneys:** **Slip form** — continuous movement, 100–300 mm/hr, 24×7 operation, hydraulic jacks on yokes, jack rods embedded in concrete.
>
> ☐ **9. Lift Cores (High-Rise):** **Jump form** (self-climbing) for repetitive floor cores. Each cycle: cast 2.5–3.5 m → harden 12–24 hr → climb → repeat.
>
> ☐ **10. Circular Water Tanks:** Steel curved forms or plywood over curved ribs. Hoop stress: $\sigma = PD/2t$. Continuous pour preferred. Test waterproofing before backfilling.
>
> ☐ **11. Master Formula:** $P_{max} = \min(\gamma_c H, \gamma_c C_w(0.6 + 4R/T))$ — **memorize this.**
>
> ☐ **12. Striking Time:** Side forms 12–24 hr; Soffit forms: 7/14/21 days based on span (< 4.5 m / 4.5–6 m / > 6 m).
>
> ☐ **13. Camber Rule:** Beams L/500 to L/250; Slabs L/360 to L/240.
>
> ☐ **14. Deflection Limit:** Plywood sheathing $\delta \leq L/270$.
>
> ☐ **15. Slip Form Layer Thickness:** 150–200 mm per layer; rate of rise = 100–300 mm/hr.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundations, Walls, Columns, Beams, and Slabs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Foundations, Walls, Columns, Beams, and Slabs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Formwork for Structural Elements

**Formwork** (also called *shuttering* or *moulds*) is a temporary structure used to support fresh concrete until it gains sufficient strength to be self-supporting. In the context of KTU Module 2, we focus on the design philosophy, load transfer mechanism, and construction methodology of formwork for the **five primary structural elements** of any RCC building: Foundations, Walls, Columns, Beams, and Slabs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Formwork is a *temporary construction* whose purpose is to mould fresh concrete to the desired shape and to support it until it becomes structurally self-sufficient. As per IS 14687:1999, the design life of formwork is typically short (a few days to weeks), but its structural integrity is non-negotiable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete Bakery" Intuition

Imagine baking a cake:
- The **cake batter** = Wet concrete (fluid, needs a container)
- The **baking tray/mould** = Formwork
- The **oven heat** = Cement hydration (gives strength)
- The **cooling time** = Curing period
- The **cake holds its shape** = Concrete becomes self-supporting

Just as you cannot pour cake batter on a table and expect a shaped cake, you cannot pour concrete without formwork. For different "cake shapes" (foundations, walls, columns, beams, slabs), you need **different moulds with different strengths and supports** — this is the essence of Module 2.

## 1.3 Why This Module Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Weightage:** This topic is consistently tested in Part A (3 marks) for definitions and diagrams, and Part B (14 marks) for design calculations, especially in columns and beams where lateral pressure of fresh concrete governs the design.

## 1.4 Classification of Formwork by Structural Element

| Structural Element | Primary Formwork Type | Key Design Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Foundation (Footing)** | Open trough / Stepped form | Earth pressure + uplift buoyancy |
| **Walls (Shear/RCC)** | Vertical panels with walers | Lateral pressure of fresh concrete |
| **Columns** | Box-type (4 panels) | Lateral pressure + bulking effect |
| **Beams** | Soffit + two sides | Bottom shuttering + side pressure |
| **Slabs** | Soffit with propping | Dead load of wet concrete + live load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric References (As per KTU 2024):**
> - Density of fresh concrete: **25 kN/m³**
> - Density of reinforced concrete: **25 kN/m³**
> - Unit weight of formwork timber (sal wood): **8 kN/m³**
> - Standard lateral pressure coefficient: **P = γ × h** (hydrostatic for low rate of pour)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Design Philosophy (Common to All Elements)

Every formwork design — whether for a footing or a slab — follows **four cardinal steps**:

1. **Determine the loads** acting on the formwork (dead, live, lateral, environmental).
2. **Compute the maximum bending moment and shear** in the sheathing, joists, and supports.
3. **Check the stresses** in plywood, timber, or steel members against permissible limits.
4. **Ensure deflection** does not exceed **L/270** for visible surfaces and **L/180** for hidden surfaces (as per IS 14687).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these four steps?** Because KTU examiners reward students who *systematically* present their solution. Skipping the "load calculation" step costs 2–3 marks instantly.

## 2.2 Loads Acting on Formwork (KTU Mandatory Theory)

### 2.2.1 Vertical Loads (Foundations, Columns, Beams, Slabs)

- **Dead Load of Wet Concrete (W_c):** The self-weight of the freshly placed concrete.

$$W_c = \gamma_c \times V = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3 \times V$$

where $V$ is the volume of concrete in the formwork zone.

- **Dead Load of Formwork (W_f):** Self-weight of the shuttering, props, and bearers.

$$W_f = \text{Unit weight of material} \times \text{Volume of formwork}$$

- **Live Load (W_L):** Weight of workmen, equipment, impact from concrete pouring, and heaping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Value for Live Load on Formwork = 1.5 to 2.0 kN/m²** (for slabs) and **1.0 kN/m²** (for columns/walls per IS 14687).

### 2.2.2 Lateral Pressure (Walls and Columns)

Fresh concrete behaves like a **dense fluid** for a short duration, exerting hydrostatic pressure on the vertical formwork faces. However, because concrete begins to stiffen, the pressure does **not** increase indefinitely with depth. This is governed by **CIRIA / Peurifoy / IS 14687** formulas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Peurifoy's Formula (Most commonly used in KTU):**
> 
> $$P = \gamma_c \cdot R \quad \text{(for } R < 2.0 \text{ m/hr)}$$
> 
> $$P = \gamma_c \cdot \left(1.4 + \frac{4.2}{R}\right) \quad \text{(for } 2.0 \leq R \leq 3.0 \text{ m/hr)}$$
> 
> $$P = \gamma_c \cdot \left(1.4 + \frac{4.2}{R} + 0.04R\right) \quad \text{(for } R > 3.0 \text{ m/hr)}$$
> 
> where $R$ is the rate of pour in m/hr, and $P$ is the maximum lateral pressure in kN/m².

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \gamma_c \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic lateral pressure (foundations) | kN/m² |
| 2 | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot R$ | Peurifoy (low pour rate) | kN/m² |
| 3 | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \left(1.4 + \frac{4.2}{R}\right)$ | Peurifoy (medium pour) | kN/m² |
| 4 | $W_c = 25 \cdot t$ | Slab self-weight for thickness $t$ | kN/m² |
| 5 | $M = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$ | Bending moment (simply supported) | kN·m |
| 6 | $M = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{10}$ | Bending moment (continuous) | kN·m |
| 7 | $V = \frac{w \cdot L}{2}$ | Shear force (simply supported) | kN |
| 8 | $S_b = \frac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress check | N/mm² |
| 9 | $\delta = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | Deflection of sheathing | mm |
| 10 | $F_{prop} = \frac{W_{total}}{N_{props}}$ | Load per prop | kN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permissible Stresses (Timber - Sal Wood):**
> - Bending: **11.7 N/mm²** (or 12 N/mm² for quick reference)
> - Shear: **1.2 N/mm²**
> - Modulus of Elasticity: **12,000 N/mm²**

## 2.4 Formwork for Each Element — Design Logic

### 2.4.1 FOUNDATIONS (Isolated / Combined / Raft)

- **Formwork Type:** Side shutters only (since the bottom rests on PCC/blinding concrete).
- **Loading:** Lateral earth pressure + slight hydrostatic pressure from wet concrete.
- **Key Concern:** **Dewatering** (if water table is high) and **buoyancy check**.
- **Material:** Timber planks (25–40 mm thick) braced with struts.

### 2.4.2 WALLS

- **Formwork Type:** Two parallel vertical panels (sheathing) connected by **wall ties** through PVC sleeves.
- **Loading:** **Lateral pressure** of fresh concrete (dominant load).
- **Components:** Sheathing → Studs → Walers → Strongbacks → Tie system.
- **Design Check:** Bending of sheathing between studs, and tensile strength of wall ties.

### 2.4.3 COLUMNS

- **Formwork Type:** Box of 4 panels clamped by **column clamps (yokes)**.
- **Loading:** Maximum lateral pressure (full hydrostatic until stiffening).
- **Special Concern:** **Rate of pour (R)** is critical because columns are deep and narrow.
- **Clamp Spacing:** Designed to resist the lateral bursting pressure.

### 2.4.4 BEAMS

- **Formwork Type:** Soffit (bottom) + two side shutters.
- **Loading:** Bottom soffit carries full vertical load; sides carry only initial hydrostatic head.
- **Components:** Soffit plate → Joists → Bearer → Props (or supports from slab below).

### 2.4.5 SLABS

- **Formwork Type:** Plywood sheathing on joists on bearers on props.
- **Loading:** Dead load of slab + live load of workers/equipment.
- **Critical Step:** **Prop spacing and slab thickness** dictate the design of the entire system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In high-rise construction in Kerala, **Mivan formwork** (aluminium) is widely used for walls and slabs due to its rapid cycle time. The design logic in this module directly applies to conventional timber and modern aluminium formwork systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Lateral Pressure on Column Formwork (Peurifoy's Method)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A column of size **300 mm × 450 mm** is being filled at a rate of **R = 1.5 m/hr**. Determine the maximum lateral pressure on the formwork using Peurifoy's formula. Take $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the formula based on rate of pour.**

Since $R = 1.5 \text{ m/hr} < 2.0 \text{ m/hr}$, we use the **first case** of Peurifoy's formula:

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot R$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$P_{max} = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3 \times 1.5 \text{ m/hr}$$

**Step 3: Compute the result.**

$$P_{max} = 37.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Convert to standard units.**

$$P_{max} = 37.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.0375 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Hydrostatic check (Upper bound limit).**

The hydrostatic pressure at full column height (assume $h = 3 \text{ m}$) is:

$$P_{hydro} = \gamma_c \cdot h = 25 \times 3 = 75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Since $P_{max} = 37.5 < 75 \text{ kN/m}^2$, the **Peurifoy value governs**. This confirms that concrete stiffening reduces the actual pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The reason Peurifoy's formula gives lower pressure than hydrostatic is because the inner concrete column begins to **set from the bottom upward**. The outer layers near the formwork stiffen first, reducing the effective fluid head on the shutters.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Design of Slab Formwork Sheathing

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Design the **plywood sheathing** for a 150 mm thick RCC slab. The joists are spaced at **300 mm centre-to-centre**. Take the permissible bending stress in plywood as **5.0 N/mm²** (for moisture-resistant MR grade).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the total vertical load on the sheathing.**

$$W_{slab} = \gamma_c \times t = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3 \times 0.150 \text{ m} = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$W_{live} = 1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (IS 14687 standard)}$$

$$W_{total} = 3.75 + 1.5 = 5.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the line load on a 1 m wide strip of sheathing.**

The sheathing acts as a continuous beam over joists. For a 1 m wide strip:

$$w = 5.25 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the maximum bending moment.**

For a continuous beam (multiple spans), the KTU standard formula is:

$$M = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{10}$$

where $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$. Substituting:

$$M = \frac{5.25 \times (0.3)^2}{10} = \frac{5.25 \times 0.09}{10} = 0.04725 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Convert to N·mm units.**

$$M = 0.04725 \times 10^6 = 47,250 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5: Required section modulus.**

Rearranging the bending stress equation:

$$S_b = \frac{M}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_{req} = \frac{M}{S_b} = \frac{47,250}{5.0} = 9,450 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 6: Select plywood thickness.**

For 12 mm thick plywood (1000 mm wide), section modulus is:

$$Z_{prov} = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \times (12)^2}{6} = 24,000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 7: Verification.**

Since $Z_{prov} = 24,000 \text{ mm}^3 > Z_{req} = 9,450 \text{ mm}^3$, the 12 mm plywood is **safe**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deflection Check (Often missed in KTU exams!):**
> 
> $$\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{270} = \frac{300}{270} = 1.11 \text{ mm}$$
> 
> The actual deflection of 12 mm plywood under 5.25 kN/m² load is well within limits for MR grade plywood.

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Prop Load in Beam Formwork

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A beam of size **230 mm × 450 mm** with a clear span of **4 m** is to be supported by props. Determine the load per prop if props are placed at **1 m centre-to-centre** along the beam length.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Self-weight of beam concrete (per metre run).**

$$W_{beam} = 0.230 \times 0.450 \times 25 = 2.5875 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2: Self-weight of slab (tributary width considered for T-beam).**

Assuming slab thickness 150 mm and effective flange width 1.0 m on each side:

$$W_{slab} = 0.150 \times 1.0 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3: Total dead load on beam (per metre).**

$$W_{DL} = 2.5875 + 3.75 = 6.3375 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4: Live load (1.5 kN/m² on slab tributary).**

$$W_{LL} = 1.5 \times 1.0 = 1.5 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 5: Total load per metre.**

$$W_{total} = 6.3375 + 1.5 = 7.8375 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 6: Load per prop (at 1 m spacing).**

$$F_{prop} = 7.8375 \times 1.0 = 7.84 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Standard prop capacity check.**

Standard adjustable steel props (IS 2750) have a safe capacity of **20–30 kN** for typical spans. Hence, **2 props per metre** (or 1 prop at 1 m spacing) is sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip for KTU:** Always add a **factor of safety of 1.5** when selecting prop capacity to account for eccentric loading and lateral instability.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Critical Design Checks

| Structural Element | Governing Load | Critical Member | KTU Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| Foundation | Lateral earth + concrete | Side shutter plank | Bending + Strut capacity |
| Wall | Lateral pressure (Peurifoy) | Wall tie | Tensile strength |
| Column | Lateral pressure (high pour) | Column clamp | Bursting pressure |
| Beam | Vertical (self + slab) | Prop + Soffit | Axial + Bending |
| Slab | Vertical (slab + live) | Plywood sheathing | Bending + Deflection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Formwork System Hierarchy (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Concrete Pour] --> B[Loads Generated]
    B --> C[Vertical Loads: 25 kN per m cubed]
    B --> D[Lateral Loads: Peurifoy Formula]
    C --> E[Slab Formwork System]
    C --> F[Beam Formwork System]
    C --> G[Foundation Formwork System]
    D --> H[Wall Formwork System]
    D --> I[Column Formwork System]
    E --> E1[Plywood Sheathing]
    E --> E2[Joists at 300 mm c/c]
    E --> E3[Bearers]
    E --> E4[Adjustable Props]
    F --> F1[Soffit Plate]
    F --> F2[Side Shutters]
    F --> F3[Beam Bottom Props]
    G --> G1[Side Shutter Planks]
    G --> G2[Struts and Braces]
    H --> H1[Sheathing Panels]
    H --> H2[Vertical Studs]
    H --> H3[Horizontal Walers]
    H --> H4[Wall Ties with Sleeves]
    I --> I1[Four Side Panels]
    I --> I2[Column Clamps or Yokes]
    I --> I3[Corner Brackets]
    E4 --> J[Load Transfer to Floor Below]
    F3 --> J
    G2 --> K[Load Transfer to PCC Bed]
    H4 --> L[Resists Lateral Pressure]
    I2 --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Column Formwork Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ColumnFormwork
        direction TB
        P1[Side Panel A] --> P2[Side Panel B]
        P2 --> P3[Side Panel C]
        P3 --> P4[Side Panel D]
        P4 --> P1
        CL1[Column Clamp Level 1] -.-> P1
        CL1 -.-> P2
        CL2[Column Clamp Level 2] -.-> P2
        CL2 -.-> P3
        CL3[Column Clamp Level 3] -.-> P3
        CL3 -.-> P4
        CL4[Column Clamp Level 4] -.-> P4
        CL4 -.-> P1
    end
    PC[Fresh Concrete Pressure] --> P1
    PC --> P2
    PC --> P3
    PC --> P4
    PC --> CL1
    PC --> CL2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Slab Formwork Load Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slab Concrete 25 kN per m cubed] --> B[Plywood Sheathing]
    B --> C[Joists 300 mm c/c]
    C --> D[Bearers 1 m c/c]
    D --> E[Props 1 m c/c]
    E --> F[Floor Below Slab]
    G[Live Load 1.5 kN per m squared] --> B
    H[Formwork Self Weight] --> B
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Wall Formwork Tie System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Concrete Pressure] --> B[Left Sheathing]
    A --> C[Right Sheathing]
    B --> D[Left Stud]
    C --> E[Right Stud]
    D --> F[Left Waler]
    E --> G[Right Waler]
    F --> H[Wall Tie with PVC Sleeve]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Tensile Force Resists Pressure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams?** KTU examiners reward clear sketches. In a 14-mark question, a well-labeled diagram fetches **2–3 marks** even before the calculation begins. Always include a free-body diagram in your answer sheet.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define formwork. List the four main requirements of a good formwork as per IS 14687.**

**Model Answer:**

**Formwork:** A temporary structure or mould used to support and shape fresh concrete until it hardens sufficiently to be self-supporting.

**Four Main Requirements (IS 14687):**
1. **Strength** — to resist dead and live loads without failure.
2. **Rigidity** — to maintain shape and prevent bulging (deflection ≤ L/270).
3. **Tightness** — to prevent leakage of cement slurry (causes honeycombing).
4. **Smoothness** — to produce a quality finish on exposed concrete surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> Additional requirements include **ease of stripping**, **reusability**, and **economy**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is Peurifoy's formula? State its significance in formwork design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Peurifoy's Formula** is used to calculate the **maximum lateral pressure** exerted by fresh concrete on vertical formwork (walls and columns):

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot R \quad \text{(for R < 2.0 m/hr)}$$

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot \left(1.4 + \frac{4.2}{R}\right) \quad \text{(for 2.0 ≤ R ≤ 3.0 m/hr)}$$

**Significance:**
- It accounts for the **rate of pour (R)**, which is the most critical parameter affecting pressure.
- It is **more realistic** than the hydrostatic assumption $\gamma_c \cdot h$ because concrete begins to stiffen during placement.
- It allows **economical design** of column clamps and wall ties by avoiding over-conservatism.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the different types of formwork used for **foundations**, **walls**, and **columns** with neat sketches.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A column of size **400 mm × 400 mm** has a height of **3.0 m** above the floor. The rate of concrete pour is **1.2 m/hr**. Design the spacing of column clamps if each clamp has a safe working load of **15 kN**. Use Peurifoy's formula. Take $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**1. Foundation Formwork:**
- Used for isolated footings, combined footings, and raft foundations.
- Consists of **side shutters** (timber planks 25–40 mm thick) braced by **struts** and **props**.
- The bottom is open (rests on PCC blinding), so no bottom formwork is needed.
- **Loads:** Lateral earth pressure + slight concrete pressure.

**2. Wall Formwork:**
- Two parallel panels of plywood or timber sheathing.
- Supported by **vertical studs** and **horizontal walers**.
- Held together by **wall ties** (with PVC sleeves for easy removal).
- **Loads:** Lateral pressure (dominant) computed via Peurifoy's formula.

**3. Column Formwork:**
- **Box-type** formwork made of 4 panels (one for each face).
- Joined at corners using **column clamps (yokes)** or **bolt-and-nut systems**.
- **Clamps** provide the external reaction to resist the bursting lateral pressure.
- **Loads:** Maximum lateral pressure (full hydrostatic until stiffening).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Maximum Lateral Pressure using Peurifoy's formula.**

Since $R = 1.2 \text{ m/hr} < 2.0 \text{ m/hr}$:

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot R = 25 \times 1.2 = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Hydrostatic check.**

$$P_{hydro} = 25 \times 3.0 = 75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Since $P_{max} = 30 < 75 \text{ kN/m}^2$, Peurifoy value **governs**.

**Step 3: Total lateral force on column formwork.**

Force per metre height:

$$F = P_{max} \times \text{width} = 30 \times 0.4 = 12 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4: Total force over 3 m height.**

$$F_{total} = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Number of clamps required.**

$$N = \frac{F_{total}}{Safe Load} = \frac{36}{15} = 2.4 \approx 3 \text{ clamps}$$

**Step 6: Clamp spacing.**

$$S = \frac{H}{N} = \frac{3.0}{3} = 1.0 \text{ m c/c}$$

**Step 7: Final answer.**

**Provide 3 column clamps at 1.0 m centre-to-centre spacing.**

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **components of slab and beam formwork** with a labeled diagram. List the loads acting on slab formwork.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design the **plywood sheathing** for a **150 mm thick slab** with joists spaced at **400 mm c/c**. Permissible bending stress in plywood is **5 N/mm²**. Take live load = **1.5 kN/m²** and $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Components of Slab Formwork (top to bottom):**
1. **Plywood sheathing** (12 mm thick) — direct contact with concrete.
2. **Joists** (timber battens, 50×100 mm) — support sheathing at 300–400 mm c/c.
3. **Bearers** (timber planks, 75×100 mm) — support joists at 1 m c/c.
4. **Props/Acrow spans** — vertical adjustable steel members to bearers.

**Components of Beam Formwork:**
1. **Soffit plate** (bottom shutter).
2. **Side shutters** (two vertical panels).
3. **Beam bottom props** (heavy-duty).

**Loads on Slab Formwork:**
- Dead load of wet concrete: $25 \times 0.150 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- Dead load of formwork (assumed): $0.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- Live load (workers + equipment): $1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- **Total:** $5.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Total load on slab (per m²).**

$$W = 25 \times 0.150 + 0.5 + 1.5 = 5.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Line load on 1 m wide strip of sheathing.**

$$w = 5.75 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3: Maximum bending moment (continuous beam, L = 0.4 m).**

$$M = \frac{w L^2}{10} = \frac{5.75 \times (0.4)^2}{10} = \frac{5.75 \times 0.16}{10} = 0.092 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Convert to N·mm.**

$$M = 0.092 \times 10^6 = 92,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5: Required section modulus.**

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_b} = \frac{92,000}{5} = 18,400 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 6: Section modulus of 12 mm plywood (1000 mm wide).**

$$Z_{prov} = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \times 12^2}{6} = 24,000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 7: Verification.**

Since $Z_{prov} = 24,000 > Z_{req} = 18,400 \text{ mm}^3$, **12 mm plywood is safe.**

**Step 8: Final answer.**

**Use 12 mm thick MR-grade plywood sheathing with joists at 400 mm c/c.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the live load (1.5 kN/m²)** — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $wL^2/8$ instead of $wL^2/10$** for continuous spans — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Not converting kN·m to N·mm** before plugging into stress formulas — costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the deflection check** — costs 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not drawing a labeled diagram** for "explain the components" type questions — costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

### A. Definitions & Concepts
- ☐ Formwork = temporary mould for fresh concrete.
- ☐ Shuttering = another term for formwork (used interchangeably in India).
- ☐ Centering = formwork specifically for arches/curved members.
- ☐ Staging = the prop-and-bearer system supporting soffit formwork.

### B. Critical Numerical Values to Memorize
- ☐ Unit weight of concrete = **25 kN/m³** (or 24 kN/m³ as per older IS).
- ☐ Live load on formwork = **1.5–2.0 kN/m²**.
- ☐ Permissible bending stress in sal wood = **11.7 N/mm²**.
- ☐ Permissible bending stress in plywood (MR grade) = **5.0 N/mm²**.
- ☐ Deflection limit = **L/270** (visible) or **L/180** (hidden).

### C. Peurifoy's Formula (Most Important)
- ☐ $P = \gamma_c R$ (for $R < 2$ m/hr).
- ☐ $P = \gamma_c (1.4 + 4.2/R)$ (for $2 \leq R \leq 3$ m/hr).
- ☐ $P = \gamma_c (1.4 + 4.2/R + 0.04R)$ (for $R > 3$ m/hr).

### D. Element-Wise Key Points
- ☐ **Foundation:** Side shutters only; check buoyancy and earth pressure.
- ☐ **Walls:** Wall ties + walers + studs; Peurifoy formula governs.
- ☐ **Columns:** Box-type with column clamps; clamp spacing controls design.
- ☐ **Beams:** Soffit + sides; prop load is critical.
- ☐ **Slabs:** Plywood + joists + bearers + props; deflection check is mandatory.

### E. Common Examiner Traps
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** clearly (e.g., "assuming continuous span").
- ☐ Always **convert units consistently** (kN·m → N·mm).
- ☐ Always **draw a free-body diagram** for design questions.
- ☐ Always mention **IS code reference** (IS 14687, IS 2750) to score full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** For the 14-mark Part B question, follow the exact KTU valuation pattern — *Assumptions → Loads → Bending Moment → Section Check → Deflection Check → Final Selection*. This structure alone can fetch you **3–4 extra marks** in the KTU University Examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Considerations for High-Rise Buildings and Bridges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Special Considerations for High-Rise Buildings and Bridges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Special Formwork Considerations

**Special Considerations for High-Rise Buildings and Bridges** in formwork engineering refer to the unique design, structural, logistical, and safety provisions required when the construction of tall vertical structures (high-rise buildings, chimneys, silos) or long-span/elevated horizontal structures (bridges, viaducts) deviates significantly from conventional low-rise formwork practice. These considerations address the influence of **concrete pressure**, **wind loading**, **vertical/horizontal transportation**, **cycle time**, **structural stability**, and **specialized moving systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET742 – Module 2):**
> Special formwork for high-rise construction and bridges involves systems that must resist higher concrete pressures due to increased rates of placement, sustain lateral wind loads on tall formwork faces, support self-weight and working loads at considerable heights, and incorporate mechanized climbing/moving devices to maintain construction efficiency and safety.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are stacking **lego blocks** to build a tall tower. For the first few layers, you can simply hold the structure with your hands. But as the tower grows taller, you need a **scaffolding platform that climbs up with you** (just like a mountaineer). Also, the wind blows harder the higher you go, so your platform must be **anchored firmly** to the tower itself rather than resting on the ground.

Similarly, in high-rise formwork:
- The formwork cannot rest on the ground at great heights (it sags and destabilizes).
- It must **hang from or climb along** the previously cast concrete.
- The **concrete pouring rate is faster** (using pumps), so the formwork must resist higher lateral pressures.
- **Wind forces** become dominant design criteria.

For bridges, imagine building a **road across a deep valley**. You cannot put scaffolding from the ground. Instead, you use a **cantilever platform that extends outward** from the pier, gradually building the deck segment by segment — much like a **tightrope walker extending a pole outward** for balance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:**
> High-rise buildings are conventionally defined as structures exceeding **75 m (≈ 246 ft)** in height (per CTBUH – Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat). Beyond this height, formwork design shifts from gravity-dominated to lateral-load-dominated behavior.

---

## 1.3 Why Conventional Formwork Fails at Height/Span

| Limitation in Conventional Formwork | Reason it Fails at High-Rise / Bridge Scale |
|---|---|
| **Ground-supported shores** | Excessive self-weight induces elastic shortening and settlement at the top |
| **Slow pour rates** | Modern high-rise uses pumps → 3 to 6 m/hr → extreme lateral pressure |
| **Manual striking/refixing** | Cycle time becomes uneconomical beyond 10 storeys |
| **Static bracing** | Wind pressure on formwork face scales with height² |
| **Timber formwork** | Cannot survive the rigorous cycle of climbing 50+ times |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Special Formwork Systems

### 1.4.1 For High-Rise Buildings
1. **Jump Form (Climbing Form)** – Lifted in discrete jumps by crane.
2. **Slip Form** – Continuously sliding vertical formwork.
3. **Self-Climbing / Automatic Climbing System (ACS / SCF)** – Hydraulically lifted without crane.
4. **Self-Raising Form (Lift Form)** – Lifts on hydraulic jacks, typically for core walls.
5. **Flying Form (Table Form)** – Crane-lifted large floor units (for slabs of repetitive floors).

### 1.4.2 For Bridges
1. **Movable Scaffolding System (MSS)** – Ground/structure supported, spans between piers.
2. **Form Travelers** – Cantilever construction from pier.
3. **Incremental Launching Form (ILF)** – Casting segments behind abutment, then launching.
4. **Balanced Cantilever Formwork** – Segmental construction from pier.
5. **Stay-Cable Formwork** – For cable-stayed bridge pylons and decks.

> [!TIP]
> **Module 2 Focus (KTU):** Emphasis is on design philosophy, pressure considerations, load combinations (wind + fresh concrete), system selection, and cycle-time optimization — not detailed proprietary catalog data.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Special Concrete Pressure Considerations in High-Rise Pumping

When concrete is placed by **boom pump or line pump**, the rate of rise at the formwork face is significantly higher than traditional crane-and-bucket placement. The fresh concrete behaves as a **thixotropic fluid** for a limited time, after which it stiffens and self-supports.

### 2.1.1 ACI 347 Modified Pressure Equation (High-Rise Context)

For walls and columns with **high rates of placement and use of superplasticizers / retarders**, the standard ACI equation is modified. The maximum lateral pressure $P_{max}$ is taken as the lesser of:

$$P_{max} = \min\begin{cases} C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{785\,R}{T + 17.8}\right) \\[6pt] C_w \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H \end{cases}$$

where the variables are defined below.

### 2.1.2 Detailed Explanation of Each Term

- $C_w$ = **Unit weight coefficient** of concrete ($C_w = 1.0$ for normal-weight, $C_w = 0.85$ for lightweight concrete below 1760 kg/m³, and a linear interpolation between 1760–2240 kg/m³).
- $C_c$ = **Chemistry coefficient** ($C_c = 1.0$ for Type I/III cement without retarders; $C_c = 1.2$ for Type I/III cement with retarders; $C_c = 1.4$ for Type II/V cement; $C_c = 1.2$ for Type III cement with retarders). The coefficient accounts for slower stiffening due to chemical admixtures.
- $R$ = **Rate of placement** in m/hr (typically **3 to 6 m/hr** for pumped concrete in high-rise cores).
- $T$ = **Concrete temperature at placement** in °C (typical values 15 °C to 32 °C in Kerala climate).
- $\rho$ = Density of concrete (**2400 to 2500 kg/m³** for normal weight).
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**).
- $H$ = **Height of fresh concrete** in the form (m).
- $7.2$ = Empirical constant with units of kN/m², representing the hydrostatic baseline pressure independent of $R$.
- $785\,R$ = Product expressing pressure increase due to higher rate of rise, with the constant 785 chosen to give kN/m² when $R$ is in m/hr.
- $17.8$ = Empirical constant (°C) inside the denominator that softens the influence of $R$ as $T$ increases.
- $C_w \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ = The **full hydrostatic limit** (when the concrete is fluid long enough to develop full liquid head).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "minimum" condition?**
> If concrete stiffens quickly (low $T + 17.8$ term, or $H$ is small), the first expression governs. If the form is tall and concrete remains fluid, full hydrostatic pressure (second expression) governs. The actual lateral pressure is the **lesser of the two** because once concrete sets, it no longer transmits liquid pressure.

---

## 2.2 Wind Load on Tall Formwork (High-Rise)

Formwork panels on tall structures act as **cladding** during construction (before the permanent structure takes over). Wind exerts lateral force on the exposed formwork face.

### 2.2.1 Wind Pressure Formulation (IS 875 Part 3 / ASCE 7 Compatible)

The design wind pressure $P_z$ at height $z$ is given by:

$$P_z = 0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_z^{\,2} \cdot C_d \cdot K_a$$

where each parameter is explained below.

- $\rho_a$ = **Air density** (1.22 kg/m³ at sea level, 15 °C).
- $V_z$ = **Design wind speed at height $z$** in m/s (basic wind speed $V_b$ modified by terrain, topography, and height factors).
- $C_d$ = **Drag / pressure coefficient** for formwork face (typically 1.2 to 1.4 for flat panels, up to 1.7 with sharp bracing projections).
- $K_a$ = **Area reduction factor** (for large tributary areas, statistical wind gust averaging reduces peak pressure; for formwork usually taken as 1.0 to be conservative).
- $0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_z^{\,2}$ = The **dynamic pressure** of the wind in Pa.

### 2.2.2 Height Variation of Wind Speed (Power Law)

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot \left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)^{1/n}$$

- $V_b$ = Basic wind speed (map value, **39 m/s for Kerala coastal zone, 33 m/s for inland as per IS 875 Part 3 Zone mapping**).
- $k_1$ = Risk coefficient (1.0 for normal, 1.08 for important structures).
- $k_2$ = Terrain, roughness & height factor (varies **0.98 at z=10 m to 1.32 at z=100 m** in Terrain Category 2).
- $k_3$ = Topography factor (1.0 for flat, up to 1.36 for hills/ridges).
- $z$ = Height above ground in m.
- $z_0$ = Reference height (10 m).
- $1/n$ = Power law exponent (**0.14 for Terrain Cat 2 (open terrain), 0.20 for urban Terrain Cat 3**).
- $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are the **statistical, exposure, and topographic multipliers** that adjust the basic wind speed to the design value at any specific location and height.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Load Case for High-Rise Formwork:**
> $$P_{design} = P_{concrete} + P_{wind} + P_{dynamic\,pump\,surge}$$
> The pump surge component is typically taken as **± 15 to 25 %** of the static lateral pressure during initial discharge and restart events.

---

## 2.3 Formwork Cycle Time and Productivity

In high-rise construction, formwork cost is dominated by **cycle time**, not material cost. The cycle time $T_c$ per floor is:

$$T_c = T_{erect} + T_{pour} + T_{cure} + T_{strip} + T_{relocate}$$

- $T_{erect}$ = Time to set the form (hrs).
- $T_{pour}$ = Time to place concrete (hrs).
- $T_{cure}$ = Time before stripping (hrs; controlled by min. compressive strength, often **10 N/mm² for soffit stripping**).
- $T_{strip}$ = Time to dismantle (hrs).
- $T_{relocate}$ = Time to lift/jump to next level (hrs).
- $T_c$ = Total cycle time per floor in hrs.

For target cycle times of **3 to 4 days per floor** in high-rise, self-climbing systems become essential.

---

## 2.4 Bridge Formwork – Cantilever Construction

In **balanced cantilever** bridge construction, the form traveler (a steel truss structure) extends symmetrically on both sides of the pier to cast segments. The bending moment at the pier due to a cantilevered segment of length $L$ and self-weight $w$ is:

$$M_{pier} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{2}$$

- $M_{pier}$ = Bending moment at the pier root (kN·m).
- $w$ = Distributed self-weight of the cantilever segment (kN/m).
- $L$ = Length of cantilevered segment from pier (m).
- $w \cdot L^2 / 2$ = Moment from a uniformly loaded cantilever projecting from a fixed root.

> [!WARNING]
> **Asymmetry is the most critical hazard:** In balanced cantilever, if the segments on both sides are not within **1 tonne** of each other, the pier experiences an unbalanced moment that may crack the previously cast segments. This is a common KTU problem topic.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

| # | Formula / Concept | Application | Key Unit | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{max} = C_w C_c (7.2 + 785R/(T+17.8))$ | Lateral pressure (modified ACI 347) | kN/m² | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $P_{max} = C_w \rho g H$ | Hydrostatic limit | kN/m² | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $V_z = V_b k_1 k_2 k_3 (z/z_0)^{1/n}$ | Wind speed at height | m/s | ★★★★ |
| 4 | $P_z = 0.5 \rho_a V_z^2 C_d K_a$ | Wind pressure on formwork | Pa (kN/m²) | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $M_{pier} = w L^2 / 2$ | Cantilever segment moment at pier | kN·m | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $T_c = T_{erect} + T_{pour} + T_{cure} + T_{strip} + T_{relocate}$ | Floor cycle time | hours | ★★★ |
| 7 | $P_{boom} = \rho g h_{pump}$ (for verification of pump pressure) | Pump line pressure at discharge | kN/m² | ★★★ |
| 8 | $F_{tie} = P_{max} \cdot A_{tie\,spacing}$ | Tie-rod design force | kN | ★★★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Lateral Pressure on Core Wall of a 30-Storey Building

**Given:**
- Normal weight concrete, $\rho = 2500$ kg/m³, $C_w = 1.0$
- Type I cement with retarder, $C_c = 1.2$
- Rate of placement $R = 4.5$ m/hr (pumped concrete)
- Concrete temperature at placement $T = 30$ °C
- Wall height between construction joints $H = 4.0$ m
- Tie-rod spacing = 1.2 m (horizontal) × 1.0 m (vertical)

**Step 1: Compute the modified ACI expression.**

The numerator constant-pressure term is $7.2$ kN/m², representing the minimum hydrostatic baseline when $R$ is very small. The rate-dependent term $785R/(T+17.8)$ is computed first, then multiplied by $C_w$ and $C_c$.

$$P_1 = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{785 \cdot R}{T + 17.8}\right)$$

Substituting $R = 4.5$ m/hr and $T = 30$ °C:

$$P_1 = 1.0 \cdot 1.2 \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{785 \cdot 4.5}{30 + 17.8}\right)$$

Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{785 \cdot 4.5}{47.8} = \frac{3532.5}{47.8} = 73.90 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Add the constant:

$$7.2 + 73.90 = 81.10 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Apply the unit-weight and chemistry coefficients:

$$P_1 = 1.0 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 81.10 = 97.32 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the hydrostatic limit.**

$$P_2 = C_w \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H$$

Substitute $\rho = 2500$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $H = 4.0$ m:

$$P_2 = 1.0 \cdot 2500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 4.0 = 98100 \text{ N/m}^2 = 98.10 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Take the lesser (minimum).**

$$P_{max} = \min(97.32,\ 98.10) = 97.32 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Design tie-rod force per tie.**

The tributary area for one tie is $A_{tie} = 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.2$ m². The tie force is:

$$F_{tie} = P_{max} \cdot A_{tie} = 97.32 \cdot 1.2 = 116.78 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Apply safety factor and select tie.**

With a factor of safety of **1.5** on tie load (per formwork code):

$$F_{design} = 1.5 \cdot 116.78 = 175.17 \text{ kN}$$

Select a **20 mm dia. high-tensile tie rod** with safe working load ≈ 180 kN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** Notice that for $H = 4.0$ m and high $R$, both expressions are nearly equal — concrete is on the verge of full hydrostatic behavior. For taller lifts (e.g., $H = 6$ m), the second expression dominates.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Wind Load on a Self-Climbing Formwork at 80 m Height

**Given:**
- Basic wind speed $V_b = 39$ m/s (Kerala coastal)
- $k_1 = 1.0$ (normal structure)
- $k_2 = 1.18$ (terrain Cat 2, z = 80 m, from IS 875 table)
- $k_3 = 1.0$ (flat terrain)
- $z_0 = 10$ m, $n = 7$ (Terrain Cat 2 → 1/n = 1/7 = 0.143)
- $C_d = 1.3$ (formwork panel)
- $K_a = 1.0$
- Air density $\rho_a = 1.22$ kg/m³
- Formwork face area $A = 12$ m² (typical core wall panel)

**Step 1: Compute design wind speed at $z = 80$ m.**

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot \left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)^{1/n}$$

$$V_z = 39 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.18 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \left(\frac{80}{10}\right)^{0.143}$$

Compute the height factor:

$$\left(\frac{80}{10}\right)^{0.143} = 8^{0.143}$$

Apply natural log: $\ln(8) = 2.079$, so $\ln(8^{0.143}) = 0.143 \cdot 2.079 = 0.297$, and $8^{0.143} = e^{0.297} = 1.346$.

$$V_z = 39 \cdot 1.18 \cdot 1.346 = 61.95 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute dynamic pressure.**

$$q_z = 0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_z^{\,2} = 0.5 \cdot 1.22 \cdot (61.95)^2$$

$$V_z^{\,2} = 3838.0$$

$$q_z = 0.5 \cdot 1.22 \cdot 3838.0 = 2341.2 \text{ Pa} = 2.341 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute total wind force on formwork face.**

$$F_{wind} = q_z \cdot C_d \cdot K_a \cdot A = 2.341 \cdot 1.3 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 12 = 36.52 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Compare to tie-rod capacity.**

The wind force must be safely transferred to the structure through ties. Check whether the 20 mm ties selected earlier (175 kN design) have adequate surplus:

$$Surplus = 175 - 36.52 = 138.48 \text{ kN (comfortable)}$$

However, the **self-climbing system's anchors** must be designed for **uplift + shear** from this wind force. Each anchor in tension carries:

$$F_{anchor} = \frac{F_{wind}}{N_{anchors}} = \frac{36.52}{6} \approx 6.09 \text{ kN/anchor}$$

This is well within standard M30 anchor capacity (~ 50 kN), so wind stability is not critical here. **The critical case is the hydrostatic pressure, not the wind**, in this example.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** As height increases beyond ~120 m, wind can become the dominant lateral design action for formwork — *not* the concrete pressure. KTU frequently tests this crossover.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Balanced Cantilever Bridge – Unbalanced Moment Check

**Given:**
- Segment weight $W = 850$ kN per segment
- Segment length from pier $L = 5$ m
- 2 segments cast on one side, 1 segment on the other side at the time of inspection

**Step 1: Compute the cantilever moment on each side.**

For the heavier side (2 segments):

$$M_{heavier} = 2 \cdot 850 \cdot \frac{5}{2} = 4250 \text{ kN·m (using }M = W \cdot L/2\text{ for cantilever)}$$

Wait — re-evaluating: each segment is at distance $L_i$ from pier. If segments are 5 m and 10 m from pier (centroids):

$$M_{heavier} = 850 \cdot 5 + 850 \cdot 10 = 4250 + 8500 = 12750 \text{ kN·m}$$

For the lighter side (1 segment at 5 m):

$$M_{lighter} = 850 \cdot 5 = 4250 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Unbalanced moment at pier.**

$$\Delta M = M_{heavier} - M_{lighter} = 12750 - 4250 = 8500 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3: Counterweight provision.**

The contractor must add counterweight to balance:

$$W_{counter} \cdot L_{counter} = 8500$$

If counterweight placed at $L = 10$ m (same as outermost segment):

$$W_{counter} = \frac{8500}{10} = 850 \text{ kN}$$

This is typically provided as **water tanks or concrete blocks** on the lighter side of the cantilever.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget the 850 kN form-traveler self-weight (~80 kN) which adds ~400 kN·m extra moment. Include **form traveler, reinforcement cage, and fresh concrete** in $W$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Rise Self-Climbing Formwork – Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pour Concrete at Lower Level] --> B[Initial Cure 12 to 18 hrs]
    B --> C[Strip Form Faces from Concrete]
    C --> D[Retract Hydraulic Jacks]
    D --> E[Climbing Shoes Anchor to Precast Embedments]
    E --> F[Hydraulic Lift Pushes Formwork Up 4.2 m]
    F --> G[Re-anchor Shoes to New Embedments]
    G --> H[Reset Form Panels to Alignment]
    H --> I[Place Reinforcement and Embedments]
    I --> J[Pour Next Lift]
    J --> A
    subgraph Wind[Lateral Stability Subsystem]
        W1[Tie Rods to Precast Anchors] --> W2[Bracing Frames]
        W2 --> W3[Working Platform Brackets]
    end
    J -.wind load.-> W1
```

## 4.2 Bridge Cantilever Form Traveler – Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pier Shaft Previously Cast] --> S[Strongback / Truss Back-Span]
    S --> C[Cantilever Nose]
    C --> F[Formwork Panel Bottom]
    F --> H[Hydraulic Suspension Bars]
    H --> A[Anchor in Pier]
    subgraph Cycle[Construction Cycle]
        X1[Move Traveler to Next Segment] --> X2[Align and Adjust Elevation]
        X2 --> X3[Place Rebar and Ducts]
        X3 --> X4[Pour Concrete Segment 3 to 5 m]
        X4 --> X5[Stress PT Tendons After 75 percent Strength]
        X5 --> X1
    end
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Formwork System Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Selection] --> H{Height greater than 75 m?}
    H -- No --> Conv[Conventional Ground Supported]
    H -- Yes --> Type{Structure Type}
    Type -- Core Walls --> JF{Repetition more than 10 floors?}
    JF -- Yes --> SCF[Self Climbing Form ACS]
    JF -- No --> JP[Jump Form with Crane]
    Type -- Slabs --> Rep{Repetitive floor plate?}
    Rep -- Yes --> FF[Flying Form Table Form]
    Rep -- No --> CS[Conventional Shoring]
    Type -- Bridge --> SP{Pier Height}
    SP -- less than 15 m --> MSS[Movable Scaffolding]
    SP -- greater than 15 m --> CB[Balanced Cantilever Form Traveler]
```

## 4.4 Load Path Schematic in Self-Climbing Formwork (Tie Force Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[Wind Load on Formwork Face] --> P[Formwork Plywood Panel]
    P --> S[Steel Wales Horizontal]
    S --> T[Tie Rods Through Wall]
    T --> A[Embedded Anchors in Cured Concrete]
    A --> M[Main Structure of Building]
    CP[Concrete Lateral Pressure] --> P
    PW[Self Weight and Working Load] --> WP[Working Platform]
    WP --> S
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between Jump Form and Self-Climbing Form with respect to (i) lifting mechanism, (ii) crane dependence, and (iii) cycle time per floor."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Jump Form | Self-Climbing Form |
|---|---|---|
| Lifting mechanism | Crane-lifted in discrete jumps | Hydraulic jacks lift the system along guide rails |
| Crane dependence | Fully dependent on tower crane | Independent of crane (uses hydraulic power pack) |
| Typical cycle time | 5 to 7 days per floor | 3 to 4 days per floor |
| Labour at lift | High (riggers, signalmen) | Low (operator + helper) |
| Cost premium | Moderate | High initial, low per-cycle |
| Wind sensitivity during lift | High (large panels in air) | Low (slides up along rails) |

*(3 marks – 1 mark per row)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why is the rate of placement $R$ a critical parameter in the design of high-rise core-wall formwork? Mention its typical range when concrete is pumped."**

**Model Answer:**
The rate of placement $R$ directly governs the **fresh concrete lateral pressure** on the formwork face. Higher $R$ means more concrete is fluid at any instant, so the formwork must resist a larger pressure head. In the ACI 347 equation, $P_{max} \propto R/(T+17.8)$, so $R$ appears linearly in the rate-dependent term. For pumped concrete in high-rise cores, $R$ typically ranges from **3 to 6 m/hr**, often reaching 8 m/hr during peak pump output. This is roughly **2 to 3 times** the rate of crane-and-bucket placement, and therefore formwork design for high-rise **must** use the pumped placement rate, not historical bucket rates. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Self-Climbing Formwork Design**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** A 35-storey residential tower in Kochi uses a self-climbing formwork system for its reinforced concrete core wall. The basic wind speed is **39 m/s**, terrain Category 2, terrain class factor $k_2 = 1.32$ at $z = 100$ m. Drag coefficient $C_d = 1.3$. Formwork panel area = 14 m². The wall is being poured at $R = 5$ m/hr using Type I cement with retarder at $T = 28$ °C, with $C_c = 1.2$ and $C_w = 1.0$. Wall height $H = 4.2$ m. Concrete density = 25 kN/m³. Determine the **design lateral pressure** as per modified ACI 347 and the **design wind force** at 100 m height. Which is the governing design action? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step climbing cycle** of a self-climbing formwork (ACS) with the help of a labelled block diagram. List **two advantages and two limitations** of self-climbing form over conventional jump form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q A (a):**

**Step 1: Compute the modified ACI pressure.**

$$P_1 = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{785R}{T + 17.8}\right)$$

$$P_1 = 1.0 \cdot 1.2 \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{785 \cdot 5}{28 + 17.8}\right)$$

$$= 1.2 \cdot \left(7.2 + \frac{3925}{45.8}\right) = 1.2 \cdot (7.2 + 85.70) = 1.2 \cdot 92.90 = 111.48 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute hydrostatic limit.**

$$P_2 = C_w \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 1.0 \cdot 25 \cdot 4.2 = 105.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

(Note: $\rho g = 25$ kN/m³ for normal-weight concrete.)

**Step 3: Governing pressure.**

$$P_{max} = \min(111.48,\ 105.0) = 105.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Wind speed at $z = 100$ m.**

$$V_z = 39 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.32 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{10}\right)^{0.143}$$

$$= 39 \cdot 1.32 \cdot 10^{0.143} = 51.48 \cdot 1.387 = 71.40 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Wind pressure.**

$$q_z = 0.5 \cdot 1.22 \cdot (71.40)^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.22 \cdot 5098 = 3110 \text{ Pa} = 3.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6: Wind force.**

$$F_{wind} = 3.11 \cdot 1.3 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 14 = 56.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Equivalent pressure from wind (for comparison).**

$$P_{wind\,eq} = \frac{F_{wind}}{A} = \frac{56.6}{14} = 4.04 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 8: Conclusion.**

Concrete pressure (105.0 kN/m²) ≫ Wind equivalent pressure (4.04 kN/m²) ⇒ **Concrete pressure governs** at this height of 100 m. Wind becomes critical only at heights beyond ~150 m for similar geometry.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q A (b):**

**Climbing Cycle (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

1. **Pour concrete** in current lift; cure to minimum 10 N/mm² (12–18 hrs).
2. **Strip form faces** (panel released from concrete using taper ties or wedge system).
3. **Retract hydraulic jacks** to release climbing shoes.
4. **Re-anchor climbing shoes** in embedded anchor sleeves cast in previous lift.
5. **Hydraulic lift** pushes entire formwork assembly (platforms, panels, bracing) vertically by 4.2 m.
6. **Reset shoes** in new anchor sleeves.
7. **Re-align panels** using screw jacks and alignment braces.
8. **Place reinforcement, MEP embedments, then pour next lift.**

**Advantages of Self-Climbing over Jump Form:**
1. **No crane time required** for formwork lifting — tower crane is freed for rebar, concrete bucket, and other critical lifts.
2. **Faster cycle** (3–4 days vs 5–7 days) and higher safety (no large suspended panel during windy conditions).
3. Better **quality control** as alignment is hydraulically set, not crane-swing set.

**Limitations:**
1. **High initial capital cost** — hydraulic power pack, climbing shoes, anchor sleeves are proprietary.
2. **Specialist operator needed** for hydraulic system; skilled-labour intensive for first project.
3. **Embedment anchors left in structure** — may interfere with waterproofing or finishing of core wall.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Bridge Formwork and Cantilever Construction**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** In a balanced cantilever construction of a segmental bridge, two segments of length **5 m each** are cast on the left of a pier before casting starts on the right. The segment weight is **900 kN** (including fresh concrete, rebar, and form traveler contribution). The pier height is **22 m**. The form traveler itself weighs **120 kN** and acts at the tip of the outermost segment. Determine the **unbalanced moment** at the pier root and the **counterweight** required on the right side if placed at **5 m from pier centerline**. Use $M_{pier} = \sum W_i \cdot L_i$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Movable Scaffolding System (MSS)** and **Form Traveler** for bridge deck construction in terms of (i) support condition, (ii) suitable span range, (iii) pier-height adaptability, and (iv) impact on traffic below. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B (a):**

**Step 1: Identify all weights on the left (heavier) side.**

- Segment 1 (closer to pier): $W_1 = 900$ kN at $L_1 = 5$ m
- Segment 2 (further from pier): $W_2 = 900$ kN at $L_2 = 10$ m
- Form traveler at tip of segment 2: $W_{FT} = 120$ kN at $L_{FT} = 10$ m

**Step 2: Compute the moment on the left side about the pier root.**

$$M_{left} = W_1 \cdot L_1 + W_2 \cdot L_2 + W_{FT} \cdot L_{FT}$$

$$M_{left} = (900 \cdot 5) + (900 \cdot 10) + (120 \cdot 10)$$

$$M_{left} = 4500 + 9000 + 1200 = 14700 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3: Moment on the right side.**

Right side has only the form traveler (in position, since it's the launching/initial setup). Assuming no segment yet on the right:

$$M_{right} = W_{FT,right} \cdot L_{FT,right} = 120 \cdot 10 = 1200 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Unbalanced moment at pier root.**

$$\Delta M = M_{left} - M_{right} = 14700 - 1200 = 13500 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5: Counterweight on the right at 5 m from pier.**

The counterweight must create a moment equal to $\Delta M$:

$$W_{counter} \cdot 5 = 13500 \Rightarrow W_{counter} = 2700 \text{ kN}$$

In practice, this is provided by **water tanks (2700 kN ≈ 270 m³ of water)** or pre-cast concrete blocks staged on the right cantilever.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget the **form traveler weight** and obtain an answer of 8500 kN·m unbalanced and 1700 kN counterweight. Including the traveler (a *must* per construction reality) gives 13500 kN·m and 2700 kN. **This 60% error** is a common KTU mark-deduction point.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B (b):**

| Parameter | Movable Scaffolding System (MSS) | Form Traveler (Cantilever Method) |
|---|---|---|
| Support condition | **Truss spans between adjacent piers**; supported at both ends | **Cantilevered from one pier**; anchored into completed segments |
| Suitable span range | **30 m to 80 m** spans (efficient for medium spans) | **80 m to 250 m+** spans (efficient for long spans) |
| Pier height adaptability | **Limited to ~30 m pier height** (ground or pier-prop assistance needed) | **Suitable for very tall piers** (e.g., 100+ m valley crossings) |
| Impact on traffic below | **MSS truss sits below deck level** — requires partial/full traffic closure during casting and launching | **No ground support needed** — traffic below remains largely undisturbed |
| Cycle time per segment | Slower (full span in one or two pours) | Faster per segment (3–5 m segments, weekly cycle) |
| Initial cost | Lower (for repeated spans on a route) | Higher (one set per pier) |
| Typical application | Viaducts with multiple equal spans | River/valley crossings with long main spans |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per major row, 1 mark for introduction/summary)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points in PECET742 Module 2:**
> 1. **Forgetting the chemistry coefficient $C_c$** — Students often write ACI 347 as $P = C_w(7.2 + 785R/(T+17.8))$ without $C_c$. For pumped concrete with retarders, this **underestimates** pressure by up to 40% → **Lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Using hydrostatic limit unconditionally** — The equation is the *minimum* of the two expressions, not the hydrostatic limit alone. → **Lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring terrain category in wind speed** — Treating $V_b$ as the design wind speed at all heights is a frequent mistake. → **Lose 2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting form-traveler self-weight in cantilever moment** — As shown in Q B(a), this leads to ~60% error in counterweight calculation. → **Lose 2 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up Jump Form and Slip Form** — Slip form moves *continuously*; Jump form moves in *discrete jumps*. Examiners test this distinction every year.
> 6. **Units confusion** — kN/m² vs Pa (1 kN/m² = 1000 Pa). Always state units explicitly in numerical answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Lateral Pressure Equation:** $P_{max} = \min\left[C_w C_c \left(7.2 + \frac{785R}{T+17.8}\right), \ C_w \rho g H\right]$ — remember both terms, take the minimum.
- [ ] **Pumped concrete rate** $R$ in high-rise: typically **3 to 6 m/hr**, much higher than bucket placement.
- [ ] **Chemistry coefficient $C_c$**: **1.0** (no retarder), **1.2** (with retarder), **1.4** (Type II/V cement). Always check.
- [ ] **Wind pressure** is **height-dependent** via the power law $(z/z_0)^{1/n}$. At 100 m+ height, wind can govern formwork design.
- [ ] **Jump Form** = crane-lifted, discrete jumps, lower cost, slower cycle. **Self-Climbing Form** = hydraulic, continuous cycle, no crane, higher cost.
- [ ] **Slip Form** = *continuous* vertical movement, used for silos, chimneys, core walls of very tall buildings (>40 storeys).
- [ ] **Flying Form (Table Form)** = crane-lifted floor units, ideal for repetitive flat-plate slabs.
- [ ] **Bridge cantilever moment:** $M_{pier} = \sum W_i L_i$. Always include **form traveler self-weight**.
- [ ] **Counterweight** in balanced cantilever: place on the lighter side at the same lever arm as the unbalanced segment.
- [ ] **Movable Scaffolding System (MSS)** for short-medium spans with ground/pier access; **Form Traveler** for long spans and tall piers.
- [ ] **Incremental Launching Method (ILM):** Cast segments behind abutment, push with hydraulic jacks, suitable for 30–60 m spans, multiple repetitions.
- [ ] **Combined load case:** $P_{design} = P_{concrete} + P_{wind} + P_{pump\,surge}$ (with pump surge = 15–25% of static).
- [ ] **Safety factor on formwork ties:** typically **1.5 to 2.0** per ACI 347.
- [ ] **KTU favourite sub-topics:** lateral pressure numerical (most frequent), wind pressure numerical, system selection table, balanced cantilever counterweight.
- [ ] **Terminology to avoid confusion:** "Tie rod" = through-wall tension member; "Waler/Wale" = horizontal beam; "Strongback" = vertical beam behind formwork; "Shoring" = vertical prop; "Reshoring" = re-propping partially cured slab.
- [ ] **Curing strength for stripping:** typically **10 N/mm²** for soffit, **15–20 N/mm²** for walls (per IS 456 / ACI 347).
- [ ] **Cycle time target for high-rise:** 3–4 days/floor (premium) to 5–7 days/floor (standard). Self-climbing systems target the lower bound.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formwork for Architectural Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formwork for Architectural Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Architectural Concrete** is defined as concrete that is permanently exposed to view and is therefore used as an architectural finish, where surface appearance, texture, colour, and dimensional accuracy are of critical importance equal to or greater than its structural function. The **formwork for architectural concrete** is a precision-engineered temporary mould system specifically designed to shape, support, and impart a desired surface finish to the freshly placed concrete, while maintaining stringent tolerances on alignment, plumbness, flatness, and surface texture as prescribed in **ACI 117 (Tolerances for Concrete Construction)** and **ACI 347 (Guide to Formwork for Concrete)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** Architectural concrete formwork is *not* ordinary formwork — it is a **finish-critical structural system** that simultaneously acts as a **mould, a texturing device, and a quality-control instrument** governing the final aesthetic and dimensional outcome of the hardened concrete.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of architectural concrete formwork like a **high-end baking mould for a wedding cake**:

- A regular cake pan only needs to hold the batter and release the cake — that is **structural formwork** (utility only).
- A wedding cake mould, however, must create intricate patterns, sharp edges, mirror-smooth surfaces, and precise decorative ridges — that is **architectural formwork** (utility + aesthetics).
- Just as a baker polishes the inside of the mould, applies a release coating, and avoids any scratch that would mar the cake's surface, the formwork engineer must use defect-free, sealed, coated panels with tightly controlled joint patterns to deliver a flawless concrete face.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** In structural formwork, the *inside* (concrete) is hidden. In architectural formwork, the *inside surface* **becomes the building's visible skin** — so every grain, scratch, joint, and tie-hole on the form face is **permanently imprinted** onto the structure.

## 1.3 Classification of Architectural Concrete (As per ACI 303R)

| Class | Surface Treatment | Formwork Standard |
|-------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Class A** | No subsequent treatment — form face IS the final finish | Highest precision, seamless panels |
| **Class B** | Surface to be painted or applied with coatings | High precision, minor repairs permitted |
| **Class C** | Surface to receive applied finishes (tile, panelling) | Standard structural-grade formwork |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked to "list types of architectural concrete," always classify per **ACI 303R** (Class A / B / C) — this is the global benchmark cited in the KTU syllabus and is treated as the *expected* answer.

## 1.4 Why Architectural Formwork is Demanding

- **Tighter Deflection Limits:** Whereas structural formwork allows **L/360** deflection, architectural formwork typically restricts deflection to **L/500** or even **L/1000** to prevent visible bulges and sag lines.
- **Surface Hardness Standards:** The form face material must resist moisture, vibration, and chemical attack from fresh concrete without warping, swelling, or delaminating.
- **Joint Watertightness:** Joints must be **leak-proof** to prevent cement paste leakage, which causes honeycombing, staining, and sand streaks.
- **Tie System Discipline:** Tie-hole patterns must follow a strict modular grid — random or untidy tie layouts are *unacceptable* because every tie hole remains visible.
- **Tolerance Band:** As per **ACI 117**, plane tolerance is typically **± 6 mm in 3 m** for architectural surfaces, versus **± 12 mm** for structural.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Concept

The successful execution of architectural concrete formwork follows a **5-stage logic chain** that the KTU examiner will expect you to articulate:

### Stage 1 — **Form Face Selection**
Choose the contact-surface material based on the desired finish:
- **HDO (High-Density Overlay) Plywood** — smooth, factory-sealed phenolic surface, most common.
- **MDO (Medium-Density Overlay) Plywood** — paintable base for site-applied textures.
- **Steel Forms (1/4 in. plate)** — mirror finish, used for Class A, reusable 100+ times.
- **Glass-Fibre Reinforced Plastic (GRP/GFRC Liners)** — for complex 3D shapes and custom textures.
- **Elastomeric / Rubber Liners** — for deep reveals and ornamental patterns.
- **Plastic / ABS Liners** — disposable, single-use, cost-effective for one-off projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HDO over structural plywood?** HDO plywood has a resin-impregnated fibre overlay with a **wearing surface of 0.30 mm** that resists moisture absorption, gives **50+ reuses**, and produces a **Class A** matte finish free of wood-grain telegraphing.

### Stage 2 — **Formwork Pressure Analysis**
The lateral pressure exerted by fresh concrete is **critical** because the panels must be stiff enough to resist bulging while remaining light enough to handle.

**For architectural concrete (low-to-medium placement rate, < 2 m/h, normal-weight concrete):**

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot H$$

where:
- $P_{max}$ = maximum lateral pressure (kN/m²)
- $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of concrete ≈ **24 kN/m³** for normal-weight
- $H$ = head of fresh concrete (m)

**For higher placement rates, ACI 347 (Eq. 2-1) gives:**

$$P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \left( 7.2 + \frac{785}{T + 17.8} + \frac{5746}{T + 17.8} \cdot \frac{R}{1.0} \right) \cdot 10^{-3} \text{ (MPa units)}$$

Simplified, the key variables are:
- $C_w$ = unit weight factor
- $C_c$ = chemistry factor (cement type, admixtures)
- $T$ = concrete temperature (°C)
- $R$ = rate of placement (m/h)

### Stage 3 — **Deflection Control of Panels**
For a simply supported form panel with uniform pressure:

$$\delta = \frac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

where:
- $\delta$ = mid-span deflection (mm)
- $w$ = uniform lateral pressure (N/mm²)
- $L$ = span between supports (mm)
- $E$ = modulus of elasticity of form face material (N/mm²)
- $I$ = moment of inertia of panel cross-section (mm⁴)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Limit:** For architectural concrete, $\delta \le L/500$ (or L/1000 for the most demanding Class A finishes). This is **2 to 3 times stiffer** than structural formwork.

### Stage 4 — **Joint & Tie Discipline**
- **Rustication Strips:** Inset wooden or plastic strips create intentional architectural reveals.
- **Snap Ties / She-Bolts:** Sized for the *actual* lateral pressure, not the structural pressure.
- **Tie Spacing:** Typically **600 mm × 600 mm** grid for architectural, against 900 mm for structural.

### Stage 5 — **Release Agent & Surface Protection**
- Use **non-staining, chemically reactive release agents** (e.g., wax-based or silicone-free).
- Apply uniformly with a **lint-free cloth** to avoid streaking.
- **Never** use form oil on Class A surfaces — it can discolour or stain the concrete.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The vertical pipe `|` symbol has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in the table below to prevent markdown table syntax breakage — as per V10 protocol.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application to Architectural Concrete |
|---|------------------|---------------|-------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \gamma_c \cdot H$ | kN/m² | Low-rate pours (< 2 m/h) |
| 2 | ACI 347 Pressure | $P_{max} = C_w C_c \left( 7.2 + \dfrac{785}{T+17.8} \right) \cdot 10^{-3}$ | MPa | General walls & columns |
| 3 | Deflection of Panel | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | mm | Stiffness check |
| 4 | Max Deflection Limit | $\delta \le L/500$ | — | Architectural (Class A/B) |
| 5 | Max Deflection Limit | $\delta \le L/1000$ | — | Architectural (Class A premium) |
| 6 | Tie Spacing Grid | $S_t \le 600$ (typ.) | mm | Architectural density |
| 7 | Plane Tolerance | $\pm 6$ in $3$ | mm | ACI 117 architectural |
| 8 | Plane Tolerance (structural) | $\pm 12$ in $3$ | mm | ACI 117 structural |
| 9 | Form Face Temp. Limit | $T_f \le 65$ | °C | To prevent thermal warping |
| 10 | Min. Reuse Cycles (HDO) | $\ge 50$ | cycles | Standard durability |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise cores & shear walls** in landmark towers (e.g., exposed-concrete façades of museums, libraries, government buildings).
- **Bridge piers & abutments** where the concrete itself is the visual signature.
- **Metro stations and airports** where architectural concrete is used for visual continuity at lower cost than stone cladding.
- **Precast cladding panels** cast against custom form liners to mimic wood-grain, brick, or geometric textures — used as architectural façades.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Formwork cost for architectural concrete can be **3 to 5 times higher** than structural formwork, but the *savings* in stone-cladding, painting, and façade treatment often justify the premium — this is the kind of practical insight KTU's "design economics" questions test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Lateral Pressure on Formwork (Hydrostatic Case)

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** Determine the maximum lateral pressure on an architectural concrete wall formwork, given:
- Wall height $H = 4.5$ m
- Concrete unit weight $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³
- Rate of placement $R = 1.0$ m/h
- Concrete temperature $T = 21$ °C
- Type I cement (no retardation)
- Internal vibration
- Normal-weight concrete

### Step 1 — Identify the Applicable Pressure Equation

Since $R = 1.0$ m/h is less than the **2.0 m/h threshold** commonly used to decide between hydrostatic and rate-based pressure, we **first check** the hydrostatic case.

$$P_{hydro} = \gamma_c \cdot H$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hydro} &= 24 \text{ kN/m}^3 \times 4.5 \text{ m} \\
P_{hydro} &= 108 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
P_{hydro} &= 108 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with ACI 347 Rate-Based Equation

For normal-weight concrete, Type I cement, internal vibration, ACI 347 gives:

$$P_{max} = C_w C_c \left[ 7.2 + \frac{785}{T + 17.8} + \frac{5746 \cdot R}{T + 17.8} \right] \cdot 10^{-3} \quad \text{(MPa)}$$

With $C_w = 1.0$ and $C_c = 1.0$ for normal-weight, Type I:

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Temperature-Adjusted Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
T + 17.8 &= 21 + 17.8 = 38.8 \text{ °C} \\
\frac{785}{38.8} &= 20.23 \\
\frac{5746 \times 1.0}{38.8} &= 148.09
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sum the Bracket Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left[ 7.2 + 20.23 + 148.09 \right] \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa} \\
P_{max} &= 175.52 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa} \\
P_{max} &= 0.1755 \text{ MPa} = 175.5 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the ACI 347 Cap and Compare

ACI 347 also caps the pressure at:

$$P_{cap} = 0.143 \gamma_c \cdot H = 0.143 \times 24 \times 4.5 = 15.44 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{WRONG — recalculate}$$

**Corrected cap formula (per ACI 347 §2.2.2):**

$$P_{max} \le \gamma_c \cdot H \quad \text{or} \quad P_{max} \le 144 \text{ kPa (whichever is lower)}$$

> The cap for walls with $R < 2.1$ m/h is **143.7 kPa** (i.e., 143.7 kN/m²).

Therefore:

$$
P_{max} = \min(175.5, \; 143.7) = 143.7 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 7 — Final Design Pressure

$$
\boxed{P_{max} = 143.7 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (governs)}}
$$

This value is used for sizing walers, ties, and panel thickness for the architectural formwork.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Deflection of Architectural Form Panel
