**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** A 19 mm thick HDO plywood panel spans **600 mm** between studs. The lateral pressure is **143.7 kN/m²** (from §3.1). The modulus of elasticity of HDO plywood is **E = 10,500 N/mm²**. Calculate the mid-span deflection and check if it satisfies the architectural tolerance of **L/500**.

### Step 1 — Convert Pressure to N/mm² Units

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= 143.7 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 143.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
w &= 0.1437 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Moment of Inertia of Plywood Section

For a 19 mm thick × 1000 mm (per metre) wide section:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} = \frac{1000 \times 19^3}{12} \\
I &= \frac{1000 \times 6859}{12} = \frac{6{,}859{,}000}{12} \\
I &= 571{,}583 \text{ mm}^4/\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Simply-Supported Beam Deflection Formula

$$
\delta = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{5 \times 0.1437 \times (600)^4}{384 \times 10{,}500 \times 571{,}583} \\
\delta &= \frac{5 \times 0.1437 \times 1.296 \times 10^{11}}{384 \times 10{,}500 \times 571{,}583} \\
\delta &= \frac{9.312 \times 10^{10}}{2.305 \times 10^{12}} \\
\delta &= 0.0404 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare with Allowable Deflection

$$
\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{500} = \frac{600}{500} = 1.20 \text{ mm}
$$

Since:

$$
\delta = 0.0404 \text{ mm} \ll \delta_{allow} = 1.20 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\text{Panel is SAFE. Deflection} = 0.04 \text{ mm}, \text{ 30 times within the architectural tolerance.}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Form Face vs Surface Finish

| Form Face Material | Surface Finish Class | Reuse Cycles | Cost Index (Rel.) | Water Resistance | Best Use |
|--------------------|----------------------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|----------|
| HDO Plywood | Class A | **50+** | 3× | Excellent | General architectural walls |
| MDO Plywood | Class B | **20–30** | 2× | Good | Paintable surfaces |
| Steel Plate (6 mm) | Class A (mirror) | **200+** | 5× | Excellent | Precast, smooth walls |
| GFRC / GRP Liner | Class A (custom) | **80+** | 4× | Excellent | 3D shapes, complex textures |
| Elastomeric Liner | Class A (ornamental) | **30–50** | 4× | Excellent | Deep reveals, mouldings |
| Plastic / ABS | Class B | **1–5** (single-use) | 1× | Excellent | One-off, low-budget |
| Rough Sawn Timber | Class C | **2–3** | 1× | Poor | Board-marked rustic finish |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Architectural Concrete Formwork System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Architectural Concrete Formwork System] --> B[Form Face Layer]
    A --> C[Secondary Support Layer]
    A --> D[Primary Support Layer]
    A --> E[Connection and Tie System]
    A --> F[Release and Surface Protection]

    B --> B1[HDO Plywood 19 mm]
    B --> B2[Form Liner GRP Plastic]
    B --> B3[Rustication Strip Reveal]
    B --> B4[Elastomeric Liner Texture]

    C --> C1[Vertical Studs 50x100 mm @ 300 mm c/c]
    C --> C2[Horizontal Walers 100x100 mm]

    D --> D1[Strongbacks 150x150 mm]
    D --> D2[Scaffold Bracing]
    D --> D3[Adjustable Props]

    E --> E1[Snap Ties 6 mm dia]
    E --> E2[She Bolts]
    E --> E3[Form Spacers]

    F --> F1[Chemically Reactive Release Agent]
    F --> F2[Edge Sealing Tape]
    F --> F3[Water Based Form Oil]

    B1 --> G[Fresh Concrete Architectural Surface]
    B2 --> G
    B3 --> G
    B4 --> G
    C1 --> H[Load Transfer Path]
    C2 --> H
    D1 --> H
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    F1 --> I[Defect Free Demould]
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G --> J[Class A Architectural Finish]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Formwork Assembly Flowchart (Sequential Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Step 1 Design and Shop Drawing] --> A2[Step 2 Selection of Form Face Material]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Fabrication of Panels and Liners]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Erection and Alignment]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Application of Release Agent]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Concrete Placement and Vibration]

```

    L0[FRESH CONCRETE FACE] --> L1[FORM LINER 5 to 25 mm]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
**Define architectural concrete. How is it classified as per ACI 303R? Mention the formwork tolerance band for each class.**

**Model Answer:**

Architectural concrete is concrete that will be permanently exposed to view and whose surface is intended to serve as the finished architectural surface. As per ACI 303R, it is classified into three classes based on the level of finish and post-treatment:

- **Class A** — No subsequent treatment. The form face **is** the final finish. Plane tolerance: **± 6 mm in 3 m** (per ACI 117).
- **Class B** — Surface will receive paint, coating, or thin overlay. Plane tolerance: **± 6 mm in 3 m** with minor repairs allowed.
- **Class C** — Surface will be covered by another finish (tile, panel, etc.). Tolerance can be relaxed to **± 12 mm in 3 m**.

Formwork for Classes A and B demands HDO plywood, GFRC liners, leak-proof joints, and **L/500** deflection limits.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**List the essential requirements of formwork for architectural concrete. State any four.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Deflection Control** — Panel deflection must not exceed **L/500** (or L/1000 for Class A premium).
2. **Tight Joints** — All joints must be leak-proof to prevent cement paste bleeding and surface staining.
3. **Smooth, Defect-Free Form Face** — No scratches, dents, or wood-grain telegraphing; HDO or steel surfaces preferred.
4. **Modular Tie Pattern** — Tie holes must follow a regular architectural grid (e.g., 600 × 600 mm), not random.
5. **Non-Staining Release Agent** — Use chemically reactive, water-based release agents free of oil contamination.
6. **Dimensional Accuracy** — Per **ACI 117**, plane tolerance of **± 6 mm in 3 m** must be maintained.
7. **Rustication & Reveals** — Architectural joint lines and decorative reveals must be built into the formwork itself.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **types of form liners** used in architectural concrete formwork. Discuss their suitability, reuse potential, and surface finish they impart.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An architectural concrete wall of **5.0 m height** is to be cast at a placement rate of **1.5 m/h**, with concrete temperature **27 °C**, normal-weight concrete ($\gamma_c$ = 24 kN/m³), Type I cement, internal vibration. Calculate the maximum lateral pressure using ACI 347 equations and check whether the **2.1 m/h cap** governs.

#### Model Solution (a) — Form Liners

Form liners are auxiliary panels attached to the formwork face to create a desired surface pattern or texture on the architectural concrete. The major types are:

| Liner Type | Material | Surface Produced | Reuse | Best Application |
|------------|----------|------------------|-------|------------------|
| **Rigid Plastic / ABS** | Vacuum-formed thermoplastic | Geometric, ribbed, fluted | 1–5 cycles | Single-use, low-cost |
| **Elastomeric (Rubber/Polyurethane)** | Cast elastomer | Deep reveals, ornate | 30–50 | Victorian / classical mouldings |
| **GFRC / GRP (Glass-Fibre Reinforced Plastic)** | Fibre-reinforced polyester | Custom 3D shapes | 80+ | Complex curves, large panels |
| **Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)** | Foam, single-use | One-off carved patterns | 1 | Sculpted façades |
| **Metal Liners (SS, Aluminium, Bronze)** | Cast or pressed metal | Permanent textures, logos | 100+ | Signature features, signage |
| **Wood-grain Liners** | Rough-sawn timber | Board-marked rustic | 5–10 | Natural, organic textures |
| **Textured Rubber Mats** | Vulcanised rubber | Brick, ashlar, ashlar stone | 30–50 | Masonry-look concrete |

**Selection criteria:** Texture depth, repetition count, surface class (A/B/C), project budget, and reusability.

#### Model Solution (b) — Pressure Calculation

**Given:**
- $H = 5.0$ m
- $R = 1.5$ m/h
- $T = 27$ °C
- $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³
- Type I cement, $C_w = 1.0$, $C_c = 1.0$

**Step 1 — Apply ACI 347 Pressure Equation:**

$$
P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \left[ 7.2 + \frac{785}{T+17.8} + \frac{5746 R}{T+17.8} \right] \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Temperature Term:**

$$
T + 17.8 = 27 + 17.8 = 44.8 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\frac{785}{44.8} = 17.52, \quad \frac{5746 \times 1.5}{44.8} = 192.41
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Bracket:**

$$
P_{max} = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left[ 7.2 + 17.52 + 192.41 \right] \times 10^{-3} = 217.13 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa} = 217.13 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Cap:**

Since $R = 1.5$ m/h < 2.1 m/h, the cap **does not** govern yet. However, ACI 347 also limits $P_{max} \le 0.143 \gamma_c H$ for walls (per some interpretations) or $\le \gamma_c H$ (per other clauses).

$$
\gamma_c H = 24 \times 5.0 = 120 \text{ kPa}
$$

But for $R < 2.1$ m/h, the **143.7 kPa** cap (i.e., 1500 psf) does **not** apply; the **whichever-is-lower** rule uses $\gamma_c H$:

$$
P_{max} = \min(217.13, \; 120) = 120 \text{ kPa}
$$

> **Final Governing Pressure: $P_{max} = 120$ kN/m²**

**Conclusion:** The hydrostatic limit (120 kPa) governs, because the **lower of the rate-based pressure and the hydrostatic pressure** must be used for design.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **materials used for the form face** in architectural concrete. Compare HDO plywood, steel, and GFRC liners in terms of finish quality, reuse, and cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An architectural form panel of **19 mm HDO plywood** is supported on studs at **450 mm c/c**. The design lateral pressure is **80 kN/m²**. If $E = 10{,}500$ N/mm², calculate the **mid-span deflection** and verify the design against the **L/500 architectural limit**.

#### Model Solution (a) — Form Face Materials

| Property | HDO Plywood | Steel Plate | GFRC / GRP Liner |
|----------|------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Material** | Phenolic-resin-overlaid birch ply | Cold-rolled carbon steel | Glass-fibre-reinforced polyester |
| **Surface Finish** | Matte smooth, no grain telegraph | Mirror-smooth, monolithic | Custom 3D textures |
| **Class Achieved** | Class A | Class A (premium) | Class A (textured) |
| **Reuse Cycles** | 50+ | 200+ | 80+ |
| **Cost (Relative)** | 3× | 5× (high initial, low per use) | 4× |
| **Weight** | Light (~12 kg/m²) | Heavy (~47 kg/m² for 6 mm) | Light (~8 kg/m²) |
| **Water Resistance** | Excellent (HDO overlay) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Best For** | General architectural walls, columns | Precast, large exposed surfaces | Sculptural façades, complex reveals |

**Conclusion:** HDO plywood offers the best balance of finish, cost, and reusability for cast-in-situ work. Steel is preferred for precast where reuse economics dominate. GFRC is chosen when 3D custom patterns are required.

#### Model Solution (b) — Deflection Check

**Given:**
- $t = 19$ mm, $L = 450$ mm, $w = 80$ kN/m² = $0.080$ N/mm², $E = 10{,}500$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia (per 1000 mm width):**

$$
I = \frac{1000 \times 19^3}{12} = \frac{1000 \times 6859}{12} = 571{,}583 \text{ mm}^4/\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Deflection Formula:**

$$
\delta = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I} = \frac{5 \times 0.080 \times 450^4}{384 \times 10{,}500 \times 571{,}583}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{5 \times 0.080 \times 4.10 \times 10^{10}}{2.305 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{1.640 \times 10^{10}}{2.305 \times 10^{12}}
$$

$$
\delta = 0.0071 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Allowable Deflection:**

$$
\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$
\delta = 0.0071 \text{ mm} \ll 0.90 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Design is SAFE}
$$

> **Conclusion:** 19 mm HDO plywood at 450 mm stud spacing is more than adequate for an 80 kPa lateral pressure. Stud spacing could be **increased to ~900 mm** before reaching the L/500 limit — useful for optimising formwork cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Hydrostatic Cap** — Many students compute only the ACI 347 rate-based pressure and forget to compare it with $\gamma_c H$. The **lower** of the two governs. **(-2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Confusing L/360 with L/500** — Using structural-grade deflection limits in an architectural concrete problem is a **fatal error** that loses the entire deflection check. **(-3 marks)**
> 3. **Wrong ACI 117 Tolerance** — Quoting structural tolerance (±12 mm) for architectural work. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Ignoring Tie Hole Pattern** — Architectural examiners deduct marks when students discuss ties without mentioning **modular grid layout**.
> 5. **Mixing up $C_w$ and $C_c$ factors** — These are not interchangeable. $C_w$ is for **unit weight** (lightweight = 0.85, normal = 1.0), $C_c$ is for **cement chemistry** (retarders/superplasticisers = 1.2, Type III = 1.2).
> 6. **Units Confusion in $\delta$ formula** — Mixing **N/mm²** with **kN/m²** without conversion is a **3-mark** deduction. Always convert pressure to N/mm² before substituting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Architectural concrete is concrete used as a **finish**, not just a structure. Formwork for it is therefore a **finish-critical system**.
- **Three Classes per ACI 303R:** **A** (no further treatment), **B** (paintable), **C** (covered). Classes A and B require architectural-grade formwork.
- **Tighter Tolerances:** Plane tolerance is **± 6 mm in 3 m** (architectural) vs **± 12 mm in 3 m** (structural).
- **Stricter Deflection:** Architectural formwork: **L/500** minimum, **L/1000** for premium Class A. Structural: **L/360**.
- **Pressure Cap Rule:** $P_{max} = \min(\text{ACI 347 rate-based},\; \gamma_c H)$. **Always apply the cap.**
- **Form Face Material Hierarchy:** HDO plywood > MDO plywood > standard plywood. Steel and GFRC liners are alternatives for premium/reusable work.
- **Form Liners:** ABS, elastomeric, GFRC, EPS, metal — chosen by **texture depth, reuse, and finish class**.
- **Rustication & Reveals:** Built **into the formwork**, not carved into the concrete afterwards.
- **Release Agents:** Use **non-staining, water-based, chemically reactive** agents. **Never** use form oil on Class A surfaces.
- **Tie Pattern:** Modular grid (e.g., 600 × 600 mm), uniform across the entire architectural surface.
- **Joint Watertightness:** Mandatory to prevent cement paste leakage, sand streaks, and honeycombing.
- **Curing & Striking:** Strip formwork only after adequate strength (typically **70%** of $f_{ck}$ for architectural surfaces to avoid edge damage).
- **ACI Codes to Remember:** **ACI 117** (tolerances), **ACI 303R** (architectural concrete), **ACI 347** (formwork design).
- **Cost Note:** Architectural formwork is **3 to 5× costlier** than structural formwork but offsets downstream façade costs.
- **Key Formula Triplet to Memorise:**
  - $P = \gamma_c H$
  - $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$
  - $\delta \le L/500$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembling and Dismantling Formwork
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembling and Dismantling Formwork

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Formwork Assembly** is the systematic, sequenced process of erecting, aligning, bracing, and securing temporary mould components (sheathing, studs, joists, walers, props, and ties) into a rigid, stable, and dimensionally accurate enclosure capable of holding freshly placed plastic concrete in the desired shape until it hardens sufficiently to be self-supporting.

**Formwork Dismantling (also called Stripping or De-shuttering)** is the reverse engineered, time-bound, and structurally critical process of safely removing the temporary mould components once the in-situ concrete has attained the **minimum stripping strength**, without damaging the concrete surface, the formwork components, or compromising site safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Stripping time is **NOT** a guess. It is governed by **IS 456:2000 (Clause 11.3)** and **IS 14687:1999** guidelines, and is directly linked to the **characteristic compressive strength** of concrete at the time of removal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake-Mould" Intuition

Imagine baking a fancy shaped birthday cake. The baker first **assembles** a metal mould (greasing, clamping, sealing joints) — this is *formwork assembly*. After pouring the batter, the baker waits until the cake is firm enough to hold its own weight but not so hard that it sticks permanently. Then the baker carefully **dismantles** the mould piece-by-piece in a planned order — this is *formwork dismantling*.

If you remove the mould too early → the cake collapses (concrete fails under self-weight).  
If you remove it too late → the cake sticks and tears (concrete spalls, formwork gets damaged).  
If you remove panels in the wrong order → the cake sags on one side (concrete deflects/cracks).

**The "cake" must be strong enough, the "mould" must be removed in sequence, and the "baker" (the site engineer) must follow a strict recipe.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The following quantitative benchmarks govern the assembling and dismantling process:

- **Minimum stripping strength of concrete: 2.5 N/mm² (≈ 25 kg/cm²)** for vertical formwork (sides of beams, columns, walls).
- **Minimum stripping strength of concrete: 70% of design characteristic strength** for horizontal formwork (soffit of slabs, beams).
- **Minimum stripping strength for props of slabs spanning > 4.5 m: 100% of design strength.**
- **Standard prop spacing: 0.9 m to 1.2 m** for slabs, depending on slab thickness and prop capacity.
- **Standard striking/re-propping interval: 3 to 7 days** depending on ambient temperature, cement type, and span.

> [!NOTE]
> The unit **N/mm²** is the SI equivalent of **MPa**. Always use N/mm² in KTU answer sheets to align with IS Code conventions.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Carries High KTU Weightage

Module 2 — *Assembling and Dismantling Formwork* — is a **favourite KTU examiner's pick** because it tests:
1. **Procedural Knowledge (Bloom Level 3 — Apply)**: Can the student write the correct sequence?
2. **Safety Awareness (Bloom Level 4 — Analyse)**: Does the student know the safe striking time?
3. **IS Code Familiarity (Bloom Level 1 — Remember)**: Can the student recall clause numbers and stripping strengths?

A 7-mark or 14-mark question on this topic is almost guaranteed in every KTU ESE cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Formwork Assembly

The assembly of any formwork system — whether conventional timber, plywood, or modular MIVAN/aluminium system — follows a strictly codified **bottom-to-top, inside-to-outside** sequence. The logic is governed by structural stability at every intermediate stage.

### 2.1.1 The Six Logical Phases of Assembly

**Phase 1 — Setting Out & Marking (Survey Phase)**
- Transfer grid lines from structural drawings onto the floor slab or foundation.
- Mark prop positions, wall alignments, and column locations using chalk and nail markers.
- Verify with theodolite/total station for multi-storey accuracy.

**Phase 2 — Sole Plates & Base Preparation (Foundation of Formwork)**
- Lay **sole plates** (timber or MS flats) on a clean, levelled floor.
- Sole plates distribute prop load over a larger area, preventing punching failure of the supporting floor.
- Typical sole plate size: **75 mm × 150 mm** timber or **75 mm × 75 mm MS flat**.

**Phase 3 — Props / Vertical Supports Installation**
- Erect adjustable steel props (Acrow props) over sole plates.
- Props are braced laterally using **horizontal and diagonal braces** to form a stable tower.
- Prop spacing is dictated by the **load-bearing capacity of the prop** and the **load from the slab/beam soffit**.

**Phase 4 — Head & Sole Boards of Props**
- Attach **head boards** (or fork heads) at the top of props to receive joists.
- These distribute the load from joists to the prop shaft uniformly.

**Phase 5 — Bearers / Joists & Plywood Sheathing**
- Lay horizontal **bearers (joists)** over head boards.
- Place **sheathing** (12 mm / 18 mm plywood) on top of joists.
- Apply release agent (form oil) on the sheathing surface to facilitate easy stripping.

**Phase 6 — Wall & Column Formwork (Vertical Members)**
- Erect sheathing on both sides of column/wall.
- Install **walers** (horizontal stiffeners) and **strongbacks** (vertical stiffeners).
- Pass **form ties** through pre-drilled holes to resist lateral concrete pressure.
- Install **scaffolding and working platforms** for concreting crew safety.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Formwork Dismantling

Dismantling is a **reverse-engineered, time-controlled** procedure governed by the principle:

> *"Vertical formwork is removed first; horizontal formwork is removed last; props are removed last of all, and only when concrete has gained full design strength."*

### 2.2.1 The Five Logical Phases of Dismantling

**Phase 1 — Pre-Stripping Inspection**
- Verify concrete strength using **cube test results** or **rebound hammer (Schmidt hammer)** readings.
- Check for any honeycombing, surface defects, or unapproved cantilevered loads.
- Confirm stripping time as per IS 456 table (see Section 2.3).

**Phase 2 — Removal of Vertical Formwork (Sides of Beams, Columns, Walls)**
- Begin stripping from the **top and work downward**.
- Loosen walers and ties gradually; never pry with a crowbar against green concrete.
- Wedge panels loose with **wooden wedges** — never use metal levers.

**Phase 3 — Removal of Horizontal Formwork (Soffit Sheathing & Joists)**
- Strip carefully without shock loading.
- Lower sheathing using rope/cable, never drop from height.

**Phase 4 — Prop Removal (Soffit of Slabs & Beams)**
- Props are removed **only after the concrete attains 70% of characteristic strength** (or 100% for spans > 4.5 m, as per IS 456).
- For multi-storey buildings, **re-propping** (re-installing props one or two floors below) is mandatory.

**Phase 5 — Cleaning, Stacking & Storage**
- Clean panels immediately to prevent concrete buildup.
- Stack plywood flat, props vertically, and timber horizontally on level ground.
- Apply a fresh coat of form oil before next reuse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Re-propping** is the practice of re-installing props on the slab below the stripped floor to share the construction load during progressive strength gain. IS 456:2000 (Clause 11.4) mandates this for spans exceeding **4.5 m**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & IS Code Tables

### 2.3.1 Stripping Time Table (As Per IS 456:2000, Clause 11.3)

| Structural Member | Minimum Stripping Time (Days) | Minimum Concrete Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical formwork (sides of columns, beams, walls) | **16 to 24 hours** | 2.5 N/mm² |
| Soffit of slabs (props left under) | **3 days** | 70% of f_ck |
| Soffit of beams (props left under) | **7 days** | 70% of f_ck |
| Removal of props of slabs spanning up to 4.5 m | **7 days** | 70% of f_ck |
| Removal of props of slabs spanning > 4.5 m | **14 days** | 100% of f_ck |
| Removal of props of beams spanning up to 6 m | **14 days** | 100% of f_ck |
| Removal of props of beams spanning > 6 m | **21 days** | 100% of f_ck |

> The values shown above are based on an ambient temperature of **27°C ± 2°C** (tropical Indian conditions). For colder climates, the times must be extended by a factor of **1.5 to 2.0**.

### 2.3.2 Critical KTU Formulas (Lateral Pressure & Prop Load)

| Equation No. | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 2.1 | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot H$ | Maximum lateral pressure on formwork (hydrostatic assumption, kN/m²) |
| 2.2 | $P = 7.5 + \frac{800 R}{T + 30}$ | Modified ACI pressure formula (kN/m²), R = rate of placement (m/h), T = concrete temp (°C) |
| 2.3 | $F_{prop} = w \cdot s_x \cdot s_y$ | Axial load on a single prop (kN), w = slab load (kN/m²), s_x, s_y = spacings (m) |
| 2.4 | $\sigma_c = \frac{F_{prop}}{A_{sole}}$ | Bearing stress on supporting floor (N/mm²) |
| 2.5 | $t_{strip} = k \cdot t_{28}$ | Stripping time (days) as a fraction of 28-day strength (k depends on member type) |
| 2.6 | $I_{eff} = 0.7 \cdot I_{gross}$ | Effective moment of inertia of plywood sheathing for deflection check (mm⁴) |
| 2.7 | $\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{270}$ | Allowable deflection of formwork member (L = span in mm) |

Where:
- $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of fresh concrete = **24 kN/m³** (for plain concrete) and **25 kN/m³** (for RCC)
- $R$ = rate of concrete placement, in m/hour
- $T$ = temperature of concrete in °C at time of placement
- $w$ = total load on slab formwork (self-weight + construction live load) ≈ **5 to 8 kN/m²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Notation Rule:** In the above table, the vertical bars used in the equation for allowable deflection are written using the LaTeX expression `\vert` semantics — never as a raw `|` character inside markdown tables. This is mandatory to preserve table syntax.

### 2.3.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Equation 2.2 (ACI Pressure Formula)** is used by site engineers to **size the walers, ties, and studs** of column/wall formwork. The lower the rate of placement R, the lower the pressure, and the lighter the formwork design can be.
- **Equation 2.3 (Prop Load)** determines the **prop spacing** in slab formwork design. A typical 150 mm thick slab at 5 kN/m² load with 1.0 m × 1.0 m prop spacing generates a 5 kN prop load — well within the 10 kN capacity of a standard Acrow prop.
- **Equation 2.7 (Allowable Deflection L/270)** is the universally accepted formwork deflection limit. It prevents the finished concrete surface from showing undulations and ridges.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Assembly Procedure (For an RCC Slab)

Below is the **exhaustive, exam-ready, step-by-step** procedure that fetches full marks in KTU 14-mark questions. Every step is logically justified.

**Step 1: Reading the Formwork Drawing**
- Refer to the **formwork drawing** (prepared by the formwork design engineer).
- Identify: grid lines, prop spacing, joist size, plywood thickness, prop height, and any special provisions (drops, openings, cantilever portions).

**Step 2: Preparing the Working Floor**
- Clean the supporting floor of debris, water, oil, and loose material.
- Check the **bearing capacity** of the floor for re-propping loads from upper floors.
- Mark prop positions with chalk on the floor in a grid pattern.

**Step 3: Laying Sole Plates**
- Place **sole plates** at every prop location.
- Use timber sole plates of size **75 mm × 150 mm** with the longer side perpendicular to the joist direction.
- Sole plates must rest on a firm, level surface.

**Step 4: Erecting Props**
- Position the **inner tube of the Acrow prop** over the sole plate.
- Adjust the prop to the approximate required height using the prop's outer sleeve.
- The prop must be **plumb (perfectly vertical)** — checked with a spirit level.
- Lateral bracing between adjacent props: install **horizontal members** at 1.5 m vertical intervals and **diagonal braces** every 4th prop.

**Step 5: Fixing Head Boards / Fork Heads**
- Fix the **head board (or U-head fork head)** at the top of each prop.
- The head board must be **rigidly connected** to the prop shaft (typically with a U-clamp or welded lugs) to prevent lateral slippage.

**Step 6: Placing Primary Bearers (Joists)**
- Place **primary joists (timber or aluminium beams)** over the head boards.
- Primary joists are typically **50 mm × 100 mm** timber or **100 mm × 50 mm × 3 mm** MS hollow sections.
- Joist spacing depends on the plywood thickness; for 12 mm plywood, joist spacing is **400 to 450 mm c/c**.

**Step 7: Placing Secondary Bearers (Strongbacks, if any)**
- For long spans, place **secondary bearers** perpendicular to primary joists to reduce the span.

**Step 8: Laying Plywood Sheathing**
- Lay **12 mm or 18 mm thick BWP (Boiling Water Proof) grade plywood** on top of the joists.
- Nail the plywood to joists using **12 mm nails** at 150 mm c/c along the joist edges.
- All plywood joints must be **tight and staggered** — no gap should exceed 2 mm.
- Apply **mould release agent (form oil)** evenly on the top surface.

**Step 9: Side Formwork of Beams (If Applicable)**
- Erect **beam side forms** of the required depth using timber or plywood.
- Brace sides with **props and inclined members** to resist concrete pressure.
- Fix **beam bottom form** between the two side forms.

**Step 10: Edge Formwork & Slab Side Shuttering**
- Erect the **slab edge formwork** at slab perimeter — these are critical to maintain slab thickness and edge alignment.

**Step 11: Final Checks Before Concreting**
- Verify levels using an **auto-level or dumpy level** (tolerance: ± 3 mm in 3 m).
- Check plumb of props.
- Check dimensions of openings and inserts.
- Apply a **final coat of form oil** if needed.
- **Get the formwork inspection checklist signed** by the site engineer and project manager before placing concrete.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Dismantling Procedure (For an RCC Slab)

**Step 1: Review Stripping Time**
- Refer to the **cube test register** to confirm concrete has achieved the required stripping strength.
- For slabs up to 4.5 m span: minimum **7 days**.
- For slabs > 4.5 m span: minimum **14 days** at **100% of f_ck**.

**Step 2: Visual Inspection of Concrete**
- Inspect soffit for any sign of **honeycombing, cracking, bleeding, or deflection**.
- Use a **rebound hammer** for non-destructive strength verification.

**Step 3: Remove Scaffolding & Working Platforms**
- Clear the floor of all loose materials, tools, and labour (except the stripping crew).
- Set up a **safety perimeter** with warning tape and signage.

**Step 4: Loosen Props Gradually**
- Lower the **prop height adjustment sleeve** by **2 to 3 turns only** — do not fully release at once.
- This allows the soffit to settle uniformly and prevents shock loading on the concrete.

**Step 5: Remove Plywood Sheathing**
- Starting from one corner, gently **tap the joints with a wooden mallet** to release the plywood.
- **Never use a metal crowbar** against the concrete surface.
- Lower the plywood with the help of ropes — never allow it to fall.

**Step 6: Remove Joists & Head Boards**
- Lift out joists and head boards carefully.
- Sort members by size for inventory and reuse.

**Step 7: Remove Props**
- After all horizontal formwork is removed, props are removed **one by one** in a planned sequence.
- The standard sequence is: **mid-span props removed first, edge props last**.
- This avoids any sudden load redistribution that could cause cracking.

**Step 8: Re-prop the Floor Below**
- For multi-storey construction, install **re-props** on the floor immediately below the stripped floor.
- Re-prop spacing: typically **2.0 m to 3.0 m c/c** depending on design.
- Re-props are kept in place for a minimum of **28 days** from the date of concrete casting.

**Step 9: Cleaning & Storage of Formwork**
- Scrape off concrete residue from plywood using a **wooden scraper** (not metal — metal damages the surface).
- Re-oil the plywood and stack it **flat and dry**.
- Store props and accessories in a covered, dry area to prevent rusting.

**Step 10: Documentation**
- Record the **date and time of stripping** in the site register.
- Note any **concrete surface defects** observed after stripping.
- Update the **formwork reuse register** with the number of pours completed per panel.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Strike What

| Member Type | Vertical Faces | Soffit Slab (≤ 4.5 m) | Soffit Slab (> 4.5 m) | Soffit Beam (≤ 6 m) | Soffit Beam (> 6 m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strip when concrete age ≥ | 24 hours | 7 days | 14 days | 14 days | 21 days |
| Strength required at stripping | 2.5 N/mm² | 70% f_ck | 100% f_ck | 100% f_ck | 100% f_ck |
| Re-propping required? | No | Yes (if span > 4.5 m) | Yes (mandatory) | Yes (mandatory) | Yes (mandatory) |
| Sequence | Top to bottom | Centre outwards | Centre outwards | Centre outwards | Centre outwards |
| Method of loosening | Wedges | Sleeve turn | Sleeve turn | Sleeve turn | Sleeve turn |

> [!NOTE]
> **Centre-outwards** means that the prop/panel located **nearest to the mid-span is removed first**, followed progressively by props towards the supports. This minimises the cantilever moment and prevents the soffit from sagging.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Prop Load Calculation

**Problem:**
A 150 mm thick RCC slab is to be supported by Acrow props spaced at 1.2 m × 1.2 m. The unit weight of RCC is 25 kN/m³. Construction live load = 1.5 kN/m². Determine the axial load on a single prop and the bearing stress on the supporting floor (assume sole plate size 150 mm × 150 mm).

**Solution:**

$$w_{slab} = t \cdot \gamma_c = 0.150 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$w_{total} = w_{slab} + w_{live} = 3.75 + 1.5 = 5.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$F_{prop} = w_{total} \times s_x \times s_y = 5.25 \times 1.2 \times 1.2$$

$$\boxed{F_{prop} = 7.56 \text{ kN}}$$

$$A_{sole} = 0.150 \times 0.150 = 0.0225 \text{ m}^2 = 22500 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_c = \frac{F_{prop}}{A_{sole}} = \frac{7560}{22500} = 0.336 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

This is well within the permissible bearing stress of **5 N/mm²** for hardened concrete, so the sole plate size is safe.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Assembly Sequence Flowchart

    A[Start: Read Formwork Drawing] --> B[Prepare Working Floor and Mark Grid]
    B --> C[Lay Sole Plates at Marked Positions]

    D --> E[Install Lateral Bracing Between Props]
    E --> F[Fix Head Boards and Fork Heads]
    F --> G[Lay Primary Joists on Head Boards]
    G --> H[Lay Plywood Sheathing on Joists]
    H --> I[Fix Beam Side and Bottom Forms]
    I --> J[Fix Slab Edge Formwork]
    J --> K[Apply Mould Release Agent]
    K --> L[Final Inspection by Site Engineer]

    M --> N[End of Assembly Phase]
```

```

> The above Mermaid diagrams are **block-level functional representations** designed for the KTU answer script. A physical cross-section drawing with dimensions is also acceptable, but block diagrams are faster to draw and score equally well in KTU's marking scheme.
