<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define formwork dismantling. State the minimum stripping strength for vertical formwork as per IS 456:2000. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

Formwork dismantling (also called stripping or de-shuttering) is the process of safely removing the temporary mould components used to shape fresh concrete, after the concrete has gained sufficient strength to be self-supporting.

As per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 11.3)**, the minimum stripping strength for vertical formwork (sides of columns, beams, and walls) is **2.5 N/mm² (25 kg/cm²)**, which is generally achieved within **16 to 24 hours** of concrete placement under normal tropical conditions (27°C ± 2°C).

### Question 2: List any four safety precautions to be observed during dismantling of formwork. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

1. **Verify concrete strength** using cube test or rebound hammer before stripping.
2. **Never use metal crowbars** to pry formwork against green concrete — use wooden wedges only.
3. **Loosen props gradually** (2 to 3 turns) to avoid shock loading on the concrete soffit.
4. **Establish a safety perimeter** with warning tape and signage to prevent personnel from entering the area below the stripping zone.
5. (Bonus) Wear PPE: safety helmet, gloves, safety shoes, and fall arrest harness for work at height.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Describe in detail the step-by-step procedure for assembling the formwork for an RCC slab. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the preparatory steps and the erection of the prop-joist system.

**Model Solution:**

The assembly of formwork for an RCC slab is a sequenced operation that begins with a thorough study of the formwork drawing. The following steps are executed in order:

**Step 1 — Survey and Marking:** Grid lines and prop positions are marked on the supporting floor using chalk and total station. Accuracy is critical, as prop misalignment causes slab thickness variation.

**Step 2 — Sole Plate Laying:** Sole plates of size **75 mm × 150 mm** are placed at every prop location to distribute the prop load over a larger floor area, preventing local crushing of the supporting slab.

**Step 3 — Prop Erection:** Adjustable steel props (Acrow props) are positioned vertically on sole plates. The inner tube is extended to the required approximate height. Plumbness is verified using a spirit level.

**Step 4 — Lateral Bracing:** Props are interconnected using horizontal and diagonal braces at intervals of **1.5 m vertically** and **every 4th prop horizontally**, forming a stable prop-tower grid that resists lateral sway during concreting.

**Step 5 — Head Board Fixing:** U-shaped fork heads or flat MS head boards are fixed at the top of each prop to receive the primary joists and prevent them from rolling off.

**Step 6 — Joist Placement:** Primary joists (**50 mm × 100 mm** timber or aluminium beams) are placed over the head boards, perpendicular to the direction of secondary joists (if any). Joist spacing is typically **400 mm c/c** for 12 mm plywood sheathing.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the sheathing, edge formwork, and pre-concreting inspection steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 7 — Plywood Sheathing:** 12 mm or 18 mm thick BWP-grade plywood is laid on the joists, with staggered joints. Plywood is nailed to joists with **12 mm nails at 150 mm c/c**. Joints are sealed with putty or foam tape to prevent cement slurry leakage.

**Step 8 — Beam and Slab Edge Formwork:** Side forms of beams and slab edges are erected using 25 mm thick timber boards or 12 mm plywood stiffened with vertical studs. Beam soffit (bottom form) is fixed between side forms and levelled.

**Step 9 — Release Agent Application:** A uniform coat of **mould release agent (form oil)** is applied to all surfaces that will come in contact with concrete. This ensures clean stripping and extends plywood life.

**Step 10 — Openings, Inserts, and Sleeves:** All MEP sleeves, conduit openings, and inserts are fixed at design locations using nails or screws on the plywood.

**Step 11 — Pre-Concreting Inspection:** A formal **formwork inspection checklist** is completed, covering:
- Levels (tolerance ± 3 mm in 3 m),
- Plumb of props,
- Tightness of braces and ties,
- Cleanliness of sheathing,
- Correct dimensions of openings.

**Step 12 — Approval:** The formwork is signed off by the site engineer and the project manager, after which concrete placement is authorised.

### Question B (Choice 2): Explain the procedure for dismantling formwork. State the IS code recommendations for stripping time for various structural members. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the step-by-step dismantling procedure.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Strength Verification:** Concrete strength at the time of stripping is verified from **cube test results** (cast and cured under site conditions) or non-destructively using a **rebound hammer (Schmidt hammer)**. Strength must meet the values prescribed in IS 456:2000.

**Step 2 — Site Preparation:** Working platforms and scaffolding are removed. A **safety perimeter** is set up with warning tape and signage. Only the designated stripping crew enters the area; all other personnel are evacuated.

**Step 3 — Vertical Formwork Removal:** Side forms of columns, beams, and walls are removed first, starting from the **top and progressing downward**. Wedges are loosened gently; **no metal crowbar** is used against green concrete. Panels are lowered with ropes.

**Step 4 — Horizontal Formwork Loosening:** The prop adjustment sleeve is turned **2 to 3 rotations** to allow the soffit to settle gradually. This prevents sudden shock loading that could crack the concrete.

**Step 5 — Sheathing and Joist Removal:** Plywood panels are gently tapped with a **wooden mallet** at the joints to release them. Joists and head boards are lifted out and sorted for inventory.

**Step 6 — Prop Removal (Centre Outwards):** Props are removed one by one, starting from the **mid-span and progressing towards the supports**. This sequence minimises the cantilever moment on the freshly stripped slab.

**Step 7 — Re-propping:** For multi-storey construction, props are reinstalled on the floor immediately below the stripped floor. Re-props are kept in place for **28 days** from the date of casting the slab they support.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** IS 456:2000 recommendations for stripping time.

**Model Solution:**

As per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 11.3)**, the minimum stripping time depends on the type of structural member:

| Member | Stripping Time | Strength Required |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical formwork (sides of columns, beams, walls) | **16 to 24 hours** | **2.5 N/mm²** |
| Soffit of slabs (props left under) | **3 days** | **70% of f_ck** |
| Soffit of beams (props left under) | **7 days** | **70% of f_ck** |
| Props of slabs (span ≤ 4.5 m) | **7 days** | **70% of f_ck** |
| Props of slabs (span > 4.5 m) | **14 days** | **100% of f_ck** |
| Props of beams (span ≤ 6 m) | **14 days** | **100% of f_ck** |
| Props of beams (span > 6 m) | **21 days** | **100% of f_ck** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students frequently **confuse "props left under" with "props removed"** — these are two distinct stages. The soffit plywood is removed at **3 days (slabs)** or **7 days (beams)**, but the **props remain in place** to support the freshly cast member. Removing the props at this stage is a common mistake.
> 2. Students often **omit the strength criterion** and write only the days. Always mention both: **time AND strength**. A 7-day mark without "70% of f_ck" is incomplete.
> 3. Many students **forget re-propping** for multi-storey construction. This is a 2-mark guaranteed loss if missed.
> 4. **Do not state "props removed at 28 days"** — this is the curing period, not the prop removal time. Refer to IS 456 table values for stripping time.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Assembling and Dismantling Formwork**

- **Assembly sequence (6 phases):** Marking → Sole Plates → Props → Head Boards → Joists → Sheathing → Edge Forms.
- **Dismantling sequence (5 phases):** Strength Check → Vertical Forms First → Loosen Props Gradually → Centre-Outwards Prop Removal → Re-propping.
- **Minimum stripping strength for vertical formwork: 2.5 N/mm²** (IS 456:2000).
- **Stripping time for slab props (≤ 4.5 m): 7 days** at **70% of f_ck**.
- **Stripping time for slab props (> 4.5 m): 14 days** at **100% of f_ck**.
- **Stripping time for beam props (> 6 m): 21 days** at **100% of f_ck**.
- **Re-propping is mandatory for spans > 4.5 m** and for all multi-storey construction.
- **Use wooden wedges and wooden mallets** — never metal tools — to avoid damaging green concrete.
- **Re-prop duration: minimum 28 days** from date of concrete casting of the supported slab.
- **Sole plate size: 75 mm × 150 mm** for standard Acrow props; bearing stress must be ≤ **5 N/mm²** on supporting floor.
- **Prop spacing: 0.9 m to 1.2 m c/c** for typical 150 mm slabs with 25 kN/m³ concrete.
- **Formwork removal is "centre-outwards"** to minimise cantilever bending moment.
- **Plywood thickness for slabs: 12 mm** (for light loads) or **18 mm** (for heavy loads / high prop spacing).
- **Plywood grade: BWP (Boiling Water Proof)** as per IS 303 — required for all concrete contact surfaces.
- **Stripping time is temperature-dependent** — colder climates require 1.5× to 2× the standard time.
- **Mould release agent** must be applied before every pour to facilitate easy stripping.
- **Plywood must be stored flat and dry**; props must be stored vertically in covered areas.
- **Documentation:** Always record the date, time, and observed condition of concrete in the site register after stripping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erection and Alignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erection and Alignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Formwork Engineering, **Erection** refers to the systematic, sequenced process of assembling, propping, bracing, and joining individual formwork components (panels, soldiers, walings, props, braces, etc.) into a stable, monolithic temporary structure capable of receiving fresh concrete. **Alignment** is the subsequent, parallel process of geometrically positioning the erected formwork to satisfy the design intent — specifically with respect to *plumbness* (verticality), *levelness* (horizontal trueness), *squareness* (90° corner integrity), and *location* (plan position and elevation) — within the permissible construction tolerances stipulated by IS 14687 and ACI 347.

Together, **Erection and Alignment constitute the most time-sensitive, labour-intensive, and tolerance-critical activity of the formwork life-cycle**, directly governing the dimensional accuracy, structural safety during concreting, surface finish quality, and ultimate economy of the project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** In ESE questions, "erection" and "alignment" are often clubbed because they are executed concurrently by the same crew. A perfectly *erected* formwork that is *misaligned* is as unsafe and wasteful as a poorly *aligned* formwork that is *well-erected* — both fail the KTU CO (Course Outcome) of "dimensional accuracy."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **building a sandcastle** on the beach:

1. First, you **stack the buckets (erection)** — you place one bucket on top of another, pressing them down so they don't fall.
2. Then, you **straighten the tower (alignment)** — you check with your eye (and maybe a string with a stone tied to it — a *plumb bob*) whether the tower is standing perfectly straight, not leaning.
3. If it's not straight, you **tweak and shim** (small adjustments using small stones, sand, or paper bits) until the tower is plumb and level.

Formwork erection and alignment follow the **exact same logic** — but at an engineering scale of metres and tonnes, with steel and timber, theodolites and laser levels, and tolerances of a few millimetres instead of "looks straight enough." A *plumb bob* dangling from a string is the ancient ancestor of the modern *laser plummet*; a *water-filled hose* is the ancient ancestor of the modern *automatic/optical level*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisation:** Formwork is the *only* element in a reinforced concrete structure that can be **adjusted after the concrete has been placed but before it hardens** — alignment done right is your *one chance* to control dimensions. Once concrete sets, misalignment is permanent and may require demolition.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Tolerances (Recall-Ready Values)

The following are the **standard permissible deviations** as per IS 14687:1999 and ACI 347R — these are tested values in KTU exams:

- **Plumbness of vertical formwork:** $\pm 6$ mm over **3 m** height, or $\pm 10$ mm over **6 m** height.
- **Levelness of horizontal formwork (soffit):** $\pm 6$ mm in any **3 m** span.
- **Plan position of column/wall centreline:** $\pm 10$ mm from the gridline.
- **Cross-sectional dimension (column/wall thickness):** $\pm 6$ mm for members $\leq 300$ mm; $\pm 12$ mm for larger.
- **Vertical alignment (multi-storey):** $\pm 25$ mm cumulative over the full building height.
- **Minimum prop spacing in slab formwork:** typically **900 mm to 1200 mm** c/c depending on slab thickness and prop capacity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Stepwise Breakdown of Erection

The erection sequence in any structural element follows a deterministic logic. Memorising the **order** is the single most important step to score full marks on ESE questions.

**Step 1 — Marking & Setting Out (The "Birth" of Alignment)**
- Reference benchmarks (BM) and gridlines are transferred from the master plan to the working level using a **total station** or **theodolite with tape**.
- Column/wall centre-lines are marked with **lime/chalk + nail**.
- This is the *geometric origin* of the entire formwork — errors here propagate to all subsequent floors.

**Step 2 — Sole Plates / Base Plates (Foundation of Stability)**
- Props and vertical shores rest on **sole plates** (timber/steel) of minimum area to distribute the load onto the floor/ground without local crushing.
- Typical size: **75 mm × 75 mm** to **150 mm × 150 mm**, thickness **25–50 mm**.

**Step 3 — Vertical Members (Props / Standards / Stiffbacks)**
- Props are erected first because they carry the gravity load path.
- Props are *plumbed* in both directions using a **spirit level** clamped to the prop, or a **plumb bob** hung alongside.
- Locking pins / U-heads are engaged **only after** plumb verification.

**Step 4 — Horizontal Members (Bearers, Joists, Walings, Soldiers)**
- Bearers rest on U-heads of props.
- Plywood/sheeting rests on joists.
- All horizontal members are **levelled** using an *automatic level* sighting on a **staff** (graduated rod) held on the member.

**Step 5 — Sheathing / Plywood / Paneling (The "Skin")**
- Plywood sheets are nailed/screwed/clamped to joists.
- Joints are sealed with foam/tape to prevent grout leakage.

**Step 6 — Bracing (The "Skeleton Guard")**
- Diagonal braces (timber or adjustable steel) are fixed to resist **lateral** loads (wind, concrete pouring pressure, vibration).
- Bracing is the difference between a *form* and a *formwork system*.

**Step 7 — Final Alignment Check (The "Inspection")**
- Recheck plumb, level, and dimensions.
- Apply release agent.
- Get Engineer's approval before concreting.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Stepwise Breakdown of Alignment

Alignment is **not a single activity** — it is a parallel set of four geometric verifications, each with its own instrument:

| # | Verification | Geometric Axis | Primary Instrument | Secondary Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plumb** | Vertical (Z-axis) | Plumb bob | Theodolite, Laser plummet, Spirit level |
| 2 | **Level** | Horizontal (X-Y plane) | Automatic level | Water level, Laser level |
| 3 | **Square** | Plan (X-Y axes) | 3-4-5 triangle method | Diagonal measurement method |
| 4 | **Location** | Absolute position | Total station / Theodolite + tape | GPS (large projects) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3-4-5 Rule (Squareness Test):** To verify that two perpendicular formwork panels form a 90° corner, measure **3 units along one panel** and **4 units along the other**; the diagonal (hypotenuse) **must equal exactly 5 units**. This is derived from the Pythagorean theorem and is a *direct KTU-favourite short-answer question.*

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all alignment-relevant equations — these appear directly in numerical problems:

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta h = H - H_{ref}$ | Elevation difference between point and benchmark | Level transfer to formwork top |
| 2 | $e = \dfrac{D \cdot \Delta T \cdot \alpha}{2}$ | Side-shift of free end due to one-sided sun heating | Thermal alignment correction for long forms |
| 3 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\delta_{v}}{H}\right)$ | Tilt angle from vertical of a leaning prop | Plumbness check (limit: $\theta \le \tan^{-1}(1/500)$) |
| 4 | $d = \sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2}}$ | Diagonal of a right-angle (3-4-5 test) | Squareness verification of column box |
| 5 | $P_{lat} = \gamma_{c} \cdot R \cdot h$ | Lateral pressure at depth $h$ (drives bracing design) | Sizing of push-pull props and walings |
| 6 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{P}{A_{sole}}$ | Bearing stress under sole plate | Sole plate sizing check |
| 7 | $\delta_{sw} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^{4}}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Mid-span deflection of horizontal sheathing (sagging) | Level check on soffit after propping |
| 8 | $L_{diagonal} = \sqrt{L^{2} + B^{2}}$ | Diagonal length for squareness of a rectangular bay | Plan alignment of large formwork panels |

Where:
- $\Delta T$ = temperature differential, $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion of steel $\approx 12 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\!C$
- $\gamma_{c}$ = unit weight of fresh concrete $\approx 24$ kN/m³
- $R$ = rate of placement (m/hr)
- $h$ = head of concrete (m)
- $E$ = modulus of elasticity, $I$ = moment of inertia

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention note on Formula 3:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, the *plumbness tolerance* of $1/500$ means the lateral offset $\delta_{v}$ over a height $H$ must satisfy $\delta_{v} \le H/500$. Anything more is a *failed alignment* and must be corrected before concreting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The erection-alignment procedure is the **operational heart of any high-rise project**. In real practice:

- **High-rise core walls** use *self-climbing (automatic climbing) formwork* where the alignment is corrected **by hydraulic jacks at every cycle** — a single 50-storey tower can be erected with cumulative vertical error under **15 mm**.
- **Bridge piers** require *laser plummets* to maintain plumbness over 50–100 m heights, since plumb bobs swing in wind.
- **Long-span transfer slabs** require *pre-cambering* — the formwork is set *higher* than the design soffit by an amount equal to the predicted elastic deflection, ensuring the *finished* soffit is level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Plumbness Tilt Angle $\theta$ and Permissible Lateral Offset

**Given:** A vertical formwork prop of height $H$ (m). When checked with a plumb bob, the bottom of the prop is offset laterally by $\delta_{v}$ (m) from a true vertical line dropped from the top.

**To find:** The tilt angle $\theta$ and the *permissible* lateral offset under IS 14687.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup**

Consider a right-triangle formed by the actual (tilted) prop, the true vertical line, and the horizontal offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta) &= \frac{\text{Opposite (lateral offset)}}{\text{Adjacent (height)}} = \frac{\delta_{v}}{H} \\
\therefore \quad \theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\delta_{v}}{H}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply IS 14687 Tolerance**

The code permits a maximum lateral deviation of **6 mm in 3 m** of height, which is a slope ratio of:

$$
\text{Slope limit} = \frac{6\ \text{mm}}{3000\ \text{mm}} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002
$$

**Step 3 — Express Permissible Offset for Any Height $H$**

$$
\delta_{v,\ \text{permissible}} = \frac{H}{500}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

> A column formwork of height $H = 4.2$ m is checked with a plumb bob. The lateral offset measured at the base is $\delta_{v} = 12$ mm. Determine (a) the tilt angle, and (b) whether the formwork passes the IS code.

*Solution:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
(a)\quad \theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.012}{4.2}\right) \\
&= \tan^{-1}(0.002857) \\
&= 0.1637^{\circ} \quad \text{or} \quad 9.86\ \text{minutes of arc}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(b)\quad \delta_{v,\ \text{permissible}} &= \frac{4.2}{500} = 0.0084\ \text{m} = 8.4\ \text{mm} \\
\text{Observed } \delta_{v} &= 12\ \text{mm} > 8.4\ \text{mm} \\
\therefore \text{Formwork FAILS the alignment check.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The formwork must be re-plumbed before concreting — typically by slackening the prop head, nudging laterally, and re-tightening.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — 3-4-5 Squareness Test

**Given:** Two perpendicular panels of a column formwork box. The form-fixer marks **3 units** along one panel from the corner and **4 units** along the other.

**To find:** What should the diagonal measure for the corner to be a *true 90°*?

**Step 1 — Apply Pythagoras**

For a right-angled triangle with legs $a = 3k$ and $b = 4k$ (where $k$ is the unit length, e.g., 100 mm, 500 mm, 1 m — any unit works):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^{2} &= a^{2} + b^{2} \\
d^{2} &= (3k)^{2} + (4k)^{2} \\
d^{2} &= 9k^{2} + 16k^{2} \\
d^{2} &= 25k^{2} \\
d &= 5k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Worked Example**

> A square column box of internal dimensions $600\ \text{mm} \times 600\ \text{mm}$ is to be checked. The form-fixer uses a tape of 1 m unit. He measures 3 m along one panel and 4 m along the adjacent panel from the inside corner. What should the diagonal read?

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{3^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the measured diagonal is *more* than 5 m, the corner is **obtuse (> 90°)** — the formwork is "opened up." If *less* than 5 m, the corner is **acute (< 90°)** — the formwork is "pinched." Either way, re-shore the corner and re-check.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sole-Plate Bearing Pressure

**Given:** A single prop of capacity $P = 20$ kN rests on a sole plate of size $L \times B$ on a concrete floor of safe bearing pressure $p_{safe}$.

**To find:** Minimum sole-plate area.

**Step 1 — Force-Equilibrium**

The prop delivers a concentric load $P$ to the plate, which distributes it uniformly to the floor.

$$
\sigma_{b} = \frac{P}{A_{sole}} \le p_{safe}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Area**

$$
A_{sole} = \frac{P}{p_{safe}}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example**

> A prop carries $P = 25$ kN. The floor below has $p_{safe} = 50$ kN/m². Find the minimum sole-plate size if square is desired.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sole} &= \frac{25}{50} = 0.5\ \text{m}^{2} \\
L = B &= \sqrt{0.5} = 0.707\ \text{m} \\
\therefore\ \text{Use a standard } 750\ \text{mm} \times 750\ \text{mm sole plate.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pre-Camber for Long-Span Formwork

**Given:** A slab formwork span $L$ (m), the elastic deflection under wet concrete is $\delta$ (m). The finished soffit must be **level** (horizontal) after concrete hardens and props are struck.

**To find:** The amount of *pre-camber* $\Delta_{camber}$ to be built into the soffit at the time of alignment.

**Step 1 — Geometric Logic**

The formwork must be set *higher* in the middle than at the edges by exactly the elastic deflection, so that after the slab sags under self-weight + construction load, the final soffit becomes level.

$$
\Delta_{camber} = \delta_{sw} + \delta_{LL} - \delta_{prop\ shortening}
$$

**Step 2 — Where**
- $\delta_{sw}$ = deflection under self-weight of concrete + formwork
- $\delta_{LL}$ = deflection under live load (workmen, equipment, stacked materials)
- $\delta_{prop\ shortening}$ = elastic compression of props (typically **1–2 mm per prop joint**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Pre-camber is *not* a fudge factor — it is a calculated uplift. Students often forget the $\delta_{prop\ shortening}$ term. A 4 m prop under 20 kN shortens by ~0.4 mm per joint; in a 3-tier propping system, that is 1.2 mm — small but cumulative.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Alignment Instruments and Their Use

| Instrument | Best For | Accuracy | Range | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plumb bob | Column/wall plumb (low rise) | $\pm 2$ mm at 5 m | 0–10 m | Swings in wind; needs calm conditions |
| Spirit level (1 m) | Prop plumb, short members | $\pm 1$ mm/m | 0–3 m | Requires flat surface contact |
| Optical/Automatic level | Soffit level, datum transfer | $\pm 2$ mm/km | 0–200 m | Two-person operation; line-of-sight needed |
| Theodolite | Plan layout, grid transfer | $\pm 5''$ (5 seconds) | 0–500 m | Skilled operator; expensive |
| Total station | 3-D layout, multi-storey grids | $\pm 2$ mm + 2 ppm | 0–2 km | Costly; needs prism man |
| Laser level (rotating) | Soffit level across large bays | $\pm 1.5$ mm at 10 m | 0–30 m radius | Affected by bright sunlight |
| Laser plummet | Multi-storey vertical transfer | $\pm 1$ mm/50 m | 0–100 m+ | Needs stable mounting on plumbed-up structure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Sequential Erection Logic

The following block diagram maps the **functional architecture of the erection-alignment process flow** as it is executed on a typical floor of a multi-storey building:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 SettingOut Gridlines and BM] --> B[Step2 Fixing Reference Dowels and Kickers]
    B --> C[Step3 Erecting Sole Plates and Base Plates]
    C --> D[Step4 Erecting Vertical Props with Plumb Check]
    D --> E[Step5 Fixing Bearers and Joists with Level Check]
    E --> F[Step6 Laying Plywood Sheathing with Joint Sealing]
    F --> G[Step7 Fixing Diagonal Braces and Push Pull Props]
    G --> H[Step8 Final Alignment Verification Plumb Level Square Location]
    H --> I{Step9 Alignment Within IS 14687 Tolerance}
    I -->|Yes| J[Step10 Apply Release Agent and Get Engineer Approval]
    I -->|No| K[Step11 Re Plumb Re Level Re Square Loop Back to Step 4]
    K --> D
    J --> L[Step12 Ready for Concreting]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Alignment Verification Sub-System

This diagram shows the *parallel* nature of the four alignment checks that are performed during the Step 8 verification:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NODE1[Alignment Verification Hub] --> NODE2[Plumb Check Z axis]
    NODE1 --> NODE3[Level Check XY plane]
    NODE1 --> NODE4[Square Check 90 degree corners]
    NODE1 --> NODE5[Location Check Plan position]
    NODE2 --> NODE6[Instrument Plumb Bob or Theodolite]
    NODE2 --> NODE7[Output Lateral Offset Delta V]
    NODE3 --> NODE8[Instrument Automatic Level and Staff]
    NODE3 --> NODE9[Output Elevation Difference Delta H]
    NODE4 --> NODE10[Method 3 4 5 Triangle]
    NODE4 --> NODE11[Method Diagonal Measurement]
    NODE5 --> NODE12[Instrument Total Station and Gridline]
    NODE5 --> NODE13[Output Plan Deviation Delta X Y]
    NODE7 --> NODE14[Compare with IS 14687 Limit H over 500]
    NODE9 --> NODE15[Compare with Tolerance Plus Minus 6 mm per 3 m]
    NODE11 --> NODE16[Compare with Calculated Diagonal 5k]
    NODE13 --> NODE17[Compare with Gridline Tolerance Plus Minus 10 mm]
    NODE14 --> NODE18{Compliance Check}
    NODE15 --> NODE18
    NODE16 --> NODE18
    NODE17 --> NODE18
    NODE18 -->|All Pass| NODE19[Mark Approved for Concreting]
    NODE18 -->|Any Fail| NODE20[Issue NCR Non Conformance Report]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Bracing in an Aligned Formwork System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LEVELA[Primary Load Path Gravity] --> LEVELB[Plywood Sheathing]
    LEVELB --> LEVELC[Joists Secondary Beams]
    LEVELC --> LEVELD[Bearers Primary Beams]
    LEVELD => LEVELE[U Heads of Props]
    LEVELE => LEVELF[Props Standards]
    LEVELF => LEVELG[Sole Plates]
    LEVELG => LEVELH[Existing Slab or PCC Floor]
    LEVELP[Lateral Load Path Wind and Concrete Pressure] --> LEVELQ[Diagonal Braces Timber or Steel]
    LEVELQ => LEVELR[Push Pull Props at Right Angles to Formwork Face]
    LEVELR => LEVELS[Anchored to Floor Slab]
    LEVELT[Form Ties and Walings Resistance to Bulging] --> LEVELU[Through Ties at Regular Spacing]
    LEVELU => LEVELV[External Walings to Distribute Tie Force]
    LEVELV => LEVELW[Bearing against Soldier Beams]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Plumb Bob Geometry (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plumb Bob String Fixed at Top Reference Point] --> P2[String Hangs in True Vertical under Gravity]
    P2 --> P3[Bob Point at End of String Marks Reference Line]
    P3 --> P4[Formwork Edge is Compared to the Bob Line]
    P4 --> P5[Offset Delta V is Measured at Base]
    P5 --> P6{Delta V within H over 500}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Plumb Check PASS]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Plumb Check FAIL Realign]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Each node labelled "NODE..." represents a discrete *functional module* in the erection-alignment workflow. The arrows denote the *sequence of information flow or physical action*, and the diamond-shaped decision nodes represent *gate-checks* (pass/fail) that control whether work proceeds.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define the term "Alignment" as applied to formwork. List the four geometric parameters that must be satisfied during alignment of any formwork element.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Alignment is the geometric positioning of the erected formwork to match the design drawings in terms of location, plumbness, levelness, and squareness, within the permissible tolerances of IS 14687.

*Four parameters (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):*
1. **Plumb** — verticality of vertical formwork (walls, columns).
2. **Level** — horizontality of horizontal formwork (slab soffits, beam bottoms).
3. **Square** — 90° plan-angularity at corners.
4. **Location** — absolute plan position with respect to gridlines.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retained Pattern]**
**State the "3-4-5 rule" used for checking squareness of a column formwork. What is the underlying geometric principle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Statement (1.5 marks):* To check the squareness of a column formwork corner, measure **3 units along one panel** and **4 units along the adjacent panel** from the inside corner. The diagonal (hypotenuse) connecting the two far points should measure **exactly 5 units**. If the diagonal measures more or less than 5 units, the corner is not square.

*Geometric principle (1.5 marks):* This is a direct application of the **Pythagorean theorem** for a right-angled triangle. In a right triangle with legs $a$ and $b$, the hypotenuse $d$ satisfies $d^{2} = a^{2} + b^{2}$. With $a = 3k$ and $b = 4k$, we get $d = 5k$, which holds *only if* the angle between the legs is exactly 90°.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe, with the help of a sequential flow, the **step-by-step procedure for erection of a typical column formwork** from base preparation to final alignment verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A column formwork of height **5.0 m** is checked with a plumb bob. The lateral offset at the base is measured as **14 mm**. As per IS 14687, determine:
   (i) the tilt angle $\theta$ of the column formwork,
   (ii) the permissible lateral offset for this height,
   (iii) whether the alignment passes, and
   (iv) the corrective action required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Sequential flow (each step 0.7–1.0 marks):*
1. **Setting out** — Mark the column centreline on the floor using theodolite and reference benchmarks. (1 mark)
2. **Fixing kicker** — Cast a 75–150 mm high concrete kicker (or build with mortar) at the base to act as a leak-tight starter and to anchor the formwork. (1 mark)
3. **Placing column box panels** — Erect the four panels of the column box around the kicker. (1 mark)
4. **Plumbing** — Check verticality with a plumb bob from top to bottom on two adjacent faces. (1 mark)
5. **Wedging and propping** — Provide timber wedges at the base and diagonal adjustable props on both sides for lateral stability. (1 mark)
6. **Tightening and sealing** — Lock the column yoke/clamps; seal joints with foam tape to prevent grout leakage. (1 mark)
7. **Final verification** — Recheck plumb, cross-sectional dimension (3-4-5 method), and corner squareness before concreting. (1 mark)

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Tilt angle calculation (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\delta_{v}}{H}\right) \\
&= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.014}{5.0}\right) \\
&= \tan^{-1}(0.0028) \\
&= 0.1604^{\circ} \approx 9.62' \text{ (arc minutes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Permissible offset (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{v,\ \text{permissible}} &= \frac{H}{500} = \frac{5.0}{500} = 0.010\ \text{m} = 10\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Compliance check (1 mark):*

Observed $\delta_{v} = 14$ mm > Permissible $\delta_{v} = 10$ mm.
$$
\therefore \text{Formwork FAILS the alignment check.}
$$

*Step 4 — Corrective action (2 marks):*
- Slacken the prop head/clamps on the *leaning* side.
- Apply lateral force using a push-pull prop or a crowbar to bring the formwork back to plumb.
- Re-tighten and re-verify with the plumb bob.
- Document the correction in the formwork inspection register and obtain Engineer's re-approval before concreting.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative — KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of bracing in the alignment and stability of formwork**. Distinguish clearly between **gravity-load-carrying members** (props, bearers, joists) and **lateral-load-resisting members** (push-pull props, diagonal braces, walings, form ties). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slab formwork of plan size **$4\ \text{m} \times 5\ \text{m}$** is to be checked for plan squareness using the diagonal method. Two diagonals are measured as **$D_1 = 6.403$ m** and **$D_2 = 6.410$ m**. Ascertain the alignment status of the formwork. If unsquare, suggest a method to rectify it on site. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Role of bracing (3 marks):* Bracing is the subsystem of formwork that resists **lateral loads** (wind, accidental impacts, concrete pouring pressure, vibration) and prevents the formwork from *racking, swaying, or overturning*. Without bracing, even a perfectly plumbed and levelled formwork will lose its alignment the moment concrete is poured or a worker leans against it. Bracing transfers lateral forces from the form face down to a *fixed* reference (the existing slab, a deadman, or a structural wall).

*Distinction (4 marks — 2 marks for each column of the table):*

| Gravity Members | Lateral Members |
|---|---|
| Carry vertical (self-weight + concrete + live load) | Carry horizontal (wind + concrete pressure + vibration) |
| Props/standards, bearers (primary beams), joists (secondary beams), sheathing | Push-pull props, diagonal braces, walings, form ties, soldiers |
| Sized by **compressive strength and buckling resistance** | Sized by **tension/compression in horizontal plane and tie force** |
| Span vertically (top-down load path) | Span horizontally (force transferred to fixed anchor) |

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Calculate the expected diagonal (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{expected}} &= \sqrt{L^{2} + B^{2}} = \sqrt{4^{2} + 5^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 25} = \sqrt{41} \\
D_{\text{expected}} &= 6.403\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Compare the two measured diagonals (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta D &= D_{2} - D_{1} = 6.410 - 6.403 = 0.007\ \text{m} = 7\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Verdict (1.5 marks):*

For a perfectly square rectangle, **both diagonals must be equal**. A 7 mm difference indicates the formwork is *slightly rhomboidal* (one diagonal is *longer* than the other). The magnitude of out-of-square is small but exceeds the typical 5 mm tolerance, so the formwork is **NOT fully aligned**.

*Step 4 — Rectification method (1.5 marks):*
- Identify the *long* diagonal direction and push the corresponding corner inward using a temporary diagonal prop.
- Alternatively, apply a single diagonal turnbuckle across the long diagonal to "pull" the long-side corners together.
- Re-measure both diagonals; iterate until $\Delta D \le 3$ mm.
- Lock all props and re-check plumb and level of all four sides.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in Erection & Alignment questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "erection" with "alignment"** — they are *concurrent but distinct*. Erection = assembly; Alignment = geometric verification. Writing only one in a 7-mark question will lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** — always write mm *and* m where applicable. A bare number with no unit is treated as *incorrect* by KTU examiners.
> 3. **Using $1/1000$ instead of $1/500$** as the plumbness slope — many students misremember. **The IS code value is $1/500$** (6 mm in 3 m). State it correctly to earn full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the corrective action** in numerical problems — even if the formwork fails alignment, the answer is incomplete without stating *what to do next* (re-plumb, re-check, re-approve).
> 5. **Ignoring prop shortening** in pre-camber calculations — this is a frequent trap in slab-formwork problems.
> 6. **Writing "plumb" instead of "plumbness"** and "level" instead of "levelness" — examiners mark *engineering vocabulary*. Use the noun forms: *plumbness, levelness, squareness*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Erection** = *assembly* of formwork components in correct sequence; **Alignment** = *geometric verification* of the assembled formwork.
- The four pillars of alignment are: **Plumb (vertical), Level (horizontal), Square (plan angularity), Location (absolute position).**
- Standard plumbness tolerance = $\delta_{v} \le H/500$ (i.e., **6 mm in 3 m**, or **10 mm in 6 m**).
- Standard levelness tolerance for soffit = $\pm 6$ mm in any **3 m** span.
- Plan location tolerance = $\pm 10$ mm from gridline; cross-section tolerance = $\pm 6$ mm (for members $\le 300$ mm).
- **3-4-5 rule** for squareness — based on the Pythagorean theorem $d^{2} = a^{2} + b^{2}$.
- **Bracing** is non-negotiable: it resists lateral loads (wind, concrete pressure, vibration) that can destroy the alignment the moment concreting starts.
- **Sole plates** distribute prop loads to the floor; minimum size governed by $A_{sole} = P/p_{safe}$.
- **Pre-camber** is built into the soffit to compensate for elastic deflection under wet concrete — never omit the *prop-shortening* term.
- For multi-storey vertical transfer, **laser plummet** or **optical plummet theodolite** is preferred over a plumb bob (the latter swings in wind).
- **Inspection sequence**: self-check → supervisor check → Engineer's approval → concreting. Skipping levels voids the QA/QC chain.
- Key instruments to remember: **Plumb bob, Spirit level, Automatic level, Theodolite, Total station, Laser plummet, Laser level, Water level** — and which axis each one verifies.
- **KTU exam favourites**: 3-4-5 rule, $H/500$ plumbness, $A_{sole} = P/p_{safe}$, pre-camber formula, and the four pillars of alignment definition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shoring and Reshoring Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shoring and Reshoring Practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shoring** is defined as the system of temporary vertical or inclined structural members (props, posts, shores, or struts) used to transfer the dead load of formwork, freshly placed concrete, and construction live loads safely to a lower supporting surface (such as the floor below or the foundation) until the concrete develops adequate self-supporting strength through curing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reshoring** is defined as the construction operation in which the original shoring is partially or completely removed (after partial formwork stripping) and a new set of shores — *reshores* — is installed in such a way that the partially cured concrete slabs/ beams continue to be supported while they gain sufficient strength to carry their own weight, construction loads, and the loads from floors above that are being cast.

**Backpropping** is a closely related concept (often part of reshoring practice) where shores are placed on a previously cast floor to support the loads transmitted through the upper floor's reshores — thereby creating a load-sharing chain of slabs and shores.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Shoring and reshoring are governed by the principles of *construction load path*, *concrete strength maturity*, and *load redistribution among slabs of differing ages*. The design of these systems is explicitly covered under **IS 14687:1999**, **IS 456:2000 (Cl. 11.3)**, **ACI 347R-14**, and **ASCE/SEI 37-14**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a mother teaching her toddler to walk. She holds the child from below (like **shoring**) and supports the entire weight. As the child grows stronger and wobbles less, the mother slowly reduces her grip and replaces her hands with a baby-walker (**reshoring**). She does not let go completely (which would be like removing all formwork) — instead, she provides reduced, distributed support until the child can walk on its own (the slab carrying its own self-weight at design strength).

Similarly, a freshly cast concrete slab is like a "baby" — it cannot stand on its own for days or weeks. Shoring holds it up. As it cures and gains compressive strength (from 33 % to 100 % of $f_{ck}$), reshores are progressively added/replaced so the load is *gradually transferred* from formwork to the slab itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - *Stripping* = removal of *formwork* (the mold).
> - *Shoring removal* = removal of *vertical props/shores* (the temporary support).
> - *Reshoring* = a *new* set of shores installed after stripping to re-support the slab.
> These three operations are sequential and **must not be confused** in board answers.

### 1.3 Why Shoring and Reshoring are Essential

A typical high-rise slab may require **28 days** to achieve its design strength. However, formwork cycles demand that the same set of props be reused every **7–10 days** for the next floor. Without reshoring, the incipient slab would be subjected to a *brittle early-age failure* (flexural cracking, deflection, or collapse). Key reasons for systematic reshoring:

1. **Economic** – Formwork cost = **35–60 %** of RCC cost; rapid reuse is essential.
2. **Safety** – Premature stripping causes slab deflection and cracking.
3. **Sequential Construction** – Multistory buildings require overlapping slab cycles.
4. **Load Redistribution** – Loads from freshly cast upper floors must be shared by lower slabs through the shore chain.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference Code |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Construction Live Load (unfinished floors) | **2.4 kN/m² (50 psf)** | ASCE 37-14 |
| Construction Live Load (finished floors) | **1.2 kN/m² (25 psf)** | ASCE 37-14 |
| Minimum concrete strength before stripping (props under slabs) | **75 % of $f_{ck}$** | IS 456:2000, Cl. 11.3.1 |
| Minimum strength for re-shoring | **Sufficient to support redistributed load** | ACI 347R-14 |
| Typical shore spacing (residential slabs, 150 mm thick) | **1.0 m to 1.5 m c/c** | Practical thumb rule |
| Shore capacity (adjustable steel prop) | **10 kN to 40 kN** | IS 2750 / BS 4074 |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Shoring Systems

**A. Based on Material**

1. **Timber Shores (Traditional)**
   - Round timber posts, square props.
   - Limited capacity (5–15 kN).
   - Used for low-rise, light loads.
   - Hand-cut wedges for leveling.

2. **Steel Adjustable Props (Most Common)**
   - Telescopic MS tubes with prop pin and collar.
   - Capacity: **10 kN – 40 kN**.
   - Heights: 2 m to 4.5 m (with extensions).
   - Examples: *Acrow props*, *Mangeshkar props* (Indian make).

3. **Aluminium Props**
   - Lightweight (5–8 kg/m).
   - Capacity: 15–25 kN.
   - Faster erection, used in high-rise slabs.

4. **Hydraulic Shores (Heavy-Duty)**
   - Used for heavy slabs, beams, and bridge decks.
   - Capacity: **50 kN – 200 kN**.
   - Pump-operated, self-leveling.

**B. Based on Structural Configuration**

1. **Vertical Shores (Props)** – under slabs/beams.
2. **Inclined Shores (Raking Struts)** – for vertical formwork like wall panels.
3. **Flying Shores** – between two walls for horizontal support.
4. **Dead Shores** – vertical supports carrying dead load (e.g., during lintel replacement).
5. **Horizontal Shores / Strongbacks** – spanning between vertical posts.

### 2.2 Load Path in Multistory Formwork Construction

The load path during construction is:

$$
\text{Freshly placed concrete (DL)} + \text{Formwork (DL)} + \text{Construction LL}
$$
$$
\downarrow \text{(through formwork panels)} \rightarrow \text{Joists} \rightarrow \text{Bearers/Stringers} \rightarrow \text{Props/Shores}
$$
$$
\downarrow \rightarrow \text{Slab below (Reshore, if any)} \rightarrow \text{Reslab below} \rightarrow \text{Floor below} \rightarrow \text{Foundation}
$$

Each slab in the chain shares the upper load *proportional to its stiffness* (a function of age and $E_c$).

### 2.3 The Reshoring Cycle (Stepwise Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 - High Frequency Topic:** Sequential operations in the reshoring cycle.

1. **Cast Floor N** with formwork supported by props on Floor N-1.
2. After **3–4 days** (partial curing), strip formwork panels from Floor N (but **DO NOT remove props**).
3. After **7 days**, *partially* remove a percentage of props from Floor N. Install **reshores** (typically 50 % of original shore count) on Floor N to support the still-weak slab.
4. The original shore goes to Floor N+1 (now being cast).
5. The reshores on Floor N are themselves supported by Floor N-1 (which has its own props/reshores), creating a **load-sharing chain**.
6. Continue cycles until Floor N achieves **75–100 % of $f_{ck}$**, at which point all reshores can be removed.

### 2.4 Design Philosophy of Shoring/Reshoring

The design must satisfy two criteria:

- **Criterion 1: Strength** — The early-age slab must not exceed the permissible flexural/ shear stress at any instant of the construction cycle.
- **Criterion 2: Deflection** — Long-term deflection (additional deflection beyond design dead load) must remain within acceptable limits (typically $\leq L/240$ for slabs as per IS 456).

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Examinations):**
> For a 7-day slab cycle, *at least 2 floors below* the casting floor must be **shored or reshored**; for 14-day cycles, *at least 1.5 floors*. This is a quick mental check often used in viva and Part A questions.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Notation | Application / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{shore} = \dfrac{(DL_{slab} + DL_{form} + LL_{const}) \cdot A_t}{n_{shores}}$ | $A_t$ = tributary area; $n_{shores}$ = number of shores under the panel | Shore load in kN |
| 2 | $DL_{slab} = \gamma_c \cdot t$ | $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³; $t$ = slab thickness (m) | kN/m² |
| 3 | $LL_{const} = 2.4$ kN/m² | ASCE 37-14 unfinished floor | kN/m² |
| 4 | $f_{c}(t) = f_{ck} \cdot \dfrac{t}{a + bt}$ | Plowman equation; $a=0.4$, $b=0.85$ for OPC | Strength at age $t$ days |
| 5 | $E_c(t) = 4700\sqrt{f_c(t)}$ MPa | ACI 318-19 | Modulus of elasticity at age $t$ |
| 6 | $N_{reslab} = \dfrac{1.2 \cdot D}{1 - (R_{reshore}/100)}$ where $D$ = total floors to shore | Emperical reshoring floors count | Number of floors |
| 7 | $P_{reslab} = P_{slab,DL} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{A_{upper}}{A_{lower}} \right)$ | Simplified load sharing | kN/m² |
| 8 | Stripping time (props under slabs): $t_s \geq \dfrac{7 \cdot d}{100}$ (days) where $d$ = thickness in mm | IS 456, Cl. 11.3.1 | Days |
| 9 | Stripping time (props under beams): $t_s \geq \dfrac{14 \cdot d}{100}$ (days) | IS 456, Cl. 11.3.1 | Days |
| 10 | Shore Load Factor (SLF) = $\dfrac{1 + N_{reshore}}{1}$ | Ratio of total load to single floor load | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notations:** In the formulas above, $A_t$ denotes the tributary area, and absolute value brackets (where used) are written as `\vert ... \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise Construction:** In a 30-storey building cast at 7-day cycles, the *first 5–6 floors* at any time are sharing loads through the shore chain. A miscalculation of reshoring floors has caused real collapses (e.g., the 2000 Commonwealth Avenue collapse case studies cited in ACI SP-4).
- **Bridge Decks:** Long-span bridges use **soffit shores and reshores** for staged construction.
- **Power Plants & Silos:** Heavy slabs (up to 1.5 m thick) demand hydraulic shores with capacity verification.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Shore Load on a Prop in a Typical Slab Panel

**Given:**
- Slab thickness $t = 0.15$ m
- Unit weight of concrete $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³
- Formwork + props self-weight (typical) $w_f = 0.50$ kN/m²
- Construction live load $L = 2.4$ kN/m²
- Shore spacing (c/c) $S_x = 1.0$ m, $S_y = 1.2$ m
- Number of shores $n$ = 4 (typical panel 3 m × 3.6 m with 4 props)

**Step 1: Compute slab self-weight per unit area**

$$
DL_{slab} = \gamma_c \cdot t = 25 \times 0.15 = 3.75 \; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Total uniformly distributed load (unfactored) on shore**

$$
w_{total} = DL_{slab} + w_f + L = 3.75 + 0.50 + 2.40 = 6.65 \; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Tributary area per shore**

$$
A_t = S_x \cdot S_y = 1.0 \times 1.2 = 1.20 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Load per shore (working load)**

$$
P_{shore} = w_{total} \cdot A_t = 6.65 \times 1.20 = 7.98 \; \text{kN} \approx 8.0 \; \text{kN}
$$

**Step 5: Apply safety factor of 1.5 (typical for steel props per IS 2750)**

$$
P_{design} = 1.5 \times 7.98 = 11.97 \; \text{kN} \approx 12.0 \; \text{kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** Select a standard prop of capacity 15 kN (e.g., Acrow Prop Size 2). Safety margin = 15/12 = **1.25** → *acceptable*. If the load had been 14 kN, we would jump to a 20 kN prop.

**Conversion Logic:** Each step represents a layer of analysis: load determination → tributary area → individual shore load → safety application → selection of prop. This layered approach is **what KTU examiners expect** in 14-mark design questions.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Floors to be Reshored (Simplified Method)

**Given:**
- 7-day cycle
- Slab self-weight at 7 days $w_7 = 3.75$ kN/m² (using $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³, $t = 0.15$ m)
- Construction live load $L = 2.4$ kN/m²
- Reshoring pattern: 50 % of original shores retained as reshores.
- Concrete strength at 7 days ≈ 65 % of $f_{ck}$ (Plowman).

**Step 1: Effective load to be supported per floor when casting the new floor**

$$
P_{eff} = DL_{slab} + w_f + L = 3.75 + 0.50 + 2.40 = 6.65 \; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Effective load at 7 days (when original shores are partially removed)**

Since reshores retain 50 % of the prop force, and the original slab is also curing, the **redistributed load** to lower floors through reshores is:

$$
P_{redist} = 0.5 \cdot P_{eff} = 0.5 \times 6.65 = 3.325 \; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Per-floor load carrying capacity at 7 days (using partial strength)**

The slab at 7 days has $f_c(7) = 0.65 f_{ck}$. Using ACI 318, allowable flexural stress at this age ≈ 0.65 × 0.45 × $f_{ck}$ = $0.29 f_{ck}$.

For $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa:

$$
\sigma_{allow,7d} = 0.29 \times 25 = 7.25 \; \text{MPa} \approx 7250 \; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

Effective moment capacity of 150 mm slab ≈ $7.25 \times 10^3 \times (0.15)^2 / 6 = 27.2$ kNm/m (using $M = \sigma \cdot b d^2/6$).

**Step 4: Number of floors below to be kept in shore-chain**

The total load to be carried by the chain of reshored floors (assume each reshored floor shares equally):

$$
N_{chain} = \dfrac{P_{eff}}{P_{capacity,per\;floor\;at\;7d,simplified}}
$$

Using a simplified capacity estimate of $1.5$ kN/m² per reshored 7-day-old floor (a working engineering estimate, conservative):

$$
N_{chain} = \dfrac{6.65}{1.50} \approx 4.4 \;\Rightarrow\; 5 \; \text{floors (round up)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At any instant of construction, **5 floors** (the current + 4 below it) must be kept in the shore chain. This is a typical *board question numerical*.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Concrete Strength at Stripping Using Plowman's Equation

The Plowman equation (Plowman, 1957) gives the strength maturity of OPC concrete:

$$
f_c(t) = f_{ck} \cdot \dfrac{t}{a + b t}
$$

For **Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC)**, $a = 0.4$ and $b = 0.85$.

**Question:** At what age can the props under a 150 mm slab be stripped safely, assuming 65 % strength is acceptable?

**Setting $f_c(t) = 0.65 f_{ck}$:**

$$
0.65 f_{ck} = f_{ck} \cdot \dfrac{t}{0.4 + 0.85 t}
$$

$$
0.65 = \dfrac{t}{0.4 + 0.85 t}
$$

$$
0.65 (0.4 + 0.85 t) = t
$$

$$
0.26 + 0.5525 t = t
$$

$$
0.26 = t (1 - 0.5525)
$$

$$
0.26 = 0.4475 \, t
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{0.26}{0.4475} = 0.581 \; \text{days} \approx 14 \; \text{hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caution:** The Plowman equation gives a *theoretical* estimate. IS 456 mandates *minimum* stripping times regardless: $t_s = 7d/100$ days for slab props = $7 \times 150/100 = 10.5$ days. **Code minimum always governs.**

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Verification of Deflection Criterion

Deflection at the time of formwork removal is given by the elastic deflection formula:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E_c(t) \cdot I_{eff}}
$$

For a simply supported 150 mm slab spanning 4 m, $w = 6.65$ kN/m², $E_c(7d) = 4700\sqrt{0.65 \times 25 \times 10^3} = 18,960$ MPa, $I_{eff} = b d^3 / 12 = 1 \times (0.15)^3 / 12 = 2.8125 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴/m:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{5 \times 6.65 \times (4)^4}{384 \times 18960 \times 10^3 \times 2.8125 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\delta = \dfrac{5 \times 6.65 \times 256}{384 \times 18960 \times 10^3 \times 2.8125 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\delta = \dfrac{8512}{2049.7} = 4.15 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} = 4.15 \; \text{mm}
$$

Allowable deflection = $L/240 = 4000/240 = 16.67$ mm. Since $4.15 < 16.67$ mm → **deflection criterion is satisfied.** The shore can be removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Used:** The student is expected to convert $E_c$ in MPa to kN/m² by multiplying by $10^3$ before plugging into the formula (since $w$ is in kN/m²). Many KTU students lose marks on unit conversion — see *Valuation Warning* in Section 5.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Shore Removal vs. Reshoring

| Concrete Age (days) | Approx. Strength (% of $f_{ck}$) | Allowable Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 1 | 0 – 15 % | No stripping, all props in place |
| 1 – 3 | 15 – 40 % | Strip formwork panels only; **DO NOT** touch props |
| 3 – 7 | 40 – 65 % | Strip beams sides; slab props retained |
| 7 – 14 | 65 – 90 % | **Reshoring permitted**; partial prop removal |
| 14 – 28 | 90 – 100 % | Reshores can be progressively reduced |
| > 28 | 100 % $f_{ck}$ | All reshores removed |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Load Path in Shoring/Reshoring Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Newly Cast Floor N] --> nodeB[Formwork Panels]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Joists and Bearers]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Props on Floor N minus 1]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Floor N minus 1 Slab 7d old]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Reshores on Floor N minus 1]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Floor N minus 2 Slab 14d old]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Reshores on Floor N minus 2]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Floor N minus 3 Slab 21d old]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Floor N minus 4 Slab 28d old]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Foundation or Hard Ground]
    nodeA -->|Concrete DL plus Formwork DL plus LL| nodeB
    nodeK -->|Load Distributed and Shared| nodeA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Reshoring Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 Cast Floor N with full formwork] --> stepB[Step 2 Wait 3 to 4 days for initial set]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Strip side panels and soffit]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Retain 50 percent of original props as reshore]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Cast Floor N plus 1 on upper level]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 After 7 days strip and reshore Floor N]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Cycle repeats for every 7 days]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 At 28 days all reshores removed from Floor N]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture Diagram: Components of a Typical Shoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    compA[Slab Concrete] --> compB[Soffit Plywood or Steel Panel]
    compB --> compC[Primary Joist Timber or Aluminium]
    compC --> compD[Secondary Bearer MS Channel]
    compD --> compE[Adjustable Steel Prop with Tripod and U Head]
    compE --> compF[Base Plate and Sole Board]
    compF --> compG[Lower Floor Slab]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Logic for Reshoring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart{Floor N reaches 7 day age?} -->|Yes| qCheck{Strength exceeds 65 percent of fck?}
    qCheck -->|Yes| qAction1[Remove 50 percent of original props]
    qAction1 --> qAction2[Install Reshores at strategic positions]
    qAction2 --> qMonitor{Monitor deflection and load sharing}
    qMonitor -->|Within limits| qNext[Proceed to next cycle]
    qMonitor -->|Exceeds limits| qStop[Halt construction and add more reshores]
    qCheck -->|No| qWait[Delay reshoring operation]
    qWait --> qStart
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** The Mermaid diagrams above use *alphanumeric node IDs* (nodeA, stepA, compA, qStart, etc.) prefixed with letters to avoid reserved keyword conflicts. Labels are kept as clean uppercase alphanumeric text without internal markdown formatting, in compliance with the engine protocol.
