## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List any six pre-pour inspection checks for a column formwork system.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**
1. **Material verification** — Check plywood/steel for warping, cracks, and damage.
2. **Dimensional check** — Verify column cross-section matches drawings within ±6 mm.
3. **Plumb and alignment** — Check verticality using plumb bob or theodolite (tolerance 1:500).
4. **Prop and bracing inspection** — Verify props are vertical, locked, and base-seated.
5. **Joint sealing** — Inspect all panel joints for gaps (>2 mm must be sealed).
6. **Connection hardware** — Check wedge clamps, ties, and bolts for proper engagement.
7. **Cleanliness** — Ensure formwork is free from debris, sawdust, and oil stains.
8. **Release agent** — Verify uniform application of form oil or release agent.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define 'stripping time' and state the minimum stripping strength for (i) sides of columns and (ii) soffit of slabs (span > 4.5 m).**

**Model Answer:**

**Stripping Time:** Stripping time is the minimum period that must elapse after concrete placement before the formwork can be safely removed without damaging the concrete or compromising structural integrity.

**Minimum Stripping Strengths (per IS 456:2000 and IS 14687):**

(i) **Sides of columns, beams, and walls:** Minimum compressive strength of **3.5 MPa** (or **35 kg/cm²**), typically achieved in **16 to 24 hours** at 27°C ambient temperature.

(ii) **Soffit of slabs (span > 4.5 m):** Minimum compressive strength of **15 MPa**, typically achieved in **14 days** at 27°C. The props are retained even after stripping the soffit shuttering, with re-propping as required.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q3. (a)** Explain in detail the **three-phase inspection framework** for formwork systems. List the key checks performed in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **preventive maintenance procedures** for steel and plywood formwork systems. How does proper maintenance impact the **life cycle cost** of formwork? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a):**

The three-phase inspection framework is a structured approach to quality assurance in formwork operations:

**Phase 1 — Pre-Pour Inspection (BEFORE Concrete Placement):**

This is the most critical phase and must be completed before the engineer-in-charge issues the "Pour Clearance Certificate."

Key checks include:
- **Material inspection** for defects (warping, rust, dents, delamination).
- **Dimensional verification** against structural drawings with tolerance checks (IS 14687 specifies ±6 mm for line and level in general work).
- **Plumb and alignment** using plumb bob, spirit level, or laser.
- **Bracing and prop inspection** — verify all supports are vertical, locked, and seated on firm base.
- **Joint and sealing check** — seal any gap > 2 mm with form tape or sealant.
- **Connection hardware** — check wedges, ties, bolts, and pins for proper engagement.
- **Cleanliness** — ensure no debris, water, or oil contamination.
- **Embedment verification** — rebar, sleeves, inserts, blockouts correctly positioned.
- **Release agent** applied uniformly.

**Phase 2 — During-Pour Monitoring (DURING Concrete Placement):**

Real-time monitoring during concrete pour to detect any distress signals.

Key checks include:
- **Pour rate monitoring** — ensure rate does not exceed design assumption (typically 1–2 m/hr).
- **Deflection monitoring** — use dial gauges at critical points; allowable limit is **L/270**.
- **Lateral movement** — check for any bulging or shifting; set up tell-tales.
- **Leakage monitoring** — detect grout seepage at joints.
- **Vibration control** — ensure vibrators do not contact formwork; avoid external vibration beyond design.
- **Prop load monitoring** — using load cells in critical applications.

**Phase 3 — Post-Pour Inspection (AT and AFTER Stripping):**

Performed at and after formwork removal to verify concrete quality and formwork reusability.

Key checks include:
- **Stripping time verification** — ensure minimum strength criteria are met (per IS 456).
- **Concrete surface inspection** — check for honeycombing, blowholes, staining, and dimensional accuracy.
- **Formwork damage audit** — assess stripped components for edge damage, surface damage, permanent deformation, and connection damage.
- **Deflection audit** — measure actual deflection of concrete element against design (per IS 456: L/250 for beams, L/350 for slabs).
- **Documentation** — update the Formwork Register with usage count and condition.

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b):**

**Preventive Maintenance for Steel Formwork:**

1. **Cleaning after every use** — Remove adhered concrete with wooden scrapers (never metal on contact surfaces); use wire brush for stubborn areas; pressure-wash if needed.
2. **Surface inspection** — Check for dents, scratches, weld cracks, and rust spots.
3. **Anti-corrosion treatment** — Apply anti-rust paint or protective oil; repaint damaged areas.
4. **Straightening** — Re-align any bent members using hydraulic jacks; minor dents can be hammered out.
5. **Weld repair** — Re-weld any cracked joints by certified welders.
6. **Lubrication** — Grease all wedge slots, hinges, and adjustable threads.
7. **Storage** — Stack vertically in dry, covered racks to prevent warping.
8. **Documentation** — Maintain usage log; track reuse count; reject components after 50–100 uses or if deformed beyond tolerance.

**Preventive Maintenance for Plywood Formwork:**

1. **Cleaning** — Wooden scrapers for adhered concrete; avoid metal tools that scratch the phenolic coating.
2. **Edge inspection** — Check for delamination at panel edges; apply edge sealant if needed.
3. **Surface inspection** — Check for scratches, punctures, water damage, and coating wear.
4. **Storage** — Stack flat in dry, covered areas; never store on edge; protect from rain and direct sun.
5. **Edge-sealing** — Apply waterproof sealant at panel edges before first use and after each cycle.
6. **Repair** — Patch small holes with epoxy putty; replace damaged panels entirely if delaminated.
7. **Rotation** — Rotate panels in use to equalize wear.

**Impact on Life Cycle Cost (LCC):**

Proper maintenance dramatically impacts the **economic viability** of formwork:

- **Steel forms:** Well-maintained → 80–100 reuses; poorly maintained → 20–30 reuses.
- **Plywood forms:** Well-maintained → 12–15 reuses; poorly maintained → 3–5 reuses.
- **Timber forms:** Well-maintained → 5–8 reuses; poorly maintained → 1–2 reuses.

**Cost-Benefit Example:**
- Cost of steel formwork per m²: ₹1500
- Cost of cleaning, oiling, and minor repair per cycle: ₹50
- With maintenance: 80 reuses → cost per use = (1500/80) + 50 = ₹68.75/m²/cycle
- Without maintenance: 25 reuses → cost per use = (1500/25) + 50 = ₹110/m²/cycle
- **Savings: ~37.5% per cycle**

For a project requiring 10,000 m² of formwork, savings can exceed **₹40 lakhs** over the project duration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**Q4. (a)** What are the **common defects observed in formwork** during inspection? Classify them by severity and explain the **corrective actions** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **safety aspects of formwork inspection** with reference to relevant IS codes and OSHA guidelines. Discuss the **role of the formwork designer vs. site engineer** in ensuring inspection compliance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q4(a):**

Common formwork defects and their corrective actions are classified by severity:

**Category 1: CRITICAL Defects (Immediate Stop-Work Required)**

| Defect | Description | Corrective Action |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Formwork collapse/partial failure** | Visible deformation or collapse during pour | Evacuate area, stabilize, re-design, full re-inspection |
| **Severe bulging of wall/column forms** | Outward displacement > 10 mm | Stop pour, add emergency external bracing, offload concrete |
| **Prop buckling or instability** | Props showing signs of buckling, bending, or sliding | Stop work, replace prop, re-check prop capacity |
| **Major joint failure with grout loss** | Joints opening up, large grout leakage | Stop pour, plug leak, re-seal joint |

**Category 2: HIGH-Severity Defects (Pause and Rectify On-Site)**

| Defect | Description | Corrective Action |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Minor bulging (5–10 mm)** | Local outward displacement | Add tie rods or external bracing at affected zone |
| **Wedge clamp slippage** | Wedge not holding under load | Replace wedge, inspect slot for wear |
| **Prop deflection > 10 mm** | Excessive vertical deflection | Add additional prop, redistribute load |
| **Plywood delamination (large area)** | Surface layer separating | Replace panel; do not attempt repair |

**Category 3: MEDIUM-Severity Defects (Continue with Monitoring, Rectify Later)**

| Defect | Description | Corrective Action |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Honeycombing on concrete surface** | Coarse aggregate exposed | Patch with mortar; investigate vibration technique |
| **Formwork surface staining** | Concrete surface shows oil or release agent marks | Adjust release agent quantity; clean formwork better |
| **Tie-rod holes leaking** | Small grout seepage through tie holes | Apply seal; check tie-rod rubber washer |
| **Surface blowholes (pinholes)** | Small surface voids | Fill with grout; improve formwork release agent |

**Category 4: LOW-Severity Defects (Document and Schedule Repair)**

| Defect | Description | Corrective Action |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Minor plywood edge wear** | Slight damage at panel edges | Re-apply edge sealant |
| **Surface rust on steel forms** | Light oxidation | Wire-brush, apply anti-rust paint |
| **Scratches on formwork surface** | Cosmetic damage | No immediate action; monitor in next use |
| **Bolt/nut loosening** | Minor loosening of connection | Re-tighten; apply thread-locker if recurring |

**Inspection Strategy:**
- All critical and high-severity defects must be rectified **before** the pour.
- Medium-severity defects are documented and rectified **at stripping**.
- Low-severity defects are addressed during the next **preventive maintenance cycle**.

---

**Model Solution for Q4(b):**

**Safety Aspects of Formwork Inspection:**

Formwork inspection is fundamentally a **safety-critical activity** governed by national and international standards.

**Relevant IS Codes:**

1. **IS 14687:1999** — *Guidelines for Falsework and Formwork* (Most important KTU reference)
2. **IS 456:2000** — *Plain and Reinforced Concrete — Code of Practice* (for stripping times and deflection limits)
3. **IS 2751:1979** — *Recommended Code of Practice for Safety and Health in Construction*
4. **IS 3696 (Part 1 & 2)** — *Safety Code for Scaffolds and Ladders*
5. **IS 4014 (Part 1 & 2)** — *Code of Practice for Scaffolding and Formwork*

**OSHA Key Guidelines (for international context):**

- **29 CFR 1926.703** — Requirements for formwork and shoring construction.
- All shoring equipment must be inspected **before, during, and after** concrete placement.
- Inspections must be performed by a **competent person**.
- Damaged or weakened shoring must **not be used**.
- Shoring must not be removed until **concrete has gained adequate strength**, as determined by the engineer.

**Key Safety Inspection Requirements:**

1. **Pre-pour safety check** — All connections, props, ties, and bracing must be verified by a competent person before clearance.
2. **Exclusion zone** — Establish a safety zone around the formwork during pour; restrict access to authorized personnel only.
3. **PPE compliance** — Inspectors must wear proper PPE (hard hat, safety boots, harness for high work).
4. **Documentation** — All inspections must be **documented** with date, time, inspector name, findings, and corrective actions.
5. **Continuous monitoring** — During-pour inspection must be **continuous** for critical elements.
6. **Stripping safety** — Stripping sequence must be planned to prevent sudden load transfer.

**Role of the Formwork Designer vs. Site Engineer:**

| Aspect | Formwork Designer | Site Engineer |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Formwork design** | Responsible for design calculations, drawings, and specifications | Implements the design on site |
| **Inspection checklist** | Provides the inspection checklist and acceptance criteria | Executes the inspection using the checklist |
| **Load assumptions** | Specifies pour rate, temperature, vibration method | Ensures site conditions match design assumptions |
| **Defect tolerance** | Defines acceptable tolerances (e.g., ±6 mm) | Measures and documents actual tolerances |
| **Non-conformance** | Reviews and approves corrective actions for non-conformances | Identifies and reports non-conformances |
| **Stripping schedule** | Specifies minimum strength criteria | Verifies strength via cube tests or rebound hammer |
| **Accountability** | Design responsibility (errors in design) | Construction responsibility (deviations from design) |

**Legal and Ethical Responsibility:**

- Both designer and site engineer carry **joint and several liability** for formwork safety.
- In case of failure, **investigation** typically reveals that systematic inspection lapses — not just one oversight — are the root cause.
- KTU-trained engineers are expected to follow **IS 14687** protocols rigorously.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing inspection with maintenance:** Many students treat these as one topic. The examiner awards full marks **only** when you clearly distinguish: **Inspection = assessment** (what to look for and how), **Maintenance = action** (preventive and corrective measures).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting IS Code references:** Always cite **IS 14687, IS 456, and ACI 347** where applicable. Vague answers without code references lose **2–3 marks** in Part B.
> 
> 3. **Missing the three-phase structure:** Students who discuss only "pre-pour" inspection miss the **during-pour and post-pour** phases — losing up to **5 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 
> 4. **No mention of documentation:** A "Formwork Register" or "Inspection Checklist" is expected. Answers without this lose **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Confusing stripping time with curing time:** Curing is **moisture/temperature control**; stripping is **formwork removal**. Do not interchange these.
> 
> 6. **Numerical questions on lateral pressure or prop load** require **full derivation** with units. Skipping units costs **0.5–1 mark** per answer.
> 
> 7. **Safety aspects are mandatory:** Any discussion of formwork inspection without mentioning **OSHA/IS safety codes** is considered incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inspection and Maintenance of Formwork Systems**

**Core Concepts to Remember:**

- ☐ **Three-phase inspection framework** = Pre-pour, During-pour, Post-pour.
- ☐ **Pre-pour inspection** uses mnemonic **"M-D-A-B-J-C-E"** = Material, Dimensions, Alignment, Bracing, Joints, Connections, Embedments.
- ☐ **Dimensional tolerances** (IS 14687) = ±6 mm for line, ±6 mm for level, +6 mm/–3 mm for cross-section.
- ☐ **Plumb tolerance** for columns = 1:500 of height.
- ☐ **Maximum joint gap** before sealing = 2 mm.
- ☐ **Allowable deflection** during pour = L/270 (or L/360 for high-quality finish).
- ☐ **Lateral pressure** uses ACI formula $P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left[ 7.2 + \frac{785R}{T+18} \right]$, minimum 30 kN/m² for walls, max 150 kN/m².
- ☐ **Stripping strength:** Sides = **3.5 MPa** (16–24 hrs); Slab props ≤4.5 m = **10 MPa** (7 days); Slab props >4.5 m = **15 MPa** (14 days); Beam props >6 m = **20 MPa** (21 days).
- ☐ **Prop FOS** = 2.0 to 2.5 (typical).
- ☐ **Pour rate** = typically **1 to 2 m/hr** for columns/walls.
- ☐ **Concrete unit weight** = **24 kN/m³** (or **25 kN/m³** with reinforcement).
- ☐ **Release agent** must be applied uniformly to prevent concrete adhesion and staining.

**Maintenance Key Points:**

- ☐ **Preventive maintenance** is performed after every use, regardless of visible damage.
- ☐ **Steel form reuse** = 50–100 cycles; **Plywood reuse** = 8–15 cycles; **Timber reuse** = 3–5 cycles.
- ☐ **Plywood panels** must be stored **flat, dry, and covered**.
- ☐ **Steel forms** must be stored **vertically in racks** to prevent warping.
- ☐ **Lubricate** all wedge slots, hinges, and adjustable threads regularly.
- ☐ **Formwork Register** is mandatory documentation — records usage count, condition, and repairs.
- ☐ **Edge sealing** of plywood is critical to prevent moisture-induced delamination.

**Codes and Standards to Remember:**

- ☐ **IS 14687:1999** — Guidelines for Falsework and Formwork.
- ☐ **IS 456:2000** — Code of Practice for Plain and Reinforced Concrete.
- ☐ **ACI 347R-14** — Guide to Formwork for Concrete (international).
- ☐ **OSHA 29 CFR 1926.703** — US Safety Standard for Formwork.
- ☐ **IS 2751:1979** — Safety and Health in Construction.

**Common Defects to Remember:**

- ☐ **Bulging** → Insufficient tie spacing or bracing.
- ☐ **Leakage** → Poor joint sealing.
- ☐ **Honeycombing** → Inadequate vibration.
- ☐ **Blowholes** → Excess release agent or poor surface.
- ☐ **Staining** → Uneven release agent application.
- ☐ **Prop buckling** → Overloading or undersized props.

**Engineer's Golden Rules:**

- ☐ **"Inspect BEFORE, monitor DURING, audit AFTER."**
- ☐ **"If in doubt, DO NOT pour."** Safety first, schedule second.
- ☐ **"Document everything."** A written record is legal protection and a quality tool.
- ☐ **"Maintenance is cheaper than replacement."** 1 hour of cleaning saves 10 hours of repair.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formwork Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formwork Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formwork Quality Control (FQC)** is the systematic, planned set of activities — including inspection, testing, measurement, and corrective action — implemented to ensure that the formwork system conforms to specified design criteria, dimensional tolerances, structural performance, and safety standards throughout all phases of construction (pre-concreting, during concreting, and post-concreting/stripping).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET742 – Formwork Engineering, Module 3), **Formwork Quality Control** is treated as an integrated discipline combining:

- **Quality Assurance (QA)** — the proactive planning and documentation system (e.g., method statements, ITPs — Inspection & Test Plans, mock-ups).
- **Quality Control (QC)** — the reactive, measurement-driven verification system (e.g., dimensional checks, plumb tests, load tests).

### Key Terminology (as per IS 14687:1999 & ACI 347R)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Tolerance** | Permissible deviation from the specified value, expressed as a positive or negative limit (e.g., $\pm 6$ mm). |
| **Plumb** | Vertical alignment of a formwork face, measured against a true vertical line. |
| **Camber** | A deliberate upward curvature built into soffit formwork to compensate for deflection under dead load. |
| **ITP** | Inspection & Test Plan — a quality document listing every check, its frequency, and acceptance criteria. |
| **Mock-up** | A full-scale trial section of formwork constructed off-site to verify workmanship, finish, and procedure. |
| **QC Hold Point** | A mandatory inspection stage beyond which work cannot proceed without written approval from the QA/QC engineer. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Surgical Check-up" of Concrete**
> Think of formwork quality control like the **pre-flight checklist of an aircraft** combined with a **chef's mise-en-place**. A surgeon doesn't simply walk into the OR; every instrument is sterilized, counted, and verified. A chef doesn't just start cooking; ingredients are prepped, measured, and arranged. Similarly, before any concrete is poured into formwork, every panel, prop, tie, and joint must be inspected, measured, and signed off. If even one prop is loose or one panel is off-plumb by **6 mm**, the resulting concrete — the "patient" or the "dish" — will carry a permanent defect that no amount of post-concreting repair can fully erase.

The formwork is the **mould** that gives shape to the concrete. Quality control is the discipline that ensures the mould itself is *right* — aligned, strong, tight, clean, and true — before the concrete (which is expensive, time-sensitive, and irreversible once placed) is poured into it.

## 1.3 Why Formwork Quality Control Matters

Concrete is a **fluid → plastic → solid** material. While it is fluid, it transmits **full hydrostatic pressure** to the formwork. Any defect in the formwork at this stage directly translates into a defect in the hardened concrete. The cost of rectifying a defective column or beam is **8 to 10 times** the cost of preventing the defect through proper QC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly links formwork quality control to **safety of workmen** (collapse prevention), **durability of concrete** (honeycomb prevention via tight joints), and **aesthetic finish** (architectural exposed concrete).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Formwork Quality Control

Quality control of formwork rests on **three operational pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Material Quality (Input Control)
- Timber, plywood, steel, aluminum, and proprietary formwork components must conform to relevant Indian Standards.
- Plywood must be **BWP (Boiling Water Proof)** grade conforming to **IS 4990**.
- Structural timber must be properly seasoned (moisture content < **20%**).
- Steel components must be free from excessive rust, dents, and weld cracks.

### Pillar 2 — Design Verification (Process Control)
- Confirm that the formwork drawing matches the **structural drawing** (column size, beam depth, slab thickness).
- Verify the **shore spacing, tie spacing, and prop capacity** against the actual concrete pressure calculated.
- Check **deflection limits** for sheathing, joists, and stringers.

### Pillar 3 — Inspection & Acceptance (Output Control)
- Conduct dimensional, plumb, and alignment checks.
- Issue a **pre-pour clearance certificate** before placing concrete.
- Monitor formwork behavior during the pour (lateral pressure, deflection, leakage).
- Document stripping time and post-stripping condition of concrete.

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Formwork Inspection Stages

A KTU-compliant formwork QC procedure follows this staged hierarchy:

1. **Stage 0 — Document Review** — Verify formwork design drawings, method statements, and material certificates.
2. **Stage 1 — Material Inspection** — Check plywood thickness, prop condition, tie integrity, and clamp soundness.
3. **Stage 2 — Pre-Assembly Inspection** — Verify base preparation (sole plates, ground compaction).
4. **Stage 3 — Assembly Inspection (In-Process)** — Continuous monitoring during erection: plumb, level, alignment, joint tightness.
5. **Stage 4 — Pre-Pour Inspection (HOLD POINT)** — Final dimensional check, cleaning, release agent application, and sign-off.
6. **Stage 5 — During-Pour Monitoring** — Watch for deflection, leakage, tie loosening, prop settlement.
7. **Stage 6 — Post-Pour & Stripping Inspection** — Check concrete finish, record any bulging or misalignment, document stripping time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pre-Pour Inspection is the single most critical QC stage.** No concrete shall be poured without the QA/QC engineer's signed clearance. This is a mandatory **HOLD POINT** under any KTU-compliant ITP.

## 2.3 Dimensional Tolerances for Formwork (KTU / IS 14687 / ACI 347R)

These are the most-frequently-asked values in KTU exams. Memorize the right-hand column.

| Formwork Element | Permissible Deviation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Vertical alignment (plumb) of columns/walls** | $\pm 6$ mm per 3 m height, max $\pm 25$ mm per storey |
| **Horizontal alignment (level) of soffit** | $\pm 6$ mm in any 3 m, max $\pm 12$ mm per bay |
| **Cross-sectional dimension of column/beam** | $+6$ mm, $-3$ mm (concrete dimension) |
| **Position of embedded items (inserts, sleeves)** | $\pm 25$ mm horizontal, $\pm 6$ mm vertical |
| **Distance between formwork faces (wall thickness)** | $-3$ mm to $+6$ mm |
| **Slab thickness** | $-3$ mm to $+6$ mm |
| **Beam soffit level** | $\pm 6$ mm |
| **Lateral tie spacing variation** | $\pm 50$ mm |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations for PECET742.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot H$ | Maximum hydrostatic pressure (full fluid head) | kN/m² |
| 2 | $P_{max} = 23.5 \cdot \dfrac{H}{T + 1.8}$ | Modified ACI pressure for walls (when $H < 4.2$ m) | kN/m² |
| 3 | $P_{max} = 23.5 \cdot \dfrac{H}{T + 1.8} \le 23.5 H$ | ACI pressure for columns (capped at hydrostatic) | kN/m² |
| 4 | $P_{max} = 4.8 + \dfrac{785}{T + 17.8}$ | CIRIA pressure for columns | kN/m² |
| 5 | $w = \gamma_c \cdot R$ | Lateral load on formwork per unit area | kN/m² |
| 6 | $\delta_{allow} = \dfrac{L}{360}$ | Allowable deflection for sheathing (visual) | mm |
| 7 | $\delta_{allow} = \dfrac{L}{270}$ | Allowable deflection for structural members | mm |
| 8 | $F_{tie} = P \cdot A_{tie}$ | Design load on a form tie | kN |
| 9 | $P_{prop} = \dfrac{q \cdot A_{trib}}{n_{props}}$ | Load on each prop | kN |
| 10 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{A_{bearing}}$ | Bearing stress on sole plate | N/mm² |
| 11 | $SF = \dfrac{F_{ultimate}}{F_{working}}$ | Factor of Safety (min 2.0 for props) | — |
| 12 | $f_{ck} \ge \dfrac{3P}{b \cdot d}$ | Shear check for plywood sheathing | N/mm² |
| 13 | $\Delta_{prop} = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Elastic shortening of prop | mm |
| 14 | $M_{allow} = \sigma_{allow} \cdot S$ | Allowable bending moment in joist | kN·m |

Where:
- $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of fresh concrete = **24 kN/m³** (normal weight), **25 kN/m³** (with reinforcement).
- $H$ = vertical rate of pour / head of concrete, in m.
- $T$ = temperature of concrete, in °C.
- $R$ = rate of pour, in m/hr.
- $L$ = span of member, in mm.
- $A_{trib}$ = tributary area per prop, in m².
- $q$ = total design pressure, in kN/m².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by every formwork design engineer to size sheathing, joists, stringers, props, and ties. The **lateral pressure formula** is the most critical — underestimate it, and the formwork bursts; overestimate it, and the project becomes uneconomical. KTU examiners test this directly.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = \gamma_c H$ assumes concrete behaves as a dense liquid. This is true at the moment of placement, before initial set begins.
- **Modified ACI pressure** $P = 23.5 H/(T+1.8)$ reflects that concrete *partially sets* during the pour. Higher $T$ → faster set → lower effective pressure.
- **Deflection limit** $L/360$ is an aesthetic criterion (no visible sag). $L/270$ is the structural criterion (no cracking of the member itself).
- **Factor of Safety of 2.0** on props accounts for eccentric loading, accidental impact, and material variability.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of ACI Lateral Pressure on Formwork

The American Concrete Institute (ACI 347R) provides the most widely used lateral pressure equation for column formwork. The derivation logic is as follows:

**Step 1: Identify the variables.**
- Let $H$ = height of fresh concrete in the form, in metres.
- Let $T$ = temperature of concrete at placement, in °C.
- Let $R$ = rate of rise / rate of pour, in m/hr.
- $\gamma_c = 23.5$ kN/m³ (taken as 150 lb/ft³ in SI metric).

**Step 2: Express the pressure build-up.**
Concrete exerts hydrostatic pressure initially, but as it sets, the lower layers stop contributing additional pressure. The effective pressure $P$ is therefore lower than $\gamma_c H$ and depends on the setting time.

**Step 3: Empirical equation.**

$$P_{max} = 23.5 \cdot \dfrac{H}{T + 1.8}$$

with the upper limit:

$$P_{max} \le 23.5 \cdot H \quad \text{(full hydrostatic cap)}$$

and the lower limit:

$$P_{max} \ge 23.5 \cdot R \quad \text{(minimum pressure)}$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**
Suppose a column formwork is filled with concrete at a rate of $R = 1.5$ m/hr, and the concrete temperature is $T = 27$ °C. The total column height is $H = 4.0$ m.

Compute the numerator first:

$$23.5 \cdot H = 23.5 \cdot 4.0 = 94.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Compute the denominator:

$$T + 1.8 = 27 + 1.8 = 28.8$$

Now compute the equation:

$$P_{max} = \dfrac{94.0}{28.8} = 3.26 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Check the minimum pressure limit:

$$23.5 \cdot R = 23.5 \cdot 1.5 = 35.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Since computed $P_{max} = 3.26$ kN/m² is **less than** the minimum, the governing value is the minimum:

$$P_{max} = 35.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

This is a critical step often missed by students. The minimum pressure rule prevents under-design when $H$ is small but $R$ is large.

## 3.2 Derivation of Allowable Spacing of Props (Plywood Sheathing)

**Step 1: Define the loading.**
The sheathing (plywood) acts as a continuous beam or simply-supported strip spanning between joists. The load on the sheathing is the lateral pressure $P_{max}$ (kN/m²) from the fresh concrete.

Convert to a line load:

$$w = P_{max} \cdot b \quad \text{[kN/m]}$$

where $b$ is the width of the strip considered (take $b = 1$ m for unit analysis).

**Step 2: Compute maximum bending moment.**
For a simply supported strip:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$$

**Step 3: Apply the bending stress check.**

$$f_{b,allow} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z}$$

where $Z$ is the section modulus of the sheathing (per m width).

Solving for the allowable span $L$:

$$L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \cdot f_{b,allow} \cdot Z}{w}}$$

**Step 4: Check deflection.**
For a simply supported strip with uniform load:

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$$

Setting $\delta_{max} = L/360$:

$$L_{max,d} = \left( \dfrac{384 E I}{5 \cdot 360 \cdot w} \right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**
Consider 18 mm thick BWR plywood ($E = 8500$ N/mm², $f_b = 12$ N/mm², $I = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 18^3}{12} = 486\,000$ mm⁴/m, $Z = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 18^2}{6} = 54\,000$ mm³/m).

Given $w = 35.25$ kN/m² × 1 m = 35.25 N/mm = 0.03525 N/mm.

Moment-based span:

$$L_m = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \cdot 12 \cdot 54000}{0.03525}}$$

$$L_m = \sqrt{1.470 \times 10^8} = 1212 \text{ mm} \approx 1.21 \text{ m}$$

Deflection-based span:

$$L_d = \left( \dfrac{384 \cdot 8500 \cdot 486000}{5 \cdot 360 \cdot 0.03525} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$L_d = \left( \dfrac{1.586 \times 10^{12}}{63.45} \right)^{1/3} = \left( 2.5 \times 10^{10} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$L_d \approx 2924 \text{ mm} \approx 2.92 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Take the lesser.**
Deflection governs, but to satisfy both: $L_{max} = \min(L_m, L_d) = 1.21$ m. However, deflection limit $L/360$ is very generous for temporary works; KTU practice is to use the **moment-based span** of **1.21 m** as the governing value for prop spacing design.

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **complete worked numerical example** — typical of a KTU 14-mark question structure.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Defects → Causes → QC Remedies

| Defect Observed | Probable Cause | QC Remedy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bulging of column/wall** | Insufficient lateral ties, over-pour rate, undersized sheathing | Recheck tie spacing; reduce pour rate; redesign sheathing |
| **Honeycombing at base** | Loss of mortar through leaky joints, poor compaction | Seal joints with foam tape; use release agent; vibrate properly |
| **Misalignment of column** | Improper plumb check, base not level, prop settlement | Reset column on levelled base; add diagonal braces |
| **Soffit sag (visible in slab)** | Inadequate prop spacing, premature stripping, undersized stringers | Increase prop density; delay stripping; redesign stringer |
| **Crazing/cracking on stripped surface** | Over-vibration, formwork absorption of water, delayed curing | Use BWP plywood; apply release agent; begin curing immediately |
| **Tie hole leakage (grout loss)** | Oversized tie hole, loose cone, damaged washer | Replace tie hardware; use water-bar tie cones |
| **Stepping at construction joint** | Misaligned stop-end, deformed plywood edge | Re-shore stop-end; use rigid steel stop-end |
| **Colour variation on exposed concrete** | Different release agents, varying absorption rates of plywood | Standardize release agent; use new plywood for visible surfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This matrix is a **high-yield 14-mark answer** if asked "Explain common formwork defects and their quality control measures."
