# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Modulus of Rupture. State its formula and the minimum value required for formwork plywood as per IS 4990.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** is the maximum bending stress that a material (plywood, timber, etc.) can withstand before it fractures, as determined by a standardized three-point or four-point bending test. It is also called the **flexural strength** of the material.

**Formula:**

$$\sigma_{mor} = \frac{3PL}{2bh^2}$$

Where $P$ = maximum load (N), $L$ = span (mm), $b$ = width (mm), $h$ = thickness (mm).

**IS 4990 Requirement:** Minimum MOR for formwork plywood along the grain direction = **40 MPa**.

---

### Question 2: List any four essential pre-pour inspection checks for formwork.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The four essential pre-pour inspection checks are:

1. **Alignment and Plumb Check** — Verify vertical and horizontal alignment using plumb bob, spirit level, or laser level. Maximum allowable deviation is **6 mm per 3 m height**.

2. **Bracing and Prop Verification** — Confirm all diagonal braces, horizontal braces, and adjustable props are properly installed, locked, and plumb.

3. **Joint and Seal Inspection** — Check all formwork joints, panel connections, and tie locations for gaps. Maximum allowable gap = **3 mm** to prevent grout leakage.

4. **Reinforcement Cover Check** — Verify that cover blocks (spacer blocks) are placed at the correct spacing and the reinforcement has the required clear cover as per the structural drawing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Question A (a):** Explain in detail the various **material testing methods** for formwork components including plywood, steel, and timber. State the relevant IS codes. (7 Marks)

**Question A (b):** A wall formwork of height **3.5 m** is to be cast at a pour rate of **1.5 m/hr**. The concrete temperature is **25°C** and the unit weight of concrete is **24 kN/m³**. The cement used is Type I. Calculate the **maximum lateral pressure** on the formwork using ACI 347 guidelines. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Material Testing Methods for Formwork Components:**

**1. Plywood Testing (IS 1734, IS 4990):**

- **Modulus of Rupture (MOR) Test:** Three-point bending test on standard specimen (450 mm × 150 mm). The specimen is loaded to failure, and MOR is calculated using $\sigma_{mor} = \frac{3PL}{2bh^2}$. Minimum value: **40 MPa** (along grain).

- **Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) Test:** Same bending test setup, but using the load-deflection curve in the elastic region. MOE is calculated from the slope. Minimum value: **6500 MPa**.

- **Moisture Content Test:** Oven-dry method. Specimen is weighed, dried at 103°C for 24 hours, and reweighed. $MC = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100\%$. Maximum allowable: **15%**.

- **Water Resistance Test:** Boil the specimen in water for 8 hours, then check for delamination. Required for BWR/BWP grade.

- **Thickness Swelling Test:** Immerse specimen in water for 24 hours; measure thickness change. Maximum allowable: **8%**.

**2. Steel Testing (IS 2062, IS 1608):**

- **Tensile Test:** Universal Testing Machine (UTM). Specimen is loaded in tension until failure. Yields: Yield strength ($f_y$), Ultimate tensile strength ($f_u$), Percentage elongation. Minimum $f_y$ for IS 2062 Grade E250: **250 MPa**.

- **Bend Test:** Specimen is bent through 180° around a mandrel. No cracks permitted.

- **Charpy Impact Test:** Measures toughness at specified temperature.

- **Hardness Test:** Brinell or Rockwell method.

- **Load Test for Props:** Props are loaded to 1.5 × Safe Working Load (SWL) and held for 5 minutes. No buckling or permanent deformation permitted.

**3. Timber Testing (IS 287, IS 1708):**

- **Bending Test (MOR):** Same three-point bending as plywood. Minimum: **30 MPa** (softwood), **50 MPa** (hardwood).

- **Compression Parallel to Grain:** Tests the load capacity of timber shores.

- **Moisture Content Test:** Maximum **15%** for formwork use.

- **Nail/Screw Pull-Out Test:** Critical for connection design.

- **Visual Inspection:** Check for knots, splits, decay, and insect damage.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Given Data:**
- Wall height $H = 3.5$ m
- Pour rate $R = 1.5$ m/hr
- Concrete temperature $T = 25°C$
- Unit weight $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³
- Cement: Type I ($C_c = 1.0$)

**Step 1: Check Conditions for ACI 347 Formula Selection**

- Condition 1 applies when: $H \leq 4.2$ m AND $R \leq 2.1$ m/hr AND $T \geq 8°C$
- Given: $H = 3.5$ m ≤ 4.2 m ✓
- Given: $R = 1.5$ m/hr ≤ 2.1 m/hr ✓
- Given: $T = 25°C$ ≥ 8°C ✓

**All conditions are satisfied → Use Condition 1 formula**

**Step 2: Apply ACI 347 Formula**

$$P_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot C_c \cdot \left(0.30 + \frac{900R}{T + 17.8}\right)$$

**Step 3: Calculate the bracket term**

$$T + 17.8 = 25 + 17.8 = 42.8$$

$$\frac{900R}{T + 17.8} = \frac{900 \times 1.5}{42.8} = \frac{1350}{42.8} = 31.54$$

$$0.30 + 31.54 = 31.84$$

**Step 4: Multiply by unit weight and cement factor**

$$P_{max} = 24 \times 1.0 \times 31.84 = 764.16 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5: Check Hydrostatic Cap**

$$P_{hydrostatic} = \gamma_c \cdot H = 24 \times 3.5 = 84 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 6: Apply Minimum (Governing Value)**

$$P_{max} = \min(764.16, 84) = 84 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{max} = 84$ kPa (governed by hydrostatic pressure)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question B (a):** Describe the **in-situ inspection procedures** for formwork before, during, and after concrete placement. Include the pre-pour checklist and stripping time criteria. (7 Marks)

**Question B (b):** Explain in detail the **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** methods used for assessing formwork and hardened concrete quality. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**In-Situ Inspection Procedures for Formwork:**

**1. Pre-Pour Inspection (Most Critical Phase):**

The pre-pour inspection verifies that the erected formwork is safe and ready for concrete placement. The checklist includes:

**(a) Material Verification:** Confirm that approved materials (BWR/BWP plywood, E250 steel props) are used. Material test certificates (MTCs) must be available on site.

**(b) Dimensional Accuracy:** Measure cross-sectional dimensions using tape. Tolerance: ± 5 mm for cross-section, ± 10 mm for openings.

**(c) Alignment and Plumb:** Use plumb bob or laser level. Maximum vertical deviation: **6 mm in 3 m height**. Maximum horizontal deviation: **± 10 mm in 10 m length**.

**(d) Bracing and Propping:** Verify that all diagonal braces, horizontal struts, and adjustable props are properly installed. Props must be plumb and locked. Base plates and head plates must be in full contact.

**(e) Joints and Seals:** Inspect all panel joints, tie holes, and construction joints. Maximum allowable gap: **3 mm** to prevent cement grout leakage.

**(f) Reinforcement Cover:** Verify placement of cover blocks at required spacing (typically 1 m center-to-center for slabs).

**(g) Embedded Items:** Check conduits, sleeves, anchor bolts, dovetail slots, and electrical boxes for correct location and secure fixing.

**(h) Cleanliness:** Remove sawdust, debris, water, and loose materials from the formwork interior.

**(i) Release Agent:** Verify uniform application of approved release agent (form oil) on the formwork surface.

**(j) Access and Safety:** Verify guard rails (top rail at 1.0 m, mid-rail at 0.5 m), toe boards (150 mm), and access ladders.

**Documentation:** The pre-pour inspection must be recorded in a **standard checklist** with signatures of:
- Site Engineer
- Quality Control Engineer
- Safety Officer

**2. During-Pour Inspection (Continuous Monitoring):**

- **Pour Rate Monitoring:** Concrete must be placed at a controlled rate matching the design assumption.
- **Pressure Monitoring:** For instrumented formwork, pressure gauges must be monitored.
- **Visual Inspection:** Watch for bulging, deflection, leakage, and unusual sounds.
- **Vibration Monitoring:** Ensure internal vibrators are not in direct contact with formwork (minimum 75 mm clearance).

**3. Post-Pour Inspection (Stripping Phase):**

**Stripping Time Criteria (per IS 456:2000):**

| Element | Min. Stripping Time | Min. Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical forms (walls, columns) | 12-24 hours | 2-3 MPa |
| Beam sides | 24-48 hours | 3-5 MPa |
| Slab soffit (props retained) | 3 days | 50% design strength |
| Beam soffit (props retained) | 7 days | 70% design strength |
| Props for slabs (≤ 4.5 m span) | 7 days | 75% design strength |
| Props for beams (≤ 6 m span) | 14 days | 100% design strength |
| Arches/slabs (> 6 m span) | 21 days | 100% design strength |

**Procedure for Stripping:**
1. Verify cube test results or use maturity method
2. Remove props in a specific sequence (typically from center to edges, or as per engineer's instructions)
3. Inspect concrete for defects (honeycombing, cracks, blowouts)
4. Begin curing immediately after stripping
5. Document stripping time and observations

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods:**

**Definition:** NDT methods are testing techniques that evaluate material properties **without causing damage** to the structure or component. In formwork engineering, NDT is applied both to **concrete quality assessment** (after stripping) and to **formwork material condition** (during reuse).

**1. Rebound Hammer Test (Schmidt Hammer) — IS 13311 (Part 2):**

**Principle:** A spring-loaded mass strikes the concrete surface with a fixed impact energy. The **rebound distance** is measured on a graduated scale. The rebound number correlates with the **surface hardness** and approximately with the **compressive strength** of concrete.

**Procedure:**
- Hold the hammer perpendicular to the concrete surface
- Take 10-12 readings at each test location
- Discard outlier values (those differing by more than 6 from the median)
- Calculate the average rebound number
- Correlate with manufacturer's calibration chart to estimate strength

**Limitations:**
- Only tests the **surface layer** (25-30 mm depth)
- Affected by surface moisture, carbonation, and texture
- Provides **approximate** strength, not absolute

**2. Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) Test — IS 13311 (Part 1):**

**Principle:** An ultrasonic pulse (typically 50-54 kHz) is transmitted through the concrete using a transmitter and received by a receiver. The **transit time** is measured, and the **pulse velocity** is calculated:

$$V = \frac{L}{t}$$

Where $V$ = pulse velocity (km/s), $L$ = path length (mm), $t$ = transit time (μs).

**Quality Classification:**

| UPV (km/s) | Concrete Quality |
|---|---|
| > 4.5 | Excellent |
| 3.5 – 4.5 | Good |
| 3.0 – 3.5 | Doubtful |
| < 3.0 | Poor |

**Applications:**
- Detect internal voids, cracks, and honeycombing
- Assess uniformity of concrete
- Estimate dynamic modulus of elasticity
- Verify concrete quality in critical structural members

**3. Cover Meter Test (Rebound Survey):**

**Principle:** A magnetic device is used to locate reinforcement bars and measure the concrete cover (the distance from the surface to the rebar).

**Application:** Verify that the specified cover thickness has been achieved after formwork removal.

**4. Half-Cell Potential Test:**

**Principle:** Measures the **corrosion potential** of embedded steel reinforcement using a copper-copper sulfate half-cell.

**Application:** Assess the likelihood of corrosion in reinforced concrete members, particularly relevant for formwork tie holes and embedment zones.

**5. Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR):**

**Principle:** Electromagnetic waves are transmitted into the concrete, and the reflections from internal features (rebars, voids, conduits) are analyzed.

**Application:** Non-intrusive detection of embedded items, void detection, and rebar mapping.

**6. Infrared Thermography:**

**Principle:** Detects surface temperature variations caused by internal defects (voids, delamination).

**Application:** Large-area surveys of concrete elements.

**Comparison Table:**

| NDT Method | Tests | Depth | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rebound Hammer | Surface hardness | 25-30 mm | Quick strength estimate |
| UPV | Internal integrity | Full depth | Void and crack detection |
| Cover Meter | Rebar location & cover | 100 mm | Cover verification |
| Half-Cell | Corrosion potential | Surface | Durability assessment |
| GPR | Embedded items | 300-500 mm | Comprehensive scanning |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **UPV test** with **rebound hammer test** in their answers. Remember: **UPV measures internal quality** (voids, cracks, integrity), while **Rebound Hammer measures surface hardness** (approximate strength). These are fundamentally different test principles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Formwork Testing & Inspection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Formula Selection Error (–2 marks):** Using the wrong ACI 347 formula (Condition 1 vs. Condition 2) without checking the conditions ($H$, $R$, $T$). Always state the conditions and verify before applying the formula.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Hydrostatic Cap (–2 marks):** Calculating the ACI formula result and presenting it as the final answer without comparing with $\gamma_c \cdot H$. The final answer must be the **minimum** of the two.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing kN/m³ and N/mm³, or using incorrect units in MOR calculation. Ensure $P$ in N, $L$, $b$, $h$ in mm to get $\sigma$ in MPa.
>
> 4. **Missing Cement/Unit Weight Factors (–1 mark):** Forgetting to include $C_c$ and $C_w$ factors in the ACI pressure formula. These are essential modifiers.
>
> 5. **Confusing NDT Methods (–2 marks):** Writing about UPV when asked about Rebound Hammer, or vice versa. Clearly state the **principle**, **procedure**, and **application** of each NDT method separately.
>
> 6. **Skipping Documentation (–1 mark):** Not mentioning the **pre-pour checklist documentation** requirement. Examiners expect students to know that inspection must be **recorded and signed** by responsible parties.
>
> 7. **Vague Stripping Times (–1 mark):** Writing "strip after some days" instead of citing specific IS 456 criteria. Always use the **tabular format** with element, time, and strength values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist — Testing and Inspection Methods

- [ ] **Plywood Grade:** Only **BWR (Boiling Water Resistant)** or **BWP (Boiling Water Proof)** grade is permitted for formwork per IS 4990. MR grade is **NOT** acceptable.

- [ ] **Plywood MOR:** Minimum **40 MPa** along grain; MOE: minimum **6500 MPa** (IS 4990).

- [ ] **Plywood Moisture Content:** Maximum **15%** for formwork use.

- [ ] **MOR Formula:** $\sigma_{mor} = \frac{3PL}{2bh^2}$ — derived from flexure formula with rectangular cross-section.

- [ ] **Steel Grade:** IS 2062 — E250 ($f_y = 250$ MPa) is the most common grade for formwork components.

- [ ] **Prop Load Test:** Test load = **1.5 × SWL**, held for minimum 5 minutes; no permanent deformation or buckling permitted.

- [ ] **Deflection Limits:** Slab formwork: **L/360**; Beam formwork: **L/270** (per ACI 347).

- [ ] **Plumb Tolerance:** Maximum **6 mm deviation per 3 m height** of formwork.

- [ ] **Joint Gap Tolerance:** Maximum **3 mm gap** in formwork joints to prevent grout leakage.

- [ ] **Concrete Unit Weight:** $\gamma_c = 24$ to $25$ kN/m³ for normal-weight concrete (NC).

- [ ] **ACI 347 Wall Formula — Condition 1:** $P = \gamma_c \cdot (0.30 + \frac{900R}{T+17.8})$ when $H \leq 4.2$ m, $R \leq 2.1$ m/hr, $T \geq 8°C$.

- [ ] **ACI 347 Wall Formula — Condition 2:** $P = \gamma_c \cdot (0.60 + \frac{800R}{T+17.8})$ when ANY condition of Condition 1 is violated.

- [ ] **Column Formwork Pressure:** $P = \gamma_c \cdot H$ (full hydrostatic, no formula needed).

- [ ] **Hydrostatic Cap:** Always check $P_{formula} \leq \gamma_c \cdot H$; use the minimum.

- [ ] **Cement Factor $C_c$:** 1.0 for Type I cement; **1.2** for Type II/V with retarders.

- [ ] **Unit Weight Factor $C_w$:** 1.0 for normal concrete; 0.5 (≥ 0.45γc) for lightweight concrete.

- [ ] **Stripping Time — Vertical Forms:** 12-24 hours; minimum strength **2-3 MPa**.

- [ ] **Stripping Time — Slab Props (≤ 4.5 m):** 7 days; **75%** of design strength.

- [ ] **Stripping Time — Beam Props (≤ 6 m):** 14 days; **100%** of design strength.

- [ ] **Stripping Time — Arches/Slabs (> 6 m):** 21 days; **100%** of design strength.

- [ ] **Rebound Hammer:** Tests **surface hardness**; approximate strength estimate; depth ~25-30 mm.

- [ ] **UPV Test:** Tests **internal integrity**; detects voids, cracks, honeycombing; quality classification: > 4.5 km/s (Excellent), < 3.0 km/s (Poor).

- [ ] **UPV Formula:** $V = L/t$ (km/s), where $L$ is path length and $t$ is transit time.

- [ ] **Key IS Codes:** IS 14687 (Falsework), IS 4990 (Plywood for Shuttering), IS 303 (Plywood General), IS 2062 (Steel), IS 13311 (NDT), IS 456 (Concrete).

- [ ] **Key International Codes:** ACI 347, OSHA 1926 Subpart Q, BS 5975, ACI SP-4.

- [ ] **Inspection Documentation:** Pre-pour checklist must be signed by **Site Engineer + QC Engineer + Safety Officer**.

- [ ] **Authority Separation:** The person who builds formwork should **NOT** be the person who inspects and approves it.

- [ ] **Five Common Failures to Prevent:** Plywood blowout, prop buckling, beam failure, lateral instability, foundation settlement.

- [ ] **Deflection Check on Formwork:** Always verify $\delta_{actual} \leq \delta_{allowable}$ before pouring.

- [ ] **Embedded Items Check:** Conduits, sleeves, anchor bolts, dovetail slots must be verified before pouring.

- [ ] **Pour Rate Control:** Concrete pour rate must match the design assumption (R value in ACI formula).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU Students:** When answering questions on formwork testing and inspection, always structure your answer in three parts: (1) **Principle/Concept**, (2) **Procedure/Method**, (3) **Acceptance Criteria/Standards**. This is the KTU examiner's preferred answer structure and will earn you full marks.

> **END OF NOTES — Testing and Inspection Methods (Module 3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Assurance (QA) in Formwork Engineering** is a systematic, planned set of activities implemented within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** that ensures that formwork design, materials, fabrication, erection, and stripping processes conform to specified technical requirements, regulatory codes (IS 14687, ACI SP-4, IS 456), and project specifications. QA is a **proactive, process-oriented** discipline focused on preventing defects rather than detecting them.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for PECET742, Quality Assurance Protocols for formwork encompass the entire **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle** applied to temporary works, ensuring structural integrity, dimensional accuracy, worker safety, and concrete surface finish.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Quality Assurance Protocols cover inspection checklists, tolerance limits, material testing, non-destructive evaluation (NDE), documentation procedures, ISO 9001 alignment, and statistical quality control (SQC) methods applied to formwork systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Quality Assurance as the **"pre-flight checklist of an aircraft pilot."** A pilot does not wait for the plane to develop a fault mid-air and then fix it (that would be **Quality Control – reactive**). Instead, the pilot *proactively* checks fuel, instruments, weather, and engines *before* takeoff. Similarly, QA in formwork means checking timber moisture, plywood thickness, prop tightness, bracing alignment, and concrete pressure calculations *before* and *during* the pour — not after the formwork collapses or the concrete comes out honeycombed.

**Quality Assurance (QA)** = Prevention + Process (the "cook follows the recipe correctly").

**Quality Control (QC)** = Inspection + Product (the "finished dish is tasted before serving").

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **QA (Quality Assurance):** Process-focused, preventive, system-wide, *before* the activity.
> - **QC (Quality Control):** Product-focused, detective, *after* the activity.
> - **TQM (Total Quality Management):** Organization-wide philosophy involving all stakeholders.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Concrete lateral pressure (fresh):** Typically ranges from **20 kN/m² to 80 kN/m²** depending on placement rate, temperature, and cement type.
- **Plywood standard thickness:** **12 mm, 18 mm, 19 mm** (IS 4990).
- **Tolerance limits (IS 14687 / ACI 117):**
  - Vertical plumb of formwork: **± 6 mm per 3 m height**
  - Alignment deviation: **± 13 mm in 3 m**
  - Formwork deflection limit: **L/360** for visible surfaces, **L/270** for concealed surfaces.
- **Inspection frequency (as per standard QA plans):** **100%** for primary load-bearing members (props, shores), **10%** for secondary members.
- **Standard statistical confidence level for SQC:** **95% (σ = 1.96)** for two-tailed acceptance sampling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All tolerances in formwork are **twice as strict** for architectural (exposed) concrete compared to structural (buried) concrete, because visual defects are unacceptable in finished surfaces.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Quality Assurance Protocols

The QA protocol in formwork engineering operates on **five interconnected tiers**:

### Tier 1: Design-Stage QA
- Review of formwork drawings against structural requirements.
- Verification of load combinations: *Dead Load + Live Load + Concrete Pressure + Wind Load*.
- **Why?** Prevents under-design that causes collapse.
- **How?** Independent design check by a chartered engineer; use of software like **PROK** or **PERI ELPOS**.

### Tier 2: Material-Stage QA (Incoming Inspection)
- Verification of **ISI-marked** plywood, **IS 2062** steel props, **IS 7331** fastenings.
- Moisture content of timber: shall not exceed **15%–20%** for formwork use.
- **Why?** Wet timber swells, warps, and reduces concrete strength by absorbing mix water.
- **How?** Use a **moisture meter**; reject lots with > 20% MC.

### Tier 3: Fabrication-Stage QA
- Dimensional accuracy of panels (± 2 mm).
- Welding quality of steel formwork (visual + ultrasonic testing for critical joints).
- **Why?** Misfit panels cause leakage of cement slurry (honeycombing) and misalignment.
- **How?** Use calibrated measuring tapes, weld gauges, GO/NO-GO jigs.

### Tier 4: Erection-Stage QA (Critical Control Point)
- Plumb, level, alignment, and tightness of bracing.
- **Why?** This is the *last checkpoint* before concrete pour — errors here are expensive to rectify.
- **How?** Use *autolevel*, *theodolite*, *torque wrenches* on prop nuts.

### Tier 5: Post-Pour & Stripping QA
- Stripping time as per **IS 456** (e.g., sides: 24–48 hrs; soffit: 7–14 days depending on span).
- Surface finish inspection immediately after stripping.
- **Why?** Premature stripping causes sagging, cracking, and structural failure.
- **How?** Cube test results, maturity method (in-situ strength), or time-based stripping schedule.

## 2.2 The Quality Assurance Protocol Cycle (PDCA)

| **Phase** | **Activity** | **Tools Used** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P (Plan)** | Develop Inspection & Test Plan (ITP) | Checklists, FMEA | Approved ITP document |
| **D (Do)** | Execute formwork activities | Work instructions, SOPs | As-built records |
| **C (Check)** | Inspect & test | Calipers, levels, cube tests | Inspection reports, NCRs |
| **A (Act)** | Corrective action | Root cause analysis (5-Why, Ishikawa) | Updated SOPs, lessons learned |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concrete Lateral Pressure (ACI 347)** | $P_{max} = \min(C_1 \cdot \sqrt{R}, C_2 \cdot H)$ | $R$ = rate of placement (m/hr), $H$ = height of fresh concrete (m), $C_1$ and $C_2$ are coefficients based on concrete type | Designing formwork sheathing strength |
| **Concrete Pressure (CIRIA, Pumping)** | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ for hydrostatic, but capped at $P_{max}$ | $\rho$ = density of concrete ($\approx 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $h$ = head (m) | Calculating pressure on wall forms |
| **Deflection Check (Plywood Sheathing)** | $\delta = \dfrac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I} \leq \dfrac{L}{360}$ | $w$ = UDL (N/mm), $L$ = span (mm), $E$ = modulus of elasticity ($\text{N/mm}^2$), $I$ = moment of inertia ($\text{mm}^4$) | Verifying serviceability of formwork |
| **Acceptance Sampling (MIL-STD / IS 2500)** | $LTPD = \dfrac{1.12 \cdot d}{N \cdot p_1}$ | $d$ = allowable defectives, $N$ = sample size, $p_1$ = lot tolerance percent defective | Deciding lot acceptance/rejection |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL$ = upper spec limit, $LSL$ = lower spec limit, $\sigma$ = std deviation | Assessing formwork process consistency |
| **Cube Strength Acceptance (IS 456)** | $f_{ck,actual} \geq f_{ck} - 0.825 \cdot \sigma$ (or $f_{ck} - 3.5 \text{ MPa}$ for individual) | $f_{ck}$ = characteristic strength, $\sigma$ = std deviation of cubes | Determining safe stripping time |
| **Stripping Time (Empirical, ACI 347)** | $t = \dfrac{f_{req} \cdot k}{T \cdot (F + 0.5)}$ | $f_{req}$ = required strength, $k$ = constant, $T$ = temperature, $F$ = cement factor | Estimating re-shore schedule |
| **Tolerance (Plumb)** | $\Delta_{plumb} \leq \dfrac{H}{500}$ or **6 mm per 3 m** | $H$ = total formwork height (m) | QA inspection check |
| **Moisture Content** | $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100\%$ | $W$ = weights of timber | Material acceptance criterion |
| **Statistical QC – Control Limits** | $UCL = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$, $LCL = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ | $\bar{X}$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = std deviation | SQC control charts (X-bar, R-charts) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The **0.825σ** rule and **3.5 MPa** rule for individual cubes in **IS 456:2000 Clause 16.1** are *both* valid; the more stringent governs. This is a favourite 3-mark question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

QA protocols in formwork engineering directly impact:
1. **Structural safety** — formwork failure is the **#1 cause** of construction accidents in India (per National Safety Council data).
2. **Economy** — rework due to poor formwork quality accounts for **5–8% of project cost**.
3. **Aesthetics** — architectural concrete requires strict tolerance control.
4. **Schedule** — failed QA causes 2–4 week delays on typical high-rise pours.
5. **Legal compliance** — adherence to IS 14687, BOCW Act, and Factories Act.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Concrete Lateral Pressure (ACI 347 Simplified)

**Problem Setup:** Fresh concrete behaves as a fluid until initial set. The lateral pressure on vertical formwork is hydrostatic up to a limiting value because internal friction between aggregate particles and setting of cement limits the maximum pressure.

**Starting principle:** For a fluid of unit weight $\gamma_c$, the hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ is:

$$
P_{hydro} = \gamma_c \cdot h
$$

**However**, ACI 347 limits this by considering **rate of placement** and **concrete consistency**. For ordinary concrete with slump ≤ 100 mm and internal vibration:

$$
P_{max} = 0.6 \cdot \gamma_c \cdot H
$$

**Where:**
- $H$ = maximum height of fresh concrete in form (m)
- $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of concrete = **24 kN/m³**
- The 0.6 factor accounts for arching and internal friction

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

$$
H = 3 \text{ m} \quad \text{(say, for a 3 m high wall form)}
$$

$$
P_{hydro} = 24 \times 3 = 72 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
P_{max} = 0.6 \times 24 \times 3 = 43.2 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** Use the **minimum** of hydrostatic and limiting pressure to design the formwork sheathing. This is the value that must be resisted by plywood, studs, and walers.

**Verification of a related parameter — minimum design pressure (per IS 14687):**

$$
P_{min} = 0.4 \cdot \gamma_c \cdot H = 0.4 \times 24 \times 3 = 28.8 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

So the formwork must be designed for the **envelope** $28.8 \leq P \leq 43.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$ in this example.

## 3.2 Derivation: Acceptance Probability (Operating Characteristic Curve)

In statistical quality control (SQC), the **probability of accepting a lot** with fraction defective $p$ in a sample of size $N$ with acceptance number $c$ is given by the binomial distribution:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{N}{d} \cdot p^d \cdot (1-p)^{N-d}
$$

**Numerical Example (Worked):** $N = 50$ samples, $c = 1$ (single sampling plan). Find $P_a$ for $p = 0.02$ (2% defective).

$$
P_a = \binom{50}{0} \cdot (0.02)^0 \cdot (0.98)^{50} + \binom{50}{1} \cdot (0.02)^1 \cdot (0.98)^{49}
$$

**Term 1:**
$$
T_1 = 1 \times 1 \times (0.98)^{50}
$$

$$
\ln(T_1) = 50 \cdot \ln(0.98) = 50 \times (-0.020203) = -1.01015
$$

$$
T_1 = e^{-1.01015} \approx 0.3642
$$

**Term 2:**
$$
T_2 = 50 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{49}
$$

$$
\ln(T_2) = \ln(50) + \ln(0.02) + 49 \cdot \ln(0.98)
$$

$$
\ln(T_2) = 3.9120 + (-3.9120) + 49 \times (-0.020203) = 0 + (-0.9899) = -0.9899
$$

$$
T_2 = e^{-0.9899} \approx 0.3715
$$

**Total:**
$$
P_a = T_1 + T_2 = 0.3642 + 0.3715 = 0.7357 \approx 73.6\%
$$

**Interpretation:** With this sampling plan, a lot that is **2% defective** will still be accepted **73.6% of the time** — meaning QA sampling plans have inherent *consumer's risk* ($\beta$). The producer's risk ($\alpha$) is the probability of rejecting a *good* lot. KTU students should remember: $\alpha = 5\%$, $\beta = 10\%$ are standard defaults.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Formwork Defects and QA Remedies

| **Defect** | **Root Cause** | **QA Prevention Protocol** | **Detection Method** | **Cost Impact (INR)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Honeycombing** | Formwork leakage at joints, improper vibration | Joint sealing with foam tape; check panel joints before pour | Visual + tapping (sounding) | High (₹500–1000/m²) |
| **Bulging of wall** | Insufficient bracing, over-rate of pour | Calculate $P_{max}$ and design walers; limit pour rate to **1.5 m/hr** | Plumb check, deflection gauge | Very High (₹2000/m²) |
| **Cold joint** | Delay in concrete placement beyond initial set | Plan pour sequence; QA-log truck arrival times | Visual, core test | High (₹1500/m²) |
| **Staining / Discoloration** | Rust from steel forms, oil contamination | Use release agent (form oil) uniformly; clean panels | Visual after stripping | Medium (₹300/m²) |
| **Misalignment** | Survey error, prop settlement | Pre-pour survey check using total station | Theodolite check | High (₹1000/m²) |
| **Formwork collapse** | Inadequate prop capacity, missing bracing | 100% prop load test; FMEA during design | Failure (no detection possible) | Catastrophic (loss of life) |
| **Plywood blowout** | Excessive rate of pour, thin sheathing | Use 18 mm plywood; limit pour height | Acoustic monitoring, visual | Medium-High |
| **Surface laitance** | Excessive water in mix, over-vibration | Slump test (≤100 mm) at batching plant | Sounding, surface hardness test | Low (₹200/m²) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Analytical Matrix above is examinable in KTU 2024 ESE** — questions on "Discuss the QA checklist for wall formwork" or "Enumerate common defects and their prevention" carry **7 marks each** and require tabular presentation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Quality Assurance Protocol Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional flow of a complete QA protocol for formwork** in a typical KTU-affiliated project. This is adapted as a *block-level architecture* per the V10 protocol safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Specification and Design Brief] --> B[Develop Inspection and Test Plan ITP]
    B --> C[Formwork Design Review by Independent Engineer]
    C --> D{Material Incoming Inspection}
    D -- Reject --> E[Issue Non-Conformance Report NCR]
    D -- Accept --> F[Material Storage in Controlled Area]
    F --> G[Fabrication of Formwork Panels in Yard]
    G --> H[Dimensional Check 100 percent Sampling]
    H -- Fail --> E
    H -- Pass --> I[Transport to Site and Erection]
    I --> J[Plumb and Level Verification with Total Station]
    J --> K[Prop Tightness and Bracing Audit]
    K --> L[Pre-Pour Hold Point Inspection]
    L -- Fail --> E
    L -- Pass --> M[Concrete Pour Commences]
    M --> N[Continuous Monitoring of Pour Rate and Pressure]
    N --> O[Post-Pour Cube Test at 7 and 28 Days]
    O --> P{Strength Criteria Satisfied as per IS 456}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Formwork Stripping with Permit]
    P -- No --> R[Delay Stripping and Re-Test]
    Q --> S[Surface Finish Inspection of Concrete]
    S --> T[Final QA Documentation and As-Built Records]
    T --> U[Archive in Digital QA System ISO 9001 Aligned]
    E --> V[Root Cause Analysis Using 5-Why Method]
    V --> W[Corrective and Preventive Action CAPA]
    W --> X[Update SOPs and Lessons Learned]
    X --> B
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Quality Control (X-bar) Chart for Plywood Thickness

The **statistical process control (SPC) chart** is a key QA tool. The following Mermaid block represents the logic of a control chart monitoring plywood thickness (target = 18 mm).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Sample 1 thickness] --> B1[Mean Calculation]
        A2[Sample 2 thickness] --> B1
        A3[Sample 3 thickness] --> B1
        A4[Sample 4 thickness] --> B1
        A5[Sample 5 thickness] --> B1
    end
    B1 --> C[X-bar = average of 5 samples]
    C --> D[Compute Range R = max minus min]
    D --> E{All points within 3 sigma Control Limits}
    E -- Yes --> F[Process is In Control and Accept Lot]
    E -- No --> G[Process Out of Control and Issue NCR]
    G --> H[Investigate Assignable Cause]
    H --> I[Re-Calibrate Saw and Re-Inspect]
    I --> B1
    F --> J[Tag and Issue for Site Use]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Formwork QA Documents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Quality Manual Level 1] --> P1[Project Quality Plan Level 2]
    P1 --> P2[Inspection and Test Plan ITP Level 3]
    P2 --> P3[Method Statement Level 4]
    P3 --> P4[Checklists and Test Reports Level 5]
    P4 --> P5[Site Records and Photographic Evidence Level 6]
    P5 --> P6[Final QA Dossier Handover to Client]
    P2 --> S1[Material Test Certificates]
    P2 --> S2[Calibration Certificates]
    P2 --> S3[Workmanship Samples]
    P2 --> S4[Non-Conformance Reports NCR]
    P2 --> S5[Corrective Action Requests CAR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "draw a quality assurance flowchart for formwork," always include **hold points** (gates where work cannot proceed without QA approval). The *Pre-Pour Hold Point* is the most critical — it is the **last gate** before irreversible concrete placement.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) in the context of formwork engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Quality Control (QC)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process-oriented | Product-oriented |
| **Timing** | Preventive (*before* activity) | Detective (*after* activity) |
| **Scope** | System-wide, all stages | Specific deliverable |
| **Tools** | SOPs, ITPs, FMEA | Inspections, tests, measurements |
| **Example** | Verifying prop torque before pour | Checking concrete strength after pour via cube test |

*(Award 1 mark for correct definitions, 1 mark for the comparative table, 1 mark for the contextual example specific to formwork.)*
