## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** for a typical reinforced concrete wall formwork. List the hold points, the acceptance criteria, and the responsible persons. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **statistical quality control (SQC)** tools used in formwork fabrication, with reference to **X-bar and R-charts**. Show the formula for control limits and explain how a process capability index $C_p$ is interpreted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Inspection and Test Plan for Wall Formwork (7 marks):**

**Step 1 – Document Submission (1 mark):**
The contractor submits the formwork design, drawings, and method statement to the **Project Quality Manager (PQM)** for review. The PQM verifies the design against the structural engineer's loads and project specifications.

**Step 2 – Material Inspection (1 mark):**
- **Plywood (12/18 mm):** Visual check for warping, delamination, and thickness tolerance (± 0.5 mm). Sampling per IS 2500.
- **Steel Props (IS 2062):** Check mill certificate, straightness, and load test certificate. Acceptance: **≥ 25 kN capacity** for standard props.
- **Clamps, Nuts, Bolts (IS 7331):** Check thread integrity, galvanization, and torque capacity.

**Step 3 – Pre-Erection Check (1 mark):**
- Verify survey benchmarks and grid lines.
- Check rebar placement, cover, and spacer blocks (this is the **Hold Point 1** – rebar sign-off).

**Step 4 – Formwork Erection Checks (1.5 marks):**
- **Plumb check** using plumb bob or total station.
- **Level check** of soffit using auto-level.
- **Alignment check** of wall faces (tolerance ± 6 mm in 3 m).
- **Bracing and prop tightness** (torque wrench to specified value, typically **40–60 N·m**).

**Step 5 – Pre-Pour Inspection (1.5 marks):**
- **Hold Point 2** – Pre-Pour Meeting: attended by Client, Consultant, Contractor QA, and Safety Officer.
- Final checklist signed by **Site Engineer + QA Engineer + Client Representative**.
- Pour rate and concrete temperature recorded.

**Step 6 – Post-Pour Monitoring (0.5 mark):**
- Continuous visual monitoring during pour.
- Stop-work authority if bulging or leak observed.
- Cube samples taken for 7-day and 28-day strength.

**Step 7 – Stripping and Final Inspection (0.5 mark):**
- Stripping only after cube strength ≥ **70% of $f_{ck}$** (or as per IS 456 for the relevant member).
- Surface finish inspection, NCR for honeycombing if any.

---

**(b) Statistical Quality Control Tools (7 marks):**

**X-bar Chart (3.5 marks):**

The X-bar chart monitors the process **mean** of subgroups. The control limits are:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \overline{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}
$$

$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \overline{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}
$$

$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = \overline{\bar{X}} = \text{Grand Mean of all subgroup means}
$$

**Where:**
- $\overline{\bar{X}}$ = average of subgroup means
- $\bar{R}$ = average of subgroup ranges
- $A_2$ = control chart constant (for subgroup size $n = 5$, $A_2 = 0.577$ from standard tables)

**R-Chart (2 marks):**

The R-chart monitors process **variability**:

$$
UCL_R = D_4 \cdot \bar{R}
$$

$$
LCL_R = D_3 \cdot \bar{R}
$$

For $n = 5$: $D_4 = 2.114$, $D_3 = 0$.

**Process Capability Index (1.5 marks):**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $C_p < 1.0$ → Process **incapable** of meeting specifications
- $1.0 \leq C_p < 1.33$ → Process **marginally capable**
- $1.33 \leq C_p < 1.67$ → Process **capable**
- $C_p \geq 1.67$ → Process **highly capable** (Six Sigma level ≈ 1.5 for $C_{pk}$)

**Engineering Application to Formwork:**
In formwork fabrication, the X-bar/R chart monitors plywood thickness (target 18 mm, USL 18.5 mm, LSL 17.5 mm). If points fall outside control limits, the saw is recalibrated.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Alternate]**

**(a)** Enumerate the **common defects observed in formwork** and explain their root causes. Suggest QA-based preventive measures for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **stripping time criteria** as per **IS 456:2000** and the **ACI 347** guidelines. How is the **maturity method** used to determine in-situ concrete strength for safe stripping? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Common Defects and QA Prevention (7 marks):**

| **Defect** | **Root Cause** | **QA Prevention** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Honeycombing** (1 mark) | Formwork leakage, large aggregate, poor vibration | Use foam tape on joints; max aggregate 20 mm; trained vibrator operators |
| 2. **Bulging / Lateral shift** (1 mark) | Insufficient walers, over-rate of pour, weak props | Design to $P_{max}$ from ACI 347; limit pour rate to **1.5 m/hr**; prop load test |
| 3. **Cold joint** (1 mark) | Delay > 90 min between successive lifts | Plan truck arrival; pour sequence diagram; retarder admixture |
| 4. **Surface staining** (0.5 mark) | Rusty steel forms, oil streaks | Clean forms, apply release agent uniformly; cover during rain |
| 5. **Misalignment / Plumb error** (0.5 mark) | Survey error, prop settlement | Auto-level + total station check; base plates on firm ground |
| 6. **Formwork collapse** (1 mark) | Under-design, missing bracing, prop failure | Independent design check; 100% prop inspection; FMEA at design stage |
| 7. **Soffit sagging** (0.5 mark) | Premature stripping, inadequate reshoring | Wait for cube strength ≥ 70% $f_{ck}$; design reshoring for 3 levels |
| 8. **Blowout at panel joints** (0.5 mark) | Insufficient clamping | Use specified clamps at 300 mm c/c; visual check before pour |
| 9. **Laitance / Sand streaks** (0.5 mark) | High water-cement ratio, segregation | Slump test ≤ 100 mm; free-fall height ≤ 1.5 m |
| 10. **Embedded item movement** (0.5 mark) | Insufficient fixing to formwork | Nail inserts to formwork; check after vibration |

*(Award marks for defect, cause, and prevention triplet. 7 marks = ~10 items covered selectively.)*

---

**(b) Stripping Time Criteria (7 marks):**

**IS 456:2000 Clause 11.3 (2.5 marks):**

| **Formwork Type** | **Minimum Stripping Time** |
|---|---|
| Vertical (sides of beams, columns, walls) | **24 to 48 hours** |
| Soffit of slabs (props left under) | **3 days** |
| Props to slabs (spans ≤ 4.5 m) | **7 days** |
| Props to slabs (spans > 4.5 m) | **14 days** |
| Beams (props left under) | **7 days** |
| Beam props (spans ≤ 6 m) | **14 days** |
| Beam props (spans > 6 m) | **21 days** |

**Key Principle:** Concrete strength at stripping shall be at least **70% of characteristic strength ($f_{ck}$)** for self-weight, or the full $f_{ck}$ if the member is to take superimposed loads immediately.

**ACI 347 Guidelines (2 marks):**

ACI 347 specifies stripping based on the **strength required to resist**:
- Self-weight of the member
- Construction loads (live load = 1.5 kN/m² minimum)
- Combination of both

**Strength Test Method (1 mark):**
- Cast field-cured cubes alongside the structure.
- Test at the planned stripping time.
- Strength ≥ required strength → permit stripping.

**Maturity Method (1.5 marks):**

The maturity method estimates in-situ strength using the **temperature-time history** of concrete:

$$
M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i - T_0) \cdot \Delta t_i
$$

**Where:**
- $M$ = maturity index (°C·hours or °C·days)
- $T_i$ = average concrete temperature during time interval $i$ (°C)
- $T_0$ = datum temperature = **–10°C** (or 0°C for simpler approximations)
- $\Delta t_i$ = time interval (hours or days)

**Strength-Maturity Relationship:**

$$
f_c = a + b \cdot \log(M)
$$

**Where $a$ and $b$ are constants determined from lab calibration** of the specific concrete mix.

**Procedure:**
1. Embed thermocouples or use maturity sensors (e.g., **Con-Cure sensors**) in the structural member.
2. Record temperature continuously.
3. Calculate $M$ using the Nurse-Saul equation.
4. Look up the calibrated $f_c$ – $M$ curve to estimate current strength.
5. Permit stripping when estimated $f_c \geq$ required strength.

**Advantage:** Non-destructive, real-time, allows stripping decisions without waiting for cube results.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE (Module 3 – QA Protocols):**
> 1. **Confusing QA with QC** — many students write "QA is checking after the work" → *lose 1 full mark*. Always state QA is **preventive, process-focused**.
> 2. **Forgetting Hold Points** — an ITP without *Hold Points* gets only 4/7 marks. Always mention *Pre-Pour Hold Point*.
> 3. **No numerical example in SQC question** — for a 7-mark SQC question, failing to compute $C_p$ with numbers costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Stripping time = 24 hours for ALL members** — wrong! IS 456 differentiates between vertical and horizontal, and span length.
> 5. **Citing ACI 318 instead of ACI 347** — for *formwork* the correct reference is **ACI 347** (formwork for concrete), not ACI 318 (concrete design).
> 6. **Mixing up $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$** — $C_p$ assumes process is centered; $C_{pk}$ accounts for off-centering. KTU expects the distinction.
> 7. **No diagram in long answers** — for a 14-mark question, a flowchart or schematic adds **1–2 marks** in valuation (especially for ITP questions).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE – Module 3, Quality Assurance Protocols:**

- ✅ **QA is preventive and process-oriented; QC is detective and product-oriented.**
- ✅ **PDCA cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) is the backbone of any QA system.
- ✅ **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** must include **Hold Points** — especially the *Pre-Pour Hold Point*.
- ✅ **Acceptance tolerances (IS 14687 / ACI 117):**
  - Plumb: ± 6 mm in 3 m
  - Alignment: ± 13 mm in 3 m
  - Deflection: L/360 (visible), L/270 (concealed)
- ✅ **Concrete lateral pressure** = minimum of (hydrostatic, ACI limit) — typically 30–80 kN/m².
- ✅ **Cube strength acceptance (IS 456 Cl. 16.1):** $f_{ck,act} \geq f_{ck} - 0.825\sigma$ (and individual cube ≥ $f_{ck} - 3.5$ MPa).
- ✅ **Stripping times (IS 456 Cl. 11.3):** Vertical 24–48 h, slabs 3 d, props 7–21 d depending on span.
- ✅ **SQC tools:** X-bar chart (mean), R-chart (variability), $C_p$ index (capability), $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ constants from tables.
- ✅ **Maturity method** uses temperature-time integral: $M = \Sigma (T_i - T_0) \cdot \Delta t$.
- ✅ **Common formwork defects:** Honeycombing, bulging, cold joint, staining, misalignment, collapse, sagging, blowout, laitance.
- ✅ **Standards to remember:**
  - **IS 14687** – Falsework and formwork (Indian)
  - **IS 456:2000** – Plain and Reinforced Concrete (Indian)
  - **ACI 347** – Formwork for Concrete (American)
  - **ACI SP-4** – Formwork for Concrete (handbook)
  - **IS 2062** – Steel for general structural purposes
  - **IS 4990** – Plywood for concrete shuttering
- ✅ **Typical pour rate limit:** 1.5 m/hr (for ordinary concrete) to control lateral pressure.
- ✅ **Prop torque:** 40–60 N·m (standard) for safe bracing.
- ✅ **Statistical confidence for SQC:** 95% (k = 1.96) for two-tailed; 99.73% (k = 3) for 3-sigma control charts.
- ✅ **Total Quality Management (TQM)** involves customer focus, continuous improvement (Kaizen), employee involvement, and fact-based decision making.
- ✅ **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a key QA tool to identify potential formwork failures at the design stage.
- ✅ **Root cause analysis methods:** 5-Why analysis, Ishikawa (fishbone) diagram, Pareto chart.
- ✅ **Documentation hierarchy:** Quality Manual → Project Quality Plan → ITP → Method Statement → Checklists → Site Records.
- ✅ **NCR (Non-Conformance Report)** is issued when material or work fails to meet specifications; closed only after **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action).
- ✅ **In KTU 14-mark questions**, always include a **diagram/flowchart** — it can fetch an extra 1–2 marks.
- ✅ **For the maturity method**, the Nurse-Saul equation is the standard; the Plowman equation is an advanced alternative.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Practical Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies and Practical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Case Studies and Practical Applications** in the context of Formwork Engineering refer to the systematic documentation, critical analysis, and post-construction evaluation of real-world formwork projects — encompassing successful executions, documented structural failures, cost optimization exercises, and innovative applications in special structures. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic trains the student to bridge the gap between theoretical design (Modules 1 & 2) and field execution by interpreting empirical evidence drawn from bridge, dam, high-rise, tunnel, and industrial formwork projects.

A **Formwork Case Study** is typically structured around four pillars:
1. **Project Profile** — type of structure, geometry, concrete volume, location, and timeline.
2. **Formwork System Adopted** — conventional timber, plywood, steel, aluminium, modular (e.g., Doka, PERI, Mivan), climbing, or jump-form.
3. **Performance Metrics** — cycle time, repetition factor, labour productivity, deflection, and cost per square metre.
4. **Lessons Learned** — failure mechanisms, root cause analysis (RCA), and corrective design philosophy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module is largely *qualitative-analytical*. Marks are awarded for: (a) correct identification of the failure mode, (b) quantitative reasoning around cost/cycle time, and (c) drawing engineering judgments that connect code provisions to field realities.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Case File"

Imagine formwork engineering theory as a **medical textbook** and a real construction site as a **hospital ward**. A *case study* in this module is the **patient's case file** — it consolidates symptoms (deflections, honeycombing, bulging), diagnosis (root cause: inadequate bracing, premature stripping, over-pressure), treatment (retropropping, redesign), and the doctor's note (lessons learned). 

Just as a medical intern studies case files to become a better doctor, a formwork engineer studies documented case studies (collapsed slabs, failed shores, failed climbing systems) to internalize *why* design codes (IS 14687, ACI 347, CIRIA Report 108) prescribe the limits they do. **Codes are written in the ink of past failures** — this module decodes that ink.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Statistic in Formwork Engineering:** Approximately **35% to 60%** of the cost of a concrete structure is attributable to its formwork (material + labour + accessories). Even a **5% efficiency gain** in formwork utilization on a high-rise project can translate to **crores of rupees** in savings — which is why case-study-driven design optimization is a high-value engineering skill.

## 1.3 Scope of the Module

The practical applications surveyed under this module broadly fall into these families:

| Family of Application | Typical Structure | Dominant Formwork Type |
|---|---|---|
| Repetitive Vertical Construction | High-rise residential towers | **Mivan / Aluform / Tunnel Form** |
| Heavy Civil Concrete | Dams, cooling towers, chimneys | **Slip-form, Jump-form** |
| Bridge Superstructure | Segmental, balanced cantilever | **Launching Gantry, Cantilever Form** |
| Underground Structures | Metro tunnels, caverns | **Travelling Steel Form** |
| Nuclear & Containment | Reactor buildings, safety shells | **Custom Climbing / Lined Forms** |
| Repair & Rehabilitation | Strengthening of existing RCC | **Suspended Scaffolding, Custom Carriage** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Archetypes of Formwork Case Studies

A KTU examiner expects the student to recognize that formwork case studies cluster into **three analytical archetypes**. Mastering the *type* of a case is half the answer.

### 2.1.1 Archetype A — Failure / Collapse Case Studies
- **Trigger:** Loss of lateral pressure resistance, premature stripping, inadequate bracing, settlement of base, inadequate shore capacity.
- **Common Failure Locations:** Slab-soffit, beam sides, column sides, wall-bulkhead junctions, shore heads.
- **Engineering Lesson:** Quantify the *margin of safety* that was lost and map it to a specific code clause.

### 2.1.2 Archetype B — Successful / Optimized Execution Case Studies
- **Trigger:** Achievement of short cycle times (often 3–4 days/floor), high repetition factor (>100), low cost per m².
- **Engineering Lesson:** Identify which design and management decisions drove the efficiency — and whether they are transferable.

### 2.1.3 Archetype C — Innovative / Special Structure Case Studies
- **Trigger:** Application of a non-standard formwork system to a complex geometry (e.g., hyperbolic cooling tower, free-form architectural shell, high-tower jump-form climbing on sloped surface).
- **Engineering Lesson:** Translate the *innovation* into a generalized design heuristic.

## 2.2 The Six Standard Analytical Questions

When a case study is presented in a KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) question, the examiner expects answers structured around these six questions — in this order:

1. **WHAT** formwork system was used? (Naming + brief description)
2. **WHY** was that system chosen? (Decision drivers: cost, speed, repetition, geometry)
3. **HOW** was the system designed? (Pressure assumptions, member sizing, ties, bracing)
4. **WHAT WENT WRONG / WHAT WENT RIGHT?** (Performance evaluation)
5. **WHAT WERE THE ROOT CAUSES / SUCCESS DRIVERS?** (RCA or success-factor mapping)
6. **WHAT ARE THE TAKEAWAYS?** (Generalized engineering lesson)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Always explicitly answer the "WHY" — it carries 2–3 marks by itself in a 14-mark question.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (For Case-Study Quantitative Analysis)

While the topic is qualitative, the following formulas are *invariably* tested in either Part A (definitions) or Part B (calculation-based case discussion). Memorize the **bolded** entries.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Formwork Cost as % of Concrete Cost** | $C_{fw}\% = \dfrac{C_{formwork}}{C_{concrete\ structure}} \times 100$ | **%** | Industry benchmark: **35%–60%** |
| 2 | **Amortized Cost per Use** | $C_{use} = \dfrac{C_{capital} + C_{maint} \times n}{n \times A}$ | Rs/m² | $n$ = number of repetitions, $A$ = contact area |
| 3 | **Repetition Factor (RF)** | $RF = \dfrac{\text{Total contact area}}{\text{Total formwork area procured}}$ | dimensionless | $RF > 50$ is "good" for high-rise |
| 4 | **Cycle Time (CT)** | $CT = t_{strip} + t_{erect} + t_{pour} + t_{cure\ to\ strike}$ | hours/floor | Mivan target: **48–72 hrs** |
| 5 | **Lateral Pressure (ACI 347, simplified for case use)** | $p = \min\left(\gamma_c H,\ C_1 + \dfrac{C_2 R}{T}\right)$ | kN/m² | For analysis of *failure* cases |
| 6 | **Hydrostatic Equivalent Head** | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{p_{max}}{\gamma_c}$ | m | Compare to actual pour height $H$ |
| 7 | **Bulging Deflection Check** | $\delta = \dfrac{w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | mm | For plywood sheathing |
| 8 | **Shore Load Capacity Required** | $P_{shore} = w_{slab} \cdot A_{trib} \cdot SF$ | kN | $SF = 1.5$ to **2.0** typical |
| 9 | **Labour Productivity (LP)** | $LP = \dfrac{\text{Formwork area (m²)}}{\text{Man-hours}}$ | m²/MH | Indian benchmark: **0.15 – 0.30 m²/MH** |
| 10 | **Project Formwork Cost Estimate** | $C_{total} = C_{mat} + C_{lab} + C_{access} + C_{maint}$ | Rs | Used in cost-comparison case studies |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe character in tables:** Notice that the formulas use `\vert` only in the table description column (column 2) to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. In your own exam sheets, the absolute-value/lateral-pressure bars in $p = \min(\dots)$ are typeset in standard LaTeX and do **not** require the pipe character.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Formula — Real Engineering Utility

- **Repetition Factor (RF):** *Why* does it matter? Because in any cost optimization case study, the entire economic argument of choosing aluminium / Mivan over conventional timber hinges on $n$. If a building has fewer than 20 repetitions per panel, Mivan is uneconomical — the *case study conclusion* depends on this.
- **Cycle Time (CT):** *Why* is it important? Because every 1-day reduction in CT per floor of a 40-floor tower saves **40 days of project duration** — directly translating to financing cost savings (often **Rs 5–10 lakh/day** on large projects).
- **Shore Load $P_{shore}$:** *Why* is it central to failure case studies? Because **>70% of documented formwork collapses** originate at the *shore level* — not the soffit — meaning the student must demonstrate quantitative capacity checks, not just qualitative blame.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Exercise 1 — Cost Optimization Case Study

**Case Statement (KTU-style):** A contractor is evaluating two formwork systems for a 30-storey residential tower (slab area per floor = 350 m², total = 10,500 m²). 
- **System X:** Conventional timber-plywood, capital cost = Rs 800/m², can be reused 8 times.
- **System Y:** Aluminium (Mivan-type), capital cost = Rs 4,500/m², can be reused 120 times.
- Labour cost is Rs 250/m² for both systems.
- Maintenance cost is 10% of capital cost spread over total reuses.

**Task:** Compute the amortized cost per m² of contact area for each system and recommend.

### Step 1 — Capital Cost per m² of Use for System X (Timber)

The amortized capital cost per m² per use is:

$$
C_{X,capital} = \dfrac{800}{8} = \text{Rs }100 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Maintenance Cost per m² per Use for System X

Total maintenance over 8 reuses = 10% of Rs 800 = Rs 80.
Per-use maintenance:

$$
C_{X,maint} = \dfrac{80}{8} = \text{Rs }10 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Amortized Cost for System X

$$
C_{X,total} = C_{X,capital} + C_{X,maint} + C_{labour} = 100 + 10 + 250
$$

$$
C_{X,total} = \text{Rs }360 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Capital Cost per m² per Use for System Y (Aluminium)

$$
C_{Y,capital} = \dfrac{4500}{120} = \text{Rs }37.50 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Maintenance Cost per m² per Use for System Y

$$
C_{Y,maint} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 4500}{120} = \dfrac{450}{120} = \text{Rs }3.75 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Amortized Cost for System Y

$$
C_{Y,total} = 37.50 + 3.75 + 250 = \text{Rs }291.25 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

### Step 7 — Recommendation

Since $C_{Y,total} < C_{X,total}$:

$$
\boxed{C_{Y,total} = 291.25 \text{ Rs/m}^{2} \;<\; C_{X,total} = 360 \text{ Rs/m}^{2}}
$$

**System Y (Aluminium) is recommended.** Savings per m² = Rs 68.75. Total project savings = $68.75 \times 10{,}500 = $ **Rs 7,21,875** (≈ Rs 7.2 lakhs), excluding the additional savings from shorter cycle time (faster shuttering → reduced project duration → reduced interest on borrowed capital).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why repetition factor dominates:** The $1/n$ relationship in the amortized cost formula means aluminium's high capital cost is offset almost entirely by its 15× higher reuse count. The student must always compute *both* the per-use cost *and* a payback/breakeven check.

### Step 8 — Breakeven Repetition Count for System Y

Setting $C_{X,total} = C_{Y,total}$:

$$
\dfrac{4500 + 0.10 \times 4500}{n} + 250 = 360
$$

$$
\dfrac{4950}{n} = 110 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \dfrac{4950}{110} = 45
$$

So System Y becomes cheaper only after **45 repetitions** — confirming that Mivan is unsuitable for buildings with low repetition. This is the **single most important quantitative conclusion** of any Mivan-vs-timber case study.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Exercise 2 — Failure Case Analysis (Quantitative)

**Case Statement:** A slab panel (6 m × 4 m) supported on 12 numbers of adjustable steel shores collapsed 18 hours after concreting. The slab thickness is 200 mm. Each shore was rated 20 kN safe working load. Compute the actual load per shore and the *Factor of Safety* that was violated.

### Step 1 — Weight of Fresh Concrete Slab (per m²)

Self-weight of fresh concrete (use $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³):

$$
w_{slab} = \gamma_c \times t = 25 \times 0.20 = 5.0 \text{ kN/m}^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Add Live Load on Green Concrete (IS 456, min. 1.5 kN/m²)

$$
w_{design} = 5.0 + 1.5 = 6.5 \text{ kN/m}^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Tributary Area per Shore

The shores were placed in a regular grid. With 12 shores on a 6 m × 4 m panel:

$$
A_{trib} = \dfrac{6 \times 4}{12} = 2.0 \text{ m}^{2} \text{ per shore}
$$

### Step 4 — Load per Shore

$$
P_{shore} = w_{design} \times A_{trib} = 6.5 \times 2.0 = 13.0 \text{ kN}
$$

This is *less* than the rated 20 kN — so the rated capacity is *not* the failure cause! The examiner expects the student to dig deeper.

### Step 5 — Re-strike / Premature Loading Check

The collapse occurred at 18 hours. The cube strength of concrete at 18 hours is typically only ~**25%** of the 28-day strength. If the slab was being re-shored or loaded with stacked material (e.g., brick pallets, cement bags), the impact is significant.

**Imposed construction load** during early age (often unaccounted for):

$$
P_{early} = (5.0 + 1.5 + q_{construction}) \times A_{trib}
$$

If $q_{construction} = 5.0$ kN/m² (stacked materials, equipment, manpower):

$$
P_{early} = (5.0 + 1.5 + 5.0) \times 2.0 = 23.0 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 6 — Factor of Safety Realized

$$
SF_{realized} = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{P_{early}} = \dfrac{20.0}{23.0} = 0.87
$$

$$
\boxed{SF_{realized} \approx 0.87 < 1.0 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{COLLAPSE is consistent with the data.}}
$$

### Step 7 — Engineering Conclusion

The *root cause* is **premature striking combined with unaccounted construction live load** — not shore rating. **Lesson:** Always design shores for the *worst-expected construction live load*, not merely the slab self-weight, and never strip before specified re-shoring strength is achieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Pattern for KTU:** Examiner awards 7 marks for arriving at the correct $SF_{realized}$ and an additional 7 marks for correctly identifying the *root cause* (premature loading / unaccounted live load) and prescribing the *corrective measure* (re-design of construction-stage loading, use of re-shoring, maturity-based striking).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting Formwork for a Given Project

The following matrix is a KTU-typical comparison tool that students may be asked to *fill in* during the ESE:

| Selection Criterion | Conventional Timber | Plywood + Steel Props | Aluminium (Mivan) | Climbing / Jump-Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost (Rs/m²) | 500 – 900 | 800 – 1,400 | 4,000 – 5,500 | 8,000 – 15,000 |
| Repetitions | 4 – 8 | 20 – 40 | 100 – 150 | 200+ |
| Cycle Time (days/floor) | 7 – 10 | 5 – 7 | **3 – 4** | 4 – 6 |
| Labour Skill | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Suitable for | Small, irregular | Mid-rise | Repetitive high-rise | Towers, chimneys |
| Formwork Cost as % of Structure | 50 – 60% | 40 – 50% | **30 – 35%** | 35 – 45% |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Root Cause Analysis (Fishbone-Style Flow) for Formwork Failure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[FORMWORK FAILURE EVENT] --> B1[Deflection / Cracking]
    A0 --> B2[Bulging of Sheathing]
    A0 --> B3[Collapse of Shore / Prop]
    A0 --> B4[Panel Blowout]

    B1 --> C1[Inadequate Plywood Thickness]
    B1 --> C2[Excessive Spacing of Bearers]

    B2 --> C3[Lateral Pressure > Design Value]
    B2 --> C4[Inadequate Waler / Tie Spacing]
    C3 --> D1[High Rate of Pour]
    C3 --> D2[Use of Superplasticizer without pressure re-check]
    C3 --> D3[Retardation of concrete in hot weather]

    B3 --> C5[Premature Striking]
    B3 --> C6[Unaccounted Construction Live Load]
    B3 --> C7[Base Settlement of Prop]

    B4 --> C8[Inadequate Bulkhead Anchoring]
    B4 --> C9[Form Tie Failure]

    C1 --> E1[ROOT CAUSE: Design Error]
    C2 --> E1
    C4 --> E1
    C5 --> E2[ROOT CAUSE: Construction Stage Error]
    C6 --> E2
    C7 --> E2
    C8 --> E3[ROOT CAUSE: Detailing Failure]
    C9 --> E3
    D1 --> E4[ROOT CAUSE: Concrete Mix - Placement Mismatch]
    D2 --> E4
    D3 --> E4

    E1 --> F1[LESSON: Recompute per IS 14687 / ACI 347]
    E2 --> F2[LESSON: Maturity-based striking + load audit]
    E3 --> F3[LESSON: Detailing audit at junctions]
    E4 --> F4[LESSON: Pressure-time curve must match mix design]

    F1 --> G1[CORRECTED FORMWORK DESIGN]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Formwork System Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[START: Define Project] --> P1{Building Height?}
    P1 -->|Up to 4 storeys| P2[Conventional Timber / Plywood]
    P1 -->|5 to 15 storeys| P3{Repetition Factor RF?}
    P1 -->|15 storeys and above| P4[Modular Aluminium / Mivan]

    P3 -->|RF less than 30| P5[Plywood + Steel Props]
    P3 -->|RF 30 to 60| P6[Semi-System Aluminium]
    P3 -->|RF greater than 60| P7[Full Mivan System]

    P2 --> Q1{Need Speed?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2[Use Timber - Lowest Capital]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3[Use Plywood with Quick-Strip]

    P5 --> R1[Verify 30 to 50 Repetitions Achievable]
    P6 --> R2[Verify Cycle Time Target 5 to 6 days]
    P7 --> R3[Target 3 to 4 Day Cycle]
    P4 --> R3

    Q2 --> S[END: System Selected]
    Q3 --> S
    R1 --> S
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Cycle-Time Reduction Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT VARIABLES"]
        A1[Concrete Grade]
        A2[Slab Area per Floor]
        A3[Formwork System]
        A4[Number of Crews]
        A5[Weather Conditions]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["CYCLE TIME REDUCTION DRIVERS"]
        B1[Early Stripping with Re-shoring]
        B2[Use of Self-Compacting Concrete]
        B3[Modular Table Forms]
        B4[Parallel Activities - Rebar + MEP + Form]
        B5[Chemical Accelerators]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["PERFORMANCE METRICS"]
        C1[Cycle Time in hrs per floor]
        C2[Cost per m²]
        C3[Labour Productivity m² per MH]
        C4[Reuse Count]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    A4 --> B4
    A5 --> B5
    A1 --> B2

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C2
    B1 --> C3
    B4 --> C3
    B1 --> C4
    B3 --> C4

    C1 --> OUT[OPTIMIZED FORMWORK EXECUTION]
    C2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
    C4 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Failure-Case Documentation Template (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[CASE STUDY ENTRY] --> F1[Project Metadata]
    F1 --> F1a[Structure Type]
    F1 --> F1b[Location and Date]
    F1 --> F1c[Stakeholders]

    F0 --> F2[Formwork System Used]
    F2 --> F2a[System Description]
    F2 --> F2b[Design Code Adopted]
    F2 --> F2c[Member Sizes and Spacing]

    F0 --> F3[Failure or Success Description]
    F3 --> F3a[What Happened]
    F3 --> F3b[Time of Event]
    F3 --> F3c[Extent of Damage]

    F0 --> F4[Root Cause Analysis]
    F4 --> F4a[Design Stage Issues]
    F4 --> F4b[Construction Stage Issues]
    F4 --> F4c[Material or Detailing Issues]

    F0 --> F5[Engineering Lessons]
    F5 --> F5a[Code Compliance Check]
    F5 --> F5b[Corrective Measure]
    F5 --> F5c[Transferable Heuristic]

    F5a --> Z[KTU EXAMINER-READY SUMMARY]
    F5b --> Z
    F5c --> Z
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**"Why are case studies considered an essential part of formwork engineering education and practice? List any four reasons with brief explanation."**

**Model Answer (≈ 80–100 words):**
1. **Failure Prevention** — Case studies of past collapses (e.g., shore failures, slab blowouts) sensitize designers to failure modes that codes alone do not make vivid.
2. **Decision Calibration** — Real cost and cycle-time data allow comparison of conventional vs. modular systems under authentic site conditions, calibrating the engineer's judgement.
3. **Innovation Transfer** — Special-structure case studies (cooling towers, bridges, tunnels) document *non-standard* solutions that are not in textbooks.
4. **Risk Quantification** — A documented failure case forces quantification of safety factors, lateral pressures, and load combinations under real field variables.
5. **Continuous Improvement** — Industry-wide case-study databases (PERI, Doka, CIRIA) act as living references that update code provisions over time.

*(Any 4 well-explained points secure full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**"Define 'Repetition Factor' in formwork engineering. State its typical range for high-rise residential construction using modular aluminium formwork."**

**Model Answer:**
**Repetition Factor (RF)** is the dimensionless ratio of *total formwork contact area required* to *total formwork area procured*, indicating how many times the same formwork set is reused.

$$
RF = \dfrac{\text{Total Contact Area of Concrete (m}^2\text{)}}{\text{Total Formwork Area Procured (m}^2\text{)}}
$$

**Typical Range for High-Rise Residential with Aluminium Formwork:** $RF = 100$ to $150$ (sometimes reported up to 200 for very tall, geometrically repetitive towers). Below $RF = 30$, aluminium systems become uneconomical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer EITHER Question B1 OR Question B2

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks) — Option A**

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]`
>
> **(a)** A 25-storey residential tower has a typical floor slab area of 320 m². The contractor is comparing **conventional timber-plywood** (capital cost Rs 850/m², 8 reuses) with **aluminium modular formwork** (capital cost Rs 4,800/m², 120 reuses). Labour cost is Rs 280/m² for both systems. Compute the amortized cost per m² for each system and recommend the more economical option. **Assume maintenance = 8% of capital cost spread over total reuses.** **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **two documented case studies** of formwork failure (one in a high-rise building and one in a heavy civil structure), focusing on the **root cause, the engineering lesson learned, and the corrective design philosophy** that would have prevented the failure. **Use proper technical terminology.** **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — B1(a)

**Step 1 — Capital cost per use (Timber, System X):**

$$
C_{X,cap} = \dfrac{850}{8} = \text{Rs }106.25 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Maintenance per use (Timber):**

$$
C_{X,maint} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 850}{8} = \dfrac{68}{8} = \text{Rs }8.50 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Total amortized cost (Timber):**

$$
C_{X,total} = 106.25 + 8.50 + 280 = \text{Rs }394.75 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Capital cost per use (Aluminium, System Y):**

$$
C_{Y,cap} = \dfrac{4800}{120} = \text{Rs }40.00 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Maintenance per use (Aluminium):**

$$
C_{Y,maint} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 4800}{120} = \dfrac{384}{120} = \text{Rs }3.20 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Total amortized cost (Aluminium):**

$$
C_{Y,total} = 40.00 + 3.20 + 280 = \text{Rs }323.20 \text{ per m}^{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

Since $C_{Y,total} = 323.20 < 394.75 = C_{X,total}$:

$$
\boxed{\text{System Y (Aluminium) is more economical.}}
$$

**Total project savings** = $(394.75 - 323.20) \times 320 \times 25 = 71.55 \times 8000 = $ **Rs 5,72,400.**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — B1(b)

**Case Study 1 — High-Rise: Shore Collapse During Slab Striking (Documented Indian Residential Project, Generic Profile):**
- **What:** A 22-storey tower's 5th-floor slab collapsed during the early striking of shores at ~36 hours.
- **Root Cause:** The contractor had stacked **~150 cement bags** on the freshly cast slab to facilitate work on the floor above, imposing an unaccounted construction live load of ~5 kN/m². Combined with self-weight, the per-shore load exceeded the rated capacity, with a realized SF < 1.0.
- **Engineering Lesson:** *Form shores must be designed for construction-stage loads, not just self-weight of the green concrete.* The slab is *not* a working platform until it reaches the specified re-shoring strength (typically 70% of $f_{ck}$ for 28-day target).
- **Corrective Design Philosophy:** Introduce a *maturity-based striking schedule* (e.g., equivalent age of 3 days at 20°C), enforce a *loading permit* protocol for material stacking, and audit shores at every floor with calibrated load cells or jacking tests.

**Case Study 2 — Heavy Civil: Cooling Tower Slip-Form Failure (Documented Thermal Power Project, Generic Profile):**
- **What:** A 150 m natural-draft cooling tower undergoing slip-form concreting experienced a localized sheathing blowout at ~70 m elevation, with ~40 m³ of green concrete lost.
- **Root Cause:** Sudden *retardation of concrete setting* due to a cold-weather front increased the effective lateral pressure beyond the design value (the slip-form was designed for $p = 60$ kN/m²; actual approached ~85 kN/m²). Additionally, the bulkhead anchoring at the lift joint was inadequate.
- **Engineering Lesson:** *Concrete lateral pressure is highly sensitive to setting time, admixtures, and ambient temperature.* The slip-form must be designed with a *pressure envelope* that accounts for the *worst-case retardation* during the construction window.
- **Corrective Design Philosophy:** Implement *real-time pressure monitoring* with sacrificial pressure cells on the slip-form yokes, design for $1.5\times$ the ACI 347 estimated pressure as a safety envelope, and *pre-qualify the concrete mix* for the *expected ambient temperature range*, switching to an accelerating admixture if cold fronts are forecast.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Most students *narrate* the case study but do not *quantify* the failure. To secure the full 7 marks in part (b), you must (a) name the *specific structural element* that failed, (b) identify the *exact physical quantity* that was exceeded (pressure, deflection, load, or SF), and (c) prescribe a *specific code-aligned corrective measure* (cite IS 14687 / ACI 347 / CIRIA 108 where relevant). A purely qualitative answer scores at most 3 of 7 marks.

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks) — Option B**

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]`
>
> **(a)** A 200 mm thick slab is being cast over an area of 6 m × 4 m, supported on 12 adjustable steel shores. Each shore has a safe working load of 18 kN. The fresh concrete self-weight is 25 kN/m³, and an additional construction live load of 4 kN/m² is expected on the green slab. **Check whether the shore system is safe.** Compute the realized factor of safety. **What is the most likely failure mode if the system fails?** **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a **clearly labelled flow diagram or block diagram**, explain the **methodology of conducting a formwork case-study analysis** in practice. Your answer should cover: (i) data collection, (ii) root cause identification, (iii) lessons learned, and (iv) translation into design guidelines. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — B2(a)

**Step 1 — Slab self-weight per m²:**

$$
w_{sw} = 25 \times 0.20 = 5.0 \text{ kN/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Total design load per m² (ULS-style for construction stage):**

$$
w_{design} = w_{sw} + w_{LL} = 5.0 + 4.0 = 9.0 \text{ kN/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Tributary area per shore:**

$$
A_{trib} = \dfrac{6 \times 4}{12} = 2.0 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Load per shore:**

$$
P_{shore} = w_{design} \times A_{trib} = 9.0 \times 2.0 = 18.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor of Safety realized:**

$$
SF_{realized} = \dfrac{P_{SWL}}{P_{shore}} = \dfrac{18.0}{18.0} = 1.0
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{SF_{realized} = 1.0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{The system is at the verge of failure, NOT safe.}}
$$

A safe design requires $SF \geq 1.5$ to $2.0$. The *most likely failure mode* is **buckling of the shore prop** (since the load is at the prop's rated capacity with zero margin) or **base-plate punching failure** into the supporting slab below. Premature collapse during striking is also probable if concrete strength is below the re-shoring threshold.

**Step 7 — Corrective Action:**
- Reduce shore spacing to provide $A_{trib} \leq 1.5$ m², OR
- Use higher-capacity shores (≥ 30 kN SWL), OR
- Restrict construction live load to ≤ 1.5 kN/m² during early age.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — B2(b) — Case-Study Analysis Methodology

The student is expected to present a structured, sequential flow. The model answer integrates the four required sub-parts as a *block-level process*:

> **[Step 1 — Data Collection]:** Gather (i) project metadata (type, height, date, location, contractor), (ii) formwork design records (drawings, member sizes, spacing, code adopted), (iii) execution records (pour rate, ambient conditions, sequence), (iv) failure/success description (location, extent, time of event, photographs, post-event reports).
>
> **[Step 2 — Root Cause Identification]:** Apply a **Fishbone (Ishikawa) framework** with branches: *Design*, *Material*, *Execution*, *Detailing*, *Environment*, *Management*. For each branch, ask "What is the *primary contributor*?" Use quantitative checks (e.g., recompute lateral pressure, recompute shore load, recompute deflection) to *verify or reject* each hypothesis. Cross-reference with IS 14687, ACI 347, and CIRIA Report 108.
>
> **[Step 3 — Lessons Learned]:** Document (a) the *quantitative margin* by which the design was exceeded, (b) the *code clause* that was violated, and (c) the *transferable design heuristic* (e.g., "For piles of height > 4 m using superplasticizer, adopt $p = 1.5 \times$ ACI nominal").
>
> **[Step 4 — Translation into Design Guidelines]:** Convert the case study into (i) a *checklist item* for future projects, (ii) a *design rule* in the firm's standard operating procedure, and (iii) a *training input* for site engineers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (B2 Part b):** Students often write a *generic* description of case-study analysis without binding it to **formwork engineering specifically**. Every step of your flow must explicitly use *formwork* vocabulary (lateral pressure, shore, tie, waler, re-shoring, stripping time, repetition factor) — not generic project-management vocabulary. A non-formwork-specific answer will be capped at 4/7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Define** case study as: *systematic documentation + critical analysis + engineering lessons* from real formwork projects.
- [ ] **Recognize three archetypes:** Failure, Success, and Innovation.
- [ ] **Apply the six standard questions** (WHAT, WHY, HOW, OUTCOME, RCA, TAKEAWAY) in every answer.
- [ ] **Recall key benchmark numbers:**
  - Formwork cost = **35%–60%** of total concrete structure cost.
  - Aluminium Mivan repetitions = **100–150**; breakeven ≈ **45 reuses**.
  - Typical cycle time (Mivan) = **3–4 days/floor**; (Conventional) = **7–10 days/floor**.
  - Construction live load on green slab = **1.5–5.0 kN/m²** (often unaccounted!).
  - Required shore SF = **1.5 to 2.0**.
  - Lateral pressure envelope for slip-form = up to **1.5× ACI 347 nominal**.
- [ ] **Quantify, do not narrate:** Every case-study answer must include a *numerical check* (load, pressure, SF, cost, time).
- [ ] **Always cite the root cause** — not just describe the failure.
- [ ] **Connect to code:** IS 14687, ACI 347, CIRIA 108 — the examiner awards 1–2 marks for correct code reference.
- [ ] **Use block diagrams / flow charts** in long answers — the methodology question (B2b) is *visual* in nature.
- [ ] **Common pitfall:** Confusing "formwork failure" with "concrete failure" — they are *not* the same. The formwork is the *temporary structure*; the concrete is the *permanent structure*. State explicitly which one failed.
- [ ] **Repetition Factor (RF)** is the single most important economic lever — always compute breakeven $n$ in cost-comparison questions.
- [ ] **Mivan (aluminium) is NOT always cheaper** — for low-RF projects (< 30), conventional timber wins.
- [ ] **Cycle time savings = financing cost savings** — this is the hidden economic argument in high-rise case studies.
- [ ] **Failure of shores, not soffits, dominates collapse statistics** — always check shore capacity with construction-stage live loads included.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Case Studies and Practical Applications | PECET742 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Real-World Formwork Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Real-World Formwork Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Analysis of Real-World Formwork Projects* refers to the systematic post-construction and concurrent evaluation of executed formwork systems on actual building, infrastructure, and industrial projects. It encompasses the assessment of **structural performance**, **economic viability**, **cycle time efficiency**, **safety records**, **quality of finished concrete**, and **lessons learned** from field-level case studies, with the objective of refining design, specification, and execution strategies for future projects.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, this analysis is mapped to **Course Outcome CO3 (Module 3)**: *Analyze, evaluate, and critique real-world formwork applications to derive engineering judgment, cost-effective solutions, and safety-conscious design decisions.*

### Sub-Domains of Real-World Project Analysis

The analytical framework is built upon four pillars:

1. **Performance Pillar** – Structural adequacy, deflection control, and load transfer behavior.
2. **Economic Pillar** – Cost per square meter, reuse cycles achieved, and wastage percentages.
3. **Temporal Pillar** – Cycle time achieved versus planned, strip-and-re-erect productivity.
4. **Safety Pillar** – Incident records, near-miss reports, and compliance with **IS 14687:1999** and **OSHA 1926.703** provisions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Movie Director's Cut"**
>
> Imagine a film director who finishes shooting a movie. The *director's cut* is the post-production review — "Did the lighting work? Did the actors perform as expected? Where did the budget overrun? What will I do differently next time?" The Analysis of Real-World Formwork Projects is essentially the *director's cut* of the formwork engineer. Once the slab is cast, columns stripped, and walls stripped clean, the engineer reviews footage (site records, photographs, RFIs, pour logs) to ask: *Did the formwork perform as designed? Was it economical? Was it safe? What failed? What surprised us?*
>
> The "audience" for this review is every future project — the lessons learned reduce repetition of mistakes and amplify best practices.

A second intuition is to think of it as a **post-mortem with a forward-looking prescription** — every failed wedge, every bulging wall, every delay teaches the industry something quantifiable.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following key performance indicators (KPIs) are used universally in real-world formwork project analysis:

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Source / Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| Formwork Cost | **₹ per m² of contact area** | CPWD DSR / Industry average |
| Reuse Cycles | **Number (typically 50–200)** | System manufacturer data |
| Stripping Time | **Hours / days** | IS 456:2000 Table 11 |
| Cycle Time | **Days per floor** | Project scheduling software |
| Labour Productivity | **m² of contact area per manday** | IS 7272 |
| Deflection Limit | **Span / 360 to Span / 270** | ACI SP-4 / IS 14687 |
| Lateral Pressure | **kN/m² (max 95–100 for 1.5 m pour rate)** | ACI 347 / CIRIA 108 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECET742):** The Module 3 syllabus specifically demands that students study **at least two real case studies** of formwork projects (one successful and one failed), evaluate their technical and economic parameters, and propose design/process improvements grounded in **Indian Standard codes and ACI guidelines.**

---

## 1.4 Significance in B.Tech Civil Engineering Practice

For a KTU 2024 B.Tech Civil Engineering graduate, the ability to *read, dissect, and learn from real projects* is what differentiates a textbook engineer from a site-ready professional. Employers (L&T, Tata Projects, Shapoorji Pallonji, NCC, Afcons) routinely test candidates on case-study-based questions during placement interviews, demanding familiarity with both **success stories** (e.g., rapid metro viaduct construction) and **catastrophic failures** (e.g., formwork collapse inquiries).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Real-World Project Analysis

The analytical process is structured into a **six-step investigative protocol** that engineers, auditors, and researchers follow when dissecting a real-world formwork project.

### Step 1 — Data Acquisition
- Gather **as-built drawings**, **shop drawings**, **pour cards**, **BBS (Bar Bending Schedules)**, and **inspection logs**.
- Source **Daily Progress Reports (DPRs)**, **incident registers**, and **quality control test results** (slump, cube strength).
- This step answers: *What was the design intent, and what was actually built?*

### Step 2 — Quantitative Performance Benchmarking
- Compare the **actual cycle time** with the **planned cycle time**.
- Quantify the **formwork cost** as a percentage of the total structural cost (typically **8–12%** for buildings, **15–22%** for bridges).
- Compute the **labour productivity** index: m²/manday.

### Step 3 — Defect and Failure Mapping
- Identify honeycombing, bulges, offsets, leakages, tie-rod failures, and prop settlements.
- Plot these defects on **plan-and-section overlays** to detect patterns (e.g., all defects at corners, or all at high pour rates).

### Step 4 — Root-Cause Analysis (RCA)
- Apply the **5-Why technique** or **Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram**.
- Determine whether the root cause is **design-driven**, **material-driven**, **execution-driven**, or **environment-driven**.

### Step 5 — Economic and Schedule Impact Quantification
- Translate delays and defects into **monetary loss** (e.g., ₹50,000 per day delay for a typical 30-storey project).
- Compute **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs.

### Step 6 — Lessons Learned and Design Improvement
- Codify findings into a **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** update.
- Issue **revised specifications** for tie-rod spacing, prop spacing, shore bracing, and pour-rate limits.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield formulas used in real-world formwork project analysis. Master these for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) numerical/analytical questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Cost per m}^2 = \frac{\text{Total Formwork Cost}}{\text{Total Contact Area}}$ | Unit cost metric | ₹ / m² |
| 2 | $\text{Reuse Factor} = \frac{\text{Total m}^2 \text{ of formwork used}}{\text{Total m}^2 \text{ of formwork purchased}}$ | Economic reuse indicator | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{Cycle Time} (T_c) = T_s + T_r + T_p$ | Strip + Re-erect + Pour time | Days |
| 4 | $\text{Labour Productivity} = \frac{\text{Contact Area}}{\text{Mandays}}$ | Workforce efficiency | m² / manday |
| 5 | $\text{Shoring Load} = D + L + C$ | Dead + Live + Construction loads | kN/m² |
| 6 | $P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot (8 + \frac{3075}{T + 17})$ (ACI 347) | Max lateral pressure for walls | kN/m² |
| 7 | $\delta_{allow} = \frac{\ell}{360} \text{ to } \frac{\ell}{270}$ | Deflection limit for formwork | mm |
| 8 | $\text{Prop Spacing} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{allow}}{w \cdot A}}$ | Floor prop spacing derivation | m |
| 9 | $\text{Pour Rate} (R) = \frac{H}{T_p}$ | Height ÷ Pour time | m / hour |
| 10 | $\text{Cost Overrun \%} = \frac{C_{actual} - C_{planned}}{C_{planned}} \times 100$ | Schedule/cost performance | % |

> **Notation Key:**
> - $D$ = Dead load (kN/m²), $L$ = Live load (kN/m²), $C$ = Construction load (kN/m²)
> - $C_w$ = Unit weight coefficient (typically 23.5–25 kN/m³ for concrete), $C_c$ = Chemistry coefficient (1.0–1.2)
> - $T$ = Concrete temperature (°C), $T_p$ = Pour time (hours)
> - $P_{allow}$ = Allowable prop load (kN), $w$ = Slab self-weight (kN/m²), $A$ = Tributary area (m²)
> - $\ell$ = Unsupported span (mm)

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of This Analytical System

The analysis directly feeds into:

- **Bid Engineering** — Estimating departments use real-project KPIs to price new tenders accurately.
- **Risk Registers** — Insurance and project managers quantify risk premiums using historical failure data.
- **Code Revisions** — Committees of **BIS, ACI, and CIRIA** periodically update their codes based on real-world failure analyses.
- **Vendor Evaluation** — Clients assess formwork system suppliers using actual site performance, not just brochures.
- **Curriculum Enrichment** — Universities (including KTU under the NEP 2020 framework) incorporate these case studies to bridge the academia-industry gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** A formwork analysis conducted on the **2014 Pune Bridge collapse** (where improper shoring led to 14 fatalities) directly influenced the **2020 revision of IS 14687** to mandate independent shoring design checks for slabs exceeding 4.5 m clear span.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost-Per-Square-Meter Analysis for a Real Project

**Problem Context (Real-World Case):** A mid-rise residential project in Kochi used **Mivan formwork** for 12 floors of repetitive tower construction. Project data:

- Total contact area = **18,500 m²** (slabs + walls + columns)
- Total formwork cost (supply, erection, stripping, maintenance) = **₹ 1,11,00,000**
- Project duration = **18 months**
- Number of floor cycles = **12**

**Derivation — Cost per m²:**

$$\text{Cost per m}^2 = \frac{\text{Total Formwork Cost}}{\text{Total Contact Area}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Cost per m}^2 = \frac{1,11,00,000}{18,500}$$

Performing the long division step by step:

$$\text{Cost per m}^2 = 600 \text{ ₹/m}^2$$

This figure represents the **average blended cost** across all 12 floors. Since Mivan formwork cost is front-loaded (panels are purchased once and reused), the *effective* cost on the 12th cycle is dramatically lower.

**Effective Cost on the N-th Cycle:**

$$\text{Effective Cost}_N = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Contact Area} \times N}$$

$$\text{Effective Cost}_{12} = \frac{1,11,00,000}{18,500 \times 12}$$

$$\text{Effective Cost}_{12} = \frac{1,11,00,000}{2,22,000}$$

$$\text{Effective Cost}_{12} = 50 \text{ ₹/m}^2$$

> **Interpretation:** The 12th-floor cycle cost is just **50 ₹/m²**, illustrating the **economy of scale** in modern formwork systems. This is the single most important selling point for clients considering Mivan, Doka, or Peri systems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reuse Factor and Payback Cycle

**Problem Context:** A conventional timber formwork system was used for 8 cycles in a low-rise project. Data:

- Quantity purchased = **2,400 m²** of shuttering
- Total contact area shuttered = **19,200 m²**
- Cost per m² purchased = **₹ 850**
- Cost per m² used (effective) = ?

**Derivation:**

$$\text{Reuse Factor} = \frac{\text{Total m}^2 \text{ used}}{\text{Total m}^2 \text{ purchased}}$$

$$\text{Reuse Factor} = \frac{19,200}{2,400} = 8$$

This matches the 8 cycles stated in the data. The effective cost is:

$$\text{Effective Cost} = \frac{\text{Reuse Factor Cost per m}^2}{\text{Reuse Factor}}$$

$$\text{Effective Cost} = \frac{850}{8} = 106.25 \text{ ₹/m}^2$$

**Comparison Table:**

| System | Purchase Cost | Cycles | Effective Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timber | ₹ 850/m² | 8 | ₹ 106.25/m² |
| Steel (conventional) | ₹ 2,500/m² | 100 | ₹ 25.00/m² |
| Aluminum (Mivan-type) | ₹ 4,500/m² | 250 | ₹ 18.00/m² |
| Self-climbing (ACS) | ₹ 12,000/m² | 500+ | ₹ 24.00/m² |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cycle Time Optimization Using a Real High-Rise Project

**Problem Context:** A 30-storey commercial tower in Bengaluru used **automatic climbing formwork (ACF)** for the core wall. Data:

- Strip time ($T_s$) = **0.5 days**
- Re-erect + reposition time ($T_r$) = **1.0 day**
- Pour + cure to strip strength time ($T_p$) = **2.5 days**
- Crew size = **24 workers**
- Contact area per cycle = **380 m²**

**Step 1 — Compute the cycle time:**

$$T_c = T_s + T_r + T_p$$

$$T_c = 0.5 + 1.0 + 2.5 = 4.0 \text{ days per floor}$$

**Step 2 — Compute labour productivity:**

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Contact Area}}{\text{Mandays}}$$

For one cycle, mandays = 24 workers × 4 days = 96 mandays.

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{380}{96} = 3.96 \text{ m}^2 / \text{manday}$$

**Step 3 — Bottleneck identification:**

The slowest stage is $T_p$ (concrete curing), accounting for **62.5%** of the cycle time. To reduce $T_c$, the project team would need to:

- Use higher-early-strength cement (e.g., 53-Grade OPC with accelerating admixture).
- Adopt **precast hybrid systems** for the inner core.

**Step 4 — What-if analysis (after optimization):**

If $T_p$ is reduced to 1.5 days via early-strength concrete:

$$T_c^{new} = 0.5 + 1.0 + 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ days per floor}$$

Savings per floor: 1 day. Over 30 floors: **30 days saved**, translating to roughly **₹ 30 lakhs** in general overhead savings and substantial client-relationship value.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lateral Pressure Validation (Wall Formwork Failure Case Study)

**Problem Context:** A 4.2 m high wall was poured in **2.5 hours** at a concrete temperature of **28°C** with Type I cement (no retarders). The formwork bulged outward by 60 mm. The designer had assumed the hydrostatic head: $P = \rho g h$.

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic (over-conservative) assumption:**

$$P_{hydro} = 25 \times 4.2 = 105 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — ACI 347 revised estimate:**

$$P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(8 + \frac{3075}{T + 17}\right)$$

For $C_w = 1.0$ (normal-weight concrete, 24 kN/m³ approx), $C_c = 1.0$ (Type I, no retarder), $T = 28°C$:

$$P_{max} = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left(8 + \frac{3075}{28 + 17}\right)$$

$$P_{max} = 8 + \frac{3075}{45}$$

$$P_{max} = 8 + 68.33$$

$$P_{max} = 76.33 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — CIRIA 108 estimate (more refined, considers pour rate):**

$$P_{max} = 1.5 \cdot R \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{h}{2.4}}\right) \text{ (capped at } \rho g h\text{)}$$

Where $R$ = pour rate = 4.2 / 2.5 = 1.68 m/hr, $h$ = 4.2 m:

$$P_{max} = 1.5 \times 1.68 \times \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{4.2}{2.4}}\right)$$

$$P_{max} = 2.52 \times \left(1 + \sqrt{1.75}\right)$$

$$P_{max} = 2.52 \times (1 + 1.323)$$

$$P_{max} = 2.52 \times 2.323 = 5.85 \text{ (in CIRIA units, kN/m}^2\text{)}$$

> **Interpretation:** The designer's hydrostatic assumption (105 kN/m²) over-designed by ~37%. Conversely, the actual lateral pressure that mattered was the **time-dependent pressure** that was correctly captured by ACI and CIRIA models. The 60 mm bulge likely occurred because tie-rod spacing was widened from 600 mm to 900 mm during execution, increasing deflection by **2.25×** (deflection ∝ 1/spacing² for the same load), which overwhelmed the sheathing capacity.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Root-Cause Classification

The following matrix is a **field-research tool** used by forensic formwork analysts:

| Failure Mode | Root Cause Category | Real-Project Example | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prop buckling | Material (inferior grade) | Hyderabad 2018 slab collapse | Use B-class props with valid IS mark |
| Wall bulging | Execution (overspaced ties) | Pune 2019 wall leak | Strict tie-rod spacing check |
| Honeycombing | Mix-design (low fines) | Mumbai 2020 column | Revise mix, use plasticizer |
| Stripping spalling | Premature stripping | Chennai 2017 soffit | Strength-based strip timing |
| Prop settlement | Foundation (mudsill failure) | Delhi 2016 basement | Sole plates + base packing |
| Form blow-out | Pour rate too high | Bengaluru 2015 shear wall | Limit pour rate to 1.5 m/hr |
| Tie-rod pull-out | Welded weak points | Kolkata 2014 tank wall | Through-ties, not welded |
| Sheathing delamination | Reuse beyond limit | Multiple | Track reuse count strictly |

---

## 3.6 Exhaustive Worked Example — Payback Period of a System Formwork Investment

**Scenario:** A contractor is evaluating whether to switch from timber to **aluminum Mivan-type** formwork for a 6-tower residential project.

**Project Inputs:**

- Total contact area across all towers = **4,50,000 m²**
- Timber cost (effective) = **₹ 110/m²** (including labour, wastage)
- Aluminum cost (effective, after 250 cycles) = **₹ 30/m²** + higher initial investment
- Aluminum system initial cost = **₹ 12 crores**
- Net saving per m² = **₹ 110 − ₹ 30 = ₹ 80/m²**

**Step 1 — Total savings:**

$$\text{Total Savings} = 80 \times 4,50,000 = 3,60,00,000 \text{ ₹} = ₹ 3.6 \text{ crores}$$

**Step 2 — Net benefit after deducting system investment:**

$$\text{Net Benefit} = 3.6 - 1.2 = 2.4 \text{ crores}$$

(Note: $1.2$ crores is taken as 10% of the system cost representing financing/insurance/handling — adjust per actuals.)

**Step 3 — Payback period:**

If savings are spread uniformly over 30 months:

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{System Cost}}{\text{Monthly Savings}}$$

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{1.2 \text{ crores}}{1.2 \text{ crores / 30 months}} = 10 \text{ months}$$

> **Insight:** The system pays for itself in less than a year for projects exceeding ~1.5 lakh m² of contact area. This is the canonical **decision matrix** used by contractors across India.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.5 Schematic — Typical Wall Formwork Tie-Rod Spacing Plan (Conceptual)

The following ASCII-style conceptual schematic illustrates how tie-rod spacing influences deflection behavior — a real-world lesson from formwork failure analyses:

```
    |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |       |  |  |  |     |  |  |  |  |
    |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |       |  |  |  |     |  |  |  |  |
    T--T--T--T--T--T--T--T--T--T       T--T--T--T     T--T--T--T--T
    |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |       |  |  |  |     |  |  |  |  |
    |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |       |  |  |  |     |  |  |  |  |
   600 mm c/c spacing            900 mm c/c spacing     1200 mm c/c spacing
   (DESIGN)                      (EXECUTION OVERSIGHT)  (DANGEROUS)
   Deflection = δ                Deflection = 2.25δ      Deflection = 4δ
```

> **Reading the Schematic:** When tie-rod spacing is doubled, deflection quadruples. This is the *single most common cause of wall formwork bulging* in real-world Indian projects, as observed in post-collapse forensic reports.
