- **Slipform construction** (continuous vertical structures like chimneys, silos)
- **Jump-form construction** (core walls of high-rise buildings)
- **Heated formwork** (cold-weather concreting below 5°C)
- **Architectural/concrete formwork** (exposed decorative surfaces)
- **Formwork for curved and shell structures**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Minimum striking time** for ordinary formwork: **7 days** (KTU/KIS Code standard)
> - **Minimum striking time** for props/soffits (spans > 4.5 m): **14 days**
> - **Standard cycle time** for high-rise floor formwork: **4 to 7 days per floor**
> - **Concrete temperature threshold** for heated formwork: **5°C ambient**
> - **Maximum slipform rate** for silos/chimneys: **300 mm/hour**

### 1.4 Formwork Productivity & Cycle Time
**Cycle Time (T_c)** is the total elapsed time (in days or hours) required to complete one full repetition of formwork operations on a given structural unit — from initial erection through concrete pouring, curing, and stripping back to re-erection at the next location.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a *bus shuttle service* between two stops. The bus departs every "T_c" minutes. To increase passenger throughput, you don't add more buses on the same route; you reduce the round-trip time. Similarly, in formwork, **reducing the cycle time increases the output rate** of the formwork sets.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Formwork Project Planning
The planning process is broken down into the following sequential activities:

1. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):** Decompose the entire formwork scope into manageable work packages (e.g., foundation formwork, column formwork, beam formwork, slab formwork).
2. **Quantity Take-Off (QTO):** Compute the exact contact area (m²) of formwork required for each structural element using the formula:
   - **Formwork Area (A_f) = Perimeter of contact × Height/Length**
3. **Cycle Time Calculation:** Determine the productivity rate and number of formwork sets required.
4. **Resource Planning:** Allocate carpenters, helpers, cranes, and formwork sets.
5. **Schedule Preparation:** Create Gantt charts, bar charts, or line-of-balance diagrams.
6. **Cost Estimation:** Compute ownership cost, rental cost, and labor cost.

### 2.2 Number of Formwork Sets — The Core Planning Equation

The most critical decision in formwork planning is determining **how many sets of formwork** to procure. This is governed by:

$$N = \frac{T_c}{T_p} + 1$$

Where:
- $N$ = Number of formwork sets required
- $T_c$ = Total cycle time (days) for one set to return to the same location
- $T_p$ = Concrete pouring/placement cycle interval (days) between successive pours

**Why this works:** The "+1" represents the formwork set currently in use (active pour). If your cycle time is 12 days and you pour a new slab every 4 days, you need 12/4 + 1 = **4 sets** of formwork.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N = \frac{T_c}{T_p} + 1$ | Number of formwork sets | Sets | ★★★★★ |
| $C_{total} = C_m + C_l + C_o + C_r$ | Total formwork cost | ₹ | ★★★★★ |
| $P = \frac{V_c}{T_c}$ | Productivity (concrete volume per cycle) | m³/day | ★★★★ |
| $R_{reuse} = \frac{A_{total}}{A_{set}}$ | Number of reuses per form set | Times | ★★★★★ |
| $S_{eff} = \frac{P_{load}}{A_{contact}}$ | Effective pressure on formwork | kN/m² | ★★★ |
| $V_{slip} = \frac{H_{total}}{T_{slip}}$ | Slipform rate of progress | mm/hr | ★★★★ |
| $T_{strip} \geq k \cdot f_{ck}^{0.5}$ | Minimum stripping time (empirical) | Days | ★★★ |
| $\eta_{form} = \frac{C_{form}}{C_{struct}} \times 100$ | Formwork cost as % of structural cost | % | ★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by project managers to optimize procurement, by quantity surveyors for tender preparation, and by site engineers for daily progress monitoring. In Kerala's high-rise residential boom (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut), accurate cycle-time calculation is a **board exam favourite**.

### 2.4 Steps in Formwork Management Workflow
1. **Pre-Construction:** Design, drawings, BBS, QTO, vendor selection.
2. **Material Receipt & Inspection:** Check panels, props, accessories.
3. **Erection:** As per approved shop drawings.
4. **Inspection & Approval:** Before concrete pour (check plumb, level, alignment).
5. **Concrete Pouring:** Monitor rate of pour to control lateral pressure.
6. **Curing & Stripping:** Time-based stripping as per IS 456 / KTU standards.
7. **Cleaning, Oiling & Storage:** Preparation for next cycle.
8. **Documentation:** Maintain records of reuses, damages, and recovery.

### 2.5 Special Conditions — Technical Overview

**A. Underwater Formwork:**
- Uses **fabric forms (forms filled with mortar)** or **prefabricated steel/GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) shells**.
- Concrete is placed by **tremie pipe method** to displace water.
- Formwork must resist **hydrostatic pressure** plus dynamic concrete pressure.

**B. Slipform Construction:**
- A continuously moving formwork system that advances at a controlled rate (typically 150–300 mm/hour).
- Used for silos, chimneys, cooling towers, and tall bridge piers.
- Requires **24/7 continuous concreting** until the structure reaches its full height.

**C. Jump Form System:**
- A self-climbing formwork that "jumps" from one casting level to the next using hydraulic jacks.
- Used for **core walls of high-rise buildings** (Kerala's typical 25–60 storey towers).
- Operates on a **3-day cycle** typically.

**D. Heated Formwork (Cold Weather Concreting):**
- Heating elements (electric resistance wires or hot water pipes) embedded in the formwork.
- Maintains concrete temperature above **10°C** during initial curing.
- Critical when ambient temperature falls below **5°C**.

**E. Architectural Formwork:**
- Uses textured liners, rubber formliners, or **controlled permeability formwork (CPF)** liners.
- CPF allows air and water to escape but retains cement, producing a denser, more durable surface with reduced blowholes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Number of Formwork Sets Equation

Let us derive the formula for the number of formwork sets $N$ required for a multi-storey construction project.

**Given:**
- A multi-storey building with identical floors.
- Each floor requires a fixed time $T_c$ (cycle time) for a given formwork set to be erected, concrete poured, cured, stripped, and re-erected on the next floor.
- New concrete is poured at an interval of $T_p$ days (i.e., a new floor is started every $T_p$ days).

**Step 1:** Let us define the total number of pours in time $T$:
$$n_{pours} = \frac{T}{T_p}$$

**Step 2:** Each formwork set, after being used for one pour, becomes available for reuse after $T_c$ days. So in time $T$, a single set can complete:
$$n_{cycles} = \frac{T}{T_c} \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3:** For all pours to proceed without interruption, the total productive cycles from $N$ sets must equal or exceed the total pours demanded:
$$N \times \frac{T}{T_c} \geq \frac{T}{T_p}$$

**Step 4:** Cancelling $T$ from both sides:
$$N \geq \frac{T_c}{T_p}$$

**Step 5:** Since $N$ must be a whole number and we add 1 for the formwork set currently engaged in pouring:
$$\boxed{N = \frac{T_c}{T_p} + 1}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A residential tower in Kochi has 20 identical floors. The formwork cycle time for one floor is 18 days. The contractor plans to pour a new floor every 6 days. Calculate the number of formwork sets required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.
- $T_c$ = 18 days (cycle time)
- $T_p$ = 6 days (pouring interval)

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.
$$N = \frac{T_c}{T_p} + 1$$

$$N = \frac{18}{6} + 1$$

$$N = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ sets}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.
A minimum of **4 sets of formwork** is required. If only 3 sets were procured, the contractor would face a bottleneck because a new floor is ready for pouring every 6 days, but each formwork set takes 18 days to become available again.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Total Formwork Cost Equation

The total formwork cost for a project is the sum of material, labor, overhead, and reuse-related costs.

**Step 1:** Material cost (one-time procurement):
$$C_m = A_{total} \times R_m \times \left(1 - \frac{n-1}{n} \cdot S_r\right)$$

Where $S_r$ is the salvage recovery factor and $n$ is the number of reuses.

**Step 2:** Labor cost (erection + stripping):
$$C_l = A_{total} \times L_e + A_{total} \times L_s$$

Where $L_e$ = labor cost per m² for erection, $L_s$ = labor cost per m² for stripping.

**Step 3:** Overhead cost (handling, storage, transport):
$$C_o = A_{total} \times H_o$$

**Step 4:** Recovery credit at end of project:
$$C_r = A_{total} \times S_v$$

**Step 5:** Net total cost:
$$C_{total} = C_m + C_l + C_o - C_r$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Slipform Rate Calculation

**Problem:** A 60 m tall chimney is to be constructed using slipform. Construction must be completed in 25 days of continuous 24-hour operation. Calculate the slipform rate.

**Step 1:** Convert total time to hours.
$$T_{slip} = 25 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hr/day} = 600 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the slipform rate formula.
$$V_{slip} = \frac{H_{total}}{T_{slip}}$$

$$V_{slip} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ mm}}{600 \text{ hr}}$$

$$V_{slip} = 100 \text{ mm/hr}$$

**Step 3:** Verification against KTU standard range.
Since 100 mm/hr < 300 mm/hr (the KTU standard maximum), the rate is feasible. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Special Formwork Systems

| Parameter | Slipform | Jump Form | Underwater | Heated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Best Application** | Tall vertical structures | Core walls | Submerged pile caps | Cold climates |
| **Cycle Time** | Continuous (mm/hr) | 2–4 days/floor | Single pour | N/A |
| **Labor Skill Required** | High | Medium | Very High | High |
| **Cost per m²** | Moderate | High | Very High | High |
| **Weather Dependency** | Sensitive (wind) | Low | Independent of air | Critical |
| **Reuse Efficiency** | Excellent | Good | Single use | Good |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | High | High | Medium | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Formwork Project Planning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Project Initiation] --> node2[Work Breakdown Structure]
    node2 --> node3[Quantity Take-Off]
    node3 --> node4[Cycle Time Estimation]
    node4 --> node5[Calculate Number of Sets N]
    node5 --> node6[Resource Planning Labour and Crane]
    node6 --> node7[Cost Estimation]
    node7 --> node8[Schedule Preparation Gantt Chart]
    node8 --> node9[Approval and Procurement]
    node9 --> node10[Site Execution and Monitoring]
    node10 --> node11[Final Documentation and Handover]
    node10 -.Feedback Loop.-> node4
```

### 4.2 Formwork Management Cycle (On-Site Execution Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Material Receipt] --> nodeB[Quality Inspection]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Erection as per Drawing]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Pre-Pour Inspection Checklist]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Concrete Pouring]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Time-Based Curing]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Stripping Operation]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Cleaning and Oiling]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Re-Erection on Next Floor]
    nodeI -.-> nodeD
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting Special Formwork

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startSpecial[Special Condition Identified] --> q1{Is structure vertical and tall greater 30 m}
    q1 -->|Yes| slip[Use Slipform]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is it a high-rise core wall}
    q2 -->|Yes| jump[Use Jump Form]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Is element submerged in water}
    q3 -->|Yes| under[Use Underwater Formwork with Tremie]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Is ambient temperature below 5 deg C}
    q4 -->|Yes| heat[Use Heated Formwork]
    q4 -->|No| q5{Is exposed architectural finish required}
    q5 -->|Yes| cpf[Use CPF or Textured Liners]
    q5 -->|No| conv[Use Conventional Formwork]
```

### 4.4 Formwork Cost Composition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    total[Total Formwork Cost] --> mat[Material Cost 35 to 50 percent]
    total --> lab[Labor Cost 30 to 40 percent]
    total --> ovh[Overhead 10 to 15 percent]
    total --> rcv[Recovery Credit 5 to 10 percent]
    mat --> mat1[Initial Procurement]
    mat --> mat2[Less Salvage Value]
    lab --> lab1[Carpenters]
    lab --> lab2[Helpers]
    lab --> lab3[Supervisors]
    ovh --> ovh1[Crane Charges]
    ovh --> ovh2[Storage and Transport]
    ovh --> ovh3[Form Release Agent]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define formwork cycle time and state any two factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**
Formwork cycle time is the total duration required for a formwork set to complete one full operation sequence — erection, concrete pouring, curing, and stripping — before it can be re-used at the next location.

Two factors affecting cycle time:
1. **Concrete strength gain rate** — governed by cement type, ambient temperature, and use of admixtures. Faster strength gain allows earlier stripping.
2. **Crane availability and lifting capacity** — efficient material handling reduces idle time between cycles.

*(Examiner's tip: A 3-mark answer needs the definition + 2 valid points. Avoid vague statements like "labour skill" without elaboration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of a release agent (form oil) in formwork management?**

**Model Answer:**
A release agent (form oil) is a chemical compound applied to the formwork surface before concrete pouring to prevent adhesion between the hardened concrete and the formwork panel.

**Role:**
1. **Easy stripping** — reduces stripping labor and time, improving cycle efficiency.
2. **Improved surface finish** — minimizes surface blowholes, staining, and discolouration of concrete.
3. **Extended formwork life** — prevents moisture absorption and corrosion of timber/steel forms, increasing the number of reuses.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Question Paper Format:** Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each. Students must answer ONE full question (both sub-parts).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for formwork project planning** for a multi-storey residential building project. Highlight the importance of cycle time and number of formwork sets. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The step-by-step procedure for formwork project planning is as follows:

1. **Study of Structural Drawings:** Review RCC drawings to identify all formwork-demanding elements — foundations, columns, beams, slabs, shear walls, and stairs.

2. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):** Divide the formwork scope into packages such as:
   - Package 1: Foundation formwork
   - Package 2: Column formwork
   - Package 3: Beam and slab formwork
   - Package 4: Special formwork (lift core, canopy, etc.)

3. **Quantity Take-Off (QTO):** Calculate formwork contact area for each element:
   - **Column formwork area** = Perimeter × Height
   - **Beam formwork area** = (2 × side depth + bottom width) × Length
   - **Slab formwork area** = Length × Width

4. **Determination of Cycle Time ($T_c$):** Estimate based on:
   - Stripping time as per IS 456 (7 days for sides, 14 days for props)
   - Crane productivity
   - Crew size and skill

5. **Calculation of Number of Sets (N):**
   $$N = \frac{T_c}{T_p} + 1$$

6. **Resource Planning:** Allocate carpenters (typically 1 carpenter per 8–10 m²/day), helpers, cranes, and formwork sets.

7. **Scheduling:** Prepare a Gantt chart showing the sequence of formwork activities for each floor, ensuring no clashes with reinforcement, MEP, and concreting activities.

8. **Cost Estimation:** Compute total formwork cost using:
   $$C_{total} = C_m + C_l + C_o - C_r$$

9. **Risk and Safety Planning:** Identify risks (formwork failure, falls) and prepare a Job Safety Analysis (JSA).

**Importance of Cycle Time:** A shorter cycle time reduces the total project duration, improves cash flow, and lowers the cost of capital. A well-planned cycle time is the single most important driver of high-rise formwork efficiency.

**Importance of Number of Formwork Sets:** Procuring the correct number of sets (no more, no less) balances capital cost against schedule delay. Over-procurement wastes capital; under-procurement causes bottlenecks and delays.

---

**(b)** A contractor has to construct a 30-storey building with identical floors. The estimated formwork cycle time per floor is **21 days**, and the contractor plans to start a new floor every **7 days**. Calculate:
(i) Number of formwork sets required.
(ii) Total formwork area if the contact area per floor is **2,500 m²**.
(iii) Cost of formwork per m² and total cost if material cost is ₹**1,200/m²**, labor is ₹**450/m²**, and overhead is ₹**150/m²**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Number of Formwork Sets:**

$$N = \frac{T_c}{T_p} + 1 = \frac{21}{7} + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ sets}$$

**(ii) Total Formwork Area:**

$$A_{total} = \text{Number of floors} \times \text{Area per floor}$$

$$A_{total} = 30 \times 2{,}500 = 75{,}000 \text{ m}^2$$

**(iii) Total Formwork Cost:**

Cost per m²:
$$C_{per\,m^2} = 1{,}200 + 450 + 150 = ₹1{,}800/\text{m}^2$$

Total cost:
$$C_{total} = 75{,}000 \times 1{,}800 = ₹13{,}50{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C_{total} = ₹13.5 \text{ Crores}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe in detail **slipform construction** under the following heads: (i) Principle, (ii) Components, (iii) Procedure, (iv) Applications, and (v) Advantages and Limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Principle:** Slipform is a continuously moving formwork system in which the formwork is jacked up vertically at a slow, controlled rate (typically 100–300 mm/hour) while concrete is poured continuously into the top of the form. The form slides upward, leaving behind hardened concrete that retains the shape.

**(ii) Components:**
- **Form panels** (steel, 1.0–1.2 m high)
- **Yokes** (steel frames holding the form panels)
- **Hydraulic jacks** (100–200 kN capacity)
- **Jack rods** (25–32 mm dia. steel pipes embedded in concrete)
- **Working platform** (for placing concrete, rebar, and embedding items)
- **Suspended scaffolding** (for finishing and inspection)

**(iii) Procedure:**
1. Erect the initial 1.2 m height of formwork on the foundation.
2. Place reinforcement and embedments in the form.
3. Pour concrete in layers of 150–200 mm.
4. Start jacking at 100–150 mm/hour once initial set occurs.
5. Continue 24/7 operation; the form slides upward continuously.
6. On reaching the design height, fix the cap and dismantle the form.

**(iv) Applications:** Silos, chimneys, cooling towers, tall bridge piers, lift core walls in industrial structures.

**(v) Advantages:**
- Fast construction (no cycle delays).
- Monolithic, joint-less structure.
- Economical for tall vertical structures.

**Limitations:**
- Requires continuous 24/7 concreting and skilled labour.
- Sensitive to weather (high winds disrupt operation).
- Cannot accommodate abrupt design changes.

---

**(b)** A **60 m tall chimney** is to be slipformed. The work is to be completed in **30 days of continuous operation**. Calculate:
(i) The slipform rate in mm/hour.
(ii) The volume of concrete required if the chimney has a uniform outer diameter of 6 m and wall thickness of 400 mm.
(iii) The number of truck mixers required if each truck delivers 6 m³ of concrete and pouring must be completed within 2 hours per pour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Slipform Rate:**

Total time = 30 days × 24 hours = 720 hours

$$V_{slip} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ mm}}{720 \text{ hr}} = 83.33 \text{ mm/hr}$$

**(ii) Volume of Concrete:**

Outer diameter = 6 m → Outer radius = 3 m
Inner diameter = 6 − 2(0.4) = 5.2 m → Inner radius = 2.6 m

$$V = \pi (R^2 - r^2) \times H = \pi (3^2 - 2.6^2) \times 60$$

$$V = \pi (9 - 6.76) \times 60 = \pi \times 2.24 \times 60$$

$$V = 3.1416 \times 134.4 = 422.23 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Number of Truck Mixers:**

Concrete required in 2 hours = 422.23 m³
Each truck = 6 m³

$$\text{Number of trucks} = \frac{422.23}{6} = 70.37 \approx 71 \text{ trucks}$$

Therefore, a minimum of **71 truck mixers** must be deployed for the pour.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in the N formula:** This is the most common error, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units (mm/hr vs m/hr):** Always state units clearly; slipform rate is typically in **mm/hr**.
> 3. **Ignoring the salvage value** in cost calculations — for a 14-mark question, examiners expect you to mention recovery credit.
> 4. **In slipform questions, students often forget that the rate must be ≤ 300 mm/hr** to be feasible — failing to verify this costs marks.
> 5. **In jump form questions, don't confuse it with slipform** — jump form operates in cycles (2–4 days/floor), while slipform is continuous.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Formwork cost = 35% to 60% of RCC structural cost** — drive planning accordingly.
- [x] **Number of formwork sets:** $N = \frac{T_c}{T_p} + 1$
- [x] **Minimum stripping time** (KTU/IS 456): **7 days** for sides, **14 days** for soffit/props of spans > 4.5 m.
- [x] **Slipform rate:** typically **100–300 mm/hr**; must operate **24/7 continuously**.
- [x] **Jump form** is preferred for **core walls of high-rise** (Kerala's residential towers).
- [x] **Underwater formwork** is paired with **tremie pipe concreting** to avoid washout.
- [x] **Heated formwork** is mandatory when ambient temperature < **5°C** to ensure strength gain.
- [x] **Release agent** improves stripping, finish, and re-use life.
- [x] **Cycle time reduction** is the single biggest productivity lever in formwork management.
- [x] **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is the first step of any formwork planning exercise.
- [x] **Gantt chart** and **Line of Balance (LOB)** are the two most common scheduling tools.
- [x] **CPF (Controlled Permeability Formwork)** produces denser, more durable concrete surfaces.
- [x] **Jump form cycle time:** typically **2 to 4 days per lift**.
- [x] **Slipform disadvantages:** wind-sensitive, requires continuous labour, no design changes mid-way.
- [x] **Formwork productivity** = $\frac{V_c}{T_c}$ (m³/day) — a key KPI for site managers.
- [x] **Safety first:** Always include JSA (Job Safety Analysis) in formwork project planning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

topics.title: Project Planning and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Planning and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Project Planning and Management** in the context of formwork engineering refers to the systematic application of scientific and engineering principles coupled with managerial techniques to effectively plan, organize, direct, and control the resources (material, manpower, machinery, money, and time) required for the design, erection, operation, and stripping of formwork systems in a construction project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Project Planning in formwork engineering is the strategic backbone that determines *what* is to be built, *how* it is to be built, *when* each activity must be executed, *who* is responsible, and *which* resources (shores, props, panels, brackets) are required — all while optimizing cost, time, and safety.

The two principal quantitative techniques used in KTU-evaluated formwork scheduling are:
- **CPM (Critical Path Method)** — Deterministic, time-oriented network analysis.
- **PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)** — Probabilistic, three-time-estimate analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you are preparing a traditional Onam Sadhya (Kerala feast). You cannot start serving the *payasam* before the *avial* is ready. Each dish has dependencies, some can be cooked in parallel (rice and sambar), and one critical dish (the slow-cooked *erissery*) dictates the entire meal's finish time. **Project Planning and Management is exactly this — a recipe (network) for a building project where the formwork is the "slow-cooked erissery": it sets the schedule, drives the budget, and any delay cascades through every other activity.**

Think of formwork as the **"spine of a building project"**. It supports the concrete (vertebrae) until it cures, and its erection-stripping cycle defines the rhythm of the entire construction. Without proper planning, even the best-designed formwork becomes a bottleneck.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams

| Term | Definition (as per KTU expected answer style) |
|---|---|
| **Activity** | A specific task consuming time and resources (e.g., "erection of slab formwork for Level 2"). |
| **Event (Node)** | A milestone representing the start or completion of one or more activities. |
| **Dummy Activity** | A logical dependency with **zero duration** and **zero cost**, used to maintain network logic. |
| **Critical Path** | The longest-duration path through the network; it determines the **minimum project duration**. |
| **Float (Slack)** | The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying project completion. |
| **Earliest Start (ES)** | The earliest time an activity can begin (forward pass). |
| **Latest Finish (LF)** | The latest time an activity can finish without delaying the project (backward pass). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units to Remember:** Activity duration is measured in **days** (typical) or **hours/weeks**. Float is in the same time unit. The total project duration is conventionally called the **"Project Completion Time" $T_p$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Project Planning Cycle

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures formwork project management into a five-phase logical flow:

1. **Project Definition & Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):** Decompose the formwork scope (e.g., foundation → columns → beams → slabs → walls) into manageable work packages.
2. **Activity Definition & Sequencing:** Identify each formwork-related activity and establish logical dependencies (finish-to-start, start-to-start, finish-to-finish).
3. **Duration Estimation:** For CPM, use a single deterministic estimate; for PERT, use three estimates.
4. **Network Construction:** Build an Activity-on-Arrow (AOA) or Activity-on-Node (AON) diagram.
5. **Critical Path Identification & Resource Leveling:** Determine the critical path, compute floats, and level resources (shores, props, gangs of carpenters).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in formwork:** Formwork is typically the most resource-intensive activity in a reinforced concrete building (often **30%–60% of the structural cost** and **35%–60% of the project timeline**). A one-day delay in formwork erection for a floor cycle propagates to concreting, curing, de-shuttering, and the next floor cycle.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Network Logic

- **Why CPM?** → Construction projects have **well-defined, repeatable activities** (formwork erection, striking, re-erection). Historical data exists, so a deterministic time estimate is reliable.
- **Why PERT?** → Research, prototype, or first-of-its-kind formwork systems have **uncertainty** in duration, hence three time estimates (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic).
- **How floats are used in formwork:** A formwork activity with **Total Float > 0** is a candidate for resource smoothing — crews and props can be temporarily shifted to critical activities without delaying the project.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PERT Expected Time ($t_e$)** | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | $t_o$ = Optimistic, $t_m$ = Most likely, $t_p$ = Pessimistic (all in days) |
| 2 | **PERT Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | Variance $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ |
| 3 | **Project Variance (Critical Path)** | $\sigma_{cp}^2 = \sum \sigma_i^2$ | Sum of variances of all activities on the critical path |
| 4 | **Probability of Completion ($Z$)** | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{cp}}$ | $T_s$ = Scheduled time, $T_e$ = Expected project duration |
| 5 | **Earliest Start (ES)** | $ES_i = \max(EF_j)$ for all predecessors $j$ | Forward pass quantity (days) |
| 6 | **Earliest Finish (EF)** | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | $D_i$ = duration of activity $i$ |
| 7 | **Latest Finish (LF)** | $LF_i = \min(LS_k)$ for all successors $k$ | Backward pass quantity (days) |
| 8 | **Latest Start (LS)** | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | (days) |
| 9 | **Total Float (TF)** | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$ | (days) |
| 10 | **Free Float (FF)** | $FF_i = ES_{successor} - EF_i$ | (days) |
| 11 | **Critical Path Duration** | $T_p = \max(\text{Path durations})$ | (days) |
| 12 | **Project Cost Optimization** | $C_T = C_{direct} + C_{indirect}$ | $C_T$ = Total cost, minimized at optimum duration |
| 13 | **Crash Cost Slope** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta t}$ | $\Delta C$ = Increase in cost, $\Delta t$ = Reduction in time (Rs/day) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The **variance in PERT applies ONLY to the critical path activities**, not to all activities in the network. This is the single most-frequently-mistaken concept in KTU valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Kerala's high-rise residential boom (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut), contractors use CPM-PERT hybrid networks to plan **formwork cycles** (typically 7–10 days per floor). The Critical Path often runs through:
- **Vertical element formwork** (columns/walls) →
- **Slab soffit formwork** (table form / flying form) →
- **Reinforcement placement** →
- **Concrete pour** →
- **Curing period (governed by de-shuttering strength)** →
- **Re-propping and re-cycling**.

Identifying this path allows the project manager to apply **crashing** (overtime, additional gangs, high-early-strength cement) only where it actually reduces project duration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PERT Expected Time & Variance (Beta Distribution Approach)

PERT assumes activity duration follows a **Beta distribution** because it is bounded, asymmetric, and best fits empirical construction data. The three parameters $(t_o, t_m, t_p)$ correspond to specific percentile points.

**Step 1 — Define the Beta distribution moments.**

For a Beta distribution, the mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ are:

$$\mu = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$

**Step 2 — Derive the variance term.**

The variance of a Beta distribution approximated by these three estimates is:

$$\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Justification of the "$\div 6$" rule.**

The reasoning is: the **range** $(t_p - t_o)$ typically covers **6 standard deviations** of a hypothetical normal distribution, hence:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example (KTU-style).**

> A formwork activity "Erection of column formwork" has:
> $t_o = 4$ days, $t_m = 6$ days, $t_p = 14$ days.

Expected time:

$$t_e = \dfrac{4 + 4(6) + 14}{6} = \dfrac{4 + 24 + 14}{6} = \dfrac{42}{6} = 7 \text{ days}$$

Variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{14 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{5}{3}\right)^2 = \dfrac{25}{9} \approx 2.78 \text{ days}^2$$

Standard deviation:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{2.78} \approx 1.667 \text{ days}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Forward Pass (Earliest Event Time) Logic

Let $E_j$ denote the **Earliest Event Time** at node $j$, and $D_{ij}$ the duration of activity $(i \to j)$.

**Step 1 — Base condition.** The earliest event time of the start node is zero:

$$E_1 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursive forward pass formula.** For any node $j$ reached by activities from multiple predecessor nodes $i$:

$$E_j = \max_{i} \left( E_i + D_{ij} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical demonstration on a 6-node formwork network.**

Consider the following AOA network for a small slab formwork cycle:

| Activity | Description | From $\to$ To | Duration (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Setup props & bearers | $1 \to 2$ | 2 |
| B | Lay plywood sheets | $2 \to 3$ | 3 |
| C | Erect beam sides | $2 \to 4$ | 4 |
| D | Fix Y-pieces & heads | $3 \to 5$ | 2 |
| E | Beam bottom setup | $4 \to 5$ | 1 |
| F | Final alignment & leveling | $5 \to 6$ | 2 |

Forward Pass Calculation:

$$E_1 = 0$$
$$E_2 = E_1 + D_A = 0 + 2 = 2$$
$$E_3 = E_2 + D_B = 2 + 3 = 5$$
$$E_4 = E_2 + D_C = 2 + 4 = 6$$
$$E_5 = \max(E_3 + D_D, \; E_4 + D_E) = \max(5+2, \; 6+1) = \max(7, 7) = 7$$
$$E_6 = E_5 + D_F = 7 + 2 = 9$$

**Project Duration** $T_p = E_6 = \mathbf{9}$ **days**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Backward Pass (Latest Event Time) Logic

Let $L_i$ be the **Latest Event Time** at node $i$, and the project duration $T_p = E_{\text{end}}$.

**Step 1 — Base condition.** The latest event time of the end node equals the project duration:

$$L_n = E_n = T_p$$

**Step 2 — Recursive backward pass formula.** For any node $i$ with multiple successors $j$:

$$L_i = \min_{j} \left( L_j - D_{ij} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical demonstration (continuing the same network).**

$$L_6 = 9$$
$$L_5 = L_6 - D_F = 9 - 2 = 7$$
$$L_4 = L_5 - D_E = 7 - 1 = 6$$
$$L_3 = L_5 - D_D = 7 - 2 = 5$$
$$L_2 = \min(L_3 - D_B, \; L_4 - D_C) = \min(5-3, \; 6-4) = \min(2, 2) = 2$$
$$L_1 = L_2 - D_A = 2 - 2 = 0$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Float & Critical Path Identification

For each activity $(i \to j)$:

$$TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - D_{ij}$$

Or equivalently:

$$TF_{ij} = LS - ES = LF - EF$$

Applying to the sample network:

| Activity | $E_i$ | $D_{ij}$ | $E_j$ | $L_i$ | $L_j$ | $TF$ | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (1–2) | 0 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 2 | $2-0-2 = 0$ | **Yes** |
| B (2–3) | 2 | 3 | 5 | 2 | 5 | $5-2-3 = 0$ | **Yes** |
| C (2–4) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 2 | 6 | $6-2-4 = 0$ | **Yes** |
| D (3–5) | 5 | 2 | 7 | 5 | 7 | $7-5-2 = 0$ | **Yes** |
| E (4–5) | 6 | 1 | 7 | 6 | 7 | $7-6-1 = 0$ | **Yes** |
| F (5–6) | 7 | 2 | 9 | 7 | 9 | $9-7-2 = 0$ | **Yes** |

**Result:** $TF = 0$ for **every** activity $\Rightarrow$ **ALL paths are critical** in this small cycle (a common feature in tightly-coupled formwork operations).

**Critical Path(s):** $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 5 \to 6$ and $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6$, both with duration $\mathbf{9}$ days.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Project Cost — Time Trade-off (Crashing)

The total project cost is:

$$C_T(t) = C_{direct}(t) + C_{indirect}(t)$$

- $C_{direct}$ increases as duration is reduced (overtime, extra gangs).
- $C_{indirect}$ decreases as duration is reduced (shorter site overhead, equipment rent).

**Cost slope** for crashing an activity:

$$\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{C_{crash} - C_{normal}}{D_{normal} - D_{crash}} \quad \text{(Rs per day)}$$

**Algorithm (KTU standard):**
1. Compute normal path durations and identify critical path.
2. Crash the activity on the critical path with the **lowest cost slope**.
3. Re-evaluate the network; new critical path may appear.
4. Stop when further crashing increases $C_T$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Typical Float Behaviour in a Formwork Cycle

| Activity Type | Typical Float | Decision Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Slab soffit formwork erection | **Zero** (critical) | No delay tolerated; full resource priority |
| Beam-side formwork | Small (1–2 days) | Monitor tightly |
| Column formwork stripping | Moderate (2–4 days) | Candidate for resource reuse to next floor |
| Wall formwork (one side) | High (3–5 days) | Smoothing candidate |
| Prop de-shuttering (after 14 days) | High (5–7 days) | Back-shore planning opportunity |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Network Diagram — Formwork Cycle (AOA Format)

```mermaid
graph LR
  n1((Start)):::startNode -->|A: Prop Setup - 2d| n2((A))
  n2((A)):::midNode -->|B: Plywood Lay - 3d| n3((B))
  n2((A)):::midNode -->|C: Beam Sides - 4d| n4((C))
  n3((B)):::midNode -->|D: Y-Heads Fix - 2d| n5((D))
  n4((C)):::midNode -->|E: Beam Bottoms - 1d| n5((D))
  n5((D)):::midNode -->|F: Leveling - 2d| n6((End)):::endNode

  classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef midNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
  classDef endNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Project Planning Workflow (KTU Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  P1[Step 1: Define Project Scope] --> P2[Step 2: Work Breakdown Structure]
  P2 --> P3[Step 3: Activity Definition]
  P3 --> P4[Step 4: Duration Estimation CPM or PERT]
  P4 --> P5[Step 5: Network Construction AOA or AON]
  P5 --> P6[Step 6: Forward and Backward Pass]
  P6 --> P7[Step 7: Identify Critical Path]
  P7 --> P8{Is Project Duration Acceptable?}
  P8 -- Yes --> P9[Step 8: Resource Leveling and Allocation]
  P8 -- No --> P10[Step 9: Crashing and Cost-Time Optimization]
  P10 --> P6
  P9 --> P11[Step 10: Execution and Monitoring]
  P11 --> P12[Step 11: Progress Tracking and Earned Value Analysis]
  P12 --> P13[Step 12: Project Closure and Documentation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Gantt-Style Resource Flow

```mermaid
gantt
  title Typical Slab Formwork Cycle 9 Days
  dateFormat  D
  axisFormat  %d

  section Critical Path A
  Prop and Bearer Setup  :a1, 0, 2d
  Plywood Sheet Lay     :a2, after a1, 3d
  Y-Piece and Head Fix  :a4, after a2, 2d
  Final Leveling        :a6, after a4, 2d

  section Parallel Path B
  Beam Side Erect       :a3, after a1, 4d
  Beam Bottom Setup     :a5, after a3, 1d
```

## 4.4 Interpretation Guide for Examiners

- **Green start node** indicates the project initiation milestone.
- **Orange mid nodes** represent intermediate events where logic is enforced.
- **Pink end node** marks project completion.
- **Bold edges** in the network represent critical activities; any delay here delays the entire project.
- The Gantt chart visually confirms the parallel execution of beam-side erection (Path B) and slab preparation (Path A), a hallmark of efficient formwork planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Critical Path Method (CPM) and explain its significance in formwork project planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** is a deterministic project management technique used to plan and control construction activities by identifying the longest sequence of dependent tasks (the critical path) that determines the **minimum project completion time**. (1 mark)
In formwork engineering, CPM is significant because formwork operations (erection, striking, re-erection) consume the largest share of project time and resources, and the critical path typically passes through these activities. (1 mark)
By identifying the critical path, the project manager can focus crashing efforts, resource allocation, and supervision on those formwork activities that directly govern the project duration. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Total Float and Free Float in network analysis with a suitable formwork example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Total Float (TF)** is the amount of time an activity can be delayed **without delaying the entire project completion**. (1 mark)
**Free Float (FF)** is the amount of time an activity can be delayed **without delaying the early start of any immediate successor activity**. (1 mark)
**Formwork example:** If "Erection of column formwork" (duration 3 days) has an ES of day 5 and its successor "Beam formwork" has an ES of day 10, then the FF for column formwork = $10 - (5+3) = 2$ days. The TF may be larger (e.g., 4 days) because the project has an overall buffer. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**A construction project for a multi-storey residential building in Kochi involves the following formwork activities. Construct the network, determine the critical path, project duration, and floats for all activities.**

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|---|---|---|
| A (Foundation formwork) | — | 4 |
| B (Plinth beam formwork) | A | 3 |
| C (Column formwork – GF) | B | 5 |
| D (Slab formwork – GF) | C | 6 |
| E (Beam formwork – GF) | C | 4 |
| F (Column formwork – FF) | D, E | 5 |
| G (Slab formwork – FF) | F | 6 |

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Draw the network and find the critical path and total project duration.

### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Network Construction:**

```
        ┌──A(4)──►(2)──B(3)──►(3)──C(5)──►(4)──┬──D(6)──►(5)──┐
Start(1)                                       └──E(4)──►(5)'──┴──F(5)──►(6)──G(6)──►(7) End
```

To maintain correct logic (both D and E depend on C, but F depends on BOTH D and E), we introduce a **dummy activity** from node 5 (end of D) to node 5' (end of E), or vice versa.

**Step 2 — Forward Pass:**

$$E_1 = 0$$
$$E_2 = 0 + 4 = 4$$
$$E_3 = 4 + 3 = 7$$
$$E_4 = 7 + 5 = 12$$
$$E_5 = E_4 + D_D = 12 + 6 = 18$$
$$E_{5'} = E_4 + D_E = 12 + 4 = 16$$
$$E_6 = \max(E_5, E_{5'}) + D_F = \max(18, 16) + 5 = 18 + 5 = 23$$
$$E_7 = E_6 + D_G = 23 + 6 = 29$$

**Project Duration $T_p = 29$ days. (3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Backward Pass:**

$$L_7 = 29$$
$$L_6 = 29 - 6 = 23$$
$$L_5 = 23 - 5 = 18$$
$$L_{5'} = 23 - 5 = 18$$
$$L_4 = \min(L_5 - 6, L_{5'} - 4) = \min(12, 14) = 12$$
$$L_3 = 12 - 5 = 7$$
$$L_2 = 7 - 3 = 4$$
$$L_1 = 4 - 4 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Identify Critical Path:**

The critical path is the path with $TF = 0$:

$$\text{Start} \to A \to B \to C \to D \to F \to G \to \text{End}$$
$$1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 7$$

**Critical Path Duration = 4 + 3 + 5 + 6 + 5 + 6 = 29 days** ✓ (4 marks for correct identification)

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Calculate the Total Float (TF) and Free Float (FF) for activities E and F, and comment on the resource planning implications.

### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

| Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E | 12 | 16 | 14 | 18 | 2 | 2 |
| F | 18 | 23 | 18 | 23 | 0 | 0 |

**For Activity E:**
$$TF_E = L_{5'} - E_4 - D_E = 18 - 12 - 4 = 2 \text{ days}$$
$$FF_E = E_6 - E_{5'} = 23 - 16 - 4 = 3 \text{ days}... \text{(recheck using proper formula) } FF_E = E_5 - EF_E = 18 - 16 = 2 \text{ days}$$

**For Activity F:**
$$TF_F = L_6 - E_5 - D_F = 23 - 18 - 5 = 0 \text{ days} \; (\text{critical})$$
$$FF_F = E_7 - EF_F = 29 - 23 = 6 \text{ days}$$

Wait — for F, since F is on the critical path, TF = FF = 0.

**Resource Planning Implications (2 marks):**
- Activity E has a float of 2 days, allowing the contractor to **temporarily divert carpenter gangs** from beam formwork to slab-related critical activities during peak demand, without delaying the project.
- Activity F is critical, so **no delay is tolerable**; extra resources (additional props, double-shift work) should be planned.
- The total project of **29 days** can be shortened by **crashing F or D** (lowest cost slope activities on the critical path).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**For a formwork engineering project, the following three-time estimates are given for five activities lying on the critical path. Compute the expected project duration, standard deviation, variance, and the probability of completing the project 3 days ahead of schedule.**

| Activity | $t_o$ (days) | $t_m$ (days) | $t_p$ (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A — Foundation formwork | 3 | 5 | 11 |
| B — Plinth beam formwork | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| C — Column formwork | 4 | 6 | 14 |
| D — Slab formwork | 5 | 8 | 15 |
| E — Beam formwork | 3 | 5 | 13 |

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Compute the expected time and variance for each activity, and find the expected project duration.

### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Formula Reminder:**
$$t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}, \quad \sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$$

**Activity A:**
$$t_e = \dfrac{3 + 20 + 11}{6} = \dfrac{34}{6} = 5.67 \text{ days}$$
$$\sigma_A^2 = \left(\dfrac{11-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{8}{6}\right)^2 = 1.78 \text{ days}^2$$

**Activity B:**
$$t_e = \dfrac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4.00 \text{ days}$$
$$\sigma_B^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.44 \text{ days}^2$$

**Activity C:**
$$t_e = \dfrac{4 + 24 + 14}{6} = \dfrac{42}{6} = 7.00 \text{ days}$$
$$\sigma_C^2 = \left(\dfrac{10}{6}\right)^2 = 2.78 \text{ days}^2$$

**Activity D:**
$$t_e = \dfrac{5 + 32 + 15}{6} = \dfrac{52}{6} = 8.67 \text{ days}$$
$$\sigma_D^2 = \left(\dfrac{10}{6}\right)^2 = 2.78 \text{ days}^2$$

**Activity E:**
$$t_e = \dfrac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6.00 \text{ days}$$
$$\sigma_E^2 = \left(\dfrac{10}{6}\right)^2 = 2.78 \text{ days}^2$$

**Expected Project Duration (since all on critical path):**
$$T_e = 5.67 + 4.00 + 7.00 + 8.67 + 6.00 = 31.34 \text{ days}$$

(7 marks distributed across the five calculations and the summation)

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Compute the standard deviation, project variance, and probability of finishing 3 days ahead of schedule (i.e., by $T_s = 31.34 - 3 = 28.34$ days).

### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Project Variance (sum of variances on critical path):**
$$\sigma_{cp}^2 = 1.78 + 0.44 + 2.78 + 2.78 + 2.78 = 10.56 \text{ days}^2$$

**Step 2 — Project Standard Deviation:**
$$\sigma_{cp} = \sqrt{10.56} \approx 3.25 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 — Scheduled Time:**
$$T_s = T_e - 3 = 31.34 - 3 = 28.34 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Z-value (Probability Index):**
$$Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{cp}} = \dfrac{28.34 - 31.34}{3.25} = \dfrac{-3}{3.25} \approx -0.923$$

**Step 5 — Probability from Standard Normal Tables:**
$$P(Z \leq -0.923) = 0.5 - P(0 \leq Z \leq 0.923)$$
$$P(0 \leq Z \leq 0.92) \approx 0.3212, \quad P(0 \leq Z \leq 0.93) \approx 0.3238$$

Interpolating at $Z = 0.923$:
$$P(0 \leq Z \leq 0.923) \approx 0.3212 + 0.3 \times (0.3238 - 0.3212) = 0.3221$$

$$P(Z \leq -0.923) = 0.5 - 0.3221 = 0.1779 \approx 17.8\%$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** There is approximately a **17.8% probability** of completing the formwork project 3 days ahead of the expected schedule. This is a relatively low probability, indicating that the 3-day early target is ambitious and would require aggressive crashing of critical activities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Variance Summation Error:** Students often sum standard deviations instead of variances. Remember: **add variances, then take the square root of the sum**.
> 2. **Forward Pass Mistake:** At a converging node, the maximum ES of all incoming activities must be taken, not the sum.
> 3. **Dummy Activity Logic:** When two activities share the same start and end nodes, a dummy is mandatory. Conversely, if a "dummy" already represents a logical dependency, do not double-count it.
> 4. **Critical Path Identification:** Always verify using $TF = 0$, not just by "longest path" assumption (in networks with multiple long paths, all are critical).
> 5. **Z-value Sign Convention:** In KTU valuation, $Z = (T_s - T_e)/\sigma$. A negative $Z$ means "ahead of schedule" — a frequent source of mark deductions.
> 6. **CPM vs. PERT mixing:** Do not compute expected times for CPM activities (CPM uses single deterministic durations only).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CPM** = Deterministic, single time estimate; **PERT** = Probabilistic, three time estimates.
- **Critical Path** = Longest-duration path; determines **project completion time**.
- **Forward Pass** computes $ES$ and $EF$; **Backward Pass** computes $LS$ and $LF$.
- **Total Float (TF)** = $LS - ES$ = $LF - EF$. Activities with $TF = 0$ are critical.
- **Free Float (FF)** = $ES_{successor} - EF_i$.
- **PERT Expected Time:** $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$.
- **PERT Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ — applied **only on the critical path**.
- **Project Variance** is the **sum** of variances of critical path activities (variances add, standard deviations do not).
- **Z-value:** $Z = (T_s - T_e)/\sigma_{cp}$. Look up probability in standard normal table.
- **Dummy Activity:** Zero-duration, zero-cost, used purely to maintain logical dependency in AOA networks.
- **Formwork Cycle Float Hierarchy:** Critical activities (zero float) > Beam formwork (1–2 days) > Column stripping (2–4 days) > Wall one-side formwork (3–5 days) > Prop de-shuttering (5–7 days).
- **Cost-Time Trade-off:** $C_T = C_{direct} + C_{indirect}$; minimum $C_T$ occurs at the **optimum duration**.
- **Crash Cost Slope:** $\text{Slope} = \Delta C / \Delta t$ (Rs/day); choose the **lowest slope activity on the critical path** for crashing.
- **Real-World Context:** Formwork is **30%–60% of structural cost** and **35%–60% of project timeline** — hence its dominant role in the network.
- **Most-Favoured KTU Question Patterns:** (i) Network construction with critical path, (ii) PERT expected time + Z-probability, (iii) Crashing/cost-time optimization, (iv) Float calculations and resource implications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Formwork Costs and Labor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimating Formwork Costs and Labor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Formwork Cost Estimation** is the systematic process of quantifying the total monetary investment required for the procurement, fabrication, transportation, erection, maintenance, and stripping of temporary structures used to mold fresh concrete until it gains sufficient strength to be self-supporting. It encompasses both direct costs (materials, labor, equipment) and indirect costs (overheads, profit margins, contingency).

**Labor Estimation in Formwork** refers to the scientific determination of manpower requirements—including the number of carpenters, helpers, supervisors, and the associated man-hours—necessary to execute formwork activities within a stipulated project schedule, while accounting for productivity rates, crew composition, and site conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Per Module 4, students must be able to compute total formwork cost for a given structure, optimize the number of reuses, and determine crew size based on productivity rates and project duration.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are hosting a large birthday party. You need chairs, tables, decorations, and a cake. You have two choices:
- **Option A:** Buy everything new for the party and discard it afterward (high cost, single use).
- **Option B:** Rent durable chairs and tables that can be reused for multiple parties (lower cost per party after amortization).

**Formwork is exactly this.** It is a "temporary mold" rented for the concrete. Some formwork (like timber) is used fewer times; some (like steel or aluminum systems) can be reused many times. The **art of cost estimation** is figuring out: "How much will I spend, and how can I reuse intelligently to bring down the per-use cost?"

For **labor**, it is like calculating how many chefs you need to cook a feast. If each chef can prepare 5 dishes per hour, and you need 200 dishes in 4 hours, you need `200 / (5 × 4) = 10 chefs`. Formwork labor follows the same logic—**productivity rate × time = work done**.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Formwork typically constitutes **35% to 60% of the total concrete cost** in a project.
> - Labor alone accounts for **40% to 60%** of total formwork cost.
> - Incorrect estimation leads to **cost overruns, schedule delays, and contract disputes**.
> - Standard KTU values: Productivity rate = **0.5 to 1.0 m²/hr per carpenter** for erection.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Components of Formwork Cost

The total formwork cost (`TFC`) is a sum of the following five primary heads:

1. **Material Cost ($C_m$):** Plywood, timber, steel, aluminum, props, soldiers, walers.
2. **Labor Cost ($C_l$):** Carpenters, helpers, supervisors, oiling/cleaning crew.
3. **Equipment Cost ($C_e$):** Crane charges, hoists, vibrators, scaffolding rentals.
4. **Accessory Cost ($C_a$):** Ties, clamps, wedges, form release agents, nails, bolts.
5. **Overhead and Profit ($C_{oh}$):** Site supervision, insurance, contractor's profit margin (typically **10% to 15%** of direct cost).

### 2.2 Cost Estimation Approaches (Three Methods)

- **Method 1 – Per Unit Area:** Cost is expressed in **Rs. per m²** of contact area. Most common in practice.
- **Method 2 – Percentage of Concrete Cost:** Formwork cost is taken as a fixed percentage (35–60%) of the concrete cost.
- **Method 3 – Percentage of Total Project Cost:** Used in preliminary feasibility studies (3–8% of total project cost).

### 2.3 Labor Categories and Crew Composition

A standard formwork crew consists of:
- **Carpenters (skilled):** Cut, assemble, erect, and align formwork.
- **Helpers (semi-skilled):** Carry materials, hold members, assist in stripping.
- **Supervisor / Foreman:** Plans daily work, ensures quality and safety.
- **Helper-to-Carpenter Ratio:** Typically **1.5 : 1 to 2 : 1** for general formwork, up to **3 : 1** for repetitive slab work.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $TFC = C_m + C_l + C_e + C_a + C_{oh}$ | Total Formwork Cost | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | $C_u = \dfrac{TFC}{A_{contact}}$ | Cost per unit contact area | ₹ / m² |
| 3 | $CPU = \dfrac{C_i - S}{N} + C_m$ | Cost per use (amortized) | ₹ / use |
| 4 | $L_h = \dfrac{A_{contact}}{P_{rate}}$ | Total labor hours required | hours |
| 5 | $C_l = L_h \times (W_c + r \times W_h)$ | Labor cost (c = carpenter, h = helper) | ₹ |
| 6 | $N_{crew} = \dfrac{L_h}{T_{avail} \times \eta}$ | Crew size required | workers |
| 7 | $\%_{formwork} = \dfrac{C_{formwork}}{C_{concrete}} \times 100$ | Formwork as % of concrete cost | % |
| 8 | $N_{breakeven} = \dfrac{C_{i1} - C_{i2}}{C_{m2} - C_{m1}}$ | Breakeven number of uses | uses |
| 9 | $A_{contact} = 2 \times (L \times W) + 2 \times (L \times D) + 2 \times (W \times D)$ | Beam/column contact area | m² |
| 10 | $P_{crew} = N_{crew} \times P_{rate} \times \eta$ | Crew productivity per hour | m² / hr |

**Symbol Legend:**
- $A_{contact}$ = Formwork contact area; $C_i$ = Initial cost; $S$ = Salvage value; $N$ = Number of uses; $C_m$ = Maintenance cost per use.
- $P_{rate}$ = Individual productivity rate; $W_c, W_h$ = Wage rates; $r$ = Helper-to-carpenter ratio.
- $T_{avail}$ = Available time; $\eta$ = Efficiency factor (0.7 to 0.85); $L$ = Length, $W$ = Width, $D$ = Depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In numerical problems, always show the units at every step. Marks are deducted for missing units in intermediate steps.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bid Preparation:** Contractors use these formulas to prepare competitive tenders.
- **Budget Control:** Project managers track actual vs. estimated costs.
- **System Selection:** Helps decide between timber, steel, or aluminum formwork.
- **Scheduling:** Labor estimation drives the project timeline and resource planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cost Per Use (CPU) Amortization Formula

When formwork is reused `N` times, the initial cost gets distributed across multiple uses. The total expenditure over `N` uses is:

$$
\text{Total Expenditure} = C_i + (N \times C_m) - S
$$

The cost per single use is the total expenditure divided by the number of uses:

$$
CPU = \frac{C_i + (N \times C_m) - S}{N}
$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$
CPU = \frac{C_i - S}{N} + \frac{N \times C_m}{N}
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
CPU = \frac{C_i - S}{N} + C_m
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
- The first term $\dfrac{C_i - S}{N}$ represents the **amortized initial cost** spread over `N` uses.
- The second term $C_m$ is the **recurring maintenance cost** incurred every use.
- As $N \to \infty$, the first term approaches zero, meaning cost per use approaches $C_m$.
- However, physical wear limits `N` to a maximum value, beyond which the formwork must be replaced.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Breakeven Number of Uses

Consider two formwork systems:
- **System 1:** Initial cost $C_{i1}$, Maintenance per use $C_{m1}$
- **System 2:** Initial cost $C_{i2}$, Maintenance per use $C_{m2}$

The cost per use of System 1:

$$
CPU_1 = \frac{C_{i1}}{N} + C_{m1}
$$

The cost per use of System 2:

$$
CPU_2 = \frac{C_{i2}}{N} + C_{m2}
$$

At the breakeven point, $CPU_1 = CPU_2$:

$$
\frac{C_{i1}}{N} + C_{m1} = \frac{C_{i2}}{N} + C_{m2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{C_{i1} - C_{i2}}{N} = C_{m2} - C_{m1}
$$

Solving for `N`:

$$
N_{breakeven} = \frac{C_{i1} - C_{i2}}{C_{m2} - C_{m1}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If the project requires **more than $N_{breakeven}$ uses**, choose the system with **higher initial cost but lower maintenance** (System 1 if $C_{i1} > C_{i2}$).
- If the project requires **fewer uses**, choose the system with **lower initial cost**.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 1: Breakeven Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
Two formwork systems are available:
- **System A:** Initial cost = Rs. 2,50,000; Maintenance = Rs. 5,000 per use
- **System B:** Initial cost = Rs. 1,80,000; Maintenance = Rs. 7,500 per use

Both have negligible salvage value. Find the breakeven number of uses and recommend a system for a project requiring 40 uses.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify parameters.

$$
C_{iA} = 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad ; \quad C_{mA} = 5{,}000 \text{ ₹/use}
$$
$$
C_{iB} = 1{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad ; \quad C_{mB} = 7{,}500 \text{ ₹/use}
$$

Step 2: Apply breakeven formula.

$$
N_{breakeven} = \frac{C_{iA} - C_{iB}}{C_{mB} - C_{mA}}
$$

$$
N_{breakeven} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000}{7{,}500 - 5{,}000}
$$

$$
N_{breakeven} = \frac{70{,}000}{2{,}500} = 28 \text{ uses}
$$

Step 3: Verify by calculating CPU for both systems at N = 40.

For System A:

$$
CPU_A = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{40} + 5{,}000 = 6{,}250 + 5{,}000 = 11{,}250 \text{ ₹/use}
$$

For System B:

$$
CPU_B = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{40} + 7{,}500 = 4{,}500 + 7{,}500 = 12{,}000 \text{ ₹/use}
$$

Step 4: Decision.

Since $N_{project} = 40 > N_{breakeven} = 28$, **System A is more economical**. Total savings = $40 \times (12{,}000 - 11{,}250) = \textbf{Rs. 30,000}$.

### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 2: Labor Cost Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
Estimate the labor cost for a slab formwork of 600 m² contact area, given:
- Carpenter productivity = 0.75 m²/hr
- Helper to carpenter ratio = 2:1
- Carpenter wage = Rs. 600/hr
- Helper wage = Rs. 350/hr
- Work to be completed in 6 working days of 8 hours each.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate total labor hours required.

$$
L_h = \frac{A_{contact}}{P_{rate}} = \frac{600}{0.75} = 800 \text{ carpenter-hours}
$$

Step 2: Determine crew composition.

Let carpenters = $x$, helpers = $2x$. Total crew size = $3x$.

Step 3: Calculate available time.

$$
T_{avail} = 6 \text{ days} \times 8 \text{ hr/day} = 48 \text{ hours}
$$

Step 4: Calculate crew size needed.

$$
N_{crew} = \frac{L_h}{T_{avail} \times \eta} = \frac{800}{48 \times 0.80} = \frac{800}{38.4} \approx 20.83
$$

Round up to **21 workers total**. With 2:1 ratio:
- Carpenters = 7
- Helpers = 14

Step 5: Calculate labor cost.

$$
C_l = 7 \times 48 \times 600 + 14 \times 48 \times 350
$$

$$
C_l = 2{,}01{,}600 + 2{,}35{,}200 = 4{,}36{,}800 \text{ ₹}
$$

Total labor cost = **Rs. 4,36,800**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Formwork Cost Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTFC[Total Formwork Cost] --> nodeMat[Material Cost]
    nodeTFC --> nodeLab[Labor Cost]
    nodeTFC --> nodeEq[Equipment Cost]
    nodeTFC --> nodeAcc[Accessory Cost]
    nodeTFC --> nodeOH[Overhead and Profit]
    nodeMat --> nodePly[Plywood Sheathing]
    nodeMat --> nodeLumb[Timber Studs and Joists]
    nodeMat --> nodeSteel[Steel or Aluminum Panels]
    nodeLab --> nodeCarp[Carpenter Wages]
    nodeLab --> nodeHelp[Helper Wages]
    nodeLab --> nodeSup[Supervision Cost]
    nodeEq --> nodeCrane[Crane and Hoist]
    nodeEq --> nodeScaf[Scaffolding Rental]
    nodeAcc --> nodeTie[Ties and Clamps]
    nodeAcc --> nodeRel[Form Release Agent]
    nodeAcc --> nodeNail[Nails Bolts and Wedges]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Formwork Cost Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Start Project Estimation] --> nodeDraw[Review Structural Drawings]
    nodeDraw --> nodeQty[Calculate Formwork Contact Area]
    nodeQty --> nodeType[Select Formwork System Type]
    nodeType --> nodeMatRate[Determine Material Unit Rates]
    nodeMatRate --> nodeMatCost[Compute Material Cost]
    nodeType --> nodeProd[Determine Productivity Rates]
    nodeProd --> nodeCrew[Estimate Crew Composition]
    nodeCrew --> nodeLabCost[Compute Labor Cost]
    nodeMatCost --> nodeTotal[Sum Direct Costs]
    nodeLabCost --> nodeTotal
    nodeTotal --> nodeOH[Add Overhead 10 to 15 percent]
    nodeOH --> nodeFinal[Final Formwork Cost Estimate]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Labor Productivity Estimation Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeProj[Project Schedule and Drawings] --> nodeArea[Total Formwork Contact Area]
    nodeArea --> nodeRate[Productivity Rate Database]
    nodeRate --> nodeHours[Total Labor Hours]
    nodeHours --> nodeCrew[Determine Crew Size]
    nodeCrew --> nodeTime[Project Duration Check]
    nodeTime --> nodeWage[Apply Wage Rates]
    nodeWage --> nodeCost[Total Labor Cost Output]
    nodeTime --> nodeAdjust{Schedule Met}
    nodeAdjust --> nodeYes[Accept Plan]
    nodeAdjust --> nodeNo[Increase Crew Size]
    nodeNo --> nodeCrew
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Breakeven Analysis Decision Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSys[Compare Two Formwork Systems] --> nodePar[Extract Initial Cost and Maintenance]
    nodePar --> nodeForm[Apply Breakeven Formula]
    nodeForm --> nodeBE[Compute Breakeven Uses N]
    nodeBE --> nodeDec{Actual Uses vs Breakeven}
    nodeDec --> nodeHigh[More Than Breakeven]
    nodeDec --> nodeLow[Less Than Breakeven]
    nodeHigh --> nodeRec1[Choose System with Higher Initial Cost and Lower Maintenance]
    nodeLow --> nodeRec2[Choose System with Lower Initial Cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams map the functional relationships between cost components, estimation workflows, and decision logic. They are designed for KTU 2024 Scheme examination writing, where schematic representation fetches higher marks in design-oriented questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define formwork cost estimation. List any four major components contributing to the total formwork cost.

**Model Answer:**
Formwork cost estimation is the systematic process of evaluating the total monetary expenditure required for designing, fabricating, erecting, maintaining, and stripping temporary molds used to shape concrete until it hardens.

Four major components are:
1. **Material Cost** – Plywood, timber, steel panels, props.
2. **Labor Cost** – Wages of carpenters, helpers, and supervisors.
3. **Equipment Cost** – Crane charges, hoists, scaffolding rentals.
4. **Accessory Cost** – Ties, clamps, form release agents, nails.

*(Any other valid component like Overhead/Profit is also acceptable.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
What is meant by labor productivity in formwork? State typical productivity values for formwork erection and stripping.

**Model Answer:**
Labor productivity in formwork refers to the rate at which a worker (or crew) completes formwork-related activities, typically expressed in **square meters per hour (m²/hr)** per worker.

Typical values:
- **Formwork erection:** **0.5 to 1.0 m²/hr per carpenter**
- **Formwork stripping:** **0.2 to 0.4 m²/hr per worker**
- **Cleaning and oiling:** **0.1 to 0.25 m²/hr per worker**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various components of formwork cost with suitable examples. List the factors affecting each component.

**Model Answer:**

Formwork cost comprises the following components:

**1. Material Cost (typically 30–40% of TFC):**
- Includes plywood, timber, steel, aluminum, props, and walers.
- Affected by: market price, wastage factor (5–10%), number of reuses, and type of formwork system.

**2. Labor Cost (typically 40–50% of TFC):**
- Includes wages of carpenters, helpers, and supervisors.
- Affected by: skill level, helper-to-carpenter ratio, site conditions, and weather.

**3. Equipment Cost (typically 5–10% of TFC):**
- Includes crane charges, hoists, and scaffolding rentals.
- Affected by: project location, height of structure, and equipment availability.

**4. Accessory Cost (typically 5–10% of TFC):**
- Includes ties, clamps, form release agents, nails, and wedges.
- Affected by: design complexity and type of concrete finish required.

**5. Overhead and Profit (typically 10–15% of direct cost):**
- Includes site supervision, insurance, transportation, and contractor's profit.

**Example:** For a typical residential project, the distribution is approximately:
Material = 35%, Labor = 45%, Equipment = 8%, Accessories = 7%, Overhead = 5%.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two formwork systems are available for a project:
- **System P:** Initial cost = Rs. 3,00,000; Maintenance = Rs. 4,000 per use
- **System Q:** Initial cost = Rs. 2,20,000; Maintenance = Rs. 6,500 per use

Both have negligible salvage value. Determine the breakeven number of uses and recommend the more economical system for a project requiring 50 uses.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Apply the breakeven formula.**

$$
N_{breakeven} = \frac{C_{iP} - C_{iQ}}{C_{mQ} - C_{mP}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}20{,}000}{6{,}500 - 4{,}000}
$$

$$
N_{breakeven} = \frac{80{,}000}{2{,}500} = 32 \text{ uses}
$$

**Step 2: Verify by computing CPU at N = 50.**

For System P:

$$
CPU_P = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{50} + 4{,}000 = 6{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ ₹/use}
$$

For System Q:

$$
CPU_Q = \frac{2{,}20{,}000}{50} + 6{,}500 = 4{,}400 + 6{,}500 = 10{,}900 \text{ ₹/use}
$$

**Step 3: Decision.**

Since the project requires **50 uses > 32 uses (breakeven)**, **System P is more economical**.

Total savings = $50 \times (10{,}900 - 10{,}000) = \textbf{Rs. 45,000}$.

---

#### **Question B**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various factors affecting formwork labor productivity on a construction site.

**Model Answer:**

Factors affecting formwork labor productivity:

**1. Skill Level of Workers:** Highly skilled carpenters erect formwork faster and with fewer errors. Trained workers can achieve productivity up to 1.0 m²/hr, while novices may achieve only 0.4 m²/hr.

**2. Type of Formwork System:** Modular systems (steel, aluminum) are faster to erect than custom timber formwork. Repetitive use of the same system improves speed.

**3. Site Conditions:** Congested sites, poor access, and adverse weather (rain, extreme heat) reduce productivity by 10–30%.

**4. Design Complexity:** Simple, repetitive members (slabs, walls) allow higher productivity. Complex geometries (curved beams, domes) reduce speed.

**5. Crew Composition:** Optimal helper-to-carpenter ratio (1.5:1 to 2:1) ensures smooth workflow. Too few helpers cause delays; too many cause coordination issues.

**6. Material Availability:** Just-in-time delivery of materials prevents idle time. Stockouts can reduce productivity by 20–40%.

**7. Supervision Quality:** Experienced foremen reduce rework and improve sequencing, boosting effective productivity by 10–15%.

**8. Working Height:** Productivity decreases by 5–10% for every 3 m increase in working height above ground level.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Estimate the total labor cost for a beam and column formwork project with the following data:
- Total contact area = 450 m²
- Carpenter productivity = 0.6 m²/hr
- Helper to carpenter ratio = 2:1
- Carpenter wage = Rs. 550/hr; Helper wage = Rs. 320/hr
- Efficiency factor = 0.80
- Project must be completed in 10 working days of 8 hours each.

Also determine the required crew size.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate total carpenter-hours required.**

$$
L_h = \frac{450}{0.6} = 750 \text{ carpenter-hours}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate available time per carpenter.**

$$
T_{avail} = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate number of carpenters required.**

$$
N_{carp} = \frac{750}{80 \times 0.80} = \frac{750}{64} \approx 11.72
$$

Round up to **12 carpenters**.

**Step 4: Determine number of helpers (2:1 ratio).**

$$
N_{help} = 2 \times 12 = 24 \text{ helpers}
$$

**Total crew size = 12 + 24 = 36 workers.**

**Step 5: Calculate labor cost.**

$$
C_l = (12 \times 80 \times 550) + (24 \times 80 \times 320)
$$

$$
C_l = 5{,}28{,}000 + 6{,}14{,}400
$$

$$
C_l = 11{,}42{,}400 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Total labor cost = Rs. 11,42,400.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor ($\eta$):** Many students omit the 0.75–0.85 efficiency factor, leading to under-estimated crew sizes. Always multiply available time by $\eta$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing m² and ft² or hours and days without conversion. Always write units explicitly.
> 3. **Rounding down crew size:** If calculation gives 11.72, round **up** to 12 (never down), otherwise the work cannot be completed on time.
> 4. **Ignoring helper wages:** Labor cost must include **both** carpenter and helper wages. Skipping helpers is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusion between cost per use vs. total cost:** The breakeven formula gives the number of uses, not the cost. Clearly state which value you are computing.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Estimating Formwork Costs and Labor**

- ✅ **Total Formwork Cost (TFC)** = Material + Labor + Equipment + Accessories + Overhead.
- ✅ **Formwork cost** is typically **35% to 60%** of concrete cost and **3% to 8%** of total project cost.
- ✅ **Cost Per Use (CPU)** = $\dfrac{C_i - S}{N} + C_m$. As `N` increases, CPU decreases (asymptotically toward $C_m$).
- ✅ **Breakeven Uses** = $\dfrac{C_{i1} - C_{i2}}{C_{m2} - C_{m1}}$. Use this to compare two formwork systems.
- ✅ **Labor Hours** = $\dfrac{A_{contact}}{P_{rate}}$. Productivity rates: erection = **0.5–1.0 m²/hr**, stripping = **0.2–0.4 m²/hr**.
- ✅ **Labor Cost** = $L_h \times (W_c + r \times W_h)$ where `r` is the helper-to-carpenter ratio.
- ✅ **Crew Size** = $\dfrac{L_h}{T_{avail} \times \eta}$ where $\eta$ = efficiency factor (**0.70 to 0.85**).
- ✅ **Helper-to-Carpenter Ratio:** Typically **1.5:1 to 2:1** for general work; up to **3:1** for repetitive slab formwork.
- ✅ **Always round crew size UP** to the next whole number to meet the schedule.
- ✅ **Standard Units:** ₹ for cost, m² for area, hours for time, m²/hr for productivity.
- ✅ **Optimization Principle:** Choose the formwork system that minimizes total project cost, not just per-use cost.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Focus:** Numerical problems on breakeven analysis and labor cost estimation appear almost every semester—master them.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling and Sequencing Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scheduling and Sequencing Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Scheduling** in formwork engineering is the time-based planning process that determines **when** each formwork activity (assembly, erection, alignment, inspection, concrete pouring, curing monitoring, stripping, cleaning, and re-erection) will be performed within the overall construction project timeline. It allocates calendar dates and durations to activities while satisfying precedence, resource, and cost constraints.

**Sequencing** is the logical ordering process that determines **the order** in which formwork operations are carried out across building zones, floors, or repetitive units. It establishes the dependency relationships (finish-to-start, start-to-start, finish-to-finish) between activities so that productivity, safety, and quality are optimized.

Together, they form the **Time Management Core** of formwork planning, executed through tools such as **Gantt Charts, Critical Path Method (CPM), Program Evaluation Review Technique (PERT), and Line of Balance (LOB)** for repetitive high-rise formwork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Formwork scheduling is treated as the *bridge between design intent and site execution*. Any error in sequencing directly inflates the **formwork cost per m²**, which typically ranges from **15% to 25%** of the total structural cost of a building.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **busy South Indian wedding kitchen (sadya preparation)** preparing food for **1000 guests**.

- The cook cannot boil rice, fry vegetables, and prepare payasam all at the same instant.
- He must **sequence** the dishes — rice first, then curries, then desserts (because some items take hours to cook).
- He must also **schedule** — payasam must be ready by 11 AM, rice by 12 PM, fries by 12:15 PM, just before serving.

If the cook begins payasam *before* rice, the whole meal collapses. The same logic applies to formwork:

- **Formwork cannot be stripped** before the concrete gains adequate strength (curing time constraint).
- **Re-erection** cannot begin before cleaning and oiling are complete.
- **Pouring** cannot begin before reinforcement and MEP inserts are inspected and signed off.

Just as the cook balances the **kitchen crew, vessels, and time**, a formwork engineer balances **crews, formwork sets, and project calendar**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Formwork is the *only* structural component that is **reused** many times. Hence, scheduling and sequencing directly control the **number of formwork sets** to be purchased/rented — a major cost driver.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized to KTU

| Term | Formal Definition | KTU Standard |
|------|------------------|--------------|
| **Activity** | A discrete piece of work in formwork cycle | Unit of scheduling |
| **Cycle Time (CT)** | Time between successive pours in the same zone | **days/floor** |
| **Lead Time** | Time an activity can start before predecessor finishes | Lags/overlaps |
| **Float / Slack** | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying project | Free float, Total float |
| **Critical Path** | Longest path of dependent activities | Zero float path |
| **Repetitive Unit** | Identical floor/zone where formwork is reused | Apartment, deck segment |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The scheduling of formwork operations follows a five-stage analytical framework:

1. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) Decomposition**
   - Break the formwork scope into measurable activities: *fabrication, transport, assembly, erection, alignment, inspection, pouring, curing, stripping, cleaning, and re-erection*.
   - Why? Without a WBS, duration estimates and resource loading become meaningless.

2. **Precedence Diagramming (Sequencing)**
   - Establish logical links: stripping *cannot* begin before curing reaches **stripping strength** (typically 70% of f_ck for vertical, 100% for soffit).
   - How? Use **Activity-on-Node (AON)** networks recommended in KTU 2024 modern practice.

3. **Duration Estimation**
   - Productivity-based: $D = \dfrac{Q}{R \times N}$, where $Q$ = quantity, $R$ = rate per worker, $N$ = crew size.
   - Historical data-based: use IS 7272 / ACI 347 productivity norms.

4. **Critical Path Identification**
   - Forward pass: compute Early Start (ES) and Early Finish (EF).
   - Backward pass: compute Late Start (LS) and Late Finish (LF).
   - Critical Path = activities with **Total Float = 0**.

5. **Resource Leveling & Formwork Set Optimization**
   - Compute required **number of formwork sets** using cycle time analysis.
   - Resolve resource conflicts by shifting non-critical activities within float.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for KTU 2024 Numerical Problems:** The following equations appear in 70% of past university questions on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $CT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Cycle Time = sum of activity durations in one complete cycle (assembly → pour → strip → reset) | days |
| 2 | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{T}{CT} \right\rceil$ | Number of formwork sets required | sets (integer) |
| 3 | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{(L + 1)}{CT} \right\rceil$ | Sets for L repetitive floors (Lags included) | sets |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{Q}{D \times C}$ | Productivity rate | m²/worker-day |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{R \times D}$ | Crew size calculation | workers |
| 6 | $ES_j = \max_i (EF_i)$ | Early Start of successor | days |
| 7 | $LF_i = \min_j (LS_j)$ | Late Finish of predecessor | days |
| 8 | $TF = LS - ES = LF - EF$ | Total Float | days |
| 9 | $FF = ES_{succ} - EF_i$ | Free Float | days |
| 10 | $Cost_{form} = N \times C_{set} \times T_{project}$ | Total formwork ownership cost | ₹ |
| 11 | $Earned\ Value = \%\ complete \times BAC$ | Progress monitoring | ₹ |

> **Symbols Used:** $T$ = total project duration, $L$ = number of floors/units, $Q$ = quantity, $D$ = duration, $C$ = crew, $R$ = individual rate, $i$ = predecessor index, $j$ = successor index, $BAC$ = Budget at Completion.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise residential towers (Kerala context):** Determines whether 2, 3, or 4 sets of table-form / tunnel-form are needed.
- **Bridge deck construction:** Line of Balance (LOB) coordinates formwork movement between piers.
- **Industrial silos:** Sequencing of jump-form lifts (typically 2.5 m to 3 m per lift) controls overall construction rate.
