- **Monetary impact:** An error of even **2 extra formwork sets** in a 20-floor building can inflate formwork cost by **₹40–60 lakhs** for a typical project.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The Number-of-Sets formula $N = \lceil T / CT \rceil$ is the **single most repeated numerical** in KTU university exams on this topic. Always show the ceiling function explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Formwork Sets Required

**Statement (Given):** A high-rise residential project in Kochi has **L = 20 floors**. Each floor requires formwork for **2 days of activity overlap** with the next floor. The total project duration for the formwork phase is **T = 90 days**. Determine the minimum number of formwork sets required for uninterrupted pouring.

**Step 1 — Define the cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT &= \text{Time between two successive pour cycles on adjacent floors} \\
   &= t_{erect} + t_{strip} + t_{reset} + t_{cure\ lag}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume from site data: $t_{erect} = 2\ days$, $t_{strip} = 1\ day$, $t_{reset} = 1\ day$, $t_{lag} = 2\ days$.

**Step 2 — Compute CT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT &= 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 \\
   &= 6\ days\ per\ floor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Number-of-Sets formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lceil \frac{(L-1) \times \text{overlap} + CT}{CT} \right\rceil \\
  &= \left\lceil \frac{(20-1) \times 2 + 6}{6} \right\rceil \\
  &= \left\lceil \frac{38 + 6}{6} \right\rceil \\
  &= \left\lceil \frac{44}{6} \right\rceil \\
  &= \lceil 7.33 \rceil \\
  &= 8\ sets
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Logical verification:**

If only 7 sets were used, the last floor (20th) would finish at:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{7\ sets} &= 7 \times 6 = 42\ days\ of\ pure\ cycling
\end{aligned}
$$

But 19 inter-floor overlaps require $19 \times 2 = 38\ days$. Adding 6 days for the first-floor cycle: $42 < 44$, meaning 7 sets cause a **delay of 2 days**. Hence **8 sets** are mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer Writing Rule:** Always carry the **ceiling brackets $\lceil \cdot \rceil$** throughout the calculation, and add a one-line verbal justification in the end. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this step.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Crew Size for Formwork Productivity

**Problem:** A project needs to erect **1200 m²** of column formwork in **8 days**. Standard rate is **40 m² per worker per day**. Determine crew size.

**Step 1 — Apply productivity formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{Q}{D \times C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and solve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
40 &= \frac{1200}{8 \times C} \\
8 \times C &= \frac{1200}{40} = 30 \\
C &= \frac{30}{8} = 3.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Round up to integer (you cannot have 0.75 of a worker):**

$$
C = 4\ workers
$$

**Step 4 — Adjusted actual duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{actual} &= \frac{1200}{40 \times 4} = 7.5\ days \quad (\text{rounded to 8 days})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget to round up the crew. Examiners deduct marks if you write $C = 3.75$ without commenting on practical rounding.

---

### 3.3 Forward & Backward Pass — Small AON Network

A simple formwork network for one floor has the following activities:

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| A — Formwork Fabrication | None | 4 |
| B — Transport to Site | A | 2 |
| C — Erection | B | 3 |
| D — Reinforcement | C | 4 |
| E — Pouring | C | 1 |
| F — Curing | E | 7 |
| G — Stripping | F | 1 |
| H — Inspection | D | 1 |

**Forward Pass (ES, EF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES_A &= 0,\quad EF_A = 4 \\
ES_B &= 4,\quad EF_B = 6 \\
ES_C &= 6,\quad EF_C = 9 \\
ES_D &= 9,\quad EF_D = 13 \\
ES_E &= 9,\quad EF_E = 10 \\
ES_F &= 10,\quad EF_F = 17 \\
ES_G &= 17,\quad EF_G = 18 \\
ES_H &= 13,\quad EF_H = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration:** $\max(EF_G, EF_H) = \max(18, 14) = 18\ days$.

**Backward Pass (LS, LF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF_G &= 18,\quad LS_G = 17 \\
LF_F &= 17,\quad LS_F = 10 \\
LF_E &= 10,\quad LS_E = 9 \\
LF_H &= 18,\quad LS_H = 17 \\
LF_D &= 17,\quad LS_D = 13 \\
LF_C &= \min(LS_D, LS_E) = \min(13,9) = 9 \\
LS_C &= 9 - 3 = 6 \\
LF_B &= 6,\quad LS_B = 4 \\
LF_A &= 4,\quad LS_A = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Float Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF_A &= LS_A - ES_A = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
TF_C &= LS_C - ES_C = 6 - 6 = 0 \\
TF_E &= LS_E - ES_E = 9 - 9 = 0 \\
TF_F &= LS_F - ES_F = 10 - 10 = 0 \\
TF_G &= LS_G - ES_G = 17 - 17 = 0 \\
TF_B &= LS_B - ES_B = 4 - 4 = 0 \\
TF_D &= LS_D - ES_D = 13 - 9 = 4 \quad (\text{Non-critical}) \\
TF_H &= LS_H - ES_H = 17 - 13 = 4 \quad (\text{Non-critical})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Path:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G$ (18 days).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Formwork Activity Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start of Floor Cycle] --> A2[Formwork Fabrication Off-site]
    A2 --> A3[Transport to Floor Location]
    A3 --> A4[Erection of Formwork]
    A4 --> A5[Alignment and Prop Setting]
    A5 --> A6{Quality Check Pass?}
    A6 -- No --> A7[Rework and Re-alignment]
    A7 --> A5
    A6 -- Yes --> A8[Reinforcement and MEP Insertion]
    A8 --> A9[Concrete Pouring]
    A9 --> A10[Curing Period 7 to 14 days]
    A10 --> A11{Concrete Strength Achieved?}
    A11 -- No --> A10
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[Stripping of Formwork]
    A12 --> A13[Cleaning and Oiling]
    A13 --> A14[Move to Next Floor]
    A14 --> A15[More Floors Remaining?]
    A15 -- Yes --> A4
    A15 -- No --> A16[End of Project]
```

### 4.2 Sequencing Strategy for High-Rise Formwork

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1[ZONE A]
        S1A[Floor 1 Pour Day 0]
        S1B[Floor 2 Pour Day 6]
        S1C[Floor 3 Pour Day 12]
    end
    subgraph S2[ZONE B]
        S2A[Floor 1 Pour Day 2]
        S2B[Floor 2 Pour Day 8]
        S2C[Floor 3 Pour Day 14]
    end
    subgraph S3[ZONE C]
        S3A[Floor 1 Pour Day 4]
        S3B[Floor 2 Pour Day 10]
        S3C[Floor 3 Pour Day 16]
    end
    S1A --> S1B --> S1C
    S2A --> S2B --> S2C
    S3A --> S3B --> S3C
    S1A -. Shared Formwork Set .-> S2A
    S2A -. Shared Formwork Set .-> S3A
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Formwork Set Quantity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Compute Cycle Time CT] --> D2[Identify Project Duration T]
    D2 --> D3[Apply Formula N equals Ceil T divided by CT]
    D3 --> D4{N satisfies overlap constraints?}
    D4 -- No --> D5[Increase N by 1 set]
    D5 --> D6[Recheck overlap and cost]
    D6 --> D4
    D4 -- Yes --> D7[Finalize N formwork sets]
    D7 --> D8[Compare rental vs purchase cost]
    D8 --> D9[Issue procurement order]
```

### 4.4 Critical Path Method Node Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[Node A: Fabrication Duration 4] --> N2[Node B: Transport Duration 2]
    N2 --> N3[Node C: Erection Duration 3]
    N3 --> N4[Node D: Reinforcement Duration 4]
    N3 --> N5[Node E: Pouring Duration 1]
    N4 --> N6[Node H: Inspection Duration 1]
    N5 --> N7[Node F: Curing Duration 7]
    N7 --> N8[Node G: Stripping Duration 1]
    N6 --> N8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The longest path in this AON network is `A → B → C → E → F → G` with a total of **18 days**. Activities `D` and `H` are non-critical with float of 4 days.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define cycle time in formwork scheduling. How does it affect the number of formwork sets required?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cycle time (CT) is the total time required to complete one full sequence of formwork activities — erection, pouring, curing, stripping, cleaning, and re-erection — on a single repetitive unit. It is the time gap between two successive pours on adjacent floors. The number of formwork sets required is computed as $N = \lceil T / CT \rceil$, where $T$ is the total project duration. A shorter cycle time reduces the number of sets needed, lowering capital cost; a longer cycle time forces procurement of additional sets, raising cost. Hence, cycle time is the **single most critical driver** of formwork economy in repetitive high-rise construction.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between Free Float and Total Float in formwork scheduling with examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Total Float (TF):** The time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **project completion date**. $TF = LS - ES$.
  - *Example:* Inspection activity has TF = 4 days, meaning it may start anywhere from Day 9 to Day 13 without delaying final stripping.
- **Free Float (FF):** The time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **early start of any successor activity**. $FF = ES_{successor} - EF_{current}$.
  - *Example:* If Reinforcement finishes on Day 13 but Inspection (its successor) is constrained to start on Day 17, then FF = 4 days.

**Difference:** TF considers overall project delay; FF considers only local successor impact. Critical activities always have $TF = FF = 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1: Numerical on Number of Formwork Sets

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

A 25-storey residential tower in Thrissur uses **aluminium table-form system**. Each floor requires:
- Erection: 2 days
- Pouring: 1 day
- Curing: 7 days (minimum)
- Stripping: 1 day
- Cleaning and resetting: 1 day

The formwork for the next floor is erected using a **lag of 4 days** after pouring of the previous floor (i.e., the next floor erection starts 4 days after the current pour, allowing overlap).

**(a)** Compute the cycle time and the **minimum number of formwork sets** required if the project must be completed in **T = 120 days** for the formwork phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the contractor plans to **rent** formwork at **₹18,000 per set per month**, compare the cost of using **the computed number of sets** versus **one additional set**. Discuss the trade-off in terms of schedule acceleration. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cycle Time Computation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify the cycle components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{erect} &= 2\ days \\
t_{pour} &= 1\ day \\
t_{cure\ lag} &= 4\ days\ (\text{overlap lag}) \\
t_{strip} &= 1\ day \\
t_{reset} &= 1\ day
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute cycle time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT &= t_{erect} + t_{pour} + t_{cure\ lag} + t_{strip} + t_{reset} \\
   &= 2 + 1 + 4 + 1 + 1 \\
   &= 9\ days\ per\ floor
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Number of sets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lceil \frac{T}{CT} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{120}{9} \right\rceil = \lceil 13.33 \rceil = 14\ sets
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** 14 sets × 9 days = 126 days of pure cycle coverage, but with 24 inter-floor overlaps requiring $24 \times 4 = 96$ days of pre-overlap time, total = $96 + 9 = 105\ days$ ≤ 120 days. ✓

**Answer:** **14 formwork sets are required.**

---

**(b) Cost Trade-off (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Cost of 14 sets for 4 months (120 days ≈ 4 months):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{14} &= 14 \times 18{,}000 \times 4 = ₹10{,}08{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Cost of 15 sets (one additional set) for the same 4-month rental:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{15} &= 15 \times 18{,}000 \times 4 = ₹10{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Marginal cost of the extra set:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= C_{15} - C_{14} = ₹72{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Schedule benefit of the extra set:
With 15 sets, the cycle throughput improves — possible to reduce overlap lag from 4 days to 3 days, saving 1 day per floor across 24 floors = **24 days saved on the formwork phase**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Value of 24 days} &= 24 \times \text{overhead/day} = 24 \times ₹50{,}000 = ₹12{,}00{,}000\ \text{(approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The extra ₹72,000 investment for the 15th set translates to a potential **24-day schedule acceleration**, yielding significant savings in site overheads, equipment idling, and client penalty avoidance. The trade-off is **economically justified** in delay-sensitive contracts.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2: CPM-Based Sequencing with Float Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

A formwork activity network for a typical floor has the following data:

| Activity | Description | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| A | Formwork Delivery to Site | None | 3 |
| B | Wall Formwork Erection | A | 4 |
| C | Column Formwork Erection | A | 3 |
| D | Slab Soffit Erection | B, C | 5 |
| E | Beam Formwork Erection | B | 2 |
| F | Concrete Pouring | D, E | 2 |
| G | Curing | F | 7 |
| H | Formwork Stripping | G | 2 |

**(a)** Draw the **AON network**, perform **forward and backward pass**, and identify the **critical path** and project duration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the client insists on a **5-day reduction in project duration**, suggest a **crashing strategy** with a minimum cost slope approach. Assume crashing costs (per day): A = ₹15,000, B = ₹20,000, C = ₹12,000, D = ₹25,000, E = ₹18,000, F = ₹30,000, G = ₹5,000, H = ₹10,000. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Network Analysis (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Forward Pass (ES, EF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES_A &= 0,\quad EF_A = 3 \\
ES_B &= 3,\quad EF_B = 7 \\
ES_C &= 3,\quad EF_C = 6 \\
ES_D &= \max(EF_B, EF_C) = 7,\quad EF_D = 12 \\
ES_E &= EF_B = 7,\quad EF_E = 9 \\
ES_F &= \max(EF_D, EF_E) = 12,\quad EF_F = 14 \\
ES_G &= EF_F = 14,\quad EF_G = 21 \\
ES_H &= EF_G = 21,\quad EF_H = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration = 23 days.**

Step 2 — Backward Pass (LS, LF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF_H &= 23,\quad LS_H = 21 \\
LF_G &= 21,\quad LS_G = 14 \\
LF_F &= 14,\quad LS_F = 12 \\
LF_E &= 12,\quad LS_E = 10 \\
LF_D &= 12,\quad LS_D = 7 \\
LF_C &= LS_D = 7,\quad LS_C = 4 \\
LF_B &= \min(LS_D, LS_E) = \min(7,10) = 7,\quad LS_B = 3 \\
LF_A &= \min(LS_B, LS_C) = \min(3,4) = 3,\quad LS_A = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Total Float:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF_A &= 0 \\
TF_B &= 0 \\
TF_C &= LS_C - ES_C = 4 - 3 = 1 \\
TF_D &= 0 \\
TF_E &= LS_E - ES_E = 10 - 7 = 3 \\
TF_F &= 0 \\
TF_G &= 0 \\
TF_H &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Path:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow F \rightarrow G \rightarrow H$ (Duration = 23 days).
**Non-critical:** Activity C (Float = 1), Activity E (Float = 3).

---

**(b) Crashing Strategy (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Only **critical activities** can be crashed to reduce duration: A, B, D, F, G, H.

Step 2 — Crash cost per day (lowest first):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= ₹5{,}000\ per\ day \\
H &= ₹10{,}000\ per\ day \\
A &= ₹15{,}000\ per\ day \\
B &= ₹20{,}000\ per\ day \\
D &= ₹25{,}000\ per\ day \\
F &= ₹30{,}000\ per\ day
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Crash sequence for 5 days:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Day 1:} &\quad \text{Crash } G\ (₹5{,}000) \rightarrow \text{Duration} = 22 \\
\text{Day 2:} &\quad \text{Crash } H\ (₹10{,}000) \rightarrow \text{Duration} = 21 \\
\text{Day 3:} &\quad \text{Crash } G\ (₹5{,}000)\ \text{if possible, else A} \rightarrow 20 \\
\text{Day 4:} &\quad \text{Crash } A\ (₹15{,}000) \rightarrow 19 \\
\text{Day 5:} &\quad \text{Crash } B\ (₹20{,}000) \rightarrow 18
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Total crashing cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{crash} &= 5{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 20{,}000 = ₹55{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. Forgetting that **only critical path activities** can be crashed; crashing C (float = 1) or E (float = 3) wastes money without reducing duration. **−2 marks**
> 2. Not re-evaluating the critical path after each crash — a new path may become critical. **−1 mark**
> 3. Writing crash cost without showing **step-wise allocation**. **−1 mark**
> 4. Omitting the **ceiling brackets** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the Number-of-Sets formula. **−1 mark**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cycle Time (CT)** is the heartbeat of repetitive formwork scheduling — it is the **sum of all activity times** in one pour cycle for a single floor.
- **Number of Formwork Sets** $N = \lceil T / CT \rceil$ directly controls capital and rental cost.
- **Productivity formula** $P = Q / (D \times C)$ is used to compute crew size and validate durations.
- **Critical Path Method (CPM)** identifies activities with **zero float** that govern the project duration.
- **Free Float (FF)** is the local delay allowance; **Total Float (TF)** is the project-level delay allowance.
- **Crashing** must always start from the **lowest cost-slope activity on the critical path**, and the path must be re-evaluated after each crash.
- **Line of Balance (LOB)** is preferred for high-rise repetitive formwork because it visualizes flow versus time across floors.
- **Lead time** in formwork = time advantage gained by overlapping activities (e.g., stripping a beam formwork 7 days after pour while slab curing continues).
- **Resource leveling** is essential when multiple crews compete for the same crane or staging area.
- **Buffer time** of 10–15% should be added to estimated durations to account for monsoon delays in Kerala (May–September).
- The **formwork cycle must be synchronized** with the **concrete supply chain** (batching plant, transit mixers, pump location) to avoid idle crews.
- **Safety sequencing**: Stripping sequence must follow the **engineer's approved stripping plan** — typically slabs first, then beams, then columns.
- **AON (Activity-on-Node)** is preferred over **AOA (Activity-on-Arrow)** in modern KTU 2024 practice because of simpler dummy activity handling.
- For high-rise projects, **Gantt charts** (MS Project / Primavera P6) are mandatory for progress tracking and **Earned Value Analysis (EVA)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Management Tools and Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Management Tools and Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Project Management Tools and Techniques" in Formwork Engineering?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Project Management Tools and Techniques** are a structured set of analytical, graphical, and mathematical methodologies used to plan, schedule, monitor, and control the sequence, duration, cost, and resources of repetitive formwork operations in a building or infrastructure project. They convert a complex construction programme into measurable activities, durations, dependencies, and resource flows.

In the context of **Formwork Engineering (PECET742)**, these tools help the site engineer answer four practical questions for every slab cycle, wall pour, and column lift:

1. **What** activities must happen? *(Scope definition)*
2. **In what order** must they happen? *(Logic / dependency)*
3. **How long** will each take? *(Duration estimation)*
4. **Which activities are critical** — a one-day delay here = a one-day delay in handover? *(Critical Path)*

---

### 1.2 The Six Core Tools (KTU High-Yield List)

| # | Tool | One-Line Meaning |
|---|------|------------------|
| 1 | **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** | A hierarchical decomposition of the *total project* into *work packages*. |
| 2 | **Gantt Chart (Bar Chart)** | Activities plotted as horizontal bars against a **calendar time axis**. |
| 3 | **Milestone Chart** | Key checkpoints (e.g., *strip-out of 5th floor slab*) marked on a time axis. |
| 4 | **Network Diagram (AOA / AON)** | Logic-based diagram showing *predecessor–successor* relationships. |
| 5 | **Critical Path Method (CPM)** | Deterministic network analysis — finds the **longest path** = shortest project duration. |
| 6 | **Program Evaluation & Review Technique (PERT)** | Probabilistic network analysis using *optimistic, most likely, pessimistic* times. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** For PECET742, examiners expect you to apply these tools to a **formwork cycle** — e.g., striking, hoisting, cleaning, oiling, re-erecting, and re-propping — not to a generic manufacturing project. Always frame your answers in a *formwork-shuttering context*.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Daily Train Commute"

Imagine a **Kerala Express train journey from Trivandrum to Kasaragod** with 14 stops.

- **WBS** = Breaking the journey into "boarding", "ticket check", "meals", "sleep", "disembark".
- **Gantt Chart** = A timetable printed along a horizontal 24-hour axis.
- **Network Diagram** = A route map showing which stop comes after which.
- **Critical Path** = The *sequence of stops that takes the longest* — delay at any one of these stations delays the train's arrival.
- **PERT** = Saying *"the train will take **8 hours ± 1 hour**"* because of traffic variability.
- **Line of Balance (LOB)** = A flow of identical trains (one after the other) on the same track — like identical floor cycles on a high-rise.

Just as the railway cannot reschedule the longest stretch, the formwork engineer cannot shorten the **critical path** without adding **resources** (more gangs, more cranes, more panels).

---

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Memorize These)

- **Project Duration ($T_p$):** Measured in **days** or **weeks**; KTU expects *days* unless stated.
- **Activity Duration ($D_{ij}$):** Time between **Event *i*** (start node) and **Event *j*** (end node).
- **Float / Slack:** Slack time an activity has before it becomes critical.
  - **Total Float (TF)** = $LST - EST$ = $LFT - EFT$
  - **Free Float (FF)** = $EFT_{j} - EST_{i} - D_{ij}$
- **Standard Normal Variate (Z):**
$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e}$$
where $T_s$ = scheduled time, $T_e$ = expected duration, $\sigma_e$ = standard deviation of critical path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants for PERT:** The PERT beta-distribution assumes a range of **±3σ** between optimistic and pessimistic estimates. Hence the famous factor of **6** in the expected-time formula. The unit of variance is **days²**; never **days**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain — From Project to Critical Path

A formwork project moves through four analytical layers:

1. **Scope Layer — WBS:** Break the building (e.g., a 12-storey tower) into *basement, typical floor (×10), terrace, services*. Break each floor into *columns, walls, slabs, beams*.
2. **Activity Layer:** Convert WBS into *activities* with measurable durations (e.g., "Strip props at 14 days" = 2 days).
3. **Logic Layer — Network:** Define **predecessors** (you cannot *pour slab* before *erect slab formwork*).
4. **Computation Layer — CPM/PERT:** Compute **EST, EFT, LST, LFT, Float**, identify the **critical path**.

> [!TIP]
> The **critical path is the longest-duration path through the network**. It dictates the **minimum project duration** and contains **only zero-float activities**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the only reference you need at the exam hall for this topic. Memorize it.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Expected Time (PERT)** | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | $t_o$ = optimistic, $t_m$ = most likely, $t_p$ = pessimistic; in **days** |
| 2 | **Standard Deviation (PERT)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | In **days** |
| 3 | **Variance (PERT)** | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | In **days²** |
| 4 | **Project Variance (sum of path variances)** | $\sigma_e^2 = \sum \sigma_i^2$ | Sum of variances of activities **on the critical path only** |
| 5 | **Probability of Completion** | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e}$ | Use standard normal table |
| 6 | **Early Start (ES)** | $ES_j = \max(ES_i + D_{ij})$ | $i$ = predecessor |
| 7 | **Early Finish (EF)** | $EF_j = ES_j + D_{ij}$ | |
| 8 | **Late Finish (LF)** | $LF_i = \min(LF_j - D_{ij})$ | Backward pass |
| 9 | **Late Start (LS)** | $LS_i = LF_i - D_{i}$ | |
| 10 | **Total Float (TF)** | $TF = LS - ES$ or $LF - EF$ | TF = 0 → **Critical** |
| 11 | **Free Float (FF)** | $FF = ES_{j} - EF_{i}$ | (j = successor) |
| 12 | **Independent Float (IF)** | $IF = ES_{j} - LF_{i} - D_{ij}$ | Rarely asked |
| 13 | **Minimum Project Duration** | $T_p = \max(\text{path lengths})$ | Length of **critical path** |
| 14 | **Line of Balance Output Rate** | $R = \dfrac{N}{D}$ | $N$ = number of units (floors), $D$ = total duration in days |
| 15 | **Crew / Gang Balance** | $n = \dfrac{\text{Quantity of work}}{\text{Output rate} \times \text{Duration}}$ | Used in formwork cycle design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-syntax rule applied:** All absolute-value / vertical bar symbols inside table cells have been replaced with $\vert$ or written out, so the markdown table is not corrupted.

---

### 2.3 The Forward Pass and Backward Pass — Operational Logic

**Forward Pass (Left → Right):** Determines **earliest times**.

- At the **start node**: $ES = 0$.
- For each activity $(i \to j)$:
  $ES_j = \max_{\text{all predecessors } i} (ES_i + D_{ij})$
  $EF_j = ES_j + D_{ij}$

**Backward Pass (Right → Left):** Determines **latest allowable times**.

- At the **end node**: $LF = EF$ of the final activity.
- For each activity $(i \to j)$:
  $LF_i = \min_{\text{all successors } j} (LF_j - D_{ij})$
  $LS_i = LF_i - D_{ij}$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** In formwork, the forward pass tells the engineer *"we can pour slab 5 on Day 22"*, while the backward pass tells the trades *"you must finish column props by Day 18, or you delay the pour"*. The difference is the *float* — a managerial cushion.

---

### 2.4 Why PERT, Not Just CPM, in Formwork?

Formwork activities are subject to **weather, labour absenteeism, breakdown of cranes, and supply delays** in plywood / props. Therefore their durations are **probabilistic, not deterministic**. **PERT** captures this uncertainty via the **beta distribution assumption**, which is the standard KTU expects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answers, **always state the three PERT assumptions explicitly** to score the 1-mark "assumption" sub-part:
> 1. Activity durations follow a **Beta distribution**.
> 2. The **critical path remains unchanged** throughout the project (this is the weakest assumption).
> 3. Activity durations are **statistically independent**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The PERT Expected Time Formula — Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU occasionally asks: *"Derive the PERT expected time formula"* (3–4 marks). Here is the textbook derivation using the Beta distribution.

**Step 1 — Beta Distribution PDF:**

The Beta distribution with parameters $(a, b)$ over the interval $[t_o, t_p]$ has the form:

$$
f(t) = \frac{1}{B(a,b)} \cdot \frac{(t - t_o)^{a-1}(t_p - t)^{b-1}}{(t_p - t_o)^{a+b-1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume Symmetric Beta (a = b = 4):**

The most-likely time $t_m$ is the **mode** of the distribution, which for a symmetric Beta is the midpoint of the range *if* $a = b$. For the standard PERT simplification, we take $a = b = 4$ because activity duration histograms in construction typically show a slight peak near $t_m$.

Setting the derivative of the PDF to zero and solving for the mode:

$$
\text{Mode} = t_m = \frac{a-1}{a+b-2} \cdot (t_p - t_o) + t_o
$$

Substituting $a = b = 4$:

$$
t_m = \frac{3}{6}(t_p - t_o) + t_o = \frac{t_o + t_p}{2}
$$

But this gives a *symmetric* mode, which is **not** what PERT uses. PERT instead uses the **mean** of the Beta distribution as the expected time.

**Step 3 — Mean of the Beta Distribution:**

$$
E[t] = \mu = \frac{a}{a+b} \cdot (t_p - t_o) + t_o
$$

Substitute $a = b = 4$:

$$
\mu = \frac{4}{8}(t_p - t_o) + t_o = \frac{1}{2}(t_p - t_o) + t_o
$$

So the symmetric-Beta mean would be the *midpoint*, not what PERT uses. The **real PERT assumption** is *asymmetric* Beta where $a + b = 8$ and $a \ne b$, with $t_m$ as the mode. Solving the mode equation for $a$:

$$
t_m - t_o = \frac{a-1}{a+b-2} (t_p - t_o)
$$

With $a + b = 8$, then $a + b - 2 = 6$:

$$
t_m - t_o = \frac{a-1}{6} (t_p - t_o)
$$

$$
a = 1 + \frac{6(t_m - t_o)}{t_p - t_o}
$$

Plug into the mean:

$$
t_e = \mu = \frac{a}{8}(t_p - t_o) + t_o
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

Substitute $a$ from above:

$$
t_e = \frac{1}{8}\left[1 + \frac{6(t_m - t_o)}{t_p - t_o}\right](t_p - t_o) + t_o
$$

Distribute:

$$
t_e = \frac{1}{8}(t_p - t_o) + \frac{6(t_m - t_o)}{8} + t_o
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator of the second term by $1$:

$$
t_e = \frac{t_p - t_o}{8} + \frac{3(t_m - t_o)}{4} + t_o
$$

Bring to a common denominator of **8**:

$$
t_e = \frac{t_p - t_o + 6(t_m - t_o) + 8t_o}{8} = \frac{t_p - t_o + 6t_m - 6t_o + 8t_o}{8}
$$

Simplify the $t_o$ coefficients: $-1 - 6 + 8 = +1$:

$$
t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students write $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + t_m + t_p}{3}$ (arithmetic mean) and lose 2 marks. The **weighted PERT formula with weight 4 on $t_m$** is the one examiners want.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — CPM on a Formwork Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the exact type of 14-mark question KTU asks in Module 4.** A small G+2 building has 6 formwork-related activities. Compute ES, EF, LS, LF, Floats, and identify the critical path.

**Activity Table (Formwork Project):**

| Activity | Description | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| A | Mobilize formwork panels | — | 4 |
| B | Erect column formwork | A | 5 |
| C | Erect beam & slab formwork | A | 6 |
| D | Place reinforcement | B, C | 4 |
| E | Pour concrete | D | 2 |
| F | Cure & strike formwork | E | 7 |

**Step 1 — Forward Pass to get ES & EF:**

- **A:** $ES = 0$, $EF = 0 + 4 = 4$
- **B:** $ES = EF_A = 4$, $EF = 4 + 5 = 9$
- **C:** $ES = EF_A = 4$, $EF = 4 + 6 = 10$
- **D:** $ES = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(9, 10) = 10$, $EF = 10 + 4 = 14$
- **E:** $ES = EF_D = 14$, $EF = 14 + 2 = 16$
- **F:** $ES = EF_E = 16$, $EF = 16 + 7 = 23$

> **Project duration $T_p$ = 23 days**

**Step 2 — Backward Pass to get LS & LF (start from F):**

- **F:** $LF = 23$ (project end), $LS = 23 - 7 = 16$
- **E:** $LF = LS_F = 16$, $LS = 16 - 2 = 14$
- **D:** $LF = LS_E = 14$, $LS = 14 - 4 = 10$
- **C:** $LF = LS_D = 10$, $LS = 10 - 6 = 4$
- **B:** $LF = LS_D = 10$, $LS = 10 - 5 = 5$
- **A:** $LF = \min(LS_B, LS_C) = \min(5, 4) = 4$, $LS = 4 - 4 = 0$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Float:**

| Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? |
|----------|----|----|----|----|------|-----------|
| A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | ✅ |
| B | 4 | 9 | 5 | 10 | 1 | ❌ |
| C | 4 | 10 | 4 | 10 | 0 | ✅ |
| D | 10 | 14 | 10 | 14 | 0 | ✅ |
| E | 14 | 16 | 14 | 16 | 0 | ✅ |
| F | 16 | 23 | 16 | 23 | 0 | ✅ |

> **Critical Path: A → C → D → E → F**
> **Project Duration: 23 days**

> [!TIP]
> **Formwork Insight:** Activity B (column formwork) has 1 day float. The engineer can therefore *delay* the column crew by 1 day without delaying slab pour, which is useful when the **crane is busy with the beam formwork on Day 5**. This is exactly the kind of *practical trade-off* KTU loves to test.

---

### 3.3 PERT Probability Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **This sub-question is the "probability" variant worth 7 marks.** Same network, but durations are now *three estimates*.

**Given three-time estimates (in days):**

| Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ |
|----------|--------|--------|--------|
| A | 2 | 4 | 8 |
| B | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| C | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| D | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| E | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| F | 5 | 7 | 11 |

**Step 1 — Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma$ for each:**

Activity A: $t_e = (2 + 4(4) + 8)/6 = (2 + 16 + 8)/6 = 26/6 = 4.33$ days
$\sigma_A = (8 - 2)/6 = 1.00$ day, $\sigma_A^2 = 1.00$

Activity B: $t_e = (3 + 4(5) + 7)/6 = 30/6 = 5.00$ days
$\sigma_B = (7 - 3)/6 = 0.67$ day, $\sigma_B^2 = 0.44$

Activity C: $t_e = (4 + 4(6) + 8)/6 = 36/6 = 6.00$ days
$\sigma_C = (8 - 4)/6 = 0.67$ day, $\sigma_C^2 = 0.44$

Activity D: $t_e = (2 + 4(4) + 6)/6 = 24/6 = 4.00$ days
$\sigma_D = (6 - 2)/6 = 0.67$ day, $\sigma_D^2 = 0.44$

Activity E: $t_e = (1 + 4(2) + 3)/6 = 12/6 = 2.00$ days
$\sigma_E = (3 - 1)/6 = 0.33$ day, $\sigma_E^2 = 0.11$

Activity F: $t_e = (5 + 4(7) + 11)/6 = 44/6 = 7.33$ days
$\sigma_F = (11 - 5)/6 = 1.00$ day, $\sigma_F^2 = 1.00$

**Step 2 — Find critical path using $t_e$:**

- Path 1: A–B–D–E–F = $4.33 + 5.00 + 4.00 + 2.00 + 7.33 = 22.66$ days
- Path 2: A–C–D–E–F = $4.33 + 6.00 + 4.00 + 2.00 + 7.33 = 23.66$ days ← **Longest**

> **Critical Path: A–C–D–E–F**, $T_e = 23.66$ days

**Step 3 — Compute project standard deviation $\sigma_e$:**

$$
\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_E^2 + \sigma_F^2
$$

$$
\sigma_e^2 = 1.00 + 0.44 + 0.44 + 0.11 + 1.00 = 2.99 \text{ days}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{2.99} = 1.73 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of completion in 25 days:**

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e} = \frac{25 - 23.66}{1.73} = \frac{1.34}{1.73} = 0.77
$$

From standard normal tables: $P(Z \le 0.77) = 0.7794$ or **77.94%**

> **Conclusion:** There is approximately a **78% probability** that the formwork project will be completed within 25 days.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) — Formwork of a G+10 Residential Tower

> The following Mermaid block renders a hierarchical block diagram of how a formwork project is decomposed for execution.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["FORMWORK PROJECT G PLUS 10 TOWER"]
    n1["BASEMENT AND PLINTH FORMWORK"]
    n2["TYPICAL FLOOR FORMWORK X 10"]
    n3["TERRACE AND MUMTY FORMWORK"]
    n4["STAIRCASE AND LIFT CORE"]
    n5["SUPPORT ACTIVITIES"]

    n11["RAFT SLAB SHUTTERING"]
    n12["BASEMENT WALLS SHUTTERING"]
    n13["PLINTH BEAM SHUTTERING"]

    n21["COLUMN FORMWORK"]
    n22["WALL FORMWORK"]
    n23["BEAM AND SLAB FORMWORK"]
    n24["REPROPPING AND STRIPPING"]

    n31["TERRACE SLAB FORMWORK"]
    n32["PARAPET WALL FORMWORK"]

    n41["STAIRCASE FORMWORK"]
    n42["LIFT CORE WALLS FORMWORK"]

    n51["FORMWORK MATERIAL LOGISTICS"]
    n52["SAFETY AND SCAFFOLDING"]
    n53["QUALITY CHECKPOINTS"]

    rootA --> n1
    rootA --> n2
    rootA --> n3
    rootA --> n4
    rootA --> n5

    n1 --> n11
    n1 --> n12
    n1 --> n13

    n2 --> n21
    n2 --> n22
    n2 --> n23
    n2 --> n24

    n3 --> n31
    n3 --> n32

    n4 --> n41
    n4 --> n42

    n5 --> n51
    n5 --> n52
    n5 --> n53
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** Each leaf node (e.g., `n21` = Column Formwork) becomes an *activity* with a duration in the network diagram. The WBS is *scope*; the network is *time*.

---

### 4.2 Network Diagram (Activity-on-Node, AON) for the Formwork Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeStart(["START"])
    actA["A: Mobilize panels Dur 4"]
    actB["B: Column formwork Dur 5"]
    actC["C: Beam Slab formwork Dur 6"]
    actD["D: Place rebar Dur 4"]
    actE["E: Pour concrete Dur 2"]
    actF["F: Cure and strike Dur 7"]
    nodeEnd(["END"])

    nodeStart --> actA
    actA --> actB
    actA --> actC
    actB --> actD
    actC --> actD
    actD --> actE
    actE --> actF
    actF --> nodeEnd
```

> [!TIP]
> **AON vs AOA:** KTU 2024 syllabus accepts both, but **AON is preferred** because each node clearly shows the activity and its duration. **AOA (Activity-on-Arrow)** uses arrows for activities and nodes for events, but needs *dummy activities* for parallel paths, which often confuses students in the exam hall.

---

### 4.3 Critical Path Method (CPM) Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["STEP 1: Define activities and predecessors"]
    stepB["STEP 2: Estimate duration D for each activity"]
    stepC["STEP 3: Draw network diagram AON or AOA"]
    stepD["STEP 4: FORWARD PASS compute ES and EF"]
    stepE["STEP 5: Project duration Tp equals max EF"]
    stepF["STEP 6: BACKWARD PASS compute LS and LF"]
    stepG["STEP 7: Compute Total Float TF equals LS minus ES"]
    stepH["STEP 8: Identify critical path where TF equals 0"]
    stepI["STEP 9: Apply crashing or resource levelling if needed"]
    stepJ["STEP 10: Update schedule and re-run if changed"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ -.->|"if scope changes"| stepA
```

---

### 4.4 Line of Balance (LOB) — Multi-Floor Repetitive Formwork

> The **Line of Balance (LOB)** technique is specifically used for **repetitive formwork cycles** (typical floors in a high-rise). Each floor = one unit, plotted on the Y-axis; time on the X-axis.

```mermaid
graph TD
    lobStart["DEFINE REPETITIVE UNITS"]
    lobStep1["List floor-wise formwork activities"]
    lobStep2["Determine output rate R equals N divided by D"]
    lobStep3["Draw diagonal lines slope equals 1 over R"]
    lobStep4["Mark buffer zone between successive floors"]
    lobStep5["Detect interference or congestion"]
    lobStep6["Optimize crew size to avoid buffer violation"]

    lobStart --> lobStep1
    lobStep1 --> lobStep2
    lobStep2 --> lobStep3
    lobStep3 --> lobStep4
    lobStep4 --> lobStep5
    lobStep5 --> lobStep6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** LOB is *especially important* for high-rise formwork because the cycle (strike → hoist → clean → oil → re-erect) repeats **floor after floor**. The line of balance helps avoid **resource conflicts** between the floor being struck and the floor being erected.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the Critical Path Method (CPM). State any two of its limitations when applied to a repetitive formwork project.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CPM** is a deterministic, network-based project management technique that uses a single time estimate for each activity to determine the **longest path** through the project network. The longest path is the **critical path** and dictates the **minimum project duration**. Activities on the critical path have **zero total float**.

> **Limitations (any two):**
> 1. CPM assumes activity durations are **fixed and known**; this is rarely true in formwork due to weather, labour variability, and crane availability.
> 2. CPM is **not ideal for repetitive formwork cycles** — it does not capture *resource continuity* or *gang balancing* between successive floors.
> 3. CPM does not provide a **probabilistic estimate** of completion; hence PERT is preferred for uncertain activity durations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*In a PERT network, the three-time estimates for a column formwork activity are: optimistic = 6 days, most likely = 9 days, pessimistic = 18 days. Calculate the expected time and standard deviation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Expected time:**

$$
t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} = \frac{6 + 4(9) + 18}{6} = \frac{6 + 36 + 18}{6} = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} = \frac{18 - 6}{6} = 2 \text{ days}
$$

> **Answer:** $t_e = 10$ days; $\sigma = 2$ days

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU ESE Model – Choice 1]**

> **The construction of a 4-storey office building involves the following formwork-related activities. Construct the network, calculate ES, EF, LS, LF, and total float for each activity, identify the critical path, and determine the minimum project duration.**

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (days) |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| A — Mobilize formwork | — | 5 |
| B — Erect column formwork | A | 6 |
| C — Erect wall formwork | A | 7 |
| D — Erect beam-slab formwork | B, C | 8 |
| E — Place reinforcement | D | 5 |
| F — Pour concrete | E | 2 |
| G — Cure and strike | F | 9 |

---

**Model Solution (Sub-part a — 7 marks): Network and Forward Pass**

**Step 1 — Network (AON format):**

A → B → D → E → F → G
A → C ↗

**Step 2 — Forward Pass (compute ES, EF):**

- A: $ES = 0$, $EF = 0 + 5 = 5$
- B: $ES = 5$, $EF = 5 + 6 = 11$
- C: $ES = 5$, $EF = 5 + 7 = 12$
- D: $ES = \max(11, 12) = 12$, $EF = 12 + 8 = 20$
- E: $ES = 20$, $EF = 20 + 5 = 25$
- F: $ES = 25$, $EF = 25 + 2 = 27$
- G: $ES = 27$, $EF = 27 + 9 = 36$

> **Project duration $T_p$ = 36 days**

---

**Model Solution (Sub-part b — 7 marks): Backward Pass, Float, Critical Path**

**Step 1 — Backward Pass (compute LS, LF) from G:**

- G: $LF = 36$, $LS = 36 - 9 = 27$
- F: $LF = 27$, $LS = 27 - 2 = 25$
- E: $LF = 25$, $LS = 25 - 5 = 20$
- D: $LF = 20$, $LS = 20 - 8 = 12$
- C: $LF = 12$, $LS = 12 - 7 = 5$
- B: $LF = 12$, $LS = 12 - 6 = 6$
- A: $LF = \min(6, 5) = 5$, $LS = 5 - 5 = 0$

**Step 2 — Total Float Table:**

| Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? |
|----------|----|----|----|----|----|-----------|
| A | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | ✅ |
| B | 5 | 11 | 6 | 12 | 1 | ❌ |
| C | 5 | 12 | 5 | 12 | 0 | ✅ |
| D | 12 | 20 | 12 | 20 | 0 | ✅ |
| E | 20 | 25 | 20 | 25 | 0 | ✅ |
| F | 25 | 27 | 25 | 27 | 0 | ✅ |
| G | 27 | 36 | 27 | 36 | 0 | ✅ |

> **Critical Path: A → C → D → E → F → G**
> **Minimum Project Duration: 36 days**
> **Activities with Float: B has 1-day float.**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU ESE Model – Choice 2]**

> **The following table gives the three-time estimates (in days) for a formwork project with 7 activities. Using PERT:**
> **(a) Calculate the expected time and standard deviation for each activity, identify the critical path, and find the expected project duration. (7 marks)**
> **(b) Find the probability of completing the project in (i) 30 days and (ii) 26 days. (7 marks)**

| Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | Predecessor |
|----------|--------|--------|--------|---------------|
| A | 3 | 4 | 5 | — |
| B | 2 | 3 | 4 | A |
| C | 4 | 5 | 6 | A |
| D | 3 | 4 | 9 | B |
| E | 2 | 3 | 4 | C |
| F | 1 | 2 | 3 | D, E |
| G | 4 | 5 | 12 | F |

---

**Model Solution (Sub-part a — 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma$ for each activity:**

| Activity | $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$ | $t_e$ (days) | $\sigma = (t_p - t_o)/6$ | $\sigma^2$ |
|----------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------------------|------------|
| A | $(3 + 16 + 5)/6$ | 4.00 | 0.33 | 0.11 |
| B | $(2 + 12 + 4)/6$ | 3.00 | 0.33 | 0.11 |
| C | $(4 + 20 + 6)/6$ | 5.00 | 0.33 | 0.11 |
| D | $(3 + 16 + 9)/6$ | 4.67 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| E | $(2 + 12 + 4)/6$ | 3.00 | 0.33 | 0.11 |
| F | $(1 + 8 + 3)/6$ | 2.00 | 0.33 | 0.11 |
| G | $(4 + 20 + 12)/6$ | 6.00 | 1.33 | 1.78 |

**Step 2 — Identify paths and durations:**

- Path 1: A–B–D–F–G = $4 + 3 + 4.67 + 2 + 6 = 19.67$ days
- Path 2: A–C–E–F–G = $4 + 5 + 3 + 2 + 6 = 20.00$ days ← **Longest**

> **Critical Path: A–C–E–F–G**, $T_e = 20.00$ days

**Step 3 — Project variance and standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_E^2 + \sigma_F^2 + \sigma_G^2
$$

$$
\sigma_e^2 = 0.11 + 0.11 + 0.11 + 0.11 + 1.78 = 2.22 \text{ days}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{2.22} = 1.49 \text{ days}
$$

---

**Model Solution (Sub-part b — 7 marks): Probability Calculations**

**(i) Probability of completion in 30 days:**

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e} = \frac{30 - 20}{1.49} = \frac{10}{1.49} = 6.71
$$

From standard normal table, $P(Z \le 6.71) \approx 1.0000$ (essentially 100%)

> **Interpretation:** A 30-day target is *very generous* relative to the expected 20 days. There is virtually a 100% chance of meeting it. This is a *trivially achievable* schedule.

**(ii) Probability of completion in 26 days:**

$$
Z = \frac{26 - 20}{1.49} = \frac{6}{1.49} = 4.03
$$

From standard normal table, $P(Z \le 4.03) \approx 0.99997$ (still essentially 100%)

> **Interpretation:** Even a 26-day target gives >99.99% probability. The project has very high confidence of meeting any reasonable deadline because the *expected* time is only 20 days, and $\sigma$ is small.

> **Formwork Engineering Insight:** When probability is *too* high (close to 1.0), the project is **under-scheduled** — resources may be underutilized. The engineer should consider **resource levelling** to deploy the idle crew elsewhere.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–4 marks in PECET742 Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Using arithmetic mean $\frac{t_o + t_m + t_p}{3}$ instead of PERT's $\frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$.** Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Summing standard deviations** instead of variances. The standard deviation of the critical path is the **square root of the sum of variances**, not the sum of standard deviations.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract 1 from path counts** in dummy-activity AOA diagrams, leading to wrong node numbering.
> 4. **Computing Free Float where Total Float is asked** (or vice versa). Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Ignoring the formwork context** — KTU deducts marks if the answer describes a *generic* project instead of a *formwork cycle* with activities like *striking, oiling, hoisting, re-erecting*.
> 6. **Using $T_s < T_e$** without checking — this gives a **negative Z** value, and students often forget to look up $P(Z \le -0.5)$ instead of $P(Z \le 0.5)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the exam hall — 90 seconds is enough.**

- [ ] **WBS** decomposes *project → deliverables → work packages → activities*; it answers *what* must be done.
- [ ] **Gantt Chart** plots activities on a calendar axis; it shows *when* activities happen but does **not** show logic between them.
- [ ] **Milestone Chart** shows *key events* (e.g., *first floor slab cast*, *formwork stripped at 14 days*).
- [ ] **CPM** is *deterministic* — uses one duration per activity — and finds the **longest path** = **critical path** = **minimum project duration**.
- [ ] **PERT** is *probabilistic* — uses **three estimates** and a **Beta distribution** assumption.
- [ ] **Master PERT formula:** $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ — weight **4 is fixed on $t_m$**, not 3.
- [ ] **Master variance formula:** $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$, units are **days²**.
- [ ] **Forward pass:** $ES = \max(\text{pred. EF})$, $EF = ES + D$.
- [ ] **Backward pass:** $LF = \min(\text{succ. LS})$, $LS = LF - D$.
- [ ] **Total Float:** $TF = LS - ES = LF - EF$. **TF = 0 ⟹ Critical Activity**.
- [ ] **Free Float:** $FF = ES_{\text{successor}} - EF_{\text{activity}}$; does not affect project end-date.
- [ ] **Probability of completion:** $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e}$; read from **standard normal table**.
- [ ] **LOB (Line of Balance)** is used for **repetitive formwork** in high-rise buildings; it shows *gang continuity*.
- [ ] **Critical path may have multiple paths** of equal longest length — all are critical.
- [ ] **Resource levelling** is performed *after* CPM; it does not change the project duration.
- [ ] **Crashing** (adding resources) is used to *shorten* the critical path at additional cost.
- [ ] **Formwork-specific terms** to use in answers: *cycle time, striking, re-propping, flying form, table form, panel gang, vertical transportation* — these show the examiner you understand the *domain*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formwork in Special Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Formwork in Special Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Formwork in Special Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Formwork in Special Conditions** refers to the specialized temporary mold systems, supporting structures, and ancillary arrangements engineered to retain freshly placed concrete in its desired shape until it achieves sufficient strength, *under challenging environmental, geometric, or operational conditions* that deviate from standard casting environments.

In standard construction, formwork is used for ordinary beams, slabs, and columns cast at ambient temperature on accessible ground. However, **special conditions** introduce constraints that demand fundamentally different design philosophies, materials, and construction techniques.

### 1.2 Classification of Special Conditions

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes special conditions into the following major families:

| S.No. | Category | Example Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Environmental Conditions** | Underwater, Hot weather (> 35 °C), Cold weather (< 5 °C), High humidity |
| 2 | **Geometric Conditions** | Shells, Folded plates, Domes, Arches, Curved walls, Spherical structures |
| 3 | **Operational Conditions** | Slipform, Jumpform, Climbing form, Tunnel formwork, Self-climbing systems |
| 4 | **Tall Structure Conditions** | High-rise cores, Chimneys, Silos, TV towers, Bridge piers |
| 5 | **Repair & Rehabilitation** | Patchwork formwork, Jacketing of columns, Underpinning |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy: "The Costume Designer of Concrete"**
> Imagine concrete is liquid chocolate being poured into a mould. In your kitchen, the mould rests calmly on a counter at room temperature. But what if you had to pour that chocolate *underwater* (think deep-sea divers), or in a *blizzard* (Antarctic expedition), or to make a *giant igloo shape* (curved shell)? You'd need a special costume — waterproof, insulated, or sculpted. **Formwork in special conditions is exactly that "special costume"** — it is engineered to defy the unusual environment while still holding the liquid concrete in the precise shape until it hardens.

### 1.4 Why Standard Formwork Fails Under Special Conditions

Standard timber/plywood formwork fails because:

* **Hydrostatic pressure** under deep water exceeds the bending capacity of normal plywood sheets.
* **Thermal gradients** cause uneven concrete setting, leading to cracking if forms are not insulated.
* **Curved geometry** cannot be achieved with flat plywood without segmental fabrication.
* **Continuous slipforming** demands a moving form that must resist concrete pressure while being jacked upward — a fundamentally different structural system.

### 1.5 Key Design Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics**
> * Hydrostatic Pressure: $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ where $\rho$ = **2400 kg/m³** for wet concrete, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
> * Maximum permissible fresh concrete lateral pressure: **150 kN/m²** (IS 14687)
> * Cold weather threshold: Ambient temperature **< 5 °C**
> * Hot weather threshold: Ambient temperature **> 35 °C** or concrete temp **> 32 °C** at placement
> * Typical slipform jacking rate: **150 mm to 300 mm per hour**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sub-Topic 1 — Underwater Formwork

### 2.1.1 Theory and Operational Principle

Underwater formwork is used for bridge piers, dam foundations, harbor structures, and offshore platforms. The formwork must be:

* **Watertight** to prevent washout of cement from the concrete.
* **Heavy and anchored** to resist buoyancy forces.
* **Constructed to permit concrete placement via tremie pipe or bottom-dump bucket.**

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1**: Assess hydrostatic head (water depth) at the construction site.
* **Step 2**: Calculate the design lateral pressure using $P = \rho_{w} \cdot g \cdot h_w$ where $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
* **Step 3**: Add concrete lateral pressure: $P_c = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot h_c$.
* **Step 4**: Total design pressure $P_{total} = P_c + P_w$.
* **Step 5**: Design formwork as a water-retaining structure, increasing the safety factor to **1.5** instead of the standard **1.33**.
* **Step 6**: Use **sheet piling + tremie seal** at the bottom and **steel forms with rubber gaskets** for joints.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Application**
> The Bandra-Worli Sea Link (Mumbai) and the Kochi Metro underwater piers used underwater formwork sealed with prefabricated steel panels and tremie-placed concrete to ensure monolithic pier construction up to **15 m** below Mean Sea Level.

---

## 2.2 Sub-Topic 2 — Cold Weather Formwork

### 2.2.1 Theory

When ambient temperature drops below **5 °C**, the hydration of cement slows dramatically. If concrete freezes before reaching a strength of **3.5 MPa**, the ice crystals disrupt the matrix, causing **irreversible damage** (typically a **40–50% strength loss**).

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1**: Preheat mixing water (max **60 °C**) and aggregates to maintain concrete placement temperature above **5 °C**.
* **Step 2**: Use **insulated formwork** with rigid polystyrene or mineral wool boards (thermal conductivity $k \le 0.04 \text{ W/m·K}$).
* **Step 3**: Extend the **form striking time** — minimum **7 days** or until concrete reaches **50% of characteristic strength**.
* **Step 4**: Apply **steam curing blankets** or **heated enclosures** around the form.
* **Step 5**: Avoid using frozen ground as formwork base.

### 2.2.3 Critical Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle**
> "Concrete must not freeze before it sets." The formwork in cold weather acts as a **thermal envelope**, not merely a mould. The thermal resistance $R = \frac{t}{k \cdot A}$ (where $t$ = insulation thickness, $A$ = surface area) must be designed to retain hydration heat.

---

## 2.3 Sub-Topic 3 — Hot Weather Formwork

### 2.3.1 Theory

Above **35 °C**, concrete loses workability rapidly due to accelerated hydration and evaporation. Problems include **plastic shrinkage cracking**, **cold joints**, and **strength loss**.

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1**: Use **reflective aluminum formwork** or **white-painted plywood** to reduce solar heat gain.
* **Step 2**: Pre-cool aggregates and use chilled mixing water.
* **Step 3**: Add **retarders** (e.g., lignosulfonates) to delay setting.
* **Step 4**: Schedule pours for **early morning or late evening**.
* **Step 5**: Begin **wet curing** within **30 minutes** of finishing.
* **Step 6**: Use **form-release agents** with high water retention properties.

### 2.3.3 Numerical Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (ACI 305)**
> For every **1 °C** rise in concrete temperature above **20 °C**, the water requirement increases by approximately **1.5%**, leading to potential strength loss if not compensated with additional cement.

---

## 2.4 Sub-Topic 4 — Slipform Construction

### 2.4.1 Theory

**Slipforming** is a continuous casting method in which the formwork is **jacked upward (or sideways) at a controlled rate** as concrete is placed, eliminating horizontal construction joints.

### 2.4.2 Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1**: Erect the slipform assembly (yokes, jacks, working platform, forms).
* **Step 2**: Place **layer 1** of concrete (typically **250 mm** high).
* **Step 3**: After initial set, commence **jacking** at rate **v = 150–300 mm/hour**.
* **Step 4**: Continuously place concrete in **150–200 mm** layers.
* **Step 5**: The form slides upward while leaving the hardened concrete shell behind.
* **Step 6**: On completion, dismantle the form by sliding it down or by controlled de-jacking.

### 2.4.3 Critical Design Considerations

* **Jack spacing**: typically **1.2 m to 1.8 m** on centers.
* **Yoke design**: must resist **horizontal pressure from wet concrete** AND **vertical jacking load**.
* **Concrete consistency**: must be **workable but cohesive** — typical slump **50–80 mm**.
* **Slip rate** $v$ depends on setting time $t_s$: $v = \frac{h_s}{t_s}$ where $h_s$ = form height (usually **1.0 to 1.2 m**).

---

## 2.5 Sub-Topic 5 — Climbing Formwork (Jumpform)

### 2.5.1 Theory

Climbing formwork is anchored to the previously cast concrete lift via **anchors embedded in the structure**, and "jumps" upward to the next level. Used for **high-rise cores, shear walls, and chimneys**.

### 2.5.2 Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1**: Cast Lift 1 with the formwork supported on the foundation.
* **Step 2**: After curing, **embed anchors** in the hardened concrete.
* **Step 3**: **Detach** the form from the cured concrete.
* **Step 4**: **Crane-lift** (or self-climb) the form to Lift 2.
* **Step 5**: Re-anchor and repeat.

### 2.5.3 Distinction from Slipform

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slipform vs. Jumpform**
> * **Slipform**: Continuous motion, no horizontal joints, used for tall uniform sections (silos, chimneys).
> * **Jumpform**: Discrete lifts (one per cycle), horizontal joints allowed, used for high-rise cores.

---

## 2.6 Sub-Topic 6 — Tunnel Formwork

### 2.6.1 Theory

**Tunnel form** is a self-propelled, room-sized form that casts **walls and slabs monolithically in a single pour**, producing a cell-shaped structural unit. Used in **mass housing projects** where repetition is high.

### 2.6.2 Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1**: Position the tunnel form at the cell location.
* **Step 2**: Place reinforcement and embedments.
* **Step 3**: Close the form and pour concrete monolithically.
* **Step 4**: Cure for **12–24 hours**.
* **Step 5**: **Strip** the form by collapsing the side and roof panels.
* **Step 6**: **Roll-out** the form on rails to the next cell.
* **Step 7**: Repeat — achieving a **24-hour cycle** per cell.

### 2.6.3 Advantages

* Eliminates joints between walls and slabs.
* Productivity up to **400 m²/day** per crew.
* Excellent waterproofing due to monolithic construction.

---

## 2.7 Sub-Topic 7 — Formwork for Shells, Domes, and Folded Plates

### 2.7.1 Theory

Curved concrete surfaces (shells, domes) cannot be formed with flat plywood. Specialized **warped timber, segmented steel, or fabric-formed** systems are used.

### 2.7.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Domes

* **Step 1**: Establish a **center** (rotating or stationary).
* **Step 2**: Lay **ribs** (meridional members) at calculated angular spacing.
* **Step 3**: Connect ribs with **latitudinal rings** (hoops).
* **Step 4**: Cover with **segmental plywood panels** or **wire mesh + plaster**.
* **Step 5**: Apply **form-release agent** carefully to all curved surfaces.

### 2.7.3 Shell Thickness and Formwork Cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Note**
> Shell structures reduce concrete volume by **40–60%** compared to solid slabs but require formwork that is **2 to 3 times more expensive per m²** due to its custom geometry.

---

## 2.8 Sub-Topic 8 — Formwork for High-Rise Columns and Silos

### 2.8.1 Theory

Columns exceeding **30 m** height or with circular cross-section (silos, chimneys) require **self-supported steel column forms** that can be lifted by crane or by hydraulic climbing systems.

### 2.8.2 Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1**: Use **circular steel forms** (2-piece or 3-piece "panel" construction) for circular sections.
* **Step 2**: Provide **horizontal stiffener rings** at **300–500 mm** spacing.
* **Step 3**: Equip each panel with **lifting lugs** and **adjustable braces**.
* **Step 4**: After concrete achieves **70% strength**, strip and re-lift for the next lift.
* **Step 5**: For silos, integrate with **slipform** to achieve continuous casting.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — All Key Formulas for Formwork in Special Conditions**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure from wet concrete | kPa or kN/m² |
| 2 | $P_{uw} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_w$ | Hydrostatic pressure from water (underwater) | kN/m² |
| 3 | $P_{design} = 1.5 \cdot (P_c + P_w)$ | Design pressure for underwater (SF = 1.5) | kN/m² |
| 4 | $v_{slip} = \frac{h_s}{t_s}$ | Slipform jacking rate | mm/hour |
| 5 | $R = \frac{t}{k \cdot A}$ | Thermal resistance of insulation | K/W |
| 6 | $t_{strip} = k_c \cdot T_{cure}$ | Stripping time correction (cold weather) | days |
| 7 | $F_{buoy} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_f$ | Buoyancy force on underwater form | kN |
| 8 | $M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$ | Max bending moment (simply supported) | kN·m |
| 9 | $Q_{max} = \frac{1.5 \cdot W \cdot h_c \cdot t}{S + 0.17 h_c}$ | Concrete lateral pressure (CIRIA) | kN/m² |
| 10 | $P_{silo} = \frac{4 \cdot V \cdot \rho_c \cdot g}{D}$ | Bursting pressure in circular forms | kN/m² |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the above table, the absolute value bars `|...|` and the literal "OR" have been removed to comply with markdown table syntax. All other formulas use standard LaTeX notation outside the table.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Special Condition | Practical Application | Engineering Sector |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Underwater | Bridge piers, dam foundations, jetties | Marine, Hydro |
| Cold weather | Himalayan roads, Arctic pipelines | Mountain, Oil & Gas |
| Hot weather | Middle East high-rises, GCC infrastructure | Real Estate |
| Slipform | Chimneys, silos, TV towers | Industrial |
| Jumpform | High-rise cores, shear walls | Real Estate |
| Tunnel form | Mass housing (LIG/MIG) | Affordable Housing |
| Shell/Dome | Stadiums, planetariums, warehouses | Sports, Civic |
| Climbing | Bridge pylons, tall piers | Infrastructure |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lateral Pressure on Slipform Walls

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A circular silo of internal diameter **$D = 6 \text{ m}$** is being slipformed with concrete of density **$\rho_c = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. The slipform height is **$h_s = 1.2 \text{ m}$** and the initial setting time is **$t_s = 4 \text{ hours}$**. Calculate:
1. The hydrostatic pressure on the form base.
2. The required slipform jacking rate.
3. The hoop tension in the formwork panel.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 6 \text{ m} \\
\rho_c &= 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
h_s &= 1.2 \text{ m} \\
t_s &= 4 \text{ hours} \\
g &= 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute the hydrostatic pressure at the form base.**

This step computes the maximum lateral pressure exerted by the wet concrete column of height $h_s$.

$$
P = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot h_s
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2400 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \\
P &= 28{,}252.8 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
P &= 28.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **maximum lateral pressure** acting on the formwork at its base. It is less than the **150 kN/m²** IS 14687 limit, hence acceptable.

**Step 2: Compute the slipform jacking rate.**

The slip rate must match the initial setting time so that the concrete leaves the form at the top just as it has set.

$$
v_{slip} = \frac{h_s}{t_s}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{slip} &= \frac{1.2 \text{ m}}{4 \text{ hours}} \\
v_{slip} &= 0.30 \text{ m/hour} \\
v_{slip} &= 300 \text{ mm/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

This rate is within the typical range of **150–300 mm/hour**, confirming design feasibility.

**Step 3: Compute the hoop tension in the formwork.**

For a thin cylindrical form under internal pressure, the hoop (circumferential) tensile force per unit length is:

$$
T = \frac{P \cdot D}{2}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{28.25 \times 6}{2} \\
T &= 84.75 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute hoop stress in 6 mm steel plate form.**

Assuming the formwork panel is made of steel plate of thickness **$t_p = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$**:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{T}{t_p}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{hoop} &= \frac{84.75}{0.006} \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= 14{,}125 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= 14.125 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Check against allowable stress.**

For mild steel formwork, allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 0.6 \cdot f_y$ where $f_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{allow} &= 0.6 \times 250 = 150 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_{hoop} (14.125 \text{ MPa}) &< \sigma_{allow} (150 \text{ MPa}) \quad \text{SAFE}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary**
> * Max lateral pressure = **28.25 kN/m²**
> * Jacking rate = **300 mm/hour**
> * Hoop tension = **84.75 kN/m**
> * Hoop stress = **14.125 MPa** (SAFE)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Buoyancy Force on Underwater Formwork

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

An underwater formwork box of dimensions **3 m × 3 m × 2 m** is to be installed at a depth of **8 m** in seawater ($\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The formwork weighs **15 kN** in air. Calculate:
1. The buoyancy force on the formwork.
2. The minimum dead-weight (ballast) required to keep the form in place.

### 3.2.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 3 \text{ m} \\
B &= 3 \text{ m} \\
H &= 2 \text{ m} \\
d &= 8 \text{ m (water depth)} \\
\rho_w &= 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
W_f &= 15 \text{ kN} \\
g &= 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute the displaced volume.**

The volume of the formwork submerged equals its outer volume (assuming full submersion):

$$
V_f = L \times B \times H
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= 3 \times 3 \times 2 \\
V_f &= 18 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the buoyancy force.**

By Archimedes' principle, the upward buoyancy force equals the weight of the displaced water:

$$
F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_f
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 18 \\
F_b &= 181{,}054.5 \text{ N} \\
F_b &= 181.05 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute net upward force.**

$$
F_{net} = F_b - W_f
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= 181.05 - 15 \\
F_{net} &= 166.05 \text{ kN (upward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the required ballast weight.**

To prevent uplift, ballast must exceed the net upward force with a safety factor of **1.25**:

$$
W_{ballast} = 1.25 \cdot F_{net}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ballast} &= 1.25 \times 166.05 \\
W_{ballast} &= 207.56 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Practical interpretation.**

This ballast can be provided by **concrete kentledge blocks** placed on top of the formwork, or by **anchor bolts** drilled into the seabed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary**
> * Buoyancy force = **181.05 kN**
> * Net uplift = **166.05 kN**
> * Minimum ballast = **207.56 kN** (with SF = 1.25)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cold Weather Formwork Insulation Thickness

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A wall formwork is to be insulated with polystyrene boards (thermal conductivity **$k = 0.035 \text{ W/m·K}$**) in a cold region. The hydration heat generated per m² of wall is **$Q = 200 \text{ W/m}^2$** and the ambient temperature is **$T_a = -5 \text{ °C}$**. The minimum allowable concrete temperature is **$T_c = 5 \text{ °C}$**. Calculate the required insulation thickness $t$.

### 3.3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 200 \text{ W/m}^2 \\
k &= 0.035 \text{ W/m·K} \\
T_a &= -5 \text{ °C} \\
T_c &= 5 \text{ °C} \\
\Delta T &= T_c - T_a = 10 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Apply Fourier's law of heat conduction.**

The heat lost through the insulation must equal the heat generated by hydration at steady state:

$$
Q = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{t}
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for thickness $t$.**

$$
t = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{Q}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{0.035 \times 10}{200} \\
t &= \frac{0.35}{200} \\
t &= 0.00175 \text{ m} \\
t &= 1.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor.**

For practical insulation, multiply by a safety factor of **3** to account for wind, joints, and humidity:

$$
t_{design} = 3 \times 1.75 = 5.25 \text{ mm}
$$

In practice, use the next available commercial thickness: **$t_{standard} = 25 \text{ mm}$** polystyrene.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary**
> * Theoretical thickness = **1.75 mm**
> * Design thickness (with SF 3) = **5.25 mm**
> * Recommended commercial thickness = **25 mm**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Formwork Type vs. Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for KTU Exam Problems**

| Condition | Best Formwork Type | Cycle Time | Key Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard column | Timber/plywood | 1–3 days | Cost |
| Tall silo | Slipform | Continuous | Jack alignment |
| High-rise core | Jumpform | 3–5 days/lift | Crane availability |
| Mass housing | Tunnel form | 24 h/cell | Repetition |
| Underwater pier | Steel + tremie | Single pour | Buoyancy |
| Cold weather | Insulated plywood | 5–7 days | Hydration |
| Hot weather | Reflective steel | 1–2 days | Evaporation |
| Dome / Shell | Segmental timber | Single pour | Geometry |
| Bridge pylon | Self-climbing | 2–4 days/lift | Wind load |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Jack Load in Slipform

### 3.5.1 Problem

A slipform of total weight **$W = 80 \text{ kN}$** is lifted by 12 hydraulic jacks. Each jack has a jacking force capacity of **15 kN**. Determine:
1. Load per jack.
2. Whether the system is adequate.

### 3.5.2 Solution

**Step 1: Load per jack.**

$$
F_{jack} = \frac{W}{N_{jacks}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{jack} &= \frac{80}{12} \\
F_{jack} &= 6.67 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with capacity.**

$$
F_{jack} (6.67 \text{ kN}) < F_{capacity} (15 \text{ kN}) \quad \text{SAFE}
$$

Utilization factor:

$$
\eta = \frac{6.67}{15} = 0.444 = 44.4\%
$$

This is below the typical recommended utilization of **70%**, hence very safe.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Formwork Selection

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision flow** for selecting the correct formwork type under special conditions.

    B0 -- High Humidity --> C5[FRP or Coated Plywood]
    A0 --> D0{Geometric Complexity}
    D0 -- Straight Members --> E1[Flat Panel Form]
    D0 -- Curved Shell --> E2[Segmental or Warped Form]
    D0 -- Dome --> E3[Centring with Ribs and Hoops]
    D0 -- Folded Plate --> E4[Angular Segmental Panels]

    F0 -- Discrete Lifts --> G2[Jumpform or Climbing Form]
    F0 -- Mass Housing --> G3[Tunnel Form on Rails]
    F0 -- Single Pour --> G4[Conventional Form]
    C1 --> H0[Final Selection]
    C2 --> H0
    C3 --> H0
    C4 --> H0
    C5 --> H0

    E2 --> H0
    E3 --> H0
    E4 --> H0

    G2 --> H0
    G3 --> H0

```

```
