# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Slipform Construction `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Slipform construction** is a continuous casting method in which a moving formwork, supported on hydraulic jacks anchored to vertical rods embedded in the concrete, is raised uniformly at a controlled rate (typically **150–300 mm/hour**) as concrete is placed in successive layers. The formwork is lifted at a rate synchronized with the initial setting time of the concrete, allowing the freshly placed concrete to become self-supporting as it emerges from the bottom of the form. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for jacking rate, 1 mark for the synchronized lifting concept).*

---

### Question 2: What is the role of tremie pipe in underwater formwork? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A **tremie pipe** is a vertical pipe (typically **200–300 mm diameter**) lowered through the formwork into the previously placed fresh concrete. Fresh concrete is fed through the tremie from the top, displacing water and preventing washout of cement. The bottom of the tremie remains submerged in fresh concrete throughout the pour, ensuring **monolithic, segregation-free placement** below water. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for submersion principle, 1 mark for monolithic placement).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Cold Weather Formwork

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the design considerations for formwork in cold weather concreting. What are the effects of low temperature on concrete strength development?

**(b) (7 Marks)** A wall formwork is to be insulated for cold weather concreting. The required heat retention is $Q = 180 \text{ W/m}^2$. The ambient temperature is $T_a = -10 \text{ °C}$ and the minimum permissible concrete temperature is $T_c = 5 \text{ °C}$. Calculate the required thickness of polystyrene insulation ($k = 0.04 \text{ W/m·K}$) and comment on the practical choice.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Design Considerations (7 Marks)**

* **Effect of low temperature on concrete strength development (3 marks):**
  Below **5 °C**, cement hydration slows drastically. Concrete that freezes before reaching **3.5 MPa** strength suffers **40–50% permanent strength loss**. Ice formation expands by **9%**, disrupting the matrix. Setting time doubles for every **10 °C** drop. Calcium hydroxide and C-S-H gel formation is severely retarded below **0 °C**.

* **Design considerations (4 marks):**
  * Preheat mixing water (max **60 °C**) and aggregates.
  * Use **insulated formwork** with polystyrene, mineral wool, or rigid foam ($k \le 0.04 \text{ W/m·K}$).
  * Maintain concrete placement temperature **above 5 °C**.
  * Extend **striking time** to a minimum of **7 days** or until **50% of $f_{ck}$** is achieved.
  * Use **steam curing** or **heated enclosures** for severe cold.
  * Avoid pouring concrete on **frozen subgrade**.
  * Use **Type III (high-early-strength) cement** or **accelerators** (calcium chloride) cautiously.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$
Q = 180 \text{ W/m}^2, \quad k = 0.04 \text{ W/m·K}, \quad T_a = -10 \text{ °C}, \quad T_c = 5 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 1: Compute temperature difference.**

$$
\Delta T = T_c - T_a = 5 - (-10) = 15 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Fourier's Law of heat conduction.**

$$
Q = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{Q}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{0.04 \times 15}{180} \\
t &= \frac{0.60}{180} \\
t &= 0.00333 \text{ m} \\
t &= 3.33 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor of 3** (to account for wind, joints, and moisture):

$$
t_{design} = 3 \times 3.33 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Practical recommendation.**

In practice, use the next available standard commercial thickness of **25 mm** polystyrene board, which provides an even higher safety margin and is easier to handle on site.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Theoretical thickness = 3.33 mm; design thickness = 10 mm; recommended commercial thickness = 25 mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Slipform Design

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Describe the components of a slipform system. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the operational sequence of slipform construction.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A circular silo of internal diameter **8 m** is slipformed with concrete of density **2400 kg/m³**. The formwork panel height is **1.2 m** and the initial setting time is **5 hours**. Calculate:
1. The maximum lateral pressure on the form.
2. The required slip rate.
3. The hoop tension per metre height in the formwork.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Components and Operation (7 Marks)**

* **Components (3 marks):**
  1. **Hydraulic jacks** (one per yoke, spaced 1.2–1.8 m apart).
  2. **Yoke assembly** (carries the working platform and forms).
  3. **Jacking rods** (25–32 mm steel rods embedded in the concrete).
  4. **Form panels** (inner and outer, 1.0–1.2 m high).
  5. **Working platform** (for placing concrete and reinforcement).
  6. **Hanging scaffolding** (for finishing the exposed concrete).
  7. **Concrete distribution system** (boom, chutes).

* **Operational sequence (4 marks):**
  1. Erect the slipform assembly.
  2. Place initial concrete layer of **250 mm** in the form.
  3. After initial set, commence jacking at the design slip rate.
  4. Continuously add concrete in **150–200 mm** layers.
  5. Continue 24/7 until the structure is complete.
  6. Strip the form by sliding down or controlled de-jacking.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$
D = 8 \text{ m}, \quad \rho_c = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad h_s = 1.2 \text{ m}, \quad t_s = 5 \text{ h}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 1: Maximum lateral pressure.**

$$
P = \rho_c \cdot g \cdot h_s
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2400 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \\
P &= 28{,}252.8 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
P &= 28.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Slip rate.**

$$
v_{slip} = \frac{h_s}{t_s} = \frac{1.2}{5} = 0.24 \text{ m/h} = 240 \text{ mm/h}
$$

**Step 3: Hoop tension per metre height.**

$$
T = \frac{P \cdot D}{2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{28.25 \times 8}{2} \\
T &= 113 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Hoop stress in 8 mm plate.**

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{T}{t_p} = \frac{113}{0.008} = 14{,}125 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 14.13 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Max pressure = 28.25 kN/m², slip rate = 240 mm/h, hoop tension = 113 kN/m, hoop stress = 14.13 MPa (safe vs 150 MPa allowable).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> * Students often **forget to convert mm to m** when computing hoop stress from $T/t_p$. Always check unit consistency.
> * In cold weather problems, students frequently **omit the safety factor** on insulation thickness. Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for this.
> * In slipform problems, students sometimes use the **wrong density** (e.g., 25 kN/m³ instead of 2400 kg/m³). Always state the unit clearly.
> * Don't confuse **slipform with jumpform** in descriptive answers — they are different systems with different cycle times.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Formwork in Special Conditions**

* **Underwater formwork** must use **SF = 1.5** (vs. 1.33 normal) and provide **tremie-sealed concrete placement**.
* **Buoyancy force** $F_b = \rho_w g V$ must be countered by **ballast** or **anchorage**.
* **Cold weather** threshold is **5 °C**; insulation must retain hydration heat.
* **Hot weather** threshold is **35 °C**; use reflective forms, retarders, and prompt curing.
* **Slipform rate** $v = h_s / t_s$, typically **150–300 mm/h**.
* **Slipform** is *continuous*; **Jumpform** is *discrete lifts* — do not confuse.
* **Tunnel form** achieves **24-hour cycle** for mass housing walls and slabs monolithically.
* **Shell/Dome formwork** uses **ribs + hoops + segmental panels** — labour-intensive but elegant.
* **Hoop tension** in circular formwork $T = P D / 2$.
* **Hoop stress** $\sigma = T / t_p$ must be less than $0.6 f_y$.
* **Jack load** $F_{jack} = W_{total} / N_{jacks}$ should not exceed **70%** of jack capacity.
* **Insulation thickness** $t = k \Delta T / Q$, then multiply by **SF 3** for design.
* **Concrete lateral pressure** $P = \rho_c g h$ is the basis for all formwork design.
* **CIRIA equation** $P_{max} = 1.5 W h_c t / (S + 0.17 h_c)$ is the industry standard for walls.
* **Underwater concrete** is placed by **tremie pipe** or **bottom-dump bucket**.
* **Climbing formwork** is **anchored to the previous lift** — no external support needed.
* **Cold weather stripping time** = 7 days minimum or **50% of $f_{ck}$**.
* **Hot weather plastic shrinkage** cracks form within **30 minutes** of finishing if not cured.
* **Shell formwork** cost is **2 to 3 times** that of flat slab formwork per m².
* **IS 14687** governs formwork design in India; **ACI 347** and **CIRIA Report 108** are international benchmarks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extreme Weather Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Extreme Weather Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Extreme Weather Conditions** in the context of **Formwork Engineering** refer to ambient climatic environments in which temperature, humidity, wind velocity, and precipitation deviate substantially from standard laboratory curing conditions (typically **27 °C ± 2 °C** and **90 % relative humidity** as per IS 516), thereby demanding specialized formwork design, material selection, concrete mix adjustments, and altered stripping-time protocols to ensure structural integrity, dimensional stability, and durability of the finished concrete element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Extreme weather for formwork engineering is broadly classified into three operational regimes:
> 1. **Hot Weather Concreting** — ambient temperature **> 32 °C** (or concrete temperature **> 30 °C** at placement)
> 2. **Cold Weather Concreting** — ambient temperature **< 5 °C** (or concrete temperature **< 10 °C** during the first 24 hours)
> 3. **Wet/Adverse Weather Concreting** — relative humidity **> 90 %** with active precipitation, monsoon-driven humidity, or wind speed **> 25 km/h** causing rapid surface evaporation

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine baking a cake in three different ovens:

- **Normal Weather** is like a perfectly calibrated oven at 180 °C — the cake rises evenly, sets properly, and is ready in exactly the time the recipe says.
- **Extreme Hot Weather** is like cranking the oven to 250 °C — the outside hardens too fast, the inside stays raw, and water evaporates so fast that the cake cracks on top.
- **Extreme Cold Weather** is like opening the oven door in a snowstorm — the cake stops rising altogether because the yeast (cement) literally "goes to sleep" and refuses to react.

The **formwork** is the *cake mould*. In hot weather, the mould heats up and dehydrates the concrete; in cold weather, the mould itself can become a heat sink freezing the hydration. The skill of the formwork engineer is to **insulate, shade, heat, or ventilate** this mould so the "cake" (concrete) sets uniformly regardless of what nature is doing outside.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Standard Curing Temperature:** $T_{ref} = 27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (IS 516)
> - **Datum Temperature (Maturity Concept):** $T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (Plowman / Nurse-Saunders)
> - **Freezing Point of Water in Capillary Pores:** $\approx -1\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $-2\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - **Maximum Permissible Concrete Temperature at Placement:** $T_{c,max} = 32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (IS 7861 Part 1)
> - **Minimum Permissible Concrete Temperature at Placement:** $T_{c,min} = 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (IS 7861 Part 2)
> - **Critical Evaporation Rate (Plastic Shrinkage Trigger):** $E_{cr} = 1.0\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$ (ACI 305)
> - **Activation Energy of OPC Hydration:** $E_a \approx 40{,}000\,\text{J/mol}$ to $45{,}000\,\text{J/mol}$

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Formwork Engineering

The formwork is the **first line of thermal and moisture defence** for fresh concrete. It controls:
- Heat dissipation (hot weather)
- Heat retention (cold weather)
- Moisture retention (hot/dry/windy)
- Water ingress protection (rain/monsoon)
- Lateral pressure regime (rate of pour affected by temperature)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love questions linking *formwork* with *concrete behaviour* under extreme conditions. Always tie your answer back to the formwork — never treat this as a pure concrete technology question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Extreme Weather Regimes

### 2.1.1 Hot Weather Concreting
Operational triggers per **IS 7861 (Part 1) : 1975** & **ACI 305R-10**:
- High ambient temperature (>$32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- Low relative humidity (<$50\,\%$)
- High wind velocity (>$25\,\text{km/h}$)
- Solar radiation intensity

**Effects on formwork & concrete:**
1. **Accelerated cement hydration** → faster setting, lower workability retention
2. **Increased water demand** → loss of slump (~25 mm drop per 10 °C rise)
3. **Higher formwork pressure** → faster initial set = higher lateral pressure peak (though shorter duration)
4. **Plastic shrinkage cracking** → evaporation exceeds bleeding rate
5. **Thermal gradients** → differential expansion of formwork vs. concrete
6. **Reduced ultimate strength** if water is added at site (re-tempering)

**Formwork engineering countermeasures:**
- Use **light-coloured/reflective formwork** (aluminium, white-coated plywood)
- **Pre-wet timber formwork** to prevent absorption of mix water
- Provide **shading & windbreaks** around formwork
- Use **retarding admixtures** in concrete
- Schedule pours during **early morning/late evening**
- Use **insulated formwork** in extreme cases to reduce peak hydration temperature

### 2.1.2 Cold Weather Concreting
Operational triggers per **IS 7861 (Part 2) : 1981** & **ACI 306R-10**:
- Ambient temperature <$5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for more than 3 consecutive days
- Air temperature <$10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ during 24 hours after placement

**Effects on formwork & concrete:**
1. **Retarded hydration** → strength gain slows dramatically (near zero below $5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$)
2. **Freezing of fresh concrete** → if temperature drops below $-1\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ before initial set (~24% strength), water expands ~9% causing irreparable damage
3. **Reduced formwork pressure** → fluid pressure persists longer as set is delayed
4. **Higher stripping times** → formwork must remain until sufficient maturity
5. **Condensation on cold formwork surfaces** → local weak zones

**Formwork engineering countermeasures:**
- **Insulated formwork** (expanded polystyrene-lined plywood)
- **Heated enclosures & tented formwork** with steam/hot air blowers
- **External heating blankets** wrapped around vertical formwork
- Use of **Type III (high-early-strength) cement** or accelerators
- **Pre-warming aggregates and mixing water** (water can be heated to max $60\,^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- **Extended formwork striking time** — minimum 7 days for vertical, 14 days for soffit

### 2.1.3 Wet / Monsoon / High-Humidity Weather
Operational triggers:
- Continuous rainfall during placement
- RH > 90 %
- Standing water in formwork

**Effects on formwork & concrete:**
1. **Washout of cement paste** from top surface → weak laitance
2. **Increased water-cement ratio** at surface → lower surface strength, dusting
3. **Formwork saturation** → plywood swelling, reduced re-use cycles
4. **Corrosion of steel formwork** fasteners and accessories
5. **Difficulties in vibration** due to water ingress
6. **Delayed finishing operations**

**Formwork engineering countermeasures:**
- **Plastic sheet covers** over open formwork
- **Drainage provisions** in formwork base
- **Waterproof plywood / phenolic-coated formwork**
- **Galvanized steel formwork** for monsoon zones
- **Tarpaulin shelters** over the placement zone
- **Pre-monsoon storage** of formwork materials in dry godowns

## 2.2 The Concrete Temperature Equation (KTU High-Yield Formula)

The most tested formula on this topic is the **Concrete Placement Temperature Formula**, derived from the principle of thermal equilibrium among mix constituents.

Let:
- $T_c$ = concrete temperature at placement ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $T_cem$ = cement temperature ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $T_s$ = saturated surface-dry aggregate temperature ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $T_w$ = mixing water temperature ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $M_a$ = mass of aggregates (kg)
- $M_c$ = mass of cement (kg)
- $M_w$ = mass of mixing water (kg)
- $c_a, c_c, c_w$ = specific heats: aggregates (0.92–1.0), cement (1.13), water (4.18) kJ/kg·K

$$T_c = \frac{(0.22 \cdot T_{cem} \cdot M_c) + (T_s \cdot M_a) + (T_w \cdot M_w)}{0.22 \cdot M_c + M_a + M_w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.22** is the *thermal coefficient* combining specific heat of cement (~1.13) with the conventional batch water-to-cement ratio baseline. This is the **ACI 305 / IS 7861 standard form** that KTU expects.

## 2.3 Evaporation Rate from Concrete Surface (Nomograph Basis)

The **ACI 305R Nomograph Equation** (Menzel/Lenz formula) governs the rate of evaporative water loss:

$$E = 0.044 \cdot (T_c - T_a)^{0.78} \cdot V^{0.45} \cdot (RH)^{-0.37}$$

Where:
- $E$ = evaporation rate in $\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$
- $T_c - T_a$ = difference between concrete and air temperature ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $V$ = wind velocity in $\text{km/h}$
- $RH$ = relative humidity expressed as a percentage

**Plastic shrinkage cracking** is imminent when $E > 1.0\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$.

## 2.4 The Maturity Concept (Cold Weather Stripping Decisions)

The **Nurse-Saunders Maturity Function** (ASTM C1074) estimates the in-situ strength development of concrete:

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i - T_0) \cdot \Delta t$$

Where:
- $M$ = maturity index ($^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{hours}$ or $^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{days}$)
- $T_i$ = average concrete temperature during time interval $i$ ($^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $T_0$ = datum temperature = $-11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (or 0 °C simplified)
- $\Delta t$ = time interval (hours)

The **Plowman Equation** (USBR) refines this with a logarithmic correction:

$$M = \sum (T + 11) \cdot \Delta t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Datum $T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ is used because below this temperature, cement hydration essentially ceases in standard OPC systems. This is the **Nurse-Saunders convention** and appears repeatedly in KTU questions.

## 2.5 Strength-Maturity Relationship (Plowman / Freiesleben-Hansen)

The compressive strength at any maturity can be related to the strength at standard maturity through the **Arrhenius-type rate constant**:

$$S = S_{ref} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\frac{M}{k}}\right)$$

Or, in the more rigorous **Freiesleben Hansen / Pedersen** form:

$$S = S_{\infty} \cdot e^{-\left(\frac{\tau}{M}\right)^{\alpha}}$$

Where:
- $S_{\infty}$ = ultimate strength (asymptote)
- $\tau$ = characteristic maturity constant
- $\alpha$ = shape parameter (typically 0.6 – 1.0 for OPC)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below is your single-source revision sheet for any numerical question on this topic. Memorize the variables and units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Concrete placement temperature | $T_c = \dfrac{(0.22 T_{cem} M_c) + (T_s M_a) + (T_w M_w)}{0.22 M_c + M_a + M_w}$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Hot/cold weather mix design |
| 2 | Evaporation rate (ACI 305) | $E = 0.044\,(T_c - T_a)^{0.78} \cdot V^{0.45} \cdot (RH)^{-0.37}$ | $\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$ | Plastic shrinkage assessment |
| 3 | Critical evaporation threshold | $E_{cr} = 1.0$ | $\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$ | Trigger for protective measures |
| 4 | Maturity index (Nurse-Saunders) | $M = \sum (T_i - T_0)\,\Delta t$ | $^\circ\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$ | Cold weather stripping time |
| 5 | Datum temperature | $T_0 = -11$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Maturity baseline |
| 6 | Plowman maturity | $M = \sum (T_i + 11)\,\Delta t$ | $^\circ\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$ | Equivalent age calculation |
| 7 | Strength-maturity (exponential) | $S = S_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-M/k}\right)$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ or MPa | Predicted stripping strength |

> - Activation energy $E_a = 40$ to $45\,\text{kJ/mol}$
> - Acceleration factor at $38\,^\circ\text{C}$ vs. $23\,^\circ\text{C}$ $\approx 2.0$
> - Retardation factor at $5\,^\circ\text{C}$ vs. $23\,^\circ\text{C}$ $\approx 0.4$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| High-rise construction (Kerala monsoons) | Tented formwork & waterproof sheathing |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Concrete Placement Temperature Formula

### 3.1.1 Underlying Principle
For a freshly mixed concrete batch, the total heat content of the constituents must equal the heat content of the resulting concrete mass (conservation of energy, assuming no losses during the brief mixing process).

**Heat balance equation:**

$$Q_{cement} + Q_{aggregate} + Q_{water} = Q_{concrete}$$

$$M_c \cdot c_{cem} \cdot (T_c - T_{cem}) + M_a \cdot c_a \cdot (T_c - T_s) + M_w \cdot c_w \cdot (T_c - T_w) = 0$$

### 3.1.2 Substitute the Standard Specific Heats
- $c_{cem} = 1.13\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$ — empirical approximation
- $c_w = 4.18\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$
- $c_a = 0.92\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$ (typical for siliceous aggregate)
- $c_a \approx 0.22$ as a normalized coefficient (this is the **empirical ACI factor** combining aggregate specific heat with the typical aggregate-to-cement ratio)

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Expansion
Expanding the heat balance:

$$M_c \cdot c_{cem} \cdot T_c - M_c \cdot c_{cem} \cdot T_{cem} + M_a \cdot c_a \cdot T_c - M_a \cdot c_a \cdot T_s + M_w \cdot c_w \cdot T_c - M_w \cdot c_w \cdot T_w = 0$$

Grouping $T_c$ terms on one side:

$$T_c \cdot (M_c \cdot c_{cem} + M_a \cdot c_a + M_w \cdot c_w) = M_c \cdot c_{cem} \cdot T_{cem} + M_a \cdot c_a \cdot T_s + M_w \cdot c_w \cdot T_w$$

### 3.1.4 Apply the ACI 305 Normalization
Since $c_{cem}/c_w \approx 0.27$ and $c_a/c_w \approx 0.22$ (using typical aggregate-to-water and aggregate proportioning), the **practical ACI 305 simplified form** uses $0.22$ as the combined coefficient:

$$T_c = \frac{(0.22 \cdot T_{cem} \cdot M_c) + (T_s \cdot M_a) + (T_w \cdot M_w)}{0.22 \cdot M_c + M_a + M_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **final KTU board-expected form**. Do not present the rigorous thermochemistry form unless specifically asked; the simplified ACI form is what examiners look for.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Hot Weather Concreting

**Problem:** A concrete mix is prepared in Kerala summer conditions with the following data:
- Cement: $M_c = 350\,\text{kg}$ at $T_{cem} = 65\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (stored in open yard)
- Aggregates: $M_a = 1800\,\text{kg}$ at $T_s = 38\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (sun-exposed stockpile)
- Mixing water: $M_w = 175\,\text{kg}$ at $T_w = 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Ambient air: $T_a = 40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Wind speed: $V = 20\,\text{km/h}$
- Relative humidity: $RH = 45\,\%$

**Determine:**
(a) The concrete placement temperature $T_c$.
(b) The evaporation rate $E$ from the fresh concrete surface.
(c) Whether plastic shrinkage precautions are necessary.

### 3.2.1 Solution for (a) — Concrete Temperature

$$T_c = \frac{(0.22 \cdot T_{cem} \cdot M_c) + (T_s \cdot M_a) + (T_w \cdot M_w)}{0.22 \cdot M_c + M_a + M_w}$$

Compute numerator:

$$0.22 \cdot 65 \cdot 350 = 5{,}005$$

$$38 \cdot 1800 = 68{,}400$$

$$30 \cdot 175 = 5{,}250$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 5{,}005 + 68{,}400 + 5{,}250 = 78{,}655$$

Compute denominator:

$$0.22 \cdot 350 = 77$$

$$77 + 1800 + 175 = 2{,}052$$

$$T_c = \frac{78{,}655}{2{,}052} = 38.33\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

### 3.2.2 Solution for (b) — Evaporation Rate

$$E = 0.044 \cdot (T_c - T_a)^{0.78} \cdot V^{0.45} \cdot (RH)^{-0.37}$$

$$T_c - T_a = 38.33 - 40 = -1.67\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Use absolute value for the rate of evaporative driving potential:

$$E = 0.044 \cdot (1.67)^{0.78} \cdot (20)^{0.45} \cdot (45)^{-0.37}$$

Step 1: $(1.67)^{0.78}$
Using $\ln(1.67) = 0.5128$, then $0.78 \times 0.5128 = 0.4000$, so $e^{0.4000} = 1.492$.

Step 2: $(20)^{0.45}$
Using $\ln(20) = 2.9957$, then $0.45 \times 2.9957 = 1.3481$, so $e^{1.3481} = 3.849$.

Step 3: $(45)^{-0.37}$
Using $\ln(45) = 3.8067$, then $-0.37 \times 3.8067 = -1.4085$, so $e^{-1.4085} = 0.2445$.

Step 4: Combine:

$$E = 0.044 \times 1.492 \times 3.849 \times 0.2445$$

$$E = 0.044 \times 1.4035$$

$$E \approx 0.0618\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$$

### 3.2.3 Solution for (c) — Plastic Shrinkage Risk

Compare $E$ with $E_{cr} = 1.0\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$:

$$E = 0.0618 < 1.0 \implies \text{NOT critical under these conditions}$$

> **However**, the concrete temperature $T_c = 38.33\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ **exceeds the IS 7861 limit of $32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$** — therefore:
> - **Reject the mix** OR
> - **Cool the constituents** (chilled water, ice flakes, shaded aggregates)
> - **Retard the cement** to delay flash set

### 3.2.4 Engineering Conclusion
This is a classic *two-part* KTU question where the student must evaluate **both** the evaporation rate *and* the placement temperature independently. Many students stop after one — that's a 3–4 mark loss in a 14-mark question.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Cold Weather Maturity

**Problem:** A concrete slab is cast in Manali (HP) winter conditions. The concrete temperature was recorded every 4 hours for the first 24 hours:

| Interval | Avg. Concrete Temp $T_i$ (°C) | Duration $\Delta t$ (h) |
|---|---|---|
| 0–4 h | 18 | 4 |
| 4–8 h | 14 | 4 |
| 8–12 h | 11 | 4 |
| 12–16 h | 8 | 4 |
| 16–20 h | 6 | 4 |
| 20–24 h | 4 | 4 |

**Determine:**
(a) The maturity index $M$ at 24 hours using $T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.
(b) The equivalent age at standard temperature $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.
(c) The implication for formwork striking time.

### 3.3.1 Solution for (a) — Maturity Index

Using $M = \sum (T_i - T_0) \cdot \Delta t$:

- Interval 1: $(18 - (-11)) \times 4 = 29 \times 4 = 116$
- Interval 2: $(14 + 11) \times 4 = 25 \times 4 = 100$
- Interval 3: $(11 + 11) \times 4 = 22 \times 4 = 88$
- Interval 4: $(8 + 11) \times 4 = 19 \times 4 = 76$
- Interval 5: $(6 + 11) \times 4 = 17 \times 4 = 68$
- Interval 6: $(4 + 11) \times 4 = 15 \times 4 = 60$

$$M = 116 + 100 + 88 + 76 + 68 + 60 = 508\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$$

### 3.3.2 Solution for (b) — Equivalent Age

Equivalent age $t_e$ is the time that would produce this maturity at standard $T_{ref} = 27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$t_e = \frac{M}{T_{ref} - T_0} = \frac{508}{27 - (-11)} = \frac{508}{38} = 13.37\,\text{hours}$$

This means **24 hours of cold curing is equivalent to only 13.37 hours of standard curing** — roughly a 44 % retardation.

### 3.3.3 Solution for (c) — Formwork Striking Implication

Standard slab striking time at $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$: typically **7 days** for soffit of small spans.

Equivalent days at 27 °C curing = $7 \times \frac{13.37}{24} = 3.9$ days → stripping is **unsafe** at the standard 7-day mark in cold weather.

**Recommended extended striking time** (using $E_a / R$ rule): roughly $7 \times 1.8 \approx 12.6$ days in this cold profile.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using $T_0 = 0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (a simplification used in some texts). KTU strictly expects $T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ unless the question states otherwise. This is a 2-mark trap.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Hot vs. Cold vs. Wet Weather

| Parameter | Hot Weather | Cold Weather | Wet / Monsoon |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary trigger | $T_a > 32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $T_a < 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $RH > 90\,\%$ + rain |
| Formwork pressure profile | Sharp early peak, short duration | Sustained fluid pressure, long duration | Variable; tied to drainage |
| Formwork material stress | Thermal expansion buckling | Freeze-thaw damage of timber | Plywood swelling & corrosion |
| Concrete setting time | Reduced by 30 – 50 % | Increased by 50 – 100 % | Slightly delayed (humid) |
| Plastic shrinkage risk | **Very high** | Negligible | Low (high humidity) |
| Strength at 7 days (relative) | 110 – 130 % of $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ cure | 60 – 80 % of $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ cure | 95 – 100 % of $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ cure |
| Stripping time adjustment | **Reduce** by 20 – 30 % | **Extend** by 50 – 100 % | Standard |
| Dominant formwork concern | Heat absorption, moisture loss | Heat loss, freezing at interface | Water ingress, swelling |
| Key IS code | IS 7861 Part 1 | IS 7861 Part 2 | IS 7861 Part 1 (general) |
| Key ACI code | ACI 305R-10 | ACI 306R-10 | ACI 305R / 308 |

## 3.5 Lateral Pressure on Formwork in Hot vs. Cold Weather

The **ACI SP-4 / CIRIA 108** lateral pressure formula on a temperature-adjusted basis:

$$p_{max} = \gamma \cdot C_w \cdot R^{0.3} \quad \text{(with temperature correction)}$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of concrete
- $C_w$ = unit weight coefficient
- $R$ = rate of pour (m/h)
- Multiplier for temperature: $K_T = \frac{T_c + 11}{38}$ (Plowman factor)

In **hot weather** ($T_c$ high): $K_T$ is large → pressure decays faster, peak pressure is high but short-lived.
In **cold weather** ($T_c$ low): $K_T$ is small → full hydrostatic pressure may persist, requiring stronger formwork ties and wailers.

    C1 --> C2[Compute Evaporation Rate E via ACI 305]
