# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Q: Define hot weather concreting as per IS 7861 (Part 1). List any four precautions to be taken during formwork operations in hot weather.**

**Model Answer (Expected Length: 8–10 lines):**

**Definition:** As per IS 7861 (Part 1) : 1975, hot weather concreting refers to any combination of high air temperature, low relative humidity, high wind velocity, and solar radiation that impairs the quality of fresh or hardened concrete, or otherwise results in abnormal properties of concrete. Operationally, this is taken as **ambient temperature above 32 °C** or **concrete temperature above 30 °C** at the time of placement.

**Four Precautions during Formwork Operations:**

1. **Pre-wetting timber formwork** to prevent absorption of mix water and to avoid rapid slump loss at the concrete-formwork interface.
2. **Use of light-coloured or reflective formwork surfaces** (aluminium panels, white-coated plywood) to reduce solar heat absorption and limit the rise of concrete temperature at the surface.
3. **Provision of shading and wind barriers** around the formwork periphery to reduce wind-induced evaporation and surface drying.
4. **Use of retarder-type admixtures** in the concrete and **scheduling of pours during early morning or late evening hours** to avoid peak ambient temperatures.
5. *(Optional 5th point)* Application of **curing compounds or evaporation retarders** immediately after finishing, before the formwork side shutters are stripped.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: What is the maturity concept of concrete? How is it used in deciding formwork striking time in cold weather?**

**Model Answer (Expected Length: 8–10 lines):**

**Definition:** The **maturity concept** (Nurse-Saunders, 1951) is a method of estimating the in-situ strength of concrete by combining the effects of time and temperature during the curing period into a single index called the **maturity index**, expressed in °C·hours or °C·days.

**Formula:**

$$M = \sum (T_i - T_0) \cdot \Delta t, \quad \text{where } T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Application in Cold Weather Formwork Striking:**

- Concrete temperature is monitored continuously via embedded sensors in the formwork.
- Cumulative maturity is computed and compared with the **target maturity** corresponding to the desired stripping strength (e.g., 70 % of $f_{ck}$ for soffit stripping).
- Formwork is struck **only when the computed maturity exceeds the target**, ensuring the concrete has gained sufficient strength regardless of retarded hydration.
- This avoids both premature stripping (which causes structural failure) and unnecessary delay (which slows construction).

> This is a standard 3-mark definition question. Expect follow-up sub-parts in Part B.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1. [14 Marks — KTU University Exam, Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **effects of hot weather on concrete** and outline the **specific formwork design and operational modifications** required to ensure quality construction under hot weather conditions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A concrete mix is to be placed in Trivandrum summer conditions. Given the following data, calculate the **concrete placement temperature** and the **evaporation rate from the fresh concrete surface**. State whether plastic shrinkage cracking precautions are necessary.

**Data given:**
- Cement: $M_c = 400\,\text{kg}$ at $T_{cem} = 70\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Aggregates: $M_a = 1850\,\text{kg}$ at $T_s = 42\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Mixing water: $M_w = 200\,\text{kg}$ at $T_w = 32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 38\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Wind speed: $V = 15\,\text{km/h}$
- Relative humidity: $RH = 55\,\%$

**[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B1(a) — Hot Weather Effects & Formwork Modifications

**1. Effects of Hot Weather on Concrete:**

1. **Accelerated cement hydration:** Higher temperatures speed up the hydration reaction, reducing the initial setting time. This is particularly problematic for transporting, placing, and finishing concrete before it stiffens.
2. **Increased water demand and rapid slump loss:** Concrete loses workability rapidly. Approximately **25 mm of slump is lost for every 10 °C rise in temperature** above the standard 27 °C.
3. **Plastic shrinkage cracking:** When the rate of evaporation from the surface exceeds the rate of bleeding, tensile stresses develop in the still-plastic surface, causing characteristic map-pattern cracking.
4. **Higher peak temperature rise in mass concrete:** Increased thermal gradient between the core and surface leads to thermal cracking during cooling.
5. **Reduction in 28-day strength:** If extra water is added at the site to restore workability (re-tempering), the effective water-cement ratio increases, lowering ultimate strength by 10–15 %.
6. **Increased tendency for cold joints** due to faster setting of previously placed layers.
7. **Drying shrinkage and creep** are aggravated in the long term.

**2. Formwork Design and Operational Modifications:**

1. **Material Selection:**
   - Use **light-coloured formwork** (white-pigmented phenolic-coated plywood or aluminium) to reflect solar radiation and reduce surface heating.
   - Use **steel formwork** where multiple reuses are expected, as it is dimensionally stable.
   - Avoid **dark-coloured formwork** (untreated timber, black-coated steel) that absorbs heat.

2. **Pre-wetting of Timber Formwork:**
   - Saturate timber formwork with water **at least 24 hours** before concrete placement. This prevents the dry timber from absorbing mix water from the fresh concrete, which would otherwise cause a weak, porous surface layer and rapid slump loss.

3. **Shading and Wind Protection:**
   - Erect **temporary shade nets or tarpaulins** above and around the formwork to reduce direct solar gain and wind-induced evaporation.
   - Use **windbreak screens** when wind speed exceeds **25 km/h**.

4. **Scheduling and Concrete Delivery:**
   - Schedule concrete pours during the **coolest hours** (early morning 4–7 AM or late evening 6–10 PM).
   - Minimize **transportation time** by coordinating batching plant, transit mixers, and placement crew.
   - Use **chilled water or ice flakes** (up to 50 % replacement of mixing water) to lower the initial concrete temperature.

5. **Admixture Strategy:**
   - Incorporate **retarding admixtures** (lignosulphonate, sugar-based) to extend working time.
   - Avoid chloride-based accelerators that aggravate corrosion of reinforcement.

6. **Modified Stripping Protocol:**
   - The accelerated strength gain in hot weather allows **earlier stripping** (typically 20–30 % reduction in standard striking time).
   - However, **immediate curing** with water, curing compounds, or wet burlap is essential to prevent plastic shrinkage and surface drying.

7. **Curing and Surface Protection:**
   - Apply **evaporation retarders** (aliphatic alcohol emulsions) as soon as the surface is finished.
   - Begin **water curing within 2–3 hours** of finishing and continue for a minimum of **7 days** (or as per IS 456).

8. **Formwork Pressure Implications:**
   - Since hydration is faster, the **lateral pressure peak occurs earlier** but decays faster. Formwork ties and braces must be designed for a **higher short-duration peak**, not the lower standard 24-hour pressure.
   - Rate of pour should be **modulated** to avoid pressure spikes.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if the student links *each* effect of hot weather to a *specific* formwork response. Generic statements like "use good formwork" fetch zero marks. Always state the **material, action, and reason**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B1(b) — Numerical Problem

**Step 1: Apply the Concrete Placement Temperature Formula**

$$T_c = \frac{(0.22 \cdot T_{cem} \cdot M_c) + (T_s \cdot M_a) + (T_w \cdot M_w)}{0.22 \cdot M_c + M_a + M_w}$$

Compute each term of the numerator:

$$0.22 \times 70 \times 400 = 6{,}160$$

$$42 \times 1850 = 77{,}700$$

$$32 \times 200 = 6{,}400$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 6{,}160 + 77{,}700 + 6{,}400 = 90{,}260$$

Compute the denominator:

$$0.22 \times 400 = 88$$

$$88 + 1850 + 200 = 2{,}138$$

$$T_c = \frac{90{,}260}{2{,}138} = 42.22\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Apply the ACI 305 Evaporation Rate Formula**

$$E = 0.044 \cdot (T_c - T_a)^{0.78} \cdot V^{0.45} \cdot (RH)^{-0.37}$$

$$\Delta T = \vert T_c - T_a \vert = \vert 42.22 - 38 \vert = 4.22\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Substitute:

$$E = 0.044 \cdot (4.22)^{0.78} \cdot (15)^{0.45} \cdot (55)^{-0.37}$$

Compute each exponential term:

**Term 1:** $(4.22)^{0.78}$
$\ln(4.22) = 1.4397$, $0.78 \times 1.4397 = 1.1230$, $e^{1.1230} = 3.073$

**Term 2:** $(15)^{0.45}$
$\ln(15) = 2.7081$, $0.45 \times 2.7081 = 1.2186$, $e^{1.2186} = 3.382$

**Term 3:** $(55)^{-0.37}$
$\ln(55) = 4.0073$, $-0.37 \times 4.0073 = -1.4827$, $e^{-1.4827} = 0.2270$

Combine:

$$E = 0.044 \times 3.073 \times 3.382 \times 0.2270$$

$$E = 0.044 \times 2.360$$

$$E = 0.1038\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$$

**Step 3: Decision on Plastic Shrinkage Precautions**

$$E = 0.1038\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h} \quad \text{vs.} \quad E_{cr} = 1.0\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$$

Since $E \ll E_{cr}$, **evaporation-based plastic shrinkage cracking is NOT critical** at this site under these conditions.

**However**, the **concrete placement temperature $T_c = 42.22\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ exceeds the IS 7861 upper limit of 32 °C** by more than 10 °C. This is a serious violation that demands immediate corrective action:

**Recommended Corrective Actions:**
1. **Cool the cement** by storing in shaded silos (target $T_{cem} < 50\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
2. **Cool the aggregates** by water spraying on stockpiles (target $T_s < 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
3. **Use chilled water or ice** for mixing (target $T_w \approx 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
4. **Add a retarding admixture** to delay flash set.
5. **Re-pour during night** to reduce $T_a$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often compute $E$ and conclude "no problem" because $E < 1.0$. This is **incomplete** — the $T_c$ violation is a separate and more serious concern. Always evaluate **both** criteria and present both conclusions. Skipping the $T_c$ check costs 3–4 marks.

---

### Question B2. [14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Discuss the **effects of cold weather on concrete and formwork operations**. Describe in detail the **protective measures, heated enclosure systems, and maturity-based stripping protocols** that should be adopted during winter concreting. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A concrete column is cast in Shimla winter conditions. The concrete temperature recorded at 6-hour intervals is given below. Using the **maturity concept with $T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$**, determine:

1. The **maturity index** at 48 hours.
2. The **equivalent age** at $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.
3. Whether the formwork can be stripped at 48 hours if the required maturity for striking is **$1100\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$**.

| Interval | Avg. $T_i$ (°C) | $\Delta t$ (h) |
|---|---|---|
| 0–6 h | 16 | 6 |
| 6–12 h | 13 | 6 |
| 12–18 h | 10 | 6 |
| 18–24 h | 7 | 6 |
| 24–30 h | 6 | 6 |
| 30–36 h | 4 | 6 |
| 36–42 h | 3 | 6 |
| 42–48 h | 2 | 6 |

**[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B2(a) — Cold Weather Effects & Protective Measures

**1. Effects of Cold Weather on Concrete:**

1. **Retardation of cement hydration:** The hydration reaction rate roughly halves for every 10 °C drop in temperature. Below 5 °C, hydration becomes extremely slow; below 0 °C, it effectively ceases.
2. **Risk of freezing before initial set:** If the concrete freezes before it attains a strength of approximately **3.5 MPa (24 % of $f_{ck}$ for M20)**, the expansion of freezing water disrupts the cement matrix and causes **irreparable damage** — typically a 50 % strength loss.
3. **Delayed strength gain:** Concrete cured at 5 °C achieves only **60–80 % of the 28-day strength** of concrete cured at 27 °C at the same age.
4. **Extended formwork striking time:** Standard striking times must be increased by 50–100 %.
5. **Increased formwork lateral pressure:** Because the concrete remains fluid for a longer time, full hydrostatic pressure may act on the formwork for a longer duration. Ties and wailers must be designed for this.
6. **Surface damage from thermal gradients:** Rapid cooling of the surface, especially in cold winds, causes surface crazing and reduces near-surface strength.

**2. Effects of Cold Weather on Formwork:**

1. **Timber formwork** absorbs moisture and may swell, distort, or warp, reducing dimensional accuracy.
2. **Steel formwork** becomes a heat sink, accelerating heat loss from the concrete and lowering its temperature.
3. **Formwork ties and fasteners** may contract, loosening joints.
4. **Condensation** on cold formwork surfaces creates water films that weaken the concrete-formwork bond zone.

**3. Protective Measures and Heated Enclosure Systems:**

1. **Pre-warming of constituents:**
   - Heat mixing water to a maximum of **60 °C** (above this, flash set of cement can occur).
   - Heat aggregates by steaming or by circulating hot air through stockpiles.
   - Pre-warm the formwork using steam hoses or hot air blowers.

2. **Insulated formwork:**
   - Line formwork with **expanded polystyrene (EPS)** or **polyurethane foam** sheets.
   - Use **double-skinned formwork** with an insulating air gap.

3. **Heated enclosures (tenting):**
   - Erect **tarpaulin or polyethylene enclosures** around the placement zone.
   - Use **indirect-fired heaters** (no CO₂ or CO contact with concrete surface, as these cause carbonation defects).
   - Maintain enclosure temperature at **$10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ minimum** for the first 24 hours, then $5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for the next 3–5 days.
   - Use **steam curing** for precast elements.

4. **Cement and admixture selection:**
   - Use **Type III (high-early-strength) cement** or **Type I cement with accelerators** (non-chloride based to avoid corrosion).
   - **Reduce water-cement ratio** by using plasticizers, since less water means less freezable liquid.

5. **Maturity-based stripping protocol:**
   - Embed **temperature sensors** in the formwork and concrete.
   - Compute cumulative maturity using the Nurse-Saunders equation.
   - Strike the formwork only when the **target maturity for the required stripping strength** is achieved (e.g., $1100\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$ for typical slab soffit stripping).
   - Document the maturity record as part of the **quality assurance file**.

6. **Protection after stripping:**
   - Continue to **insulate the exposed concrete** with curing blankets or straw.
   - Avoid sudden temperature drops greater than **$3\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ per hour** during the first 24 hours after stripping.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B2(b) — Maturity Calculation

**Step 1: Compute Maturity for Each Interval**

$$M = \sum (T_i - T_0) \cdot \Delta t = \sum (T_i + 11) \cdot \Delta t$$

| Interval | $T_i$ (°C) | $(T_i + 11)$ | $\Delta t$ (h) | $M_i$ (°C·h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 16 | 27 | 6 | 162 |
| 2 | 13 | 24 | 6 | 144 |
| 3 | 10 | 21 | 6 | 126 |
| 4 | 7 | 18 | 6 | 108 |
| 5 | 6 | 17 | 6 | 102 |
| 6 | 4 | 15 | 6 | 90 |
| 7 | 3 | 14 | 6 | 84 |
| 8 | 2 | 13 | 6 | 78 |

**Step 2: Total Maturity at 48 hours**

$$M_{48} = 162 + 144 + 126 + 108 + 102 + 90 + 84 + 78 = 894\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$$

**Step 3: Equivalent Age at 27 °C**

$$t_e = \frac{M_{48}}{T_{ref} - T_0} = \frac{894}{27 - (-11)} = \frac{894}{38} = 23.53\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 4: Decision on Formwork Stripping**

Required maturity for striking: $M_{req} = 1100\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$
Computed maturity at 48 hours: $M_{48} = 894\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$

Since $M_{48} = 894 < 1100 = M_{req}$, **formwork CANNOT be stripped at 48 hours**.

**Step 5: Determine when stripping becomes safe**

Additional maturity required: $1100 - 894 = 206\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$

If the temperature stabilizes at **$2\,^{\circ}\text{C}$** after 48 h, hourly maturity rate = $(2 + 11) = 13\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$.

Additional time needed: $206 / 13 = 15.85\,\text{hours}$ ≈ **16 more hours**

**Total stripping time: $48 + 16 = 64$ hours (approximately 2.7 days) from casting.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students compute maturity but forget to **convert to equivalent age** and then **compare with the required maturity value**. The question explicitly states "$1100\,^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{h}$ required" — this is a direct maturity comparison, NOT an age comparison. Converting to equivalent age is optional here; comparing $M$ directly with $M_{req}$ is the cleaner approach.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in This Topic (Deduce Marks Lost):**
> 1. **Confusing $T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ with $T_0 = 0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$** in the maturity equation. KTU strictly follows Nurse-Saunders convention. **Loss: 2 marks per question.**
> 2. **Using the simplified 0.22 form** when the question expects a *rigorous* specific-heat calculation. Read the question carefully — note whether it says "as per ACI 305" or "derive from first principles." **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 3. **Stopping at the evaporation rate check** without separately evaluating the placement temperature limit. These are two independent KTU criteria. **Loss: 3–4 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** of $V$ (km/h) and $RH$ (percentage) in the evaporation formula. The formula is dimensionally specific. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Omitting the formwork-specific discussion** and answering purely from a concrete technology perspective. The course is *Formwork Engineering*, not *Concrete Technology*. **Loss: 2–3 marks per question.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Read Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Hot Weather Trigger:** $T_a > 32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (IS 7861 Part 1) / $T_c > 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ at placement.
- ✅ **Cold Weather Trigger:** $T_a < 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for > 3 consecutive days (IS 7861 Part 2 / ACI 306).
- ✅ **Wet Weather Trigger:** $RH > 90\,\%$ + active precipitation; protect formwork from standing water.
- ✅ **Critical Evaporation Rate:** $E_{cr} = 1.0\,\text{kg/m}^2\text{/h}$ — above this, plastic shrinkage cracking is imminent.
- ✅ **Concrete Placement Temperature Formula:** $T_c = \dfrac{0.22\,T_{cem}\,M_c + T_s\,M_a + T_w\,M_w}{0.22\,M_c + M_a + M_w}$
- ✅ **Maximum Permissible $T_c$:** $32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ — violation demands chilled water, ice, or shaded aggregates.
- ✅ **Minimum Permissible $T_c$:** $5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ — violation demands heated water, pre-warmed aggregates, accelerator cement.
- ✅ **Maturity Datum:** $T_0 = -11\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (Nurse-Saunders convention).
- ✅ **Maturity Formula:** $M = \sum (T_i + 11)\,\Delta t$ with units of °C·h.
- ✅ **Equivalent Age:** $t_e = M / (T_{ref} - T_0)$ where $T_{ref} = 27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- ✅ **Slump Loss Rule of Thumb:** ~$25\,\text{mm}$ slump loss per $10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ rise in $T_c$.
- ✅ **Hot Weather Stripping Time:** **Reduce** by 20–30 % (accelerated strength gain).
- ✅ **Cold Weather Stripping Time:** **Extend** by 50–100 % (retarded strength gain).
- ✅ **Freezing Damage Threshold:** Concrete must reach ~3.5 MPa (24 % of $f_{ck}$ for M20) before first freeze.
- ✅ **Formwork Color Rule:** Light colours (white, aluminium) for hot weather; dark colours only acceptable in cold weather (heat retention).
- ✅ **Pre-wet Timber Formwork:** Mandatory in hot weather; do **not** pre-wet in cold weather (causes ice formation).
- ✅ **Evaporation Retarders:** Aliphatic alcohol emulsions applied immediately after finishing in hot/dry/windy conditions.
- ✅ **Heated Enclosures:** Maintain ≥ $10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for first 24 h, then ≥ $5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for next 3–5 days.
- ✅ **Indirect-Fired Heaters Only:** No direct combustion gases in contact with concrete (CO₂ causes surface carbonation).
- ✅ **Maturity-Based Stripping:** Embed sensors, compute $M$ hourly, strike when $M \geq M_{target}$ for required strength.
- ✅ **Reinforcement Cover:** Increase cover by **15 mm in coastal/marine extreme weather** as per IS 456.
- ✅ **Curing Compounds:** Use resin-based (acrylic) for hot weather; avoid wax-based in cold (slippery surface).
- ✅ **Monsoon Formwork:** Use **phenolic-coated plywood** or **galvanized steel**; provide **drainage holes** in soffit formwork.
- ✅ **Wind Effect:** Wind speed > $25\,\text{km/h}$ increases evaporation dramatically — install wind barriers.
- ✅ **Thermal Expansion of Formwork:** Steel $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ — account for diurnal temperature swing in long pours.
- ✅ **Lateral Pressure:** Hot weather → sharp pressure peak, short duration; Cold weather → sustained hydrostatic pressure.
- ✅ **Type of Cement for Hot Weather:** OPC + retarder; for Cold Weather: Type III (HESC) or Type I + accelerator.
- ✅ **Maximum Water Heating:** $60\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (above this, cement flash-sets in the mixer).
- ✅ **Cooling Methods for Hot Weather:** Chilled water, ice flakes (up to 50 % water replacement), liquid nitrogen injection (mass concrete).
- ✅ **Protection After Stripping in Cold Weather:** Insulating blankets, straw, temporary enclosures to prevent thermal shock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for This Module:**
> "Link every climatic effect to a **formwork response**. Numerical questions on placement temperature and maturity are scoring — practice them. Always check **both** $T_c$ limit **and** $E$ limit in hot weather questions."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Underwater Formwork
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Underwater Formwork

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underwater Formwork** is a specialized temporary structure designed and erected to retain and shape **freshly placed concrete** (or grout/pre-packed aggregate) in locations where the construction interface lies **below the water table** or is otherwise submerged. It must resist **hydrostatic pressure**, **buoyancy forces**, **current loads**, and **wash-out of cement fines** while enabling monolithic placement of structural concrete without dewatering the site.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, underwater formwork is grouped under **"Special Formwork Systems"** and forms the technical bridge between conventional formwork design and **marine / sub-aqueous structural engineering**. It is most commonly applied in:

- Pile caps and pier shafts of **bridges crossing rivers or backwaters**
- **Cofferdams** and base slabs of pump houses
- **Intake / outfall structures** of hydro-power and desalination plants
- **Dock walls, quay walls, and caissons** of harbour works
- **Underground metro stations** and diaphragm walls

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Submerged Bucket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy – Filling a Submerged Bucket with Cement Dough**

Imagine trying to bake a cake *inside a swimming pool*. The water keeps trying to lift the batter away from the mold and wash the finer ingredients out of it. To succeed, you would:
1. **Trap the batter** in a rigid, sealed bucket (the formwork).
2. **Pour the batter from below** through a tube that reaches the bottom, so it rises from the *bottom up* and never touches the water directly (the **tremie method**).
3. **Anchor the bucket** to the pool floor so water pressure cannot push it away (resisting **buoyancy**).
4. **Choose a batter that thickens quickly** so water cannot dilute it (use of **anti-washout admixtures** and **rich concrete**).

This is exactly the engineering challenge that underwater formwork solves. The formwork is the "submerged bucket," the tremie is the delivery tube, and the buoyancy anchors are the tie-downs.

## 1.3 Why Underwater Formwork is a Distinct Discipline

Conventional formwork on dry land is governed by the lateral pressure of fresh concrete alone. Underwater formwork has **four** simultaneous load systems acting on it:

| S.No | Load System | Source | Typical Magnitude |
|:----:|:------------|:-------|:------------------|
| 1 | **Hydrostatic Pressure** | Surrounding water column | $P = \rho_w g h$ |
| 2 | **Buoyancy / Uplift** | Displaced water volume | $F_b = \rho_w \, g \, V$ |
| 3 | **Differential Concrete Pressure** | Heavier concrete (ρ ≈ 24 kN/m³) vs water (ρ ≈ 10 kN/m³) | $P_c = (\rho_c - \rho_w) g h$ |
| 4 | **Hydrodynamic / Current Loads** | Tidal flow, waves, river current | $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w v^2 C_d$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Insight:** When the formwork is filled with fresh concrete, the **net inward pressure** on the formwork face is the *difference* between the outside hydrostatic and the inside concrete-fluid pressure, because both faces are fluid-like during placement. This is why underwater formwork is **thinner and lighter** than equivalent land-based formwork of the same height.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember

> **Density of fresh concrete:** $\rho_c \approx 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (≈ **24 kN/m³**)
> **Density of seawater:** $\rho_w \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (≈ **10.25 kN/m³**)
> **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_w \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (≈ **10 kN/m³**)
> **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** ≈ **10 kPa per metre depth** (fresh water)
> **Typical tremie pipe diameter:** **200 mm – 300 mm** (Ø 250 mm most common)
> **Minimum tremie embedment in fresh concrete:** **1.0 m – 1.5 m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Underwater Formwork Systems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme classifies underwater formwork into **five principal families**. Each is matched to a specific concrete-placement method.

### A) Tremie Formwork (Most Common)
A **closed-top / open-bottom** form suspended from above, with concrete delivered through a vertical tremie pipe that discharges at the **bottom** of the pour. The formwork is *not* dewatered; concrete displaces water as it rises.

### B) Bag-work Formwork
Used for **irregular rock surfaces** or seal courses. **Fabric bags** (jute, polypropylene, or nylon) are filled with **mortar or grout** and stacked by divers to build the *form itself*. The bag is the form *and* a part of the finished concrete.

### C) Pre-packed Aggregate (Intrusion) Formwork
Coarse aggregate is placed inside the formwork *first*, then a **cement-sand grout** is injected from the bottom up through **pre-embedded grout pipes**. The grout displaces water and fills the voids between pre-placed aggregates.

### D) Caisson Formwork
Large **watertight box** (open at top and bottom) that is **sunk into position** by its own weight or by kentledge. The bottom is then sealed by **tremie concrete**, after which the caisson is dewatered and the inner concrete placed in the dry.

### E) Slurry-Wall (Diaphragm Wall) Formwork
A trench is excavated under **bentonite slurry**, which stabilises the walls. Concrete is placed by tremie, **displacing the slurry**, forming a structural wall *in situ*.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Philosophy

### Step 1 – Identify the Governing Load Case
Before any formwork is fabricated, the engineer must ask: **"Is the formwork open to water on one or both faces?"**
- **Single-face exposure (typical):** Outer face → water; Inner face → fresh concrete.
- **Double-face exposure:** Formwork fully submerged and filled (e.g., hollow box caisson).

### Step 2 – Compute Hydrostatic Pressure
The lateral hydrostatic pressure at any depth $h$ below the water surface is **linear** with depth.

### Step 3 – Compute Differential Concrete Pressure
Because concrete is heavier than water, the *net* inward pressure on the formwork face equals the difference between the two fluid heads.

### Step 4 – Account for Buoyancy
If the formwork is **sealed**, the entire enclosed air volume experiences an **upward buoyant force** equal to the weight of displaced water.

### Step 5 – Check for Wash-out & Segregation
Concrete that is dropped through water loses cement fines. Therefore, **never let concrete fall freely** through water – always use tremie, pump, or pre-packed grout.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam favourites** for PECET742. Master the units – the KTU examiner often deducts marks for unit inconsistency.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth | $P = \rho_w g h$ | $\rho_w$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h$ in m | Pressure on outer face of submerged form |
| 2 | Hydrostatic force per metre width | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w g h^2$ | $F$ in N/m | Total lateral load on a vertical panel of height $h$ |
| 3 | Differential concrete pressure | $P_{diff} = (\rho_c - \rho_w) g h$ | $\rho_c \approx 2400$ kg/m³ | Net inward pressure when form is filled with fresh concrete |
| 4 | Buoyant force on sealed form | $F_b = \rho_w \, g \, V$ | $V$ = displaced volume in m³ | Uplift on closed formwork – must be resisted by tie-downs |
| 5 | Current-induced drag | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w v^2 C_d A$ | $v$ = velocity (m/s), $C_d$ ≈ 1.2 for blunt forms | Hydrodynamic load in rivers / tidal zones |
| 6 | Required tie-down force | $T = F_b - W_{form} - W_{conc}$ | All in kN | Tension in anchor bolts or kentledge weight |
| 7 | Tremie pipe discharge velocity | $v = \sqrt{2gH}$ | $H$ = head of concrete in pipe | Ensures self-flowing grout at outlet |
| 8 | Initial tremie charge volume | $V_0 = \tfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 (L + h)$ | $D$ = pipe dia, $L$ = pipe length, $h$ = embedment | Volume of plug concrete to seal pipe bottom |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The principles of underwater formwork are not academic – they govern the safety of every **Kerala backwater bridge** built on pile foundations, the **Vizhinjam International Seaport** breakwater, and the **Kochi Metro** underground stations built using slurry walls. Civil engineers who master this topic directly influence the constructability of India's coastal infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Net Lateral Pressure on a Tremie-Filled Form

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A vertical formwork panel is submerged in **fresh water** to a depth of **6 m**. Concrete is being placed by tremie from the bottom. Derive the *net* lateral pressure on the formwork face at depths of 0 m, 3 m, and 6 m below the waterline.

### Step 1 – Write the pressure equation on the OUTER face
The outer face is in contact with **stationary water** of density $\rho_w$.

$$
P_{out}(h) = \rho_w \, g \, h
$$

Substituting standard values:

$$
P_{out}(h) = (1000 \text{ kg/m}^3)(9.81 \text{ m/s}^2)(h)
$$

$$
P_{out}(h) = 9810 \, h \;\; \text{Pa}
$$

### Step 2 – Write the pressure equation on the INNER face
The inner face is in contact with **fresh concrete** of density $\rho_c$. Because the concrete is fluid during placement, it exerts fluid-like hydrostatic pressure (using $\gamma_c = \rho_c g$).

$$
P_{in}(h) = \rho_c \, g \, h
$$

$$
P_{in}(h) = (2400)(9.81)(h) = 23544 \, h \;\; \text{Pa}
$$

### Step 3 – Compute the NET inward pressure
The formwork panel is pushed **outward** by water on the outside and pushed **inward** by concrete on the inside. The net pressure on the form is:

$$
P_{net}(h) = P_{in}(h) - P_{out}(h) = (\rho_c - \rho_w) g h
$$

Substituting the densities:

$$
P_{net}(h) = (2400 - 1000)(9.81)(h) = (1400)(9.81)(h)
$$

$$
P_{net}(h) = 13734 \, h \;\; \text{Pa}
$$

### Step 4 – Tabulate the net pressure at required depths

| Depth $h$ (m) | $P_{out}$ (kPa) | $P_{in}$ (kPa) | $P_{net}$ (kPa) |
|:-------------:|:----------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 3 | 29.43 | 70.63 | **41.20** |
| 6 | 58.86 | 141.26 | **82.40** |

### Step 5 – Engineering interpretation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> At the **base of a 6 m form**, the formwork is pushed **inward** by **82.4 kPa** (≈ 0.82 bar). This is the design pressure for the formwork panel and its bracing. **This is roughly half** of what a 6 m land-based formwork would experience (which is governed by concrete alone, ≈ 141 kPa). Hence underwater formwork is **structurally lighter** but **buoyancy-critical**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Buoyancy Tie-Down Force for a Sealed Caisson

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A hollow steel formwork box (dimensions **4 m × 3 m × 3 m high**) is sealed at the top and lowered into seawater ($\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³). The empty form weighs **45 kN**. Determine the **minimum downward force** that anchor bolts must provide so the form does not float.

### Step 1 – Compute the displaced volume
When fully submerged, the form displaces its **external volume**:

$$
V = L \times B \times H = 4 \times 3 \times 3 = 36 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 2 – Compute the buoyant force

$$
F_b = \rho_w \, g \, V
$$

$$
F_b = (1025)(9.81)(36)
$$

$$
F_b = 361{,}989 \text{ N} \approx 362 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 3 – Write the vertical equilibrium equation
For the form not to float, the **sum of downward forces** must equal or exceed the buoyant force.

$$
W_{form} + T_{anchors} \geq F_b
$$

$$
T_{anchors} \geq F_b - W_{form}
$$

$$
T_{anchors} \geq 362 - 45 = 317 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 4 – Design the anchor system
If we use **4 anchor bolts** (one at each corner), each bolt must resist:

$$
T_{bolt} = \frac{317}{4} = 79.25 \text{ kN}
$$

Choosing a safety factor of **2.0** (KTU recommended for temporary works):

$$
T_{design} = 2 \times 79.25 = 158.5 \text{ kN per bolt}
$$

A standard **M36 grade 8.8** bolt (ultimate tensile capacity ≈ 510 kN) is more than adequate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Students often use the **density of fresh water (1000 kg/m³)** even for seawater problems. Always read the problem – Kerala backwaters and Arabian Sea are **seawater**, not fresh water.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Initial Tremie Charge Volume

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A tremie pipe of **250 mm diameter** and **12 m length** is to be used. The pipe outlet must embed into fresh concrete by at least **1.5 m**. Determine the initial plug volume required to seal the pipe and start the pour.

### Step 1 – Total length to be filled with concrete plug
The plug must fill the entire pipe length plus the embedded portion already in the pour:

$$
L_{total} = L_{pipe} + h_{embedment}
$$

$$
L_{total} = 12 + 1.5 = 13.5 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 – Cross-sectional area of the tremie pipe

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.250)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3 – Required plug volume

$$
V_0 = A \times L_{total} = 0.04909 \times 13.5
$$

$$
V_0 = 0.663 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 4 – Convert to practical batching units

$$
V_0 = 0.663 \text{ m}^3 \approx 0.66 \text{ m}^3
$$

This is the **volume of the very first batch** of concrete (the "go-devil" or plug) that must be placed to seal the tremie. It is typically **15–20% richer** than the design mix to ensure a watertight seal.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Underwater Formwork Methods

| Criterion | Tremie Formwork | Bag-Work | Pre-packed Aggregate | Caisson | Slurry Wall |
|:----------|:----------------|:---------|:---------------------|:--------|:------------|
| **Water depth suitability** | ≤ 50 m | ≤ 30 m | ≤ 50 m | Unlimited (sinks itself) | ≤ 60 m |
| **Concrete quality** | High (anti-washout needed) | Moderate (mortar) | Excellent (no segregation) | High (after dewatering) | High |
| **Skilled labour** | Moderate | **High (divers)** | High | High | **Very high** |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | High |
| **Speed of construction** | Fast | Slow | Medium | Slow | Medium |
| **Typical KTU application** | Pile cap, quay wall base | Irregular rock seal | Mass concrete under water | Bridge pier foundation | Metro diaphragm wall |
| **Tolerance on shape** | ± 25 mm | ± 100 mm | ± 50 mm | ± 15 mm | ± 50 mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Tremie Formwork Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of materials, forces, and construction steps in a typical tremie-based underwater formwork system. (This block-level mapping is used because a literal cross-section drawing is best drawn on paper; here we capture all functional interactions.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Survey and Bed Preparation] --> B[Fabricate Formwork Panel with Tie-Down Lugs]
    B --> C[Lower Formwork to Riverbed Using Crane]
    C --> D[Anchor Form to Bed Piles or Deadmen]
    D --> E[Install Tremie Pipe Inside Form]
    E --> F[Place Initial Plug Concrete Volume 0.66 cubic metre]
    F --> G[Lift Tremie Slightly to Release Plug]
    G --> H[Concrete Rises and Displaces Water]
    H --> I[Maintain Embedment 1.0 to 1.5 metre]
    I --> J[Continue Tremie Pour in Steady Lifts]
    J --> K[Formwork Filled to Design Level]
    K --> L[Hold Form 7 Days for Concrete Gain]
    L --> M[Strike Formwork and Inspect Surface]

    P[Hydrostatic Pressure from Outside] -.-> C
    Q[Buoyancy Uplift Force] -.-> D
    R[Anti-Washout Admixture in Mix] -.-> F
    S[Diver Inspection at Each Stage] -.-> H
    T[Cement Grout Loss to Water if Embedment Lapses] -.-> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Solid arrows = sequential construction flow. Dotted arrows = governing **loads / risks** that act in parallel at every stage. KTU examiners often ask students to identify the *critical control points* in such a flow – here they are nodes **D, F, and I**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram – Comparison of Underwater Formwork Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Underwater Formwork] --> A[Tremie Formwork]
    Root --> B[Bag Work Formwork]
    Root --> C[Pre Packed Aggregate Formwork]
    Root --> D[Caisson Formwork]
    Root --> E[Slurry Wall Formwork]

    A --> A1[Concrete poured from bottom through pipe]
    B --> B1[Fabric bags stacked by divers as form and seal]
    C --> C1[Coarse aggregate placed first, grout injected later]
    D --> D1[Watertight box sunk into position, dewatered]
    E --> E1[Trench stabilised by bentonite slurry, filled by tremie]

    A1 --> A2[Best for pile caps and bridge piers]
    B1 --> B2[Best for irregular rock surfaces]
    C1 --> C2[Best for mass concrete pours]
    D1 --> D2[Best for deep water foundations]
    E1 --> E2[Best for metro and basement walls]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Force Diagram – Net Pressure on a Tremie Form

```
         WATER SURFACE
         |    |    |
         |    |    |
         |    |    |     P_out (water) increases linearly
         |    |    |         ↓ pushing OUTWARD
         |    v    |     P_in  (concrete) increases linearly
         |   ===   |         ↑ pushing INWARD
         |   ===   |
         |   ===   |     Net pressure = P_in − P_out
         |   ===   |                   = (ρ_c − ρ_w) g h
         |   ===   |                   = 13.73 kPa per metre depth
         |   ===   |
         |_________|
         BED LEVEL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how the net pressure is **constant rate of increase with depth**. This is the *defining* characteristic that distinguishes underwater formwork design from land-based formwork, where concrete pressure follows the **ACI 347 / CIRIA 108** bilinear curves.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define underwater formwork. List any four situations in civil engineering where it is used.**
*(Model Answer – 3 marks)*

> **Definition (1 mark):** Underwater formwork is a temporary mould or retaining structure that holds freshly placed concrete in its desired shape while it sets, when the construction interface is **below the water table** or submerged in a body of water.
>
> **Any four situations (2 marks – ½ mark each):**
> 1. Pile caps and pier shafts of **bridges across rivers or backwaters**
> 2. **Cofferdam** base seals for pumphouses and intake structures
> 3. **Quay walls and dock structures** in harbours
> 4. **Diaphragm walls** of underground metro stations
> 5. **Intake / outfall tunnels** of hydro-power and desalination plants

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is a tremie pipe? Why is the tremie pipe outlet always kept embedded 1.0–1.5 m into the fresh concrete during underwater concreting?**
*(Model Answer – 3 marks)*

> **Tremie pipe (1 mark):** A tremie pipe is a **vertical, watertight conduit** (typically 200–300 mm diameter steel or HDPE pipe) with a hopper at the top, used to place concrete **below water level** without it coming into contact with the water.
>
> **Reason for embedment (2 marks):**
> 1. To **prevent the entry of water** into the pipe, which would cause wash-out of cement and segregation of aggregates.
> 2. To ensure that the **rising concrete column** inside the formwork pushes the water *upward and outward* rather than mixing with it.
> 3. It maintains a **continuous hydrostatic seal** so the concrete flows out only at the bottom of the pipe as a coherent mass.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 4 Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **principle and procedure of tremie method** of underwater concreting with a neat functional diagram. Discuss the role of **anti-washout admixtures** in the mix design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hollow **sealed timber formwork box** measuring **3 m × 2 m × 2 m high** is to be lowered into a river of fresh water. The empty form weighs **18 kN**. Compute the **minimum total downward tie-down force** required to keep the form submerged at the design depth, using a **factor of safety of 1.5**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) – 7 marks

**Principle (3 marks):**
The tremie method works on the principle that **concrete is placed at the bottom of the pour** and **rises upward by displacement** of the water above. Because the concrete is discharged *below* the rising concrete surface, it never falls freely through water and is not subject to wash-out. The tremie pipe remains **continuously filled with concrete** throughout the pour, acting as a **hydraulic seal** against the surrounding water.

**Procedure (3 marks) – numbered steps:**

1. **Erect and anchor** the formwork at the bed location, ensuring it is rigid and watertight.
2. **Position the tremie pipe** vertically such that its outlet rests on the bed of the formwork.
3. **Introduce the initial plug** (a slightly richer, cohesive concrete batch) at the top hopper until it travels down the pipe and seals the bottom outlet.
4. **Lift the pipe slightly** (≈ 100–150 mm) to release the plug into the formwork.
5. **Maintain continuous concrete supply** so the pipe remains full at all times.
6. **Keep the pipe outlet embedded 1.0–1.5 m** into the freshly placed concrete to prevent water ingress.
7. **Withdraw the tremie gradually** as the concrete level rises, never exposing the outlet to water.
8. **Continue until the form is filled** to the design level, then hold position for 7 days before striking.

**Role of anti-washout admixtures (1 mark):**
Admixtures such as **cellulose ethers, polyacrylamide, or modified starches** increase the **cohesion and viscosity** of the fresh concrete, preventing cement fines from being washed out by water. They improve the **resistance to segregation** and allow concrete to retain its designed strength even after placement through water.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) – 7 marks

**Given:**
- Form dimensions: 3 m × 2 m × 2 m
- $\rho_w$ (fresh water) = 1000 kg/m³
- $W_{form} = 18$ kN
- Factor of safety (FoS) = 1.5

**Step 1 – Displaced volume of the form (1 mark):**

$$
V = 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 – Buoyant force (1 mark):**

$$
F_b = \rho_w \, g \, V = (1000)(9.81)(12)
$$

$$
F_b = 117{,}720 \text{ N} \approx 117.72 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 – Net uplift force (1 mark):**

$$
F_{uplift} = F_b - W_{form} = 117.72 - 18 = 99.72 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 – Apply factor of safety (1 mark):**

$$
T_{design} = FoS \times F_{uplift} = 1.5 \times 99.72
$$

$$
T_{design} = 149.58 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 – Distribute to anchor bolts (1 mark):**
If **4 anchor bolts** are used, each must carry:

$$
T_{bolt} = \frac{149.58}{4} \approx 37.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 – Select bolt size (1 mark):**
A standard **M20 grade 8.8** bolt has an ultimate tensile capacity of ≈ 125 kN, well above the 37.4 kN demand. So **4 nos. M20 bolts** are adequate.

**Step 7 – Result statement (1 mark):**
> **The minimum total downward tie-down force is 149.58 kN, which can be provided by 4 anchor bolts of M20 grade 8.8 capacity.**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **pre-packed aggregate (intrusion grouting) method** of underwater construction. Compare its advantages and limitations with the **tremie method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **tremie pipe of 300 mm diameter** and **15 m length** is to be used for concreting a pile cap. Calculate: **(i)** the **initial plug volume** required, and **(ii)** the **outlet discharge velocity** of the grout if the concrete head in the pipe is **18 m**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) – 7 marks

**Description of Pre-packed Aggregate Method (4 marks):**

1. The formwork is erected and **coarse aggregate (20–40 mm)** is first placed inside the form, either by skip or by tremie.
2. A network of **grout injection pipes** (typically 25–40 mm diameter) is pre-positioned, with outlets reaching the bottom of the aggregate mass.
3. A **cement-sand grout** (with water-cement ratio ≈ 0.45 and plasticiser for flowability) is then **pumped from the bottom upward** through these pipes.
4. The grout **displaces water upward** and **intrudes** into the voids between the pre-placed aggregates.
5. The pour is continued until grout emerges at the surface, indicating full filling.
6. The result is a **dense, homogeneous concrete** with no segregation because the aggregates are never dropped through water.

**Comparison with Tremie Method (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tremie Method | Pre-packed Aggregate |
|:-------|:--------------|:---------------------|
| Aggregate placement | With concrete (in mix) | Placed first as a mass |
| Cement wash-out risk | Moderate (controlled by admixtures) | Very low |
| Strength of finished concrete | Good | **Superior (less voids)** |
| Equipment complexity | Lower (single tremie) | Higher (grout plant + injection pipes) |
| Speed of pour | Faster | Slower |
| Best suited to | Moderate depths, normal concrete | **Mass concrete, deep foundations** |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) – 7 marks

**Given:**
- Tremie diameter $D = 300$ mm = 0.300 m
- Pipe length $L = 15$ m
- Embedment $h_{emb}$ = 1.5 m (assumed, as not specified, KTU standard)
- Concrete head $H = 18$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (i) – Initial plug volume (3 marks):**

Step 1: Cross-sectional area of pipe:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.300)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 2: Total length to be filled with plug:

$$
L_{total} = L + h_{emb} = 15 + 1.5 = 16.5 \text{ m}
$$

Step 3: Plug volume:

$$
V_0 = A \times L_{total} = 0.07069 \times 16.5
$$

$$
V_0 = 1.166 \text{ m}^3 \approx 1.17 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Part (ii) – Outlet discharge velocity (4 marks):**

Step 1: Apply Torricelli's law (idealised, neglecting friction):

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

Step 2: Substitute values:

$$
v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 18}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{353.16}
$$

$$
v \approx 18.79 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 3: Engineering comment (for full marks):
This theoretical velocity is high; in practice, **friction losses in the pipe and grout viscosity** reduce the actual outlet velocity to **8–12 m/s**. The high theoretical value confirms that the tremie flow is **self-sustaining** once started, provided the hopper is kept full.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the density differential** in net pressure calculations (writing $P = \rho_c g h$ instead of $(\rho_c - \rho_w) g h$). This costs **2 marks** in most valuation scripts.
> 2. **Mixing up fresh water and seawater densities** – Kerala coast means seawater unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Skipping the factor of safety** in buoyancy tie-down design – KTU always expects FoS to be **explicitly stated** (typically 1.5–2.0).
> 4. **Not mentioning tremie embedment** (1.0–1.5 m) when describing the tremie procedure – this single line often fetches the difference between 5 and 7 marks.
> 5. **Units inconsistency** – using $g = 9.81$ m/s² with density in kg/m³ to get force in **N** but reporting the final answer in **kN** without conversion. Always show: $F$ (N) $\div 1000$ = $F$ (kN).

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Underwater Formwork**

- **Definition** – Formwork that retains concrete placed below the waterline; works against hydrostatic pressure and buoyancy.
- **Five main types** – Tremie, Bag-work, Pre-packed aggregate, Caisson, Slurry wall.
- **Tremie principle** – Place concrete at the *bottom*, let it rise by displacement; pipe outlet always **embedded 1.0–1.5 m** in fresh concrete.
- **Hydrostatic pressure** – $P = \rho_w g h$; linear with depth; gradient **10 kPa/m** in fresh water, **10.25 kPa/m** in seawater.
- **Net inward pressure on tremie-filled form** – $P_{net} = (\rho_c - \rho_w) g h = 13.73 \, h$ kPa (where $h$ is in metres) – this is the design pressure.
- **Buoyancy tie-down force** – $T = (FoS) \times (F_b - W_{form})$, with **FoS = 1.5–2.0**; $F_b = \rho_w g V$.
- **Initial tremie plug volume** – $V_0 = (\pi D^2 / 4) \times (L + h_{emb})$; typically **15–20% richer** mix.
- **Anti-washout admixtures** – Cellulose ethers / polyacrylamide; prevent cement wash-out through water.
- **Materials** – Plywood-faced timber, steel plate, fibreglass-reinforced plastic (FRP) for curved surfaces; sheet piles for cofferdams.
- **Curing** – Submerged concrete cures *slower*; **minimum 7 days** before striking formwork, **14 days** for structural load transfer.
- **Quality control** – **Diver inspection** at every stage; **cube samples** taken by tremie at 1 m, 3 m, 5 m depths; **core testing** at 28 days.
- **KTU favourite question types** – (i) Tremie procedure + sketch, (ii) Buoyancy tie-down numerical, (iii) Plug volume numerical, (iv) Comparison of methods.
- **Standard values to commit to memory** – $\rho_c = 24$ kN/m³, $\rho_w = 10$ kN/m³ (fresh), $\rho_w = 10.25$ kN/m³ (sea), tremie Ø = **200–300 mm**, embedment = **1.0–1.5 m**, FoS = **1.5–2.0**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unusual Shapes and Complex Geometries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unusual Shapes and Complex Geometries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unusual Shapes and Complex Geometries** in formwork engineering refer to *non-prismatic, non-orthogonal, free-form, and doubly-curved concrete structures* whose geometry deviates from conventional rectangular/symmetric forms. These include **shells, folded plates, domes, hyperbolic paraboloids (hypars), waffle slabs, ribbed slabs, twisted columns, sculptural façades, and architecturally free-form (NURBS-based) surfaces**, requiring customized, parametric, and often digitally-fabricated formwork systems.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this topic falls under advanced formwork design where the *geometry of the structure dictates the formwork system*, rather than the formwork being a simple repetition of standard panels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Origami vs. The Cardboard Box**
> Think of conventional formwork as a **cardboard box** — flat, rectangular, easy to mass-produce. Now imagine you have to mould concrete into the shape of a **3D origami crane**. You cannot simply bend cardboard; you need to cut, score, laminate, and sometimes 3D-print custom panels. *Unusual shapes in formwork are exactly this transition* — from mass-produced flat panels to **custom, sculpted, often single-use moulds** that capture complex curves, twists, and double curvatures.

## 1.3 Classification of Unusual Shapes

| **Category** | **Typical Examples** | **Curvature Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Shells | Cylindrical, spherical, conical shells | Single curvature (developable) or double curvature (non-developable) |
| Folded Plates | V-shaped, trapezoidal folds | Piecewise planar (developable) |
| Domes | Spherical, ribbed, geodesic | Double curvature (synclastic) |
| Hyperbolic Paraboloids (Hypars) | Saddle-shaped roofs | Double curvature (anticlastic) |
| Free-Form Surfaces | Sculptural façades, blob architecture | Double curvature (free-form NURBS) |
| Special Elements | Twisted columns, tree columns, waffle slabs | Mixed geometry |

## 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

- **Standard unit weight of fresh concrete:** **24 to 25 kN/m³** (IS 875 Part 5 baseline)
- **Standard unit weight of plywood:** **6.5 to 7.5 kN/m³** (moisture-dependent)
- **Minimum radius of curvature** for plywood bending without fracture: **~600 mm** (for 12 mm MR-grade plywood)
- **Standard formwork pressure (max):** up to **95 to 100 kN/m²** for high pour rates
- **Tolerance for complex geometry:** typically **±3 mm to ±6 mm** on surface deviations (per ACI 347)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Unusual Shapes Demand Specialized Formwork

The fundamental engineering challenge in complex geometries is that **standard flat panels cannot capture curvature**. This triggers the following cascading requirements:

1. **Surface discretisation** — A smooth 3D surface (e.g., NURBS surface) must be broken into small flat or slightly-curved panels (faceting).
2. **Custom fabrication** — Each panel is unique; CNC cutting or robotic thermoforming is required.
3. **Load path analysis** — Concrete in a doubly-curved shell acts primarily in *membrane compression*, reducing bending moments but increasing formwork *out-of-plane buckling* risks.
4. **Stripping complexity** — Curved surfaces trap the formwork; stripping must follow the *axis of minimum curvature*.

## 2.2 Types of Complex Geometries — Engineering Logic

### 2.2.1 Shells (Cylindrical, Spherical, Conical)

- **Developable shells (cylindrical, conical):** Can be unrolled onto a flat plane → easier formwork (bent plywood or steel).
- **Non-developable shells (spherical, hypar):** Cannot be unrolled flat → require *segmented* or *flexible membrane* formwork.

### 2.2.2 Folded Plates

- Formed by joining flat slabs at angles along hinge lines.
- Formwork = *planar panels* joined at hinges; structurally efficient, visually striking.

### 2.2.3 Domes

- A doubly-curved surface where both principal curvatures have the **same sign (synclastic)**.
- Formwork options: timber rib-and-lathe, fabric (stretched membrane), CNC-milled EPS (Expanded Polystyrene) lost formwork.

### 2.2.4 Hyperbolic Paraboloids (Hypars)

- Saddle-shaped: one principal curvature is **positive**, the other is **negative** (anticlastic).
- Mathematically defined by the equation: $z = \frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2}$
- Famous example: Felix Candela's *Bacardí Rum Factory* (Mexico, 1960).

### 2.2.5 Free-Form / NURBS Surfaces

- **NURBS = Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines** — the standard mathematical representation for free-form architecture.
- Defined by control points, weights, knots, and degree.
- Formwork fabricated via **robotic 3D printing, CNC milling of EPS foam, or flexible fabric formwork**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are tested regularly in KTU examinations for formwork and structural design courses.

| **#** | **Formula / Parameter** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gaussian Curvature (dome) | $K = \kappa_1 \cdot \kappa_2$ | m⁻² | Classifies shell as synclastic/anticlastic |
| 2 | Mean Curvature | $H = \frac{\kappa_1 + \kappa_2}{2}$ | m⁻¹ | Membrane/shell bending energy |
| 3 | Hyperbolic paraboloid surface | $z = \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2}$ | m | Hypar shell geometry |
| 4 | Shell surface area (sphere) | $A = 4\pi R^2$ | m² | Dome formwork area |
| 5 | Conical surface area (lateral) | $A = \pi r \ell$ where $\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$ | m² | Cone formwork area |
| 6 | Cylindrical surface area (lateral) | $A = 2\pi r h$ | m² | Barrel vault formwork area |
| 7 | Concrete lateral pressure (ACI) | $p = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left( 7.2 + \dfrac{785 R}{T + 17.8} \right)$ | kN/m² | Formwork design pressure |
| 8 | Maximum pressure (limit) | $p_{max} = 0.95 H$ (kN/m²) ... 95 kN/m² max | kN/m² | Vertical pour boundary |
| 9 | Hypar edge length (straight line) | Straight-line hypar: edges are straight; surface is doubly ruled | — | Unique property used in formwork panels |
| 10 | Slenderness of formwork panel | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{t_{min}}$ | — | Determines whether panel needs stiffeners |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why straight-line edges on a hypar matter:** A hyperbolic paraboloid is a *doubly-ruled surface* — meaning it can be generated entirely by straight lines. This **dramatically simplifies formwork**, because flat timber planks can be aligned along these rulings to approximate the curved surface with straight, easy-to-fabricate members.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace hangers & shipyards** use *free-form concrete* for large column-free enclosures.
- **Religious architecture** (mosques, cathedrals) extensively uses *domes* and *hypars* — Formwork engineering is critical.
- **Tensile / fabric formwork** (Werner Sobek, Kennet Snelson) — fabric membranes are inflated; concrete is sprayed over them, achieving complex shapes with minimum material.
- **Bridges** with curved soffits (e.g., banded prestressed bridges) require *curved soffit formwork*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Surface Area of a Hyperbolic Paraboloid (Hypar)

A hypar is defined by:

$$
z = \frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2}
$$

The general surface area of a surface $z = f(x, y)$ over a domain $D$ in the $xy$-plane is:

$$
A = \iint_D \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\right)^2} \, dx \, dy
$$

**Step 1 — Compute partial derivatives:**

$$
\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = \frac{2x}{a^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = -\frac{2y}{b^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the integrand:**

$$
\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2x}{a^2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{2y}{b^2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{4x^2}{a^4} + \frac{4y^2}{b^4}}
$$

**Step 3 — Set up the double integral over a square domain $D = [-L, L] \times [-L, L]$:**

$$
A = \int_{-L}^{L} \int_{-L}^{L} \sqrt{1 + \frac{4x^2}{a^4} + \frac{4y^2}{b^4}} \, dx \, dy
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Engineering Approximation:**

For *shallow hypars* (which is the typical case in architecture), $\frac{4x^2}{a^4}$ and $\frac{4y^2}{b^4}$ are small compared to 1. We can use the binomial approximation $\sqrt{1 + \epsilon} \approx 1 + \frac{\epsilon}{2}$ for small $\epsilon$:

$$
\sqrt{1 + \frac{4x^2}{a^4} + \frac{4y^2}{b^4}} \approx 1 + \frac{2x^2}{a^4} + \frac{2y^2}{b^4}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate term by term:**

$$
A \approx \int_{-L}^{L} \int_{-L}^{L} \left[ 1 + \frac{2x^2}{a^4} + \frac{2y^2}{b^4} \right] dx \, dy
$$

$$
A \approx (2L)(2L) + \frac{2}{a^4} \int_{-L}^{L} x^2 \, dx \int_{-L}^{L} dy + \frac{2}{b^4} \int_{-L}^{L} y^2 \, dy \int_{-L}^{L} dx
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the standard integrals:**

$$
\int_{-L}^{L} dx = 2L \qquad \int_{-L}^{L} x^2 \, dx = \frac{2L^3}{3}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back:**

$$
A \approx 4L^2 + \frac{2}{a^4} \cdot \frac{2L^3}{3} \cdot 2L + \frac{2}{b^4} \cdot \frac{2L^3}{3} \cdot 2L
$$

$$
A \approx 4L^2 + \frac{8L^4}{3a^4} + \frac{8L^4}{3b^4}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 4L^2 \left[ 1 + \frac{2L^2}{3a^4} + \frac{2L^2}{3b^4} \right]}
$$

This gives the **engineering formwork area** of a shallow hypar — used to compute plywood quantity, bracing, and labour estimates.

## 3.2 Derivation: Classification of a Surface by Gaussian Curvature

For a surface $z = f(x, y)$, the *first fundamental form coefficients* are:

$$
E = 1 + \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)^2, \quad F = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}, \quad G = 1 + \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\right)^2
$$

The *second fundamental form coefficients* are:

$$
L = \frac{\partial^2 z / \partial x^2}{\sqrt{1 + z_x^2 + z_y^2}}, \quad M = \frac{\partial^2 z / \partial x \partial y}{\sqrt{\dots}}, \quad N = \frac{\partial^2 z / \partial y^2}{\sqrt{\dots}}
$$

The **Gaussian curvature** is:

$$
K = \frac{LN - M^2}{EG - F^2}
$$

**Step 1 — For a sphere of radius R:** $z = \sqrt{R^2 - x^2 - y^2}$

$$
K = \frac{1}{R^2} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{SYNCLASTIC (dome)}
$$

**Step 2 — For a hypar:** $z = \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2}$

$$
L = \frac{2/a^2}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{4x^2}{a^4} + \frac{4y^2}{b^4}}}, \quad N = \frac{-2/b^2}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{4x^2}{a^4} + \frac{4y^2}{b^4}}}, \quad M = 0
$$

$$
K = \frac{LN - 0}{EG - F^2} = \frac{LN}{EG - F^2} = \frac{\text{negative}}{positive} < 0
$$

$$
\boxed{K_{hypar} < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ANTICLASTIC (saddle)}}
$$

This negative Gaussian curvature is what makes hypar formwork challenging yet formable using *straight-line ruling*.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choice of Formwork for Complex Geometry

| **Geometry Type** | **Curvature Class** | **Recommended Formwork** | **Reuse Cycles** | **Approx. Relative Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cylindrical shell | Single (developable) | Bent plywood, steel panels | High (10–20) | Low |
| Spherical dome | Double (synclastic) | Timber rib + lagging, CNC EPS, fabric | Low (1–3) | High |
| Hyperbolic paraboloid | Double (anticlastic) | Straight timber planks on ruled surface | Medium (3–6) | Medium |
| Folded plate | Piecewise planar | Flat panels at angles | High (15+) | Low |
| Free-form NURBS | Double (free) | CNC EPS, 3D-printed, fabric | Single use | Very High |
| Waffle slab | Cellular | Standard dome moulds (steel/PP) | Very High (50+) | Low (per use) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

The following Mermaid diagram maps the engineering workflow from geometric input to formwork stripping, for unusual shapes:

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 — Similar Pattern]**

### **Q1. What is a hyperbolic paraboloid shell? Why is its formwork relatively easier to construct than a spherical dome of the same span?** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A hyperbolic paraboloid (hypar) is a doubly-curved saddle-shaped surface defined mathematically as $z = \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2}$, with **negative Gaussian curvature** (one principal curvature positive, the other negative). Its key feature relevant to formwork is that it is a **doubly-ruled surface** — meaning the entire curved surface can be generated by *two families of straight lines*. As a result, the formwork can be built using **straight timber planks** aligned along these rulings, eliminating the need for complex curved panels. In contrast, a spherical dome has *positive* Gaussian curvature and **cannot be formed from straight members**, requiring rib-and-lathe, fabric, or CNC-milled EPS formwork. Hence, hypar formwork is simpler for the same span. `[3 marks]`

---

### **Q2. List any three formwork systems suitable for doubly-curved free-form concrete surfaces and briefly state the principle of each.** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **CNC-milled EPS (Expanded Polystyrene) Lost Formwork** — A 3D EPS block is milled to the exact NURBS surface using a robotic cutter, then coated with a thin plywood or fibreglass skin. The EPS remains as permanent insulation.
2. **Fabric / Tensile Membrane Formwork** — A flexible fabric (e.g., PVC-coated polyester) is tensioned over a supporting frame to define the curved surface; concrete is sprayed or poured against it. The fabric's tension creates the desired curvature.
3. **3D-Printed (Additive) Formwork** — Robotic arms extrude a sand/polymer mixture layer-by-layer to build a one-time mould of arbitrary geometry, optimised for minimal material use.

`[3 marks]`

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Pattern, PECET742 Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the classification of *unusual shapes* in concrete construction. Discuss in detail the formwork considerations and construction methods for **shells, domes, and folded plates** with suitable sketches. `[7 marks]`

**(b)** A hyperbolic paraboloid roof is defined by $z = \dfrac{x^2}{25} - \dfrac{y^2}{16}$, with $x \in [-5, 5]$ m and $y \in [-5, 5]$ m. Estimate its **formwork surface area** using the small-curvature binomial approximation. Comment on the *practical engineering implications* of this result. `[7 marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Classification of unusual shapes:**

Unusual shapes in concrete formwork are classified based on **Gaussian curvature ($K$)** into:

1. **Single curvature (developable) surfaces** — $K = 0$. Examples: cylindrical shells, conical shells. Can be unrolled flat.
2. **Double curvature — synclastic** — $K > 0$. Examples: spherical domes, ellipsoidal caps. Non-developable.
3. **Double curvature — anticlastic** — $K < 0$. Examples: hyperbolic paraboloids (hypars), saddle shells.
4. **Piecewise planar / non-curved** — Examples: folded plates, faceted shells.

**Shells — Formwork Considerations:**

- Developable cylindrical shells: use *bent plywood* or *steel curved panels*. Critical concern is the **bending radius vs. minimum curvature** (typically 600 mm for 12 mm plywood).
- Formwork must resist the **out-of-plane buckling load** from the wet concrete.
- Lateral pressure follows ACI 347: $p = C_w C_c \left(7.2 + \dfrac{785R}{T+17.8}\right)$ kN/m².

**Domes — Formwork Considerations:**

- Spherical domes: *non-developable* → require faceted timber rib-and-lathe or *geodesic* formwork.
- Modern practice: **CNC-milled EPS substrate** laminated with 6 mm plywood, giving 1–2 mm surface tolerance.
- Stripping is done by *piecemeal removal* along meridional lines to avoid damage.

**Folded Plates — Formwork Considerations:**

- Composed of flat slabs joined along hinge lines → formwork is *planar panels* connected at angles.
- Economical, reusable, and uses standard plywood.
- Bracing must resist the *lateral thrust* from the inclined slabs. `[7 marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Given:**
$$
z = \frac{x^2}{25} - \frac{y^2}{16}, \quad x \in [-5, 5], \quad y \in [-5, 5]
$$

So $a^2 = 25$ and $b^2 = 16$, with $L = 5$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute the surface area integrand:**

$$
\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} = \frac{2x}{25}, \qquad \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} = -\frac{2y}{16} = -\frac{y}{8}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)^2 = \frac{4x^2}{625}, \qquad \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\right)^2 = \frac{4y^2}{256} = \frac{y^2}{64}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface area integral:**

$$
A = \int_{-5}^{5} \int_{-5}^{5} \sqrt{1 + \frac{4x^2}{625} + \frac{y^2}{64}} \, dx \, dy
$$

**Step 3 — Binomial approximation** (terms are small for $x, y \in [-5, 5]$):

$$
\sqrt{1 + \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2} \approx 1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4x^2}{625} + \frac{y^2}{64}\right) = 1 + \frac{2x^2}{625} + \frac{y^2}{128}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate term by term:**

$$
A \approx \int_{-5}^{5} \int_{-5}^{5} \left( 1 + \frac{2x^2}{625} + \frac{y^2}{128} \right) dx \, dy
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term:**

Term 1: $\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{5} \int_{-5}^{5} 1 \, dx \, dy = 10 \times 10 = 100 \, \text{m}^2$

Term 2: $\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{5} \int_{-5}^{5} \frac{2x^2}{625} \, dx \, dy = \frac{2}{625} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot 5^3}{3} \cdot 10 = \frac{2}{625} \cdot \frac{250}{3} \cdot 10 = \frac{5000}{1875} = 2.667 \, \text{m}^2$

Term 3: $\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{5} \int_{-5}^{5} \frac{y^2}{128} \, dx \, dy = \frac{1}{128} \cdot 10 \cdot \frac{2 \cdot 5^3}{3} = \frac{1}{128} \cdot \frac{2500}{3} \cdot 10 = \frac{25000}{384} = 65.10 \, \text{m}^2$

Wait — let me recheck Term 3:

$$
\int_{-5}^{5} y^2 \, dy = \left[\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-5}^{5} = \frac{125 - (-125)}{3} = \frac{250}{3}
$$

$$
\int_{-5}^{5} dx = 10
$$

$$
\text{Term 3} = \frac{1}{128} \times \frac{250}{3} \times 10 = \frac{2500}{384} \approx 6.51 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Sum:**

$$
A \approx 100 + 2.667 + 6.51 = \boxed{109.18 \, \text{m}^2}
$$

**Practical Engineering Implications:**

1. The flat projection area is **100 m²**, but the actual curved formwork area is **~109.18 m²** — an **overrun of 9.18%**.
2. This 9.18% extra material must be budgeted for plywood, bracing, and labour.
3. The hypar is *shallow* (curvature terms are small) — confirming the validity of the binomial approximation.
4. Formwork design must account for the **straight-line ruling**: the formwork can be built using straight planks, dramatically reducing fabrication cost. `[7 marks]`

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **fabric formwork** and **CNC-milled EPS formwork** systems for free-form concrete. Compare their advantages and limitations for a 30 m diameter sculptural canopy. `[7 marks]`

**(b)** A **spherical dome** of radius $R = 10$ m is to be cast monolithically with a 200 mm thick concrete shell. Estimate: (i) the **total wet concrete weight** on the formwork at peak, and (ii) the **lateral pressure on formwork** assuming a pour rate of $R = 1.5$ m/hr and concrete temperature $T = 27°C$. Use ACI 347 simplified equation. `[7 marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Fabric Formwork:**

A flexible, high-strength fabric (woven PVC, PTFE, or aramid) is stretched or draped over a *primary support frame* to form the curved mould surface. Concrete is sprayed (shotcrete) or poured against the fabric; the fabric's pre-tension defines the curvature.

*Advantages:*
- Extremely lightweight; minimal material.
- Adapts to *any doubly-curved* surface.
- Reusable for up to 20–30 cycles.
- Excellent surface finish (smooth, low porosity).

*Limitations:*
- Requires careful tensioning to control shape.
- Sensitive to wind during erection.
- Limited to *thin shell* applications (≤300 mm).

**CNC-Milled EPS Formwork:**

A digital 3D model (NURBS) is fed to a multi-axis CNC hot-wire cutter, which sculpts a large block of Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) into the exact negative of the desired surface. A thin plywood or GFRP skin is laminated over the EPS, which then acts as a mould.

*Advantages:*
- Captures *any* free-form geometry with sub-millimetre precision.
- EPS remains as permanent thermal insulation — a *composite form-and-insulation* system.
- Reduces dead load on the supporting falsework.

*Limitations:*
- Single-use (EPS is not recovered).
- Higher initial CNC cost.
- EPS off-cuts create waste management issues.

**Comparison for 30 m sculptural canopy:**

For a 30 m diameter canopy with intricate doubly-curved geometry, **CNC-milled EPS** is preferred because it gives *sub-millimetre precision* and the EPS adds insulation value, which fabric alone cannot. Fabric formwork is more suitable for *repetitive shell* structures where the form can be reused. `[7 marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Given:**
- Radius $R_{dome} = 10$ m
- Shell thickness $t = 200$ mm = 0.20 m
- Pour rate $R = 1.5$ m/hr
- Concrete temperature $T = 27°C$
- Unit weight of concrete $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³

**Part (i) — Total wet concrete weight:**

We treat the dome as a thin spherical cap. For a **full hemisphere**, the volume of the wet concrete shell is:

$$
V = 2\pi R^2 \cdot t = 2 \pi (10)^2 (0.20) = 2\pi \times 100 \times 0.20 = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \, \text{m}^3
$$

Total weight:

$$
W = V \cdot \gamma_c = 125.66 \times 24 = \boxed{3015.9 \, \text{kN}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Lateral pressure (ACI 347 simplified):**

The ACI 347 equation:

$$
p = C_w C_c \left[ 7.2 + \frac{785 R}{T + 17.8} \right] \quad \text{kN/m}^2
$$

Take $C_w = 1.0$ (ordinary cement), $C_c = 1.0$ (no retarders):

$$
p = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \left[ 7.2 + \frac{785 \times 1.5}{27 + 17.8} \right]
$$

$$
p = 7.2 + \frac{1177.5}{44.8} = 7.2 + 26.28
$$

$$
p \approx 33.48 \, \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Check upper limit (per ACI):** $p_{max} = 0.95 H$ kN/m², where $H$ is the head of concrete. Since $p < 95$ kN/m², our value governs. Also check:

$$
p_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot R \cdot t_{max} = 24 \times 1.5 \times \text{(effective head)}
$$

Effective head $\approx R \cdot t_{pour}/2$ ... we use the calculated 33.48 kN/m² as the design pressure. `[7 marks]`

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams (PECET742):**
> 1. **Confusing synclastic and anticlastic surfaces.** Always state Gaussian curvature sign explicitly when classifying shells.
> 2. **Forgetting units in pressure formulas.** ACI 347 gives $p$ in **kN/m²**, but the multiplier 785 is calibrated for **°C and m/hr** — converting wrong loses full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the step-by-step integration.** KTU examiners require *every intermediate term* of the double integral to be visible. Skipping from integrand to final answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing sketches.** Even a poor-quality freehand sketch of the hypar, dome, or shell is worth **1 to 2 marks** in part (a) 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Ignoring practical implications.** A pure mathematical answer without engineering comment (cost, constructability, stripping) loses marks in the *application* cognitive level.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Unusual Shapes & Complex Geometries:**

- ☐ A **hyperbolic paraboloid** is defined by $z = \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2}$ and has **negative Gaussian curvature** ($K < 0$).
- ☐ A **spherical dome** has $K > 0$ (synclastic) and is **non-developable**.
- ☐ A **cylindrical shell** is developable (can be unrolled flat) — cheapest formwork for curved surfaces.
- ☐ Hypars are **doubly-ruled** — they can be formed entirely by *straight* timber planks, simplifying formwork.
- ☐ **Formwork area** of a hypar is computed by:
$$
A = \iint_D \sqrt{1 + z_x^2 + z_y^2} \, dx \, dy
$$
- ☐ For shallow shells, use the **binomial approximation** to integrate quickly.
- ☐ **Fabric formwork**, **CNC-milled EPS**, and **3D-printed** moulds are the three primary systems for free-form (NURBS) surfaces.
- ☐ **ACI 347 pressure:** $p = C_w C_c \left[7.2 + \dfrac{785R}{T+17.8}\right]$ kN/m², with $p_{max} = 95$ kN/m².
- ☐ **Minimum bending radius** for 12 mm MR plywood ≈ **600 mm**; tighter curves need kerfed or layered plywood.
- ☐ **Stripping order** for curved formwork: follow the *minimum curvature axis* to avoid wedging and concrete spalling.
- ☐ **Tolerance** for complex geometry finish: **±3 mm to ±6 mm** (per ACI 347).
- ☐ **Free-form architecture** (NURBS) is the modern frontier — driven by BIM (Building Information Modelling) and robotic fabrication.
- ☐ Always state *both* the mathematical classification and the practical constructability in your KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
