topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Formwork for Repair and Rehabilitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Formwork for Repair and Rehabilitation** refers to a specialized category of temporary (and sometimes permanent) moulds, shutters, and supporting assemblies designed to confine, shape, and support repair mortars, grouts, epoxy compounds, micro-concretes, and jacketing concretes used in the **restoration, strengthening, and retrofitting** of existing damaged or deteriorated structural members. Unlike conventional formwork for new construction, repair formwork must conform to irregular, often non-prismatic geometries of distressed members while accommodating **low-slump, thixotropic, or non-shrink repair materials** that develop very high initial head pressures followed by rapid stiffening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET742 – Module 4):** *Formwork for repair works must address access constraints, hydrostatic pressure of high-fluidity grouts, early-age strength gain, bond to substrate, and the unique geometry of distressed members. Reference standards include IS 14687, ACI 546R, and fib Bulletin 102.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Surgical Cast" Analogy:** Think of formwork for repair and rehabilitation exactly like a **fibreglass cast placed over a broken arm**. The cast (formwork) must:
> 1. Fit the *irregular shape* of the existing arm (no straight factory edge — it's customized to the damaged member).
> 2. Hold the *healing plaster* (repair mortar/grout) tightly against the bone so it sets in the exact contour.
> 3. Allow the doctor to *inject* the healing material from a small opening (grout ports) rather than pouring it from above.
> 4. Be removed after curing without damaging the freshly healed tissue (non-invasive stripping).
>
> Just as a fracture orthopaedic cast differs from a sculpture mould, repair formwork differs from new-construction formwork in geometry, pressure regime, and access logistics.

### 1.3 Why a Dedicated Formwork System is Needed for Repair

Conventional new-construction formwork assumes:
- **Straight, prismatic, repetitive members**
- **Continuous supply of fresh concrete** from the top
- **Predictable lateral pressure** following ACI/IS formulas
- **Accessible soffits and sides**

Repair works violate **all four assumptions** simultaneously. Therefore, repair formwork must be:

| Property | New Construction | Repair & Rehabilitation |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Prismatic, repetitive | Irregular, unique, non-prismatic |
| Material fluidity | Slump 75–150 mm | Thixotropic / flowable / injectable |
| Pressure regime | Predictable (Pmax formula) | High initial, rapid decay (often **>100 kPa**) |
| Access | Full scaffolding | Limited (elevated, confined, overhead) |
| Stripping | After 24–72 hrs | Often within 4–12 hrs (early strength gain) |
| Reusability | 50–100 cycles | Often single-use or 1–3 cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constant to Remember:** The lateral pressure of high-fluidity cementitious grouts (often used in jacketing) can exceed **150 kPa** within the first 30 minutes — significantly higher than the ~50–80 kPa typical for new column pours. This mandates **stronger ties, closer bracing, and pressure-relief vents**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Repair Formwork Systems

#### A. By Function
1. **Containment Formwork** — Holds repair material against the substrate (e.g., column jacketing moulds).
2. **Injection Formwork** — Sealed mould with grout ports for pressure injection of epoxy/polyurethane resins.
3. **Stay-in-Place (SIP) Formwork** — Permanent lost formwork (e.g., GFRP panels, precast concrete shells) that becomes part of the section.
4. **Access & Support Formwork** — Working platforms and needling systems that carry load while the member is repaired.

#### B. By Geometry Adaptability
- **Rigid Panel Formwork** — Plywood, steel, aluminium panels cut/joined to fit irregular members.
- **Flexible Membrane Formwork** — Textile/fabric forms (e.g., **Fabriform**, **Neoform**) inflated or tensioned to create curved or complex shapes around existing members.
- **Modular Clamp Systems** — Proprietary steel collars and clamps (e.g., **Halfen**, **Leviat** systems) for column and beam jacketing.

### 2.2 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Methodology

The "why" and "how" behind repair formwork design follow this structured sequence:

1. **Condition Assessment (Why):** Identify substrate geometry, defect location, crack pattern, and access restrictions. The formwork *must* be tailored because no two distressed members are identical.
2. **Material Selection (Why):** Choose repair material — polymer-modified mortar, epoxy grout, micro-concrete, or shotcrete. Each dictates a different pressure and stripping time.
3. **Pressure & Load Calculation (How):** Compute lateral pressure on formwork using modified equations for thixotropic fluids.
4. **Formwork Fabrication (How):** Cut, assemble, and seal panels — typically **on-site** rather than factory-prefabricated.
5. **Installation (How):** Erect formwork around the distressed member, ensuring a tight seal at the interface to prevent leakage of fluid grout.
6. **Pouring / Injection (How):** Fill via tremie pipes, grout ports, or pump ports from the bottom upward (to displace air).
7. **Curing & Early Stripping (Why):** Strip carefully once repair material reaches sufficient strength — often **24 hrs** for polymer-modified mortars but only **4–6 hrs** for rapid-set epoxies.
8. **Quality Inspection (Why):** Check for voids, honeycombing, and bond at the substrate interface (typically via hammer tap or ultrasonic pulse velocity).

### 2.3 Lateral Pressure of Repair Materials (Thixotropic Fluids)

Conventional ACI/IS lateral pressure formulas (e.g., the ACI 347 / CIRIA 108 equations) are calibrated for **normal-weight, plastic concrete** placed at conventional rates. Repair materials are often:

- **Highly thixotropic** (gel-like at rest, fluid when agitated)
- **Non-shrink** (contain expansive additives)
- **Polymer-modified** (different density and viscosity)

For these, the effective lateral pressure on formwork is given by a **modified hydrostatic expression capped by a maximum design pressure**:

$$
P_{max} = \min \left( \rho \cdot g \cdot h, \; P_{cap} \right)
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of repair material (typically **2000–2300 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = effective head of fluid repair material (m)
- $P_{cap}$ = empirical cap based on thixotropy and setting time

For **cementitious grouts with high fluidity and retarded set**, CIRIA PR91 recommends:

$$
P_{max} = C_1 \cdot \sqrt{R} + C_2 \cdot K \cdot \sqrt{H}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = rate of placement (m/hr)
- $H$ = hydrostatic head (m)
- $K$ = temperature coefficient
- $C_1, C_2$ = empirical constants (**28 and 8** respectively, in kPa units, for retarded grouts)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for KTU Board Exam — Module 4, Repair Formwork:**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | kPa | Lower-bound pressure for any fluid grout |
| 2 | CIRIA PR91 (Grout) | $P_{max} = 28\sqrt{R} + 8 K \sqrt{H}$ | kPa | Retarded cementitious grouts |
| 3 | Thixotropic Cap | $P_{cap} \leq 150$ | kPa | Maximum allowed design pressure |
| 4 | Grout Column Self-Weight | $W = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$ | kN | Vertical load on bottom formwork |
| 5 | Tie Force | $F_t = P_{max} \cdot A_{panel} / n_{ties}$ | kN | Sizing of form ties |
| 6 | Stripping Time (Polymer Mortar) | $t_{strip} \approx 24$ | hours | Polymer-modified cementitious |
| 7 | Stripping Time (Epoxy Grout) | $t_{strip} \approx 4–6$ | hours | Rapid-set epoxy systems |
| 8 | Pressure Relief Vent Spacing | $s_v \leq 1.5$ | m | Maximum vent spacing on sealed forms |
| 9 | Minimum Substrate Bond Strength | $\geq 1.7$ | MPa | Per IS 14687 / ACI 546R |
| 10 | Jacketing Thickness (Column) | $t_j \geq 75$ | mm | Minimum per IS 15988 |

> [!WARNING]
> In all the formulas above, vertical bars for absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written using **\vert** to preserve Markdown table integrity. **Never use a bare pipe inside a formula inside a table.**

### 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

Repair formwork is critical in:
- **Heritage Conservation** — restoring heritage RCC structures without altering aesthetics.
- **Seismic Retrofitting** — column jacketing, beam wrapping, and shear wall thickening.
- **Marine & Bridge Rehabilitation** — pile jacketing, pier encapsulation against chloride attack.
- **Industrial Plant Maintenance** — restoring columns and beams in running factories where downtime is expensive.
- **Underwater Repair** — fabric formwork inflated by divers for pile encapsulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Modified Lateral Pressure for Thixotropic Repair Grout

The standard ACI 347 equation for fresh concrete lateral pressure is:

$$
P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \left( 7.2 + \frac{785 R}{T + 17.8} \right) \quad \text{(kPa)}
$$

This is calibrated for slump **≤ 175 mm** and density **≈ 2400 kg/m³**. For repair grouts with **slump > 200 mm** and density **< 2200 kg/m³** (lightweight polymer-modified systems), it over-predicts. The corrected form is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Begin with the hydrostatic upper bound:**

$$
P_{hydro} = \rho_{grout} \cdot g \cdot h_{head}
$$

Insert numerical values for a typical thixotropic grout at 12 m head and $\rho = 2100 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$
P_{hydro} = 2100 \times 9.81 \times 12 = 247{,}212 \text{ Pa} = 247.2 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thixotropy reduction factor $\phi_t$:**

Thixotropic grouts develop internal structure (gel network) rapidly. The effective lateral pressure decays as:

$$
P_{eff}(t) = P_{hydro} \cdot \phi_t(t), \qquad \phi_t(t) = e^{-k_t t}
$$

where $k_t$ is the structural build-up rate (typically **0.05–0.15 min⁻¹**).

At $t = 30$ minutes with $k_t = 0.08$:

$$
\phi_t(30) = e^{-0.08 \times 30} = e^{-2.4} \approx 0.0907
$$

$$
P_{eff}(30) = 247.2 \times 0.0907 \approx 22.4 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the CIRIA PR91 empirical formula:**

$$
P_{CIRIA} = 28\sqrt{R} + 8 K \sqrt{H}
$$

Assume placement rate $R = 2 \text{ m/hr}$, temperature coefficient $K = 1.0$, head $H = 12$ m:

$$
P_{CIRIA} = 28\sqrt{2} + 8(1.0)\sqrt{12}
$$

$$
P_{CIRIA} = 28 \times 1.414 + 8 \times 3.464
$$

$$
P_{CIRIA} = 39.59 + 27.71 = 67.30 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the governing design pressure:**

$$
P_{design} = \max \left( P_{eff}(t), \; P_{CIRIA} \right) = \max(22.4, \; 67.3) = 67.3 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor (KTU/KTU Board expects SF = 1.5 for temporary formwork):**

$$
P_{ult} = 1.5 \times 67.3 = 100.95 \text{ kPa} \approx 101 \text{ kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Despite a hydrostatic potential of **247 kPa**, the thixotropic behaviour reduces the actual pressure dramatically. However, the CIRIA empirical lower bound governs, and after applying SF 1.5, the design pressure is **~101 kPa** — still higher than conventional concrete (typically 50–80 kPa).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Formwork Selection Decision Tree

The following matrix helps select the correct repair formwork type based on field conditions:

| Repair Scenario | Geometry | Material | Access | Recommended Formwork |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Column jacketing (rectangular) | Prismatic | Micro-concrete | Open | **Modular steel clamp + plywood panels** |
| Column jacketing (circular) | Curved | Cementitious grout | Open | **Fabriform fabric / GFRP stay-in-place** |
| Beam soffit strengthening | Flat soffit | Polymer mortar | Overhead | **Suspended plywood + propping** |
| Pile underwater repair | Irregular cylindrical | Epoxy grout | Submerged | **Fabric formwork inflated by diver** |
| Wall patch repair | Small area | Hand-applied mortar | Open | **Hand-formed timber boards** |
| Seismic shear wall thickening | Vertical large area | Sprayed concrete | Scaffolding | **Shotcrete against single-side mesh** |
| Heritage column restoration | Irregular ornate | Lime-pozzolana mortar | Restricted | **Custom carved timber / 3D-printed moulds** |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Tie Force Calculation for Column Jacketing

A square column **400 × 400 mm** is to be jacketed with **75 mm thick** micro-concrete. Formwork panels are **1.0 m high** and held by **2 horizontal ties per panel** at top and bottom.

**Given:**
- Micro-concrete density $\rho = 2200 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Placement rate $R = 1.5 \text{ m/hr}$
- Temperature $T = 25°C$, so $K = 1.0$
- Effective head $H = 0.5 \text{ m}$ (single lift)

**Step 1 — CIRIA pressure:**

$$
P = 28\sqrt{1.5} + 8(1.0)\sqrt{0.5}
$$

$$
P = 28 \times 1.225 + 8 \times 0.707 = 34.30 + 5.66 = 39.96 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Hydrostatic check:**

$$
P_{hydro} = 2200 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 / 1000 = 10.79 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Governing value:**

$$
P_{design} = \max(39.96, 10.79) = 39.96 \text{ kPa} \approx 40 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Tributary area per tie:**

Each tie carries the pressure over a tributary width. For a 1.0 m high panel with 2 vertical ties (top + bottom), and assuming horizontal tie spacing of 0.5 m:

$$
A_{trib} = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Force per tie (working load):**

$$
F_{tie} = P_{design} \times A_{trib} = 40 \times 0.5 = 20 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — With safety factor 1.5:**

$$
F_{tie,ult} = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Select ties with **working capacity ≥ 30 kN** — typically **M16 high-tensile ties** with **32 kN safe working load**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Repair Formwork System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Distressed Structural Member] --> B[Condition Assessment and Surface Preparation]
    B --> C[Substrate Cleaning and Roughening]
    C --> D[Selection of Repair Material]
    D --> D1[Polymer Modified Mortar]
    D --> D2[Micro Concrete]
    D --> D3[Epoxy Grout]
    D --> D4[Shotcrete]
    D1 --> E[Formwork Selection]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1[Rigid Plywood Panels]
    E --> E2[Fabric Membrane Forms]
    E --> E3[Stay in Place GFRP]
    E --> E4[Modular Steel Clamps]
    E1 --> F[Erection and Sealing]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Grout Ports and Pressure Relief Vents]
    G --> H[Material Placement by Tremie or Pump]
    H --> I[Curing and Strength Development]
    I --> J[Formwork Stripping at Design Time]
    J --> K[Quality Inspection via Hammer Tap or UPV]
    K --> L[Completed Repair Element]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Column Jacketing with Fabric Formwork

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step A: Expose Reinforcement] --> stepB[Step B: Clean Rust and Apply Bonding Agent]
    stepB --> stepC[Step C: Wrap Fabric Form Around Column]
    stepC --> stepD[Step D: Seal Vertical Joints with Zip Ties]
    stepD --> stepE[Step E: Insert Grout Hoses at Base]
    stepE --> stepF[Step F: Pump Grout from Bottom Upward]
    stepF --> stepG[Step G: Monitor Pressure and Displacement]
    stepG --> stepH[Step H: Allow 24 Hour Cure]
    stepH --> stepI[Step I: Strip or Leave as Stay in Place]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Pressure Distribution on Repair Formwork vs Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Time Zero: Hydrostatic Pressure Peak] --> P2[5 min: Rapid Decay Begins]
    P2 --> P3[30 min: Thixotropic Gel Forms]
    P3 --> P4[2 hr: Pressure Approaches CIRIA Empirical Bound]
    P4 --> P5[24 hr: Concrete Sets Pressure Negligible]
    P5 --> P6[Strip Formwork After Strength Check]
```

### 4.4 Section Detail: Typical Column Jacketing Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    core[Original Distressed Column 400 x 400 mm] --> rough[Substrate Roughened to Amplitude 5 mm]
    rough --> bond[Bonding Slurry Applied]
    bond --> jacket[75 mm Micro Concrete Jacket]
    jacket --> fabric[Fabric or Plywood Formwork]
    fabric --> ties[Form Ties at 500 mm Centres]
    fabric --> vents[Pressure Relief Vent at Top]
    fabric --> port[Grout Port at Bottom]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*List any three differences between formwork used for new construction and formwork used for repair and rehabilitation works.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark per Point):**

1. **Geometry:** New construction formwork handles regular, prismatic, repetitive members, whereas repair formwork must be custom-fitted to **irregular, non-prismatic, distressed member geometries**.
2. **Pressure Regime:** New construction follows ACI/CIRIA standard pressure curves (~50–80 kPa), whereas repair formwork experiences **high initial pressures from thixotropic grouts**, often capped at 150 kPa.
3. **Reusability & Access:** New construction formwork is reusable for **50–100 cycles** with full access, while repair formwork is often **single-use** due to customised dimensions and restricted access (overhead, confined spaces).

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*What is the role of pressure relief vents in sealed repair formwork?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Pressure relief vents in sealed repair formwork serve three critical functions:
1. They **allow trapped air to escape** during grout injection from the bottom upward, preventing voids and honeycombing at the top of the repair.
2. They **indicate when the form is full** — grout flowing out of the vent confirms complete filling.
3. They **limit internal pressure buildup** by releasing excess fluid, preventing formwork blow-out in case of pumping overpressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often write only *one* point and forget the others. Each Part A question carries **3 marks** typically distributed as **1 + 1 + 1** for three distinct points. **Always list exactly three complete sentences.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Choice A]**
*(a)* Explain the classification of formwork systems used in repair and rehabilitation works. Discuss in detail the application of **fabric formwork** (Fabriform) in underwater pile repair.
*(b)* A rectangular column **500 × 500 mm** is to be jacketed with **100 mm thick** micro-concrete. Formwork panels are **1.2 m high** with ties at **600 mm horizontal spacing**. Calculate the **tie force** using CIRIA PR91. Assume density **2200 kg/m³**, placement rate **R = 2.0 m/hr**, temperature coefficient **K = 1.0**, and effective head **H = 1.0 m**. Use safety factor **1.5**.

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Classification of repair formwork systems **(3 Marks):**

1. **By Function:**
   - **Containment Formwork:** Holds repair material against substrate (e.g., column jacketing moulds).
   - **Injection Formwork:** Sealed mould with grout ports for pressure injection.
   - **Stay-in-Place Formwork:** Permanent lost forms (GFRP panels, precast shells).
   - **Access & Support Formwork:** Needling and shoring systems.

2. **By Material:** Timber, plywood, steel, aluminium, fabric (textile), GFRP, precast concrete.

3. **By Geometry Adaptability:** Rigid panels, flexible membrane forms, modular clamps.

**Fabric formwork in underwater pile repair (4 Marks):**

- **Construction:** A tailored, porous, woven textile sleeve is wrapped around the deteriorated pile and sealed at both ends with zip-ties or straps.
- **Activation:** Grout hoses are inserted at the base. As grout is pumped in, the fabric **inflates** under pressure, conforming to the irregular pile surface and creating the desired jacket thickness.
- **Advantages for underwater work:**
  1. **No dewatering required** — diver-friendly installation.
  2. **Conforms to irregular geometry** of corroded piles.
  3. **Controlled grout consumption** — fabric acts as a "lost form" with minimal waste.
  4. **Acts as a filter** — allows water to bleed out but retains cementitious particles, increasing density.
- **Typical application:** Marine bridge piers, jetty piles, offshore platform legs.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply CIRIA PR91 equation:

$$
P = 28\sqrt{R} + 8K\sqrt{H}
$$

$$
P = 28\sqrt{2.0} + 8(1.0)\sqrt{1.0}
$$

$$
P = 28 \times 1.414 + 8 \times 1.0 = 39.59 + 8.00 = 47.59 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Hydrostatic check:

$$
P_{hydro} = \rho g h / 1000 = 2200 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 / 1000 = 21.58 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Governing design pressure:

$$
P_{design} = \max(47.59, 21.58) = 47.59 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Tributary area per tie:

$$
A_{trib} = 1.2 \times 0.6 = 0.72 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Tie force (working):

$$
F_{tie} = 47.59 \times 0.72 = 34.26 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply safety factor 1.5:

$$
F_{tie,ult} = 1.5 \times 34.26 = 51.39 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7:** Select tie — use **M20 high-tensile ties with SWL ≥ 55 kN**.

**Final Answer:** Each tie must be designed for an ultimate force of **51.39 kN**, governing selection is **M20 high-tensile ties**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Choice B]**
*(a)* With neat sketches, describe the **step-by-step procedure for column jacketing** using micro-concrete and modular formwork. List the safety considerations.
*(b)* Discuss the **lateral pressure behaviour of thixotropic repair grouts** and derive the modified pressure formula. Compare it with the standard ACI 347 equation for normal concrete.

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step-by-step procedure for column jacketing (5 Marks):**

1. **Shore the column** using props at the slab soffit to transfer a portion of the load during jacketing.
2. **Chip the cover concrete** to expose corroded reinforcement, removing loose and delaminated material.
3. **Clean reinforcement** by wire brush or sandblasting; replace severely corroded bars with additional lap-spliced bars.
4. **Apply bonding agent** (e.g., epoxy or polymer-modified slurry) on the substrate for adhesion.
5. **Erect modular formwork panels** around the column, sealing the base with foam tape to prevent leakage.
6. **Insert tremie pipes** at the top and **pressure relief vents** at the top corners.
7. **Pour micro-concrete** from the top (or pump from bottom through grout ports).
8. **Vibrate externally** using form vibrators since internal immersion is not possible.
9. **Cure for 24 hours**, then strip formwork and inspect by hammer tap.
10. **Apply curing compound** and continue water curing for 7 days.

**Safety considerations (2 Marks):**
- Adequate **propping** to transfer dead/live load during repair.
- **Edge protection** for working at height.
- **Ventilation** when using epoxy-based bonding agents (VOC fumes).
- **Lockout-tagout** of live services if working in industrial plants.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Lateral pressure behaviour of thixotropic repair grouts (3 Marks):**

Thixotropic materials exhibit a **time-dependent shear-thinning behaviour**: they are fluid when agitated (pumped) but gel rapidly when at rest. Therefore:
- Initial pressure is **near-hydrostatic** ($\rho g h$).
- Within **5–15 minutes**, internal gel structure forms.
- Pressure **decays exponentially** with time.

**Derivation of modified pressure formula (3 Marks):**

Starting from the basic principle that effective pressure equals hydrostatic head reduced by a thixotropy factor:

$$
P_{eff}(t) = \rho g h \cdot e^{-k_t t}
$$

Where:
- $k_t$ = thixotropic build-up constant (typically **0.05–0.15 min⁻¹**)
- $t$ = elapsed time since placement

At early time ($t = 0$):

$$
P_{eff}(0) = \rho g h \quad \text{(hydrostatic peak)}
$$

At $t = 30$ min with $k_t = 0.08$:

$$
P_{eff}(30) = \rho g h \cdot e^{-2.4} \approx 0.091 \rho g h
$$

The governing design pressure is the maximum of this exponential decay and the **CIRIA empirical bound**:

$$
P_{design} = \max \left( \rho g h \cdot e^{-k_t t}, \; 28\sqrt{R} + 8K\sqrt{H} \right)
$$

**Comparison with ACI 347 (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | ACI 347 (Normal Concrete) | Modified (Thixotropic Grout) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure depends on | R, T, slump, density | R, T, head, **thixotropy** |
| Peak pressure | **~50–80 kPa** | Up to **~150 kPa** |
| Time dependence | Logarithmic decay | Exponential decay |
| Application | New construction | Repair & rehabilitation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (14 Marks):**
> 1. **Numerical Questions:** Always write the **formula first**, then **substitute values**, then **solve**. Skipping substitution steps loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sketch Questions:** Even a **simple block diagram** with labels (no artistic skill needed) fetches **2–3 extra marks** over text-only answers.
> 3. **Choice Questions:** Read both Question A and Question B fully before choosing — sometimes the easier sub-part is in the other option.
> 4. **Units:** Always mention units (kPa, kN, m³/hr). Missing units can cost **0.5–1 mark per answer**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:**

- Repair formwork deals with **irregular geometries** and **thixotropic/non-shrink materials** — fundamentally different from new-construction formwork.
- Three classifications: **by function** (containment, injection, SIP, access), **by material** (timber, steel, fabric, GFRP), **by adaptability** (rigid, flexible, modular).
- **Fabric formwork (Fabriform)** is ideal for underwater pile repair and irregular members — conforms to substrate, no dewatering needed.
- **Stay-in-place (SIP)** formwork using GFRP or precast panels eliminates stripping and adds corrosion protection.
- **CIRIA PR91 formula** for grout pressure: $P = 28\sqrt{R} + 8K\sqrt{H}$.
- **Thixotropy decay** follows: $P_{eff}(t) = \rho g h \cdot e^{-k_t t}$.
- **Hydrostatic upper bound** must always be checked: $P_{hydro} = \rho g h$.
- **Safety factor** of **1.5** is standard for temporary repair formwork per IS 14687.
- **Pressure relief vents** are mandatory on sealed injection forms — maximum spacing **1.5 m**.
- **Stripping time** for polymer-modified mortar: **24 hrs**; for epoxy grout: **4–6 hrs**.
- **Minimum jacketing thickness** (per IS 15988): **75 mm** for columns; bond strength to substrate ≥ **1.7 MPa**.
- **Grout placement** is always **bottom-up via tremie** to displace air and prevent voids.
- **External vibrators** (form vibrators) are used since internal immersion is impractical in sealed repair forms.
- **Substrate preparation** (roughening to 5 mm amplitude + bonding agent) is **non-negotiable** for repair bond.
- **IS codes referenced:** IS 14687 (repair), IS 15988 (jacketing), ACI 546R (concrete repair guide), fib 102 (repair formwork).
- **Typical maximum design pressure** for repair formwork: **150 kPa** (much higher than new construction).
- **Working platform / access formwork** must be designed for **2.5 kPa live load** minimum per IS 4014.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Concrete Repair Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Concrete Repair Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Concrete Repair Works** refer to the systematic engineering processes, materials, and formwork-supported methodologies employed to restore, rehabilitate, or upgrade the structural capacity, serviceability, durability, and aesthetic integrity of deteriorated, damaged, or under-performing reinforced concrete elements. Within the scope of the KTU PECET742 Formwork Engineering curriculum, this topic extends beyond material science by emphasizing the **formwork engineering challenges** specific to repair environments, including confined access, irregular geometries, underwater placement, overhead spraying, and partial structural continuity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4 of PECET742 links formwork design directly to repair scenarios — students must appreciate that formwork for *new* construction follows predictable geometry, whereas formwork for *repair* must conform to **as-built, irregular, and often deteriorating parent concrete surfaces**.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Concrete Repair Diagnosis

Before selecting any technique, the repair engineer must classify deterioration using the **4-C Diagnostic Matrix**:

1. **Cause** — Identify the root mechanism (corrosion, chemical attack, overload, fire).
2. **Extent** — Quantify the damaged zone (depth, area, volume, propagation).
3. **Consequence** — Evaluate structural, functional, and aesthetic impact.
4. **Course of action** — Select technique, material, and formwork system.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Patient Treatment" Model

Imagine a concrete structure as a **patient visiting a hospital**:

- The **engineer** is the *doctor* (diagnoses the illness).
- The **deterioration mechanism** is the *disease* (e.g., cancer = chloride-induced corrosion).
- The **repair material** is the *medicine*.
- The **formwork** is the *surgical support apparatus* — it holds the medicine in place, shapes the repaired anatomy, and provides the sterile environment needed for healing.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a surgeon cannot operate without clamps, retractors, and supports, a repair engineer cannot apply patch repairs, jacketing, or shotcrete without carefully designed **temporary or permanent formwork systems** tailored to the defect geometry.

### 1.4 Why Formwork Matters in Repair (Beyond Conventional Construction)

| Aspect | New Construction Formwork | Repair Works Formwork |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Predictable, designed from drawings | Irregular, conforms to as-built surface |
| **Loading** | Hydrostatic pressure of fresh concrete | Often minimal (patch, mortar) or impact (shotcrete) |
| **Access** | Open, well-scaffolded | Confined, overhead, submerged, or behind rebar |
| **Striking** | Standard cycle (12–24 hrs) | Extended due to slower-strength gain repair mortars |
| **Bond** | Monolithic | Critical — surface preparation dictates bond quality |
| **Reusability** | High (Mivan, table forms) | Low — typically single-use custom formwork |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Repair Performance Metric:** A well-executed repair should achieve a **bond strength of ≥ 1.7 MPa (250 psi)** in tension (ASTM C1583 / IS 2770 pull-off test) to the parent concrete substrate.

### 1.5 Common Deterioration Mechanisms (Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 D's of Deterioration** — memorize for KTU exams:
> 1. **Deflection** (overload, creep, loss of prestress)
> 2. **Delamination** (corrosion expansion, freeze-thaw)
> 3. **Disintegration** (sulfate attack, alkali-silica reaction)
> 4. **Discoloration** (carbonation, efflorescence, staining)
> 5. **Dusting** (poor curing, high w/c ratio, finishing errors)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Concrete Repair Techniques

Concrete repair works are broadly classified under **three strategic tiers**, each with distinct formwork implications:

#### **TIER 1 — Surface Restoration (Cosmetic / Minor Patch Repairs)**
- **Polymer-modified cementitious mortars**
- **Cementitious fairing coats**
- **Surface sealers and coatings**
- **Formwork Requirement:** Minimal — hand troweled, often no formwork needed; sometimes a *moulded former* or *temporary edge form* is used to retain patch material on vertical/overhead surfaces.

#### **TIER 2 — Structural Rehabilitation (Load-Restoring Techniques)**
- **Epoxy / Polyurethane Injection** (crack repair)
- **Shotcrete / Gunite** (large-area section restoration)
- **Jacketing** (column / beam / slab strengthening)
- **External Bonded CFRP / FRP Wraps**
- **Formwork Requirement:** Moderate to high — requires containment formwork for shotcrete rebound, jacketing moulds, pressure-sealed injection ports.

#### **TIER 3 — Major Restoration / Augmentation**
- **Preplaced Aggregate Concrete (PAC) / Intrusion Grouting**
- **Underwater Placement using Tremie or Hydrocrete**
- **Cathodic Protection Systems**
- **Enlargement Section with Bonded Overlay**
- **Formwork Requirement:** Specialized — tremie hoppers, hydrostatic formwork, sacrificial formwork, modular panels for irregular geometry.

### 2.2 Detailed Technique Analysis

#### **2.2.1 Epoxy Injection for Crack Repair**

- Applicable for **non-moving cracks** (width 0.05 mm to 6 mm).
- Process: Surface sealing with epoxy paste → Installation of injection ports (T-ports at 150–300 mm spacing) → Pressure injection of low-viscosity epoxy resin (200–400 cps) → Curing → Port removal and surface grinding.
- **Formwork Consideration:** Not a true formwork-demanding technique, but requires **temporary surface seal formwork** (epoxy putty mould) to contain resin until gel.

#### **2.2.2 Shotcrete / Gunite Repair**

- Pneumatically projected concrete (wet-mix shotcrete) or mortar (dry-mix gunite) at high velocity (60–80 m/s) onto prepared substrate.
- **Rebound loss:** 10–25% for wet-mix, 20–40% for dry-mix (must be factored in formwork design for containment).
- **Formwork Requirement:** Requires **sacrificial backing formwork** (or mesh reinforcement) on the opposite face to prevent wastage and ensure section profile. The *rebound capture formwork* is often designed as a 200–300 mm offset screen.

#### **2.2.3 Jacketing (Column / Beam Wrapping)**

- A new concrete (often with added rebar) layer is cast around the existing member to increase section capacity and confinement.
- Typical jacket thickness: 50–100 mm.
- **Formwork Requirement:** **Circular column jacket formwork** uses steel half-shells with bolted flanges; **rectangular beam jackets** use modular timber-ply panels. The **gap (15–25 mm) at the interface** is pressure-grouted with epoxy or non-shrink grout to ensure monolithic bond.

#### **2.2.4 Preplaced Aggregate Concrete (PAC)**

- Coarse aggregate is pre-packed into the formwork; the voids are then filled by injecting grout (cement + fly ash + sand + admixture) from the bottom upward.
- **Formwork Requirement:** Must be **pressure-tight** (designed for 0.5–1.0 MPa grout pressure) and have **grout injection ports at the bottom** and **vent ports at the top** to release entrapped air. Standard hydrostatic formwork design equations apply, but reduced density (~70% of conventional concrete) is used.

#### **2.2.5 Underwater Repair Techniques**

- **Tremie method** — concrete is placed through a vertical pipe reaching the pour point, displacing water upward.
- **Hydrovalve / Bagwork method** — grout-filled bags are placed by divers to form underwater patches.
- **Formwork Requirement:** Tremie requires **sealed, watertight formwork** with gasket seals; Bagwork uses **fabric formwork** (synthetic geotextile bags) that act as both formwork and water-permeable drainage layer.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Board Exams**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{bond} = \dfrac{F_{pull}}{A_{core}}$ | Pull-off force, core area | MPa | Substrate bond strength (ASTM C1583) |
| 2 | $P_{max} = \rho_{c} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Max hydrostatic pressure | kPa | Formwork design for repair mortars |
| 3 | $R_{shotcrete} = \dfrac{V_{rebound}}{V_{placed}} \times 100$ | Rebound % | % | Shotcrete formwork sizing |
| 4 | $t_{jacket} \geq 50 \text{ mm}$ for RC jackets | Min jacket thickness | mm | Column jacketing code (IS 15988) |
| 5 | $V_{grout} = n \cdot V_{voids} \cdot (1 + w_{e})$ | Grout volume with waste factor | m³ | PAC / intrusion grouting |
| 6 | $L_{port} = \dfrac{w_{crack} \times 0.75}{spacing}$ | Port length factor | mm | Epoxy injection port design |
| 7 | $f'_{c(overlay)} \geq f'_{c(substrate)} + 7 \text{ MPa}$ | Overlay strength requirement | MPa | Bonded overlay design |
| 8 | $P_{injection} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure for grout | Pa | Grout penetration in cracks |
| 9 | $\tau_{interface} = \dfrac{V}{b \cdot d}$ | Shear at repair interface | MPa | Jacketing interface shear check |
| 10 | $S_{spacing} = \dfrac{D_{port}}{2} \text{ to } D_{port}$ | Injection port spacing | mm | Epoxy injection layout |

> **Note on units:** All SI units (MPa, kPa, m, mm, kg/m³) are used consistently with KTU examination conventions.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

- **Infrastructure Longevity:** India's **National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP)** allocates substantial funds to repair works; efficient formwork can cut project costs by **15–25%**.
- **Heritage Restoration:** Adaptive formwork (3D-scanned + CNC-cut) is now used to repair colonial-era concrete structures with complex profiles.
- **Disaster Response:** Post-earthquake jacketing and shotcrete are the two highest-volume emergency repair techniques in India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Formwork Pressure for Repair Mortars (Reduced Density Form)

Repair mortars (polymer-modified, fiber-reinforced) often have lower plastic density and slower strength gain than conventional concrete, requiring extended formwork retention.

**Given:**
- Repair mortar plastic density: $\rho_{m} = 2100 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Formwork height for jacketing: $h = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Mortar initial set: $t_{set} = 4 \text{ hours}$
- Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Calculate hydrostatic pressure at the base of formwork**

$$
P_{hydro} = \rho_{m} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

Substituting values:

$$
P_{hydro} = 2100 \times 9.81 \times 1.5
$$

$$
P_{hydro} = 30,901.5 \text{ Pa} = 30.90 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to design pressure (incl. impact & vibration factor)**

For repair mortars placed by hand or chute (no pumping), impact factor $K_{i} = 1.0$:

$$
P_{design} = K_{i} \cdot P_{hydro} = 1.0 \times 30.90 = 30.90 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Plywood sheathing thickness check (Per IS 4990)**

For 12 mm plywood (allowable bending stress $f_{b} = 7.5 \text{ MPa}$), supported on studs at 300 mm c/c:

$$
M_{allow} = f_{b} \cdot \dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6}
$$

With $b = 1000 \text{ mm}$ (per meter width), $t = 12 \text{ mm}$:

$$
M_{allow} = 7.5 \times \dfrac{1000 \times 12^2}{6}
$$

$$
M_{allow} = 7.5 \times \dfrac{144000}{6} = 7.5 \times 24000 = 180000 \text{ N·mm/m} = 180 \text{ N·m/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Check stud spacing adequacy**

Required section modulus for actual pressure:

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8 \cdot f_{b}}
$$

With $w = 30.90 \text{ kPa} \times 1 \text{ m} = 30.9 \text{ kN/m}$, $L = 0.3 \text{ m}$:

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{30.9 \times 0.3^2}{8 \times 7.5 \times 10^3} = \dfrac{30.9 \times 0.09}{60000} = \dfrac{2.781}{60000}
$$

$$
Z_{req} = 4.635 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{m} = 46.35 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with available section modulus of 12 mm plywood**

For 1000 mm wide × 12 mm thick:

$$
Z_{avail} = \dfrac{1000 \times 12^2}{6} = 24000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{m} = 24 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result:** $Z_{req}$ (46.35 cm³/m) **> $Z_{avail}$ (24 cm³/m)** → The 12 mm plywood sheathing with 300 mm stud spacing is **INADEQUATE** for this 1.5 m jacketing height using 2100 kg/m³ mortar. Solution: reduce stud spacing to **200 mm c/c** OR use 18 mm plywood.

**Step 6 — Required stud spacing for 12 mm plywood**

Setting $Z_{req} = Z_{avail}$:

$$
L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \cdot f_{b} \cdot Z_{avail}}{w}}
$$

$$
L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 7.5 \times 10^6 \times 24 \times 10^{-6}}{30.9 \times 10^3}}
$$

$$
L_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1440}{30900}} = \sqrt{0.0466} = 0.216 \text{ m} \approx 215 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Use stud spacing of **200 mm c/c** for safety.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shotcrete Rebound Loss & Formwork Containment Volume

**Given:**
- Wet-mix shotcrete target thickness: $t = 75 \text{ mm}$
- Wall area to repair: $A = 12 \text{ m}^2$
- Rebound rate: $R = 18\%$ (typical wet-mix, overhead)
- Density of in-place shotcrete: $\rho = 2200 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Theoretical in-place volume required**

$$
V_{in\text{-}place} = A \cdot t = 12 \times 0.075 = 0.90 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Material that must be sprayed (accounting for rebound)**

$$
V_{spray} = \dfrac{V_{in\text{-}place}}{1 - R} = \dfrac{0.90}{1 - 0.18} = \dfrac{0.90}{0.82} = 1.098 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Rebound volume to be captured by backing formwork**

$$
V_{rebound} = V_{spray} - V_{in\text{-}place} = 1.098 - 0.90 = 0.198 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Formwork containment design (offset backing screen)**

A 250 mm offset screen behind the rebar captures 70% of rebound for reuse:

$$
V_{captured} = 0.70 \times 0.198 = 0.139 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Final material order (with 10% wastage)**

$$
V_{order} = V_{spray} \times 1.10 = 1.098 \times 1.10 = 1.208 \text{ m}^3
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Repair Technique Selection

| Criterion | Epoxy Injection | Shotcrete | Jacketing | PAC | Underwater Tremie |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Crack width** | 0.05–6 mm | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Section loss** | Minor | Moderate–Major | Major | Major | Major |
| **Formwork complexity** | Low | Medium (sacrificial) | High (custom) | Very high (sealed) | Specialized (watertight) |
| **Access constraints** | Any | Overhead OK | Open only | Confined OK | Submerged |
| **Bond requirement** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent + dowels | Grout penetration | Tremie monolith |
| **Typical strength** | 60–80 MPa (epoxy) | 30–50 MPa | 25–40 MPa | 35–55 MPa | 25–40 MPa |
| **Cost index** | Medium | High | High | Very high | Very high |
| **Speed** | Fast | Fast | Slow | Slow | Slow |

---

### 3.4 Formwork Striking Time Calculator for Repair Mortars

Conventional concrete striking is at 24–48 hours, but repair mortars (especially polymer-modified) follow **strength-based stripping criteria** per IS 456:

$$
t_{strip} = \dfrac{T_{required}}{T_{design}} \times t_{standard}
$$

For polymer-modified mortar needing 70% of $f_{ck}$ before stripping (load transfer to substrate):

$$
t_{strip} = \dfrac{0.70 \times 35}{35} \times 48 = 33.6 \text{ hours} \approx 34 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **strength-based criterion** (typically 70% of $f_{ck}$ for self-supporting repair, 100% for load-bearing repair), not just the time-based rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
