## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "shotcrete" and explain the role of "sacrificial backing formwork" in shotcrete repair works.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Shotcrete is a process in which concrete or mortar is pneumatically projected at high velocity (typically 60–80 m/s) onto a prepared surface, achieving compaction through the kinetic energy of the impact rather than by vibration.

**Sacrificial Backing Formwork (1.5 marks):** In repair works, the rear face of the repair zone is sealed using a sacrificial formwork panel (plywood, mesh, or textile) supported on temporary props. This:
1. Contains the high-velocity spray, preventing wastage.
2. Captures rebound material (10–25% rebound in wet-mix) for reuse.
3. Ensures the section profile is maintained to the design thickness.
4. Provides a continuous reaction surface for the compaction pressure of freshly sprayed shotcrete.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three causes of concrete deterioration and state the corresponding preferred repair technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Cause of Deterioration | Preferred Repair Technique |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion of reinforcement due to chloride ingress | Epoxy injection for cracks + patch repair + corrosion inhibitor coating |
| 2 | Carbonation-induced cover concrete spalling | Polymer-modified mortar patching with anti-carbonation protective coating |
| 3 | Sulfate attack causing disintegration | Removal of affected concrete + shotcrete overlay with sulfate-resisting cement |

*(1 mark each: cause identification + technique — 3 marks)*

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of **Epoxy Injection technique** for crack repair in concrete structures. Include the criteria for material selection and the role of temporary surface seal formwork. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reinforced concrete beam of dimensions 300 mm × 600 mm × 4 m is to be repaired by **jacketing** with a 75 mm thick M30 concrete overlay on three sides. Calculate the volume of concrete required for the jacket. Discuss the formwork design considerations specific to this jacketing scenario. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The epoxy injection technique involves the following systematic steps:

1. **Crack Survey and Marking (0.5 mark):** All cracks wider than 0.05 mm are mapped and marked. Moving cracks (those that open and close cyclically) are excluded — only dormant cracks qualify.

2. **Surface Cleaning (0.5 mark):** The concrete surface within 50–75 mm on either side of the crack is cleaned of dust, oil, and loose material using compressed air or wire brushing.

3. **Installation of Injection Ports (1 mark):** Surface-mounted T-ports (injection entry points) are fixed along the crack at a spacing equal to the wall thickness, typically **150–300 mm** apart. The first and last ports are placed at the crack termination points.

4. **Surface Sealing (1 mark):** The crack between the ports is sealed with an epoxy paste or polyester putty. This **temporary seal formwork** acts as a mould to contain the liquid epoxy resin during injection, preventing leakage.

5. **Mixing of Epoxy (0.5 mark):** A two-component low-viscosity epoxy resin (200–400 cps) is mixed in the exact stoichiometric ratio specified by the manufacturer.

6. **Pressure Injection (1 mark):** The mixed epoxy is injected through the lowest port using a pressure pot or pneumatic pump, at pressures of **0.2–0.7 MPa**. The resin flows into the crack by capillary action and pressure, displacing air and moisture.

7. **Curing and Quality Check (0.5 mark):** The system is allowed to cure for 24 hours. Quality is verified by **coring and compressive testing** (core strength ≥ parent concrete strength) or by ultrasonic pulse velocity testing.

8. **Finishing (0.5 mark):** Ports are removed, surface sealant is ground flush, and a protective coating may be applied.

> **Material Selection Criteria (0.5 mark):** Viscosity must be low for fine cracks; bond strength ≥ 3.5 MPa; pot life matched to injection rate; chemical compatibility with concrete; shrinkage-free curing.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Volume Calculation (3 marks):**

Beam original dimensions: 300 mm × 600 mm × 4000 mm

Jacket thickness: 75 mm on three sides (bottom + two sides)

Effective enlarged dimensions:
- Width: $300 + 2 \times 75 = 450 \text{ mm}$
- Depth: $600 + 75 = 675 \text{ mm}$

Jacket cross-sectional area:

$$
A_{jacket} = (450 \times 675) - (300 \times 600) = 303750 - 180000 = 123750 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{jacket} = 0.12375 \text{ m}^2
$$

Volume of jacket concrete:

$$
V_{jacket} = A_{jacket} \times L = 0.12375 \times 4.0 = 0.495 \text{ m}^3
$$

With 10% wastage factor:

$$
V_{order} = 0.495 \times 1.10 = 0.5445 \text{ m}^3 \approx 0.55 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Formwork Design Considerations (4 marks):**

1. **Confinement to Three Faces (1 mark):** Since the top of the beam is inaccessible, the soffit and two side formwork panels must be **self-supporting** without top ties. Side panels are held by **bolted brackets anchored to the existing beam** (resin-anchored studs) and the soffit panel rests on **needle props or hangers** through the beam.

2. **Stiffness to Prevent Deflection (1 mark):** Beam jacketing spans 4 m — **waler beams (ISMB 150 or timber 100×100 mm)** are required at 500 mm c/c to limit deflection under hydrostatic pressure from 0.5 m height concrete head.

3. **Interface Grouting (0.5 mark):** A **15–25 mm gap** must be retained between the formwork and the existing beam to allow pressure injection of non-shrink epoxy grout, ensuring monolithic bond.

4. **Striking Time (0.5 mark):** Due to extended curing of polymer-modified concrete in jacket, **striking is at 72 hours** or when 70% of $f_{ck}$ is achieved (verified by cube test).

5. **Drainage and Venting (0.5 mark):** **Vents at 1 m c/c** at the top of the formwork release trapped air during concrete placement.

6. **Surface Preparation Bond (0.5 mark):** Existing beam surface roughened to amplitude ≥ 3 mm (sandblasting or needle scaling) to achieve bond strength ≥ 1.7 MPa. Additional shear dowels (12 mm dia @ 300 mm c/c, 150 mm embedment) may be provided for composite action.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** What is **Preplaced Aggregate Concrete (PAC)**? Explain its application in repair works with a focus on the formwork design requirements for pressure-tight grouting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **shotcrete, jacketing, and preplaced aggregate concrete** as repair techniques. Prepare a comparative table covering formwork complexity, suitability, cost, and typical strength. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Preplaced Aggregate Concrete (PAC), also called **Intrusion Grouting**, is a repair technique in which coarse aggregate is first placed into the formwork cavity, and the voids are subsequently filled by injecting cement-sand-fly-ash grout from the bottom upward under pressure (typically 0.5–1.0 MPa). The grout displaces water/air and intrudes into all void spaces, producing a dense, low-shrinkage concrete.

**Application in Repair Works (2.5 marks):**

1. **Underwater Repairs:** PAC is the preferred method for repairing bridge piers, dams, and marine piles because the coarse aggregate stays in place even when washed by water.
2. **Massive Section Repairs:** Used for thick sections (>300 mm) where conventional concrete would generate excessive heat of hydration.
3. **Confined Repairs:** Where reinforcement congestion prevents effective vibration of conventional concrete.
4. **Bond Quality:** Achieves excellent bond with parent concrete due to grout penetration into substrate pores.

**Formwork Design Requirements — Pressure-Tight Grouting (3 marks):**

1. **Pressure Resistance (1 mark):** Formwork must be designed for grout pressure of **0.5–1.0 MPa** (vs. 0.03–0.05 MPa hydrostatic for conventional concrete). This demands **steel formwork** or heavily reinforced plywood with **closely spaced walers (200 mm c/c)** and **strong tie rods (16 mm dia @ 500 mm c/c)**.

2. **Injection and Vent Port Layout (1 mark):** 
   - **Grout injection ports** at 1.0–1.5 m c/c at the **bottom** of the formwork.
   - **Vent/inspection ports** at the **top** to release entrapped air and confirm complete grout filling (grout flowing out of vent = fill complete).

3. **Sealing and Joint Integrity (0.5 mark):** All formwork joints must have **gasket seals (rubber or foam tape)**; corners must be fillet-welded in steel forms; tie rod holes must use **rubber cone washers** to prevent grout leakage.

4. **Surface Preparation Bond (0.5 mark):** Substrate must be pre-wetted (saturated surface dry) to prevent grout water loss and ensure penetration.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Comparative Table**

| Parameter | Shotcrete | Jacketing | Preplaced Aggregate Concrete (PAC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formwork complexity** | Medium (sacrificial backing) | High (custom moulds) | Very high (pressure-tight) |
| **Suitable for** | Large overhead / vertical areas | Columns, beams, slabs | Massive sections, underwater |
| **Placement method** | Pneumatic projection | Casting with vibration | Aggregate pre-placed + grout injection |
| **Typical strength** | 30–50 MPa | 25–40 MPa | 35–55 MPa |
| **Cost index (relative)** | 1.2× | 1.5× | 2.0× |
| **Access constraints** | Overhead friendly | Open access only | Confined and underwater OK |
| **Section thickness** | 50–200 mm | 50–100 mm | > 300 mm |
| **Rebound / wastage** | 10–25% rebound | 5–10% formwork leakage | < 2% |
| **Speed of execution** | Fast | Slow (formwork + pour + cure) | Very slow |
| **Bond quality** | Excellent (mechanical key) | Good (with dowels + grout) | Excellent (grout penetration) |

> *[Note: Each row carries 0.5 mark; total 5 marks for table + 2 marks for one-line justification of "best choice" for a given scenario]*

**Best-C choice scenario (2 marks):**
For the repair of a **3 m × 3 m damaged soffit of an elevated water tank** with **75 mm section loss**, **shotcrete** is the best choice because it requires minimal access scaffolding, can be projected overhead without formwork on the soffit, and achieves rapid strength gain.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "gunite" with "shotcrete"** — Gunite is dry-mix (water added at nozzle); Shotcrete is wet-mix (water pre-mixed). Marks deducted if interchanged.
> 2. **Stating "formwork" only as conventional shuttering** — For repair works, mention **sacrificial formwork, geotextile formwork, hydrostatic formwork, and pressure-tight formwork**. Skipping these terms = -2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to factor rebound in shotcrete** — Direct volume calculation without rebound adjustment is treated as incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the 70% strength criterion for striking** — Examiners expect strength-based, not just time-based, striking.
> 5. **No mention of bond strength test (≥ 1.7 MPa per ASTM C1583 / IS 2770)** — Quality assurance is a high-value mark point.
> 6. **In Numerical Problems:** Forgetting the 10% wastage factor; using wrong density for repair mortars (use 2100–2200 kg/m³, not 2500 kg/m³).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- [ ] **5 D's of Deterioration:** Deflection, Delamination, Disintegration, Discoloration, Dusting.
- [ ] **4-C Diagnostic Matrix:** Cause, Extent, Consequence, Course of action.
- [ ] **Epoxy Injection:** For dormant cracks 0.05–6 mm; port spacing 150–300 mm; injection pressure 0.2–0.7 MPa.
- [ ] **Shotcrete:** Velocity 60–80 m/s; rebound 10–25% (wet) and 20–40% (dry); requires sacrificial backing formwork.
- [ ] **Jacketing:** Min thickness 50 mm; 15–25 mm interface grout gap; shear dowels @ 300 mm c/c.
- [ ] **PAC / Intrusion Grouting:** Grout pressure 0.5–1.0 MPa; bottom injection, top venting; substrate must be SSD.
- [ ] **Underwater Tremie:** Pipe must reach pour point; watertight formwork with gasket seals.
- [ ] **Bond Strength Requirement:** ≥ 1.7 MPa (ASTM C1583 pull-off test).
- [ ] **Striking Time:** 70% of $f_{ck}$ for self-supporting; 100% for load-bearing repair (per IS 456).
- [ ] **Formwork Pressure for Repair Mortars:** $P = \rho_{m} \cdot g \cdot h$ with $\rho_{m} \approx 2100$ kg/m³.
- [ ] **Codes Referenced:** IS 456, IS 15988 (Jacketing), IS 2770 (Repair), ACI 546 (Repair Code), ASTM C1583.
- [ ] **Rebound Adjustment Formula:** $V_{spray} = \dfrac{V_{in\text{-}place}}{1 - R}$.
- [ ] **Overlay Strength Rule:** $f'_{c(overlay)} \geq f'_{c(substrate)} + 7 \text{ MPa}$.
- [ ] **Differences — New vs Repair Formwork:** Irregular geometry, confined access, low reusability, extended striking, bond-critical interface.
- [ ] **Formwork Types for Repair:** Sacrificial backing, geotextile bags, steel half-shells (column jacket), pressure-tight sealed panels (PAC), tremie-hopper systems (underwater).

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always pair every repair technique with its **specific formwork system** — that is the unique KTU PECET742 angle that distinguishes your answer from a generic concrete repair note.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strengthening and Retrofitting Existing Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strengthening and Retrofitting Existing Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Strengthening and Retrofitting Existing Structures

## 1.1 Core Technical Definitions (KTU Syllabus Standardised Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — Strengthening**
> **Strengthening** is the process of *augmenting the load-carrying capacity, stiffness, ductility, or durability* of an existing structural member (or the whole structure) so that it can safely resist design loads that are **higher** than those originally anticipated, without necessarily addressing any change in code provisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — Retrofitting**
> **Retrofitting** is the process of *upgrading an existing structure* so that it becomes compliant with the **current/revised code requirements** (typically seismic, wind, or revised loading standards), even when the original design met its own era's code. It is an *intervention* driven by a change in performance demand rather than a change in imposed load alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Related Terms (Frequently asked 2-mark question)**
> - **Repair** → restores the *original* capacity lost due to damage.
> - **Rehabilitation** → combines *repair + retrofit* to bring the structure to an acceptable modern standard.
> - **Strengthening** → enhances capacity beyond the original.
> - **Retrofitting** → updates performance to satisfy *current* codes (often seismic).

### 1.2 Why Structures Need Intervention — Driving Factors

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Triggers for Strengthening/Retrofitting (must memorise)**
> 1. **Code revision** — e.g., IS 1893 (Part 1):2002/2016 seismic upgrade.
> 2. **Change of use / change in occupancy** (e.g., warehouse → data centre).
> 3. **Increase in design loads** (heavier equipment, additional floors).
> 4. **Deterioration** due to corrosion of steel, carbonation, sulphate attack, ASR (Alkali-Silica Reaction).
> 5. **Damage** from earthquakes, fire, impact, blast, or foundation settlement.
> 6. **Design / construction defects** discovered in service.
> 7. **Functional modifications** like cutting openings in slabs, removing columns (transfer structures).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ageing Athlete"

> Conceptual Analogy: Imagine an **engineer of the 1970s** as a *young athlete* who built a 10-storey building using the 1970 fitness regimen (codes). Today, the "fitness standards" (IS 1893:2016) have become much stricter, and the athlete is now 50 years old, slightly injured (corrosion), and asked to lift heavier weights (more floors / seismic zone change). The athlete cannot be replaced; he must be **retrained, supplemented, and supported** — exactly what retrofitting does. The **formwork** is the *supportive harness, splint, and gym equipment* that holds the new "muscle" (concrete jacket, FRP wrap) in place while it bonds and gains strength.

### 1.4 Role of Formwork in Strengthening/Retrofitting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why formwork is INDISPENSABLE in retrofitting** (this course's central link):
> - Almost every wet-jacketing technique (column/beam/slab/wall jacketing) requires fresh concrete or grout to be **cast against the existing structural surface**.
> - Because the existing member is **non-geometric, irregular, congested with reinforcement, and partially loaded**, the formwork must be:
>   1. **Custom-fabricated** (no two jobs identical).
>   2. **Braced against the existing structure** rather than the ground.
>   3. **Leak-proof** at the interface to prevent grout loss.
>   4. **Self-supporting** because the substrate is unyielding.
>   5. **Able to deliver a small but uniform concrete thickness** (typically **50 mm to 100 mm** for jackets).
> - The formwork itself is therefore a *structural element* during retrofit, transferring hydrostatic, vibrator, and weight loads back into the *old* structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Methods of Strengthening & Their Formwork Implications

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Strengthening Techniques

The **four principal families** of strengthening methods, each with distinct formwork requirements, are:

1. **Section Enlargement (Jacketing)** — adds concrete/mortar around existing member.
2. **Externally Bonded Reinforcement (EBR)** — steel plates or FRP bonded to surface.
3. **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Wrapping / Near-Surface Mounted (NSM) FRP**.
4. **External / Post-Tensioning** — high-strength tendons stressed after construction.
5. **Supplemental Members** — steel collars, haunches, wing walls, buttresses.

---

## 2.2 Method 1 — Concrete Jacketing (the most formwork-intensive)

### 2.2.1 Column Jacketing
- **Procedure**: Remove loose concrete, chip the surface to ≥ **5 mm amplitude roughness**, apply bond coat (cement-sand slurry with polymer modifier), tie/anchor new reinforcement cage (typically **8 mm to 12 mm dia.**, lateral ties at ≤ **100 mm c/c**), erect formwork, pour micro-concrete or self-compacting concrete (SCC).
- **Minimum jacket thickness** (IS 15988:2013 / ACI 562): **t_j ≥ 75 mm**, often 100 mm.
- **Formwork Type**: Custom curved/rectangular column forms, two-piece bolted clamshells, glass-fibre/polymer (GRP) stay-in-place forms, or shotcrete (no formwork).

### 2.2.2 Beam Jacketing
- Added at **top, bottom, or three sides (U-jacket)**.
- Requires formwork that hangs from existing soffit or rests on shores.
- New stirrups must be **anchored into the slab** by drilling and grouting.

### 2.2.3 Slab Jacketing
- Topping (overlay) typically **40–75 mm**.
- Formwork acts as a **screed rail** to achieve the required thickness; edges retained by edge forms.

---

## 2.3 Method 2 — Externally Bonded Steel Plates (EBSP)
- Plates typically **6 mm to 12 mm** thick, epoxied to tension face.
- **Formwork role**: minimal; required only for **anchoring bolts** and **protecting the plate against fire** (later encasement).

## 2.4 Method 3 — FRP Wrapping
- Carbon (CFRP), Glass (GFRP), or Aramid (AFRP) fabrics saturated in epoxy.
- **Formwork role**: NIL for wrapping; required only for the protective top-coat if used.

## 2.5 Method 4 — External Post-Tensioning
- High-tensile wires/strands stressed against the member ends.
- **Formwork role**: needed for **deviator saddles** and **anchor blocks** — small but heavily reinforced concrete housings cast against the existing member.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Equations You Must Memorise for ESE (Module 4)**

| # | Equation | Meaning of Terms | Unit | Code / Source |
|---|----------|------------------|------|---------------|
| 1 | $t_{j,\min} \geq 75 \text{ mm}$ | Minimum jacket thickness | mm | IS 15988:2013, ACI 562 |
| 2 | $\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot L_{bond}}$ | Bond stress between old & new concrete | N/mm² | IS 456, ACI 546 |
| 3 | $V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$ | Shear capacity of existing beam | kN | IS 456:2000 Cl. 40 |
| 4 | $V_{c,jacketed} = V_c + V_s^{new} + V_{s,FRP}$ | Shear capacity after jacketing | kN | ACI 440.2R-17 |
| 5 | $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right)$ | Flexural capacity (singly reinforced) | kN·m | IS 456:2000 |
| 6 | $f_{ck,jacket} \geq 0.8 f_{ck,old}$ | Required new concrete strength | N/mm² | Compatibility rule |
| 7 | $P_{n,\min} = 0.85 \phi \left[0.85 f_c' (A_g - A_{st}) + f_y A_{st}\right]$ | Axial capacity of jacketed column | N | ACI 318 / IS 456 |
| 8 | $n_{anchors} = \dfrac{V_u}{\phi V_{n,anchor}}$ | Shear-connector count at interface | — | ACI 318 App. D |
| 9 | $w/c \leq 0.45$ | Max water-cement ratio for micro-concrete | — | IS 15988 |
| 10 | $f_{lat} = \dfrac{2 t_j f_{jacket}}{D}$ | Confinement pressure (circular jacket) | N/mm² | Mander model |

> **Symbols**: $t_j$ = jacket thickness, $b$ = width, $d$ = effective depth, $f_{ck}$ = characteristic cube strength, $f_y$ = yield strength, $A_{st}$ = tension steel area, $\phi$ = strength-reduction factor, $D$ = column diameter, $V_{n,anchor}$ = nominal anchor capacity.

> **NOTE on formatting**: All vertical separators within formulas use the centred pipe or `\vert`; we deliberately avoid `|` to keep the table markdown stable.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why Study This in Formwork Engineering?
- Every strengthening/retrofit **cast-in-situ technique cannot proceed without formwork**; the formwork cost in a retrofit project routinely reaches **15–25 %** of the total retrofit cost, compared with **5–8 %** in new construction.
- The *formwork pressure* equations (concrete lateral pressure on formwork, per ACI 347 / IS 14687) are **identical** for new and retrofit pours, but the **bracing geometry** is radically different (braced against an *existing* structure of unknown stiffness).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Minimum Jacket Thickness from Bond Shear Transfer

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem
The interface between old concrete and new jacket concrete must transfer **shear stress** $\tau_v$ such that the composite section behaves monolithically. The jacket must be thick enough to:

1. Accommodate the **new longitudinal rebar** with adequate cover (≥ **25 mm**).
2. Accommodate the **new transverse ties/stirrups**.
3. Provide **bond length** for shear transfer.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $D$ = original column diameter (circular), or $b$ = side (square).
- $d_{b,l}$ = diameter of new longitudinal bar.
- $d_{b,t}$ = diameter of new tie/stirrup.
- $c_{min}$ = minimum clear cover = **25 mm** (per IS 456:2000 Cl. 26.4).

For a square column with longitudinal bars at corners and one layer of ties:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{j} &\geq c_{min} + d_{b,t} + c_{min} + d_{b,l} + c_{min} \\
t_{j} &\geq 3 c_{min} + d_{b,t} + d_{b,l} \\
t_{j} &\geq 3(25) + 8 + 16 \\
t_{j} &\geq 75 + 24 \\
t_{j} &\geq 99 \text{ mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{round up to } \mathbf{100 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation**: The 75 mm *prescriptive minimum* in IS 15988 is a **lower bound**; in practice the *constructive minimum* from cover and rebar requirements is often 90–110 mm.

### 3.1.3 Bond Shear Check at Interface
Bond shear demand at the interface:

$$
\tau_{v,demand} = \frac{V_u}{\pi D \cdot L_{jacket}} \quad \text{(circular column)}
$$

Permissible bond shear (cementitious bond, IS 15988):

$$
\tau_{v,allow} = 0.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad \text{(roughened surface, dowel pins at 300 mm c/c)}
$$

The design check:

$$
\tau_{v,demand} \leq \tau_{v,allow}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Column Jacketing Capacity Calculation

> **Question (KTU Model)**: A 400 mm × 400 mm square RC column, $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm², is reinforced with 4-#20 bars (longitudinal) and #8 ties @ 200 mm c/c. The column is to be **jacketed** by adding a 100 mm thick micro-concrete ($f_{ck,j} = 35$ N/mm²) all around with 8-#16 longitudinal bars and #8 ties @ 100 mm c/c. Determine the **enhanced axial capacity** $P_u$ of the jacketed section.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Gross Section
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{j} &= 400 + 2(100) = 600 \text{ mm} \\
A_{g,j} &= 600 \times 600 = 360{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Total Steel Area
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st,old} &= 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^{2} = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.6 \text{ mm}^{2} \\
A_{st,new} &= 8 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(16)^{2} = 8 \times 201.06 = 1608.5 \text{ mm}^{2} \\
A_{st,total} &= 1256.6 + 1608.5 = 2865.1 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Effective Concrete Strength (weighted)
$$
f_{ck,eff} = \frac{(360{,}000 - 2865.1)(35) + (2865.1 - A_{st,old})\text{ (old portion considered separately)}}{360{,}000}
$$

For a quick conservative KTU exam estimate, use the **lower** of the two strengths on the concrete portion (older is critical):

$$
f_{ck,design} = 0.8 \times 20 = 16 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \;\;\text{(on old core concrete)}
$$

> **Adopt simplified ACI 562 formulation**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= 0.80 \, \phi \Big[ 0.85 f_{ck,j} (A_{g,j} - A_{st,total}) + f_y A_{st,total} \Big] \\
P_n &= 0.80 \times 0.65 \Big[ 0.85 \times 35 \times (360{,}000 - 2865.1) + 415 \times 2865.1 \Big] \\
P_n &= 0.52 \times \Big[ 0.85 \times 35 \times 357{,}134.9 + 1{,}189{,}016.5 \Big] \\
P_n &= 0.52 \times \Big[ 10{,}625{,}766.4 + 1{,}189{,}016.5 \Big] \\
P_n &= 0.52 \times 11{,}814{,}782.9 \\
P_n &= 6{,}143{,}687 \text{ N} \\
P_n &\approx \mathbf{6144 \; kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Compare with Original Capacity
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{n,original} &= 0.65 \big[ 0.85 \times 20 \times (160{,}000 - 1256.6) + 415 \times 1256.6 \big] \\
&= 0.65 \big[ 2{,}692{,}632.4 + 521{,}489 \big] \\
&= 0.65 \times 3{,}214{,}121.4 \\
&= 2{,}089{,}179 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{2089 \; kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.5 Capacity Enhancement Factor
$$
\text{CEF} = \frac{6144}{2089} = \mathbf{2.94}
$$

> **Engineering Insight**: A 100 mm jacket **triples** the axial capacity, but the *interface bond* and *dowel action* are the real limit states. This is why **shear connectors** at ≤ **300 mm centres** are mandatory in IS 15988.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Concrete Lateral Pressure on Retrofit Formwork (Critical KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lateral pressure on the *inner* face of the retrofit formwork equals the **hydrostatic head** up to a critical height $h_c$ and remains constant above that. For SCC/micro-concrete used in jacketing:

$$
p_{max} = \gamma_c \cdot h_c
$$

For **SCC (self-compacting concrete)** with slump flow ≥ 600 mm:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{max,\,SCC} &= 0.5 \, \gamma_c \, H \quad \text{(ACI 347-14)} \\
&\text{but} \;\; p_{max,\,SCC} \geq 100 \text{ kPa (lower bound, no vibration)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For normal concrete with vibration:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{max} &= \gamma_c \cdot \min \Big[ H ; \, 0.6 + \dfrac{150}{T + 30} \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \Big] \text{ (m, kPa)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = concrete temperature in °C,
- $R$ = rate of placement (m/hr),
- $k_1$ = 1.0 for slump ≤ 100 mm; 1.2 otherwise,
- $k_2$ = 1.0 (cement type adjustment).

> **Retrofit-specific note**: Because retrofit jacket pours are **thin, tall, and confined**, the formwork sees **100 % hydrostatic pressure** for the full pour height; design the bracing for $p = \gamma_c H$, not the reduced ACI value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow — Decision Logic for Selecting a Strengthening Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Existing Structure Deficient] --> nodeB{Cause of Deficiency?}
    nodeB -- Corrosion / Damage --> nodeC[Repair + Bonded Overlay]
    nodeB -- Seismic Code Change --> nodeD[Retrofit Required]
    nodeB -- Increased Load --> nodeE[Strengthen or Add Members]
    nodeD --> nodeF{Type of Member}
    nodeF -- Column --> nodeG[RC Jacketing or FRP Wrap]
    nodeF -- Beam --> nodeH[Bottom Plate or U-Jacket]
    nodeF -- Slab --> nodeI[Topping or NSM FRP]
    nodeF -- Wall --> nodeJ[Shotcrete or FRP]
    nodeE --> nodeK{Loading Type}
    nodeK -- Axial --> nodeG
    nodeK -- Flexure --> nodeH
    nodeK -- Shear --> nodeL[Carbon FRP U-Wrap]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Custom Formwork Required]
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeI --> nodeN[Edge Forms + Screed Rails]
    nodeJ --> nodeO[Shotcrete — No Formwork]
    nodeM --> nodeP[Validate Interface Bond]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Execute Jacketing with Micro-Concrete]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Cure 7 to 14 Days]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Commissioning Test]
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The right-hand branch (nodeG → nodeM) is the *only* path that *mandates* the formwork studied in this course. All other retrofits (FRP, shotcrete, post-tensioning) require either *no* formwork or *minimal* formwork (anchor blocks, deviators).

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Formwork Assembly for Column Jacketing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Existing Column 400x400] --> nodeB[Surface Chipping to 5 mm Amplitude]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Dowel Pins at 300 c/c]
    nodeC --> nodeD[New Rebar Cage Tied In]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Two-Piece Clamshell GRP Form]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Bolt Spacers at 500 c/c]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Bracket Bracing to Existing Slab]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Pour Micro Concrete from Top]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Form Stripped at 24 hr]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Cure 7 Days Wet Hessian]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Capacity Gain Achievable]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Section (Schematic) — Beam U-Jacketing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Original Beam 300x500] --> nodeB[Bottom New Steel Plate 6 mm]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Side U-Jacket 75 mm]
    nodeC --> nodeD[New Stirrups Anchored into Slab]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Micro Concrete 35 MPa Poured in U-Form]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Three Sides Formwork Hanging from Soffit]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Pressure: gamma_c x H]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams deliberately use **plain text inside quoted labels** and **alphanumeric node IDs** (nodeA, nodeB…) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards of the engine protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PECET742]**

### Q1. Differentiate between **strengthening**, **retrofitting**, and **rehabilitation** of RC structures. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer**:

| Term | Definition | Trigger |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Strengthening** | Enhancement of load capacity beyond original design value | Increased load / function change |
| **Retrofitting** | Upgrading to satisfy *current* code provisions (e.g., seismic) | Code revision (IS 1893) |
| **Rehabilitation** | Combined *repair + retrofit* to acceptable modern state | Cumulative damage + new code |

> **Memory Anchor**: **S**trengthen = more **S**trength, **R**etrofit = new **R**ule, **R**ehabilitation = **R**estoration + upgrade.

---

### Q2. List **any three** common methods of strengthening RC columns. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer**:
1. **Concrete (RC) jacketing** with additional longitudinal bars and ties.
2. **Steel jacketing** with bolted or welded steel angles and plates.
3. **FRP wrapping** with carbon, glass, or aramid fibre composites.

*(Acceptable alternatives: external post-tensioning, section enlargement with shotcrete, supplemental steel collars.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PECET742, Module 4]**

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **methodology of RC column jacketing** as a strengthening technique. Discuss the step-by-step procedure, materials, and the **specific formwork requirements** unique to retrofit jacketing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design the **minimum jacket thickness** for a 450 mm × 450 mm column to be jacketed with 12 mm diameter longitudinal bars and 8 mm diameter ties. Apply IS 456 cover requirements and IS 15988 prescriptive minimum. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) Column Jacketing Methodology (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Condition Assessment (1 mark)**
- Visual inspection, rebound-hammer (IS 13311 Part 2), UPV (Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity) tests to determine the residual strength of the existing concrete.
- Identify cause of deficiency (corrosion, seismic, overload).

**Step 2 — Surface Preparation (1 mark)**
- Remove all loose, carbonated, or honey-combed concrete.
- Mechanically roughen to ≥ 5 mm amplitude.
- Clean with high-pressure water jet, allow to reach **SSD (Saturated Surface Dry)** condition.
- Drill and install **shear dowels** (12 mm bars, 150 mm embedment, ≤ 300 mm c/c grid).

**Step 3 — Reinforcement Addition (1 mark)**
- Add longitudinal bars (12–20 mm) with corner & intermediate bars.
- Add closely spaced ties (8–10 mm) at 100 mm c/c in plastic hinge regions (top and bottom of column).
- Lap new bars with existing bars using **100 mm weld** or **mechanical couplers** where exposed.

**Step 4 — Formwork Erection (2 marks) — *the core of this course***
- Use **two-piece bolted clamshell GRP/steel forms** sized to leave 75–100 mm jacket.
- Spacers maintain cover and ensure **uniform jacket thickness**.
- **Bracing**: formwork must be braced *against the existing structure* (slab soffit, beam, or floor) using adjustable props and brackets — not against the ground.
- The formwork must be **leak-proof at the interface**; use compressible foam tape or sealant at the base.
- All bolts and walers must be **removable** without disturbing the new concrete.
- Provide a **pouring hopper** at the top (≥ 150 mm opening) to pour micro-concrete without segregation.

**Step 5 — Pouring and Curing (1 mark)**
- Use **micro-concrete (M35+)** with 10 mm max aggregate, or **SCC**.
- Pour in **lifts of 500 mm**, lightly rod or use SCC to avoid honeycombing.
- Strip forms after **24 hours**, begin **wet curing for 7–14 days** with Hessian and water spray.

**Step 6 — Validation (1 mark)**
- Load test (as per IS 456 Cl. 36) if required.
- Non-destructive re-testing.

---

#### Model Answer — (b) Jacket Thickness Design (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & B = 450 \text{ mm (square column)} \\
& d_{b,l} = 12 \text{ mm (new longitudinal bar)} \\
& d_{b,t} = 8 \text{ mm (new tie)} \\
& c_{min} = 25 \text{ mm (IS 456 Cl. 26.4.1 for column)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 1: Constructive cover requirement on each face} \\
t_{j,req} &= 2 c_{min} + d_{b,t} + d_{b,l} + 1 c_{min,\text{interior spacing}} \\
t_{j,req} &= 25 + 8 + 12 + 25 \\
t_{j,req} &= 70 \text{ mm (for one-side jacket)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: IS 15988 prescriptive minimum} \\
t_{j,min} &= 75 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Governing value} \\
t_j &= \max(70, 75) = \mathbf{75 \; mm} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: New column size} \\
B_{j} &= 450 + 2(75) = \mathbf{600 \; mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: The minimum jacket thickness is **75 mm** on each face, increasing the column size to 600 × 600 mm. *(3 marks for the constructive calculation, 2 marks for the prescriptive IS 15988 check, 2 marks for the conclusion & final dimensions.)*

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2)

**(a)** Discuss the **formwork systems** used for column, beam, and slab jacketing. Highlight the differences between new-construction formwork and retrofit formwork. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A retrofit jacketing project uses **self-compacting micro-concrete (M40)** poured to a height of 3.5 m in a 100 mm thick column jacket. Determine the **maximum lateral pressure** on the formwork as per ACI 347-14. Take $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) Formwork for Jacketing (7 Marks)

| Element | New-Construction Formwork | Retrofit Jacketing Formwork |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Column** | Adjustable steel/plywood panels, braced to ground | Custom GRP/steel clamshells, braced *to existing structure*, leak-proof at interface |
| **Beam** | Soffit + sides, propped from floor | Hanging from existing beam/slab, U-shaped, anchored into slab by drilled bolts |
| **Slab** | Table-form / flying form | Topping overlay using edge forms + screed rails, no soffit required |
| **Bracing source** | Ground, floor below | Existing structure (uncertain stiffness) |
| **Tolerance** | Generous, ± 5 mm | Tight, ± 2 mm to fit irregular substrate |
| **Reusability** | High (50–100 cycles) | Single use (custom geometry) |
| **Cost share** | 5–8 % of project | 15–25 % of project |

**Key Engineering Challenges in Retrofit Formwork** (3 marks):
1. **Bracing into unknown substrate** — pull-out tests may be needed on the existing concrete.
2. **Sealing the interface** to prevent grout leakage.
3. **Working in occupied/partially loaded buildings** — vibration, head-room, and access constraints.
4. **Congeried reinforcement** — close ties and dowels make it impossible to vibrate, hence SCC or micro-concrete is mandatory.

---

#### Model Answer — (b) Lateral Pressure on Retrofit Formwork (7 Marks)

**Given**: SCC, $H = 3.5$ m, $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³, $T$ not specified (assume $T = 25$ °C).

**Step 1: ACI 347-14 formula for SCC** (no vibration):
$$
p_{max,\,SCC} = \gamma_c \cdot H \quad \text{ (full hydrostatic for SCC)}
$$
*with* $\; p_{max} \geq 100$ kPa (lower bound when no external vibration is applied).

**Step 2: Calculate full hydrostatic**:
$$
p_{hydro} = 25 \times 3.5 = 87.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Apply ACI lower bound for SCC**:
$$
p_{max} = \max(87.5,\; 100) = \mathbf{100 \; kPa}
$$

**Step 4: Cross-check with normal-concrete formula** (if the concrete were vibrated):
$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{max,NC} &= \gamma_c \left( 0.6 + \dfrac{150}{T+30} k_1 k_2 \right) \\
\text{with } T=25,\; k_1 = 1.2,\; k_2 = 1.0 \\
p_{max,NC} &= 25 \left( 0.6 + \dfrac{150}{55}(1.2)(1.0) \right) \\
&= 25 \left( 0.6 + 3.273 \right) \\
&= 25 \times 3.873 \\
&= 96.8 \text{ kPa} \;\; \text{but} \;\; p \geq 100 \text{ kPa always} \\
\Rightarrow p_{max,NC} &= 100 \text{ kPa (governed by lower bound)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Design the waler/brace spacing** (qualitative): For plywood formwork (allowable $p_{allow}$ = 50 kPa for 18 mm ply at 300 mm c/c), the 100 kPa pressure requires closer waler spacing of **≈ 150 mm** or thicker 25 mm plywood.

> **Final Answer**: $p_{max} = \mathbf{100 \text{ kPa}}$ — formwork must be designed for **100 kPa, not 87.5 kPa**, because of the ACI 347 lower-bound clause for SCC and thin retrofit pours.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this module**:
> 1. **Confusing "strengthening" with "retrofitting"** in a 2-mark question — examiners award 0 if the definitions are reversed. Memorise the *trigger*: load ↑ = strengthen, code ↑ = retrofit.
> 2. **Forgetting the ACI 347 lower bound of 100 kPa** for SCC — students commonly calculate $p = \gamma_c H = 87.5$ kPa and lose 2 marks for not applying the minimum.
> 3. **Ignoring the cover requirement** when finding minimum jacket thickness — the *constructive* minimum (75–100 mm) is often more than the *code* minimum (75 mm).
> 4. **Treating retrofit formwork bracing like new-construction bracing** — bracing must be *into the existing structure*, not the floor below, and this must be explicitly stated in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Skipping interface bond check** — a column jacketing calculation without a $\tau_v$ demand vs $\tau_v$ allowable comparison is incomplete (loses 2 marks).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Strengthening** = more load; **Retrofitting** = new code; **Rehabilitation** = both.
- Driving factors: code change, function change, deterioration, damage, design defect.
- **Five** principal methods: (1) Section enlargement, (2) EBR steel plate, (3) FRP wrap/NSM, (4) External post-tensioning, (5) Supplemental members.
- Column jacketing minimum thickness: **75 mm** (IS 15988) — but cover + rebar dictates **90–110 mm** in practice.
- Formwork pressure for SCC = full hydrostatic or **100 kPa** lower bound (ACI 347).
- Bracing in retrofit formwork: into *existing* structure, not the ground.
- Bond stress at interface: $\tau_{bond} = P/(bL)$; allowable ≈ **0.6 N/mm²** for roughened surface.
- Dowel pins at ≤ **300 mm c/c** for shear transfer.
- New concrete $f_{ck,j} \geq 0.8 f_{ck,old}$ (compatibility).
- Always perform non-destructive testing **before and after** intervention.
- Always perform a **load test** if the jacketed member is critical (IS 456 Cl. 36).
- Formwork cost share: **5–8 %** new build, **15–25 %** retrofit.
- Mermaid diagrams and tables are **graphical aids** — in the exam, use **neat labelled sketches** to score full marks on jacketing procedure questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies of Rehabilitation Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET742 - FORMWORK ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of Rehabilitation Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Rehabilitation in Formwork Engineering** refers to the specialized domain of temporary works design, fabrication, and deployment used for the **restoration, strengthening, repair, and retrofitting** of existing structural elements (beams, columns, slabs, bridges, heritage buildings, marine structures) where conventional new-construction formwork systems cannot be directly applied due to **geometric, spatial, and structural constraints**.

Rehabilitation formwork is fundamentally different from new-construction formwork because the "mold" must conform to a **pre-existing, often irregular, partially damaged, or distorted substrate**, rather than to a theoretical geometric ideal. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes this under the broader umbrella of "Special Formwork Systems," emphasizing adaptive geometry, load transfer through distressed parent concrete, and minimum-disturbance installation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Rehabilitation projects are a **high-weightage application area** in Module 4 because they test the student's ability to integrate knowledge of conventional formwork (Modules 1–3) with problem-solving creativity, load assessment of distressed members, and selection of non-standard materials (GRP, GFRP, textile formwork).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **tailor mending a 100-year-old coat** versus sewing a brand-new coat from a fresh pattern.

- **New construction (new coat):** You have flat fabric, standard measurements, and complete freedom to cut and shape. This is your conventional formwork — symmetric, repeatable, modular.
- **Rehabilitation (mending the old coat):** The existing fabric is torn, distorted, has patches, and you must work around the **armhole that's already there, the worn-out lining, and the irregular hem**. Your "needle and thread" become the formwork, and your "patch" becomes the fresh concrete or repair mortar. The skill lies in *conforming to imperfection while restoring function*.

In this analogy:
- **The old coat** = the existing distressed structural member
- **The patch fabric** = new repair concrete / micro-concrete / grout
- **The tailor's skill** = selection of custom formwork, GFRP, lost shutter, or shotcrete
- **The thread that holds it** = bond between old concrete and new concrete (formwork's secondary role — confining and shaping the repair material until it gains strength)

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Values

The following are **industry-standard metrics** used in rehabilitation formwork design (per IS 15988, ACI 546R, and CIRIA C579):

| Metric | Standard Value | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Repair concrete min. compressive strength | **≥ 40 MPa (M40)** | For structural jacketing |
| Bond strength at repair interface | **≥ 1.5 MPa** | Tension pull-off test (ASTM C1583) |
| Minimum repair depth | **≥ 15 mm** | For cover replacement |
| Maximum permissible vibration | **< 2.5 mm/s** | To prevent bond disruption |
| Typical formwork pressure in repair | **30–60 kN/m²** | Lower than new pours due to smaller lifts |
| Service-life extension target | **30–50 years** | Post-rehabilitation design life |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Note:** Always quote pressures in **kN/m²** (not kg/m²) and concrete grade by **M-number** (M30, M40) in KTU answers. Mixing units is a common deduction point.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Why Rehabilitation Formwork is Different

Rehabilitation projects differ from new construction in **four fundamental dimensions**. Each dimension dictates a different formwork strategy:

1. **Geometric Imperfection:**
   Existing structures have **construction tolerances, creep deformations, settlement-induced distortions, and damage-induced losses** of section. The formwork must be **surveyed, scanned (using laser scanning / photogrammetry), and custom-fabricated** to match the as-built geometry, not the design geometry.

2. **Confined Access:**
   Repair sites are often **inside basements, beneath existing beams, between closely spaced columns, or inside heritage vaults** where standard crane-handled formwork panels cannot be deployed. This forces the use of **hand-assembled modular systems, small-panel formwork, or stay-in-place formwork (SIF)**.

3. **Load Transfer Through Distressed Concrete:**
   When you pour a jacket around a damaged column, the **fresh concrete must bond to the parent concrete** and act compositely. The formwork must therefore:
   - Provide **uniform lateral confinement** to ensure intimate contact
   - Allow **escape of air and bleed water** (through weep holes or permeable formwork liners)
   - Resist **higher-than-expected hydrostatic pressure** if the column is tall and the lift is deep

4. **Reversibility & Heritage Constraints:**
   In heritage buildings, the formwork must be **removable without damaging the original fabric**. This eliminates nailed timber shuttering and pushes the designer toward **mechanical clamping, vacuum-formed GRP shells, and textile formwork**.

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Rehabilitation Formwork Design

A KTU-standard answer should always structure the discussion around these five pillars:

1. **Pillar 1 – Condition Assessment:** Non-destructive testing (rebound hammer, UPV, GPR, cover meter) to determine the extent of distress.
2. **Pillar 2 – Repair Material Selection:** Polymer-modified mortar, micro-concrete, self-compacting concrete (SCC), or epoxy grout — each with a different formwork pressure profile.
3. **Pillar 3 – Formwork Geometry:** Custom-cut, hand-fabricated, or stay-in-place.
4. **Pillar 4 – Load Path & Shoring:** Propping the distressed member before, during, and after the pour (sequence of stressing/un-stressing).
5. **Pillar 5 – Quality Assurance:** Cube tests, pull-off tests, and continuous monitoring.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** These formulas are tested directly in numerical problems. Memorize the *form*, the *units*, and the *boundary conditions*.

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application / Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Modified Lateral Pressure (Repair Concrete)** | $P_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \cdot k_{r}$ | $\rho$ = density of fresh concrete (kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = lift height (m), $k_{r}$ = repair-pressure coefficient (0.5–0.7) | Used when lift is small and SCC is used; $k_{r}$ accounts for thixotropic loss |
| **Standard Lateral Pressure (ACI SP-4)** | $P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left(7.6 + \frac{800R}{T+18}\right)$ | $C_w$ = unit weight factor, $C_c$ = chemistry factor, $R$ = rate of pour (m/hr), $T$ = concrete temp (°C) | Cap: $P_{max} \le 150 \cdot h$ kN/m² (hydrostatic limit) |
| **Shoring Load on Distressed Member** | $P_{shore} = \alpha \cdot \left( D + L_{imp} \right)$ | $\alpha$ = load redistribution factor (1.1–1.4), $D$ = dead load, $L_{imp}$ = impact load | Pre-repair propping must carry full dead load + 25% live |
| **Bond Stress at Interface** | $\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{V}{b \cdot L_{dev}}$ | $V$ = shear force at interface (N), $b$ = width (mm), $L_{dev}$ = development length (mm) | Min $\tau_{bond}$ = 1.5 MPa for monolithic action |
| **Formwork Stiffness Criterion** | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI} \le \dfrac{L}{360}$ | $w$ = distributed load, $L$ = span, $E$ = modulus, $I$ = moment of inertia | Deflection limit for visible finishes |
| **Permeable Formwork Flow Rate** | $Q = k_{p} \cdot A \cdot \Delta P$ | $k_{p}$ = permeability (m/s), $A$ = liner area, $\Delta P$ = hydrostatic head | Used to drain bleed water in thick jackets |
| **GFRP Shell Hoop Stress** | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | $P$ = internal pressure, $r$ = radius, $t$ = shell thickness | For circular column jackets using GFRP permanent form |

> [!TIP]
> Note the use of `\cdot`, `\le`, `\delta`, and `\sigma` for clean typesetting. **Never use the `|` symbol in any table row** — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as the KTU engine uses raw markdown parsers that break on `|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles studied here are applied in:
- **Bridge rehabilitation:** Carbon-fiber wrap with GFRP stay-in-place formwork
- **Heritage building restoration:** Lime-mortar-compatible timber formwork with hand-carved profiles
- **Marine pile jacketing:** Pre-fabricated GFRP shells with grouted annulus
- **Nuclear containment repairs:** Pre-stressed formwork systems to minimize crack propagation
- **Seismic retrofitting:** RC jacketing columns with custom column-formwork panels

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Formwork Pressure for a Column Jacketing Repair

**Problem Statement:**
A 4 m tall, 400 mm × 400 mm reinforced concrete column in a 30-year-old office building has been identified as **severely distressed** during a condition assessment. A 100 mm thick RC jacket is to be provided using **M40 self-compacting concrete (SCC)**. The column is to be poured in **one continuous lift** of 4 m. The concrete temperature is **28 °C** and the unit weight is **25 kN/m³**. Compute the maximum lateral pressure that the formwork must resist, using the ACI SP-4 modified approach for SCC.

### Step 1 — Identify the Inputs

We are given:
- $h = 4$ m (lift height)
- $R$ = rate of pour (assume 1.5 m/hr, typical for SCC in repair)
- $T = 28$ °C (concrete temperature)
- $C_w$ = unit weight factor (for $\gamma = 25$ kN/m³, $C_w = 0.25 \cdot \gamma - 2.5 = 0.25 \cdot 25 - 2.5 = 3.75$, but the more common tabulated value is $C_w = 1.0$ for normal-weight)
- $C_c$ = chemistry factor (for SCC with retarder, $C_c = 1.0$; for plain cement, $C_c = 1.2$)

> **Conversion Logic:** The unit weight factor in ACI SP-4 is often taken as $C_w = 1.0$ for $\gamma_c$ between 23.5 and 25 kN/m³. We will use $C_w = 1.0$ for simplicity.

### Step 2 — Apply the ACI SP-4 Equation

The standard formula is:

$$
P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left( 7.6 + \frac{800 \cdot R}{T + 18} \right) \quad \text{(kN/m}^2\text{)}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
P_{max} = 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \left( 7.6 + \frac{800 \cdot 1.5}{28 + 18} \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Term Inside the Bracket

$$
\frac{800 \cdot 1.5}{28 + 18} = \frac{1200}{46} = 26.087
$$

So the bracket becomes:

$$
7.6 + 26.087 = 33.687
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $P_{max}$

$$
P_{max} = 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 33.687 = 33.69 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Hydrostatic Cap

The hydrostatic limit is:

$$
P_{hydro} = 0.025 \cdot h = 0.025 \cdot 4 \cdot 25 = 2.5 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2 \quad \text{... wait, this is incorrect}
$$

Let me correct: hydrostatic pressure is $\gamma_c \cdot h = 25 \cdot 4 = 100$ kN/m².

The ACI cap is $P_{max} \le \gamma_c \cdot h$:

$$
P_{hydro} = 25 \cdot 4 = 100 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

Since $33.69 \le 100$, the cap does not govern.

### Step 6 — Apply the Repair Modifier $k_r$

For SCC in repair, the pressure is reduced due to thixotropy and friction along the existing column surface. Empirically, $k_r = 0.6$:

$$
P_{design} = P_{max} \cdot k_r = 33.69 \cdot 0.6 = 20.21 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 7 — Final Answer with Units

$$
\boxed{P_{design} \approx 20.2 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2}
$$

This is the **uniform design pressure** that the custom formwork panels must resist across the 4 m height.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A value of 20 kN/m² corresponds to a **2 m head of water** (since $\gamma_{water} = 9.81$ kN/m³, so 20/9.81 ≈ 2.04 m). The repair concrete therefore behaves as if it were a 2 m fluid column, not the full 4 m — a major saving in formwork cost.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Rehabilitation Scenarios

| Scenario | Distress Type | Preferred Formwork | Pressure Range (kN/m²) | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Column Jacketing | Corrosion-induced spalling | Custom GFRP shell + grout | 15–25 | Very High |
| Beam Strengthening | Flexural under-capacity | Bottom-propped + side shutter | 20–35 | High |
| Slab Overlay | Carbonation, cover loss | Permeable formwork liner | 10–18 | Medium |
| Bridge Pier Repair | Chloride ingress | Stay-in-place GFRP + epoxy grout | 25–40 | High |
| Heritage Arch Restoration | Masonry decay | Lime-compatible timber with hand carving | 5–12 | Low (specialized) |
| Shear Wall Jacketing | Seismic deficiency | Custom steel panel with ties | 30–50 | High |
| Pile Rehabilitation | Marine corrosion | Prefab GRP sleeve, grouted | 40–60 | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark questions, **always draw this matrix as a table** in your answer. Examiners award marks for structured presentation, and a well-formatted table is worth at least 1–2 presentation marks per question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Rehabilitation Formwork Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision flow and process architecture** for a typical rehabilitation project, from initial condition assessment through final quality audit. This serves as the master template for a 14-mark KTU essay question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Structure Identified] --> A2[Condition Assessment NDT]
    A2 --> A3{Is Distress Significant?}
    A3 -- No --> A4[Monitor Only]
    A3 -- Yes --> A5[Detailed Survey 3D Laser Scan]
    A5 --> A6[Formwork Geometry Finalized]
    A6 --> B1[Select Formwork Material]
    B1 --> B2[Custom Timber]
    B1 --> B3[GFRP Stay In Place]
    B1 --> B4[Textile Formwork]
    B1 --> B5[Steel Modular Panel]
    B2 --> C1[Propping and Shoring]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Calculate Lateral Pressure]
    C2 --> C3[Design Formwork Panels]
    C3 --> D1[Fabrication Workshop]
    D1 --> D2[Site Installation]
    D2 --> D3[Concrete Pour with QC]
    D3 --> D4[Curing and Monitoring]
    D4 --> E1[Pull Off Bond Test]
    E1 --> E2{Bond Strength OK?}
    E2 -- No --> E3[Rehabilitate Repair]
    E2 -- Yes --> F1[Issue Completion Certificate]
    F1 --> G1[End: Structure Restored]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B3`, `F1`) with no reserved keywords. Labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text without any bold, italic, or HTML formatting, in compliance with the engine's render rules.

## 4.2 Schematic: Cross-Section of a Typical Column Jacketing Repair

The diagram below shows the **layered architecture** of a column jacketing repair — the most commonly tested rehabilitation detail in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Existing Distressed Column] --> L2[Surface Preparation Chipping]
    L2 --> L3[Primer or Bonding Agent]
    L3 --> L4[New Reinforcement Cage]
    L4 --> L5[Formwork Spacer Blocks]
    L5 --> L6[GFRP or Timber Formwork Shell]
    L6 --> L7[Pour M40 SCC Repair Concrete]
    L7 --> L8[Curing 14 Days Minimum]
    L8 --> L9[Formwork Striking After 7 Days]
    L9 --> L10[Finished Jacketed Column]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Rehabilitation Formwork Material

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is Structure Heritage Listed?} -->|Yes| Q2[Use Lime Compatible Timber]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is Access Confined less than 600 mm?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Use Hand Assembled Small Panels]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5{Is Geometry Circular?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Use GFRP Stay In Place]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7{Is it a Marine Environment?}
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q8[Use GRP Shell with Grout]
    Q7 -->|No| Q9[Use Standard Steel Modular Panel]
    Q2 --> R1[Selected Material]
    Q4 --> R1
    Q6 --> R1
    Q8 --> R1
    Q9 --> R1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between "restoration," "rehabilitation," and "retrofitting" of structures in the context of formwork engineering. Give one example of a formwork system used in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Meaning | Formwork Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Restoration** | Returning a structure to its **original appearance and function** (e.g., heritage façade repair) | Lime-compatible hand-carved timber formwork |
| **Rehabilitation** | Improving the structure to a **desired level of performance**, possibly above the original | RC jacketing with GFRP stay-in-place formwork |
| **Retrofitting** | Upgrading the structure to meet **new code requirements** (e.g., seismic retrofit) | Carbon-fiber wrap with custom column forms |

> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for each correct term + 1 mark for the example integration. The examiner expects the answer in **tabular form** for full marks.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**List the four primary reasons why formwork design for rehabilitation projects is more complex than for new construction projects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Geometric irregularity** of the existing substrate (1 mark)
2. **Confined access** requiring hand-assembled or modular small-panel formwork (1 mark)
3. **Bond requirements** between old and new concrete imposing pressure-profile constraints (0.5 mark)
4. **Reversibility** constraints in heritage projects (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Pitfall 1:** Students forget to mention **propping / shoring of the distressed member** *before* the formwork is loaded. This is a **2-mark deduction** in 14-mark answers.
> - **Common Pitfall 2:** Students state "lateral pressure" but **omit the unit (kN/m²)** or write it as kg/m². **Always write units in SI.**
> - **Common Pitfall 3:** Students fail to mention **quality-control tests** (cube test, pull-off bond test) at the end. Examiners look for the *completion loop*.
> - **Common Pitfall 4:** Use of pipes `\vert` inside tables can break the markdown in some answer-sheet digitization portals. **Always use `\vert` or write "absolute value of" in words.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Case Study – RC Column Jacketing Using GFRP Stay-In-Place Formwork**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe, with the aid of a neat sketch, the **step-by-step procedure** for rehabilitating a 400 mm × 400 mm distressed RC column using a 100 mm thick GFRP stay-in-place (SIP) formwork jacket. Include details of (i) surface preparation, (ii) reinforcement detailing, (iii) spacer blocks, (iv) sealing of the formwork base, and (v) grout/concrete pouring.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **maximum lateral pressure** that the GFRP shell must resist, given: lift height = 3.5 m, concrete grade = M40 SCC, unit weight = 24.5 kN/m³, rate of pour = 1.2 m/hr, concrete temperature = 30 °C, and $C_c = 1.0$ for SCC with retarder. State the design pressure after applying the repair modifier $k_r = 0.65$.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Step 1 — Propping (1 mark):** Before any work begins, the distressed column is supported using **4 adjustable steel props (acrow props) at the four corners** of the slab above, transferring the dead load to the floor below. The propping remains until the new jacket gains 75% of $f_{ck}$.

2. **Step 2 — Surface Preparation (1 mark):** The existing column surface is **chipped to a depth of 15–25 mm** using a pneumatic breaker to expose sound concrete. All loose material, dust, and corroded reinforcement are removed. The exposed rebar is wire-brushed and, if section loss exceeds 20%, supplemented with new bars welded or lapped to the existing.

3. **Step 3 — Bonding Agent (0.5 mark):** A **polymer-modified cementitious bonding agent** (e.g., SBR latex-modified slurry) is brush-applied to the chipped surface in a wet-on-wet condition.

4. **Step 4 — Reinforcement Cage (1 mark):** A new cage of **8 numbers 12 mm dia. longitudinal bars with 8 mm dia. ties at 150 mm c/c** is erected around the column, maintaining a **25 mm clear cover** on all sides using **plastic spacer blocks**.

5. **Step 5 — GFRP Formwork Installation (1.5 marks):** Two semi-cylindrical GFRP shells (each ≈ 600 mm wide, 3.5 m tall, 6 mm thick) are placed around the column and **bolted together with stainless-steel straps** at 200 mm c/c. The **base is sealed** with a quick-setting cementitious mortar (M60) to prevent grout leakage. **Weep holes** (10 mm dia.) are drilled at 300 mm c/c to allow air and bleed-water escape.

6. **Step 6 — Pouring (1 mark):** M40 SCC is poured from the top through a 100 mm dia. tremie pipe inserted to within 200 mm of the base, ensuring no segregation. The pour is continuous and is vibrated externally on the GFRP shell using a form vibrator (not an internal needle, to avoid disturbing the bond).

7. **Step 7 — Curing and Striking (1 mark):** Wet Hessian wrapping is applied for **14 days** for moist curing. The external props are released only after **7 days** (formwork striking) and the **acrow props after 14 days** when cube strength exceeds 32 MPa.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the ACI SP-4 equation:**

$$
P_{max} = C_w \cdot C_c \cdot \left( 7.6 + \frac{800 \cdot R}{T + 18} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$C_w = 1.0$ (normal-weight concrete, $\gamma_c \approx 24.5$ kN/m³), $C_c = 1.0$, $R = 1.2$ m/hr, $T = 30$ °C.

$$
P_{max} = 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \left( 7.6 + \frac{800 \cdot 1.2}{30 + 18} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracket:**

$$
\frac{960}{48} = 20.0
$$

$$
7.6 + 20.0 = 27.6
$$

**Step 4 — Check the hydrostatic cap:**

$$
P_{hydro} = 24.5 \cdot 3.5 = 85.75 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

Since $27.6 \le 85.75$, the cap does not govern.

**Step 5 — Apply the repair modifier:**

$$
P_{design} = 27.6 \cdot 0.65 = 17.94 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — State the final answer:**

$$
\boxed{P_{design} \approx 18.0 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2}
$$

> **Marking Distribution for (b):** 2 marks for the formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for the hydrostatic cap check, 1 mark for the modifier, 1 mark for the final boxed answer with correct units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Case Study – Bridge Pier Rehabilitation Using Prefabricated GRP Shell**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Alternate]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 1.2 m diameter, 8 m tall bridge pier in a coastal environment shows **severe chloride-induced corrosion** with 30% rebar section loss. Propose a **complete rehabilitation scheme** using a **prefabricated Glass-Reinforced Plastic (GRP) shell with cementitious grout** as the stay-in-place formwork. Your answer must include (i) the survey and assessment, (ii) the shell design philosophy, (iii) the grout specification, and (iv) the quality-control test sequence.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The annular gap between the existing pier (1.2 m dia.) and the GRP shell (1.4 m dia., i.e., 100 mm annular gap) is to be filled with M45 non-shrink grout. Calculate (i) the volume of grout required, (ii) the hoop tensile stress in the GRP shell assuming the grout behaves as a fluid during pouring, and (iii) the bursting force per metre height. Given: GRP shell thickness $t = 8$ mm, grout unit weight $\gamma_g = 23$ kN/m³, and pour rate $R = 2$ m/hr.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Survey and Assessment (2 marks):** Underwater visual inspection by divers, **half-cell potential mapping**, **chloride profiling** (at 10 mm depth intervals up to 80 mm), and **rebar cover meter survey**. The rebar section loss is calculated from the corrosion rate. The hydrographic conditions (tidal range, wave height) are documented to decide working windows.

2. **Shell Design Philosophy (2 marks):** The GRP shell is **custom-fabricated in two half-shells** (each 4 m tall, joined at site) using **isophthalic polyester resin with E-glass fibre reinforcement**. The shell is designed as a **permanently installed formwork** that also acts as **additional confinement** (akin to a composite jacket). Internal **shear keys** are provided on the inner face for bond with the grout.

3. **Grout Specification (1.5 marks):** **M45 non-shrink, free-flowing cementitious grout** with **anti-washout admixture** (to resist washout during tidal submersion). Maximum aggregate size 6 mm. Initial setting time ≥ 4 hours. Compressive strength at 28 days ≥ 45 MPa. Bond strength to existing concrete ≥ 1.7 MPa.

4. **Quality Control (1.5 marks):**
   - **Pre-pour:** Grout trial mix cube tests, shell dimensional check, sealing inspection.
   - **During pour:** Continuous monitoring of grout head, visual check for leaks.
   - **Post-pour:** Cube tests at 7 and 28 days, **pull-off bond test** at 6 locations, **ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV) scan** to detect voids, and **load test** of the pier if specified.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Part (i) — Volume of Grout (2 marks):**

The annulus has outer dia. = 1.4 m, inner dia. = 1.2 m, height = 8 m.

$$
A_{annulus} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left( D_o^2 - D_i^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left( 1.4^2 - 1.2^2 \right)
$$

$$
= \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left( 1.96 - 1.44 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.52 = 0.4084 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
V = A_{annulus} \cdot h = 0.4084 \cdot 8 = 3.267 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

Adding 10% wastage:

$$
V_{total} = 3.267 \cdot 1.10 = 3.59 \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Part (ii) — Hoop Tensile Stress in GRP (3 marks):**

For a thin-walled cylinder under internal pressure, the hoop stress is:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}
$$

The internal pressure is the hydrostatic head of grout at the base. During pouring, the maximum head is the full 8 m:

$$
P_{max} = \gamma_g \cdot h = 23 \cdot 8 = 184 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

(Note: ACI rate-of-pour cap would give a lower value, but for non-shrink grout, the hydrostatic limit is the conservative design value as the grout has very low early stiffness and behaves as a fluid until initial set.)

The radius is the **mean radius** of the shell: $r = (1.4 - 0.008)/2 = 0.696$ m, or conservatively use $r = 0.7$ m.

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{184 \cdot 0.7}{0.008} = \frac{128.8}{0.008} = 16{,}100 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2 = 16.1 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Part (iii) — Bursting Force per Metre Height (2 marks):**

The bursting force per unit height is the integral of pressure over the diameter:

$$
F_{burst/m} = P \cdot D = 184 \cdot 1.4 = 257.6 \;\; \text{kN/m}
$$

> **Marking Distribution for (b):** 2 marks for volume (with units), 3 marks for hoop stress derivation, 2 marks for bursting force. The examiner expects a **final boxed answer** for each sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Case Studies of Rehabilitation Projects":**

- [x] **Rehabilitation ≠ New Construction** — always emphasize geometric, spatial, and bond constraints.
- [x] **Five Pillars of Design:** Assessment → Material → Geometry → Shoring → QC.
- [x] **Pressure Equation (Memorize):** $P_{max} = C_w C_c (7.6 + 800R/(T+18))$, capped at $\gamma_c h$.
- [x] **Always state the hydrostatic cap** $P \le \gamma_c h$ and check it explicitly.
- [x] **Repair modifier $k_r$** (0.5–0.7) for SCC in jacketing — examiner loves this.
- [x] **GFRP / GRP shells** are the most-tested stay-in-place formwork in KTU papers.
- [x] **Propping the distressed member** must be done *before* formwork erection — easy 2-mark grab.
- [x] **Quality Control triad:** Cube test (strength), Pull-off test (bond), UPV (void detection).
- [x] **Use SI units always** (kN/m², MPa, m³). Mixing CGS/SI loses marks.
- [x] **Heritage projects** → lime-compatible, hand-carved, fully reversible formwork.
- [x] **Marine projects** → anti-washout grout, GRP shell, tidal working window awareness.
- [x] **Tables and neat sketches** are mandatory for 7-mark and 14-mark answers.
- [x] **Avoid pipes `\vert` in tables** — use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent digitization errors.
- [x] **Always box the final numerical answer** with explicit units.
- [x] **Mention the curing period** (typically 14 days for M40+) — common omission.
- [x] **Mention striking time** (typically 24–48 hours for sides, 7 days for soffits) — common omission.
- [x] **Sequencing matters:** Prop → Chip → Bond → Reinforce → Form → Pour → Cure → Strike → Test.

> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Case-study questions test **integration** of formwork theory with **practical engineering judgment**. A correct numerical answer without context loses 30–40% of the marks. Always wrap your numbers in a *narrative*: *why* the pressure is what it is, *how* the formwork was chosen, *what* the QC protocol is. The examiner awards marks for **engineering reasoning**, not arithmetic.
